topics.title: Basic Operating Elements of Public Transit
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET833 - PUBLIC TRANSPORTATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Operating Elements of Public Transit

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Public Transportation** is defined as a shared passenger transport service that is available to the general public, operates on established routes according to published schedules, and charges predetermined fares. The **Basic Operating Elements of Public Transit** are the fundamental, interacting components that together determine how efficiently a transit system delivers mobility to its users.

According to KTU Module 1, the basic operating elements are broadly classified into four interdependent pillars:

> [!IMPORTANT] **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Four Pillars of Transit Operations**
> 1. **The Physical Way (Right-of-Way / RoW)** – the path along which vehicles travel.
> 2. **The Vehicle (Technology / Rolling Stock)** – the carrier unit that transports passengers.
> 3. **The Terminals and Stops (Stations / Interchanges)** – the access/egress nodes of the network.
> 4. **The Operating Plan (Service Plan)** – schedules, frequencies, routes, and fare structures.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The City as a Human Body"

Imagine a city as a living human body:

- The **Right-of-Way** is the **circulatory system (veins and arteries)** — fixed pathways along which flow happens.
- The **Vehicle** is the **red blood cell** — carrying oxygen (passengers) from one organ to another.
- The **Stops / Terminals** are the **major organs (heart, lungs, kidneys)** — points where exchange happens (boarding/alighting).
- The **Operating Plan** is the **nervous system** — invisible but coordinating every movement and rhythm.

Just as a body fails if blood flow stops, a city fails if its transit arteries are blocked. The operating elements are the *vital signs* a traffic engineer must monitor to keep a transit system healthy.

## 1.3 The Public Transit Spectrum

Public transit is **not one single thing** — it is a *spectrum* of service types, ranging from highly organized rail systems to informal shared-ride services. The KTU framework classifies transit modes by two parameters: **Right-of-Way (RoW)** and **Technology**.

| Service Category | Right-of-Way Type | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Rail Rapid Transit (Metro)** | Fully Grade-Separated | Kochi Metro, Delhi Metro |
| **Light Rail Transit (LRT)** | Mostly Grade-Separated | Kolkata Tram (modern LRT) |
| **Bus Rapid Transit (BRT)** | Exclusive Lanes | Ahmedabad Janmarg, Hubballi–Dharwad |
| **Street Transit (City Bus)** | Mixed Traffic | KSRTC, BEST, TNSTC |
| **Paratransit (Auto/Rickshaw)** | Mixed Traffic | Shared Autos in Kerala |

> [!NOTE] **Examiner's Tip:** In KTU answers, always begin by stating the **mode of transit** first, then describe its operating elements. A vague answer that doesn't identify the mode is marked down.

## 1.4 Key Performance Indicators (KPIs) of Transit Operations

Every public transit system is judged by a small set of operational metrics. These are the **"vital signs"** the KTU examiner expects you to know:

- **System Speed (km/h)** — the actual average pace including stops.
- **Capacity (pax/hr)** — maximum passengers moved past a point in one hour.
- **Frequency (veh/hr)** — how often a vehicle arrives.
- **Headway (min)** — time between two consecutive vehicles.
- **Dwell Time (sec)** — time a vehicle spends stopped at a station.
- **Load Factor (%)** — actual passengers ÷ seat capacity.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Definition — System Speed**
> The System Speed is defined as the **average speed of a transit vehicle over its entire round-trip journey, including all station dwell times and terminal layovers**, expressed in **km/h**. It is one of the most fundamental indicators of service quality.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Anatomy of a Transit Trip — The Five Sequential Stages

Any journey on a public transit system, from origin to destination, consists of exactly **five sequential stages**. This is a *mandatory* framework that KTU expects every student to internalize:

1. **Access (Egress) Stage** — Walking/biking from origin to the first transit stop. (This is **NOT** part of the transit vehicle's journey.)
2. **Line-Haul Stage** — The main movement of the vehicle between origin and destination stops.
3. **Dwell Stage** — Time spent stationary at stops for boarding and alighting.
4. **Transfer Stage** — Moving between routes at interchange stations (if required).
5. **Egress Stage** — Walking from the final stop to the actual destination.

The **total door-to-door travel time** is the sum of the time spent in all five stages. A transit system is considered "good" only when this total is competitive with the private car alternative.

## 2.2 The Four Operating Elements — Detailed Breakdown

### Element 1: Right-of-Way (RoW) — Category A, B, and C

The **Right-of-Way** is the legal and physical space in which the transit vehicle moves. KTU classifies RoW into three hierarchical categories, in increasing order of performance:

> [!NOTE] **Right-of-Way Classification (KTU Module 1 Standard)**
> - **Category A — Fully Grade-Separated:** Exclusive right-of-way with no intersections, no pedestrian crossings, no mixed traffic. Examples: Metro, Suburban Rail.
> - **Category B — Semi-Exclusive:** Reserved lanes separated from traffic, but still has at-grade intersections. Examples: BRT corridor, Tramway.
> - **Category C — Mixed Traffic:** The vehicle shares the road with all other traffic. Examples: City bus, auto-rickshaw.

**Engineering Implication:** As one moves from Category C to Category A, **system speed, capacity, and reliability increase, but capital cost and inflexibility also increase**. This is the central **trade-off** in transit planning.

### Element 2: The Vehicle (Rolling Stock)

The vehicle is the physical carrier. Its key design parameters directly influence operating performance:

- **Maximum Design Speed (km/h):** Theoretically attainable top speed.
- **Cruising Speed (km/h):** Sustainable operating speed.
- **Vehicle Capacity (pax):** Seated + Standing allowance.
- **Door Configuration:** Number and width of doors governs **dwell time**.
- **Acceleration/Deceleration Rates (m/s²):** Determines how quickly speed changes between stops.

For buses, typical seated capacity is **40 to 60 passengers**, while metro coaches carry **200 to 300 passengers** per unit.

### Element 3: Terminals, Stations, and Stops

Stations are classified by their **function** in the network:

- **Stops:** Minor boarding/alighting points, often unmarked or simple shelters.
- **Stations:** Major interchange or origin-destination points, with ticketing and amenities.
- **Terminals:** End-of-line points where vehicles lay over, turn around, and reset schedules.

The **spacing between stops** is a critical design variable. Tight spacing (e.g., 200 m) serves local trips; wide spacing (e.g., 1.5 km) enables faster line-haul speeds.

### Element 4: The Operating Plan (Service Plan)

The operating plan binds the physical elements into a working service. It consists of:

- **Routes:** The fixed paths a vehicle follows.
- **Schedules:** The published arrival/departure times.
- **Frequencies:** How often vehicles run per hour.
- **Fare Structure:** How revenue is collected.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following table is the **exam-ready cheat sheet** for all Module 1 numerical problems. Memorize each formula, its variables, and its units.

> [!IMPORTANT] **All formulas below appear repeatedly in KTU ESE and Module Tests. This table alone can fetch 5–8 marks.**

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Description of Variables | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Headway–Frequency Relation** | $h = \dfrac{60}{f}$ | $h$ = headway, $f$ = frequency | $h$ in min, $f$ in veh/hr |
| 2 | **Service Frequency** | $f = \dfrac{60}{h}$ | $f$ = frequency, $h$ = headway | $f$ in veh/hr, $h$ in min |
| 3 | **Round-Trip / Cycle Time** | $T_c = 2 \cdot T_{run} + T_{term}$ | $T_{run}$ = one-way running time, $T_{term}$ = terminal layover | minutes |
| 4 | **Fleet Size Required** | $N = f \cdot T_c$ | $N$ = number of vehicles, $f$ = frequency, $T_c$ = cycle time | vehicles |
| 5 | **Line Capacity (Passengers per Hour)** | $C = f \cdot V_c$ | $C$ = capacity, $f$ = frequency, $V_c$ = vehicle capacity | pax/hr |
| 6 | **Person Capacity (Platoon)** | $C_p = f \cdot V_c \cdot LF_{max}$ | $LF_{max}$ = maximum acceptable load factor (typically 1.0 to 1.25) | pax/hr |
| 7 | **System (Average) Speed** | $V_s = \dfrac{L_{total}}{T_c} \cdot 60$ | $V_s$ = system speed, $L_{total}$ = round-trip distance, $T_c$ = cycle time | km/h |
| 8 | **Dwell Time (Boarding + Alighting)** | $t_d = \max \left( t_b , t_a \right) + t_{oc}$ | $t_b$ = boarding time, $t_a$ = alighting time, $t_{oc}$ = door open/close time | seconds |
| 9 | **Travel Time Components** | $T = T_{run} + n \cdot t_d + T_{term}$ | $n$ = number of intermediate stops | minutes |
| 10 | **Productivity Index** | $P = f \cdot V_c \cdot L$ | $L$ = route length | passenger-km/hr |
| 11 | **Utilization Ratio** | $\rho = \dfrac{N_{actual}}{N_{available}}$ | $N_{actual}$ = vehicles in active service | dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| 12 | **Average Passenger Wait Time** | $W = \dfrac{h}{2}$ | $h$ = headway, assuming random passenger arrival | minutes |

> [!NOTE] **Avoid the Pipe-Symbol Trap:** In the table above, $\max()$ and $\dfrac{60}{f}$ are used intentionally to avoid breaking Markdown table syntax. When writing by hand in your answer sheet, write $\max(t_b, t_a)$ cleanly.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

The formulas above are not academic exercises. They are the **daily working tools** of a transit planner:

- **Bus Depot Manager** uses Formula 4 to determine how many buses to purchase and deploy.
- **BRT System Designer** uses Formula 5 to size the fleet for a corridor.
- **Metro Scheduling Engineer** uses Formula 3 to build the timetable.
- **Traffic Engineer** uses Formula 12 to estimate passenger satisfaction with wait times.
- **Urban Planner** uses Formula 7 to compare transit against private-car door-to-door speeds.

In Kerala, the **KSRTC** uses these exact formulas to plan long-distance schedules between Thiruvananthapuram, Kochi, and Kozhikode. The **Kochi Metro Rail Ltd (KMRL)** uses them for the Aluva–Pettah corridor.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Headway–Frequency Relationship

**Starting Premise:** Frequency is defined as the *number of vehicles that depart (or pass) a given point in one hour*. Headway is defined as the *time gap between two consecutive vehicles at that same point*.

**Step 1 — Definition of Frequency (f):**

By definition, if $n$ vehicles pass a point in $T$ hours, the frequency is:

$$f = \dfrac{n}{T}$$

**Step 2 — Definition of Headway (h):**

If $n$ vehicles pass in $T$ hours, the time gap between two successive vehicles is:

$$h = \dfrac{T}{n}$$

**Step 3 — Inverting the Equation:**

Take the reciprocal of the headway equation:

$$\dfrac{1}{h} = \dfrac{n}{T}$$

**Step 4 — Recognizing the Identity:**

The right-hand side of the inverted equation is exactly the definition of frequency. Therefore:

$$\boxed{f = \dfrac{1}{h}}$$

**Step 5 — Unit Conversion (hours to minutes):**

Since headway is usually measured in **minutes** in transit operations, but frequency is in **vehicles per hour**, multiply the numerator by 60 minutes per hour:

$$f = \dfrac{60}{h}$$

This gives the final KTU formula. The reasoning behind the conversion is: if one vehicle arrives every 5 minutes, then in 60 minutes exactly 12 vehicles will arrive, giving $f = 12$ veh/hr.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Fleet Size from Cycle Time

**Starting Premise:** A vehicle making a round-trip journey takes a total of $T_c$ minutes (cycle time) before it is *ready to start the same route again*. The route operates at frequency $f$ vehicles per hour.

**Step 1 — Time Required per Vehicle:**

A single vehicle, once dispatched, is "out of circulation" for exactly $T_c$ minutes. After completing the cycle, it can be re-dispatched.

**Step 2 — Trips Completed per Vehicle per Hour:**

In 60 minutes, one vehicle can complete:

$$\text{Trips per vehicle per hour} = \dfrac{60}{T_c}$$

**Step 3 — Total Trips Required per Hour:**

To achieve frequency $f$ (vehicles per hour), the system must complete $f$ round-trips per hour across the entire fleet.

**Step 4 — Equating Demand and Supply:**

The fleet $N$ must be large enough that all its vehicles together complete $f$ trips per hour:

$$N \cdot \dfrac{60}{T_c} = f$$

**Step 5 — Final Rearrangement:**

Multiply both sides by $T_c$, then divide by 60:

$$N = \dfrac{f \cdot T_c}{60}$$

**Step 6 — Standard KTU Form (using frequency in veh/hr):**

Since $f$ is in veh/hr and $T_c$ is in minutes, the natural form used in KTU is:

$$\boxed{N = \dfrac{f \cdot T_c}{60}}$$

**Interpretation:** This formula tells a transit operator exactly how many vehicles to procure. For example, a route with $f = 10$ veh/hr and $T_c = 90$ minutes requires:

$$N = \dfrac{10 \times 90}{60} = 15 \text{ vehicles}$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Capacity of a Transit Line

**Starting Premise:** The *line capacity* $C$ is the maximum number of passengers that can be transported past a fixed point on the route in one hour, under ideal operational conditions.

**Step 1 — Passengers per Vehicle:**

A single transit vehicle, by design, can carry $V_c$ passengers (seated + standees at the design load).

**Step 2 — Vehicles per Hour:**

In one hour, $f$ vehicles pass the reference point.

**Step 3 — Multiplication:**

The total number of passenger-occasions in one hour is simply vehicles × passengers per vehicle:

$$\boxed{C = f \cdot V_c}$$

**Step 4 — Extension to Person Capacity (with Load Factor):**

In practice, operators accept some crowding. The *passenger capacity* accounting for crowding is:

$$C_p = f \cdot V_c \cdot LF_{max}$$

where $LF_{max}$ is the maximum permitted load factor (e.g., 1.25 means 25% overcrowding is acceptable).

**Step 5 — Numerical Sanity Check:**

For a metro with 6-coach trains, $V_c = 1500$ pax, and headway $h = 3$ min $\Rightarrow f = 20$ trains/hr:

$$C = 20 \times 1500 = 30000 \text{ pax/hr}$$

This matches the design capacity of the Delhi Metro's Red Line — confirming the formula is correct.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — System (Average) Speed

**Starting Premise:** The *system speed* $V_s$ is the average speed a passenger experiences over an entire journey, including all delays.

**Step 1 — Total Distance:**

In one complete round-trip, the vehicle covers a total distance $L_{total}$ (km).

**Step 2 — Total Time:**

The same round-trip takes $T_c$ minutes (cycle time).

**Step 3 — Ratio:**

$$V_s = \dfrac{L_{total}}{T_c} \text{ km/min}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to km/h:**

Multiply by 60 minutes per hour:

$$\boxed{V_s = \dfrac{60 \cdot L_{total}}{T_c}}$$

**Step 5 — Insight:**

Note that $V_s$ is **always lower** than the maximum design speed, because $T_c$ includes dwell times and terminal layovers. This is why a bus with a top speed of 80 km/h typically has a system speed of only 20–25 km/h in a city.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Mode-wise Operating Characteristics

This matrix is a **high-yield summary** that frequently appears in KTU short-answer and 14-mark questions.

| Operating Parameter | City Bus | BRT | LRT | Metro |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Right-of-Way Category** | C (Mixed) | B (Semi-exclusive) | B (mostly A) | A (Fully separated) |
| **Typical System Speed (km/h)** | 12–20 | 20–30 | 25–40 | 30–45 |
| **Vehicle Capacity (pax)** | 40–80 | 100–160 | 200–300 | 1500–2400 |
| **Headway Range (min)** | 5–20 | 2–10 | 3–10 | 1.5–5 |
| **Dwell Time (sec)** | 15–30 | 10–20 | 15–25 | 20–40 |
| **Maximum Line Capacity (pax/hr)** | 2000 | 8000 | 15000 | 60000+ |
| **Capital Cost (per km)** | Low | Medium | High | Very High |

> [!NOTE] **Why this matrix matters in exams:** A typical KTU 14-mark question asks, *"Compare the operating characteristics of BRT and Metro on a corridor."* This matrix, with one paragraph of explanation, can answer part (a) of such a question completely.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — The Four Operating Elements and Their Interactions

The following Mermaid flowchart shows how the four operating elements of public transit interact to deliver a complete journey.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[User Need] --> A2[Operating Plan: Schedule and Route]
    A2 --> A3[Vehicle Dispatch at Terminal]
    A3 --> A4[Vehicle Travels on Right of Way]
    A4 --> A5{Station Encountered}
    A5 -->|Yes| A6[Dwell: Boarding and Alighting]
    A6 --> A4
    A5 -->|No| A7[Continue to Next Stop]
    A7 --> A4
    A4 -->|Reach Terminal| A8[Layover and Reset]
    A8 --> A3
    A3 --> A9[Passenger Reach Destination]
    A9 --> A10[Service Outcome Measured]
    A10 --> A11[KPIs: Speed, Capacity, Reliability]
    A11 --> A12[Feedback to Operating Plan]
    A12 --> A2
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Five Stages of a Transit Trip

This block diagram maps the sequential journey of a passenger through the transit system.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Origin: Home or Office] --> B2[Access Stage: Walk to Stop]
    B2 --> B3[Boarding at Origin Stop]
    B3 --> B4[Line Haul Movement on RoW]
    B4 --> B5[Intermediate Stops and Dwells]
    B5 --> B6{Transfer Required}
    B6 -->|Yes| B7[Transfer at Interchange Station]
    B7 --> B8[Second Line Haul Movement]
    B8 --> B9[Alighting at Destination Stop]
    B6 -->|No| B9
    B9 --> B10[Egress Stage: Walk to Destination]
    B10 --> B11[Final Destination Reached]
```

## 4.3 Hierarchical Classification of Public Transit Modes

This diagram shows the three-tier KTU classification of transit modes by Right-of-Way category.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    C1[Public Transit Modes] --> C2[Right of Way Type A: Grade Separated]
    C1 --> C3[Right of Way Type B: Semi Exclusive]
    C1 --> C4[Right of Way Type C: Mixed Traffic]
    C2 --> C5[Metro Rail]
    C2 --> C6[Suburban Rail]
    C3 --> C7[BRT Bus]
    C3 --> C8[Tram or LRT]
    C4 --> C9[Standard City Bus]
    C4 --> C10[Paratransit: Auto and Shared Taxi]
    C4 --> C11[Ferry or Water Transit]
```

## 4.4 Capacity Build-Up — Visualizing the Capacity Formula

This block diagram visualizes how line capacity is built up from frequency and vehicle capacity.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    D1[Line Capacity C in pax per hr] --> D2[Frequency f in veh per hr]
    D1 --> D3[Vehicle Capacity Vc in pax]
    D2 --> D4[Headway h in min]
    D2 --> D5[Number of Vehicles N]
    D4 --> D6[60 divided by h]
    D5 --> D7[Time between dispatches]
    D3 --> D8[Seats plus Standees]
    D3 --> D9[Design Load Factor]
    D6 --> D1
    D7 --> D1
    D8 --> D1
    D9 --> D1
```

> [!NOTE] **Reading Aid:** The diagrams above are written in raw uppercase text inside node labels to comply with the Mermaid node-label formatting rule (no markdown bold, italics, or HTML inside double-quoted labels).

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, OECET833]**

**Q: Define the term "Right-of-Way" in public transportation. List the three categories of Right-of-Way as per KTU Module 1 classification.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Definition (1 Mark):** *Right-of-Way* refers to the **designated physical space or path** along which a transit vehicle is permitted to travel, and the legal priority it holds over other road users at that location.
>
> **Three Categories (2 Marks — 1 each for naming + brief explanation):**
> 1. **Category A — Fully Grade-Separated:** Exclusive right-of-way with no at-grade intersections, no pedestrian crossings, and no mixing with other traffic. Example: Metro rail.
> 2. **Category B — Semi-Exclusive:** Reserved lanes for transit vehicles, but at-grade intersections may exist. Example: Bus Rapid Transit (BRT).
> 3. **Category C — Mixed Traffic:** Transit vehicles share the road with all other vehicles. Example: City bus operating in general traffic lanes.

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, OECET833]**

**Q: Differentiate between "Headway" and "Frequency" of a public transit service. State the mathematical relationship between them.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Headway (1 Mark):** Headway is defined as the **time interval between two consecutive transit vehicles** arriving at (or departing from) the same point on a route. It is measured in **minutes**.
>
> **Frequency (1 Mark):** Frequency is defined as the **number of transit vehicles passing a fixed point per unit time**, usually per hour. It is measured in **vehicles per hour**.
>
> **Mathematical Relationship (1 Mark):** The two are reciprocally related:
> $$f = \dfrac{60}{h}$$
> where $f$ is the frequency (veh/hr) and $h$ is the headway (minutes). As headway decreases (vehicles come more often), frequency increases proportionally.

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING] **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Do NOT skip the mode identification** in any 14-mark answer. Always begin by naming the transit mode (BRT, Metro, Bus) the question refers to.
> - **Always state units** with every numerical answer. A formula with the right number but no unit loses 1 mark.
> - **Avoid one-word answers** in the "Compare" sub-questions. Examiners expect *at least 3 comparative points* with brief justifications.
> - **Cycle time ≠ Running time.** Cycle time is the round-trip time including terminal layover; running time is only the one-way travel time. Mixing them up loses up to 2 marks.
> - **Do not write "passengers" without specifying** whether it is seated capacity, crush capacity, or design capacity. Always qualify with adjectives.

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, OECET833, Module 1]**

**Q: (a)** List and explain the **four basic operating elements of a public transit system** with suitable examples. *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A city bus route operates at a **headway of 8 minutes** during peak hours. Each bus has a **seating capacity of 50 passengers** and allows **25 standees** (design load). The **one-way running time is 35 minutes**, and the **terminal layover time is 10 minutes** at each end. Calculate:
  - (i) The service frequency in vehicles per hour. *(2 Marks)*
  - (ii) The cycle time. *(2 Marks)*
  - (iii) The fleet size required. *(2 Marks)*
  - (iv) The line capacity in passengers per hour. *(1 Mark)*

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Four Basic Operating Elements (7 Marks):**

The four basic operating elements of a public transit system are:

1. **Right-of-Way (RoW) — 2 Marks:**
   The right-of-way is the **designated path** along which transit vehicles move. KTU classifies it into three categories: **Category A (fully grade-separated, e.g., Metro), Category B (semi-exclusive, e.g., BRT lanes), and Category C (mixed traffic, e.g., city buses)**. The RoW type fundamentally decides the achievable speed, reliability, and cost of the system.

2. **The Vehicle (Rolling Stock) — 2 Marks:**
   The vehicle is the **physical carrier of passengers**. Its key parameters include maximum design speed, acceleration/deceleration, door configuration, and capacity. Examples: A standard KSRTC bus (capacity ~50–60), a Kochi Metro 3-coach train (capacity ~900), a tram (capacity ~200). Vehicle design directly determines dwell time and acceleration profiles.

3. **Stations, Stops, and Terminals — 1.5 Marks:**
   These are the **nodes** where passengers access or egress the system. *Stops* are minor boarding points; *stations* are major interchanges with ticketing; *terminals* are end-of-route depots where vehicles lay over. Stop spacing affects line-haul speed: wider spacing increases commercial speed but reduces accessibility.

4. **The Operating Plan (Service Plan) — 1.5 Marks:**
   The operating plan integrates routes, schedules, frequencies, and fares into a working service. It defines *when* and *where* vehicles run. Example: KSRTC's long-distance schedules, KMRL's peak-hour vs. off-peak frequencies on the Aluva–Pettah corridor.

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- Headway: $h = 8$ min
- Seating capacity: 50 pax
- Standing allowance: 25 pax
- Vehicle design capacity: $V_c = 50 + 25 = 75$ pax
- One-way running time: $T_{run} = 35$ min
- Terminal layover: $T_{term} = 10$ min (each end)

**(i) Service Frequency (2 Marks):**

$$f = \dfrac{60}{h} = \dfrac{60}{8} = 7.5 \text{ veh/hr}$$

**Interpretation:** Approximately 7 to 8 buses per hour depart each terminal during peak hours.

**(ii) Cycle Time (2 Marks):**

The round-trip cycle time is the sum of two one-way runs and both terminal layovers:

$$T_c = 2 \cdot T_{run} + 2 \cdot T_{term} = 2(35) + 2(10) = 70 + 20 = 90 \text{ min}$$

*(Note: Some textbooks count terminal time once for the round trip; we use the KTU convention of including both end layovers.)*

**(iii) Fleet Size Required (2 Marks):**

$$N = \dfrac{f \cdot T_c}{60} = \dfrac{7.5 \times 90}{60} = \dfrac{675}{60} = 11.25$$

Round up to the next whole vehicle:

$$N = 12 \text{ vehicles}$$

**Practical note:** You cannot deploy 11.25 buses; hence 12 are required. Procurement and depot planning use this rounded-up value.

**(iv) Line Capacity (1 Mark):**

$$C = f \cdot V_c = 7.5 \times 75 = 562.5 \text{ pax/hr}$$

**Answer:** The line moves approximately 562 passengers per hour in one direction at design load.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Alternative to Question A)
**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme, OECET833, Module 1]**

**Q: (a)** Explain the **five sequential stages of a transit trip** as defined in KTU Module 1. Why is the access/egress stage critical to the overall perception of transit quality? *(7 Marks)*

**(b)** A BRT corridor has a **route length of 18 km** (one way). The buses operate at a **headway of 4 minutes** during peak hour, with **vehicle capacity of 120 pax**. Average **station dwell time is 20 seconds**, there are **15 intermediate stops**, and **terminal layover is 8 minutes** at each end. **Free-flow running speed is 40 km/h**. Calculate:
  - (i) The one-way running time including dwell. *(3 Marks)*
  - (ii) The cycle time. *(2 Marks)*
  - (iii) The system (average) speed. *(2 Marks)*

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Five Stages of a Transit Trip (7 Marks):**

The five stages are:

1. **Access (Egress) Stage — 1 Mark:** Walking, cycling, or using a feeder mode to reach the *first* transit stop from the origin. This stage is outside the transit vehicle's journey but is *part of the door-to-door travel time*.

2. **Line-Haul Stage — 1.5 Marks:** The main movement of the transit vehicle between the boarding and alighting stops. This is where the transit vehicle's speed advantage over walking is realized.

3. **Dwell Stage — 1 Mark:** The time the vehicle spends stationary at each stop, allowing passengers to board and alight. Dwell time depends on door width, passenger flow, and fare collection method.

4. **Transfer Stage — 1.5 Marks:** The change between two routes at an interchange station. Each transfer adds walking + waiting time, making transit less attractive than direct services.

5. **Egress Stage — 1 Mark:** Walking from the final alighting stop to the actual destination, symmetric to the access stage.

**Why Access/Egress is Critical — 1 Mark:**

The access/egress stage is the **first and last impression** of the transit system. Studies show that passengers tolerate a *5-minute walk* to a stop, but beyond that, the perceived "transit journey" feels long even if the line-haul is fast. Hence, planners invest in **first-mile and last-mile connectivity** (pedestrian paths, bike-share, feeder buses) to make transit competitive with private cars.

**Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks):**

**Given:**
- One-way route length: $L = 18$ km
- Headway: $h = 4$ min
- Vehicle capacity: $V_c = 120$ pax
- Dwell time per stop: $t_d = 20$ sec
- Number of intermediate stops: $n = 15$
- Terminal layover each end: $T_{term} = 8$ min
- Free-flow running speed: $v_{run} = 40$ km/h

**(i) One-Way Running Time (3 Marks):**

**Pure running time (no stops):**

$$T_{run,0} = \dfrac{L}{v_{run}} = \dfrac{18}{40} = 0.45 \text{ hr} = 27 \text{ min}$$

**Total dwell time over 15 stops:**

$$T_{dwell,total} = 15 \times 20 \text{ sec} = 300 \text{ sec} = 5 \text{ min}$$

**One-way running time including dwell:**

$$T_{run} = T_{run,0} + T_{dwell,total} = 27 + 5 = 32 \text{ min}$$

**(ii) Cycle Time (2 Marks):**

$$T_c = 2 \cdot T_{run} + 2 \cdot T_{term} = 2(32) + 2(8) = 64 + 16 = 80 \text{ min}$$

**(iii) System (Average) Speed (2 Marks):**

Total round-trip distance: $L_{total} = 2 \times 18 = 36$ km

$$V_s = \dfrac{60 \cdot L_{total}}{T_c} = \dfrac{60 \times 36}{80} = \dfrac{2160}{80} = 27 \text{ km/h}$$

**Interpretation:** Despite a free-flow speed of 40 km/h, the BRT system offers an average commercial speed of 27 km/h — typical for a well-designed BRT corridor.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT] **Rapid Revision Checklist (Save this section for the night before the exam)**

- **Four Operating Elements:** Right-of-Way, Vehicle, Stations/Terminals, Operating Plan. (Mnemonic: **R-V-S-O** — "Rivers, Vehicles, Stations, Operations.")
- **Three RoW Categories:** A (Metro), B (BRT/LRT), C (Bus/Mixed). As you go from C → A, **speed, cost, and reliability all increase.**
- **Five Stages of a Transit Trip:** Access → Line-Haul → Dwell → Transfer → Egress. (Mnemonic: **A-L-D-T-E** — "All Laudable Drivers Transit Efficiently.")
- **Headway–Frequency Formula:** $f = \dfrac{60}{h}$. Lower headway = Higher frequency = Better service.
- **Cycle Time Formula:** $T_c = 2 \cdot T_{run} + T_{term}$. Always include both terminal layovers.
- **Fleet Size Formula:** $N = \dfrac{f \cdot T_c}{60}$. Always round *up* to the next whole vehicle.
- **Line Capacity Formula:** $C = f \cdot V_c$. Capacity is *frequency × vehicle size*.
- **System Speed Formula:** $V_s = \dfrac{60 \cdot L_{total}}{T_c}$. Always less than the free-flow running speed.
- **Average Passenger Wait Time:** $W = \dfrac{h}{2}$. Halving the headway halves the wait time.
- **Key Distinction:** Running time ≠ Cycle time. Running time is one-way; cycle time is round-trip including layovers.
- **Key Distinction:** Vehicle capacity ≠ Line capacity. Vehicle is per-vehicle; line is per-hour.
- **Examiner's Rule:** Always state *units*, *mode*, and *operating condition* (peak/off-peak) when answering.
- **KTU 2024 Trend:** Compare-and-contrast questions (BRT vs. Metro, Bus vs. LRT) appear at least once per question paper — memorize the analytical matrix in Section 3.5.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Public Transport Travel Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET833 - PUBLIC TRANSPORTATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Public Transport Travel Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Public Transport Travel Characteristics** refer to the systematic set of quantifiable attributes, behavioral patterns, and demographic parameters that describe how passengers interact with, use, and perceive mass transit systems. These characteristics encompass the *spatial* (trip length, origin-destination patterns), *temporal* (peak/off-peak usage, waiting time, in-vehicle time), *behavioral* (modal choice, frequency of use), and *socio-economic* (income, age, trip purpose) dimensions of public transportation demand.

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme, this topic forms the foundational analytical base for understanding **transit ridership forecasting, route planning, and level-of-service (LOS) evaluation** as prescribed in the OECET833 syllabus.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> Travel characteristics include *trip generation, trip distribution, modal split, trip assignment*, and the underlying *user behavior parameters* that govern these four-step model components. Mastery of these characteristics is mandatory for solving Part B (14-mark) problems on transit demand analysis.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a Kochi Metro station at 8:30 AM. Thousands of commuters pour in from a 1-km radius catchment, all heading towards the IT corridor at Infopark. Now ask: **Why do they choose the metro? Who are they? When do they leave? How far do they walk?**

The answers to these questions constitute **Public Transport Travel Characteristics**.

- The *distance they walk* is a **spatial characteristic**.
- The *8:30 AM concentration* is a **temporal characteristic**.
- The *choice of metro over auto* is a **behavioral characteristic**.
- The *IT professional profile* is a **socio-economic characteristic**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Travel characteristics are the "DNA" of any public transport system. Without quantifying them, no planner can design an efficient route, set an optimal fare, or schedule a vehicle. They are the input parameters for every transit model.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics (Bold Highlighting)

The following metrics are universally accepted in transit planning practice:

- **Peak Hour Factor (PHF)** = 0.25 to 0.95 (typically **0.85** for urban transit)
- **Average Walking Distance to Stop** = **400 m** (standard urban threshold)
- **Acceptable Wait Time** = **≤ 10 minutes** (or ≤ headway/2 for random arrivals)
- **Modal Split Share** = typically **20%–40%** in well-developed Indian cities
- **Load Factor (LF)** = **0.75 to 1.25** during peak hours
- **Average Trip Length (Urban Bus)** = **8–12 km**
- **Accessibility Index** = population within 400 m of stop / total population

### 1.4 Categorization of Travel Characteristics

| Dimension | Characteristics Covered | KTU Relevance |
|---|---|---|
| **Spatial** | Trip length, OD matrix, route density | High (Part B) |
| **Temporal** | Peak hour factor, travel time, frequency | Very High (Part A & B) |
| **Behavioral** | Modal choice, willingness-to-pay | High (Part B) |
| **Demographic** | Income, age, gender, car ownership | Medium (Part A) |
| **Operational** | Boarding, alighting, load factor | Very High (Part B) |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Pillars of Public Transport Travel Characteristics

The study of transit travel behavior rests on five interdependent pillars. Understanding each pillar is essential for solving KTU examination problems.

#### Pillar 1: **Trip Generation Characteristics**
This describes *why* and *how many* trips are produced by a zone.

- Trip rate (T) is influenced by:
  - Household income (I)
  - Car ownership (C)
  - Population density (D)
  - Land use type (Residential, Commercial, Industrial)
- The general form:

$$T_i = f(P_i, I_i, C_i, D_i, L_i)$$

where $P_i$ = population of zone *i*, $L_i$ = land use attribute.

#### Pillar 2: **Trip Distribution Characteristics**
Describes *where* trips go from each origin zone.

- **Gravity Model** is the workhorse:

$$T_{ij} = \frac{P_i \cdot A_j \cdot F_{ij}}{\sum_{j} A_j \cdot F_{ij}}$$

where $F_{ij}$ = friction factor (function of travel time/cost between *i* and *j*).

#### Pillar 3: **Modal Split Characteristics**
Describes *which mode* is chosen for each trip.

- **Binary Logit Model:**

$$P_{pt} = \frac{e^{U_{pt}}}{e^{U_{pt}} + e^{U_{car}}}$$

$$P_{pt} = \frac{1}{1 + e^{(U_{car} - U_{pt})}}$$

- $U_{pt}$ = utility of public transport
- $U_{car}$ = utility of private car
- Utility is a linear function of attributes: $U = \beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1 + \beta_2 X_2 + \dots$

#### Pillar 4: **Trip Assignment Characteristics**
Describes *which route* a transit trip takes.

- Based on **Generalized Cost (GC):**

$$GC = C_{ivt} + \alpha \cdot t_{wt} + \beta \cdot t_{wt} + \gamma \cdot t_{tr}$$

where $C_{ivt}$ = in-vehicle cost, $t_{wt}$ = waiting time, $t_{wt}$ = walking time, $t_{tr}$ = transfer time, and $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ = weighting coefficients.

#### Pillar 5: **Travel Time Characteristics**
The total travel time for a transit user comprises **five components** (often called the *Transit Travel Time Chain*):

$$T_{total} = t_{access} + t_{wait} + t_{transfer} + t_{ivt} + t_{egress}$$

Each component has a different *value of time* (VOT) multiplier. For example, walking time is perceived **2 to 3 times** longer than in-vehicle time.

### 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Pillar

- **Why** do we need to study these? → To **forecast demand**, design **optimal routes**, set **fares**, and plan **vehicle schedules**.
- **How** are they measured? → Through **Origin-Destination (OD) surveys**, **boarding-alighting counts**, **stated preference (SP) surveys**, and **revealed preference (RP) surveys**.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **EXAMINATION ALERT:** The following table contains every equation you are likely to need for solving Part B numerical problems on this topic.

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Boundary Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $PHF = \dfrac{V_{15, max}}{4 \cdot V_{15}}$ | Peak Hour Factor (max 15-min flow method) | Dimensionless; $0.80 \le PHF \le 1.00$ |
| 2 | $LF = \dfrac{P}{C}$ | Load Factor (passengers ÷ capacity) | $LF > 1$ indicates overcrowding |
| 3 | $h = \dfrac{3600}{f}$ | Headway (seconds) from frequency | $f$ in vehicles/hour |
| 4 | $t_{wt} = \dfrac{h}{2}$ | Average wait time (random arrival) | Assumes Poisson arrivals |
| 5 | $T_{total} = t_a + t_w + t_{tr} + t_{ivt} + t_e$ | Total travel time (transit chain) | All in minutes |
| 6 | $GC = C_{ivt} + \alpha t_w + \beta t_{tr}$ | Generalized Cost | Cost in ₹; time in min |
| 7 | $P_{pt} = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{\Delta U}}$ | Logit modal share | $\Delta U = U_{car} - U_{pt}$ |
| 8 | $A_i = \dfrac{1}{1 + e^{(\beta_0 + \beta_1 X_1)}}$ | Binary logit probability | $X_1$ = time difference |
| 9 | $V/C = \dfrac{\text{Volume}}{\text{Capacity}}$ | Volume-to-Capacity ratio | LOS: A ($\le 0.6$) to F ($> 1.0$) |
| 10 | $D_{access} = \sqrt{\dfrac{A_{stop}}{\pi}}$ | Theoretical stop spacing | $A_{stop}$ = stop area coverage |
| 11 | $T_{ij} = \dfrac{P_i A_j F_{ij}}{\sum_j A_j F_{ij}}$ | Gravity model trip distribution | Trips/hour |
| 12 | $F_{ij} = t_{ij}^{-n}$ | Friction factor (power function) | $n = 1.0$ to $2.5$ |
| 13 | $E_{walk} = \dfrac{1}{v_w} \cdot d_{walk}$ | Walk time = distance ÷ walking speed | $v_w = 1.2$ m/s (typical) |
| 14 | $R_f = \dfrac{P_{peak}}{P_{daily}}$ | Peak-to-daily ratio | Typically **0.10 to 0.15** |
| 15 | $A_{TOD} = \dfrac{\sum (P_{peak}/f)}{P_{total}}$ | Transit Opportunity Density | Density measure |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical KTU Pitfall:** When using the logit model, always compute $\Delta U$ as *higher mode utility − lower mode utility*. Reversing this sign will give a probability $> 1$ or $< 0$, costing 2–3 marks.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application | How Travel Characteristics Are Used |
|---|---|
| **Bus Route Planning** | OD data determines route alignment; trip length determines route type. |
| **Metro Feasibility Study** | Peak hour factor and load factor justify investment cost. |
| **BRTS Corridor Design** | Modal split data sets demand projections for 20-year horizon. |
| **Fare Policy** | Willingness-to-pay (WTP) from SP surveys fixes fare structure. |
| **ITS Deployment** | Real-time travel time data feeds dynamic passenger information. |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Average Wait Time from Headway (Random Arrival)

> **Statement:** For passengers arriving randomly at a transit stop, the average waiting time equals half the headway.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

Let $t$ = passenger arrival time (uniformly distributed in interval $[0, h]$), where $h$ = headway.

The waiting time for a passenger arriving at time $t$ before the next vehicle is:

$$W(t) = h - t$$

The probability density function of arrival is uniform:

$$f(t) = \frac{1}{h} \quad \text{for } 0 \le t \le h$$

The expected waiting time is:

$$\begin{aligned}
E[W] &= \int_0^h (h - t) \cdot f(t) \, dt \\
&= \int_0^h (h - t) \cdot \frac{1}{h} \, dt \\
&= \frac{1}{h} \left[ ht - \frac{t^2}{2} \right]_0^h \\
&= \frac{1}{h} \left( h^2 - \frac{h^2}{2} \right) \\
&= \frac{1}{h} \cdot \frac{h^2}{2} \\
E[W] &= \frac{h}{2}
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** For random (Poisson) arrivals, the average wait time is exactly half the headway. This is the **most-tested KTU result** in this module.

> [!NOTE]
> **Logistics of the derivation:** The "conversion logic" is straightforward: the wait time is *complementary* to the arrival time within the headway interval, and averaging a linearly decreasing function over a uniform distribution yields the midpoint value.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Peak Hour Factor (PHF) using 15-Minute Flow Method

> **Statement:** PHF relates the maximum 15-minute flow rate to the total hourly flow rate.

**Given:**
- Hourly volume $V$ = 1800 vehicles/hour
- Maximum 15-minute volume $V_{15}$ = 540 vehicles

**Step-by-Step Calculation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
PHF &= \frac{V_{15, max}}{4 \cdot V_{15, hourly}} \\
    &= \frac{V}{4 \cdot V_{15, max}} \\
    &= \frac{1800}{4 \cdot 540} \\
    &= \frac{1800}{2160} \\
    &= 0.833
\end{aligned}$$

**Interpretation:** A PHF of 0.833 indicates moderate peaking. The closer PHF is to 1.0, the more uniform the flow within the hour.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Logit Modal Split — Step-by-Step

> **Problem:** Two modes (Bus and Car) have the following utilities (in utils):
> - $U_{bus} = -0.5 - 0.08 \cdot t_{ivt} - 0.15 \cdot t_{wt} - 0.10 \cdot C$
> - $U_{car} = -0.2 - 0.05 \cdot t_{ivt} - 0.05 \cdot C$
>
> Given: $t_{ivt,bus} = 30$ min, $t_{wt,bus} = 8$ min, $C_{bus} = 20$ ₹
> Given: $t_{ivt,car} = 25$ min, $C_{car} = 60$ ₹
> Find: Probability of choosing Bus.

**Step 1:** Calculate $U_{bus}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
U_{bus} &= -0.5 - 0.08(30) - 0.15(8) - 0.10(20) \\
        &= -0.5 - 2.4 - 1.2 - 2.0 \\
        &= -6.1
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Calculate $U_{car}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
U_{car} &= -0.2 - 0.05(25) - 0.05(60) \\
        &= -0.2 - 1.25 - 3.0 \\
        &= -4.45
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Apply the Logit Formula:

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{bus} &= \frac{e^{U_{bus}}}{e^{U_{bus}} + e^{U_{car}}} \\
        &= \frac{e^{-6.1}}{e^{-6.1} + e^{-4.45}}
\end{aligned}$$

Calculate the exponentials:
- $e^{-6.1} = 0.00224$
- $e^{-4.45} = 0.01170$

Sum: $0.00224 + 0.01170 = 0.01394$

$$\begin{aligned}
P_{bus} &= \frac{0.00224}{0.01394} \\
        &= 0.1608 \\
        &= 16.08\%
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Interpretation: Only **16%** of commuters will choose the bus under these conditions. The car is preferred due to lower in-vehicle time and convenience, despite higher cost.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic Note:** The exponential function is always positive, ensuring probabilities sum to 1. The *more negative* the utility, the lower the probability. This is the cornerstone of discrete choice modeling in transit planning.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Worked Numerical Examples

| # | Problem Type | Given | Find | Final Answer |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Headway-Wait Time | $f = 12$ buses/hr | $h$ and $t_{wt}$ | $h = 300$ s; $t_{wt} = 150$ s |
| 2 | PHF | $V = 2400$, $V_{15} = 700$ | PHF | $2400/(4 \cdot 700) = 0.857$ |
| 3 | Logit | $U_1 = -1.5$, $U_2 = -2.0$ | $P_1$ | $e^{-1.5}/(e^{-1.5}+e^{-2.0}) = 0.598$ |
| 4 | Load Factor | $P = 90$, $C = 75$ | LF | $LF = 90/75 = 1.20$ (overcrowded) |
| 5 | Walk Time | $d = 600$ m, $v_w = 1.2$ m/s | $t_{walk}$ | $600/1.2 = 500$ s = 8.33 min |
| 6 | Total Travel Time | $t_a=5$, $t_w=4$, $t_{tr}=3$, $t_{ivt}=25$, $t_e=3$ | $T_{total}$ | $40$ min |
| 7 | Gravity Model | $P_i = 5000$, $A_j = 3000$, $F_{ij} = 0.5$, $\sum A_j F_j = 20000$ | $T_{ij}$ | $5000 \cdot 3000 \cdot 0.5 / 20000 = 375$ trips |
| 8 | Stop Spacing | $A_{stop} = 0.5$ km² | $D_{access}$ | $\sqrt{0.5/\pi} = 0.399$ km ≈ 400 m |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Five Pillars of Public Transport Travel Characteristics

This block diagram illustrates the interconnections between the five analytical pillars discussed in Section 2.

```mermaid

    node2 --> node3

```

### 4.2 Architecture Flow: Transit Travel Time Chain

This flow diagram visualizes how the components of total travel time connect sequentially from origin to destination.


### 4.3 Hierarchy: Modal Choice Decision Tree

This decision tree shows the hierarchical structure of factors influencing mode choice, commonly tested in KTU Part A questions.

```mermaid

    class A root

```

### 4.4 Process Flow: Travel Characteristic Survey Methodology

This block diagram represents the survey methodology used to collect travel characteristic data in real-world transit studies.

```mermaid
flowchart TB

    node2[Design Survey Instrument]:::step1
    node3[Conduct RP Survey Revealed Preference]:::step2

    node5[Collect OD Data]:::step2

    node1 --> node2
    node2 --> node3
    node2 --> node4
    node2 --> node5

    node5 --> node6
    node6 --> node7
    node7 --> node8
    node8 --> node9
    node9 --> node10

    classDef step1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef step2 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
```
