<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**

**Q1. Define the term "Peak Hour Factor (PHF)" and explain its significance in public transport planning.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Peak Hour Factor (PHF)** is defined as the ratio of the total hourly volume to four times the peak 15-minute volume within that hour. Mathematically:
>
> $$PHF = \frac{V_{60}}{4 \cdot V_{15, max}}$$
>
> where $V_{60}$ = total hourly volume and $V_{15, max}$ = maximum 15-minute volume.
>
> **Significance:**
> - **Vehicle Scheduling:** PHF determines the number of buses/trains required during peak periods to avoid overcrowding.
> - **Capacity Design:** A lower PHF (high peaking) requires higher capacity, increasing capital cost.
> - **Level of Service (LOS):** PHF affects the reliability and comfort experienced by passengers.
> - **Typical Values:** Urban transit PHF ranges from **0.80 to 0.95**; values below 0.75 indicate severe peaking and operational stress.

---

**Q2. List and briefly explain any THREE key public transport travel characteristics.**

**Model Answer:**

> The three key public transport travel characteristics are:
>
> 1. **Temporal Characteristics:** These describe the time-based patterns of transit usage, including peak hour demand, daily ridership variation, and travel time components (wait, in-vehicle, transfer). For example, Indian city buses experience a morning peak between **8:00–10:00 AM** and an evening peak between **5:30–7:30 PM**.
>
> 2. **Spatial Characteristics:** These describe the geographic distribution of trips, such as trip length, origin-destination patterns, and route density. The average bus trip length in Tier-1 Indian cities is **8–12 km**.
>
> 3. **Socio-Economic Characteristics:** These describe the demographic profile of transit users — age, income, employment status, and car ownership. In India, public transport is predominantly used by **middle and lower-income groups** and accounts for nearly **60%** of urban motorized trips.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Style]**

**Q3. What is the difference between "Revealed Preference (RP) Survey" and "Stated Preference (SP) Survey" in transit travel characteristic studies?**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Revealed Preference (RP) | Stated Preference (SP) |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Captures actual choices made by travelers | Captures hypothetical choices in designed scenarios |
| **Data Type** | Observed behavior (real trips) | Stated intentions (what-if situations) |
| **Application** | Calibrating existing mode choice models | Forecasting future mode shifts (e.g., new metro) |
| **Limitation** | Cannot evaluate non-existent alternatives | Hypothetical bias (stated ≠ actual) |
| **Example** | "How did you travel to office today?" | "Would you shift to metro if fare drops by 20%?" |

> RP provides **real-world reliability**, while SP provides **future-scenario flexibility**. Best practice is to use both in combination for robust travel characteristic analysis.

---

### 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper Style, 14 Marks]**

#### **Question A (14 Marks): Travel Time Analysis & Logit Modal Split**

> **Q.A (a)** *Explain the components of total travel time for a public transport user. For a transit trip with the following data, calculate the total travel time and the generalized cost using VOT multipliers: Access walk = 6 min, Wait time = 5 min, In-vehicle time = 28 min, Transfer time = 4 min, Egress walk = 3 min. Assume walk VOT multiplier = 2.0, wait VOT = 2.5, transfer VOT = 2.0, in-vehicle VOT = 1.0, and value of time = ₹15/hour.* **[7 Marks]**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Identify each component of the transit travel time chain (5 components as defined in Section 2.1, Pillar 5).

| Component | Time (min) | VOT Multiplier | Weighted Time (min) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Access walk | 6 | 2.0 | 12 |
| Wait time | 5 | 2.5 | 12.5 |
| Transfer time | 4 | 2.0 | 8 |
| In-vehicle time | 28 | 1.0 | 28 |
| Egress walk | 3 | 2.0 | 6 |
| **Total** | **46** | — | **66.5** |

**Step 2:** Calculate Total Travel Time:

$$T_{total} = 6 + 5 + 4 + 28 + 3 = 46 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate Generalized Cost (using VOT = ₹15/hr = ₹0.25/min):

$$\begin{aligned}
GC &= \text{(Weighted Time)} \times \text{VOT per minute} \\
   &= 66.5 \times 0.25 \\
   &= \text{₹ } 16.625
\end{aligned}$$

**Conclusion:** Although the actual travel time is **46 minutes**, the *perceived* (generalized) time cost to the passenger is **66.5 minutes**, reflecting the higher penalty for waiting and walking.

---

> **Q.A (b)** *A binary logit model is calibrated for the choice between Bus and Car with the following utility functions:*
>
> *$U_{bus} = -1.2 - 0.10 \cdot t_{ivt} - 0.20 \cdot t_{wt} - 0.05 \cdot C$*
> *$U_{car} = -0.5 - 0.06 \cdot t_{ivt} - 0.03 \cdot C$*
>
> *Given: For a typical commuter, $t_{ivt,bus} = 35$ min, $t_{wt,bus} = 10$ min, $C_{bus} = 15$ ₹, $t_{ivt,car} = 30$ min, $C_{car} = 80$ ₹. Compute: (i) The probability of choosing bus. (ii) The probability of choosing car. (iii) Comment on the result.* **[7 Marks]**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute $U_{bus}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
U_{bus} &= -1.2 - 0.10(35) - 0.20(10) - 0.05(15) \\
        &= -1.2 - 3.5 - 2.0 - 0.75 \\
        &= -7.45
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Compute $U_{car}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
U_{car} &= -0.5 - 0.06(30) - 0.03(80) \\
        &= -0.5 - 1.8 - 2.4 \\
        &= -4.7
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Compute exponentials:

- $e^{-7.45} = 0.000579$
- $e^{-4.7} = 0.009095$
- Sum = $0.000579 + 0.009095 = 0.009674$

**Step 4:** Calculate probabilities:

$$P_{bus} = \frac{0.000579}{0.009674} = 0.0599 \approx 5.99\%$$

$$P_{car} = \frac{0.009095}{0.009674} = 0.940 \approx 94.01\%$$

**Step 5:** Comment:
- The bus captures only **~6%** of commuters, indicating that the car is overwhelmingly preferred.
- The dominant deterrent is the **long wait time (10 min)** weighted by a high coefficient (0.20), plus the **lower in-vehicle time for car** (30 vs 35 min).
- **Policy Implication:** Reducing bus wait time (e.g., increasing frequency) or reducing fare would significantly shift modal share toward public transport.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks): Trip Distribution & Peak Hour Analysis**

> **Q.B (a)** *For a city with 4 zones, the trip productions and attractions are given below. Using the Gravity Model with the given friction factors and inter-zonal travel times, compute the trip distribution matrix.* **[7 Marks]**

**Given Data:**

| Zone | Production (P) | Attraction (A) | Travel Time from Zone 1 (min) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 4000 | 3500 | 0 (intra) |
| 2 | 3000 | 3200 | 15 |
| 3 | 2500 | 2800 | 25 |
| 4 | 2000 | 2000 | 35 |

**Friction Factor Function:** $F_{ij} = t_{ij}^{-1.5}$

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute friction factors from Zone 1 to all zones:

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{11} &= (5)^{-1.5} \quad \text{(assume intra-zonal time = 5 min)} \\
       &= 0.0894 \\
F_{12} &= (15)^{-1.5} = 0.0172 \\
F_{13} &= (25)^{-1.5} = 0.0080 \\
F_{14} &= (35)^{-1.5} = 0.00483
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Compute $A_j \cdot F_{1j}$ for each destination:

- Zone 1: $3500 \times 0.0894 = 312.9$
- Zone 2: $3200 \times 0.0172 = 55.04$
- Zone 3: $2800 \times 0.0080 = 22.4$
- Zone 4: $2000 \times 0.00483 = 9.66$

**Sum:** $312.9 + 55.04 + 22.4 + 9.66 = 400.0$

**Step 3:** Apply Gravity Model $T_{1j} = P_1 \cdot (A_j F_{1j}) / \sum (A_j F_{1j})$:

$$\begin{aligned}
T_{11} &= 4000 \times (312.9 / 400.0) = 3129 \text{ trips} \\
T_{12} &= 4000 \times (55.04 / 400.0) = 550.4 \text{ trips} \\
T_{13} &= 4000 \times (22.4 / 400.0) = 224.0 \text{ trips} \\
T_{14} &= 4000 \times (9.66 / 400.0) = 96.6 \text{ trips}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Verify: $\sum T_{1j} = 3129 + 550.4 + 224 + 96.6 = 4000$ ✓ (matches $P_1$)

**Conclusion:** The Gravity Model allocates the most trips to the **nearest/least-friction zone** (intra-zonal), as expected by the inverse power friction function.

---

> **Q.B (b)** *A bus route has a peak hour volume of 1800 passengers/hour. The maximum 15-minute flow during the peak hour is 540 passengers. The bus capacity is 75 passengers and the scheduled headway is 5 minutes. Calculate: (i) Peak Hour Factor (PHF), (ii) Load Factor during peak 15 minutes, (iii) Number of buses required to clear the peak 15-minute demand, (iv) Comment on whether the headway is adequate.* **[7 Marks]**

**Step-by-Step Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Calculate PHF**

$$\begin{aligned}
PHF &= \frac{V_{60}}{4 \cdot V_{15, max}} \\
    &= \frac{1800}{4 \times 540} \\
    &= \frac{1800}{2160} \\
    &= 0.833
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2: Calculate Load Factor (during peak 15 min)**

Number of buses in 15 min = $15 / 5 = 3$ buses.
Capacity offered = $3 \times 75 = 225$ passengers.

$$\begin{aligned}
LF_{15} &= \frac{540}{225} \\
        &= 2.40
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Number of buses required to clear peak 15-min demand**

To keep $LF \le 1.0$:

$$N_{required} = \frac{540}{75} = 7.2 \approx 8 \text{ buses in 15 minutes}$$

Equivalent headway = $15 / 8 = 1.875$ min (i.e., 32 buses/hour).

**Step 4: Comment on headway adequacy**

- Current headway = 5 min → 12 buses/hour → 1800/12 = 150 passengers/bus → LF = 2.0.
- A headway of **5 minutes is grossly inadequate**. The required headway to maintain acceptable load factor (LF ≤ 1.25) is:

$$f_{required} = \frac{540}{75 \times 1.25 \times 0.25} = 23.04 \approx 24 \text{ buses/hr}$$

$$h_{required} = 60/24 = 2.5 \text{ min}$$

**Conclusion:** The current 5-minute headway produces severe overcrowding ($LF = 2.4$). The headway should be reduced to **2.5 minutes** during peak hours to maintain acceptable service quality.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in This Topic:**
>
> 1. **Sign Error in Logit Models (3 marks lost):** Students often write $P = e^{U}/(1 + e^{U})$ instead of the correct formula. Always use the *difference* formulation.
> 2. **Unit Inconsistency in Headway (2 marks lost):** If frequency is in buses/hour and headway is asked in seconds, multiply by 3600. Mixing minutes and seconds loses marks.
> 3. **Forgetting VOT Multipliers (2 marks lost):** When computing generalized cost, raw travel time is *not* used — the weighted time must be computed first. Many students skip this step.
> 4. **Friction Factor Calculation (2 marks lost):** For $F_{ij} = t^{-n}$, ensure the exponent is correctly applied. Common error: writing $F = 1/t^n$ but computing as $F = 1/(t^n)$ — same thing, but students sometimes mistakenly multiply instead of divide.
> 5. **Gravity Model Summation (3 marks lost):** Always verify that the row sum of the distributed trips equals the total production. This is the simplest validation check — skipping it is a guaranteed mark-deduction.
> 6. **Peak Hour Factor Confusion (2 marks lost):** Some students invert the formula. Remember: PHF = **Hourly Volume ÷ (4 × Peak 15-min Volume)**.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**

- **Five Pillars** of public transport travel characteristics:
  1. **Trip Generation** (who travels, why, how many)
  2. **Trip Distribution** (where they go — Gravity Model)
  3. **Modal Split** (which mode — Logit Model)
  4. **Trip Assignment** (which route — Generalized Cost)
  5. **Travel Time Analysis** (5-component chain)

- **The 5-Component Travel Time Chain:** $T_{total} = t_{access} + t_{wait} + t_{transfer} + t_{ivt} + t_{egress}$

- **Key Formulas to Memorize:**
  - $PHF = V_{60}/(4 V_{15})$
  - $h = 3600/f$ (seconds)
  - $t_{wt} = h/2$ (random arrivals)
  - $LF = P/C$ (passengers/capacity)
  - $P_{pt} = 1/(1 + e^{\Delta U})$ (Logit)
  - $T_{ij} = P_i A_j F_{ij} / \sum (A_j F_{ij})$ (Gravity)
  - $F_{ij} = t_{ij}^{-n}$ (Friction factor)

- **Standard Values to Remember:**
  - Walking speed $v_w$ = **1.2 m/s**
  - Acceptable walk to stop = **400 m**
  - Value of time multiplier (wait) = **2.0–2.5×**
  - PHF range = **0.80–0.95**
  - Load Factor target ≤ **1.0** (urban) to **1.25** (acceptable peak)

- **Survey Types:**
  - **RP (Revealed Preference)** = actual trips (used for model calibration)
  - **SP (Stated Preference)** = hypothetical scenarios (used for forecasting)

- **High-Frequency KTU Question Patterns:**
  - "Calculate the probability of choosing mode X using the logit model" → 7-mark question
  - "Compute the trip distribution using the Gravity Model" → 7-mark question
  - "Determine the headway/frequency from PHF and peak demand" → 7-mark question
  - "List travel characteristics" → 3-mark Part A question

- **Pitfall Summary (Memorize!):**
  - Logit: $\Delta U$ = utility of *other* mode − utility of *this* mode
  - Gravity: row sum must equal production
  - PHF: hourly volume in numerator (not denominator)
  - Headway units: stay consistent (seconds vs minutes vs hours)

> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When a question mentions "travel characteristics," your first response should *always* classify them into **spatial, temporal, behavioral, demographic, and operational** categories. This shows the examiner you understand the conceptual framework, not just the formulas. This single move can earn you **2–3 extra marks** even on questions where the numerical part is shaky.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transit Travel Characteristics: Factors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET833 - PUBLIC TRANSPORTATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Transit Travel Characteristics: Factors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What are Transit Travel Characteristics?

**Transit Travel Characteristics** are the measurable attributes that describe *how*, *why*, *when*, *where*, and by *whom* public transportation trips are made. They form the foundational input dataset for transit planning, route design, and frequency setting exercises mandated under the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for Public Transportation Systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** Transit travel characteristics are quantifiable parameters of passenger movement on public transit systems, including trip purpose, trip length, trip frequency, travel time, waiting time, mode choice behavior, and demographic distribution of riders, which collectively describe the demand and usage pattern of a transit service.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Restaurant Choice" Analogy

Imagine you are a hungry customer choosing a restaurant in Kochi. You don't pick randomly — your choice is shaped by:

- **Your hunger level** (internal need → analogous to *trip purpose*).
- **Distance from your location** (→ analogous to *trip length / accessibility*).
- **Price, ambience, menu variety** (→ analogous to *transit service quality*).
- **Your age, income, job** (→ analogous to *socio-economic factors*).
- **Traffic congestion outside** (→ analogous to *external factors like congestion, weather*).

Similarly, a commuter choosing between a KSRTC bus and the Kochi Metro will weigh a set of *Trip Characteristics* and *Influencing Factors*. Transit planners study these characteristics to design services that attract more riders, reduce road congestion, and improve urban mobility.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 1, OECET833):** Students must be able to (a) list and classify the factors affecting transit travel, (b) explain how each factor alters demand, and (c) interpret transit trip data for a real city scenario.

## 1.3 Key Characteristic Metrics Used in Kerala Context

The standard engineering metrics reported in transit studies are:

- **Ridership (boardings/day)** — typically 2 to 12 lakh for major Indian metros.
- **Average Trip Length (km)** — Kochi Metro average ≈ **9.2 km**.
- **Peak Hour Factor (PHF)** — ratio of peak-hour to total daily ridership, usually between **0.08 and 0.15**.
- **Modal Split (%)** — share of transit among all motorized trips.
- **Accessibility Index** — percentage of population within **400 m / 800 m** of a transit stop.
- **Service Coverage** — ratio of route-km to area served.
- **Reliability** — Coefficient of Variation (CoV) of headway.

## 1.4 The Two Broad Categories of Characteristics

| Category | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Traveler Characteristics** | Who is travelling | Age, income, car ownership, household size |
| **Trip Characteristics** | Nature of the journey | Trip purpose, length, time-of-day, mode |
| **System Characteristics** | What the transit system offers | Frequency, fare, speed, comfort, reliability |
| **External Characteristics** | Surrounding conditions | Land use, weather, congestion, fuel price |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Breakdown of Factors Affecting Transit Travel

The factors influencing transit travel demand are best organized into a four-tier hierarchy used in **UTPS (Urban Transportation Planning System)** and Indian **MoHUA (Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs)** guidelines.

### Tier 1 — External / Land-Use Factors
These are largely outside the transit operator's control but dictate the *potential* ridership of a corridor.

- **Population density** — measured in persons per hectare. Higher density = higher transit propensity.
- **Employment concentration** — Central Business Districts (CBDs) act as transit anchors (e.g., MG Road, Kochi).
- **Land-use mix** — Mixed-use developments (residential + commercial) generate bidirectional trips, ideal for transit.
- **Distance from transit stop** — Ridership drops sharply beyond **400 m** walking distance.
- **Urban form** — Compact, poly-nucleated cities favour transit; sprawled cities favour cars.

### Tier 2 — Socio-Economic & Demographic Factors

- **Income level** — Lower-income groups are more transit-dependent.
- **Car ownership** — Inverse relationship with transit use.
- **Household size** — Larger households generate more non-work trips.
- **Age** — School-going children and elderly are captive riders.
- **Gender** — Women in Kerala show higher share of NMT (non-motorized) + transit trips.
- **Employment status** — Students and service-sector workers are peak transit users.

### Tier 3 — Trip / Activity Characteristics

- **Trip purpose** — Work trips are *time-sensitive*; shopping trips are *cost-sensitive*.
- **Trip length** — Transit is most competitive between **3 km and 25 km**.
- **Time of day** — Peak hours (8–11 AM, 5–9 PM) drive capacity planning.
- **Trip chaining** — Multi-stop trips discourage transit unless transfers are seamless.

### Tier 4 — Transit Service Quality Factors (the operator's lever)

- **Frequency / Headway** — Higher frequency reduces waiting time and attracts riders.
- **Fare level** — Affordability vs. cost-recovery trade-off.
- **Travel time / Speed** — Including in-vehicle time, walk time, wait time, transfer time.
- **Reliability / Punctuality** — Coefficient of Variation of headway.
- **Comfort & Safety** — Crowding, cleanliness, lighting, security personnel.
- **Accessibility** — Universal access, low-floor buses, escalators at metro stations.
- **Information availability** — Real-time apps, route maps, signage.
- **Integration** — Seamless transfers between bus–metro–auto–bike.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Pattern Note:** When asked to "classify factors," examiners expect you to write the **four-tier hierarchy** above and to give **at least two examples per tier**. Marks are split as: classification (3) + examples (3) + explanation (3) + diagram (5) for a 14-mark question.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates every formula a KTU student must memorize for the ESE (End Semester Examination) on this topic. Units are included; the absolute value and conditional symbols are written using `\vert` to preserve the table layout.

| # | Formula | Description | Units | Typical Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T_i = P_i \times R_i$ | Trip generation from zone $i$ | trips/day | — |
| 2 | $P_{transit} = \dfrac{T_{transit}}{T_{total}} \times 100$ | Transit modal split percentage | % | 20–60 % |
| 3 | $PHF = \dfrac{V_{peak}}{V_{total} \times \Delta t}$ | Peak Hour Factor (where $\Delta t$ is the analysis period in hours) | dimensionless | 0.08–0.15 |
| 4 | $A_{i} = \dfrac{S_{i}}{S_{total}} \times 100$ | Accessibility of stop $i$ (% population within walking catchment) | % | 0–100 |
| 5 | $S = \dfrac{D}{T}$ | Transit speed (distance ÷ travel time) | km/h | 12–35 |
| 6 | $CoV_h = \dfrac{\sigma_h}{\mu_h}$ | Headway reliability index | dimensionless | 0.0–0.6 |
| 7 | $P_{ij}^{transit} = \dfrac{e^{U_{ij}^{T}}}{\sum_{k} e^{U_{ij}^{k}}}$ | Multinomial logit mode-choice probability (T = transit, k = all modes) | probability (0–1) | — |
| 8 | $U_{ij}^{T} = \beta_0 + \beta_1 t_{ij} + \beta_2 c_{ij} + \beta_3 w_{ij}$ | Utility function for transit (time, cost, wait) | utils | — |
| 9 | $L_{avg} = \dfrac{\sum L_{k} \times N_{k}}{\sum N_{k}}$ | Average trip length weighted by passenger volume | km | 5–15 |
| 10 | $R_f = \dfrac{\sum (P_{i}^{obs} - P_{i}^{pred})^{2}}{\sum (P_{i}^{obs})^{2}}$ | Coefficient of determination $R^{2}$ (model fit) | 0–1 | > 0.7 (good) |

**Where:**

- $T_i$ = trips generated in zone $i$
- $P_i$ = population in zone $i$, $R_i$ = trip rate per capita
- $V_{peak}$ = peak-hour volume, $V_{total}$ = daily volume
- $S_i$ = population within catchment of stop $i$
- $\sigma_h$, $\mu_h$ = standard deviation and mean of headway
- $t_{ij}$, $c_{ij}$, $w_{ij}$ = in-vehicle time, cost, waiting time between zones $i$ and $j$

## 2.3 Real-World Utility of This Framework

This four-tier framework is the *backbone* of:

- **Comprehensive Mobility Plans (CMPs)** prepared for Smart Cities (Kochi, Thiruvananthapuram).
- **DPR (Detailed Project Reports)** for metro rail (e.g., Kochi Metro Phase II).
- **Bus Route Rationalization** exercises by KSRTC and city bus operators.
- **Parking and congestion-pricing policy** decisions by ULBs.
- **Environmental Impact Assessments (EIA)** for transit corridors.

A planner who can quantify the impact of each factor can convince a municipal commissioner to shift from supply-side road widening to demand-side transit investment — a core competency of the KTU 2024 OECET833 graduate.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Trip Rate and Transit Trip Generation

The simplest and most frequently tested relationship in KTU Module 1 is the link between population and the number of transit trips produced by a zone.

### Given
A ward (zone) in Thiruvananthapuram has:

- Population $P = 45{,}000$ persons
- Per-capita daily trip rate $R = 1.6$ trips/person/day
- Transit modal share $M = 32$ %

### Step 1 — Compute total trips generated by the zone.

Total trips generated $T_{total}$ is the product of population and trip rate.

$$
T_{total} = P \times R
$$

$$
T_{total} = 45{,}000 \times 1.6
$$

$$
T_{total} = 72{,}000 \;\text{trips/day}
$$

*Logical conversion:* Multiplying persons by trips/person cancels the "persons" unit, leaving trips.

### Step 2 — Compute transit trips only.

Transit trips are obtained by multiplying total trips with the modal share fraction.

$$
T_{transit} = T_{total} \times \dfrac{M}{100}
$$

$$
T_{transit} = 72{,}000 \times \dfrac{32}{100}
$$

$$
T_{transit} = 23{,}040 \;\text{transit trips/day}
$$

### Step 3 — Convert to peak-hour transit demand using PHF.

Assume Peak Hour Factor $PHF = 0.12$.

$$
T_{transit}^{peak} = T_{transit} \times PHF
$$

$$
T_{transit}^{peak} = 23{,}040 \times 0.12
$$

$$
T_{transit}^{peak} = 2{,}764.8 \approx 2{,}765 \;\text{transit trips in peak hour}
$$

### Step 4 — Determine number of buses required.

Assume a standard city bus capacity $C = 60$ passengers, and peak load factor $LF = 0.90$ (i.e., buses are 90 % full at peak).

Required buses per hour $N$ are obtained by:

$$
N = \dfrac{T_{transit}^{peak}}{C \times LF}
$$

$$
N = \dfrac{2{,}765}{60 \times 0.90}
$$

$$
N = \dfrac{2{,}765}{54}
$$

$$
N \approx 51.2 \Rightarrow 52 \;\text{buses/hour}
$$

**Interpretation:** The ward needs roughly **52 bus departures per hour** in the peak to meet demand — a directly actionable design number.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Modal Split Using a Simplified Logit Model

The multinomial logit model is the standard *behavioural* model tested in 14-mark KTU questions.

### Given
Two competing modes between two zones:

- **Mode A (Car):** in-vehicle time $t_A = 25$ min, cost $c_A = \text{₹}80$, wait $w_A = 0$ min.
- **Mode B (Transit):** in-vehicle time $t_B = 35$ min, cost $c_B = \text{₹}20$, wait $w_B = 8$ min.

Utility coefficients (in utils):

$$
\beta_0 = 0, \quad \beta_1 = -0.05 \;\text{per min}, \quad \beta_2 = -0.04 \;\text{per ₹}, \quad \beta_3 = -0.10 \;\text{per min wait}
$$

### Step 1 — Write the utility function.

The general utility function is:

$$
U = \beta_0 + \beta_1 \, t + \beta_2 \, c + \beta_3 \, w
$$

### Step 2 — Compute utility of Car (Mode A).

$$
U_A = 0 + (-0.05)(25) + (-0.04)(80) + (-0.10)(0)
$$

$$
U_A = -1.25 - 3.20 + 0
$$

$$
U_A = -4.45 \;\text{utils}
$$

### Step 3 — Compute utility of Transit (Mode B).

$$
U_B = 0 + (-0.05)(35) + (-0.04)(20) + (-0.10)(8)
$$

$$
U_B = -1.75 - 0.80 - 0.80
$$

$$
U_B = -3.35 \;\text{utils}
$$

### Step 4 — Apply the logit formula.

$$
P_B = \dfrac{e^{U_B}}{e^{U_A} + e^{U_B}}
$$

$$
e^{U_A} = e^{-4.45} = 0.0117
$$

$$
e^{U_B} = e^{-3.35} = 0.0350
$$

$$
P_B = \dfrac{0.0350}{0.0117 + 0.0350} = \dfrac{0.0350}{0.0467}
$$

$$
P_B \approx 0.749 \Rightarrow 74.9\%
$$

### Step 5 — Interpret the result.

> **About 75 % of commuters will choose transit** in this OD pair. The higher cost of car (₹80) and the negative weight on cost are the dominant factors — even though transit is slower (35 vs. 25 min), the **fare differential** tilts the choice strongly toward transit.

### Step 6 — Sensitivity check.

If transit fare is increased by ₹20 (new cost = ₹40):

$$
U_B^{new} = -1.75 - (0.04 \times 40) - 0.80 = -1.75 - 1.60 - 0.80 = -4.15
$$

$$
e^{U_B^{new}} = 0.0158
$$

$$
P_B^{new} = \dfrac{0.0158}{0.0117 + 0.0158} = 0.574 \Rightarrow 57.4\%
$$

**Insight:** A 100 % fare increase drops transit share by ~17 percentage points — a powerful real-world insight for fare policy in Kerala.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Sensitivity of Transit Ridership to Key Factors

The matrix below qualitatively summarizes the direction and magnitude of influence of each factor on transit ridership, calibrated to typical Indian/Kerala conditions.

| Factor | Direction of Effect | Magnitude (Low / Med / High) | Engineering Lever? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Increase in population density | ↑ Ridership | High | No (planning policy) |
| Higher car ownership | ↓ Ridership | High | No (tax/policy) |
| Higher service frequency | ↑ Ridership | High | **Yes** |
| Higher fare | ↓ Ridership | High | **Yes** |
| Better reliability (lower CoV) | ↑ Ridership | Medium | **Yes** |
| Improved walking access (sidewalks) | ↑ Ridership | Medium | **Yes** |
| Increase in income | ↓ Ridership (for bus), ↑ for AC/metro | Medium | No |
| Mixed land-use development | ↑ Ridership | High | No (zoning) |
| Bad weather (monsoon in Kerala) | ↓ Ridership at bus stops; ↑ at metro | Medium | Partial (shelters) |
| Real-time information apps | ↑ Ridership | Low–Medium | **Yes** |
| Park-and-ride facilities | ↑ Ridership | Medium | **Yes** |
| Integration (single ticketing) | ↑ Ridership | High | **Yes** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** In a 14-mark question, always present this matrix as a **summary table** at the end of your answer. Examiners award **2–3 marks** for a well-structured synthesis.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Factors Affecting Transit Travel

The block diagram below maps the four-tier hierarchy of factors and their interactions, using **alphanumeric Mermaid node identifiers** as required by the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 protocol.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[EXTERNAL LANDUSE FACTORS] --> B1[Population Density]
    A1 --> B2[Employment Concentration]
    A1 --> B3[Land Use Mix]
    A1 --> B4[Urban Form]
    A1 --> B5[Distance to Stop]

    C1[SOCIOECONOMIC FACTORS] --> D1[Income Level]
    C1 --> D2[Car Ownership]
    C1 --> D3[Household Size]
    C1 --> D4[Age and Gender]
    C1 --> D5[Employment Status]

    E1[TRIP ACTIVITY FACTORS] --> F1[Trip Purpose]
    E1 --> F2[Trip Length]
    E1 --> F3[Time of Day]
    E1 --> F4[Trip Chaining]

    G1[SERVICE QUALITY FACTORS] --> H1[Frequency and Headway]
    G1 --> H2[Fare Level]
    G1 --> H3[Travel Time and Speed]
    G1 --> H4[Reliability Punctuality]
    G1 --> H5[Comfort and Safety]
    G1 --> H6[Accessibility]
    G1 --> H7[Information Systems]
    G1 --> H8[Service Integration]

    B5 --> Z1[TRANSIT RIDERSHIP DEMAND]
    D2 --> Z1
    F2 --> Z1
    H1 --> Z1
    H2 --> Z1
    H3 --> Z1
    H4 --> Z1
    H5 --> Z1
    H6 --> Z1
    H7 --> Z1
    H8 --> Z1

    Z1 --> OUT1[Boardings per Day]
    Z1 --> OUT2[Peak Hour Load]
    Z1 --> OUT3[Modal Split Percentage]
    Z1 --> OUT4[Average Trip Length]
    Z1 --> OUT5[Revenue Earned]
```

**Reading the diagram:** Each factor feeds into the central "Transit Ridership Demand" node, which in turn produces the measurable outputs used in planning. The **service quality factors** are the only cluster that the *transit operator* can directly control — making them the most actionable.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Behavioural Trip-Choice Process

This block diagram shows the cognitive/decision sequence a commuter follows when choosing a mode, mirroring the *random utility maximization* framework used in the logit derivation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S0[Commuter with Trip Need] --> S1[Identify Available Modes]
    S1 --> S2[Estimate Attributes Time Cost Comfort]
    S2 --> S3[Form Utility for Each Mode]
    S3 --> S4[Apply Choice Rule Logit or Nested]
    S4 --> S5[Select Mode with Highest Utility]
    S5 --> S6[Execute Trip]
    S6 --> S7[Post-Trip Experience]
    S7 --> S2
```

**Reading the diagram:** The decision is *iterative* — a positive or negative experience feeds back into the next trip's utility estimate. This explains why **reliability** matters more than the **average** travel time: a one-off bad experience (e.g., a missed bus during Kerala monsoon) can permanently shift a rider to a different mode.

---

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Kerala Transit Planning Workflow

This diagram maps how the *characteristics* studied in Module 1 actually flow into a real planning exercise (e.g., a CMP for Kochi or a DPR for a new metro corridor).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    K1[Data Collection Surveys] --> K2[Transit Travel Characteristics]
    K2 --> K3[Identify Influencing Factors]
    K3 --> K4[Calibrate Demand Model]
    K4 --> K5[Forecast Future Ridership]
    K5 --> K6[Design Service Plan Routes Frequency]
    K6 --> K7[Cost and Revenue Analysis]
    K7 --> K8[Final Transit Plan and DPR]
    K8 --> K9[Implementation by KSRTC KMRL]
```

**Reading the diagram:** The blue "Transit Travel Characteristics" block is the *data foundation*. Without accurate measurement of trip rates, modal split, and accessibility, every subsequent model becomes unreliable — a fact that KTU examiners reward in answers that show end-to-end thinking.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, OECET833]
**List and briefly explain the four major categories of factors affecting transit travel demand.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The four major categories are:

1. **External / Land-Use Factors** — population density, employment concentration, land-use mix, urban form, and distance to transit stops. These define the *latent demand* for transit. **(1 mark)**
2. **Socio-Economic and Demographic Factors** — income, car ownership, household size, age, gender, and employment status, which determine *who* uses transit. **(1 mark)**
3. **Trip / Activity Characteristics** — trip purpose, trip length, time of day, and trip chaining, which describe *what kind* of journey is being made. **(0.5 mark)**
4. **Transit Service Quality Factors** — frequency, fare, travel time, reliability, comfort, accessibility, information systems, and integration — the *operator-controllable* levers. **(0.5 mark)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, OECET833]
**Define modal split and explain its significance in transit planning.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Definition (1 mark):** Modal split is the percentage share of total person-trips carried by a particular mode of transport, computed as $P_{transit} = \dfrac{T_{transit}}{T_{total}} \times 100$.
- **Significance — 3 points (2 marks):**
  1. It quantifies the *competitiveness* of transit against private modes.
  2. It is the *key output* of the mode-choice stage of the 4-step urban transport model.
  3. It guides policy — a low modal share justifies investment in metro/BRTS, while a high share supports incremental bus upgrades.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Do **NOT** write the four factors as a flat list; you will lose 2 marks. Use the **four-tier hierarchy** with explicit headings.
> - Always include a **diagram** (mermaid-style or hand-drawn block) — it is worth 2–3 marks even in 7-mark sub-questions.
> - For numerical problems, **state the formula first**, then substitute values; do not skip steps.
> - Use **Kerala-specific examples** (Kochi Metro, KSRTC, Vande Bharat stop, etc.) wherever possible to earn "context" marks.
> - Avoid one-word answers for "explain"; examiners deduct up to 30 % for insufficient depth.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, OECET833]

**(a) Classify and explain the various factors affecting transit travel characteristics. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

The factors affecting transit travel characteristics can be classified into four broad categories:

**1. External / Land-Use Factors (2 marks)**
These include population density, employment concentration, land-use mix, urban form (compact vs. sprawled), and distance from transit stops. Higher density along a corridor (e.g., the MG Road–Edappally corridor in Kochi with > 250 persons/ha) directly increases transit ridership. Studies show ridership falls by 50 % beyond a 400 m walking distance from a stop.

**2. Socio-Economic and Demographic Factors (2 marks)**
Income, car ownership, household size, age, gender, and employment status shape the *captive* vs. *choice* rider profile. In Kerala, the relatively high literacy and lower per-capita car ownership make a large share of commuters transit-dependent. Women show a higher share of walk + transit trips for non-work purposes.

**3. Trip / Activity Characteristics (1.5 marks)**
Trip purpose, trip length, time of day, and trip chaining influence mode choice. Work trips (≈ 45 % of all trips in Indian cities) are time-sensitive and shift strongly with transit speed. Trip lengths between 3 and 25 km are the "sweet spot" where transit is most competitive.

**4. Transit Service Quality Factors (1.5 marks)**
Frequency, fare, travel time, reliability, comfort, accessibility, information, and integration are operator-controlled levers. A 10 % improvement in reliability typically yields a 3–4 % rise in ridership, per Indian Railways and KMRL studies.

> *Examiner tip:* Conclude with a one-line statement such as: *"Of these, only Tier-4 factors are directly controllable by the transit operator, making them the most strategic for ridership growth."* — this earns the **synthesis mark**.

---

**(b) A ward has a population of 80,000 and a trip rate of 1.5 trips/capita/day. The transit modal share is 28 %, and the peak-hour factor is 0.11. A city bus carries 50 passengers with a peak load factor of 0.85. Calculate: (i) total daily transit trips, (ii) peak-hour transit trips, and (iii) buses required per hour in the peak. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Total daily transit trips (2 marks)**

$$
T_{total} = P \times R = 80{,}000 \times 1.5 = 1{,}20{,}000 \;\text{trips/day}
$$

$$
T_{transit} = T_{total} \times \dfrac{M}{100} = 1{,}20{,}000 \times 0.28 = 33{,}600 \;\text{transit trips/day}
$$

**(ii) Peak-hour transit trips (2 marks)**

$$
T_{transit}^{peak} = T_{transit} \times PHF = 33{,}600 \times 0.11 = 3{,}696 \;\text{trips in peak hour}
$$

**(iii) Buses required per hour (3 marks)**

Effective capacity per bus = $C \times LF = 50 \times 0.85 = 42.5$ passengers.

$$
N = \dfrac{3{,}696}{42.5} = 86.96 \approx 87 \;\text{buses/hour}
$$

**Final answer:** Total daily transit trips = **33,600**; Peak-hour transit trips = **3,696**; Required buses in peak = **~87 per hour**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2023, OECET833]

**(a) Discuss the role of transit service quality factors in attracting ridership, with reference to at least six factors. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Transit service quality is the **only set of factors the operator can directly control**, and even small improvements yield measurable ridership gains. The six most important service quality factors are:

**1. Frequency / Headway (1.5 marks)**
Higher frequency reduces passenger waiting time, a variable that travellers weight 1.5–2× more heavily than in-vehicle time. Kochi Metro's 7-minute peak headway is a key reason for its rising ridership (over 1 lakh/day in 2024).

**2. Fare Level (1 mark)**
Affordability is critical for captive riders. However, low fares hurt financial sustainability. A well-designed distance-based fare (as on Kochi Metro) balances both.

**3. Travel Time and Speed (1.5 marks)**
Includes in-vehicle time, walk time, wait time, and transfer time. A dedicated right-of-way (e.g., metro or BRTS) can lift commercial speed from 12 km/h (mixed-traffic bus) to 30+ km/h.

**4. Reliability / Punctuality (1 mark)**
Measured by the Coefficient of Variation (CoV) of headway. Reducing CoV from 0.6 to 0.2 typically increases ridership by 8–12 %.

**5. Comfort and Safety (1 mark)**
Crowding, air-conditioning, lighting, CCTV, and security personnel influence willingness to ride, especially for women, students, and elderly.

**6. Information and Integration (1 mark)**
Real-time arrival apps, integrated ticketing (Kochi One card), and seamless bus–metro–auto transfers reduce the "friction" of multi-modal journeys.

> *Synthesis (0 marks, but earns impression):* Collectively, these factors determine whether transit is **perceived as competitive with the car** — the central battleground for the next decade of urban mobility in Kerala.

---

**(b) The utilities of Car and Transit between two zones are given by $U = -0.04\,t - 0.03\,c - 0.08\,w$, where $t$ is in-vehicle time (min), $c$ is cost (₹), and $w$ is waiting time (min). For Car: $t = 30$, $c = 100$, $w = 0$. For Transit: $t = 40$, $c = 25$, $w = 10$. Compute the transit modal split using the multinomial logit model. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Utility of Car (2 marks)**

$$
U_{car} = -0.04(30) - 0.03(100) - 0.08(0)
$$

$$
U_{car} = -1.20 - 3.00 + 0 = -4.20 \;\text{utils}
$$

**Step 2 — Utility of Transit (2 marks)**

$$
U_{transit} = -0.04(40) - 0.03(25) - 0.08(10)
$$

$$
U_{transit} = -1.60 - 0.75 - 0.80 = -3.15 \;\text{utils}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the logit formula (2 marks)**

$$
P_{transit} = \dfrac{e^{-3.15}}{e^{-4.20} + e^{-3.15}}
$$

$$
e^{-4.20} = 0.0150, \quad e^{-3.15} = 0.0429
$$

$$
P_{transit} = \dfrac{0.0429}{0.0150 + 0.0429} = \dfrac{0.0429}{0.0579} = 0.741
$$

**Step 4 — State the final answer (1 mark)**

$$
P_{transit} \approx 74.1 \%
$$

**Interpretation:** Despite being 10 min slower, transit attracts 74 % of commuters because of its dramatically lower cost (₹25 vs. ₹100) and the high penalty attached to waiting time in this utility specification.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (10-second recall list before entering the exam hall):**

- ☐ Transit travel characteristics = **measurable attributes of transit trips** (who, what, when, where, why, how).
- ☐ **Four-tier classification:** Land-use → Socio-economic → Trip → Service quality.
- ☐ **400 m / 800 m** is the magic walking catchment radius for a transit stop.
- ☐ Transit competitive range: **3–25 km** trip length.
- ☐ PHF for Indian cities typically lies in **0.08–0.15**.
- ☐ Peak load factor (LF) for buses ≈ **0.85–0.90**; for metro ≈ **0.95–1.0**.
- ☐ Multinomial logit model formula: $P_k = \dfrac{e^{U_k}}{\sum_j e^{U_j}}$.
- ☐ Utility function form: $U = \beta_0 + \beta_1 t + \beta_2 c + \beta_3 w$ (with negative coefficients).
- ☐ Waiting time is weighted **1.5–2×** in-vehicle time in standard utility specifications.
- ☐ **Reliability** (low CoV) is often more important than average speed.
- ☐ Land-use mix, density, and integration are *structural* levers; frequency and fare are *operational* levers.
- ☐ For KTU numericals: always **state the formula → substitute → compute → interpret**.
- ☐ Kerala-specific case examples to memorize: **Kochi Metro, KSRTC city services, Smart City Thiruvananthapuram, Vande Bharat stops at Ernakulam**.
- ☐ Always draw a **block diagram or flowchart** in long answers — minimum 2 marks.
- ☐ Service quality factors are the *only* operator-controlled inputs; emphasize them in synthesis.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Spatial Distribution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET833 - PUBLIC TRANSPORTATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Spatial Distribution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Spatial Distribution** in the context of Public Transportation Systems refers to the **geometric arrangement of transit infrastructure** (routes, stops, terminals, and service corridors) across a given urban or regional geography, and the corresponding **allocation of transit service capacity** to match the spatial patterns of travel demand, population density, and land use.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, spatial distribution is the **first-principles planning decision** that determines:

1. **Where** transit routes should be physically located (route alignment and spacing).
2. **Where** stops should be placed along those routes (stop spacing logic).
3. **What area** the system effectively "covers" given pedestrian access tolerances (service area / catchment).
4. **How much service** should be allocated to each corridor based on the spatial intensity of demand.

The three canonical spatial distribution variables taught in KTU Module 1 are:

| Variable | Symbol | Typical Magnitude |
|---|---|---|
| Stop Spacing | $S_s$ | **400 – 800 m** (urban bus) |
| Route Spacing | $S_r$ | **800 – 1600 m** (dense urban) |
| Walk Access Radius | $r_w$ | **400 m** (≈ 5-minute walk) |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Fishing Net Over the City" Model

Imagine a city as a **lake full of fish** (potential riders), and the public transport system as a **fishing net** you cast across it.

- The **mesh size of the net** = stop spacing. Too fine (stops too close) → the fish escape between meshes (travel is too slow). Too coarse (stops too far) → fish can't reach the net (low access).
- The **distance between parallel ropes of the net** = route spacing. Determines how much of the lake surface is "covered."
- The **thickness of each rope** = service frequency. A thick rope catches more fish per unit area.

> [!NOTE]
> **Pedagogical Anchor:** Spatial distribution is the "geometry layer" of transit planning. It precedes the "frequency layer" and the "schedule layer." You cannot intelligently decide *how often* a bus should run until you decide *where the bus should go* and *where it should stop*.

---

## 1.3 Why Spatial Distribution is the *First* Question in Transit Planning

Before any operational decision (frequency, vehicle type, schedule), the planner must answer four spatial questions:

1. **Coverage Question** — What fraction of the population lives within walking distance of *any* transit stop?
2. **Accessibility Question** — How many jobs/destinations are reachable within a 30-min transit trip?
3. **Route Spacing Question** — How many parallel corridors are needed in a given district?
4. **Stop Spacing Question** — How densely should stops be placed along a single corridor?

These questions are all governed by the **walk-access tolerance** of passengers, which in transit planning is universally adopted as **400 m (¼ mile)** for buses and **800 m (½ mile)** for rail-based modes.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Spatial Distribution

### Pillar 1 — Stop Spacing ($S_s$)

Stop spacing is the **along-route distance** between consecutive stops. It is the result of a fundamental trade-off:

- **Smaller $S_s$ (denser stops):**
  - ✅ Shorter walk access distance.
  - ✅ Higher local coverage.
  - ❌ Slower commercial speed (more dwell time + acceleration losses).
  - ❌ Higher vehicle and crew cost per km.

- **Larger $S_s$ (sparser stops):**
  - ✅ Higher commercial speed → shorter journey time for long trips.
  - ✅ Lower operating cost per km.
  - ❌ Poor local coverage, longer walk access.
  - ❌ Equity loss for elderly/disabled.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Rule of Thumb (Textbook Standard):** Optimal urban bus stop spacing in dense Indian cities is typically **400 – 600 m**, balancing 5-minute walk access with acceptable commercial speed of 15–20 km/h.

### Pillar 2 — Route Spacing ($S_r$)

Route spacing is the **perpendicular distance between parallel transit corridors** serving the same general direction. It is governed by:

- Population + employment density (higher density → closer routes).
- Street network topology (grid vs. radial).
- Mode of transit (metro corridors are spaced farther apart than bus corridors because of higher speed and station cost).

### Pillar 3 — Service Area / Catchment Area ($A_c$)

The **service area** (or **catchment**) of a stop or route is the geographic zone from which passengers can reasonably walk to access transit. KTU Module 1 uses two canonical catchment geometries:

1. **Circular catchment (isolated stop):** A circle of radius $r_w$ centered on the stop.
2. **Corridor catchment (route segment):** A rectangular band of width $2r_w$ along a route segment of length $L$.

### Pillar 4 — Network Density ($\delta$)

Network density quantifies **how richly transit routes permeate** a given urban area:

$$\delta = \frac{\sum L_{route}}{A_{city}}$$

where $A_{city}$ is the urbanized area in $\text{km}^2$ and $\sum L_{route}$ is the total route-km.

---

## 2.2 The Walk-Access Tolerance — The 400 m Standard

The **walk-access tolerance** is the single most important spatial constant in transit planning. It originates from empirical studies of passenger behavior:

- A walking speed of approximately **5 km/h (80 m/min)**.
- A maximum acceptable walk time of **5 minutes** to access transit.
- $5 \text{ min} \times 80 \text{ m/min} = 400 \text{ m}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Constant:** Always use **$r_w = 0.4$ km** for bus catchment and **$r_w = 0.8$ km** for rail/metro catchment unless the question explicitly states otherwise.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet — Cheat-Sheet Table

| # | Formula | LaTeX | Units | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Walk-access radius (bus) | $r_w = 0.4$ | km | 5-min walk catchment |
| 2 | Walk-access radius (rail) | $r_w = 0.8$ | km | 10-min walk catchment |
| 3 | Service area of one stop (circle) | $A_c = \pi r_w^{\,2}$ | $\text{km}^2$ | Coverage of isolated stop |
| 4 | Service area of route segment | $A_c = 2 \, r_w \, L$ | $\text{km}^2$ | Coverage of route corridor |
| 5 | Population covered by stop | $P_c = \rho \cdot A_c$ | persons | Riders potentially served |
| 6 | Network density | $\delta = \sum L_{route} \,/\, A_{city}$ | $\text{km/km}^2$ | Route richness per area |
| 7 | Coverage ratio | $C_r = A_{covered} \,/\, A_{city}$ | dimensionless | Fraction of city covered |
| 8 | Stop density | $\sigma = N_{stops} \,/\, A_{city}$ | $\text{stops/km}^2$ | Spatial intensity of stops |
| 9 | Average inter-stop distance | $S_s = L_{route} \,/\, (N_{stops} - 1)$ | km | Mean stop spacing on route |
| 10 | Route directness (circuity) | $R_d = d_{transit} \,/\, d_{crow-fly}$ | dimensionless | Penalty for indirect routing |
| 11 | Minimum route spacing (grid) | $S_r = 2 r_w$ | km | Routes spaced to avoid coverage gap |
| 12 | Critical density for transit | $\rho_{crit} = C_{op} \,/\, (C_{rev} \cdot A_c)$ | $\text{persons/km}^2$ | Density needed to justify transit |

> [!NOTE]
> **Substitution Rule for Tables:** Wherever a vertical bar \vert would normally appear (e.g., in an absolute value or a "divided by" notation inside a table cell), the rendered table above uses slashes ( / ) or `\,/\,` LaTeX to preserve Markdown table integrity.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Spatial distribution analysis is the **first filter** in the transit planning cycle:

1. **Urban Bus Transit (BEST Mumbai, DTC Delhi, KSRTC city services):** Stop spacing decisions directly determine commercial speed and patronage.
2. **BRTS Corridors (Ahmedabad, Pune, Surat, Kochi):** Route spacing and station spacing dictate whether the BRT can compete with private vehicles.
3. **Metro Rail (Delhi, Kochi, Bengaluru):** Station spacing of 1.0–1.2 km balances walking catchment with high commercial speed.
4. **Feeder Bus Networks:** Designed with **denser** stop spacing (≈ 250–400 m) because they serve short trips from neighborhoods to metro stations.
5. **Equity Planning:** Spatial distribution maps are used to identify transit deserts (areas beyond 400 m of any stop) for corrective investment.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Service Area of a Single Isolated Stop

A transit stop is treated as a **point feature** on a 2-D plane. Passengers within walking distance $r_w$ of the point can access it. The set of all such points is geometrically a **circle of radius $r_w$**.

**Step 1.** The geometric area of a circle of radius $r$ is given by the standard formula $A = \pi r^2$.

**Step 2.** Substitute $r = r_w$:

$$A_c = \pi \, r_w^{\,2}$$

**Step 3.** Insert the KTU-standard walk-access radius for bus transit, $r_w = 0.4 \text{ km}$:

$$A_c = \pi \, (0.4)^2$$

**Step 4.** Evaluate numerically:

$$A_c = \pi \times 0.16 = 0.5027 \text{ km}^2$$

**Conclusion.** Each isolated urban bus stop in KTU convention covers a circular catchment of approximately **0.50 km²**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Service Area of a Route Segment (Corridor Catchment)

A route is a 1-D line, but passengers can access it from **both sides** within walking distance. The catchment is therefore a **rectangular band** of:

- Length = route segment length $L$.
- Width = $2 r_w$ (one $r_w$ on each side).

**Step 1.** Area of a rectangle = length × width:

$$A_c = L \times (2 r_w)$$

**Step 2.** Therefore:

$$A_c = 2 \, r_w \, L$$

**Step 3.** For a 1 km route segment with bus-standard walk radius:

$$A_c = 2 \times 0.4 \times 1.0 = 0.8 \text{ km}^2 \text{ per route-km}$$

**Step 4.** The **end-cap correction**: at the two ends of the route, the catchment adds two half-circles (= one full circle of area $\pi r_w^{\,2}$). For a long route, this is negligible; for a short feeder, it is significant.

$$A_c^{total} = 2 \, r_w \, L + \pi \, r_w^{\,2}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Minimum Route Spacing on a Grid Network

To ensure **no spatial gap** in coverage between two parallel routes, the perpendicular distance between them must be such that the catchment bands **just touch or overlap**.

**Step 1.** Each route's catchment extends $r_w$ to either side, so the total catchment width on one side of a route is $r_w$.

**Step 2.** For two parallel routes to leave **no uncovered gap**, the spacing must satisfy:

$$S_r \leq 2 \, r_w$$

**Step 3.** Substituting $r_w = 0.4$ km:

$$S_r \leq 0.8 \text{ km}$$

**Conclusion.** On a **perfect grid network**, parallel bus routes must be spaced no more than **800 m apart** to ensure 100% coverage. Wider spacing creates "transit deserts" between corridors.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Critical Population Density for Viable Transit

A transit route is **financially viable** only if revenue from its catchment at least equals operating cost. Let:

- $\rho$ = population density (persons/km²).
- $A_c$ = corridor catchment area per route-km = $2 r_w$.
- $f$ = average fare (₹/passenger).
- $C_{op}$ = operating cost per route-km (₹/km).

**Step 1.** Potential passengers per route-km per day:

$$P_{daily} = \rho \times A_c \times \text{mode share factor}$$

**Step 2.** For break-even in the simplest model:

$$\text{Revenue} = \text{Cost}$$

**Step 3.** $P_{daily} \times f = C_{op}$.

**Step 4.** Therefore the **critical (break-even) density** is:

$$\rho_{crit} = \frac{C_{op}}{f \cdot 2 r_w}$$

**Step 5.** Plug in example numbers: $C_{op} = ₹200/\text{km}$, $f = ₹10$, $r_w = 0.4$ km:

$$\rho_{crit} = \frac{200}{10 \times 0.8} = 25 \text{ persons/km}^2$$

**Conclusion.** Any corridor with density below this threshold cannot support regular bus service without subsidy.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — How Each Variable Interacts

| Change in Variable | Effect on Coverage | Effect on Commercial Speed | Effect on Cost/km | KTU Implication |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Decrease** $S_s$ (closer stops) | ↑ | ↓ | ↑ | Local/feeder routes |
| **Increase** $S_s$ (sparser stops) | ↓ | ↑ | ↓ | Express/trunk routes |
| **Increase** $S_r$ (wider route spacing) | ↓ (gaps appear) | neutral | neutral | Need demand density check |
| **Increase** $r_w$ (e.g., rail standard) | ↑↑ | neutral | neutral | Used for metro planning |
| **Increase** $\delta$ (network density) | ↑ | neutral | ↑↑ | Capital-intensive BRT/metro |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Block-Level Functional Architecture of Spatial Distribution Planning

The diagram below maps the **decision-flow architecture** of how a transit planner moves from raw urban geography to a fully spatially-distributed transit network.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Urban Geography Input] --> B0[Land Use & Density Map]
    A0 --> C0[Road Network Topology]
    A0 --> D0[Population & Employment Data]
    
    B0 --> E0[Step 1: Identify High-Demand Corridors]
    C0 --> E0
    D0 --> E0
    
    E0 --> F0[Step 2: Decide Route Spacing Sr]
    F0 --> G0{Sr less than 2 rw?}
    G0 -->|Yes| H0[100 percent Coverage Achievable]
    G0 -->|No| I0[Transit Deserts Flagged]
    I0 --> J0[Add Feeder Routes]
    
    H0 --> K0[Step 3: Place Route Alignment on Map]
    J0 --> K0
    
    K0 --> L0[Step 4: Decide Stop Spacing Ss]
    L0 --> M0{Service Type?}
    M0 -->|Local Bus| N0[Ss equals 400 to 600 m]
    M0 -->|BRT Corridor| O0[Ss equals 800 to 1000 m]
    M0 -->|Metro Rail| P0[Ss equals 1000 to 1200 m]
    M0 -->|Express Bus| Q0[Ss equals 1500 to 2000 m]
    
    N0 --> R0[Step 5: Compute Service Area Ac]
    O0 --> R0
    P0 --> R0
    Q0 --> R0
    R0 --> S0[Step 6: Compute Coverage Ratio Cr]
    S0 --> T0[Final Spatially Distributed Network]
    
    T0 --> U0[Pass to Module 2: Frequency & Scheduling]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Catchment Geometry Visualization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Route Alignment] --> B1[Segment Length L]
    A1 --> C1[Walk Radius rw equals 400 m]
    C1 --> D1[Left Side Catchment Strip]
    C1 --> E1[Right Side Catchment Strip]
    D1 --> F1[Corridor Band Width 2 rw equals 800 m]
    E1 --> F1
    B1 --> F1
    F1 --> G1[Effective Catchment Area Ac]
    G1 --> H1[Population Served Pc equals rho times Ac]
    H1 --> I1[Demand Input for Frequency Module]
```

## 4.3 Interpretation Notes for the Diagrams

- **Node A0 / B0 / C0 / D0** represent the **input layer** of geographic and demographic data.
- **E0 → F0 → G0** is the **route spacing decision loop**, including the critical inequality $S_r \leq 2 r_w$.
- **L0 → M0 → N0 / O0 / P0 / Q0** is the **mode-specific stop spacing decision tree**, where each transit mode has its own characteristic stop spacing band.
- **R0 → S0** is the **service area computation** that feeds into coverage ratio evaluation.
- **T0 → U0** marks the **handoff to Module 2**, emphasizing that spatial distribution is a *prerequisite*, not a *substitute*, for frequency planning.

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Reading Tip:** In the second diagram, follow the route alignment (Node A1) downward. The two parallel strips (D1 and E1) represent the **left-side and right-side walk catchments**, which together form the corridor band. This is the geometric basis of the formula $A_c = 2 r_w L$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper Set B]

**Q. Define the term "Spatial Distribution" as used in public transportation planning. List any two spatial-distribution variables used in transit planning.**

**Model Answer:**

Spatial distribution in public transport planning refers to the **geometric arrangement of transit routes, stops, and service areas across an urban region** to maximize coverage and accessibility of the transit system in relation to land use and population density patterns.

The two key spatial-distribution variables are:

1. **Stop Spacing ($S_s$)** — the along-route distance between consecutive stops.
2. **Route Spacing ($S_r$)** — the perpendicular distance between parallel transit corridors.

*(Acceptable second pair: Walk-Access Radius $r_w$ and Network Density $\delta$.)*

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**Q. State the standard walk-access radius used in bus transit planning and justify why this particular value is universally adopted.**

**Model Answer:**

The standard walk-access radius for bus transit is **$r_w = 400$ m** (¼ mile).

**Justification:**
This value originates from empirical passenger-behavior studies:
- Average pedestrian walking speed ≈ **5 km/h (80 m/min)**.
- Maximum acceptable access walk time ≈ **5 minutes**.
- Therefore: $5 \text{ min} \times 80 \text{ m/min} = 400$ m.

Beyond 400 m, passenger ridership drops sharply (the so-called "**distance-decay function**" of transit access).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question B1 — Option A [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Adapted]

**A metropolitan authority plans a BRT corridor of length 12 km through a residential district. The walk-access radius is 0.4 km. The population density along the corridor is 8,000 persons/km². The operating cost is ₹250 per bus-km and the average fare is ₹12.**

**(a) Compute the catchment area and the population served by the corridor. [7 Marks]**

**(b) Determine the break-even daily ridership per route-km and comment on whether the corridor is financially viable. [7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Step 1.** The corridor catchment is a rectangular band:

$$A_c = 2 \, r_w \, L$$

**Step 2.** Substitute $r_w = 0.4$ km and $L = 12$ km:

$$A_c = 2 \times 0.4 \times 12 = 9.6 \text{ km}^2$$

**Step 3.** The population served is density × area:

$$P_c = \rho \times A_c = 8000 \times 9.6 = 76{,}800 \text{ persons}$$

**Answer (a):** The corridor covers **9.6 km²** and serves approximately **76,800 residents**.

---

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1.** The break-even condition is:

$$\text{Revenue per route-km} = \text{Cost per route-km}$$

**Step 2.** Cost per route-km = ₹250. Fare = ₹12. So break-even ridership per route-km:

$$P_{BE} = \frac{C_{op}}{f} = \frac{250}{12} \approx 20.83 \text{ passengers/route-km/day}$$

**Step 3.** For 12 route-km, total break-even daily ridership:

$$P_{BE}^{total} = 20.83 \times 12 \approx 250 \text{ passengers/day}$$

**Step 4.** Realistic daily ridership from a corridor serving 76,800 residents — assuming a modest 5% transit mode share and 1.2 trips per rider per day on this corridor:

$$P_{realistic} = 0.05 \times 76{,}800 \times 1.2 \approx 4{,}608 \text{ passengers/day}$$

**Step 5.** Comparison:

$$P_{realistic} = 4{,}608 \gg P_{BE}^{total} = 250$$

**Conclusion (b):** The corridor is **highly viable**. Realistic demand is roughly **18 times** the break-even threshold, leaving substantial surplus revenue that can support higher service frequency.

---

### Question B1 — Option B [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]

**A city has a planned area of 50 km². A transit planner proposes a grid network of parallel bus routes spaced 1.2 km apart, with stops placed every 600 m. The walk-access radius is 0.4 km.**

**(a) Determine the maximum route spacing that still gives 100% area coverage, and comment on whether the proposed spacing meets this criterion. [7 Marks]**

**(b) If each route is 8 km long and the city is divided into 4 parallel corridors, calculate the total catchment area and the network density. [7 Marks]**

---

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Step 1.** For 100% coverage on a grid, the perpendicular catchment bands of adjacent routes must just touch:

$$S_r^{max} = 2 \, r_w$$

**Step 2.** Substitute $r_w = 0.4$ km:

$$S_r^{max} = 2 \times 0.4 = 0.8 \text{ km}$$

**Step 3.** The proposed spacing is 1.2 km, which is greater than 0.8 km.

**Conclusion (a):** The proposed spacing **fails** the 100% coverage criterion. There will be a "transit desert" of width $(1.2 - 0.8) = 0.4$ km between every pair of adjacent routes where no resident is within 400 m walking distance of any route. The planner must either reduce $S_r$ to ≤ 0.8 km or add feeder routes.

---

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Step 1.** Total route length = number of corridors × length per corridor:

$$\sum L_{route} = 4 \times 8 = 32 \text{ km}$$

**Step 2.** Catchment area of one 8 km route:

$$A_c^{one} = 2 \, r_w \, L = 2 \times 0.4 \times 8 = 6.4 \text{ km}^2$$

**Step 3.** Total catchment area (assuming no overlap between corridors):

$$A_c^{total} = 4 \times 6.4 = 25.6 \text{ km}^2$$

**Step 4.** Network density:

$$\delta = \frac{\sum L_{route}}{A_{city}} = \frac{32}{50} = 0.64 \text{ km/km}^2$$

**Conclusion (b):** The total catchment is **25.6 km²**, which covers 51.2% of the 50 km² city. Network density is **0.64 km/km²**, which is in the typical range for medium-density Indian cities (0.5 – 1.0 km/km²).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in Spatial Distribution Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the factor of 2** in the corridor catchment formula. Students frequently write $A_c = r_w L$ instead of $A_c = 2 r_w L$, halving the catchment and losing 1–2 marks immediately.
>
> 2. **Confusing stop spacing with route spacing.** These are perpendicular concepts — stop spacing is *along* a route, route spacing is *between* parallel routes. Examiners specifically test this distinction.
>
> 3. **Using the wrong walk-access radius.** Always check whether the question specifies bus (use 0.4 km) or rail (use 0.8 km). Defaulting to one without justification is penalized.
>
> 4. **Ignoring units.** Marks are lost when students write $A_c = 2 \times 0.4 \times 12 = 9.6$ without stating "**9.6 km²**" explicitly.
>
> 5. **Skipping the verbal conclusion.** KTU examiners award 1–2 marks for a clear concluding statement such as "Therefore, the corridor is viable / not viable / the spacing is insufficient." A numerical answer without a conclusion is incomplete.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Spatial Distribution**

- ✅ **Walk-access radius** is the foundational constant: **400 m for bus, 800 m for rail**.
- ✅ **Stop spacing** balances *access* (closer = better) vs. *speed* (farther = better). Urban bus: 400–600 m. BRT: 800–1000 m. Metro: 1000–1200 m.
- ✅ **Route spacing** must satisfy $S_r \leq 2 r_w$ for 100% coverage. Wider spacing creates transit deserts.
- ✅ **Service area of one stop** (circle): $A_c = \pi r_w^{\,2} \approx 0.50$ km² for a bus stop.
- ✅ **Service area of a route corridor** (rectangle): $A_c = 2 r_w L$.
- ✅ **Population served:** $P_c = \rho \cdot A_c$.
- ✅ **Network density:** $\delta = \sum L_{route} / A_{city}$ — benchmark 0.5–1.5 km/km² for Indian cities.
- ✅ **Break-even ridership** per route-km: $P_{BE} = C_{op} / f$.
- ✅ **Critical density** for transit viability: $\rho_{crit} = C_{op} / (f \cdot 2 r_w)$.
- ✅ **Route directness** (circuity): $R_d = d_{transit} / d_{crow-fly}$. Values close to 1.0 indicate well-aligned routes.
- ✅ **Spatial distribution is the prerequisite** for frequency and schedule planning — Modules 2 and 3 cannot be attempted without a sound spatial plan.
- ✅ **Equity consideration:** Spatial distribution maps identify underserved areas (transit deserts) for targeted investment.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Temporal Variations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET833 - PUBLIC TRANSPORTATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Temporal Variations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Public Transportation Systems (PTS)**, **Temporal Variations** refer to the systematic, recurring fluctuations in transit ridership, passenger demand, vehicle loading, and service utilization that occur across multiple time scales — ranging from within an hour (sub-hourly), through daily and weekly cycles, to seasonal and annual trends. These variations are critical inputs for **transit planning, scheduling, fleet sizing, and capacity design**, because transit demand is highly **non-uniform** and **time-dependent**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 1):**
> "Temporal variations describe how transit demand and supply characteristics change with time of day, day of week, and season of the year. They form the basis for determining peak vs. off-peak service levels, design hour, and frequency setting."

## 1.2 Key Categories of Temporal Variation

| S.No | Variation Type | Time Scale | Key Driver |
|------|---------------|-----------|------------|
| 1 | Sub-hourly / Within-hour | 5–15 minutes | Signal cycles, micro-events |
| 2 | Hourly / Daily | 1–24 hours | Peak commute, work shifts |
| 3 | Day-of-week | 7 days | Weekday vs Weekend |
| 4 | Monthly / Seasonal | 1–12 months | Tourism, weather, academic calendar |
| 5 | Annual / Long-term | Years | Urbanization, demographic shifts |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Reservoir Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy: "The Breathing City"**
> Imagine a city as a giant organism that *breathes*. During morning hours, millions of "oxygen molecules" (people) rush from residential zones to commercial zones — this is the **morning peak**. By evening, the flow reverses — the **evening peak**. On weekends, the rhythm slows down and shifts towards leisure destinations. On a rainy day or festival day, the pattern distorts further. *Temporal Variations* are simply the measured heartbeat of this breathing city. As a transit planner, your job is to ensure the "lungs" (buses, trains, metros) can expand capacity during inhalation (peaks) and contract efficiently during exhalation (off-peak), without suffocating the city or wasting fuel.

## 1.4 Standard Metrics in Transit Temporal Analysis

- **Peak Hour Factor (PHF)** — dimensionless, ranges 0.25 to 1.00
- **Design Hour Factor (DHF)** — dimensionless, typically 8–15%
- **Load Factor (LF)** — ratio, typically 0.6–1.2
- **Frequency** — vehicles per hour (vph)
- **Headway** — minutes (min)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this topic matters for KTU Board Exam:** Temporal variation analysis directly drives the calculation of fleet size, frequency of service, and the number of buses/trains required during peak hours. Almost every KTU question on transit planning or scheduling begins with a temporal variation assumption.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework

### 2.1.1 Within-Hour (Sub-Hourly) Variation

Passenger demand is not constant even within a single hour. The **highest 15-minute flow** within a peak hour is used to size capacity, because if the system is designed only for the full-hour average, it will become **overcrowded** during the surge minute.

### 2.1.2 Hour-of-Day Variation

Demand is typically plotted as a **daily ridership profile**, showing:
- **AM Peak Hour** (e.g., 8:00–9:00 AM) — home-to-work trips
- **PM Peak Hour** (e.g., 5:30–6:30 PM) — work-to-home trips
- **Off-Peak Midday** — shopping, school, medical trips
- **Evening/Night** — entertainment, return trips

### 2.1.3 Day-of-Week Variation

Weekday patterns differ from weekends:
- **Weekdays** dominated by mandatory (work/school) trips
- **Weekends** dominated by discretionary (shopping, leisure) trips
- **Holidays** may show 30–60% reduction in ridership

### 2.1.4 Seasonal Variation

- **Summer**: Tourist peaks, school vacation effect
- **Monsoon** (Kerala context): Ridership may *increase* on buses due to private vehicle avoidance
- **Festival periods** (Onam, Vishu): Spikes in intercity demand

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **cheat sheet** for solving any KTU numerical problem on temporal variations. Memorize the variable definitions and units.

| # | Formula / Metric | Expression | Variables | Units | Typical Range |
|---|------------------|------------|-----------|-------|---------------|
| 1 | **Peak Hour Factor (PHF)** | $\text{PHF} = \dfrac{V_{60}}{4 \cdot V_{15}}$ | $V_{60}$ = peak hour volume; $V_{15}$ = peak 15-min volume | dimensionless | 0.25 – 1.00 |
| 2 | **Design Hour Factor (DHF)** | $\text{DHF} = \dfrac{V_{\text{design hour}}}{V_{\text{annual avg hour}}}$ | $V$ = hourly volume | dimensionless | 0.08 – 0.15 |
| 3 | **30th Highest Hourly Volume** | Design hour = 30th highest hour of the year | — | pax/hr | — |
| 4 | **Hourly Variation Factor** | $HVF = \dfrac{V_i}{V_{\text{daily}}} \times 100$ | $V_i$ = hour i volume | % | 3% – 20% |
| 5 | **Daily Variation Factor** | $DVF = \dfrac{V_{\text{weekday avg}}}{V_{\text{7-day avg}}}$ | $V$ = daily volume | dimensionless | 1.0 – 1.5 |
| 6 | **Monthly / Seasonal Index** | $SI_j = \dfrac{\bar{V}_j}{\bar{V}_{\text{annual}}} \times 100$ | $\bar{V}$ = average volume | dimensionless | 0.5 – 2.0 |
| 7 | **Load Factor** | $LF = \dfrac{\text{Passengers carried}}{\text{Seating capacity} \times \text{Trips}}$ | — | dimensionless | 0.6 – 1.2 |
| 8 | **Required Frequency** | $f = \dfrac{P_{\text{peak}}}{C \cdot LF_{\text{design}}}$ | $P_{\text{peak}}$ = peak passenger demand; $C$ = vehicle capacity | vehicles/hr | — |
| 9 | **Headway** | $h = \dfrac{60}{f}$ | $f$ = frequency | minutes | 2 – 60 |
| 10 | **Annual Passenger Miles** | $\text{APM} = \sum (V_d \cdot L_d)$ | $V_d$ = daily volume; $L_d$ = average trip length | passenger-miles | — |

## 2.3 The 'Why' Behind Temporal Variation Analysis

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Rationale:**
> 1. **Right-sizing the fleet** — A system designed for peak hour cannot be financially sustainable if the same capacity is maintained 24 hours.
> 2. **Driver shift allocation** — Peak-hour demands more vehicles, hence more drivers.
> 3. **Energy and cost efficiency** — Off-peak services can be down-sized, saving fuel and operational cost.
> 4. **Service equity** — Temporal analysis ensures that off-peak riders (elderly, students) are not neglected.
> 5. **KTU Board Context:** Any 14-mark question on "design a bus transit system for a city" or "compute fleet size" requires this topic as the **first step**.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility

Temporal variation data feeds directly into:
- **AVL (Automatic Vehicle Location)** dispatch algorithms
- **Adaptive signal priority** for transit
- **Dynamic pricing** in metro systems
- **Platform length design** in railways
- **Headway-based timetable generation** in BRT corridors

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Peak Hour Factor (PHF) — Physical Meaning

The **Peak Hour Factor** quantifies how "peaked" the demand is *within* a single hour. If passengers arrived uniformly, PHF would equal **1.0**. The more bursty the demand, the lower the PHF.

### Derivation Walkthrough

Let the peak hour be divided into four consecutive 15-minute periods with volumes $V_1, V_2, V_3, V_4$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Peak Hour Volume: } V_{60} &= V_1 + V_2 + V_3 + V_4 \\[6pt]
\text{If uniform, each 15-min period would carry: } V_{15,\text{uniform}} &= \dfrac{V_{60}}{4} \\[6pt]
\text{But the actual peak 15-min period carries: } V_{15,\max} &= \max(V_1, V_2, V_3, V_4) \\[6pt]
\text{Since } V_{15,\max} \geq V_{15,\text{uniform}}, \text{ the system must be sized for the surge.} \\[6pt]
\text{Peak Hour Factor: } \text{PHF} &= \dfrac{V_{60}}{4 \cdot V_{15,\max}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logic of conversion:** We multiply the peak 15-min flow by 4 to project it to a hypothetical full hour, then compare with the actual hour volume. A low PHF means the surge is sharp.

### Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Worked Problem 1:**
> A bus route shows the following 15-minute boarding counts during the morning peak: 120, 180, 150, 90. Compute the PHF.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{60} &= 120 + 180 + 150 + 90 = 540 \text{ passengers} \\[6pt]
V_{15,\max} &= \max(120, 180, 150, 90) = 180 \text{ passengers} \\[6pt]
\text{PHF} &= \dfrac{540}{4 \times 180} = \dfrac{540}{720} = 0.75
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** PHF = 0.75 indicates moderate peaking. The system experiences 25% higher instantaneous demand than the hourly average.

## 3.2 Derivation: Required Fleet Frequency from Peak Demand

### Logical Steps

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Identify peak demand per hour from temporal data: } P_{\text{peak}} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 2: Choose design load factor (typically 0.85–1.0): } LF_d \\[6pt]
\text{Step 3: Determine vehicle capacity: } C \text{ (e.g., 50 for a standard bus)} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 4: Compute passengers per vehicle-hour: } Q_v = C \cdot LF_d \\[6pt]
\text{Step 5: Required frequency: } f = \dfrac{P_{\text{peak}}}{Q_v} = \dfrac{P_{\text{peak}}}{C \cdot LF_d} \\[6pt]
\text{Step 6: Headway: } h = \dfrac{60}{f} \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Numerical Example (KTU Style)

> **Worked Problem 2:**
> A route carries 1,800 passengers in the AM peak hour. Buses have 50 seats. Design load factor = 0.9. Find frequency and headway.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_v &= 50 \times 0.9 = 45 \text{ passengers per bus} \\[6pt]
f &= \dfrac{1800}{45} = 40 \text{ buses per hour} \\[6pt]
h &= \dfrac{60}{40} = 1.5 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** One bus every 90 seconds is required during the AM peak.

## 3.3 Derivation: Seasonal / Monthly Index

To compare ridership across seasons fairly, transit planners use a **Seasonal Index (SI)**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{V}_j &= \dfrac{1}{n_j} \sum_{i=1}^{n_j} V_{ij} \quad \text{(average ridership in month/season } j\text{)} \\[6pt]
\bar{V}_{\text{annual}} &= \dfrac{1}{12} \sum_{j=1}^{12} \bar{V}_j \quad \text{(annual average)} \\[6pt]
SI_j &= \dfrac{\bar{V}_j}{\bar{V}_{\text{annual}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

An index of **1.20** means the month carries 20% more demand than the yearly average.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Temporal Variation Indicators & Planning Actions

| Indicator | Low Value Means | High Value Means | Planning Action |
|-----------|----------------|------------------|-----------------|
| **PHF** | Demand bursty within hour | Demand spread evenly | Low PHF → larger reserve capacity needed |
| **DHF** | Year-round uniform demand | Strong seasonal peaks | High DHF → more peak-only services |
| **Hourly Index** | Low peak dominance | Sharp peaks | High index → express / skip-stop services |
| **Daily Index (weekday/weekend)** | Similar pattern | Strong weekend shift | Adjust fleet mix by day |
| **Seasonal Index** | Stable year-round | Tourist / academic peaks | Deploy extra seasonal services |
| **Load Factor** | Underutilized | Overcrowded | Target LF ≈ 0.85 for comfort |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow: Daily Temporal Variation Cycle of Urban Transit
