<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define Peak Hour Factor (PHF) and explain its significance in public transport planning.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Peak Hour Factor (PHF)** is the ratio of the total volume observed during the peak hour to four times the volume observed during the peak 15-minute period within that hour.

$$
\text{PHF} = \dfrac{V_{60}}{4 \cdot V_{15,\max}}
$$

**Significance (2 marks):**
1. It quantifies the **burstiness of demand** within the peak hour.
2. A low PHF (say 0.5) means demand is highly concentrated in a short surge, requiring **larger reserve capacity** to avoid overcrowding.
3. It directly influences **fleet sizing and frequency setting** for the design period.

---

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**List any four categories of temporal variations affecting transit ridership.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. **Within-hour / sub-hourly variation** — fluctuation within a 60-min period (5-min, 15-min flows).
2. **Hour-of-day / daily variation** — AM peak, PM peak, off-peak, night.
3. **Day-of-week variation** — weekday (commuter dominant) vs. weekend (leisure dominant).
4. **Seasonal / monthly variation** — tourism, monsoon, festivals, academic calendar.
*(For full 3 marks, student must state the driver for each type.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Internal Choice

> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, divided into (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Internal choice means the student answers EITHER the OR option within the SAME question number.

---

### Question B1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]` — 14 Marks

**(a)** Explain the different categories of temporal variations observed in public transport demand. Discuss how each category influences the planning of bus frequency and fleet size. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** During the morning peak hour on a city bus route, the boarding counts in four consecutive 15-minute intervals are 140, 220, 190, and 110. The bus capacity is 45 seats and the design load factor is 0.85. Compute the **Peak Hour Factor**, the **required frequency (buses/hr)**, and the **headway (minutes)**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for B1(a)

The categories of temporal variation in public transport are:

1. **Sub-hourly (within-hour) variation** — Demand fluctuates within the peak hour. Measured by PHF. A low PHF means that during a 15-minute surge, the bus may be overcrowded even if the average hour load is acceptable. **Planning action:** Reserve capacity is added to handle surges.

2. **Hour-of-day / Daily variation** — Demand has a clear morning peak and evening peak separated by a midday off-peak trough. **Planning action:** Higher frequency during peaks; lower frequency or larger headways during off-peak; skeleton services at night.

3. **Day-of-week variation** — Weekday demand is dominated by work and education trips, while weekends shift to leisure and shopping. **Planning action:** Adjust fleet allocation by day; redeploy buses for weekend leisure corridors.

4. **Seasonal / Monthly variation** — Demand rises during tourist season, festivals, and academic reopenings, and falls during off-season. **Planning action:** Deploy extra seasonal services; use **Seasonal Index (SI)** for budget allocation.

5. **Annual / Long-term variation** — Demand grows with population, urbanization, and modal shift. **Planning action:** Use trend analysis to plan **5–10 year fleet expansion**.

Each category influences fleet size by determining the **peak fleet requirement** and influences frequency by setting the **peak vs. off-peak service plan**.

#### Model Solution for B1(b)

**Given data:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_1 &= 140, \quad V_2 = 220, \quad V_3 = 190, \quad V_4 = 110 \\[6pt]
C &= 45 \text{ seats}, \quad LF_d = 0.85
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1: Peak hour volume**

$$
V_{60} = 140 + 220 + 190 + 110 = 660 \text{ passengers}
$$

**Step 2: Peak 15-minute volume**

$$
V_{15,\max} = \max(140, 220, 190, 110) = 220
$$

**Step 3: Peak Hour Factor**

$$
\text{PHF} = \dfrac{660}{4 \times 220} = \dfrac{660}{880} = 0.75
$$

**Step 4: Effective passenger capacity per bus**

$$
Q_v = C \cdot LF_d = 45 \times 0.85 = 38.25 \text{ passengers per bus}
$$

**Step 5: Required frequency**

$$
f = \dfrac{V_{60}}{Q_v} = \dfrac{660}{38.25} = 17.25 \approx 18 \text{ buses per hour}
$$

**Step 6: Headway**

$$
h = \dfrac{60}{f} = \dfrac{60}{18} = 3.33 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Final Answer (Box-style summary):**

- **PHF = 0.75**
- **Frequency ≈ 18 buses/hr**
- **Headway ≈ 3.33 min**

---

### Question B1. OR — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]` — 14 Marks

**(a)** Define **Design Hour Factor (DHF)** and explain the concept of the **30th highest hourly volume** as a design standard. Why is the 30th hour chosen and not the 1st (absolute peak)? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A transit system records the following **monthly passenger volumes** for one year (in thousands): Jan 480, Feb 460, Mar 500, Apr 520, May 540, Jun 560, Jul 590, Aug 600, Sep 550, Oct 510, Nov 490, Dec 520. Compute the **Seasonal Index** for each month and identify the **peak season**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for B1(a) — OR

**Definition:** The **Design Hour Factor (DHF)** is the ratio of the design hourly volume to the annual average hourly volume.

$$
\text{DHF} = \dfrac{V_{\text{design hour}}}{V_{\text{annual avg hour}}}
$$

**The 30th Highest Hourly Volume (Standard Design Hour):**
- The 30th highest hour of the year means there are only **29 hours** in the year with ridership higher than this design hour.
- The 30th hour typically corresponds to the **AM peak** of an **average weekday** during a **non-extreme month**.

**Why 30th hour, not 1st?**

1. The **1st highest hour** (absolute peak) often occurs during a unique event (festival, sports match, accident) and is not **representative** of normal operating conditions.
2. Designing for the absolute peak would make the system **economically unviable** because the surplus capacity would lie idle for 99.99% of the year.
3. The 30th hour strikes a balance: it is high enough to capture **recurring commuter peaks**, yet low enough to ensure **cost-effective** service design.
4. This is the standard adopted by the **Highway Capacity Manual (HCM)** and by **KTU-recommended transit design codes**.

#### Model Solution for B1(b) — OR

**Step 1: Annual total volume (sum)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
V_{\text{annual}} &= 480+460+500+520+540+560+590+600+550+510+490+520 \\[4pt]
&= 6320 \text{ thousand passengers}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Annual average monthly volume**

$$
\bar{V}_{\text{annual}} = \dfrac{6320}{12} = 526.67 \text{ thousand passengers}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Seasonal Index for each month**

$$
SI_j = \dfrac{\bar{V}_j}{\bar{V}_{\text{annual}}}
$$

| Month | Volume (000) | Seasonal Index |
|-------|-------------|----------------|
| Jan | 480 | 0.91 |
| Feb | 460 | 0.87 |
| Mar | 500 | 0.95 |
| Apr | 520 | 0.99 |
| May | 540 | 1.03 |
| Jun | 560 | 1.06 |
| Jul | 590 | 1.12 |
| Aug | 600 | 1.14 |
| Sep | 550 | 1.04 |
| Oct | 510 | 0.97 |
| Nov | 490 | 0.93 |
| Dec | 520 | 0.99 |

**Step 4: Identify Peak Season**

The **peak season is July–August** (likely a tourist + academic reopening window), with the highest Seasonal Index of **1.14 in August**.

**Engineering Recommendation:** Augment fleet by ~14% during July–August to handle the surge; offer discounted off-season passes in February to balance load.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes where KTU students lose marks on Temporal Variation questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply by 4 in PHF:** A student often writes PHF = V₆₀ / V₁₅. The correct formula is V₆₀ / (4 × V₁₅). Losing this 4 → lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Using wrong denominator for frequency:** Some students divide by seating capacity instead of (capacity × load factor). This overestimates frequency and inflates fleet size — wrong answer.
> 3. **Not stating units in headway:** "Headway = 3.33" without "minutes" → 0.5 mark cut.
> 4. **Confusing Design Hour with Peak Hour:** The design hour is a **planning** concept; the peak hour is the **observed** maximum. They are often the same hour in small cities, but conceptually different.
> 5. **Ignoring directionality:** A peak hour may have a dominant direction (inbound AM, outbound PM). Always state directional split when relevant.
> 6. **In Seasonal Index, forgetting the annual average step:** Students sometimes compute SI for one month and stop. The KTU board expects all 12 months or all 4 quarters.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Temporal variations** are time-based fluctuations in transit demand at sub-hourly, hourly, daily, weekly, seasonal, and annual scales.
- [ ] **Peak Hour Factor (PHF) = V₆₀ / (4 × V₁₅)** — measures within-hour peaking; range 0.25–1.00.
- [ ] **Lower PHF → sharper surge → larger reserve capacity required.**
- [ ] **Design Hour Factor (DHF) = Design hourly volume / Annual average hourly volume.**
- [ ] **30th highest hourly volume** is the standard design hour (not the 1st, not the average).
- [ ] **Frequency f = Peak demand / (Vehicle capacity × Design load factor).**
- [ ] **Headway h = 60 / f (minutes).**
- [ ] **Daily variation** distinguishes AM peak, PM peak, midday off-peak, and night.
- [ ] **Weekly variation:** weekday = commute-dominant; weekend = leisure-dominant.
- [ ] **Seasonal Index SI = Monthly average / Annual average** — flags peak/off-peak months.
- [ ] **Kerala-specific note:** Monsoon often *increases* bus ridership as commuters shift from two-wheelers; Onam/Vishu create intercity spikes.
- [ ] **Load Factor (LF) = Actual load / Seating capacity** — KTU targets LF ≈ 0.85 for comfort.
- [ ] **All temporal variation analyses must precede fleet sizing, frequency setting, and timetable generation** in any KTU transit design problem.
- [ ] **Always state units** (pax/hr, buses/hr, min) in numerical answers.
- [ ] **Always show the formula substitution step** — partial marks are awarded for correct setup even if the final answer slips.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Passenger Volume Analysis and Service Capacity Determination
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET833 - PUBLIC TRANSPORTATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Passenger Volume Analysis and Service Capacity Determination

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Passenger Volume Analysis (PVA)

**Passenger Volume Analysis (PVA)** is the systematic process of measuring, recording, and interpreting the number of passengers using a public transit system across a defined spatial (stop, section, route) and temporal (peak hour, daily, annual) framework. It is the *demand-side metric* of public transportation, quantifying how many people are actually being moved versus how many *could* be moved.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with the **Transit Capacity and Quality of Service Manual (TCQSM – TCRP Report 100)** and the **Indian Urban Transport Institute (IUT) guidelines**, Passenger Volume Analysis is expressed using three primary metrics:

- **Boarding and Alighting Counts (B&A Counts)**: Number of passengers getting on/off at each stop.
- **Link (Section) Volume**: Number of passengers travelling *through* a given section of the route.
- **Maximum Load Point (MLP) / Maximum Load Section (MLS)**: The single section carrying the highest number of passengers — the *bottleneck* of the line.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Passenger Volume Studies (PVS) form the empirical foundation for *every* subsequent planning decision — route design, scheduling, fleet sizing, and fare policy. A board examiner expects you to treat PVA as the "ground-truth data layer" of public transport planning.

## 1.2 Service Capacity Determination (SCD)

**Service Capacity** is the *supply-side counterpart* to passenger volume — it defines the **maximum number of passengers that a transit line, route, vehicle, or stop can carry per unit time under specified operating conditions**, without degrading service quality below an acceptable threshold.

The hierarchy of capacity in public transportation (from smallest to largest unit) is:

1. **Vehicle Capacity (V)** — passengers per vehicle.
2. **Stop/Station Capacity (S)** — passengers per stop per hour.
3. **Line/Route Capacity (C_line)** — passengers per hour per direction (**pphpd**).
4. **System/Network Capacity** — passengers per day across the network.

The universally accepted unit in the KTU 2024 syllabus is **pphpd (passengers per hour per direction)**.

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The "Water Pipe" Model

Imagine a **municipal water pipeline** running from a reservoir to a city:

- The **water flowing through the pipe right now** = Passenger Volume (what is *actually* being carried).
- The **maximum flow the pipe can sustain without bursting** = Service Capacity (what the system *can* carry).
- The **valve that opens/closes to control flow** = Headway (frequency of service).
- The **diameter of the pipe** = Vehicle Size / Capacity.
- A **pressure gauge at the narrowest point** = Maximum Load Point measurement.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core KTU Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> *"Passenger Volume Analysis quantifies the actual ridership load on a transit facility, while Service Capacity Determination establishes the theoretical and practical upper limit of passengers that the facility can transport under prevailing service standards."*

## 1.4 Why This Topic is Critical for KTU Examinations

| Aspect | Passenger Volume Analysis | Service Capacity Determination |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Empirical / Data-driven | Analytical / Engineering-based |
| **Output** | Actual ridership numbers | Maximum throughput (pphpd) |
| **Use Case** | Identify overcrowding, peak demand | Size fleet, set headway, plan extensions |
| **Standard Reference** | TCQSM Chapter 4 | TCQSM Chapters 5–7, HCM Transit |
| **KTU Exam Frequency** | High (2-mark and 14-mark questions) | Very High (numerical 14-mark questions) |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Passenger Volume Study (PVS) — Step-by-Step Methodology

The PVS procedure, as prescribed in KTU 2024 Module 1, follows a six-step protocol:

1. **Define Study Scope**: Route, direction, time window (e.g., morning peak 7:00–10:00 AM).
2. **Identify Critical Stops**: Major boarding/alighting hubs (bus depots, metro stations, interchanges).
3. **Conduct Field Counts**: Manual tally counters or Automatic Passenger Counters (APCs) record boardings (B_i) and alightings (A_i) at each stop *i*.
4. **Compute Section Volumes (V_s)**: For section between stop *i* and stop *i+1*, the volume is:
   $$V_{i,i+1} = V_{i-1,i} + B_i - A_i$$
5. **Identify Maximum Load Point (MLP)**: The section with the maximum V_s.
6. **Compute Load Factor (LF)**: Ratio of actual passengers to available capacity.

## 2.2 Service Capacity — Theoretical Framework

Service capacity of a transit line is governed by **three sequential constraints**, and the **effective capacity is the minimum of the three**:

### 2.2.1 Vehicle Capacity (C_v)

The maximum number of passengers a single vehicle can legally and safely carry, including seated and standing.

$$C_v = N_{seats} + (A_{standing} \times \rho_{standing})$$

Where:
- $N_{seats}$ = Number of seats (typically 40–60 in a standard bus, 250+ in a metro coach).
- $A_{standing}$ = Standing area in square metres.
- $\rho_{standing}$ = Standing passenger density (passengers/m²).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard KTU Density Values:**
> - **Crush Load:** $\rho_{standing} = 6$ passengers/m² (absolute physical maximum).
> - **Schedule Load (Design):** $\rho_{standing} = 4$ passengers/m² (Indian practice for buses, **3** for metros).
> - **Comfortable Load:** $\rho_{standing} = 2$ to $2.5$ passengers/m².

### 2.2.2 Minimum Headway (h_min)

The smallest possible time interval between two successive vehicles that the **stop/station dwell time and signal/track clearance** can support.

$$h_{min} = t_d + t_c$$

Where:
- $t_d$ = Dwell time at the critical stop (seconds).
- $t_c$ = Clearance/recovery time (seconds, typically 10–15 s).

### 2.2.3 Dwell Time (t_d)

Dwell time is calculated using the **boarding-bottleneck principle**:

$$t_d = \max(t_b, t_a) + t_{open} + t_{close}$$

$$t_b = P_b \times p_b \quad ; \quad t_a = P_a \times p_a$$

Where:
- $P_b, P_a$ = Passengers boarding / alighting at the stop.
- $p_b, p_a$ = Service time per boarding / alighting passenger (typically 2–3 s for buses; 1.5 s for metro with wide doors).
- $t_{open}, t_{close}$ = Door open and close times.

### 2.2.4 Line Capacity (C_line)

$$C_{line} = \frac{3600 \times C_v \times n_v}{h_{min}}$$

For a single-unit vehicle, this simplifies to:

$$C_{line} = \frac{3600 \times C_v}{h_{min}} \quad \text{[pphpd]}$$

Where $n_v$ = number of vehicles per train consist (for rail).

## 2.3 Load Factor (LF) — The Key Performance Indicator

$$LF = \frac{\text{Actual Passenger Volume}}{\text{Service Capacity (Offered)}} = \frac{\sum V_{section}}{C_v \times N_{trips}}$$

In passenger-km form:

$$LF = \frac{\text{Passenger-km}}{\text{Vehicle-km} \times C_v}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Benchmark Load Factors:**
> - **Buses (Urban):** Optimal LF = 0.70 to 0.85; > 0.90 indicates severe overcrowding.
> - **Metro Rail:** Optimal LF = 0.65 to 0.80; > 0.90 is unacceptable.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $V_{i,i+1} = V_{i-1,i} + B_i - A_i$ | Section volume recursion | passengers |
| 2 | $C_v = N_{seats} + (A_{std} \times \rho_{std})$ | Vehicle capacity | passengers |
| 3 | $t_d = \max(P_b \cdot p_b,\; P_a \cdot p_a) + t_{oc}$ | Dwell time at stop | seconds |
| 4 | $h_{min} = t_d + t_c$ | Minimum headway | seconds |
| 5 | $C_{line} = \dfrac{3600 \cdot C_v}{h_{min}}$ | Line capacity (single unit) | pphpd |
| 6 | $C_{line} = \dfrac{3600 \cdot C_v \cdot n_v}{h_{min}}$ | Line capacity (train consist) | pphpd |
| 7 | $LF = \dfrac{\sum V_{section}}{C_v \cdot N_{trips}}$ | Load Factor (count-based) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $LF = \dfrac{P\text{-}km}{V\text{-}km \cdot C_v}$ | Load Factor (passenger-km) | dimensionless |
| 9 | $f_{max} = \dfrac{3600}{h_{min}}$ | Maximum service frequency | veh/h |
| 10 | $C_{stop} = \dfrac{3600 \cdot C_v}{h_{actual}}$ | Stop-level offered capacity | pphpd |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bhubaneswar BRT Planning (India)**: PVA revealed the MLS carries 9,200 pphpd during peak hour → design capacity set at 12,000 pphpd with 60-second headway and 80-passenger articulated buses.
- **Delhi Metro Phase IV**: Capacity bottleneck identified via MLP analysis led to procurement of higher-capacity 8-coach trains.
- **KSRTC City Bus (Thiruvananthapuram)**: Load factor monitoring triggers timetable revision when LF > 0.90 for three consecutive weekdays.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Line Capacity Formula (C_line)

We derive the line capacity starting from first principles.

**Step 1:** Consider a transit line with vehicles arriving at the **Maximum Load Section (MLS)** with a constant headway $h$ (in seconds). Let each vehicle have a capacity $C_v$.

**Step 2:** The number of vehicles passing the MLS in $T$ seconds is:
$$N_{veh} = \frac{T}{h}$$

**Step 3:** Each vehicle carries at most $C_v$ passengers, so the total passengers in time $T$ is:
$$N_{pax} = C_v \cdot N_{veh} = C_v \cdot \frac{T}{h}$$

**Step 4:** Converting to **per-hour** basis by setting $T = 3600$ s:
$$C_{line} = \frac{3600 \cdot C_v}{h} \quad \text{[pphpd]}$$

**Step 5:** Substituting the minimum headway $h_{min} = t_d + t_c$ gives the **maximum** line capacity:
$$C_{line}^{max} = \frac{3600 \cdot C_v}{t_d + t_c}$$

This proves that line capacity is **inversely proportional to headway** and **linearly proportional to vehicle size**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Dwell Time Formula

We derive the door-bottleneck dwell time model.

**Step 1:** Dwell time is the sum of:
- Time to open doors ($t_{open}$).
- Time for passenger exchange (boarding + alighting).
- Time to close doors ($t_{close}$).

$$t_d = t_{open} + t_{exchange} + t_{close}$$

**Step 2:** If $P_b$ passengers board and each requires $p_b$ seconds, total boarding time = $P_b \cdot p_b$.

**Step 3:** Similarly, alighting time = $P_a \cdot p_a$.

**Step 4:** If boarding and alighting occur **simultaneously through separate doors**, the exchange time is the maximum of the two:
$$t_{exchange} = \max(P_b \cdot p_b,\; P_a \cdot p_a)$$

**Step 5:** Combining:
$$t_d = t_{open} + t_{close} + \max(P_b \cdot p_b,\; P_a \cdot p_a)$$

If doors are pre-opened (e.g., metro), then $t_{open} + t_{close} \approx 0$ and:
$$t_d \approx \max(P_b \cdot p_b,\; P_a \cdot p_a)$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example — KTU Typical 14-Mark Numerical

**Problem Statement (Modelled on KTU Pattern):**
> *"An urban bus route in Kochi has the following data:*
> - *Seats per bus = 50*
> - *Standing area = 6 m², with $\rho_{standing} = 4$ pax/m² (schedule load).*
> - *Boarding passengers at the busiest stop = 40, Alighting = 25.*
> - *Service times: $p_b = 2.5$ s, $p_a = 2.0$ s, $t_{open} + t_{close} = 4$ s.*
> - *Clearance time $t_c = 12$ s.*
>
> *Compute:*
> *(a) Vehicle capacity, Dwell time, Minimum headway, and Maximum line capacity in pphpd.*
> *(b) If the peak-hour ridership survey on the maximum load section shows 5,800 pphpd, calculate the Load Factor and comment on the service quality."*

### Part (a) Solution

**Step A1 — Vehicle Capacity $C_v$:**
$$C_v = N_{seats} + (A_{std} \times \rho_{std})$$
$$C_v = 50 + (6 \times 4) = 50 + 24 = 74 \text{ passengers}$$

**Step A2 — Dwell Time $t_d$:**
$$t_b = P_b \times p_b = 40 \times 2.5 = 100 \text{ s}$$
$$t_a = P_a \times p_a = 25 \times 2.0 = 50 \text{ s}$$
$$t_d = t_{open+close} + \max(100, 50) = 4 + 100 = 104 \text{ s}$$

> [!NOTE]
> *In KTU valuation, examiners expect explicit unit mention and "boarding-bottleneck" justification — the larger of $t_b$ and $t_a$ governs.*

**Step A3 — Minimum Headway $h_{min}$:**
$$h_{min} = t_d + t_c = 104 + 12 = 116 \text{ seconds}$$

**Step A4 — Maximum Line Capacity $C_{line}^{max}$:**
$$C_{line}^{max} = \frac{3600 \times C_v}{h_{min}} = \frac{3600 \times 74}{116}$$
$$C_{line}^{max} = \frac{266{,}400}{116} = 2296.55 \approx 2297 \text{ pphpd}$$

### Part (b) Solution

**Step B1 — Load Factor Calculation:**
$$LF = \frac{\text{Actual Pax}}{\text{Capacity Offered}} = \frac{5800}{2297} = 2.525$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert:** A common student error is to treat LF > 1.0 as a "percentage overflow". LF > 1 means the demand *exceeds* the supply — the system is **physically incapable** of carrying all passengers at the design level. Always interpret qualitatively.

**Step B2 — Engineering Interpretation:**

- $LF = 2.525$ is **mathematically impossible** under the current design parameters — it indicates the route is severely capacity-deficient.
- The theoretical maximum that **can be offered** is 2,297 pphpd, but 5,800 pphpd are demanding service.

**Step B3 — Recommendations (KTU 14-mark sub-part):**

To restore $LF \leq 0.90$, the operator must either:
1. **Reduce headway** (add more buses) — needs $h \leq \frac{3600 \times 74}{5800/0.9} = 41.3$ s.
2. **Increase vehicle capacity** — use articulated buses ($C_v = 120$).
3. **Switch to BRT / Metro corridor** — the demand justifies a mass-transit upgrade.

**Step B4 — Required Capacity at LF = 0.85:**
$$C_{required} = \frac{5800}{0.85} = 6824 \text{ pphpd}$$

This exceeds the bus mode's practical limit (~2,500 pphpd for urban buses), confirming the need for a metro/BRT intervention.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Passenger Volume Study (PVS) Methodology

This diagram maps the complete workflow of a Passenger Volume Study as prescribed in the KTU 2024 Module 1 syllabus.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Step 1: Define Study Scope - Route, Direction, Time Window] --> A2[Step 2: Identify Critical Stops - Major Hubs and Interchanges]
    A2 --> A3[Step 3: Field Data Collection - Manual Counts or APC Sensors]
    A3 --> A4[Step 4: Tabulate Boardings Bi and Alightings Ai at Each Stop]
    A4 --> A5[Step 5: Compute Section Volume Vi using Recursion Formula]
    A5 --> A6[Step 6: Identify Maximum Load Section MLS]
    A6 --> A7[Step 7: Compute Load Factor LF and Peak Hour Factor]
    A7 --> A8[Output: PVA Report with Ridership Profile and MLP Map]
    A1 -.uses.-> M1[KTU Module 1 - TCQSM Methodology]
    A5 -.uses.-> M1
    A7 -.uses.-> M1
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Service Capacity Hierarchy and Constraint Chain

This block architecture shows the **three sequential constraints** that govern transit line capacity, and how the **effective capacity** is the minimum of the three.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[Constraint 1: Vehicle Capacity Cv] --> C1[Bus / Coach / Train Unit Specification]
    B2[Constraint 2: Dwell Time td] --> C2[Stop / Station Dwell Analysis]
    B3[Constraint 3: Clearance Time tc] --> C3[Signal and Track Recovery Time]
    C1 --> D1[Effective Capacity equals Minimum of the Three]
    C2 --> D1
    C3 --> D1
    D1 --> E1[Output: Line Capacity in pphpd]
    E1 --> F1[Compare with Demand from PVA]
    F1 --> G1{Load Factor LF within 0.7 to 0.85?}
    G1 -- Yes --> H1[Service is Adequate]
    G1 -- No --> H2[Action: Reduce Headway or Upgrade Mode]
    B1 -.depends on.-> S1[Seating and Standing Density Standards]
    B2 -.depends on.-> S2[Passenger Boarding Alighting Volume]
    B3 -.depends on.-> S3[Signaling and Safety Regulations]
```

## 4.3 Process Flow — From Volume Survey to Capacity Decision

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[PVA Data: Boardings, Alightings, Section Volumes] --> P2[Compute Maximum Load Point MLP]
    P2 --> P3[Compute Peak Hour Demand in pphpd]
    P3 --> P4[Compute Vehicle Capacity Cv]
    P4 --> P5[Compute Dwell Time td at Critical Stop]
    P5 --> P6[Compute Minimum Headway hmin]
    P6 --> P7[Compute Maximum Line Capacity Cline max]
    P3 --> P8[Compare Demand vs Capacity]
    P7 --> P8
    P8 --> P9{Is Demand less than or equal to Capacity?}
    P9 -- Yes --> P10[System is Adequate at Current Configuration]
    P9 -- No --> P11[Compute Capacity Deficit]
    P11 --> P12[Recommend Fleet Augmentation or Mode Shift]
    P12 --> P13[Re-design Headway and Vehicle Type]
    P13 --> P5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The Mermaid blocks intentionally avoid physical drawings (which are unreliable in ASCII/text rendering) and instead present a **functional architecture view** — exactly what KTU examiners reward in design-type 14-mark questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Q1]`

**Define Passenger Volume Analysis. List any four metrics used in PVA with one-line descriptions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Passenger Volume Analysis (PVA)** is the systematic study of the number of passengers using a public transit system over a specified route, time, and direction. It quantifies the *actual* ridership load on a transit facility and is the empirical foundation for capacity planning.

**Four Metrics Used in PVA:**
1. **Boardings (B_i):** Number of passengers entering the transit vehicle at stop *i*. (0.75 marks)
2. **Alightings (A_i):** Number of passengers leaving the transit vehicle at stop *i*. (0.75 marks)
3. **Section/Link Volume (V_s):** Number of passengers travelling through a given section of the route, computed recursively from boardings and alightings. (0.75 marks)
4. **Maximum Load Point (MLP):** The single section of the route that carries the highest number of passengers during the analysis period. (0.75 marks)

---

### Question 2 — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Q2]`

**Distinguish between 'Crush Load' and 'Schedule Load' in the context of transit vehicle capacity. State the standard passenger density values used for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Crush Load | Schedule Load |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Absolute physical maximum number of passengers a vehicle can carry, with extreme overcrowding. | Normal maximum number of passengers a vehicle is designed to carry under regular service conditions. |
| **Standing Density** | $\rho_{std} = 6$ passengers/m² (0.75 marks) | $\rho_{std} = 4$ pax/m² for buses; 3 pax/m² for metro (1.5 marks) |
| **Service Quality** | Highly uncomfortable, unsafe, used only as upper limit. (0.5 marks) | Comfortable, safe, used for daily fleet planning. (0.25 marks) |

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Two questions of 14 marks each, with internal choice (Q3 OR Q4, Q5 OR Q6). Each 14-mark question has two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Q3]`

**An urban bus corridor in Trivandrum has the following operating data:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Seating capacity per bus | 45 |
| Standing area | 5.5 m² |
| Schedule load standing density | 4 pax/m² |
| Service time per boarding passenger | 2.5 s |
| Service time per alighting passenger | 2.0 s |
| Door open + close time | 3 s |
| Clearance time at critical stop | 15 s |
| Boardings at critical stop | 35 |
| Alightings at critical stop | 20 |
| Peak hour demand at MLP | 4,500 pax/hour |

**(a)** Compute the **vehicle capacity, dwell time, minimum headway, and maximum line capacity** in pphpd. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compute the **load factor** at the MLP and recommend the appropriate **headway and fleet size** (assume one-way travel time = 90 minutes) to bring LF below 0.85. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part A (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Vehicle Capacity $C_v$:**
$$C_v = 45 + (5.5 \times 4) = 45 + 22 = 67 \text{ passengers} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 2 — Dwell Time $t_d$:**
$$t_b = 35 \times 2.5 = 87.5 \text{ s}$$
$$t_a = 20 \times 2.0 = 40 \text{ s}$$
$$t_d = 3 + \max(87.5, 40) = 3 + 87.5 = 90.5 \text{ s} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 3 — Minimum Headway $h_{min}$:**
$$h_{min} = 90.5 + 15 = 105.5 \text{ s} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum Line Capacity $C_{line}^{max}$:**
$$C_{line}^{max} = \frac{3600 \times 67}{105.5} = \frac{241{,}200}{105.5} \approx 2286.26 \text{ pphpd} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

---

#### Model Solution — Part A (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Load Factor:**
$$LF = \frac{4500}{2286.26} \approx 1.97$$

This indicates **severe capacity deficit** (LF >> 1.0). (1 mark)

**Step 2 — Required Capacity for LF = 0.85:**
$$C_{required} = \frac{4500}{0.85} \approx 5294 \text{ pphpd} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 3 — Required Headway:**
$$h_{req} = \frac{3600 \times 67}{5294} \approx 45.56 \text{ s} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 4 — Fleet Size for One-Way Travel Time of 90 minutes:**

Cycle time $T_c = 2 \times 90 = 180$ minutes.

$$N_{fleet} = \frac{T_c}{h_{req}} = \frac{180 \times 60}{45.56} \approx 237 \text{ buses} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the cycle time multiplier (×2)** for fleet size when travel is one-way.
> 2. **Not justifying the choice of LF = 0.85** — state it as the design standard.
> 3. **Mixing units** — headway must be in *seconds* in the formula but in *minutes* for fleet division.
> 4. **Skipping the qualitative comment** — examiners award the final 1 mark for stating "mode shift to BRT/Metro is recommended" when the required fleet exceeds 200 buses for a single corridor.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Q3 Alt]`

**(a)** Explain the **transit line capacity equation** with all variables. Discuss how the three parameters (vehicle capacity, dwell time, clearance time) interact to determine the effective capacity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A metro rail system uses 6-coach trains with each coach having 50 seats and 12 m² standing area. Standing density is 3 pax/m². Dwell time at the busiest station is 35 s and clearance time is 20 s. Compute the **maximum line capacity in pphpd**. If the peak-hour demand is 45,000 pphpd, evaluate whether the system is adequate. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Question B (a) [7 marks]

**Transit Line Capacity Equation:**
$$C_{line} = \frac{3600 \times C_v \times n_v}{t_d + t_c}$$

Where:
- $C_{line}$ = Maximum line capacity in **passengers per hour per direction (pphpd)**.
- $C_v$ = Passenger capacity of one vehicle.
- $n_v$ = Number of vehicles per train consist.
- $t_d$ = Dwell time at the critical station (seconds).
- $t_c$ = Track/signal clearance and recovery time (seconds).
- 3600 = Conversion factor from seconds to hours.

**Interaction of the Three Parameters (5 marks for explanation):**

1. **Vehicle Capacity ($C_v$):** Increases linearly with $C_v$. Larger coaches or higher-density standing (crush load) raise capacity, but at comfort cost. (1.5 marks)

2. **Dwell Time ($t_d$):** The *binding constraint* in urban rail. Each additional second of dwell reduces annual capacity by $\frac{C_v \times n_v}{t_d + t_c}$ pphpd per second added. Dwell time is governed by passenger exchange volume and platform-train interface design. (1.5 marks)

3. **Clearance Time ($t_c$):** Fixed by signalling system (typically 12–25 s). CBTC signalling systems reduce $t_c$ to ~8 s, dramatically increasing capacity. (1 mark)

**Conclusion:** The **effective capacity is the minimum** of these three sequential constraints. Improving one without the others (e.g., larger trains but longer dwells) yields no benefit — the **slowest link dominates**. (1 mark)

---

#### Model Solution — Question B (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Per-Coach Capacity:**
$$C_v = 50 + (12 \times 3) = 50 + 36 = 86 \text{ passengers} \quad \text{(1.5 marks)}$$

**Step 2 — Train Consist Capacity:**
$$C_{train} = 86 \times 6 = 516 \text{ passengers} \quad \text{(1 mark)}$$

**Step 3 — Headway:**
$$h_{min} = 35 + 20 = 55 \text{ s} \quad \text{(0.5 marks)}$$

**Step 4 — Maximum Line Capacity:**
$$C_{line}^{max} = \frac{3600 \times 516}{55} = \frac{1{,}857{,}600}{55} = 33{,}774.5 \text{ pphpd} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluation Against Demand:**
$$LF = \frac{45{,}000}{33{,}774.5} = 1.33$$

Since $LF > 1.0$, the system is **inadequate** — demand exceeds capacity by 33%. (1 mark)

**Step 6 — Recommendation:**
- Required capacity = $\frac{45{,}000}{0.85} = 52{,}941$ pphpd.
- Required headway = $\frac{3600 \times 516}{52{,}941} \approx 35.1$ s.
- **Practical action:** Upgrade signalling to CBTC (reduces $t_c$ to ~8 s) and add platform screen doors to reduce $t_d$. (1 mark)

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in This Topic (KTU 2024 Examiner Insights):**
> 1. **Forgetting the recursion step** in section volume — examiners test if you know $V_{i,i+1}$ depends on cumulative loading.
> 2. **Using 3600 inconsistently** — this is the *seconds-to-hours* factor; omitting it gives errors of 60×.
> 3. **Confusing $h_{min}$ (minimum possible headway) with $h_{actual}$ (operational headway)** — capacity is computed using $h_{min}$.
> 4. **Not interpreting LF qualitatively** — KTU awards the final 1–2 marks for engineering judgement, not just arithmetic.
> 5. **Mixing "capacity" with "ridership"** — capacity is *supply*, volume is *demand*. This is a frequent conceptual trap.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **PVA (Demand Side):** Boardings + Alightings → Section Volume → MLP → Load Factor.
- **SCD (Supply Side):** $C_v$ → $t_d$ → $h_{min}$ → $C_{line}$ in **pphpd**.
- **Three Constraints to Capacity:** Vehicle, Dwell, Clearance — effective capacity = **minimum of the three**.
- **Standard Densities:** Crush = 6 pax/m²; Schedule (Bus) = 4 pax/m²; Schedule (Metro) = 3 pax/m².
- **Golden Formula:** $C_{line} = \frac{3600 \cdot C_v}{h_{min}}$ (single unit) — memorize verbatim.
- **Section Volume Rule:** $V_{i,i+1} = V_{i-1,i} + B_i - A_i$ — apply cumulatively.
- **Load Factor Benchmarks:** Bus (0.70–0.85), Metro (0.65–0.80), **LF > 1.0 = failure mode**.
- **Dwell Time:** $t_d = \max(P_b \cdot p_b, P_a \cdot p_a) + t_{open+close}$ — **boarding-bottleneck rule**.
- **Fleet Size:** $N = \frac{\text{Cycle Time (min)}}{\text{Headway (min)}}$ — remember the **×2** for round-trip.
- **TCQSM Reference:** Always cite **TCRP Report 100** (TCQSM, 3rd Edition) for capacity methodology in KTU answers.
- **Key Indian Standards:** MoHUA **NUTP** (National Urban Transport Policy), **UTTIPEC** for Delhi, **CUMTA** for Kerala.
- **Practical Hierarchy:** Vehicle → Stop → Line → Network — capacity reduces (or bottlenecks) as you go up.
- **Quick LF Check:** If LF > 0.90 → "Severe Overcrowding"; LF = 0.70–0.85 → "Optimal"; LF < 0.40 → "Underutilised, revise schedule".
- **Unit Vigilance:** pphpd, veh/h, s (headway & dwell), m² (standing area) — examiners deduct for unit errors.
- **Engineering Judgement:** Always end 14-mark answers with a recommendation — fleet augmentation, mode shift, or signalling upgrade.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Introduction to Transit Service Planning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET833 - PUBLIC TRANSPORTATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Introduction to Transit Service Planning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transit Service Planning (TSP)** is the systematic, data-driven, and multi-stage engineering process of designing, evaluating, and optimizing public transportation networks and services to satisfy existing and future passenger mobility demands within an urban or regional context, while balancing **service effectiveness**, **operational efficiency**, **equity**, and **financial sustainability**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, transit service planning forms the **strategic backbone** of all subsequent public transport operations — including route design, scheduling, frequency setting, vehicle allocation, and fare structure. It is the *upstream* decision-making layer that dictates what happens in *downstream* stages (timetabling, crew scheduling, vehicle dispatching).

The hierarchy of transit planning (as per Transit Capacity and Quality of Service Manual – TCQSM framework, which KTU follows) is:

| Planning Level | Time Horizon | Output |
|---|---|---|
| Strategic Planning | 10–20 years | Long-range goals, corridor identification |
| Tactical Planning (Service Planning) | 3–5 years | Route structure, network design, service standards |
| Operational Planning | < 1 year | Schedules, run-cutting, crew rosters |
| Day-to-Day Operations | Real-time | Vehicle dispatch, incident management |

> [!NOTE]
> For your KTU exams, **"Service Planning" specifically refers to Tactical Planning** — the level where routes, stops, frequencies, and service spans are determined.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

Think of transit service planning as **designing the menu and seating arrangement of a restaurant**:

- **Strategic Planning** = Deciding *what kind* of restaurant to open (fine dining vs. fast food) — this is choosing the *type* of transit (metro vs. bus).
- **Service Planning** = Designing the *menu items* (which dishes to offer) and *table layout* (where customers sit) — this is choosing *which routes* to run and *where* stops are placed.
- **Operational Planning** = Deciding *how many chefs* work the *lunch shift* and *which tables* they handle — this is timetabling, vehicle scheduling, and crew assignment.

The chef (transit planner) must know:
- What customers want (travel demand surveys)
- How much time they have (peak vs. off-peak)
- What ingredients are available (budget, fleet size)
- How to keep everyone satisfied (level of service standards)

## 1.3 Why Transit Service Planning Matters

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Statistic for KTU Viva:** Public transport systems worldwide carry **billions of passenger-kilometers per year**. For example, Indian Railways alone carries over 8 billion passengers annually. A *single percentage improvement* in route efficiency translates to crores of rupees in savings and lakhs of commuter-hours saved.

The **"3 E's"** of transit service planning (a common KTU exam ask):
- **Effectiveness** — Does it meet passenger needs?
- **Efficiency** — Is it delivered at minimum cost?
- **Equity** — Is service fairly distributed across society?

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Transit Service Planning

The KTU 2024 syllabus breaks transit service planning into **five interlinked pillars**:

### Pillar 1: Demand Analysis
- **Origin-Destination (O-D) surveys** — Where are trips starting and ending?
- **Ridership forecasting** — Predicting future demand using the *4-step model* (Trip Generation → Trip Distribution → Mode Split → Traffic Assignment).
- **Peak vs. Off-Peak demand** — Critical for service span and frequency.

### Pillar 2: Network Design
- **Route layout** — Radial, grid, hub-and-spoke, feeder-trunk, or hybrid.
- **Stop spacing** — Trade-off between **accessibility** (closer stops) and **speed** (fewer stops).
- **Route directness** — Ratio of airline distance to route distance.

### Pillar 3: Service Frequency & Span
- **Frequency (f)** — Vehicles per hour on a route.
- **Service Span** — Hours of operation per day.
- **Headway (h)** — Time between consecutive vehicles: $h = \frac{60}{f}$.

### Pillar 4: Modal Integration
- Coordination between **bus, metro, rail, IPT (Intermediate Public Transport)** like autos and taxis, and **NMT (Non-Motorized Transport)** like cycling and walking.
- Last-mile connectivity strategies.

### Pillar 5: Performance Evaluation
- Service must be continuously monitored using **Key Performance Indicators (KPIs)** like:
  - **Passenger-kilometers traveled (PKT)**
  - **Vehicle-kilometers operated (VKO)**
  - **Load factor (LF)**
  - **Service reliability (on-time performance)**

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This is your **exam-ready cheat sheet**. Memorize the units, boundary conditions, and the *meaning* of each variable.

| # | Formula / Metric | Variables Explained | Typical Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Headway (h)} = \frac{60}{f}$ | $f$ = service frequency | minutes | Setting schedule gaps |
| 2 | $\text{Load Factor (LF)} = \frac{P_{\text{actual}}}{P_{\text{capacity}}} \times 100$ | $P_{\text{actual}}$ = passengers onboard, $P_{\text{capacity}}$ = seating+standing | % | Crowding assessment |
| 3 | $\text{PKT} = \sum (P_i \times d_i)$ | $P_i$ = passengers on leg $i$, $d_i$ = distance of leg $i$ | passenger-km | Total demand served |
| 4 | $\text{VKT} = \sum (V_j \times L_j)$ | $V_j$ = vehicles on leg $j$, $L_j$ = length of leg $j$ | vehicle-km | Supply offered |
| 5 | $\text{Route Directness} = \frac{D_{\text{airline}}}{D_{\text{route}}}$ | $D$ = distance | ratio (0 to 1) | Network efficiency |
| 6 | $\text{Cost Recovery Ratio (CRR)} = \frac{\text{Fare Revenue}}{\text{Operating Cost}}$ | — | ratio or % | Financial sustainability |
| 7 | $\text{Operating Speed} (V_o) = \frac{D_{\text{route}}}{T_{\text{total}}}$ | $T_{\text{total}}$ = end-to-end travel time | km/h | Service speed benchmark |
| 8 | $\text{Ridership Productivity} = \frac{\text{Total Passengers}}{\text{VKT}}$ | — | pax/km | System efficiency |
| 9 | $\text{Mode Share} = \frac{T_{\text{transit}}}{T_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | $T$ = trips by mode | % | Modal split analysis |
| 10 | $\text{Service Coverage} = \frac{\text{Pop. within 400m of stop}}{\text{Total Pop.}} \times 100$ | 400m = standard walk catchment | % | Accessibility measure |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often confuse **VKT (Vehicle-Kilometers Traveled)** with **PKT (Passenger-Kilometers Traveled)**. Remember: **VKT measures supply (what you run)**, **PKT measures demand (what you serve)**.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Urban Planners** use TSP outputs to decide where to permit high-density developments (TOD — Transit-Oriented Development).
- **Civil Engineers** design bus stops, BRT corridors, and metro stations based on the planner's network design.
- **Traffic Engineers** manage signal priority for transit vehicles based on service frequency.
- **Policy Makers** allocate subsidies using the CRR (Cost Recovery Ratio) computed during planning.

## 2.4 The Service Planning Process (Stepwise)

1. **Define Goals & Objectives** — Increase ridership? Reduce congestion? Improve equity?
2. **Collect Data** — O-D surveys, census, GPS data, AFC (Automatic Fare Collection) data.
3. **Analyze Existing Services** — Identify gaps, overlaps, underserved areas.
4. **Forecast Future Demand** — Using the 4-step model or simpler growth-factor methods.
5. **Design Alternative Networks** — Generate 2–3 candidate route plans.
6. **Evaluate Alternatives** — Using KPIs, cost-benefit analysis, and multi-criteria decision analysis.
7. **Select & Implement** — Roll out the chosen plan in phases.
8. **Monitor & Refine** — Continuous improvement cycle.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Headway-Frequency-Dwell Time Relationship

This is a **frequently asked 14-mark derivation** in KTU. The relationship governs how often a bus must arrive to clear passengers without overcrowding.

**Given:**
- Peak-hour passenger demand on a route segment: $P$ = 1200 pax/hr
- Bus capacity: $C$ = 60 pax
- Average dwell time at busiest stop: $t_d$ = 30 seconds = 0.5 min

**Find:** Required frequency $f$ and headway $h$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Write the basic capacity equation for a transit route segment.

The maximum number of passengers that can be carried per hour, given a fleet of buses each with capacity $C$ arriving every $h$ minutes, is:

$$
P_{\text{capacity}} = \frac{C \times 60}{h}
$$

**Explanation:** If a bus arrives every $h$ minutes, then in 60 minutes the number of buses is $60/h$. Each carries at most $C$ passengers. So total hourly capacity is $C \times (60/h)$.

**Step 2:** Equate capacity to demand to find the *minimum* required headway.

$$
P = \frac{C \times 60}{h_{\text{min}}}
$$

Rearranging:

$$
h_{\text{min}} = \frac{C \times 60}{P}
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute numerical values.

$$
h_{\text{min}} = \frac{60 \times 60}{1200} = \frac{3600}{1200} = 3 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Step 4:** Convert headway to frequency.

$$
f = \frac{60}{h} = \frac{60}{3} = 20 \text{ buses/hour}
$$

**Step 5:** Check if dwell time is the binding constraint.

A bus must be able to complete its dwell + travel between successive arrivals. The minimum achievable headway cannot be less than the dwell time at the busiest stop.

$$
h_{\text{achievable, min}} = t_d + t_{\text{clearance}} \approx 0.5 + 0.25 = 0.75 \text{ min}
$$

Since $h_{\text{min}} = 3 \text{ min} \gg 0.75 \text{ min}$, **dwell time is not the binding constraint**. The route is feasible.

**Final Answer:** $f = 20$ buses/hour, $h = 3$ minutes.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Load Factor and Service Adequacy

**Given:** A bus route segment with:
- 2 buses arrive per cycle
- Each bus has capacity $C = 50$ pax
- Average passengers per bus at maximum load point: $P_{\text{actual}} = 45$ pax

**Find:** Load factor and interpret.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Recall the load factor formula.

$$
\text{LF} = \frac{P_{\text{actual}}}{P_{\text{capacity}}} \times 100
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute.

$$
\text{LF} = \frac{45}{50} \times 100 = 90\%
$$

**Step 3:** Interpret using KTU standard service benchmarks.

| Load Factor | Service Quality |
|---|---|
| LF $\leq$ 50% | Under-utilized — consider reducing frequency |
| 50% < LF $\leq$ 80% | Optimal service range |
| 80% < LF $\leq$ 100% | Crowded but acceptable for short peaks |
| LF > 100% | Overcrowded — increase frequency immediately |

**Final Answer:** LF = 90% → **Crowded but acceptable**; planner may consider increasing frequency during peak hours.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Route Directness and Network Efficiency

**Given:** A bus route connecting Origin O to Destination D.
- Airline (straight-line) distance: $D_{\text{airline}} = 5 \text{ km}$
- Actual route distance: $D_{\text{route}} = 7.5 \text{ km}$ (route detours via 2 major markets)

**Find:** Route Directness Ratio and assess.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Apply the formula.

$$
\text{RD} = \frac{D_{\text{airline}}}{D_{\text{route}}} = \frac{5}{7.5} = 0.667
$$

**Step 2:** Interpret using KTU benchmarks.

| Route Directness | Service Quality |
|---|---|
| RD $\geq$ 0.90 | Excellent — nearly direct |
| 0.75 $\leq$ RD < 0.90 | Good |
| 0.60 $\leq$ RD < 0.75 | Acceptable — minor detour justified |
| RD < 0.60 | Poor — consider rerouting |

**Final Answer:** RD = 0.667 → **Acceptable**; the detour is justified if it serves high-demand markets en route.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparative Transit Modes

This is a **frequently asked table-type question** in KTU exams.

| Parameter | City Bus | BRT | Metro Rail | Suburban Rail |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Capacity (pax/hr/direction) | 1,000–4,000 | 5,000–15,000 | 20,000–60,000 | 30,000–80,000 |
| Operating Speed (km/h) | 15–20 | 25–35 | 30–40 | 40–60 |
| Capital Cost (₹/km, approx) | 2–5 Cr | 30–60 Cr | 200–400 Cr | 100–250 Cr |
| Right-of-Way | Mixed traffic | Dedicated lane | Fully segregated | Fully segregated |
| Service Coverage | High (flexible) | Medium | Low (fixed corridor) | Low |
| Best Suited For | Low-medium demand | High-density corridors | Very high demand | Inter-city commuter |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram 1: Transit Service Planning Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Define Goals and Objectives] --> B[Collect Data - O-D Surveys, Census, GPS]
    B --> C[Analyze Existing Services]
    C --> D[Forecast Future Demand]
    D --> E[Design Alternative Networks]
    E --> F[Evaluate Alternatives using KPIs]
    F --> G{Decision - Select Best Plan}
    G --> H[Phased Implementation]
    H --> I[Monitor and Refine]
    I --> C
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram 2: Hierarchical Planning Levels

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    L1[Strategic Planning - 10 to 20 years] --> L2[Tactical Service Planning - 3 to 5 years]
    L2 --> L3[Operational Planning - less than 1 year]
    L3 --> L4[Day to Day Operations - Real Time]
    L1 -. Long range goals .-> L1
    L2 -. Route and network design .-> L2
    L3 -. Schedules and crew .-> L3
    L4 -. Vehicle dispatch .-> L4
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram 3: Block-Level Functional Architecture of Transit Service Planning

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT
        I1[Demand Data]
        I2[Network Data]
        I3[Resource Data]
        I4[Policy Data]
    end
    subgraph PROCESSING
        P1[Demand Analysis]
        P2[Network Design]
        P3[Service Standards]
        P4[Resource Allocation]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT
        O1[Route Plan]
        O2[Timetable]
        O3[Fleet Plan]
        O4[Fare Policy]
    end
    subgraph FEEDBACK
        F1[KPI Monitoring]
        F2[Passenger Surveys]
        F3[Cost Audit]
    end
    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P2
    I3 --> P4
    I4 --> P3
    P1 --> O1
    P2 --> O1
    P3 --> O2
    P4 --> O3
    O1 --> F1
    O2 --> F1
    O3 --> F3
    F1 --> P1
    F2 --> P2
    F3 --> P4
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram 4: The 3 E's Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Transit Service Planning] --> B[Effectiveness]
    A --> C[Efficiency]
    A --> D[Equity]
    B --> B1[Meets passenger needs]
    B --> B2[Coverage and frequency]
    C --> C1[Minimum cost operation]
    C --> C2[Resource optimization]
    D --> D1[Fair access for all]
    D --> D2[Subsidy for poor routes]
```

## 4.5 Mermaid Diagram 5: Transit Network Topologies

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    N1[Radial Network] --> N1D[All routes converge to city center]
    N2[Grid Network] --> N2D[Routes on perpendicular streets]
    N3[Hub and Spoke] --> N3D[Routes converge at major hubs]
    N4[Feeder Trunk] --> N4D[Small buses feed main corridors]
    N5[Hybrid Network] --> N5D[Combines multiple topologies]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Transit Service Planning. List its hierarchical levels. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

> Transit Service Planning is the systematic process of designing public transport networks, routes, frequencies, and service spans to meet passenger demand efficiently and equitably.

**Hierarchical levels of transit planning:**
1. Strategic Planning (long-range, 10–20 years)
2. Tactical/Service Planning (3–5 years) — *this is the focus of Module 1*
3. Operational Planning (schedules, < 1 year)
4. Day-to-Day Operations (real-time)

---

### Question 2: What are the "3 E's" of transit service planning? Explain each briefly. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The 3 E's of transit service planning are:
1. **Effectiveness** — The degree to which the service meets passenger mobility needs (measured by ridership, coverage, frequency).
2. **Efficiency** — The degree to which the service is delivered at minimum cost (measured by cost per passenger-km, VKT productivity).
3. **Equity** — The fair distribution of service across all socio-economic groups and geographies (measured by service coverage in low-income areas, fare affordability).

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

### QUESTION A: 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the step-by-step process of transit service planning with a neat flowchart. List the major data inputs required at each stage. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A bus route has a peak demand of 2400 passengers per hour in the maximum load section. Each bus has a capacity of 80 passengers. Calculate: (i) the minimum headway, (ii) the required frequency, and (iii) comment on the feasibility if the average dwell time at the busiest stop is 45 seconds. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

The transit service planning process consists of 8 sequential stages:

1. **Define Goals & Objectives** — Inputs: City mobility policy, target mode share, budget ceiling.
2. **Collect Data** — Inputs: Origin-Destination (O-D) surveys, household travel surveys, GPS data from existing fleets, Automatic Fare Collection (AFC) data, census demographics, land-use plans.
3. **Analyze Existing Services** — Inputs: Route maps, current ridership data, complaint logs.
4. **Forecast Future Demand** — Inputs: Population growth projections, employment projections, proposed infrastructure projects.
5. **Design Alternative Networks** — Inputs: Candidate route corridors, interchange points, demand matrices.
6. **Evaluate Alternatives** — Inputs: Cost data, ridership forecasts, multi-criteria scoring weights.
7. **Select & Implement** — Inputs: Final approved plan, phased rollout schedule.
8. **Monitor & Refine** — Inputs: KPIs (KPIs = Key Performance Indicators), passenger satisfaction surveys.

> **Full marks (7/7)** requires a labelled flowchart showing all 8 stages and at least 2 data inputs per stage.

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:** $P = 2400$ pax/hr, $C = 80$ pax, $t_d = 45$ sec $= 0.75$ min

**Step 1:** Write the capacity-headway equation.

$$
P = \frac{C \times 60}{h_{\text{min}}}
$$

**Step 2:** Solve for headway.

$$
h_{\text{min}} = \frac{C \times 60}{P} = \frac{80 \times 60}{2400} = \frac{4800}{2400} = 2 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Step 3:** Calculate frequency.

$$
f = \frac{60}{h_{\text{min}}} = \frac{60}{2} = 30 \text{ buses/hour}
$$

**Step 4:** Feasibility check.

A bus needs $t_d = 0.75$ min for dwell time plus a minimum signal/clearance time of approximately 0.25 min, so the *absolute minimum achievable headway* is:

$$
h_{\text{abs min}} = 0.75 + 0.25 = 1.0 \text{ min}
$$

Since $h_{\text{min}} = 2 \text{ min} > 1.0 \text{ min}$, the required headway is **achievable** and the service plan is **feasible**.

> **Conclusion:** The plan is feasible. 30 buses per hour are required, with a headway of 2 minutes.

---

### QUESTION B: 14 Marks (Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Compare the different transit network topologies (radial, grid, hub-and-spoke, feeder-trunk) with neat diagrams. State one advantage and one disadvantage of each. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A metro corridor has the following data: route length = 25 km, one-way running time = 50 minutes, turnaround time at each terminal = 10 minutes, peak-hour headway = 5 minutes. Calculate: (i) the round-trip time, (ii) the fleet size required, and (iii) the achievable commercial speed. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

| Topology | Description | Advantage | Disadvantage |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Radial** | All routes converge to a central point (CBD) | High CBD accessibility | Overloads center; poor peripheral connectivity |
| **Grid** | Routes run on perpendicular streets | High coverage, redundancy | Indirect trips require transfers |
| **Hub-and-Spoke** | Routes feed into major interchange hubs | Efficient long-distance trips | Requires high-capacity hubs; less direct |
| **Feeder-Trunk** | Small buses feed main high-capacity corridors | Excellent last-mile connectivity | Requires precise schedule synchronization |
| **Hybrid** | Mix of two or more topologies | Tailored to city shape | Complex to plan and operate |

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Given:** $L = 25$ km, $T_{\text{run}} = 50$ min, $T_{\text{turn}} = 10$ min, $h = 5$ min

**Step 1:** Round-trip time.

$$
T_{\text{rt}} = 2 \times T_{\text{run}} + 2 \times T_{\text{turn}}
$$

$$
T_{\text{rt}} = 2 \times 50 + 2 \times 10 = 100 + 20 = 120 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Step 2:** Fleet size (using the standard formula).

$$
N = \frac{T_{\text{rt}}}{h} = \frac{120}{5} = 24 \text{ trains}
$$

**Step 3:** Achievable commercial speed.

Commercial speed = total round-trip distance / total round-trip time.

$$
V_c = \frac{2 \times L}{T_{\text{rt}}} = \frac{2 \times 25}{120} = \frac{50}{120} = 0.4167 \text{ km/min}
$$

Converting to km/h:

$$
V_c = 0.4167 \times 60 = 25 \text{ km/h}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The metro requires 24 trains and operates at a commercial speed of 25 km/h.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors:** Always convert dwell time from *seconds to minutes* before comparing with headway in minutes. Mixing units is the #1 reason students lose 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing Round-Trip Time:** Some students forget to double the turnaround time. If both terminals have 10 min layover, total turnaround is $2 \times 10 = 20$ min, not 10 min.
> 3. **Skipping the Flowchart:** In 7-mark theory questions, a *labelled diagram* is mandatory. Pure text without a diagram typically caps your score at 5/7.
> 4. **Not Interpreting Numerical Results:** Always add a concluding sentence — e.g., "Thus the plan is feasible" or "The route is overcrowded." A bare numerical answer loses 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Vivas and Exams:**

- [x] Transit Service Planning = **Tactical Planning** (3–5 year horizon), distinct from Strategic and Operational planning.
- [x] **Headway** $h = 60/f$ (minutes); **Frequency** $f = 60/h$ (buses/hr).
- [x] **3 E's of TSP** = **Effectiveness, Efficiency, Equity**.
- [x] **Load Factor (LF)** benchmarks: < 50% underused, 50–80% optimal, 80–100% crowded, > 100% overcrowded.
- [x] **Route Directness** = $D_{\text{airline}} / D_{\text{route}}$; benchmark = $\geq 0.75$ for "good" service.
- [x] **Service coverage** uses **400 m** walking catchment radius around stops.
- [x] **PKT** = demand measure; **VKT** = supply measure.
- [x] **Capacity equation:** $P = (C \times 60)/h$.
- [x] **Round-trip time** = $2 \times \text{running time} + 2 \times \text{turnaround time}$.
- [x] **Fleet size** = $T_{\text{rt}} / h$.
- [x] **Commercial speed** = (round-trip distance) / (round-trip time) in km/h.
- [x] **Network topologies:** Radial, Grid, Hub-and-Spoke, Feeder-Trunk, Hybrid.
- [x] **Four-step demand model:** Trip Generation → Trip Distribution → Mode Split → Traffic Assignment.
- [x] Transit modes ranked by capacity: City Bus < BRT < Metro < Suburban Rail.
- [x] Standard walk distance to bus stop: **400 m** (≈ 5 min walk).
- [x] Always check **dwell time constraint** when designing high-frequency routes.

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Introduction to Transit Service Planning**
