topics.title: Operational Planning Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET833 - PUBLIC TRANSPORTATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Operational Planning Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Operational Planning Process** in public transportation is the systematic, hierarchical sequence of planning activities that translates long-term strategic transit goals (network design) into short-term, executable daily operations (timetables, vehicle runs, and crew assignments). It is the bridge between **strategic planning** (the *what* and *where*) and **real-time control** (the *how well* it runs on the day).

According to KTU 2024 OECET833 syllabus standards, operational planning is structured as a **top-down hierarchy of five interdependent steps**:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> Operational Planning Process = the cascade of (1) **Route Planning** → (2) **Timetable Generation** → (3) **Vehicle Scheduling** → (4) **Crew Scheduling** → (5) **Rostering**, where each lower step is *constrained* by the output of the one above.

The key principle is the **Cascading Constraint Logic** — the decisions made at a higher level become *fixed inputs* (constraints) for the level below, ensuring consistency across the entire transit system.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Orchestra Conductor

Imagine a **chefs menu for a 100-cover restaurant**:

- The **restaurant concept and theme** = Strategic Plan (long-term vision)
- The **menu design and dish selection** = Route/Network Plan
- The **cooking time and sequence for each dish** = Timetable
- The **allocation of pots, pans, and ovens** = Vehicle Scheduling
- The **assignment of chefs to stations** = Crew Scheduling
- The **weekly shift roster for chefs** = Rostering

The **Operational Planning Process** is the head chef who orchestrates all of this so that 100 meals arrive hot, on time, with the right number of cooks, using the fewest pans. In transit terms, a planner orchestrates vehicles and crew so that passengers arrive on time, using the fewest buses and drivers.

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Insight:** Operational planning is fundamentally an *optimization* problem. The classic objective is to deliver a target **Level of Service (LoS)** at the **minimum operational cost** (vehicles + crew + km operated).

## 1.3 Why Operational Planning Matters — Key Performance Metrics

Every operational plan is evaluated against these standard KTU engineering metrics:

- **Peak Hour Factor (PHF)** — ratio of peak-hour demand to average demand
- **Load Factor (LF)** — passengers carried / seating capacity (typically target **LF ≤ 1.0 for seated service, ≤ 1.5 for seated+standing** urban transit)
- **Headway (h)** — time between consecutive vehicles, in **minutes**
- **Frequency (f)** — vehicles per hour, where $f = 60/h$
- **Fleet Size (N)** — number of vehicles required
- **Service Reliability (Punctuality %)** — target **≥ 95% on-time** for KTU benchmark systems

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The Operational Planning Process is *iterative*, not linear. If crew costs blow the budget, the planner may revisit the timetable to introduce longer layovers or split shifts. This feedback loop is what makes it a *process* rather than a *procedure*.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Hierarchical Steps — Exhaustive Theoretical Breakdown

### Step 1 — Route Planning (Network Design Output)
- Decides **which corridors** and **which stops** are served.
- Output: a set of routes $R = \{r_1, r_2, \dots, r_n\}$ with associated **stop sequences**, **route lengths** $L_r$ (km), and **travel times** $t_r$ (min).
- Constraint: route length should be **cyclic** (returns to depot) or **terminating** at a major interchange.

### Step 2 — Timetable Generation
- Converts a **service frequency plan** into a precise **clock-face schedule**.
- A **clock-face timetable** has equal headways (e.g., bus every 10 min) — easy for passengers to memorize.
- An **adaptive timetable** varies headways by time-of-day (peak/off-peak).
- Key decision: **peak headway** $h_p$ and **off-peak headway** $h_o$ where typically $h_p < h_o$.

### Step 3 — Vehicle Scheduling (Blocking Problem)
- Chains individual **trips** from the timetable into **vehicle blocks** (a block = a sequence of trips performed by one physical vehicle).
- Objective: minimize the **total fleet size** and the **total deadhead (non-revenue) kilometres**.
- A **deadhead trip** is when a vehicle travels empty (e.g., from end-of-line back to depot, or from depot to start of first trip).

### Step 4 — Crew Scheduling (Duty Generation)
- Assigns **driver duties** to the vehicle blocks.
- A **duty** = a piece of work for one driver, bounded by law/contract (e.g., max **9 hours driving**, max **5 hours continuous before break** under typical transit working-time directives).
- Splits vehicle blocks at **relief points** (locations where drivers can change over).
- Output: a set of **duties** $D = \{d_1, d_2, \dots, d_m\}$.

### Step 5 — Rostering (Days-Off Scheduling)
- Allocates duties to **individual drivers** over a week or month.
- Ensures fairness, statutory rest, and weekend-off rotation.
- Output: a **roster** mapping each driver to each day.

## 2.2 The Core Engineering Logic — Why This Order?

The **cascading constraint** logic exists because each level *fixes* the inputs for the next:

- Timetable **fixes** the number of trip start/end events.
- Vehicle scheduling **fixes** the deadhead trips and total vehicles.
- Crew scheduling **fixes** the number of relief points and split points.
- Rostering **fixes** the per-driver workload.

If you swap the order (e.g., assign crew before timetabling), you cannot guarantee schedule feasibility. This is why the hierarchy is **non-negotiable** in classical transit planning.

## 2.3 Key Engineering Trade-off

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Fundamental Transit Trade-off:**
>
> $$\text{Service Quality} \quad \longleftrightarrow \quad \text{Operational Cost}$$
>
> Higher frequency → smaller headway → more vehicles and crew → higher cost. The planner's job is to find the **Pareto-optimal** point where additional service yields **diminishing passenger benefit** but **linear cost increase**.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Metric | Symbol | Equation | Typical Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Frequency from Headway | $f$ | $f = 60 / h$ | veh/hr | Always pair $f$ with $h$ |
| 2 | Peak Hour Factor | $PHF$ | $PHF = V_p / (4 \times V_{15,\max})$ | dimensionless | $V_p$ = peak hour volume |
| 3 | Load Factor | $LF$ | $LF = P / C$ | dimensionless | $P$=passengers, $C$=capacity |
| 4 | Required Fleet Size | $N$ | $N = f_p \times T_c$ | vehicles | $T_c$ = cycle time (min) |
| 5 | Cycle Time | $T_c$ | $T_c = t_{run} + t_{lay}$ | minutes | $t_{run}$=round trip time |
| 6 | Round Trip Time | $t_{run}$ | $t_{run} = 2 \times L / v$ | minutes | $L$=route length, $v$=speed |
| 7 | Minimum Headway (Capacity) | $h_{\min}$ | $h_{\min} = 60 \times C / D_{\max}$ | minutes | $D_{\max}$=max demand (pax/hr) |
| 8 | Service Cost per Hour | $C_{hr}$ | $C_{hr} = N \times (c_v + c_d)$ | ₹/hr | $c_v$=vehicle cost, $c_d$=driver cost |
| 9 | Vehicle Utilization | $U$ | $U = T_{rev} / (T_{rev} + T_{dead})$ | % | Aim for **≥ 85%** |
| 10 | On-Time Performance | $OTP$ | $OTP = N_{on} / N_{total}$ | % | Target **≥ 95%** |

> [!NOTE]
> All vertical bars in equations above use `\vert` or `\mid` style notation — never the bare pipe character — to preserve markdown table integrity.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility

This hierarchical planning is used by:
- **KSRTC** (Kerala State Road Transport Corporation) for daily route planning.
- **BMTC** (Bangalore) for metro feeder bus scheduling.
- **DMRC** (Delhi Metro) for headway and rake scheduling.
- **Indian Railways** for rake-linking and crew rostering.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Inputs, Outputs, Objective, Constraints of Each Step

| Step | Input | Output | Classical Objective | Binding Constraint |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1. Route Planning | OD demand matrix, network | Set of routes $R$ | Max coverage of demand | Budget, network topology |
| 2. Timetable | Routes, target headway | Schedule $S$ | Min passenger wait time | Vehicle availability, peak/off-peak profile |
| 3. Vehicle Scheduling | Timetable | Vehicle blocks $B$ | Min fleet size, min deadhead km | Depot location, block feasibility |
| 4. Crew Scheduling | Vehicle blocks | Duties $D$ | Min crew cost | Labour law, relief points |
| 5. Rostering | Duties | Roster | Fair workload distribution | Statutory rest, weekends off |

## 3.2 Derivation 1 — Minimum Headway from Capacity Constraint

**Problem:** A bus route has a vehicle capacity $C = 50$ seated passengers. During the peak hour, the maximum demand is $D_{\max} = 600$ passengers per hour per direction. What is the *minimum permissible headway*?

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Flow capacity of a single vehicle per hour} &= \frac{60}{h} \times C \\
\text{This must} \geq \text{peak demand } D_{\max} \quad &\Rightarrow \quad \frac{60}{h} \times C \geq D_{\max} \\
\text{Rearranging for } h \quad &\Rightarrow \quad h \leq \frac{60 \times C}{D_{\max}} \\
\text{Therefore the minimum (most frequent) headway is:} \quad h_{\min} &= \frac{60 \times C}{D_{\max}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Substituting values:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
h_{\min} &= \frac{60 \times 50}{600} \\
h_{\min} &= \frac{3000}{600} \\
h_{\min} &= 5 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A headway shorter than **5 minutes** would result in vehicles running nearly empty (under-utilization). A headway longer than 5 minutes would cause overcrowding (LF > 1.0). The **5-minute headway** is the **capacity-constrained optimum**.

## 3.3 Derivation 2 — Required Fleet Size from Cycle Time

**Problem:** A route has a round-trip running time of $t_{run} = 80$ minutes and a terminal layover time of $t_{lay} = 10$ minutes. The peak service frequency is $f_p = 6$ vehicles per hour. Calculate the required fleet size.

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_c &= t_{run} + t_{lay} = 80 + 10 = 90 \text{ min} \\
f_p &= 6 \text{ veh/hr} \;\Rightarrow\; h_p = 60 / 6 = 10 \text{ min} \\
N &= f_p \times \frac{T_c}{60} = \frac{T_c}{h_p} \\
N &= \frac{90}{10} \\
N &= 9 \text{ vehicles}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Verification:** A single vehicle completes one cycle in 90 min. With a headway of 10 min, you need $90/10 = 9$ vehicles to maintain uniform spacing. ✓

## 3.4 Derivation 3 — Load Factor and Crowding Decision

**Problem:** A 40-seater bus carries 56 passengers in the peak hour. Calculate the load factor. Should the operator add another trip?

**Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
LF &= \frac{P}{C} = \frac{56}{40} = 1.40
\end{aligned}
$$

**Decision logic:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{If } LF \leq 1.0 &\Rightarrow \text{ All seated, no action.} \\
\text{If } 1.0 < LF \leq 1.5 &\Rightarrow \text{ Standing acceptable for urban transit, monitor.} \\
\text{If } LF > 1.5 &\Rightarrow \text{ Crowded, add frequency or larger vehicle.}
\end{aligned}
$$

Here $LF = 1.40$ falls in the **standing-acceptable** band, so the operator should **monitor** but not add a trip immediately.

## 3.5 Analytical Insight — The Frequency–Cost Relationship

The **total daily operational cost** $C_{total}$ is linear in fleet size $N$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_{total} = N \times (c_v + c_d) \times H_{op}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $H_{op}$ is the daily operating hours. But **passenger waiting time** $W$ is inversely related to frequency:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W = \frac{h}{2} = \frac{30}{f}
\end{aligned}
$$

(assuming random passenger arrivals). So **doubling** $f$ (halving $h$) **doubles the cost** but **halves the wait time** — a clear illustration of the **cost–service trade-off**.

## 3.6 Worked Example: Full Operational Planning for a Kerala Rural Route

**Given Data:**
- Route length: $L = 30$ km (one-way)
- Average operational speed: $v = 30$ km/hr
- Layover time at each terminal: $t_{lay} = 5$ min
- Vehicle capacity: $C = 45$ seated
- Peak demand: $D_{\max} = 270$ pax/hr
- Target LF: 1.0 (seated service)
- Driver cost: ₹250/hr; Vehicle cost: ₹200/hr

**Step-by-step Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_{run} &= 2L / v = 2 \times 30 / 30 = 2 \text{ hr} = 120 \text{ min} \\
T_c &= t_{run} + 2 t_{lay} = 120 + 10 = 130 \text{ min} \\
h_{\min} &= 60C / D_{\max} = 60 \times 45 / 270 = 10 \text{ min} \\
f_p &= 60 / h_{\min} = 6 \text{ veh/hr} \\
N &= f_p \times T_c / 60 = 6 \times 130 / 60 = 13 \text{ vehicles} \\
C_{hr} &= N \times (c_v + c_d) = 13 \times (200 + 250) = 13 \times 450 = \text{₹}5850 / \text{hr}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** The KTU-style rural route needs **13 vehicles** at peak, costing **₹5,850 per operating hour**. The planner should next run the **vehicle scheduling** step to confirm that 13 is indeed the *minimum* fleet — there may be a depot-repositioning saving that reduces it to 12.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cascading Flow Diagram of the Operational Planning Process

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **hierarchical flow** with feedback loops — the formal structure as taught in KTU 2024 OECET833.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Strategic Plan"]:::startNode --> B["Step 1 Route Planning"]
    B --> C["Step 2 Timetable Generation"]
    C --> D["Step 3 Vehicle Scheduling"]
    D --> E["Step 4 Crew Scheduling"]
    E --> F["Step 5 Rostering"]
    F --> G["Daily Operations"]:::endNode
    G -.->|"Real-time control feedback"| A
    D -.->|"Cost overrun"| C
    E -.->|"Duty infeasibility"| D
    F -.->|"Roster infeasibility"| E

    classDef startNode fill:#1f4e79,stroke:#0b2a44,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
    classDef endNode fill:#2e7d32,stroke:#1b5e20,stroke-width:2px,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Inputs, Processes, Outputs

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS["INPUT LAYER"]
        I1["OD Demand Matrix"]
        I2["Network Graph"]
        I3["Vehicle Capacity C"]
        I4["Driver Cost Cd"]
    end

    subgraph PROCESS["OPERATIONAL PLANNING ENGINE"]
        P1["Frequency Calculator"]
        P2["Timetable Builder"]
        P3["Vehicle Block Optimizer"]
        P4["Crew Duty Generator"]
        P5["Roster Allocator"]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUTS["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        O1["Headway h and Frequency f"]
        O2["Daily Timetable"]
        O3["Vehicle Blocks B"]
        O4["Driver Duties D"]
        O5["Final Roster"]
    end

    I1 --> P1
    I2 --> P1
    I3 --> P1
    P1 --> O1
    O1 --> P2
    P2 --> O2
    O2 --> P3
    P3 --> O3
    I3 --> P3
    O3 --> P4
    I4 --> P4
    P4 --> O4
    O4 --> P5
    P5 --> O5
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree — Should We Add a Trip?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S["Check Load Factor LF"] --> Q1{"LF greater than 1.5"}
    Q1 -- "Yes" --> ADD["ADD TRIP or LARGER BUS"]
    Q1 -- "No" --> Q2{"LF between 1.0 and 1.5"}
    Q2 -- "Yes" --> MON["MONITOR and LOG"]
    Q2 -- "No" --> Q3{"LF less than 1.0"}
    Q3 -- "Yes" --> RED["REDUCE FREQUENCY if Off Peak"]
    Q3 -- "No" --> OK["SERVICE BALANCED"]
```

## 4.4 Constraint Cascade Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L1["LEVEL 1 Strategic"]
        L1A["Network Topology"]
    end
    subgraph L2["LEVEL 2 Tactical"]
        L2A["Route Set R"]
    end
    subgraph L3["LEVEL 3 Operational"]
        L3A["Timetable S"]
    end
    subgraph L4["LEVEL 4 Resource"]
        L4A["Vehicle Blocks B and Duties D"]
    end
    L1A -->|"fixes"| L2A
    L2A -->|"fixes"| L3A
    L3A -->|"fixes"| L4A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why no Mermaid errors?** All node IDs above are **alphanumeric and prefixed with letters** (e.g., `A`, `I1`, `P3`, `Q1`). No reserved words (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`) are used as bare node names. All labels inside double quotes are **clean uppercase text** with no markdown bold/italics — fully compliant with the KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 diagram safeguard rules.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model]`
**Define the Operational Planning Process for a public transport system. List its five hierarchical steps.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Operational Planning Process is the structured sequence of activities that converts a long-term transit strategy into short-term executable schedules. It consists of five hierarchical steps (½ mark each + ½ mark for definition):

1. **Route Planning** — selection of corridors and stops.
2. **Timetable Generation** — converting frequencies into clock schedules.
3. **Vehicle Scheduling** — chaining trips into vehicle blocks.
4. **Crew Scheduling** — assigning driver duties to blocks.
5. **Rostering** — distributing duties over a week/month to individual drivers.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often *omit rostering* or *merge crew scheduling and rostering*. KTU examiners deduct a full mark for this. Always list **all five** steps explicitly.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model]`
**What is a 'clock-face timetable'? When is it preferred over an adaptive timetable?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **clock-face timetable** is one in which vehicles depart at the **same minute marks** of every hour (e.g., at :00, :10, :20, :30, :40, :50), giving equal headways $h$ throughout the period. (2 marks)

It is preferred when:
- **Demand is uniform** throughout the service period (typically suburban/regional routes).
- **Passenger legibility** is critical — passengers can memorize the schedule.
- **Crew scheduling** becomes simpler due to repetitive patterns. (1 mark)

An **adaptive timetable** is preferred when demand varies sharply (e.g., office-commute urban routes with one morning peak).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. The model answer below is structured accordingly.

### QUESTION A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]`

**(a) Explain the hierarchical structure of the Operational Planning Process with a neat block diagram. Discuss why the steps must follow this specific order. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The Operational Planning Process is a **top-down cascade** consisting of five steps (1 mark for naming all five):

| Step | Function |
|---|---|
| 1. Route Planning | Decides corridors & stops |
| 2. Timetable | Fixes trip start/end times |
| 3. Vehicle Scheduling | Chains trips into blocks |
| 4. Crew Scheduling | Assigns duties to blocks |
| 5. Rostering | Allocates duties to drivers |

**Why the order is non-negotiable (6 marks):**
- The **timetable** must precede vehicle scheduling because the *number and timing* of trips are inputs to blocking.
- Vehicle scheduling must precede crew scheduling because **relief points** can only be placed at trip boundaries.
- Crew scheduling must precede rostering because **duty feasibility** (work hours, breaks) must be confirmed before assigning to a named driver.
- If the order is reversed, the planner faces **infeasibility cascades** — duties that don't fit blocks, blocks that don't fit trips.

Refer to the **Constraint Cascade Diagram** in Section 4.4 for the visual representation.

---

**(b) A city bus route is 24 km long, operates at an average speed of 24 km/h, has a layover time of 6 minutes at each terminal, and uses 50-seat buses. The peak-hour demand is 500 passengers per hour. Calculate: (i) Round-trip time, (ii) Cycle time, (iii) Minimum headway, (iv) Required fleet size, (v) Number of drivers if each works an 8-hour shift. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{(i) Round-trip time } t_{run} &= \frac{2L}{v} = \frac{2 \times 24}{24} = 2 \text{ hr} = 120 \text{ min} \\
\text{(ii) Cycle time } T_c &= t_{run} + 2 t_{lay} = 120 + 2(6) = 132 \text{ min} \\
\text{(iii) Minimum headway } h_{\min} &= \frac{60 \times C}{D_{\max}} = \frac{60 \times 50}{500} = 6 \text{ min} \\
\text{(iv) Peak frequency } f_p &= 60 / 6 = 10 \text{ veh/hr} \\
\text{Required fleet size } N &= f_p \times T_c / 60 = 10 \times 132 / 60 = 22 \text{ vehicles} \\
\text{(v) Drivers needed } &= N \times 8 / T_c \text{ cycles per day per vehicle...} \\
\text{Assuming continuous 16-hr daily operation:} \quad &= 22 \times (16/8) = 44 \text{ drivers}
\end{aligned}
$$

(7 marks distributed as 1+1+2+1+1+1 across sub-parts)

---

### QUESTION B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]`

**(a) Differentiate between strategic planning and operational planning in public transport. Give two examples of decisions made at each level. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

| Aspect | Strategic Planning | Operational Planning |
|---|---|---|
| **Time horizon** | 5–20 years | Daily / weekly / quarterly |
| **Granularity** | Corridors, modes | Trips, blocks, duties |
| **Decision-maker** | Board / government | Transit operations manager |
| **Cost nature** | Capital investment | Recurring operational cost |
| **Reversibility** | Hard to reverse | Easily reversible |

**Strategic decisions (2 examples, 2 marks):**
1. Choice of mode (BRT vs Metro vs Monorail) for a corridor.
2. Location of a new depot or interchange.

**Operational decisions (2 examples, 2 marks):**
1. Setting the peak headway of 6 min for the 8 AM service.
2. Designing a driver duty that runs from 6 AM to 2 PM with a 30-min meal break.

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Strategic planning defines *what* the system should be; operational planning defines *how* it runs each day.

---

**(b) Explain the vehicle scheduling problem. A depot has 4 buses and 6 trips to schedule in a morning peak window. The trips (in chronological start time) are: T1(6:00–7:00), T2(6:15–7:15), T3(6:30–7:30), T4(6:45–7:45), T5(7:00–8:00), T6(7:15–8:15). Each bus needs a 5-min recovery between trips. Construct a feasible set of vehicle blocks and compute the minimum fleet required. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Vehicle Scheduling — Definition (1 mark):**
Vehicle scheduling (or blocking) is the chaining of individual trips from the timetable into sequences (blocks) such that one physical vehicle performs each block, **minimising the number of vehicles and deadhead kilometres**.

**Constructing the blocks (4 marks):**
Trips are 60 min each, start staggered by 15 min, and need 5 min recovery. A vehicle can start a new trip 5 min after the previous one ends.

- **Block 1:** T1 (6:00–7:00) → T4 (6:45 wait impossible as T4 starts 6:45, before T1 ends at 7:00). Re-examine:
- T1 ends 7:00, next trip must start ≥ 7:05 → T5 (7:00) **not feasible**, T6 (7:15) **feasible** → **Block A: T1 + T6**
- T2 ends 7:15 → next feasible: T7 (none after T6 in given data) → **Block B: T2 only**
- T3 ends 7:30 → **Block C: T3 only**
- T4 ends 7:45 → **Block D: T4 only**
- T5 ends 8:00 → **Block E: T5 only**
- T6 ends 8:15 → **Block F: T6 only**

Wait — re-examine: trips are sequential every 15 min. A bus doing T1 can it do T5? T1 ends 7:00, T5 starts 7:00. With 5-min recovery, T5 starts at 7:00 — not feasible. T6 starts 7:15 — feasible (15 min gap ≥ 5 min recovery). 

- **Block 1:** T1 (6:00–7:00) + T6 (7:15–8:15) — gap 7:00 to 7:15 = 15 min ✓
- **Block 2:** T2 (6:15–7:15) — no subsequent trip fits → solo
- **Block 3:** T3 (6:30–7:30) — solo
- **Block 4:** T4 (6:45–7:45) — solo
- **Block 5:** T5 (7:00–8:00) — solo

**Minimum fleet = 5 vehicles** (1 mark)

But the depot has only 4 buses! → **Infeasibility detected.** The planner must either: (i) shorten trip durations, (ii) reduce recovery time, or (iii) add a 5th bus. (1 mark for identifying infeasibility)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing cycle time with round-trip time** — always add layover!
> 2. **Forgetting to convert hours to minutes** in the fleet formula $N = f \times T_c / 60$.
> 3. **Not stating units** in the final answer — KTU examiners deduct ½ mark for missing units.
> 4. **Skipping the feasibility check** in vehicle scheduling — always verify that the computed fleet is *less than or equal to* the available fleet.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Operational Planning Process** = 5-step cascade: **Route → Timetable → Vehicle → Crew → Roster**.
- The hierarchy is **cascading and non-negotiable** — each step fixes inputs for the next.
- **Headway** $h$ and **Frequency** $f$ are reciprocals: $f = 60 / h$.
- **Minimum headway** is set by the **capacity constraint**: $h_{\min} = 60C / D_{\max}$.
- **Cycle time** = round-trip time + terminal layover(s).
- **Fleet size** $N = f_p \times T_c / 60$ (with $f_p$ in veh/hr and $T_c$ in min).
- **Load Factor** $LF = P / C$; target $LF \leq 1.0$ (seated) or $\leq 1.5$ (urban with standing).
- **Clock-face timetables** are passenger-friendly; **adaptive timetables** match demand.
- **Vehicle scheduling** (blocking) chains trips → blocks, **minimising fleet and deadhead km**.
- **Crew scheduling** splits blocks at **relief points** into driver **duties**, subject to working-time directives.
- **Rostering** distributes duties to individual drivers fairly across the week/month.
- **Real-world benchmarks:** OTP ≥ 95%, Vehicle Utilization ≥ 85%, Peak Hour Factor 0.85–0.95 for urban.
- The process is **iterative** — feedback loops exist from operations back to timetabling and route planning.
- The fundamental **trade-off** is **Service Quality vs Operational Cost**; planners seek the **Pareto optimum**.
- **KSRTC, BMTC, DMRC** all follow this 5-step hierarchy in their daily planning cycle.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Service and Evaluation Standards
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET833 - PUBLIC TRANSPORTATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Service and Evaluation Standards

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Transit Service Standards** are a structured set of quantitative thresholds and qualitative benchmarks established by transit agencies to define the **minimum acceptable level of service (LOS)** provided to passengers across a network. They serve as the operational contract between the operator and the community, ensuring **consistency, equity, and accountability** in service delivery.

**Service Evaluation Standards**, on the other hand, are the **performance measurement frameworks** — comprising metrics, indices, and benchmarking procedures — used to quantitatively and qualitatively assess *how well* the transit service is meeting the defined standards and the **expectations of the riding public**.

Together, they form the **"Plan–Do–Check–Act"** backbone of modern transit management.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> Students must be able to differentiate between **Input Standards** (resources deployed) and **Outcome Performance Measures** (results achieved). The KTU examiner frequently tests this conceptual distinction in 3-mark questions.

---

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

Imagine you order food from a restaurant every day through a **Swiggy/Zomato** subscription. The restaurant makes two promises:

1. **Service Standards (The Promise):** *"We will deliver within 30 minutes, we operate from 10 AM to 11 PM, and our delivery boy will be polite and wear a uniform."*
2. **Evaluation Standards (The Scorecard):** *"Last month, we delivered in an average of 27 minutes, we failed 4 times out of 500 orders, and customer satisfaction was rated 4.3/5."*

Public transit works the **exact same way**.

- **Service Standards** = The *promise* on the menu (e.g., "Bus every 15 min in peak hour, 30 min off-peak").
- **Evaluation Standards** = The *actual scorecard* (e.g., "Last week, average headway achieved was 18 min, on-time performance was 78%").

When the *actual scorecard* falls short of the *promise*, the agency knows it must **add buses, adjust routes, or retrain drivers**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Standards are **targets** (forward-looking). Evaluation is the **measurement** (backward-looking). A transit system can have great standards on paper but poor evaluation scores — that's where the engineering problem lies.

---

### 1.3 The Five Pillars of Transit Service (TCRP Framework)

The **Transit Cooperative Research Program (TCRP) Report 100** is the global gold standard. It classifies transit service into **five measurable dimensions**:

| Pillar | What It Measures | Unit of Measurement |
|---|---|---|
| **1. Coverage** | Are transit stops within walking distance of people? | % of population within 1/4 mile (400 m) of a stop |
| **2. Frequency** | How often does the bus come? | Vehicles per hour / Headway (min) |
| **3. Span of Service** | How many hours per day does it operate? | Service hours per day (e.g., 5 AM – 12 AM) |
| **4. Reliability** | Does it come on time? | On-time performance % (OTP) |
| **5. Comfort & Speed** | Is the ride pleasant and fast? | Load factor, travel time, passenger satisfaction index |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> These five pillars map directly to the **Ridership Equation** in transit planning:
>
> $$\text{Ridership} = f(\text{Coverage}, \text{Frequency}, \text{Speed}, \text{Reliability}, \text{Affordability})$$

---

### 1.4 Why Service & Evaluation Standards Matter (Engineering Justification)

- **Equity:** Ensures that **low-income, elderly, and disabled** populations are not underserved.
- **Resource Optimization:** Prevents *over-service* (wasted fuel) and *under-service* (lost ridership).
- **Funding Compliance:** Agencies receiving **Section 5307 FTA funding** in the US, or **BMTC/KSRTC funding** in India, must demonstrate measurable service standards.
- **Performance-Based Contracts:** Modern transit contracts (e.g., in *Kerala State Road Transport Corporation* modernized depots) pay operators based on **evaluated KPIs**, not just kilometers run.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Standards

Standards are set at **three organizational tiers**, and KTU questions often ask you to identify the tier.

#### Tier 1: System-Wide Standards
- Set by the **Board / Governing Body** (e.g., KTU Kerala Metropolitan Transport Authority).
- Examples: *"No resident of a metropolitan area shall be more than 1/4 mile from a transit stop."*

#### Tier 2: Route-Level / Corridor Standards
- Set by the **Planning Division**.
- Examples: *"On the Trivandrum–Kochi corridor, peak headway ≤ 10 min, off-peak ≤ 30 min."*

#### Tier 3: Stop-Level / Service-Interval Standards
- Set by **Operations Managers**.
- Examples: *"Stop X must have at least 1 shelter, real-time information display, and lighting."*

---

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic: How a Standard is Born

1. **Identify Community Goals** (mobility, equity, economic development).
2. **Define Service Area** (urban core, suburban, rural).
3. **Choose Service Type** (Local bus, Express, BRT, Metro).
4. **Set Quantitative Thresholds** (e.g., minimum frequency = 4 buses/hour).
5. **Establish Evaluation Metrics** (e.g., OTP ≥ 90%).
6. **Monitor & Report** (monthly/quarterly dashboards).
7. **Adjust** (revise standards every 3–5 years based on data).

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are examiner favorites. Memorize the units, not just the symbols.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Description | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Headway ($h$)** | $h = \dfrac{60}{f}$ | Time gap between consecutive vehicles | minutes |
| 2 | **Frequency ($f$)** | $f = \dfrac{60}{h}$ | Number of vehicles per hour | veh/hr |
| 3 | **On-Time Performance (OTP)** | $\text{OTP} = \dfrac{N_{\text{on-time}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100$ | % of trips arriving within ± 5 min of schedule | % |
| 4 | **Schedule Adherence (SA)** | $\text{SA} = \left(1 - \dfrac{\sum \vert t_a - t_s \vert}{\sum t_s}\right) \times 100$ | Mean absolute deviation from schedule | % |
| 5 | **Load Factor (LF)** | $\text{LF} = \dfrac{P}{C} \times 100$ | Passengers $P$ vs. Capacity $C$ | % |
| 6 | **Passenger Miles per Vehicle Mile (PM/VM)** | $\text{PM/VM} = \dfrac{\sum (P_i \times d_i)}{\sum (V_i \times d_i)}$ | Productivity / efficiency index | ratio |
| 7 | **Cost Recovery Ratio (CRR)** | $\text{CRR} = \dfrac{R}{C_{\text{op}}}$ | Revenue $R$ vs. Operating cost $C_{\text{op}}$ | ratio (0–1) |
| 8 | **Service Coverage Ratio** | $\text{SCR} = \dfrac{A_{\text{served}}}{A_{\text{total}}}$ | Area served within 400 m buffer | ratio |
| 9 | **Service Hours per Capita (SHC)** | $\text{SHC} = \dfrac{H_{\text{total}}}{N_{\text{pop}}}$ | Equity indicator | hr/person-yr |
| 10 | **Average Travel Speed** | $v = \dfrac{d}{t}$ | Door-to-door / in-vehicle speed | km/h |
| 11 | **Reliability Buffer Index (RBI)** | $\text{RBI} = \dfrac{(P_{95} - P_{50})}{P_{50}}$ | Extra time needed to be on time 95% of the time | ratio |
| 12 | **Vehicle Utilization** | $\text{VU} = \dfrac{\text{Revenue km}}{\text{Fleet size} \times \text{Days}}$ | Productivity of fleet | km/veh/day |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING RULE FOR KTU EXAMS:** When writing $\vert t_a - t_s \vert$ (absolute value) on your answer sheet, always enclose it in parentheses: $(\vert t_a - t_s \vert)$. This avoids marking confusion.

---

### 2.4 The Four Standard Categories (Engineering Classification)

#### Category A: **Coverage Standards**
- **Definition:** Spatial accessibility to the network.
- **Benchmark:** ≥ **90%** of residents within **400 m (1/4 mile)** of a transit stop in urban areas; **800 m (1/2 mile)** in rural.
- **Application:** Used in *Kerala's KSRTC* rural expansion planning.

#### Category B: **Frequency & Span Standards**
- **Definition:** Temporal availability.
- **Benchmarks:**
  - **Urban Core (Peak):** 10–15 min headway
  - **Urban Core (Off-Peak):** 20–30 min
  - **Suburban:** 30–60 min
  - **Rural:** 60–120 min
  - **Span:** Minimum **16 hours/day** (5 AM – 9 PM) for urban; **12 hours** for rural.

#### Category C: **Reliability & Punctuality Standards**
- **Definition:** Consistency of service.
- **Benchmarks:**
  - OTP ≥ **90%** (arrivals within ± 5 min)
  - Headway adherence ≥ **85%**
  - Missed trips ≤ **1%**

#### Category D: **Comfort, Safety & Information Standards**
- **Definition:** Quality of the passenger experience.
- **Benchmarks:**
  - **Peak load factor** ≤ **125%** (crush load) for buses; ≤ **150%** for rail.
  - **Off-peak LF** ≤ **70%**.
  - **ADA compliance** (wheelchair accessibility) for **100%** of fleet.
  - **Real-time information** at **major stops**.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Planning:** Standards guide *route additions, frequency increases, fleet procurement*.
- **Operations:** Standards drive *daily dispatching decisions*.
- **Funding:** Federal/state funding (e.g., India's **Smart Cities Mission**) requires adherence to standards.
- **Public Trust:** Transparent reporting builds **citizen confidence** in public transport.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Relationship Between Headway, Frequency, and Fleet Size

**Given:** A bus route of length $L$ = 30 km, average round-trip time $T_r$ = 90 minutes.

**Step 1: Define the variables.**
- $f$ = Frequency (vehicles per hour)
- $h$ = Headway (minutes between buses)
- $N$ = Number of buses required on the route (fleet size)

**Step 2: Relate headway to frequency.**

Since frequency is the number of vehicles per hour and headway is the time gap:

$$f = \frac{60}{h}$$

where $h$ is in minutes.

**Step 3: Relate cycle time to fleet size.**

The total round-trip time $T_r$ must be covered by a sequence of vehicles spaced at headway $h$. The number of vehicles needed to maintain frequency $f$ on a cycle of $T_r$ minutes is:

$$N = \frac{T_r}{h} = \frac{T_r \cdot f}{60}$$

**Step 4: Worked numerical example.**

Given $L = 30$ km, $T_r = 90$ min, target headway $h = 15$ min (peak).

$$N = \frac{90}{15} = 6 \text{ buses}$$

**Step 5: Verify using frequency.**

$$f = \frac{60}{15} = 4 \text{ veh/hr}$$

$$N = \frac{T_r \cdot f}{60} = \frac{90 \times 4}{60} = 6 \text{ buses} \quad \checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Trick:** If a question gives *cycle time* and *headway*, divide. If it gives *cycle time* and *frequency*, multiply by $T_r$ and divide by 60. **Do not mix units.**

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: On-Time Performance (OTP) with Worked Problem

**Problem:** A bus route had **120 scheduled trips** in a day. Of these, **18** were early, **15** were late, and the rest were on time. A trip is "on time" if it arrives **within 0 to +5 minutes** of the schedule. Calculate OTP.

**Step 1: Identify what counts as "on time."**

"On time" = arrived between **0 min early and +5 min late** relative to schedule.

**Step 2: Compute the number of on-time trips.**

$$N_{\text{total}} = 120$$

Early trips (arrived before schedule, e.g., 3 min early) = **18**

Late trips (arrived more than 5 min after schedule) = **15**

On-time trips = $120 - 18 - 15 = 87$

**Step 3: Apply the OTP formula.**

$$\text{OTP} = \frac{N_{\text{on-time}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100$$

$$\text{OTP} = \frac{87}{120} \times 100 = 72.5\%$$

**Step 4: Interpretation.**

A 72.5% OTP **fails the typical transit standard of ≥ 90%**. The agency must investigate: traffic congestion, crew issues, vehicle breakdowns, or schedule design problems.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Many students treat "late" and "early" as the same (i.e., "not on time"). That is **correct** in the strict definition, but be careful: some systems allow **0 to +3 min** (transit) vs. **0 to +5 min** (commuter rail). **Always read the threshold.**

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Load Factor (LF) and Crowding Assessment

**Problem:** A city bus has a **seated capacity of 40** and a **standing allowance of 20** (crush load = 60). During the morning peak, an average of **52 passengers** are on board. Calculate LF and assess service quality.

**Step 1: Define capacity.**

$$C = C_{\text{seated}} + C_{\text{stand}} = 40 + 20 = 60$$

**Step 2: Apply the LF formula.**

$$\text{LF} = \frac{P}{C} \times 100 = \frac{52}{60} \times 100 = 86.67\%$$

**Step 3: Apply standards.**

| LF Range | Service Quality |
|---|---|
| 0 – 50% | Underutilized (consider reducing service) |
| 50 – 80% | Comfortable, efficient |
| 80 – 100% | Crowded but acceptable |
| 100 – 125% | Standees, uncomfortable |
| > 125% | Unacceptable (violates standard) |

**Step 4: Conclusion.**

At **86.67%**, the bus is **crowded but within the acceptable peak-hour range**. The agency may consider **adding one more trip** during the peak window.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4: Service Coverage Ratio (SCR) for a City

**Problem:** A city has a **total area of 500 km²**. A 400 m buffer is drawn around every bus stop. The total area covered by these buffers is **320 km²**, but **40 km²** of this overlaps with non-residential land (parks, water bodies). Calculate the **effective SCR**.

**Step 1: Compute effective coverage area.**

$$A_{\text{eff}} = A_{\text{buffer}} - A_{\text{non-res}} = 320 - 40 = 280 \text{ km}^2$$

**Step 2: Apply the SCR formula.**

$$\text{SCR} = \frac{A_{\text{eff}}}{A_{\text{total}}} = \frac{280}{500} = 0.56 = 56\%$$

**Step 3: Standard check.**

The standard is **≥ 90%** for urban areas. The city **fails** this standard. **Recommendation:** Add routes to unserved neighborhoods.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Mapping Standards to Outcomes

| Standard Set | Metric | Target | Outcome if Failed | Engineering Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Coverage | SCR | ≥ 90% (urban) | Transit desert | Add new routes |
| Frequency | Peak headway | ≤ 15 min | Passenger wait fatigue | Add vehicles on route |
| Span | Service hours | ≥ 16 hr/day | Night workers stranded | Extend operating window |
| Reliability | OTP | ≥ 90% | Loss of trust | Add run time, TSP, dedicated lanes |
| Comfort | Peak LF | ≤ 125% | Crushed loads, accidents | Add trips, larger vehicles |
| Information | Real-time PIS at major stops | 100% | Ridership loss | Install AVL/GPS systems |
| Accessibility | ADA compliance | 100% | Legal liability, exclusion | Refurbish fleet with lifts/ramps |

---

### 3.6 Numerical Integration: A Multi-Metric City Performance Scorecard

Suppose a mid-sized Indian city reports the following monthly KPIs:

| Metric | Value | Standard | Pass/Fail |
|---|---|---|---|
| SCR | 85% | ≥ 90% | ❌ Fail |
| Peak headway | 18 min | ≤ 15 min | ❌ Fail |
| OTP | 88% | ≥ 90% | ❌ Fail |
| Peak LF | 115% | ≤ 125% | ✅ Pass |
| Span | 14 hr | ≥ 16 hr | ❌ Fail |
| Accessibility | 75% | 100% | ❌ Fail |

**Score = 1/5 = 20% compliance.** This indicates a **systemic service gap**, not isolated failures. The agency must launch a **comprehensive service improvement plan** rather than a piecemeal fix.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Flow: The Transit Service Standard Lifecycle

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Community Goals & Vision] --> B[Define Service Area and Type]
    B --> C[Set Quantitative Standards]
    C --> D[Deploy Transit Service]
    D --> E[Collect Operational Data]
    E --> F[Compute Evaluation Metrics]
    F --> G{Meet Standards?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Maintain and Monitor]
    G -- No --> I[Identify Gaps and Plan Improvements]
    I --> C
    H --> E
```

**Interpretation:** This closed-loop diagram shows that transit service standards are **not static**. They are continuously refined through the **Plan–Do–Check–Act (PDCA)** cycle, ensuring the system evolves with city needs.

---

### 4.2 Hierarchical Standards Tree (Three-Tier Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    T1[System Wide Standards Set by Board] --> T2[Route Level Standards Set by Planning Division]
    T1 --> T3[Stop Level Standards Set by Operations Manager]
    T2 --> T2a[Peak Headway Threshold]
    T2 --> T2b[Off Peak Headway Threshold]
    T2 --> T2c[Service Span Window]
    T2 --> T2d[Reliability Targets]
    T3 --> T3a[Shelter Provision]
    T3 --> T3b[Real Time Display Installation]
    T3 --> T3c[Lighting and Safety Features]
    T3 --> T3d[Accessibility Compliance]
```

**Interpretation:** This diagram shows how a **Board-level policy** cascades down into **route-level numerical targets** and finally into **stop-level physical infrastructure standards**. KTU students should be able to map any given standard (e.g., "wheelchair access") to the correct tier.

---

### 4.3 The Five-Pillar Service Evaluation Framework (TCRP)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Coverage Pillar] --> R[Ridership Outcome]
    P2[Frequency Pillar] --> R
    P3[Span Pillar] --> R
    P4[Reliability Pillar] --> R
    P5[Comfort and Speed Pillar] --> R
    R --> Decision{Standards Met?}
    Decision -- Yes --> Fund[Funding Approval and Expansion]
    Decision -- No --> Reform[Service Improvement Plan]
```

**Interpretation:** The **five pillars** jointly determine **ridership**, which in turn determines whether the system merits **continued funding** (e.g., Smart Cities Mission, JnNURM, AMRUT in India). Failing any one pillar can suppress ridership disproportionately.

---

### 4.4 Block-Level Architecture: Transit Performance Monitoring System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Data_Collection_Layer
        AVL[Automatic Vehicle Location AVL Sensors]
        APC[Automatic Passenger Counters APC]
        FARE[Electronic Fare Collection System]
        SOS[Passenger Complaint Module]
    end
    subgraph Processing_Layer
        DB[Central Data Warehouse]
        ENGINE[Performance Engine]
    end
    subgraph Output_Layer
        DASH[Real Time Operator Dashboard]
        PUB[Public Performance Report]
        ALERT[Service Alert System]
    end
    AVL --> DB
    APC --> DB
    FARE --> DB
    SOS --> DB
    DB --> ENGINE
    ENGINE --> DASH
    ENGINE --> PUB
    ENGINE --> ALERT
```

**Interpretation:** Modern transit agencies deploy a **three-layer architecture**: data collection (sensors & smart cards), processing (cloud-based analytics), and output (dashboards for operators, reports for the public, and alerts for passengers). This is the *digital backbone* of transit evaluation.

---

### 4.5 Standard-vs-Actual Gap Analysis Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M[Measure Actual Performance] --> C{Compare Against Standard}
    C -- Headway Gap --> H[Action Add Buses on Route]
    C -- Load Factor Gap --> L[Action Add Capacity or Trips]
    C -- OTP Gap --> O[Action Schedule Re design or Bus Lanes]
    C -- Coverage Gap --> CV[Action New Routes to Underserved Areas]
    C -- Span Gap --> S[Action Extend Operating Hours]
    C -- Accessibility Gap --> A[Action Refurbish Fleet with Ramps]
```

**Interpretation:** This is a **decision-support flowchart** that any KTU student should be able to draw in an exam. Each "gap" maps to a specific engineering intervention, demonstrating the **closed-loop linkage** between evaluation and operations.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define "Service Standards" in public transportation. List any four dimensions on which transit service standards are typically set.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Service Standards** in public transportation are pre-defined **quantitative thresholds and qualitative benchmarks** that establish the minimum acceptable level of service to be delivered to passengers.

**Four dimensions (any four, 0.5 mark each + 0.5 mark for definition = 3 marks):**

1. **Coverage** — spatial reach of the network.
2. **Frequency / Headway** — temporal availability of service.
3. **Span of Service** — hours per day the service operates.
4. **Reliability** — punctuality and on-time performance.
5. *(Optional)* Comfort, Safety, Accessibility, Information.

> [!NOTE]
> **Marking Pattern:** 1 mark for definition + 2 marks for listing 4 dimensions correctly.

---

#### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
**What is "On-Time Performance" (OTP)? How is it calculated, and what is the typical industry benchmark for urban bus transit?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
On-Time Performance is the percentage of scheduled transit trips that arrive at their timing points **within an acceptable deviation window** (typically **0 to +5 minutes** of the published schedule).

**Formula (1 mark):**

$$\text{OTP} = \frac{N_{\text{on-time}}}{N_{\text{total}}} \times 100$$

**Benchmark (1 mark):**
The typical industry benchmark for urban bus transit is **OTP ≥ 90%** (per TCRP and FTA guidance). Some premium BRT systems target **≥ 95%**.

---

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Modified]**

**(a)** Explain the **five pillars of transit service evaluation** as per the TCRP framework. For each pillar, state one standard metric and its typical benchmark value. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A bus route in a Tier-2 Indian city is **25 km long** with an **average round-trip time of 100 minutes**. The agency wants to maintain a **peak-hour headway of 12 minutes**. Calculate:
   (i) The required **frequency** in veh/hr.
   (ii) The **fleet size** required on the route.
   (iii) If the **actual observed headway** during a particular peak hour was 18 minutes due to a traffic signal failure, calculate the **headway deviation percentage**. Comment on whether this meets the standard. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Five Pillars of Transit Service Evaluation (7 marks):**

| # | Pillar | Metric | Typical Benchmark |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Coverage** | % population within 400 m of a stop | ≥ 90% (urban) |
| 2 | **Frequency** | Peak headway | ≤ 15 min (urban) |
| 3 | **Span of Service** | Service hours per day | ≥ 16 hr (urban) |
| 4 | **Reliability** | On-Time Performance (OTP) | ≥ 90% |
| 5 | **Comfort & Speed** | Peak Load Factor | ≤ 125% |

*(Each row: 1 mark for pillar name + 0.4 mark for metric = 7 marks total)*

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Required frequency (2 marks):**

$$f = \frac{60}{h} = \frac{60}{12} = 5 \text{ veh/hr}$$

**(ii) Fleet size required (2 marks):**

$$N = \frac{T_r}{h} = \frac{100}{12} = 8.33 \approx 9 \text{ buses}$$

*(Take ceiling: 9 buses)*

**Alternative check using frequency:**

$$N = \frac{T_r \cdot f}{60} = \frac{100 \times 5}{60} = 8.33 \approx 9 \text{ buses} \quad \checkmark$$

**(iii) Headway deviation (3 marks):**

$$\text{Deviation} = \frac{h_{\text{actual}} - h_{\text{scheduled}}}{h_{\text{scheduled}}} \times 100$$

$$\text{Deviation} = \frac{18 - 12}{12} \times 100 = 50\%$$

**Comment (included in 3 marks):**
A 50% headway deviation is **severe** and **fails the standard** (typical tolerance is ± 20%). Passengers experience much longer waits, leading to mode shift, ridership loss, and overcrowding on the next bus. **Recommendation:** Deploy Transit Signal Priority (TSP) or add a bus to the route.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** In part (b)(ii), students often forget to **take the ceiling** of 8.33. Always round **up** for fleet size, never down. If you write "8 buses," you lose 1 mark.

---

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** Differentiate between **Service Standards** and **Service Evaluation Standards**. Why is it important for a transit agency to maintain both? Discuss with the help of an example. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The table below shows data from a bus route for one week. Compute:
   (i) The **weekly average OTP**.
   (ii) The **average peak load factor**, given the bus capacity is **50 passengers**.
   (iii) Comment on whether the service meets the **typical transit standard benchmarks** and suggest **two engineering interventions**. **(7 marks)**

| Day | Total Trips | On-Time Trips | Peak-Hour Passengers |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mon | 100 | 85 | 58 |
| Tue | 100 | 88 | 55 |
| Wed | 100 | 82 | 60 |
| Thu | 100 | 90 | 52 |
| Fri | 100 | 87 | 56 |
| Sat | 80 | 76 | 45 |
| Sun | 60 | 58 | 30 |

---

#### **Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Differentiation (7 marks):**

| Aspect | Service Standards | Service Evaluation Standards |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Targets / Promises | Measurements / Scorecards |
| **Time Orientation** | Forward-looking | Backward-looking |
| **Set By** | Policy makers / Board | Operations / Planning data |
| **Example** | "Peak headway ≤ 15 min" | "Last week, average headway was 17 min" |
| **Purpose** | Set expectations for service quality | Assess whether standards are being met |

**Why both are important (3 marks):**
- **Without standards**, there is no benchmark to measure performance → accountability is lost.
- **Without evaluation**, the agency cannot know if the promises are being kept → public trust erodes.
- **Together**, they enable **continuous improvement** through the PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) cycle.

**Example (2 marks):**
Kerala's KSRTC sets a standard: *"Intercity buses will maintain ≥ 85% OTP."* The Evaluation Division measures this monthly. In July, the measured OTP was 78% — failing the standard. The agency responded by adding run time to schedules and deploying TSP in Trivandrum city, raising OTP to 91% in August.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Weekly average OTP (3 marks):**

Total trips = $100+100+100+100+100+80+60 = 640$

On-time trips = $85+88+82+90+87+76+58 = 566$

$$\text{OTP} = \frac{566}{640} \times 100 = 88.44\%$$

**(ii) Average peak load factor (2 marks):**

Total peak passengers = $58+55+60+52+56+45+30 = 356$

Average peak passengers per day = $\frac{356}{7} = 50.86$

$$\text{LF}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{50.86}{50} \times 100 = 101.71\%$$

**(iii) Comments & Interventions (2 marks):**

- **OTP (88.44%)** is **below the 90% benchmark** → ❌ **Fails the standard**.
- **Load Factor (101.71%)** is **above 100% but below the 125% peak threshold** → ✅ **Acceptable** but indicates crowding.

**Two engineering interventions:**
1. **Add 1–2 extra trips during peak hours** to reduce LF and improve OTP.
2. **Install Transit Signal Priority (TSP)** at major junctions to reduce delay and improve punctuality.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks Lost in Part (b):**
> 1. **Forgetting to total 7 days** in the OTP calculation (most students use 5 weekdays only — **loses 1 mark**).
> 2. **Confusing OTP and load factor units** — OTP is **%**, LF is also **%**, but their interpretation differs.
> 3. **Not writing units** (veh/hr, %, min) → KTU deducts **0.5 mark per missing unit**.

---

---

### TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this section and stick it on your wall before the exam.**

- [ ] **Service Standards = Targets** (forward-looking); **Evaluation Standards = Results** (backward-looking).
- [ ] **Five Pillars (TCRP):** Coverage, Frequency, Span, Reliability, Comfort.
- [ ] **Headway formula:** $h = 60/f$ (always in **minutes**).
- [ ] **Fleet size:** $N = T_r / h$ — always **round up**.
- [ ] **OTP formula:** $(\text{on-time trips} / \text{total trips}) \times 100$, threshold window = **0 to +5 min**.
- [ ] **Load Factor threshold:** Peak ≤ **125%** (bus), ≤ **150%** (rail).
- [ ] **Coverage threshold:** ≥ **90%** of urban population within **400 m** of a stop.
- [ ] **Span threshold:** ≥ **16 hours/day** (urban), ≥ **12 hours/day** (rural).
- [ ] **Schedule Adherence** uses mean absolute deviation — know the formula.
- [ ] **Reliability Buffer Index (RBI)** uses **95th and 50th percentile** travel times.
- [ ] **Service Coverage Ratio (SCR)** must subtract **non-residential areas** from buffer coverage.
- [ ] **PDCA Cycle** is the backbone of transit service improvement — draw it in **any "explain" question**.
- [ ] **ADA / Accessibility** = **100%** of fleet must be wheelchair-accessible.
- [ ] **Cost Recovery Ratio** is **NOT** a service standard (it's a financial metric) — examiners may trick you.
- [ ] **Three Tiers of Standards:** System-wide → Route-level → Stop-level. Map every example to a tier.
- [ ] **Remember units in every numerical answer** — KTU deducts marks for missing units.
- [ ] **Final exam mantra:** *"Standards are promises. Evaluation is accountability. Engineering is the bridge between them."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Requirements and Collection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET833 - PUBLIC TRANSPORTATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Data Requirements and Collection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Public Transportation Systems (PTS)**, **Data Requirements and Collection** refers to the systematic identification, classification, acquisition, and processing of quantitative and qualitative information essential for planning, designing, operating, evaluating, and improving public transit infrastructure and services. It forms the foundational **Phase 1 (Inventory & Data Gathering)** of the classical **Transportation Planning Process (TPP)** as defined in KTU 2024 Scheme.

The data gathered underpins the **Four-Step Model**: Trip Generation → Trip Distribution → Modal Split → Trip Assignment, and supports performance benchmarking using metrics such as **Passenger Kilometers Traveled (PKT)**, **Load Factor (LF)**, and **Service Frequency**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Data collection is NOT just about numbers — it is a structured engineering exercise comprising **Inventory Studies, Operational Studies, and Opinion/Behavioural Studies**, each with distinct methodologies, accuracies, and cost implications.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a doctor diagnosing a patient. Before prescribing treatment, the doctor MUST collect:
- **Medical history** (Secondary data) — patient's past records
- **Blood test reports** (Primary data) — freshly collected
- **Symptoms interview** (Opinion survey) — patient's perception

**Public Transportation Planning works identically.** Before designing a BRT corridor or re-routing a KSRTC service, a transport engineer MUST collect:
- **Census & socio-economic data** (Secondary)
- **Origin-Destination surveys** (Primary)
- **Rider perception surveys** (Opinion)

Without this data, any planning decision is **"blindfolded engineering"** — theoretically elegant but practically catastrophic.

---

### 1.3 Categories of Data Requirements

Public transportation data is broadly classified into three pillars:

| Pillar | Sub-Category | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **System Supply Data** | Physical Infrastructure | Route length, stop spacing, depot locations |
| **System Supply Data** | Operational Data | Frequency, headway, fleet size, schedule adherence |
| **System Demand Data** | Travel Behaviour | O-D matrices, mode choice, trip purpose |
| **System Demand Data** | User Characteristics | Income, age, vehicle ownership |
| **Environmental Data** | Land Use | Population density, employment zones |
| **Environmental Data** | Network Data | Road geometry, intersection control |

> [!NOTE]
> **Bold Key Metric:** The **minimum accuracy** required for transportation planning data is **±10% at 95% confidence level** (as per IRC:SP:19 and KTU 2024 guidelines). This threshold dictates sample sizes for all primary surveys.

---

### 1.4 Why is Data Collection Critical? (The "Why")

- **Justifies Investment:** DPR (Detailed Project Report) for any metro/BRT requires statistically validated data.
- **Enables Modelling:** Without O-D data, the Gravity Model cannot be calibrated.
- **Performance Evaluation:** KPIs like **Passenger per Hour per Direction (PPHPD)** are entirely data-dependent.
- **Policy Decisions:** Subsidy calculations, fare restructuring, and route rationalization all originate from ridership data.
- **Legal & Audit Compliance:** KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE) emphasizes evidence-based decision making.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Data Collection Methods

Data collection is bifurcated into two super-classes:

#### A. Primary Data (First-Hand Collection)
Data collected **fresh** for a specific study purpose. High accuracy, high cost, time-intensive.

**Primary Techniques Used in PTS:**

1. **Origin-Destination (O-D) Surveys**
   - *Home Interview Survey (HIS)*
   - *Roadside Interview Survey (RSI)*
   - *Postcard/Trip Diary Method*
   - *License Plate Matching*
   - *GPS-Based Tracking*

2. **Traffic Volume Counts**
   - Manual (with enumerators)
   - Automatic (pneumatic tubes, video, inductive loops)
   - Often used to establish **Annual Average Daily Traffic (AADT)**

3. **Ridership & Passenger Counts**
   - On-board surveys
   - Farebox data analysis
   - AFC (Automatic Fare Collection) data from smart cards

4. **Travel Time & Delay Studies**
   - Floating Car Method (Average speed technique)
   - Moving Observer Method (for bus routes)

5. **Stated Preference (SP) Surveys**
   - Captures *hypothetical* choices — crucial for **Mode Choice Modelling** in the Four-Step Model.

6. **Revealed Preference (RP) Surveys**
   - Captures *actual* travel choices.

---

#### B. Secondary Data (Existing Records)
Pre-collected data from government/private agencies. Lower cost, faster, but may lack granularity.

**Common Sources for Kerala/Bharat Context:**
- **Census of India** (population, workers, vehicle ownership)
- **KSRTC / Kochi Metro operational records**
- **Motor Vehicle Department (MVD) registration data**
- **Economic Surveys** (income, employment)
- **Master Plans / Development Plans** (land use)
- **NCRB / Police data** (accidents)
- **Google Mobility Reports, MoHUA reports**

---

### 2.2 Sample Size Determination (The Backbone of Primary Surveys)

The **sample size (n)** for any transportation survey is determined using statistical principles. For large populations (typical in transit planning), the **infinite population formula** is used:

$$n = \frac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{e^2}$$

Where:
- $Z$ = Standard normal variate (1.96 for 95% confidence, 2.576 for 99%)
- $p$ = Estimated population proportion (use 0.5 for maximum sample size if unknown)
- $e$ = Acceptable margin of error (typically 0.05)

For **finite populations** (e.g., specific bus route passengers), apply the **finite population correction (FPC)**:

$$n_{adj} = \frac{n}{1 + \dfrac{n-1}{N}}$$

Where $N$ = total population size.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb for KTU:** For urban O-D surveys in Indian cities, minimum sample size = **1% to 2% of total trips** or **1 household per 500–1000 population**, whichever yields a larger sample.

---

### 2.3 Expansion Factor (The Critical Bridge)

Once data is sampled, it must be **expanded to represent the entire population**. The expansion factor is the inverse of the sampling fraction:

$$EF = \frac{N}{n}$$

For **stratified sampling** (e.g., stratifying by income groups), the **stratum-specific expansion factor** is:

$$EF_h = \frac{N_h}{n_h}$$

Total estimated trips for the whole population:

$$T_{total} = \sum_{h=1}^{L} (EF_h \times \bar{x}_h \times n_h)$$

Where $L$ = number of strata, $\bar{x}_h$ = mean trip rate in stratum $h$.

---

### 2.4 Ridership and Performance Metrics (Key Formulas)

| Metric | Formula | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Passenger Kilometers Traveled (PKT)** | $\sum (P_i \times d_i)$ | passenger-km | Service productivity |
| **Place Kilometers Offered (PKO)** | $\sum (\text{Seats} \times d_i)$ | seat-km | Capacity provision |
| **Load Factor (LF)** | $\dfrac{PKT}{PKO} \times 100$ | % | Route efficiency |
| **Passenger per Hour per Direction (PPHPD)** | $\dfrac{\text{Total Passengers}}{\text{Hours} \times \text{Directions}}$ | pphpd | Lane capacity design |
| **Average Trip Length (ATL)** | $\dfrac{PKT}{\text{Total Passengers}}$ | km | Route design |
| **Headway (H)** | $\dfrac{60}{f}$ where $f$ = frequency | minutes | Service planning |
| **Cycle Time (CT)** | $2 \times (\text{Run time} + \text{Recovery time})$ | minutes | Fleet calculation |
| **Fleet Size (FS)** | $\dfrac{CT \times f}{60}$ | buses | Procurement |

---

### 2.5 Sampling Techniques Used in PTS

1. **Simple Random Sampling (SRS)** — every unit has equal chance.
2. **Stratified Sampling** — population divided into homogeneous strata (e.g., by ward, income).
3. **Systematic Sampling** — every $k^{th}$ unit chosen.
4. **Cluster Sampling** — geographic clusters (e.g., selecting 5 wards out of 50).
5. **Multi-Stage Sampling** — combination (e.g., Ward → Household → Individual).

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:** In the **Kochi Metro Rail DPR (2011)** and **Kerala State Urban Transport Project (KSUDP)**, a combination of **Stratified + Multi-Stage Sampling** was employed for O-D surveys, ensuring representation across socio-economic groups and geographic zones.

---

### 2.6 Data Collection Errors and Quality Control

| Error Type | Description | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| **Sampling Error** | Difference between sample and population | Adequate $n$, stratified design |
| **Non-Response Bias** | Refusal/absence of respondents | Call-back, incentives |
| **Response Bias** | False/inaccurate answers | Trained enumerators, validation |
| **Coding Error** | Data entry mistakes | Double entry, automated validation |
| **Temporal Bias** | Survey conducted in atypical period | Conduct in **neutral months** (avoiding festivals/exams) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Assuming all O-D survey data is accurate. Reality: **Roadside Interview Surveys (RSI)** have a known **bias toward commercial vehicles and men**, which must be corrected using expansion factors.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Sample Size for a Home Interview Survey

**Problem Statement (Typical KTU-style):** The Corporation of Kochi plans a Home Interview Survey. The city has **2,00,000 households**. Past studies suggest that **40% of households are transit users** ($p = 0.40$). Determine the required sample size for a **95% confidence level** and **±5% margin of error**.

---

**Step 1: Identify the parameters**
- Confidence level = 95% → $Z = 1.96$
- Estimated proportion $p = 0.40$
- Margin of error $e = 0.05$
- Population $N = 2,00,000$

**Step 2: Apply the infinite population formula**

$$n_0 = \frac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot (1-p)}{e^2}$$

**Step 3: Substitute values**

$$n_0 = \frac{(1.96)^2 \times 0.40 \times (1-0.40)}{(0.05)^2}$$

**Step 4: Square the Z-value**

$$(1.96)^2 = 3.8416$$

**Step 5: Compute $p(1-p)$**

$$0.40 \times 0.60 = 0.24$$

**Step 6: Compute $e^2$**

$$(0.05)^2 = 0.0025$$

**Step 7: Substitute back**

$$n_0 = \frac{3.8416 \times 0.24}{0.0025}$$

**Step 8: Numerator**

$$3.8416 \times 0.24 = 0.921984$$

**Step 9: Final division**

$$n_0 = \frac{0.921984}{0.0025} = 368.7936 \approx 369$$

**Step 10: Apply Finite Population Correction (since $N = 200,000$)**

$$n = \frac{n_0}{1 + \dfrac{n_0 - 1}{N}}$$

**Step 11: Compute the denominator**

$$1 + \frac{369 - 1}{2,00,000} = 1 + \frac{368}{2,00,000} = 1 + 0.00184 = 1.00184$$

**Step 12: Final sample size**

$$n = \frac{369}{1.00184} = 368.32 \approx 369 \text{ households}$$

**Conclusion:** Since $n_0 < 5\%$ of $N$, the FPC is negligible. **Sample required ≈ 369 households.**

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Expansion Factor for Stratified Sampling

**Problem:** A city is divided into 3 zones. Survey details:

| Zone | Households ($N_h$) | Sampled ($n_h$) | Mean trips/household ($\bar{x}_h$) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Central (CBD) | 50,000 | 200 | 3.2 |
| Suburban | 1,00,000 | 300 | 2.5 |
| Peripheral | 50,000 | 100 | 1.8 |

**Compute total estimated daily trips in the city.**

---

**Step 1: Compute stratum-specific expansion factors**

$$EF_{CBD} = \frac{50000}{200} = 250$$

$$EF_{Sub} = \frac{100000}{300} = 333.33$$

$$EF_{Peri} = \frac{50000}{100} = 500$$

**Step 2: Estimate trips in each stratum**

$$T_{CBD} = EF_{CBD} \times \bar{x}_{CBD} \times n_{CBD} = 250 \times 3.2 \times 200 = 1,60,000 \text{ trips}$$

$$T_{Sub} = 333.33 \times 2.5 \times 300 = 2,49,997.5 \approx 2,50,000 \text{ trips}$$

$$T_{Peri} = 500 \times 1.8 \times 100 = 90,000 \text{ trips}$$

**Step 3: Sum all strata**

$$T_{Total} = 1,60,000 + 2,50,000 + 90,000 = 5,00,000 \text{ trips/day}$$

**Step 4: Verify via aggregate method**

$$n_{total} = 200 + 300 + 100 = 600$$
$$N_{total} = 50,000 + 1,00,000 + 50,000 = 2,00,000$$
$$EF_{overall} = \frac{2,00,000}{600} = 333.33$$
$$\bar{x}_{overall} = \frac{(200 \times 3.2) + (300 \times 2.5) + (100 \times 1.8)}{600} = \frac{640 + 750 + 180}{600} = \frac{1570}{600} = 2.6167$$
$$T_{Total} = 333.33 \times 2.6167 \times 600 = 5,00,004 \approx 5,00,000 \text{ trips}$$

Both methods agree. ✓

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Fleet Size for a Bus Route

**Given:**
- Round trip time = 80 minutes
- Recovery time = 10 minutes (laying over)
- Desired frequency = 6 buses/hour

**Step 1: Compute Cycle Time**

$$CT = 80 + 10 = 90 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 2: Compute Headway**

$$H = \frac{60}{f} = \frac{60}{6} = 10 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 3: Compute Fleet Size**

$$FS = \frac{CT \times f}{60} = \frac{90 \times 6}{60} = \frac{540}{60} = 9 \text{ buses}$$

**Conclusion:** **9 buses** are required to maintain 10-minute headway.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: When to Use What Data Source

| Decision Needed | Recommended Data Source | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| Feasibility of a new BRT | O-D Survey + Land Use | Need demand patterns |
| Frequency adjustment on existing route | AFC / Ridership counts | Real-time demand data |
| Fare revision | Willingness-to-pay (SP Survey) | Captures price elasticity |
| Depot location | Spatial analysis of O-D + Land use | Minimizes dead mileage |
| New route planning | Home Interview Survey | Origin-Destination matrix |
| Performance audit | PKT, PKO, LF, schedule adherence | Operational KPIs |
| Environmental impact | Modal split, vehicle-km | Emissions calculation |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture: Public Transportation Data Collection Framework

The following **Mermaid Block-Level Functional Architecture** illustrates the complete data collection ecosystem, ensuring no node ID uses reserved keywords, and all labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[USER DEMAND SIDE] --> B[SOCIO ECONOMIC DATA]
```
