## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — 3 MARK QUESTIONS (Short Answer)

#### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List the various types of data required for the planning of a public transportation system. Briefly explain the difference between primary and secondary data.**

**Model Answer:**

Public transportation planning requires three broad categories of data:

1. **Travel Demand Data** — O-D matrix, trip rates, mode choice, socio-economic characteristics of users.
2. **Transportation Supply Data** — Route network, fleet size, schedules, headways, depot locations, fares.
3. **External/Contextual Data** — Land use patterns, population density, employment zones, topography, weather.

**Difference between Primary and Secondary Data:**

| Aspect | Primary Data | Secondary Data |
|---|---|---|
| **Source** | Collected fresh for the study | Already available from past records |
| **Cost** | High | Low |
| **Time** | Time-consuming | Quick to obtain |
| **Accuracy** | High (study-specific) | May be outdated or aggregated |
| **Example** | O-D survey, household interview | Census data, KSRTC records |
| **Reliability for KTU exams** | Definitive | Supplementary |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner Tip:** Always cite at least one **Kerala-specific example** (KSRTC, Kochi Metro, MoHUA) to score full marks.

---

#### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**What is a Home Interview Survey? Mention any two advantages and two limitations.**

**Model Answer:**

A **Home Interview Survey (HIS)** is a primary data collection method where trained enumerators visit selected households in person and gather detailed information about the travel behaviour of all members of the household over a specified reference period (usually the previous day).

**Information collected includes:**
- Trip purpose, origin, destination, mode, departure time, arrival time
- Socio-economic profile (age, income, vehicle ownership, occupation)
- Frequency of transit usage

**Advantages:**
1. Captures **detailed and accurate** trip data, including non-motorized and walk trips often missed in roadside surveys.
2. Allows collection of **socio-economic correlates** essential for category analysis and trip generation modelling.

**Limitations:**
1. **Expensive and time-consuming** — requires large enumerator force and supervision.
2. Suffers from **non-response bias** (households absent or refusing), and **trip under-reporting** for short or non-routine trips.

---

### PART B — 14 MARK QUESTIONS (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain in detail the various methods of conducting **Origin-Destination (O-D) surveys** in public transportation planning. Discuss the **merits and demerits** of each method. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A city with **5,00,000 households** wishes to conduct a Home Interview Survey. Past experience suggests that **30% of the population are transit users**. Determine the sample size for **95% confidence level** and **±5% error**. If the survey must achieve **±3% error**, recalculate. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **MODEL SOLUTION**

#### **Part (a) — O-D Survey Methods**

There are **six major methods** of conducting O-D surveys:

**1. Home Interview Survey (HIS)**
- *Procedure:* Enumerators visit sampled households and interview members.
- *Merits:* High accuracy, captures all modes and trip purposes, includes socio-economic data.
- *Demerits:* Expensive, time-consuming, respondent fatigue, non-response bias.

**2. Roadside Interview Survey (RSI)**
- *Procedure:* Vehicles are stopped at a roadside station and drivers interviewed.
- *Merits:* Captures through-traffic, cheaper than HIS.
- *Demerits:* Skips walk/cycle trips, biased against women/children, traffic disruption.

**3. Postcard / Mail-Back Survey**
- *Procedure:* Postcards distributed at strategic points and mailed back.
- *Merits:* Very low cost, large coverage.
- *Demerits:* Very low response rate (~10-20%), self-selection bias.

**4. Trip Diary Method**
- *Procedure:* Respondents record every trip in a diary over a day/week.
- *Merits:* Captures frequent trips, longitudinal data possible.
- *Demerits:* Diary fatigue, recall error.

**5. License Plate Matching**
- *Procedure:* Cameras record plates at entry/exit of cordon; matching yields O-D.
- *Merits:* Automated, large sample, unbiased.
- *Demerits:* Only for vehicles, privacy concerns, no purpose/mode data.

**6. GPS-Based Tracking / Mobile Phone Data**
- *Procedure:* GPS in vehicles/phones tracks movement; algorithms infer O-D.
- *Merits:* Massive samples, real-time, passive.
- *Demerits:* Privacy issues, requires data processing infrastructure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Modern Trend:** GPS and mobile phone data are increasingly the **preferred methods** in Indian smart-city projects (e.g., MoHUA's **ISCD** program).

---

#### **Part (b) — Sample Size Calculation**

**Given:**
- $N = 5,00,000$ households
- $p = 0.30$, $q = 1-p = 0.70$
- $Z = 1.96$ (95% confidence)
- $e_1 = 0.05$, $e_2 = 0.03$

**Step 1: Sample size for $e_1 = 0.05$**

$$n_0 = \frac{Z^2 \cdot p \cdot q}{e_1^2} = \frac{(1.96)^2 \times 0.30 \times 0.70}{(0.05)^2}$$

$$n_0 = \frac{3.8416 \times 0.21}{0.0025} = \frac{0.806736}{0.0025} = 322.69 \approx 323$$

**Step 2: Apply FPC (since $N$ is finite but $n_0/N < 5\%$, negligible)**

$$n \approx 323 \text{ households}$$

**Step 3: Sample size for $e_2 = 0.03$**

$$n_0 = \frac{(1.96)^2 \times 0.30 \times 0.70}{(0.03)^2} = \frac{0.806736}{0.0009} = 896.37 \approx 897$$

**Step 4: Conclusion**

| Error Level | Sample Size | Practical Implication |
|---|---|---|
| ±5% | 323 HH | Affordable for routine planning |
| ±3% | 897 HH | Required for DPR-grade accuracy |
| ±1% | 8067 HH | Reserved for metro-level mega projects |

**Inference:** Reducing the error from 5% to 3% **nearly triples** the survey cost — a classic **cost-accuracy trade-off** in transport planning.

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Discuss the **role of secondary data sources** in public transportation planning, citing at least **five specific sources** relevant to the Indian/Kerala context. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The ridership data for a KSRTC route is given below. Compute the **Average Trip Length, PKT, and Load Factor** for a single trip of the bus. **Seating capacity = 50, Total standing allowed = 10, Trip distance = 60 km, Total passengers during the trip (boarding-alighting data shown):** Stops at 0, 10, 20, 30, 40, 50, 60 km carry 60, 55, 48, 40, 30, 18, 0 passengers respectively between consecutive stops. **(7 Marks)**

---

### **MODEL SOLUTION**

#### **Part (a) — Secondary Data Sources**

Secondary data is **pre-collected information** that already exists in government archives, corporate records, or published reports. It is the **first step** in any transportation study because it is **cheaper, faster, and provides macro-level context**.

**Five Major Sources:**

1. **Census of India (Decennial)** — Population, workers, age-sex distribution, vehicle ownership by household, migration data. Census data is used to **stratify samples** and validate primary survey findings.

2. **KSRTC / Kochi Metro Operational Records** — Route-wise ridership, farebox revenue, schedule adherence, fuel consumption. These give the **supply-side picture**.

3. **Motor Vehicle Department (MVD) Registration Data** — Vehicle population by category, fuel type, age distribution. Essential for **modal split** and **emission modelling**.

4. **Master Plans / City Development Plans (CDP)** — Land use zoning, building regulations, projected population, employment densities. Critical for **trip generation** and **land use-transport interaction models**.

5. **MoHUA / KSHIP / KSUDP Reports** — Government urban transport project documents containing historical surveys, traffic studies, and investment data.

**Additional Source:** Google Mobility Reports, OpenStreetMap, IIT Madras traffic data portals.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limitation:** Secondary data may be **dated** (Census is decennial) or **aggregated** (ward-level only), necessitating **primary data supplementation**.

---

#### **Part (b) — Performance Metrics Calculation**

**Given:**
- Seating capacity = 50, Standing = 10 → **PKO factor = 60 passengers × 60 km = 3,600 pax-km**
- Section distances = 10 km each (6 sections)
- Passengers in each section: 60, 55, 48, 40, 30, 18

---

**Step 1: Compute Passenger-Kilometers Traveled (PKT)**

$$PKT = \sum (P_i \times d_i)$$

$$PKT = (60 \times 10) + (55 \times 10) + (48 \times 10) + (40 \times 10) + (30 \times 10) + (18 \times 10)$$

$$PKT = 600 + 550 + 480 + 400 + 300 + 180 = 2,510 \text{ passenger-km}$$

**Step 2: Compute Place-Kilometers Offered (PKO)**

$$PKO = 60 \text{ pax} \times 60 \text{ km} = 3,600 \text{ seat-km}$$

**Step 3: Compute Load Factor (LF)**

$$LF = \frac{PKT}{PKO} \times 100 = \frac{2,510}{3,600} \times 100 = 69.72\%$$

**Step 4: Compute Average Trip Length (ATL)**

**Total boardings** = 60 (initial passengers boarding at start) — but in transit practice, we count passengers in each section.

$$ATL = \frac{PKT}{\text{Average passengers per section}}$$

$$\text{Average passengers} = \frac{60+55+48+40+30+18}{6} = \frac{251}{6} = 41.83$$

$$ATL = \frac{2,510}{41.83} = 60.0 \text{ km}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note:** ATL here = total trip distance (60 km) because passengers were continuously on board from start. In real networks with continuous boarding/alighting, ATL would be lower.

**Step 5: Tabulate Results**

| Metric | Value | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| **PKT** | 2,510 pax-km | Productivity per trip |
| **PKO** | 3,600 seat-km | Capacity offered |
| **Load Factor** | 69.72% | Healthy (target > 70%) |
| **ATL** | 60 km | Long intercity trip |

**Inference:** The route is operating at **~70% load factor**, considered efficient for a long-distance KSRTC service. To improve LF, KSRTC could consider **express service with fewer stops** to capture inter-city demand.

---

### **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks in "Data Requirements & Collection" Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing O-D survey with Traffic Count Surveys** — they are NOT the same. O-D = where trips start/end. Traffic count = how many vehicles.
> 2. **Forgetting to apply FPC** — Examiners deduct 2 marks when the finite population correction is missed in 14-mark problems.
> 3. **Using $p = 0.5$ incorrectly** — If the problem does not specify $p$, use 0.5 for **maximum sample size**, but STATE this assumption clearly.
> 4. **Mixing up PKT and PKO** — PKT uses **passengers** × distance; PKO uses **seats** × distance. Confusing these leads to wrong LF.
> 5. **Ignoring the 95% confidence assumption** — Always mention $Z = 1.96$ explicitly in derivations.
> 6. **Not mentioning Kerala/India-specific examples** — Examiners reward **contextual answers** (KSRTC, Kochi Metro, KSUDP) with grace marks.
> 7. **Skipping units in formulas** — Always state units (pax-km, %, pphpd) for full credit.
> 8. **Listing data without categorization** — Always group into **Supply, Demand, and Environmental** for logical structure.

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Data Requirements and Collection**

- **Data is the foundation** of transportation planning; without it, the Four-Step Model collapses.
- **Three pillars**: System Supply, System Demand, and Environmental Context.
- **Primary data** = fresh, expensive, accurate (HIS, RSI, AFC, GPS, SP/RP surveys).
- **Secondary data** = existing, cheap, may be outdated (Census, MVD, Master Plans).
- **Sample size formula** (infinite): $n_0 = \frac{Z^2 p q}{e^2}$.
- **FPC formula**: $n = \frac{n_0}{1 + \frac{n_0-1}{N}}$.
- **Expansion Factor**: $EF = N/n$ — multiplies sample to estimate population.
- **Stratified sampling** is preferred for heterogeneous populations (e.g., zones, income groups).
- **O-D surveys** are the **most critical** primary surveys for transit planning.
- **SP surveys** = hypothetical choices; **RP surveys** = actual choices.
- **PKT** uses passengers; **PKO** uses seats; **LF = PKT/PKO × 100%**.
- **Headway** = 60/f; **Cycle Time = Run time + Recovery time**; **Fleet Size = CT × f / 60**.
- **Minimum accuracy standard** = ±10% at 95% confidence.
- **Non-response bias** and **temporal bias** are key survey errors to mitigate.
- **Modern trends**: GPS tracking, mobile phone data, AFC smart card analytics, AI-based origin-destination inference.
- **Always cite Kerala/India context** in KTU answers for contextual depth marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Frequency and Headway Distributions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET833 - PUBLIC TRANSPORTATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Frequency and Headway Distributions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In **Public Transportation Systems (PTS)**, the operational performance of any line-haul transit service (bus, tram, metro, BRT) is fundamentally governed by two interlinked service variables:

> [!IMPORTANT] **Frequency (f)** — The rate at which transit vehicles (buses, trains, or trams) depart from a given stop or terminal, expressed in **vehicles per hour (vph)**.  
> **Mathematically:** $f = \dfrac{N}{T}$, where $N$ = number of vehicles dispatched in time period $T$.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Headway (h)** — The time interval (in **minutes or seconds**) between the arrivals of two successive transit vehicles at a fixed point (stop, station, or terminal) travelling in the same direction on the same route.  
> **Mathematically:** $h = \dfrac{T}{N} = \dfrac{1}{f}$.

**Headway Distribution** is therefore the **statistical description of the variability** in successive time intervals between transit vehicle arrivals, which in real-world operations is never perfectly constant due to traffic signals, dwell-time fluctuations, driver behaviour, and passenger demand surges.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are standing at a KSRTC bus stop in Thiruvananthapuram waiting for a city bus to Technopark. If the schedule says a bus comes "every 10 minutes":

- The **frequency** is $f = 6$ buses/hour (the *supply rate*).
- The **headway** is $h = 10$ minutes (the *spacing between consecutive buses*).
- If buses are perfectly punctual, you will never wait more than 10 minutes.
- But in reality, traffic signals at Pattom, slow passenger boarding at Vellayambalam, and an elderly passenger at Kesavadasapuram cause the 2nd bus to come after **13 minutes** and the 3rd bus after only **6 minutes** — the headway is now a *random variable* with a *distribution*.

This variability is precisely what **Headway Distribution Analysis** quantifies, and it directly determines how long the average passenger waits at the stop.

## 1.3 Standard Performance Metrics in Bold

- **Passenger Waiting Time (W)** — average time a passenger spends waiting at a stop.
- **Load Factor (LF)** — ratio of passengers to vehicle seating capacity.
- **Coefficient of Variation of Headway (CVₕ)** — normalized measure of headway irregularity.
- **Schedule Adherence (SA)** — deviation of actual arrival from scheduled arrival.

> [!NOTE] **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight** — Frequency and Headway Distributions form the **analytical backbone** of *transit line capacity analysis* and *service reliability planning*. Mastery of the relation $f = 1/h$ and its stochastic extensions is mandatory for ESE Module 1 questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Deterministic Foundation: The Frequency–Headway Inverse

At the most basic level, transit operations assume **deterministic, perfectly regular service**. Under this assumption:

$$f = \frac{1}{h} \quad \text{and} \quad h = \frac{1}{f}$$

If a transit agency operates **12 buses per hour**, the scheduled headway is exactly **5 minutes**. This forms the **scheduled (deterministic) baseline** against which real-world irregularity is measured.

## 2.2 Why Headways Are NOT Deterministic in Practice

In the field, headways are *random variables* because of the following operational realities:

- **Traffic signal delays** at urban intersections cause stochastic stoppage times.
- **Dwell time variability** — boarding/alighting differs at every stop.
- **Driver behaviour variation** — acceleration, braking, lane-changing patterns.
- **Passenger demand surges** — peak-hour crowding slows boarding.
- **Vehicle mechanical issues** — failures, door malfunctions.
- **Bunching (platooning)** — a slow bus is caught up by the bus behind it, reducing the trailing headway to near zero and stretching the leading headway.

## 2.3 Statistical Distributions Used to Model Headways

### A. Deterministic (Constant) Headway
- Used in **schedule-based metro systems** (Kochi Metro) and theoretical models.
- All inter-arrival times are equal: $h(t) = h_0$.

### B. Poisson Distribution (Exponential Inter-Arrival Times)
- Models a **memoryless, completely random** arrival process.
- Inter-arrival times follow an **exponential distribution**: $P(h > t) = e^{-\lambda t}$, where $\lambda = f$.
- Mean headway: $E[h] = 1/\lambda$.
- Variance: $\text{Var}(h) = 1/\lambda^{2}$.
- $CV_{h} = 1$ (high variability).
- Useful when vehicles arrive **independently** without coordination.

### C. Normal (Gaussian) Distribution
- Models **small random perturbations** around a scheduled headway $h_{0}$.
- $h \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu_{h}, \sigma_{h}^{2})$.
- Appropriate for **schedule-based systems** with minor disruptions.
- $CV_{h} = \sigma_{h} / \mu_{h} < 1$ typically.

### D. Erlang (Gamma) Distribution
- Models headways as the **sum of $k$ independent exponential phases** (e.g., multiple traffic signals).
- PDF: $f(h) = \dfrac{\lambda^{k} h^{k-1} e^{-\lambda h}}{(k-1)!}$ for $h \geq 0$.
- Mean: $E[h] = k/\lambda$.
- $CV_{h} = 1/\sqrt{k}$ — lower variability as $k$ increases.

## 2.4 Passenger Waiting Time Under Stochastic Headways

The most operationally important consequence of headway variability is its effect on **average passenger waiting time**.

### Case 1: Deterministic headway with random passenger arrivals
If passengers arrive uniformly at the stop (Poisson arrivals) and headway is constant $h$:

$$E[W] = \frac{h}{2}$$

### Case 2: Deterministic headway with scheduled passenger arrivals
If passengers time their arrival to the schedule (uncommon):

$$E[W] = 0 \text{ (best case)}$$

### Case 3: Stochastic headway with random passenger arrivals (General Renewal Theory)
This is the **most realistic case** in urban bus operations. The average waiting time is:

$$E[W] = \frac{E[h^{2}]}{2 \, E[h]} = \frac{E[h]}{2} \left(1 + C_{v,h}^{2}\right)$$

where $C_{v,h} = \sigma_{h} / \mu_{h}$ is the **coefficient of variation of headway**.

> [!IMPORTANT] **Key Insight** — As $C_{v,h}$ increases (more irregular service), passenger waiting time **grows non-linearly**. Doubling the variability of headway can more than double the average wait.

### Case 4: Schedule-based headway with arrival in schedule window
For systems with published timetables where passengers arrive randomly in a scheduling window $\Delta t$:

$$E[W] = \frac{\Delta t}{2} + \text{schedule deviation penalty}$$

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield for ESE)

| # | Formula | Description | Standard Units |
|---|---------|-------------|----------------|
| 1 | $f = N / T$ | Frequency of service | vph (vehicles/hour) |
| 2 | $h = T / N = 1/f$ | Scheduled (mean) headway | minutes or seconds |
| 3 | $E[W] = h/2$ | Mean wait, deterministic h, random passengers | minutes |
| 4 | $E[W] = E[h^{2}] / (2 E[h])$ | Mean wait, stochastic h, random passengers (renewal) | minutes |
| 5 | $E[W] = (h/2)(1 + C_{v,h}^{2})$ | Mean wait in terms of CV of headway | minutes |
| 6 | $C_{v,h} = \sigma_{h} / \mu_{h}$ | Coefficient of variation of headway | dimensionless |
| 7 | $P(h > t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Exponential headway (Poisson process) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $E[h] = k/\lambda$ | Mean Erlang headway | minutes |
| 9 | $C_{v,h} = 1/\sqrt{k}$ | CV of Erlang headway | dimensionless |
| 10 | $\lambda_{\max} = f \cdot C$ | Maximum transit line throughput | pass/hr |
| 11 | $D_{t} = t_{b} + t_{a} + t_{d}$ | Total dwell time at stop (boarding + alighting + door) | seconds |
| 12 | $P(\text{bunch}) \approx e^{-2f \cdot \tau}$ | Probability of vehicle bunching (λ-τ model) | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE] **Engineering Utility** — These formulas are routinely used in *transit service planning* (KSRTC, BMTC, DMRC), *BRT corridor design* (Ahmedabad BRT), *metro scheduling* (Kochi Metro Rail Ltd.), and *transit signal priority* algorithms. The CV-based waiting time formula is the **gold standard** in transport reliability literature (Transportation Research Board – TCRP Report 100).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Deterministic Relationship $f = 1/h$

**Given:** A transit line operates for a service period of $T = 60$ minutes and dispatches $N$ vehicles in that period.

**Step 1 — Definition of frequency:** Frequency is the *rate* of vehicle dispatch:

$$f = \frac{N \text{ vehicles}}{T \text{ hours}} \quad [\text{vph}]$$

**Step 2 — Definition of mean headway:** Mean headway is the *average time gap* between consecutive vehicles. If $N$ vehicles are dispatched in $T$ minutes, there are $N - 1$ inter-vehicle gaps (approximately $N$ for large $N$):

$$h = \frac{T \text{ minutes}}{N \text{ vehicles}} \quad [\text{min/veh}]$$

**Step 3 — Take the product:**

$$f \cdot h = \frac{N}{T} \cdot \frac{T}{N} = 1$$

**Step 4 — Solve for either variable:**

$$f = \frac{1}{h} \quad \text{and} \quad h = \frac{1}{f} \qquad \blacksquare$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Mean Waiting Time for Random Arrivals and Deterministic Headway

**Given:** A bus arrives exactly every $h$ minutes. Passengers arrive at the stop following a **uniform distribution** over the headway interval (Poisson arrivals = uniform waiting-time distribution conditional on $h$).

**Step 1 — A passenger arriving at random in the interval $[0, h]$ has a uniform waiting time $W$:**

$$W \sim U(0, h)$$

**Step 2 — PDF of $W$ is constant over $[0, h]$:**

$$f_{W}(w) = \frac{1}{h}, \quad 0 \leq w \leq h$$

**Step 3 — Compute the mean by definition of expected value:**

$$E[W] = \int_{0}^{h} w \cdot f_{W}(w) \, dw = \int_{0}^{h} w \cdot \frac{1}{h} \, dw$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integral:**

$$E[W] = \frac{1}{h} \cdot \left[ \frac{w^{2}}{2} \right]_{0}^{h} = \frac{1}{h} \cdot \frac{h^{2}}{2} = \frac{h}{2} \qquad \blacksquare$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Mean Waiting Time Under Stochastic Headways (Renewal Theory)

**Given:** Successive headways $\{H_{1}, H_{2}, H_{3}, \ldots\}$ are i.i.d. random variables with mean $E[H] = \mu_{h}$ and variance $\text{Var}(H) = \sigma_{h}^{2}$. Passengers arrive in a Poisson stream at rate $\lambda_{p}$.

**Step 1 — Use the Renewal Reward Theorem.** Let $N(t)$ be the number of bus arrivals in time $[0, t]$. The expected number of buses in time $t$ is:

$$E[N(t)] = \frac{t}{\mu_{h}}$$

**Step 2 — By the inspection paradox in renewal theory, the headway $H$ that a randomly arriving passenger *sees* is size-biased.** The probability density of this *residual* headway $H^{\star}$ is:

$$f_{H^{\star}}(h) = \frac{h \cdot f_{H}(h)}{E[H]}$$

**Step 3 — The mean residual headway is therefore:**

$$E[H^{\star}] = \int_{0}^{\infty} h \cdot f_{H^{\star}}(h) \, dh = \frac{E[H^{2}]}{E[H]}$$

**Step 4 — For a Poisson arrival of passengers in a residual headway $H^{\star}$, the expected wait is half the residual headway:**

$$E[W \vert H^{\star}] = \frac{H^{\star}}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Average over the distribution of $H^{\star}$:**

$$E[W] = \frac{E[H^{\star}]}{2} = \frac{E[H^{2}]}{2 E[H]}$$

**Step 6 — Expand $E[H^{2}]$ using the variance identity** $E[H^{2}] = \text{Var}(H) + (E[H])^{2}$:

$$E[H^{2}] = \sigma_{h}^{2} + \mu_{h}^{2}$$

**Step 7 — Substitute:**

$$E[W] = \frac{\sigma_{h}^{2} + \mu_{h}^{2}}{2 \mu_{h}} = \frac{\mu_{h}}{2} + \frac{\sigma_{h}^{2}}{2 \mu_{h}}$$

**Step 8 — Factor out $\mu_{h}/2$:**

$$E[W] = \frac{\mu_{h}}{2} \left( 1 + \frac{\sigma_{h}^{2}}{\mu_{h}^{2}} \right) = \frac{h}{2} \left( 1 + C_{v,h}^{2} \right) \qquad \blacksquare$$

**Step 9 — Sanity check:** If $C_{v,h} = 0$ (deterministic headway), $E[W] = h/2$ ✓. If $C_{v,h} = 1$ (exponential/Poisson), $E[W] = h$ ✓. This is the celebrated **Welding's result** (1964) widely used in transit reliability studies.

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Erlang Headway Distribution Mean and Variance

**Given:** A bus must pass through $k$ independent "phases" (e.g., $k$ traffic signals) before reaching the stop. Each phase takes an exponential time with rate $\lambda$.

**Step 1 — The total headway is the sum of $k$ i.i.d. exponential random variables:**

$$H = X_{1} + X_{2} + \ldots + X_{k}, \quad X_{i} \sim \text{Exp}(\lambda)$$

**Step 2 — This is the Erlang($k$, $\lambda$) distribution with PDF:**

$$f_{H}(h) = \frac{\lambda^{k} h^{k-1} e^{-\lambda h}}{(k-1)!}, \quad h \geq 0$$

**Step 3 — Mean by linearity of expectation:**

$$E[H] = E\!\left[\sum_{i=1}^{k} X_{i}\right] = \sum_{i=1}^{k} E[X_{i}] = k \cdot \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{k}{\lambda}$$

**Step 4 — Variance by independence:**

$$\text{Var}(H) = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \text{Var}(X_{i}) = k \cdot \frac{1}{\lambda^{2}}$$

**Step 5 — Coefficient of variation:**

$$C_{v,h} = \frac{\sigma_{h}}{\mu_{h}} = \frac{\sqrt{k/\lambda^{2}}}{k/\lambda} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{k}} \qquad \blacksquare$$

**Insight:** As $k \to \infty$, $C_{v,h} \to 0$ (deterministic). This is the basis of the **Central Limit Theorem heuristic** that headways become more regular as more independent random phases are summed.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

> **Problem:** A KSRTC city bus route between Thrissur and Irinjalakuda operates with a mean headway of $\mu_{h} = 8$ minutes and a standard deviation of $\sigma_{h} = 4$ minutes. Compute: (a) the frequency of service, (b) the coefficient of variation, and (c) the average passenger waiting time assuming random passenger arrivals.

**(a) Frequency of service:**

$$f = \frac{1}{h} = \frac{1}{8 \text{ min}} = \frac{60}{8} = 7.5 \text{ buses/hour}$$

**(b) Coefficient of variation:**

$$C_{v,h} = \frac{\sigma_{h}}{\mu_{h}} = \frac{4}{8} = 0.5$$

**(c) Average passenger waiting time:**

$$E[W] = \frac{h}{2} \left( 1 + C_{v,h}^{2} \right) = \frac{8}{2} \left( 1 + 0.5^{2} \right) = 4 \times 1.25 = 5 \text{ minutes}$$

**Compare to the deterministic case ($C_{v,h} = 0$):** $E[W] = 4$ minutes.  
**Excess waiting time due to irregularity:** $5 - 4 = 1$ minute (a **25 % penalty**).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Headway Distribution Analysis Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Transit Service Planning] --> B[Define Operating Period T hours]
    B --> C[Determine Fleet Size N vehicles]
    C --> D[Compute Deterministic Frequency f = N/T]
    D --> E[Compute Scheduled Headway h = 1/f]
    E --> F[Deploy Vehicles in Real World]
    F --> G{Stochastic Disturbances Present?}
    G -->|Yes| H[Collect Observed Arrival Data]
    G -->|No| I[Ideal Deterministic Service]
    H --> J[Statistical Distribution Fitting]
    J --> K[Poisson Exponential]
    J --> L[Normal Gaussian]
    J --> M[Erlank k phases]
    J --> N[Empirical Distribution]
    K --> O[Estimate Mu h and Sigma h]
    L --> O
    M --> O
    N --> O
    O --> P[Compute Coefficient of Variation CVh]
    P --> Q[Apply Welding Formula]
    Q --> R[Average Waiting Time EW]
    O --> S[Service Reliability Index]
    P --> T[Bunching Probability]
    R --> U[Passenger Level of Service]
    S --> U
    T --> U
    U --> V[KTU Outcome CO1 CO2 CO3]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Relationship Between Frequency, Headway, and Capacity

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Passenger Demand lambda p pass per hr] --> B[Service Design]
    C[Vehicle Capacity C seats] --> B
    D[Operating Cost Constraint] --> B
    E[Headway Target h minutes] --> B
    B --> F[Required Frequency f = 1/h]
    F --> G[Required Fleet N = f x Round Trip Time]
    G --> H[Line Capacity f x C pass per hr]
    H --> I{Demand met?}
    I -->|Yes| J[Service Approved]
    I -->|No| K[Reduce h or Increase C]
    K --> F
    F --> L[Realised Headway Distribution]
    L --> M[Exponential or Normal or Erlang]
    M --> N[Passenger Waiting Time]
    N --> O[Reliability Metric]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence — Bunching Phenomenon

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Stop as Bus Stop
    participant Bus1 as Leading Bus
    participant Bus2 as Trailing Bus
    participant Signal as Traffic Signal
    Note over Bus1,Bus2: Scheduled headway h = 10 min
    Bus1->>Stop: Arrives at t = 0 min (delayed)
    Signal->>Bus1: Red light at 2 km, +3 min delay
    Bus1->>Stop: Arrives at t = 13 min
    Bus2->>Stop: Scheduled arrival at t = 10 min
    Bus2->>Stop: No delay, arrives at t = 10 min
    Note over Bus1,Bus2: New headway h = 13 - 10 = 3 min
    Note over Bus1,Bus2: BUS BUNCHING OCCURS
    Stop->>Stop: Passengers experience 3 min headway then 23 min gap
    Stop->>Stop: Average wait exceeds h/2 due to bunching
```

> [!NOTE] **Diagram Interpretation** — The first flowchart traces the complete analytical pathway from service planning to passenger waiting time, which is the standard approach taught in KTU Module 1. The second illustrates the engineering feedback loop where headway target drives fleet sizing and capacity. The sequence diagram captures the *bunching cascade* — the most important real-world cause of headway irregularity.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**Define the term *headway* as used in public transportation. How is it related to service frequency?**

**Model Answer:**

Headway is defined as the **time interval between two successive transit vehicles arriving at or departing from a given stop**, measured in the same direction of travel on the same route. If a transit system dispatches $N$ vehicles in $T$ hours, the average headway is:

$$h = \frac{T}{N} \text{ minutes}$$

The relationship with service frequency $f$ (vehicles per hour) is **inversely reciprocal**:

$$f = \frac{1}{h} \quad \text{and} \quad h = \frac{1}{f}$$

For example, a bus route with $h = 6$ minutes operates at $f = 10$ vph. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**What is meant by the *coefficient of variation of headway*? Why is it a better indicator of service reliability than mean headway alone?**

**Model Answer:**

The coefficient of variation of headway, denoted $C_{v,h}$, is the **ratio of the standard deviation of headway to the mean headway**:

$$C_{v,h} = \frac{\sigma_{h}}{\mu_{h}}$$

It is a **dimensionless** measure that quantifies the *irregularity* of service. It is a better reliability indicator than mean headway alone because:

1. Mean headway $h$ only describes average spacing, ignoring variability.
2. Passenger waiting time is governed by the formula $E[W] = \frac{h}{2}(1 + C_{v,h}^{2})$ — so irregularity *quadratically* worsens waiting.
3. $C_{v,h}$ allows comparison of reliability across routes with different $h$ (a route with $h=4$, $\sigma=2$ has $C_{v,h}=0.5$; same ratio of irregularity). **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of suitable diagrams and derivations, explain the **different statistical distributions** used to model transit vehicle headways. Compare the deterministic, exponential, and Erlang headway models in terms of their mean, variance, and coefficient of variation.

**(b) [7 marks]** A Kochi Metro feeder bus operates with a mean headway of $\mu_{h} = 6$ minutes and a standard deviation of $\sigma_{h} = 2$ minutes at the Aluva Metro Station stoppage. Assuming passengers arrive randomly at the bus stop:
  (i) Calculate the average passenger waiting time using the renewal-theory formula.
  (ii) What would the waiting time be if the bus operations were perfectly deterministic?
  (iii) Comment on the **percentage increase** in waiting time due to headway irregularity.

---

### Model Solution for Question A

**Solution to (a):**

The headway $H$ is the random variable describing the time between two successive vehicles. In practice, $H$ is modelled by:

**(i) Deterministic (Constant) Headway:**
- $H = h_{0}$ (constant).
- $E[H] = h_{0}$, $\text{Var}(H) = 0$, $C_{v,h} = 0$.
- Assumed in metro systems and theoretical capacity analysis. Diagrammatically: uniform horizontal bars on a time axis.

**(ii) Exponential Headway (Poisson Process):**
- $f_{H}(h) = \lambda e^{-\lambda h}$ for $h \geq 0$.
- $E[H] = 1/\lambda$, $\text{Var}(H) = 1/\lambda^{2}$, $C_{v,h} = 1$.
- Models completely random, uncoordinated bus arrivals.

**(iii) Normal Headway:**
- $H \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu, \sigma^{2})$.
- Used when there is a scheduled headway with small perturbations.

**(iv) Erlang Headway:**
- $H$ is the sum of $k$ independent exponential phases.
- $E[H] = k/\lambda$, $\text{Var}(H) = k/\lambda^{2}$, $C_{v,h} = 1/\sqrt{k}$.

**Comparison Table:**

| Model | $E[H]$ | $\text{Var}(H)$ | $C_{v,h}$ | Real-World Use |
|-------|--------|-----------------|-----------|----------------|
| Deterministic | $h_0$ | 0 | 0 | Metro rail, BRT |
| Exponential | $1/\lambda$ | $1/\lambda^2$ | 1 | Random bus arrivals |
| Normal | $\mu$ | $\sigma^2$ | $\sigma/\mu$ | Schedule with perturbation |
| Erlang($k$) | $k/\lambda$ | $k/\lambda^2$ | $1/\sqrt{k}$ | Multiple traffic signals |

**Diagrammatic representation** (described in words for the student to draw): a time axis with vertical arrows for vehicle arrivals. In deterministic case, arrows are equally spaced. In exponential case, arrows are clustered (showing bunching). In Erlang case, spacing is more uniform than exponential but still slightly variable. **(7 marks complete)**

---

**Solution to (b):**

**Given:** $\mu_{h} = 6$ min, $\sigma_{h} = 2$ min.

**(i) Stochastic waiting time:**

$$E[W_{\text{stoch}}] = \frac{\mu_{h}}{2}\left(1 + C_{v,h}^{2}\right)$$

Compute $C_{v,h}$:

$$C_{v,h} = \frac{\sigma_{h}}{\mu_{h}} = \frac{2}{6} = \frac{1}{3} \approx 0.3333$$

$$C_{v,h}^{2} = \left(\frac{1}{3}\right)^{2} = \frac{1}{9} \approx 0.1111$$

$$E[W_{\text{stoch}}] = \frac{6}{2} \times \left(1 + \frac{1}{9}\right) = 3 \times \frac{10}{9} = \frac{30}{9} = \frac{10}{3} \approx 3.33 \text{ min}$$

**(ii) Deterministic waiting time:**

$$E[W_{\text{det}}] = \frac{h}{2} = \frac{6}{2} = 3 \text{ min}$$

**(iii) Percentage increase:**

$$\% \text{ increase} = \frac{3.33 - 3.00}{3.00} \times 100\% = \frac{0.33}{3.00} \times 100\% \approx 11.11\%$$

**Comment:** Even with a moderate coefficient of variation ($C_{v,h} = 0.33$), passengers experience an **11.1 % increase in average waiting time** due solely to headway irregularity. This demonstrates why transit agencies invest in **holding control strategies**, **real-time dispatching**, and **transit signal priority** — to reduce $C_{v,h}$ and improve service reliability. **(7 marks complete)**

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – Sample Paper]

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the **average passenger waiting time formula** $E[W] = \dfrac{E[h^{2}]}{2 E[h]}$ for the case of stochastic headways and Poisson passenger arrivals. State and explain the **inspection paradox** that arises in renewal theory.

**(b) [7 marks]** A BRT corridor in Ahmedabad operates with the following headway distribution approximated as Erlang with $k = 4$ phases and parameter $\lambda = 1.0$ per minute.
  (i) Calculate the mean and variance of the headway.
  (ii) Calculate the coefficient of variation.
  (iii) Compute the average waiting time and compare it with the deterministic case.

---

### Model Solution for Question B

**Solution to (a):**

Let successive headways be $\{H_{1}, H_{2}, H_{3}, \ldots\}$, i.i.d. with mean $\mu_{h} = E[H]$ and second moment $E[H^{2}]$.

**Step 1 — Residual Life in Renewal Process:** When a passenger arrives at random, the headway in progress is the *residual* $H^{\star}$. By renewal theory:

$$E[H^{\star}] = \frac{E[H^{2}]}{E[H]}$$

**Step 2 — Inspection Paradox:** The headway observed by a random arrival is **size-biased** — long headways are over-represented because they have a higher probability of containing the random arrival point. Hence $E[H^{\star}] > E[H]$ unless $H$ is deterministic.

**Step 3 — Passenger Arrival in Residual Headway:** If the passenger arrives at time $U$ uniformly in the residual headway of length $H^{\star}$, the wait is $W = H^{\star} - U$, so $E[W \vert H^{\star}] = H^{\star}/2$.

**Step 4 — Total Expected Wait:**

$$E[W] = \frac{E[H^{\star}]}{2} = \frac{E[H^{2}]}{2 E[H]}$$

**Step 5 — Using $E[H^{2}] = \sigma_{h}^{2} + \mu_{h}^{2}$:**

$$E[W] = \frac{\mu_{h}}{2} + \frac{\sigma_{h}^{2}}{2 \mu_{h}} = \frac{h}{2}(1 + C_{v,h}^{2}) \qquad \blacksquare \quad (7 \text{ marks})$$

---

**Solution to (b):**

**Given:** Erlang($k = 4$, $\lambda = 1.0 / \text{min}$).

**(i) Mean and variance:**

$$E[H] = \frac{k}{\lambda} = \frac{4}{1.0} = 4 \text{ min}$$

$$\text{Var}(H) = \frac{k}{\lambda^{2}} = \frac{4}{1.0^{2}} = 4 \text{ min}^{2}$$

**(ii) Coefficient of variation:**

$$C_{v,h} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{k}} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{4}} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5$$

Verify: $\sigma_{h} = \sqrt{4} = 2$ min, $C_{v,h} = 2/4 = 0.5$ ✓.

**(iii) Average waiting time (stochastic):**

$$E[W] = \frac{h}{2}\left(1 + C_{v,h}^{2}\right) = \frac{4}{2}(1 + 0.25) = 2 \times 1.25 = 2.5 \text{ min}$$

**Deterministic comparison:**

$$E[W_{\text{det}}] = \frac{4}{2} = 2.0 \text{ min}$$

**Percentage increase:**

$$\frac{2.5 - 2.0}{2.0} \times 100\% = 25\%$$

**Comment:** With Erlang-4 headways, the **25 % excess waiting time** is significant enough to justify operational control measures such as **station holding** and **real-time adaptive control** to drive $C_{v,h}$ closer to zero. **(7 marks complete)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING] **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Mixing up $f$ and $h$ units** — Frequency must be in *vehicles per hour (vph)*, headway in *minutes per vehicle*. Many students forget to convert minutes → hours or vice versa.
> 2. **Forgetting the inspection paradox** — When asked to derive $E[W]$ for stochastic headways, students often directly use $E[W] = h/2$ (deterministic formula), losing **3–4 marks**.
> 3. **Confusing $C_{v,h}$ with $\sigma$** — Examiners expect $C_{v,h} = \sigma_h/\mu_h$, not just $\sigma_h$.
> 4. **Skipping the variance identity** — $E[H^2] = \sigma^2 + \mu^2$ must be shown in the derivation step.
> 5. **Not mentioning Erlang vs Exponential distinction** — Always state that Erlang is a *generalization* of exponential with $k$ phases.
> 6. **Bunching phenomenon ignored** — Real-world headway discussions must mention bunching and the lambda-tau control strategy for full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Frequency $f$** and **Headway $h$** are reciprocals: $f = 1/h$, $h = 1/f$.
- **Deterministic headway** = ideal schedule; **stochastic headway** = real-world reality.
- **Three key distributions**: Deterministic, Exponential (Poisson), Normal, and Erlang.
- **Mean waiting time (deterministic):** $E[W] = h/2$.
- **Mean waiting time (stochastic, renewal theory):** $E[W] = E[h^2] / (2 E[h])$.
- **Welding's compact form:** $E[W] = (h/2)(1 + C_{v,h}^{2})$.
- **Coefficient of variation:** $C_{v,h} = \sigma_h / \mu_h$ — dimensionless, scale-free reliability index.
- **Erlang($k$):** $C_{v,h} = 1/\sqrt{k}$; **Exponential** is Erlang-1.
- **Bunching** is the dominant real-world cause of headway irregularity.
- **Increasing $C_{v,h}$ → waiting time grows quadratically** — penalises passengers.
- **Practical implication:** Transit agencies use *holding control*, *real-time dispatching*, and *transit signal priority* to keep $C_{v,h}$ small.
- **Capacity formula:** $\lambda_{\max} = f \cdot C$ where $C$ is vehicle capacity (passengers).
- **Always convert units carefully** — minutes to hours (×60) or hours to minutes (÷60) before plugging in.
- **The derivation of $E[W]$ requires renewal theory and the inspection paradox** — write it step-by-step for full marks in ESE.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Scheduling of Service and Timetabling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET833 - PUBLIC TRANSPORTATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Scheduling of Service and Timetabling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 MODULE 1: SCHEDULING OF SERVICE AND TIMETABLING

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> *Scheduling of service and timetabling* refers to the systematic planning process of determining the **frequency of service**, **vehicle arrival/departure times**, **crew (driver/conductor) duty assignments**, and the **operational window** of a public transport route, such that the system optimally matches the **passenger demand pattern** along the corridor while satisfying operational, economic, and safety constraints.

### 1.1 Conceptual Decomposition of the Topic

The phrase *Scheduling of Service and Timetabling* is essentially a **two-tier operational planning problem**:

| Tier | Layer | What it Decides | Output |
|:----:|:------|:----------------|:-------|
| 1 | **Service Scheduling** | How often should a bus/train arrive at a stop? | Frequency $f$, Headway $h$ |
| 2 | **Timetabling** | Exactly *when* does each vehicle depart? | Timetable matrix (Time vs. Stop) |
| 3 | **Vehicle Scheduling** | Which vehicle covers which trip? | Vehicle blocks / rotations |
| 4 | **Crew Scheduling** | Which driver operates which block? | Roster / Duty chart |

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy — The Orchestra Conductor Model:**
> Imagine you are conducting a 100-piece orchestra. You must decide:
> 1. **How often** each instrument section plays (Frequency),
> 2. **Exactly at which second** the violins enter (Timetable),
> 3. **Which violinist** plays which movement (Crew/Vehicle Assignment).
> 
> *Scheduling of service and timetabling* is the **conductor's score** of a transit system — it ensures that the right "musician" (vehicle + driver) is at the right "note" (stop) at the right "beat" (time).

### 1.2 Importance in Public Transportation Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why This Topic is High-Yield for KTU Board Exams:**
> - It is the **operational core** of any urban transit agency (KSRTC, BMTC, DMRC, Kochi Metro).
> - It directly influences **Level of Service (LOS)**, **Passenger Wait Time (PWT)**, and **Fleet Utilization**.
> - Frequently tested in Part A (3 marks — definitions) and Part B (14 marks — numerical design).

### 1.3 Fundamental Performance Metrics (Standard KTU Variables)

The following standard performance metrics govern scheduling decisions:

- **Headway ($h$):** Time gap (in **minutes**) between two consecutive vehicles on the same route. Mathematically: $h = \dfrac{60}{f}$
- **Frequency ($f$):** Number of vehicles per hour on a route. Unit: **veh/hr**
- **Cycle Time ($T_c$):** Round-trip time from depot → all stops → depot.
- **Fleet Size ($N$):** Total vehicles required to operate the service.
- **Load Factor ($\rho$):** Ratio of passengers carried to seating capacity.
- **Deadhead Time:** Non-revenue travel time (depot to first stop, last stop to depot).
- **Layover / Recovery Time:** Buffer time at terminals to recover from delays.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU Convention:** Headway is expressed in **minutes**, frequency in **vehicles per hour**. Always maintain dimensional consistency: $h \times f = 60$.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Pillars of Transit Scheduling

The scheduling process is a **hierarchical, multi-stage optimization**. Each stage feeds the next:

1. **Demand Analysis** → Identify peak/off-peak passenger volumes.
2. **Frequency Determination** → Convert demand into $f$ and $h$.
3. **Timetable Generation** → Convert $f$ into absolute clock times.
4. **Vehicle Scheduling (Run Cutting)** → Assign physical vehicles to trips.
5. **Crew Scheduling & Rostering** → Assign drivers to vehicle blocks.

### 2.2 Stage 1: Frequency and Headway Determination

The frequency of a transit service is governed by the **demand-supply balance** at the **Maximum Load Point (MLP)** — usually the busiest section of the route.

**Logic Steps (KTU Board Standard):**

- **Step 1 — Identify Peak Hour Demand:** Use boarding-alighting surveys to find the **Maximum Load Section (MLS)**.
- **Step 2 — Decide Service Frequency:** Frequency must satisfy the capacity constraint.
- **Step 3 — Compute Headway:** $h = 60 / f$.
- **Step 4 — Validate against Policy:** Headway must lie within **service standards** (e.g., $h_{\min} \leq h \leq h_{\max}$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard Service Headway Limits (Indicative):**
> - **Bus (City):** $h \leq 10$ min (peak), $\leq 20$ min (off-peak)
> - **Metro/BRT:** $h \leq 5$ min (peak)
> - **Intercity:** $h \leq 60$ min

### 2.3 Stage 2: Cycle Time & Fleet Size

The **cycle time** is the time taken by a vehicle to complete one full round trip. It is the **backbone** of all scheduling calculations.

**The Cycle Time Equation (KTU Standard):**

$$T_c = 2 \times (T_{travel} + T_{terminal} + T_{recovery})$$

Where:
- $T_{travel}$ = One-way running time (origin to destination, excluding stops).
- $T_{terminal}$ = Time spent at the terminal (boarding/alighting/layover).
- $T_{recovery}$ = Layover/recovery buffer for schedule reliability.

**The Fleet Size Equation (KTU Standard):**

$$N = \left\lceil \frac{T_c \times f}{60} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{T_c}{h} \right\rceil$$

> [!CAUTION]
> **Ceiling Function Rule:** Fleet size is always rounded **UP** (not to nearest integer). You cannot deploy half a bus! KTU examiners **deduct 1 mark** for incorrect rounding.

### 2.4 Stage 3: Timetabling — Construction of the Departure Table

Once frequency $f$ is fixed, the timetable is generated by **adding headway $h$ to the first departure time $t_0$**:

$$t_i = t_0 + (i-1) \times h \quad \text{for } i = 1, 2, 3, \ldots, N_{trips}$$

Where:
- $t_0$ = First departure time from origin.
- $t_i$ = Departure time of the $i^{th}$ trip.
- $N_{trips}$ = Total number of trips in the planning window.

### 2.5 Stage 4: Vehicle Scheduling (Run Cutting)

A **vehicle block** (also called a *run* or *rotation*) is a sequence of trips assigned to one physical vehicle, starting and ending at a depot.

The objective is to **minimize the total number of vehicles used** while covering all trips — known as the **Vehicle Scheduling Problem (VSP)**, a classic operations research problem solved via the **Sertão Algorithm** (KTU reference, also known as the *earliest-departure-next-arrival* greedy method).

### 2.6 Stage 5: Crew Scheduling & Rostering

A **duty** is the working shift of a driver. Crew scheduling minimizes total crew cost while respecting:
- **Maximum driving hours** (e.g., 8 hours/day in India under Motor Transport Workers Act).
- **Mandatory rest periods.**
- **Spread-over limits.**
- **Meal break requirements.**

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER FORMULA TABLE — Memorize Before Exam Day**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables | Unit | Boundary/Notes |
|:-:|:--------|:--------|:----------|:-----|:---------------|
| 1 | Headway | $h = 60/f$ | $h$: headway, $f$: frequency | min | $h > 0$ |
| 2 | Frequency | $f = 60/h$ | — | veh/hr | $f \in \mathbb{Z}^+$ |
| 3 | Required Frequency (Capacity) | $f = P_{peak} / (C_v \times \rho_{max})$ | $P_{peak}$: peak hourly demand, $C_v$: vehicle capacity, $\rho_{max}$: max load factor | veh/hr | $\rho_{max} \leq 1$ (usually 0.85–1.0) |
| 4 | Cycle Time | $T_c = 2(T_{run} + T_{term} + T_{recov})$ | $T_{run}$: running time, $T_{term}$: terminal time, $T_{recov}$: recovery | min | One-way vs. round-trip |
| 5 | Fleet Size | $N = \lceil T_c / h \rceil$ | — | vehicles | Always round UP |
| 6 | Trip Departure Time | $t_i = t_0 + (i-1)h$ | $t_0$: first departure | min or hr | $i = 1, 2, \ldots$ |
| 7 | Round Trip Cycle | $T_c = 2 \times T_{one\text{-}way} + T_{layover}$ | — | min | Includes terminal layover |
| 8 | Total Vehicle-Hours | $VHR = N \times T_{hours\text{-}of\text{-}service}$ | $T_{hours}$: operational hours | veh-hr | Used for cost estimation |
| 9 | Average Passenger Wait Time | $PWT = h/2$ | For random arrivals | min | Uniform headway assumption |
| 10 | Effective Headway | $h_{eff} = h + 2\sigma_h$ | $\sigma_h$: schedule deviation std dev | min | Accounts for unreliability |

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where These Equations Are Used:**
> - **Kochi Metro Rail Ltd (KMRL):** Daily timetable design using cycle time + fleet equations.
> - **KSRTC Ordinary / Fast Services:** Frequency optimization on Thrissur–Palakkad corridor.
> - **ITS (Intelligent Transport Systems):** Real-time headway adjustment using $\sigma_h$.
> - **BRT Corridors (India):** Capacity-based frequency planning under MoHUA guidelines.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 DERIVATION 1: Fleet Size from Cycle Time and Headway

> **Statement:** Show that the minimum number of vehicles $N$ required to operate a route continuously with cycle time $T_c$ and headway $h$ is $N = \lceil T_c / h \rceil$.

**Step 1 — Conceptual Setup:**

Consider a bus service where:
- The first bus leaves **Origin (O)** at time $t = 0$.
- After running time $T_{run}$, it reaches **Destination (D)**.
- After terminal layover $T_{lay}$, it departs D and returns to O after another $T_{run}$.
- Total time before the **same bus** is ready for its next departure from O = $T_c$.

**Step 2 — Time Accounting for One Vehicle:**

The time elapsed between two consecutive departures of the **same** vehicle from O is exactly $T_c$ minutes.

$$T_c = 2T_{run} + 2T_{terminal} + 2T_{recovery}$$

**Step 3 — Headway Constraint:**

For passenger service quality, a new bus must depart from O every $h$ minutes. Therefore, in any time window of length $T_c$, the number of departures required is:

$$N_{trips} = \frac{T_c}{h}$$

**Step 4 — One Vehicle per Trip:**

Each of these departures must be carried out by a **distinct** physical vehicle (since one vehicle cannot be in two places at once). Therefore:

$$N_{vehicles} \geq \frac{T_c}{h}$$

**Step 5 — Integer Constraint:**

A vehicle is an indivisible unit, so $N$ must be the smallest integer $\geq T_c/h$:

$$N = \left\lceil \frac{T_c}{h} \right\rceil$$

**Q.E.D.** $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.2 DERIVATION 2: Capacity-Based Frequency Equation

> **Statement:** Derive the frequency $f$ required to satisfy a peak-hour passenger demand $P_{peak}$ given vehicle capacity $C_v$ and maximum permissible load factor $\rho_{max}$.

**Step 1 — Define Maximum Load Point (MLP) Demand:**

The MLP is the section of the route where the **passenger load on the vehicle is maximum**. Let this be $P_{peak}$ passengers per hour in one direction.

**Step 2 — Available Capacity per Vehicle:**

A single vehicle of capacity $C_v$ seats, when loaded to load factor $\rho_{max}$, carries:

$$C_{effective} = C_v \times \rho_{max} \quad \text{passengers per vehicle}$$

**Step 3 — Total Capacity Provided per Hour:**

If $f$ vehicles depart per hour, the hourly capacity supplied is:

$$C_{supplied} = f \times C_v \times \rho_{max} \quad \text{[passengers/hour]}$$

**Step 4 — Demand-Supply Balance:**

For the system to operate without overcrowding:

$$C_{supplied} \geq P_{peak}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $f$:**

$$f \times C_v \times \rho_{max} \geq P_{peak}$$

$$\boxed{f = \left\lceil \frac{P_{peak}}{C_v \times \rho_{max}} \right\rceil \quad \text{[vehicles per hour]}}$$

**Q.E.D.** $\blacksquare$

---

### 3.3 NUMERICAL EXAMPLE (KTU Board Standard Style)

> **Problem:**
> A city bus route has the following operational data:
> - One-way running time: $T_{run} = 35$ minutes.
> - Terminal layover time: $T_{lay} = 5$ minutes per terminal.
> - Peak hour passenger demand at the Maximum Load Point: $P_{peak} = 1200$ passengers/hour.
> - Vehicle capacity: $C_v = 50$ passengers.
> - Maximum permissible load factor: $\rho_{max} = 0.90$.
> - First departure: 06:00 hrs.
> 
> **Required:**
> 1. Determine the cycle time $T_c$.
> 2. Compute the required frequency $f$ and headway $h$.
> 3. Calculate the minimum fleet size $N$.
> 4. Construct the first 6 departure times from the origin.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Cycle Time:**

$$T_c = 2(T_{run} + T_{lay})$$

$$T_c = 2(35 + 5) = 2 \times 40 = 80 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 2 — Required Frequency:**

$$f = \left\lceil \frac{P_{peak}}{C_v \times \rho_{max}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{1200}{50 \times 0.90} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{1200}{45} \right\rceil = \lceil 26.67 \rceil = 27 \text{ veh/hr}$$

**Step 3 — Headway:**

$$h = \frac{60}{f} = \frac{60}{27} = 2.22 \text{ minutes per vehicle}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Note:** Since 27 veh/hr is the rounded-up value, the actual operational headway may be slightly higher (2.22 min). For practical scheduling, this is often rounded to **2 min or 2.5 min** for clock-face timetables.

**Step 4 — Fleet Size:**

$$N = \left\lceil \frac{T_c}{h} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{80}{2.22} \right\rceil = \lceil 36.04 \rceil = 37 \text{ vehicles}$$

**Step 5 — Departure Timetable:**

$$t_i = t_0 + (i-1) \times h = 6{:}00 + (i-1) \times 2.22 \text{ min}$$

| Trip $i$ | Departure Time |
|:--------:|:---------------|
| 1 | 06:00 |
| 2 | 06:02 |
| 3 | 06:04 |
| 4 | 06:07 |
| 5 | 06:09 |
| 6 | 06:11 |

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Parameter | Symbol | Formula | Direction of Increase | Impact on Cost | Impact on LOS |
|:----------|:------:|:--------|:----------------------|:---------------|:--------------|
| Headway | $h$ | $60/f$ | $\uparrow$ | $\downarrow$ | $\downarrow$ |
| Frequency | $f$ | $60/h$ | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ |
| Fleet Size | $N$ | $T_c/h$ | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ |
| Cycle Time | $T_c$ | $2(T_{run}+T_{term})$ | $\uparrow$ | $\uparrow$ | $\downarrow$ |
| Load Factor | $\rho$ | $P/(C_v \cdot f)$ | $\uparrow$ | $\downarrow$ | $\downarrow$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Trade-off Insight:** A scheduler's job is to find the **Pareto-optimal point** — minimum fleet for maximum LOS. This is the heart of KTU Part B (14-mark) questions.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 📝 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

Define the terms **headway** and **frequency** as used in public transport scheduling. State the relationship between them.

**Model Answer:**

> **Headway ($h$):** It is the time interval (in minutes) between two consecutive vehicles travelling in the same direction on the same route.
>
> **Frequency ($f$):** It is the number of vehicles per hour operating in a given direction on a route.
>
> **Relationship:** $\boxed{h = 60 / f}$ and $f = 60 / h$ — the two are inversely proportional, with units such that $h \times f = 60$ minutes.
>
> *For example, a headway of 6 minutes corresponds to a frequency of 10 vehicles per hour.*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

What is **cycle time** in transit operations? List any two components of cycle time.

**Model Answer:**

> **Cycle Time ($T_c$):** The total time taken by a transit vehicle to complete one full round trip on a route — starting from the depot, covering all stops in both directions, and returning to the originating depot.
>
> **Two Components:**
> 1. **Running time** ($T_{run}$): Time spent in actual revenue motion between stops.
> 2. **Terminal layover / recovery time** ($T_{lay}$): Buffer time at each terminus to recover from delays.
>
> *Equation:* $T_c = 2(T_{run} + T_{lay})$
>
> *Note:* In metro systems, terminal time is often called *turnaround time*.*

---

### 5.2 📝 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

### ⭐ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **step-by-step procedure for determining the frequency of service** on an urban bus route. Use the **capacity-constraint method** and clearly state the formula. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A bus route has the following data:
- One-way distance: 18 km
- Average speed (including stops): 24 km/h
- Terminal layover: 4 minutes per terminal
- Peak hour demand at the Maximum Load Point: 1800 pax/hr
- Bus capacity: 45 seats, $\rho_{max} = 0.85$
- First departure: 06:30 hrs
- Service window: 06:30 to 10:30 hrs

**Required:** (i) Cycle time, (ii) Frequency, (iii) Headway, (iv) Fleet size, (v) First 5 departure times. **[7 marks]**

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION

##### Part (a) — Procedure for Determining Frequency of Service

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Answer Structure — Use numbered points**

**1. Conduct a passenger demand survey** along the route during the design hour (usually the peak hour). Use boarding-alighting counts at every stop.

**2. Identify the Maximum Load Point (MLP) / Maximum Load Section (MLS)** — the section where passenger volume is highest. This is the **critical section** that governs design.

**3. Determine the design-hour passenger flow** $P_{peak}$ in the peak direction at the MLP.

**4. Select design vehicle capacity** $C_v$ (seats + standees for buses; crush capacity for metros).

**5. Apply capacity-constraint equation:**

$$f = \left\lceil \frac{P_{peak}}{C_v \times \rho_{max}} \right\rceil$$

**6. Compute headway:**

$$h = \frac{60}{f}$$

**7. Validate against policy headway:** $h \leq h_{policy}$ (e.g., 10 min in cities).

**8. If $f$ is fractional**, round UP to next integer; recompute the effective $h$.

**9. Document the result** in the schedule database; proceed to vehicle and crew scheduling.

##### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Step 1 — One-way running time:**

$$T_{run} = \frac{18 \text{ km}}{24 \text{ km/h}} = 0.75 \text{ h} = 45 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 2 — Cycle time:**

$$T_c = 2(T_{run} + T_{lay}) = 2(45 + 4) = 2 \times 49 = 98 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 3 — Required frequency:**

$$f = \left\lceil \frac{P_{peak}}{C_v \times \rho_{max}} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{1800}{45 \times 0.85} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{1800}{38.25} \right\rceil = \lceil 47.06 \rceil = 48 \text{ veh/hr}$$

**Step 4 — Headway:**

$$h = \frac{60}{f} = \frac{60}{48} = 1.25 \text{ min/veh}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Adjustment:** For operational feasibility and clock-face timetables, $h$ is rounded to **1.5 min** (giving effective $f = 40$ veh/hr). This reduces the fleet burden but increases effective load factor to $\rho = 1800/(45 \times 40) = 1.0$ — borderline acceptable.

**Step 5 — Fleet size:**

$$N = \left\lceil \frac{T_c}{h} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{98}{1.25} \right\rceil = \lceil 78.4 \rceil = 79 \text{ buses}$$

**Step 6 — First 5 departure times** (using $h = 1.25$ min for illustration):

| Trip # | Departure Time |
|:------:|:--------------:|
| 1 | 06:30 |
| 2 | 06:31 |
| 3 | 06:32 |
| 4 | 06:34 |
| 5 | 06:35 |

---

### ⭐ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** With a neat flowchart, describe the **complete process of timetable preparation and vehicle scheduling** for a city bus service. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** The following data pertains to a bus route. Compute the **minimum fleet size** and the **number of duty shifts** required if each driver can work a maximum of 6 hours continuously (no split duty allowed).

| Parameter | Value |
|:----------|:-----:|
| One-way travel time | 30 min |
| Terminal time | 5 min per terminal |
| Headway | 6 min |
| Service window | 05:00 to 23:00 (18 hours) |

**[7 marks]**

#### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION

##### Part (a) — Flowchart & Description

> [!NOTE]
> **Suggested Flowchart Outline (Draw in Exam):**
> 
> **Step 1:** Define route, stops, depot location.
> ↓
> **Step 2:** Conduct boarding-alighting survey → find Peak Hour Demand.
> ↓
> **Step 3:** Calculate Frequency $f = P_{peak}/(C_v \cdot \rho_{max})$.
> ↓
> **Step 4:** Calculate Headway $h = 60/f$.
> ↓
> **Step 5:** Calculate Cycle Time $T_c = 2(T_{run} + T_{lay})$.
> ↓
> **Step 6:** Calculate Fleet Size $N = \lceil T_c/h \rceil$.
> ↓
> **Step 7:** Construct Timetable using $t_i = t_0 + (i-1)h$.
> ↓
> **Step 8:** **Vehicle Scheduling (Run Cutting):** Link trips into vehicle blocks using the *Sertão / earliest-arrival* algorithm.
> ↓
> **Step 9:** **Crew Scheduling:** Assign drivers to vehicle blocks respecting duty-hour limits.
> ↓
> **Step 10:** Publish timetable, monitor schedule adherence, and revise.

##### Part (b) — Numerical Solution

**Step 1 — Cycle time:**

$$T_c = 2(T_{run} + T_{lay}) = 2(30 + 5) = 70 \text{ minutes}$$

**Step 2 — Minimum Fleet Size:**

$$N = \left\lceil \frac{T_c}{h} \right\rceil = \left\lceil \frac{70}{6} \right\rceil = \lceil 11.67 \rceil = 12 \text{ buses}$$

**Step 3 — Total Service Time:**

The service window is 18 hours = 1080 minutes.

**Step 4 — Number of Round Trips per Vehicle per Day:**

$$\text{Trips per bus} = \frac{1080}{70} = 15.43 \Rightarrow 15 \text{ full round trips per bus}$$

**Step 5 — Total Vehicle-Hours Required:**

$$VHR_{total} = N \times T_{service} = 12 \times 18 = 216 \text{ vehicle-hours}$$

**Step 6 — Number of Driver Shifts:**

Each driver works 6 hours maximum → 3 shifts per day (06:00–12:00, 12:00–18:00, 18:00–00:00).

> [!CAUTION]
> **Shifts per Vehicle per Day:** A single bus operates 18 hrs; one driver covers only 6 hrs. So drivers per bus = $18/6 = 3$.
>
> **However**, drivers need *handovers* and *shift changes at terminals*, so we need a slightly higher number.

$$\text{Total driver-shifts required} = N \times \frac{T_{service}}{T_{max\,duty}} = 12 \times \frac{18}{6} = 36 \text{ driver-shifts}$$

Adding 10% buffer for relief/standby drivers:

$$\boxed{\text{Total drivers required} = 36 \times 1.10 \approx 40 \text{ drivers}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Forgetting the ceiling function** — Writing 11.67 buses instead of 12. ❌ Lose 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing terminal time** — Applying $T_{lay}$ only once instead of twice. ❌ Lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Using $h$ in hours** without converting from minutes. ❌ Lose 1 mark.
> 4. **Omitting the maximum load point** when computing $f$. ❌ Lose 2 marks.
> 5. **Not including the recovery/layover time** in $T_c$. ❌ Lose 1 mark.

---

### 5.3 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **🎯 Master These Before You Enter the Exam Hall**

- ✅ **Headway ($h$):** Time between consecutive vehicles. Unit: **minutes**. $h = 60/f$.
- ✅ **Frequency ($f$):** Vehicles per hour. Computed from demand: $f = P_{peak}/(C_v \rho_{max})$.
- ✅ **Cycle Time ($T_c$):** Round-trip time. Includes running + terminal + recovery.
- ✅ **Fleet Size ($N$):** $N = \lceil T_c/h \rceil$. **Always round UP.**
- ✅ **Timetable equation:** $t_i = t_0 + (i-1)h$.
- ✅ **Capacity-based frequency** is governed by the **Maximum Load Point (MLP)**, not the average load.
- ✅ **Load factor** $\rho$ should ideally stay between **0.70 and 0.90** for comfort.
- ✅ **Effective headway** $h_{eff} = h + 2\sigma_h$ accounts for schedule unreliability.
- ✅ **Vehicle scheduling** uses the **Sertão algorithm** to minimize fleet.
- ✅ **Crew scheduling** must respect **maximum duty hours** (Indian MV Act: 8 hrs/day).
- ✅ **Trade-off:** Higher $f$ → better LOS, higher cost. Lower $f$ → cost-saving, poor service.
- ✅ **Clock-face timetable:** Headway rounded to integer minutes (1, 2, 3, 5, 6, 10, 12, 15, 20, 30, 60) for passenger memorability.

> [!TIP]
> **🔥 Last-Minute Memory Hook — "HFCTF-T"** (Headway, Frequency, Cycle, Timetable, Fleet):
> Think of it as: *"**H**appy **F**riends **C**atch **T**rains **F**rom **T**erminals"* — Headway → Frequency → Cycle Time → Timetable → Fleet.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Elements and Computation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET833 - PUBLIC TRANSPORTATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Elements and Computation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Public Transportation Network?

A **Public Transportation System (PTS)** is an organized, shared passenger transport service available to the general public, typically operating on fixed routes and schedules. The *Elements* of a transit system are the physical and operational building blocks (routes, stops, terminals, vehicles, depots, fare structure) that together deliver mobility.

**"Computation"** in this context refers to the quantitative performance indicators derived from these elements — metrics that engineers and planners use to evaluate whether a transit network is *efficient, equitable, and effective*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Focus:** Module 2 demands that students master (a) the structural elements of a transit network and (b) the mathematical computation of geometric and operational indices like *route spacing, stop spacing, route directness, service area, and accessibility*.

---

## 1.2 The Six Core Elements of a Transit Network

Think of a transit network the way you would think of the **road network of Kerala** — buses run on highways, stop at towns, terminate at major hubs, and use depots overnight. Similarly, every transit network contains:

| # | Element | Plain-English Meaning | Real-World Analogy (Kerala) |
|---|---------|----------------------|----------------------------|
| 1 | **Route / Line** | A defined path along which a service operates | KSRTC Route: Thiruvananthapuram → Kochi via MC Road |
| 2 | **Stop / Station** | Fixed point where passengers board/alight | Vyttila Junction, Ernakulam Town |
| 3 | **Terminal** | Origin/destination point of a route | KSRTC Central Bus Station, TVM |
| 4 | **Depot / Garage** | Overnight parking and maintenance yard | Vikas Bhavan Depot |
| 5 | **Vehicle / Rolling Stock** | The moving unit (bus, train, metro) | Volvo AC Multi-Axle, Kochi Metro Train |
| 6 | **Fare Structure** | Pricing mechanism | Distance-based KSRTC fare |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic to remember:** **R-S-T-D-V-F** = *Roads, Stops, Terminals, Depots, Vehicles, Fares*.

---

## 1.3 The Intuitive "Why" Behind Computation

> [!TIP]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Hospital Network:**
> Imagine Kerala's government hospitals. Just as the *Directorate of Health Services* measures **distance between hospitals**, **patients served per hospital**, and **travel time to nearest facility**, transit engineers measure **route spacing, stop spacing, and accessibility**. The two fields share the same logic: *coverage, density, and directness* define service quality.

Every computation in this module answers one of three questions:

1. **Coverage** — *How many people can reach the system?* → Accessibility, Service Area
2. **Efficiency** — *How well is the system laid out geometrically?* → Route Factor, Network Density
3. **Convenience** — *How often must a rider transfer or detour?* → Transfer Index, Headway

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Element 1 — Route Spacing ($S_R$)

**Definition:** The perpendicular distance between two parallel routes in a transit network.

**Why it matters:** Wider spacing = passengers walk more; narrower spacing = high cost & low speed.

**Logic Steps:**

- Step 1: Assume a uniform grid of parallel routes serving an area $A$ (in km²).
- Step 2: Each route covers a band of width equal to the spacing $S_R$ on either side.
- Step 3: Total number of parallel routes in direction $N$ = $\dfrac{2 \cdot \text{dimension}}{S_R}$ factor collapses to: $S_R = \dfrac{2A}{N \cdot L_{\text{avg}}}$.

**Standard value:** *For bus transit in urban areas, optimal $S_R$ = **0.4 to 0.8 km**. For rail/metro, it is **0.8 to 2.5 km**.*

---

## 2.2 Element 2 — Stop Spacing ($S_S$)

**Definition:** The average distance between consecutive stops on a single route.

**Why it matters:** Closer stops = slower commercial speed; farther stops = more walking but faster trips.

**Standard value:** *Urban bus = **0.3 to 0.5 km**; BRT = **0.5 to 1.0 km**; Metro = **0.8 to 1.5 km**.*

---

## 2.3 Element 3 — Service Area ($A_S$)

**Definition:** The geographic area within walking distance of a transit stop.

**Why it matters:** Determines the *coverage* of a transit line. A corridor is only useful to people living within ~400 m (5-minute walk).

---

## 2.4 Element 4 — Route Directness / Route Factor ($RF$)

**Definition:** A dimensionless number measuring how "winding" a route is compared to the straight-line origin-destination path.

**Interpretation:**
- $RF = 1.0$ → perfectly direct (straight line).
- $RF > 1.0$ → route detours.
- $RF < 1.4$ → considered good design.
- $RF > 1.6$ → inefficient; redesign needed.

---

## 2.5 Element 5 — Network Density ($ND$)

**Definition:** Total route-km of transit per km² of service area.

**Standard:** *Urban minimum = **0.6 km/km²** (TCRP recommendation); desirable = **1.5 km/km²** in dense areas.*

---

## 2.6 Element 6 — Accessibility Index

**Definition:** The percentage of a city's population living within walking distance of a transit stop.

**Standard:** *KTU/Urban Transport Planning norm: at least **80% of population** within **400 m** of a bus stop.*

---

## 2.7 Element 7 — Line-Haul Index

**Definition:** Ratio of passengers who ride the *entire* route vs. those who board/alight midway.

A high line-haul index (> 1.0 along most segments) indicates a route that is "trunk-line" in nature.

---

## 2.8 Element 8 — Transfer Index ($TI$)

**Definition:** Average number of transfers a passenger makes to complete a trip.

**Standard:** *For a well-designed network, $TI \le 1.5$ transfers per trip.*

---

## 2.9 📋 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA CHEAT SHEET

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is your **one-page revision arsenal** for Module 2 computations. Memorize the formula, symbol, and unit.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Symbols | Unit |
|---|-----------|---------|---------|------|
| 1 | **Route Spacing** | $S_R = \dfrac{2 \cdot A}{N \cdot L_{\text{avg}}}$ | $A$ = service area, $N$ = no. of routes, $L_{\text{avg}}$ = avg route length | km |
| 2 | **Stop Spacing** | $S_S = \dfrac{L_r}{N_s - 1}$ | $L_r$ = route length, $N_s$ = no. of stops | km |
| 3 | **Service Area (per route)** | $A_S = 2 \cdot W \cdot L_r$ | $W$ = walk distance (typically **0.4 km**), $L_r$ = route length | km² |
| 4 | **Total Service Area** | $A_{T} = \sum A_{S,i}$ | Sum over all routes | km² |
| 5 | **Route Factor** | $RF = \dfrac{\sum d_i}{\sum D_i}$ | $d_i$ = actual route distance, $D_i$ = straight-line O-D distance | dimensionless |
| 6 | **Network Density** | $ND = \dfrac{\sum L_r}{A_{\text{city}}}$ | $A_{\text{city}}$ = total city area | km/km² |
| 7 | **Accessibility Index** | $A_I = \dfrac{P_S}{P_T} \times 100$ | $P_S$ = population in service area, $P_T$ = total population | % |
| 8 | **Line-Haul Index** | $LHI = \dfrac{P_t}{P_{\text{link}}}$ | $P_t$ = through passengers, $P_{\text{link}}$ = total link passengers | dimensionless |
| 9 | **Transfer Index** | $TI = \dfrac{\text{Total Transfers}}{\text{Total Trips}}$ | — | trips/transfer |
| 10 | **Headway** | $H = \dfrac{60}{f}$ | $f$ = service frequency (veh/hr) | minutes |
| 11 | **Fleet Size** | $N_v = \dfrac{T_{\text{round}}}{H}$ | $T_{\text{round}}$ = round-trip time | vehicles |
| 12 | **Commercial Speed** | $V_c = \dfrac{L_r}{T_{\text{run}}}$ | $T_{\text{run}}$ = running time | km/hr |

---

## 2.10 Real-World Engineering Utility

These computations are not academic — they directly drive decisions in:

- **KSRTC route rationalization** in Kerala (which routes to merge, which to drop).
- **Kochi Metro Phase 2 corridor justification** to MoHUA.
- **AMRUT (Atal Mission) city bus funding** under Ministry of Housing & Urban Affairs.
- **Smart City Mission** projects in Kochi, Thiruvananthapuram.
- **Comprehensive Mobility Plan (CMP)** studies submitted to KTU-affiliated consultancies.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Stop Spacing & Service Area

**Problem Statement:**
A KSRTC ordinary bus route runs from **Vyttila Junction** to **Aluva** covering a length of **18 km** with **25 stops** (including both terminals). The acceptable walking distance to a stop is taken as **0.4 km** (5-minute walk at 4.8 km/hr pace).

*Compute:*
1. Average stop spacing $S_S$
2. Service area served by this route $A_S$
3. Total population served if the service area's average population density is **3,500 persons/km²**

### Step 1 — Compute Stop Spacing

The number of *intervals* between stops is $N_s - 1$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_S &= \frac{L_r}{N_s - 1} \\
&= \frac{18 \text{ km}}{25 - 1} \\
&= \frac{18}{24} \text{ km} \\
&= 0.75 \text{ km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** On average, a bus stop appears every **750 m**, which is within the standard urban bus norm of 0.3–1.0 km. ✓

---

### Step 2 — Compute Service Area

Service area is a "buffer strip" of width $2W$ (both sides) along route length $L_r$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_S &= 2 \cdot W \cdot L_r \\
&= 2 \times 0.4 \text{ km} \times 18 \text{ km} \\
&= 14.4 \text{ km}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### Step 3 — Compute Population Served

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_S &= \rho \times A_S \\
&= 3{,}500 \text{ persons/km}^2 \times 14.4 \text{ km}^2 \\
&= 50{,}400 \text{ persons}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **~50,400 residents** fall within a 5-minute walk of this bus route. This is the route's *coverage footprint*.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Route Factor & Network Density

**Problem Statement:**
A transit network serves a city of area **120 km²** and has **6 routes** as detailed below:

| Route | O-D Straight Distance $D_i$ (km) | Actual Route Distance $d_i$ (km) |
|-------|----------------------------------|----------------------------------|
| R1 | 5.0 | 6.5 |
| R2 | 7.0 | 9.1 |
| R3 | 4.5 | 6.0 |
| R4 | 6.0 | 7.5 |
| R5 | 8.0 | 11.2 |
| R6 | 5.5 | 6.9 |

*Compute:*
1. **Route Factor (RF)** for the network
2. **Network Density (ND)** given total route-km = **55 km**

### Step 1 — Compute Route Factor

$$
\begin{aligned}
RF &= \frac{\sum d_i}{\sum D_i} \\
&= \frac{6.5 + 9.1 + 6.0 + 7.5 + 11.2 + 6.9}{5.0 + 7.0 + 4.5 + 6.0 + 8.0 + 5.5} \\
&= \frac{47.2}{36.0} \\
&= 1.311
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** $RF = 1.31$ → within the *acceptable* range (RF < 1.4). Routes are reasonably direct; only minor detours exist.

---

### Step 2 — Compute Network Density

$$
\begin{aligned}
ND &= \frac{\sum L_r}{A_{\text{city}}} \\
&= \frac{55 \text{ route-km}}{120 \text{ km}^2} \\
&= 0.458 \text{ km/km}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** $ND = 0.46$ km/km² → **below** the desirable urban benchmark of 0.6 km/km². The network is **under-supplied**; planners should add ~17 km of new routes to meet the standard.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick estimate:** Additional km needed = $(0.6 - 0.458) \times 120 = 17.04$ km.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Fleet Size from Headway

**Problem Statement:**
A metro corridor has a round-trip time $T_{\text{round}} = 84$ minutes. Required headway $H = 3.5$ minutes during peak hour. Compute the fleet size.

### Step-by-Step

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_v &= \frac{T_{\text{round}}}{H} \\
&= \frac{84 \text{ min}}{3.5 \text{ min}} \\
&= 24 \text{ trains}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** A minimum of **24 trainsets** must be deployed to maintain 3.5-min headway on this corridor.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Accessibility Index

**Problem Statement:**
A town has a total population $P_T = 1{,}200{,}000$. A bus stop-density analysis reveals that **864,000** residents live within **400 m** of a stop. Compute the Accessibility Index.

### Step-by-Step

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_I &= \frac{P_S}{P_T} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{864{,}000}{1{,}200{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= 72 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** **72%** accessibility is **below** the recommended 80% threshold — the city has transit *coverage gaps* in 28% of its residential zones.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Hierarchy of Transit Network Elements

```mermaid

    PTS[Public Transportation System] --> E1[Elements Physical]
    PTS --> E2[Elements Operational]
    PTS --> E3[Elements Economic]

    E1 --> R[Route or Line]

    E1 --> T[Terminal]

```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Computation Flow for Route Spacing & Service Area

```mermaid
    D --> E[Step 5: Validate SR against 0.4 to 0.8 km Urban Norm]
    E --> F[Step 6: Compute Service Area AS equals 2W times Lr]

```
