<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Route Factor. State the desirable range of values.
`[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Route Factor (RF)** is the ratio of the actual distance traveled along a transit route to the straight-line (crow-fly) distance between its origin and destination.

$$
RF = \frac{\sum d_i}{\sum D_i}
$$

Where $d_i$ is the actual route distance and $D_i$ is the straight-line O-D distance.

- $RF = 1.0$ → perfectly direct (no detour)
- $RF \le 1.4$ → **desirable** (efficient design)
- $RF > 1.6$ → inefficient, needs redesign

A low route factor indicates efficient routing, while a high value implies detours that increase passenger travel time.

---

### Question 2: What is Stop Spacing? List two factors on which the optimal stop spacing depends.
`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Stop Spacing ($S_S$)** is the average distance between consecutive stops on a transit route, computed as:

$$
S_S = \frac{L_r}{N_s - 1}
$$

Where $L_r$ is the route length and $N_s$ is the total number of stops.

**Two factors affecting optimal stop spacing:**

1. **Walking distance tolerance** — passengers typically walk up to 400 m (5 min) to access a stop; lower tolerance → closer stops.
2. **Commercial speed requirement** — higher desired speed → wider stop spacing (fewer stops = less delay).

*(Other valid factors: land-use density, ridership volume, mode type — bus vs. metro.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

---

### 📌 Question A — Choice 1 (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]`

**A transit authority is evaluating a new BRT corridor in a tier-2 city. The corridor details are:**

- Route length: $L_r = 24$ km
- Total stops (including terminals): $N_s = 31$
- Acceptable walk distance: $W = 0.5$ km
- Population density in catchment: $\rho = 4{,}200$ persons/km²
- City area: $A_{\text{city}} = 90$ km²
- Total route-km in proposed network: $\sum L_r = 68$ km
- Round-trip time: $T_{\text{round}} = 70$ min
- Required headway: $H = 5$ min

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
*Compute the stop spacing, service area, and the population served by the BRT corridor. Comment on whether the stop spacing satisfies urban BRT norms.*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
*Compute the Network Density and Fleet Size. State whether the proposed network meets the desirable urban density standard.*

---

### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Question A

#### Part (a) Solution

**Step 1: Stop Spacing**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_S &= \frac{L_r}{N_s - 1} \\
&= \frac{24}{31 - 1} \\
&= \frac{24}{30} \\
&= 0.80 \text{ km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Service Area**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_S &= 2 \cdot W \cdot L_r \\
&= 2 \times 0.5 \times 24 \\
&= 24 \text{ km}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Population Served**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_S &= \rho \times A_S \\
&= 4{,}200 \times 24 \\
&= 1{,}00{,}800 \text{ persons}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment on Stop Spacing Norm:**
- Computed $S_S = 0.80$ km.
- Standard BRT norm: **0.5 to 1.0 km**.
- The corridor **satisfies** the BRT norm ✓ (and falls on the higher end, which favours commercial speed).

---

#### Part (b) Solution

**Step 1: Network Density**

$$
\begin{aligned}
ND &= \frac{\sum L_r}{A_{\text{city}}} \\
&= \frac{68}{90} \\
&= 0.756 \text{ km/km}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Fleet Size**

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_v &= \frac{T_{\text{round}}}{H} \\
&= \frac{70}{5} \\
&= 14 \text{ vehicles}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comments:**
- $ND = 0.756$ km/km² → **exceeds** the minimum 0.6 km/km² benchmark ✓
- $ND < 1.5$ km/km² (the "desirable" level) → adequate but not exceptional.
- A fleet of **14 BRT buses** is required to maintain 5-minute headway.
- Adding spare vehicles (~10% contingency) → recommend procuring **16 BRT buses**.

---

### 📌 Question B — Choice 2 (14 Marks)
`[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 2]`

**A city has a population of 1,500,000 spread over 150 km². A transit survey gives the following data for 4 trunk routes:**

| Route | Actual Length $d_i$ (km) | O-D Straight Distance $D_i$ (km) | Stops $N_s$ | Route Length $L_r$ (km) |
|-------|--------------------------|----------------------------------|-------------|--------------------------|
| R1 | 9.0 | 7.5 | 16 | 9.0 |
| R2 | 11.5 | 8.0 | 18 | 11.5 |
| R3 | 7.0 | 6.0 | 14 | 7.0 |
| R4 | 13.0 | 9.5 | 22 | 13.0 |

Population within 400 m of stops: $P_S = 9{,}75{,}000$.

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
*Compute the Route Factor for the network and comment on its efficiency.*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
*Compute the Accessibility Index and the average stop spacing across the network. State whether accessibility meets the 80% benchmark.*

---

### ✅ MODEL SOLUTION — Question B

#### Part (a) Solution

$$
\begin{aligned}
RF &= \frac{\sum d_i}{\sum D_i} \\
&= \frac{9.0 + 11.5 + 7.0 + 13.0}{7.5 + 8.0 + 6.0 + 9.5} \\
&= \frac{40.5}{31.0} \\
&= 1.306
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comment:**
- $RF = 1.306$ → within the **desirable range** ($< 1.4$).
- The network is **efficiently designed** with minimal detour.
- Routes R3 and R4 individually need scrutiny — R4's $RF = 13.0/9.5 = 1.368$ approaches the upper limit.

---

#### Part (b) Solution

**Step 1: Accessibility Index**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_I &= \frac{P_S}{P_T} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{9{,}75{,}000}{15{,}00{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= 65 \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Average Stop Spacing**

First, total route length:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum L_r &= 9.0 + 11.5 + 7.0 + 13.0 = 40.5 \text{ km}
\end{aligned}
$$

Total number of *intervals*:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum (N_s - 1) &= 15 + 17 + 13 + 21 = 66
\end{aligned}
$$

Average stop spacing:
$$
\begin{aligned}
\overline{S_S} &= \frac{\sum L_r}{\sum (N_s - 1)} \\
&= \frac{40.5}{66} \\
&= 0.614 \text{ km}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Comments:**
- $A_I = 65\%$ → **below** the 80% benchmark. **35% of the population lacks transit access within a 5-min walk.**
- $\overline{S_S} = 0.614$ km → within urban bus norm (0.3–0.8 km) ✓.
- **Recommendation:** Add feeder routes or new stops to lift accessibility above 80%.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Read This!**
> - ❌ **Common Mistake 1:** Using $N_s$ instead of $N_s - 1$ in stop spacing → you will lose **1 full mark** per occurrence.
> - ❌ **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting the factor of **2** in $A_S = 2WL_r$ (buffer on *both* sides of route) → you will lose 1 mark.
> - ❌ **Common Mistake 3:** Writing $RF$ without units and **without interpretation** → examiners expect a verbal comment ("efficient" or "needs redesign"). Lose 1 mark if missing.
> - ❌ **Common Mistake 4:** Computing $\sum L_r$ / $\sum N_s$ for the *fleet size* numerator — remember it's $T_{\text{round}} / H$, not route length.
> - ✅ **Pro Tip:** Always box your final numerical answer and write a **one-line interpretation sentence** below it. Examiners reward interpretation heavily under the **2024 Scheme marking scheme**.

---

## 5.3 🧠 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> [!TIP]
> **Before you enter the exam hall, tick these boxes mentally:**

- [ ] **Route Spacing** formula: $S_R = \dfrac{2A}{N \cdot L_{\text{avg}}}$
- [ ] **Stop Spacing** formula: $S_S = \dfrac{L_r}{N_s - 1}$ (note: $N_s - 1$, **not** $N_s$)
- [ ] **Service Area** formula: $A_S = 2 \cdot W \cdot L_r$ (buffer on *both* sides)
- [ ] **Route Factor** formula: $RF = \dfrac{\sum d_i}{\sum D_i}$ ; acceptable if $RF \le 1.4$
- [ ] **Network Density** formula: $ND = \dfrac{\sum L_r}{A_{\text{city}}}$; urban minimum = **0.6 km/km²**
- [ ] **Accessibility Index** formula: $A_I = \dfrac{P_S}{P_T} \times 100$; target = **≥ 80%**
- [ ] **Fleet Size** formula: $N_v = \dfrac{T_{\text{round}}}{H}$
- [ ] **Headway** formula: $H = \dfrac{60}{f}$
- [ ] **Commercial Speed** formula: $V_c = \dfrac{L_r}{T_{\text{run}}}$
- [ ] **Line-Haul Index** interpretation: high value = strong corridor; use for **BRT/Metro** justification
- [ ] **Transfer Index** standard: $TI \le 1.5$ for a well-designed network
- [ ] Always end numerical answers with a **one-line interpretation** sentence
- [ ] Remember: KTU values **methodology + units + interpretation**, not just the final number
- [ ] **Standard benchmarks to memorize:**
    - Bus stop spacing urban: **0.3 – 0.5 km**
    - BRT stop spacing: **0.5 – 1.0 km**
    - Metro stop spacing: **0.8 – 1.5 km**
    - Walking tolerance: **400 m / 5 min**
    - Route Factor acceptable: **≤ 1.4**
    - Network Density minimum: **0.6 km/km²**
    - Accessibility target: **≥ 80%**

> **End of Module 2 Notes — Elements and Computation. Best of luck for your KTU examinations!** 🎓
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Systems Approach to Transit Line Capacity
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET833 - PUBLIC TRANSPORTATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Systems Approach to Transit Line Capacity

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Transit Line Capacity** is formally defined as the *maximum number of passengers (or transit units such as trains or buses) that can be transported past a fixed point on a transit line, in one direction, per unit of time*, under prevailing operating conditions, infrastructure constraints, and the system's most restrictive (minimum) subsystem capacity.

The **Systems Approach** treats the transit line not as a single entity but as a **series of interacting subsystems**, each of which imposes its own ceiling on the flow of passengers. The overall line capacity is governed by the **bottleneck subsystem** — the one with the lowest throughput.

The three classical subsystems identified by Vuchic (the seminal framework used in KTU transit textbooks) are:

1. **Transportation Vehicle (Rolling Stock) Subsystem** — the trains, buses, or trams.
2. **Right-of-Way (Guideway) Subsystem** — the track, lanes, and signal systems.
3. **Station/Stop (Terminal) Subsystem** — platforms, boarding areas, and access infrastructure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU OECET833, Module 2):** Students are required to understand the *systems concept* — the multiplicative interaction of vehicle, way, and station capacities — and to compute the **ultimate (theoretical)** and **practical (achievable)** line capacities for a given transit mode.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **water pipeline system** carrying water from a reservoir to a city.

- The **pipe diameter** represents the *vehicle capacity* (how much water each "packet" carries).
- The **pump pressure and valve spacing** represent the *right-of-way capacity* (how frequently packets can be sent).
- The **faucet at the city end** represents the *station capacity* (how fast the city can receive water).

No matter how wide the pipe or how powerful the pump, the city can only receive water as fast as its narrowest faucet can handle. In transit engineering, the **slowest subsystem dictates the system's true capacity**.

In simple terms: **A transit line is only as strong as its weakest link.**

> [!TIP]
> **Units & Standard Metrics Used in KTU Examinations:**
> - **Capacity:** passengers per hour per direction (**pphpd**)
> - **Headway:** seconds or minutes
> - **Dwell time:** seconds
> - **Train length:** meters
> - **Vehicle capacity:** passengers per vehicle
> - **Frequency:** trains/vehicles per hour

### 1.3 Classification of Capacity (KTU Framework)

| Capacity Type | Description | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| **Vehicle Capacity** | Max passengers a single vehicle can carry | $C_v$ |
| **Train Capacity** | Vehicle capacity × number of cars | $C_t$ |
| **Way Capacity** | Max trains/vehicles per hour on the right-of-way | $C_w$ |
| **Station Capacity** | Max passengers/vehicles handled at stops | $C_s$ |
| **Line Capacity (Theoretical)** | $\min(C_w, C_s) \times C_t$ | $C_L^{max}$ |
| **Practical Line Capacity** | Line capacity × loading factor × reliability factor | $C_L^{pract}$ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Systems Approach: The Three-Subsystem Logic

The systems approach recognizes that **passenger flow is constrained at every interface**. The logic proceeds as follows:

**Step 1 — Define Vehicle Capacity ($C_v$):**
- Determined by the physical dimensions of the vehicle, seating arrangement, and crush load limit.
- $C_v = N_{seats} + N_{stand} \times L_f$ where $L_f$ is the load factor (typically 1.0 to 4.0 passengers/m² for buses, 4–6 for metros).

**Step 2 — Define Way Capacity ($C_w$):**
- This is the **maximum number of vehicles per hour** that the right-of-way can physically support.
- Limited by:
  - Minimum headway ($h_{min}$) imposed by signaling
  - Block length on the line
  - Speed restrictions and gradients
  - Junction/bottleneck sections
- $C_w = \dfrac{3600}{h_{min}}$ (in vehicles per hour)

**Step 3 — Define Station/Stop Capacity ($C_s$):**
- Limited by:
  - Platform length
  - Number of berths/docking positions
  - Dwell time ($t_d$)
  - Pedestrian flow rates on stairs, escalators, turnstiles
- $C_s = \dfrac{3600 \times n_{berths}}{t_d}$ (in vehicles per hour at the busiest station)

**Step 4 — Compute Line Capacity:**
- The **ultimate capacity** of the line in passengers per hour is the **product** of train capacity and the **minimum** of way/station capacities:
$$C_{L}^{max} = C_t \times \min(C_w, C_s)$$
- Equivalently, in passenger units:
$$C_{L}^{max} = \dfrac{3600 \times N_{car} \times P_{car}}{h_{min}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the "Minimum" Function?**
> If the way can only handle 30 trains/hour but the station can only process 25 trains/hour, the station is the bottleneck. Even with a perfect guideway, the line cannot exceed 25 trains/hour. This is the **queueing theory principle** applied to transit: throughput = rate of the slowest server.

### 2.2 The Headway–Dwell–Capacity Relationship

The **operational headway** ($h_{op}$) must satisfy:
$$h_{op} \geq h_{min}$$
and at stations, headway must be larger than dwell time:
$$h_{op} \geq t_d$$

So the **effective minimum headway** is:
$$h_{eff} = \max(h_{min}^{signaling}, t_d)$$

This directly limits the achievable frequency and, therefore, capacity.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_v = N_{seats} + N_{stand}$ | Passengers per vehicle | passengers |
| 2 | $C_t = N_{car} \times C_v$ | Passengers per train consist | passengers |
| 3 | $C_w = \dfrac{3600}{h_{min}}$ | Way capacity (vehicles/hr) | veh/hr |
| 4 | $t_d = t_{oc} + t_{cd} + t_{bo}$ | Total dwell time | seconds |
| 5 | $C_s = \dfrac{3600 \times n_{berths}}{t_d}$ | Station capacity (vehicles/hr) | veh/hr |
| 6 | $C_{L}^{max} = C_t \times \min(C_w, C_s)$ | Ultimate line capacity | pphpd |
| 7 | $C_{L}^{pract} = C_{L}^{max} \times f_{load} \times f_{rel}$ | Practical capacity | pphpd |
| 8 | $h_{eff} = \max(h_{min}^{signaling}, t_d)$ | Effective minimum headway | seconds |
| 9 | $P_{max} = \dfrac{C_t \times 3600}{h_{eff}}$ | Passengers per hour practical | pphpd |
| 10 | $L_f = \dfrac{pax}{capacity}$ | Load factor (occupancy ratio) | dimensionless |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Constants for KTU Numericals:**
> - Bus crush load: **6–7 pax/m²**
> - Metro/LRT crush load: **4–6 pax/m²**
> - Typical door opening/closing time: **2–4 seconds per door**
> - Standard passenger boarding time: **0.5–1.0 s/pax** (bus), **0.3–0.5 s/pax** (metro)
> - Standard alighting time: **0.4–0.8 s/pax**

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The systems approach to line capacity is **fundamental in the design of:**

- **Metro Rail Systems** (Kochi Metro, Bengaluru Metro) — for determining train frequency, platform length, and rolling stock procurement.
- **BRTS corridors** (Ahmedabad, Pune) — for lane configuration, signal priority, and station sizing.
- **High-Speed Rail** — for block signaling design and headway optimization.
- **Transit-Oriented Development (TOD)** — for matching residential density to expected transit throughput.

In practice, the systems approach prevents the costly error of **over-designing one subsystem** while the bottleneck elsewhere strangles the entire system. For example, a metro with very long trains but short platforms cannot achieve its theoretical vehicle capacity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Maximum Line Capacity (General Case)

We start from the basic definition of **flow**:

$$
F = \dfrac{N}{T}
$$

where $N$ is the number of passengers and $T$ is the time interval (in hours for engineering convenience).

**Step 1:** Express $N$ in terms of the **train consist** (number of cars per train, $N_{car}$, and passengers per car, $P_{car}$):

$$
N_{train} = N_{car} \times P_{car}
$$

**Step 2:** Express $T$ in terms of the **headway** $h$ (in seconds). The number of trains passing a point in 3600 seconds is:

$$
N_{trains} = \dfrac{3600}{h}
$$

**Step 3:** Therefore, the total passenger flow per hour is:

$$
F = N_{train} \times N_{trains} = N_{car} \times P_{car} \times \dfrac{3600}{h}
$$

$$
C_{L} = \dfrac{3600 \times N_{car} \times P_{car}}{h}
$$

**Step 4:** Recognize that $h$ cannot be smaller than the **effective minimum headway**, which is the larger of the signaling-imposed minimum and the station dwell time:

$$
h_{min}^{eff} = \max(h_{min}^{sig}, t_d)
$$

**Step 5:** Substituting back:

$$
C_{L}^{max} = \dfrac{3600 \times N_{car} \times P_{car}}{h_{min}^{eff}}
$$

This is the **ultimate capacity** formula. The derivation is complete and dimensionally consistent (passengers/hour).

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem (typical KTU Module 2 question):**
> A metro line operates 6-car trains. Each car has a capacity of 240 passengers (crush load). The signaling system enforces a minimum headway of 120 seconds, and the busiest station has a dwell time of 45 seconds. Compute (a) the theoretical line capacity, and (b) the practical capacity at a load factor of 0.85 and reliability factor of 0.90.

**Solution:**

**Step 1: Identify Given Values**

$$
N_{car} = 6, \quad P_{car} = 240, \quad h_{min}^{sig} = 120 \text{ s}, \quad t_d = 45 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Train Capacity**

$$
C_t = N_{car} \times P_{car} = 6 \times 240 = 1440 \text{ passengers/train}
$$

**Step 3: Compute Effective Minimum Headway**

$$
h_{min}^{eff} = \max(120, 45) = 120 \text{ s}
$$

(The signaling constraint dominates; dwell is not the bottleneck here.)

**Step 4: Compute Theoretical Line Capacity (a)**

$$
C_{L}^{max} = \dfrac{3600 \times 1440}{120}
$$

$$
C_{L}^{max} = \dfrac{5{,}184{,}000}{120}
$$

$$
C_{L}^{max} = 43{,}200 \text{ pphpd}
$$

**Step 5: Compute Practical Capacity (b)**

$$
C_{L}^{pract} = C_{L}^{max} \times f_{load} \times f_{rel}
$$

$$
C_{L}^{pract} = 43{,}200 \times 0.85 \times 0.90
$$

$$
C_{L}^{pract} = 43{,}200 \times 0.765
$$

$$
C_{L}^{pract} = 33{,}048 \text{ pphpd}
$$

**Conclusion:** The line can theoretically carry **43,200 pphpd**, but realistically carries about **33,000 pphpd** once load and reliability are factored in.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Capacity Comparison Across Modes

| Parameter | Bus (BRT) | LRT/Tram | Metro Rail | Heavy Rail |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $C_v$ (pax/veh) | 80–120 | 150–250 | 240–300 | 300–400 |
| $N_{car}$ | 1 | 1–3 | 4–8 | 8–12 |
| $h_{min}$ (s) | 30–60 | 90–180 | 90–150 | 120–240 |
| $t_d$ (s) | 20–40 | 25–45 | 30–60 | 45–90 |
| $C_L^{max}$ (pphpd) | 4,000–9,000 | 8,000–18,000 | 30,000–80,000 | 50,000–90,000 |
| Bottleneck | Dwell time | Signaling | Signaling | Signaling |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For KTU Exams:** When asked to identify the bottleneck, always compare $C_w$ (way capacity) and $C_s$ (station capacity) in the **same units (veh/hr)** and take the smaller one. Do not confuse train capacity (pax/train) with hourly flow (pax/hr).

### 3.4 Subsystem Capacity Limitation — Mathematical Proof of Bottleneck

We define the achievable line capacity as:

$$
C_{L}^{ach} = C_t \times \min(C_w, C_s)
$$

**Proof of bottleneck dominance:**

Assume $C_w < C_s$. Then vehicles can be dispatched only at rate $C_w$ per hour. Even if the station can process $C_s > C_w$ vehicles/hour, it only ever receives $C_w$ vehicles/hour. Therefore:

$$
C_{L}^{ach} = C_t \times C_w
$$

Conversely, if $C_s < C_w$, vehicles accumulate at the station (queues form), and the system cannot dispatch the next vehicle until the platform clears. Thus:

$$
C_{L}^{ach} = C_t \times C_s
$$

This proves the **min-function** logic rigorously.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Three-Subsystem Block Diagram (Systems Approach)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates how the three subsystems interact multiplicatively to produce the line capacity. The bottleneck (smallest subsystem throughput) governs the final output.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Passenger Demand Input] --> B{Vehicle Subsystem}
    B -->|Cv: Vehicle Capacity| C[Train Consist Ncar x Cv]
    C --> D{Right of Way Subsystem}
    D -->|Cw: Way Capacity 3600 divided by hmin| E{Station Subsystem}
    E -->|Cs: Station Capacity 3600 x nberths divided by td| F[Bottleneck Identifier]
    F -->|Minimum of Cw and Cs| G[Maximum Line Capacity CLmax]
    G --> H[Practical Capacity Cmax x fload x frel]
    H --> I[Final Passenger Flow pphpd]

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style D fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style E fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style F fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style G fill:#9370DB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style H fill:#20B2AA,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style I fill:#32CD32,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

### 4.2 Detailed Capacity Computation Flow (Functional Architecture)

This diagram maps the analytical flow from raw inputs (vehicle, way, station parameters) through intermediate calculations to the final capacity output.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph INPUTS[Input Parameters]
        I1[Vehicle Capacity Cv]
        I2[Number of Cars Ncar]
        I3[Minimum Headway hmin]
        I4[Dwell Time td]
        I5[Number of Berths nberths]
        I6[Load Factor fload]
        I7[Reliability Factor frel]
    end

    subgraph COMPUTE[Computational Block]
        C1[Compute Train Capacity Ct = Ncar x Cv]
        C2[Compute Way Capacity Cw = 3600 divided by hmin]
        C3[Compute Station Capacity Cs = 3600 x nberths divided by td]
        C4[Identify Bottleneck Cbottleneck = min Cw Cs]
        C5[Compute Maximum Capacity CLmax = Ct x Cbottleneck]
        C6[Apply Practical Factors CLpract = CLmax x fload x frel]
    end

    subgraph OUTPUTS[Output Results]
        O1[Maximum Capacity pphpd]
        O2[Practical Capacity pphpd]
        O3[Bottleneck Subsystem]
    end

    I1 --> C1
    I2 --> C1
    I3 --> C2
    I4 --> C3
    I5 --> C3
    I1 --> C2
    I2 --> C5
    C1 --> C5
    C2 --> C4
    C3 --> C4
    C4 --> C5
    C5 --> C6
    I6 --> C6
    I7 --> C6
    C5 --> O1
    C6 --> O2
    C4 --> O3

    style INPUTS fill:#FFFACD,stroke:#000

    style O2 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000

```

### 4.3 Bottleneck Resolution Decision Tree

The following flowchart assists in identifying the critical subsystem and recommending capacity-enhancement strategies — a frequently tested concept in KTU 14-mark problems.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Compare Cw and Cs] --> Q1{Is Cw less than Cs?}
    Q1 -->|Yes, Way is Bottleneck| W[Way Subsystem Constrained]
    Q1 -->|No, Station is Bottleneck| ST[Station Subsystem Constrained]

    W --> W1[Reduce hmin by upgrading signaling]
    W --> W2[Implement moving block or CBTC]
    W --> W3[Increase train speed uniformity]
    W --> W4[Eliminate track bottlenecks]

    ST --> ST1[Reduce dwell time by multiple doors]
    ST --> ST2[Off board fare collection]
    ST --> ST3[Wider platforms or multiple berths]
    ST --> ST4[Improved passenger distribution]

    W1 --> R[Improved Line Capacity]
    W2 --> R
    W3 --> R
    W4 --> R
    ST1 --> R
    ST2 --> R
    ST3 --> R
    ST4 --> R

    R --> END[Final Enhanced Capacity CL]

    style S fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000

    style W fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000

    style END fill:#32CD32,stroke:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> Define the term **"Transit Line Capacity"** using the systems approach. List the three subsystems that govern it.

**Model Answer:**
Transit line capacity is the maximum number of passengers that can be carried past a fixed point on a transit line per unit time, considering the constraints imposed by all interacting subsystems. The three subsystems are: (i) the **Vehicle Subsystem** (rolling stock capacity), (ii) the **Right-of-Way Subsystem** (way/guideway capacity), and (iii) the **Station/Stop Subsystem** (terminal capacity). The minimum of way and station capacities, multiplied by train capacity, gives the ultimate line capacity.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
> Distinguish between **theoretical maximum capacity** and **practical capacity** of a transit line.

**Model Answer:**
The **theoretical maximum capacity** is the absolute upper limit of passenger throughput computed from ideal vehicle, way, and station parameters without considering real-world variability. The **practical capacity** is obtained by multiplying the theoretical capacity by adjustment factors such as the **load factor** (acceptable crowding level) and the **reliability factor** (accounting for delays, schedule deviations, and breakdowns). Practical capacity is always less than or equal to theoretical capacity.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2 Choice 1]**

A proposed Bus Rapid Transit (BRT) corridor has the following characteristics:
- Vehicle type: 12-metre articulated bus with crush capacity of **160 passengers**.
- Dwell time at the busiest station: **35 seconds**.
- Minimum headway enforced by signal priority: **45 seconds**.
- Load factor assumed: **0.90**.
- Reliability factor: **0.95**.

**(a) [7 Marks]** Calculate the **theoretical line capacity** in pphpd, identifying the bottleneck subsystem.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compute the **practical line capacity** and suggest **two engineering interventions** to increase the corridor capacity.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Theoretical Line Capacity:**

Step 1: Identify variables:
- $N_{car} = 1$ (single articulated bus considered as one unit)
- $C_v = 160$ pax
- $h_{min}^{sig} = 45$ s
- $t_d = 35$ s

Step 2: Train capacity:
$$
C_t = 1 \times 160 = 160 \text{ pax/vehicle}
$$

Step 3: Effective minimum headway:
$$
h_{eff} = \max(45, 35) = 45 \text{ s}
$$

Step 4: Theoretical line capacity:
$$
C_{L}^{max} = \dfrac{3600 \times 160}{45}
$$

$$
C_{L}^{max} = \dfrac{576{,}000}{45} = 12{,}800 \text{ pphpd}
$$

Step 5: Bottleneck identification:
- Way capacity: $C_w = 3600/45 = 80$ veh/hr
- Station capacity: $C_s = 3600/35 \approx 102.86$ veh/hr
- Since $C_w < C_s$, the **right-of-way (signal headway) is the bottleneck**.

**(b) Practical Capacity and Interventions:**

Step 1: Practical capacity:
$$
C_{L}^{pract} = 12{,}800 \times 0.90 \times 0.95 = 12{,}800 \times 0.855
$$

$$
C_{L}^{pract} = 10{,}944 \text{ pphpd}
$$

Step 2: Engineering interventions to increase capacity:

**Intervention 1: Reduce minimum headway via advanced signal priority.**
Implementing a **transit signal priority (TSP) system** with adaptive green extensions can reduce $h_{min}$ from 45 s to perhaps 30 s. This would raise the line capacity substantially.

**Intervention 2: Couple vehicles (bus platooning or longer articulated buses).**
Using **18-metre bi-articulated buses** with $C_v = 240$ pax, and combining with **off-board fare collection** to reduce dwell time, would simultaneously increase both $C_t$ and reduce $t_d$.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 2 Choice 2]**

A metro rail system has the following operational data:
- 6-car train consist; each car carries **280 passengers** (crush load).
- The signaling system allows a minimum headway of **90 seconds**.
- The busiest station has a dwell time of **50 seconds** and **two platform berths**.
- Design load factor: **0.80**; Reliability factor: **0.92**.

**(a) [7 Marks]** Determine the **way capacity**, **station capacity**, and identify the **bottleneck**.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compute the **theoretical** and **practical line capacities** (in pphpd) and explain how the bottleneck can be alleviated.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Way, Station, and Bottleneck:**

Step 1: Way capacity:
$$
C_w = \dfrac{3600}{h_{min}^{sig}} = \dfrac{3600}{90} = 40 \text{ trains/hr}
$$

Step 2: Station capacity (with 2 berths):
$$
C_s = \dfrac{3600 \times 2}{t_d} = \dfrac{3600 \times 2}{50} = 144 \text{ trains/hr}
$$

Step 3: Comparison and bottleneck:
- $C_w = 40$ trains/hr
- $C_s = 144$ trains/hr
- Since $C_w \ll C_s$, the **right-of-way (signaling headway) is the bottleneck**.

**(b) Theoretical and Practical Capacities:**

Step 1: Train capacity:
$$
C_t = 6 \times 280 = 1680 \text{ pax/train}
$$

Step 2: Effective minimum headway:
$$
h_{eff} = \max(90, 50) = 90 \text{ s}
$$

Step 3: Theoretical capacity:
$$
C_{L}^{max} = \dfrac{3600 \times 1680}{90}
$$

$$
C_{L}^{max} = \dfrac{6{,}048{,}000}{90} = 67{,}200 \text{ pphpd}
$$

Step 4: Practical capacity:
$$
C_{L}^{pract} = 67{,}200 \times 0.80 \times 0.92
$$

$$
C_{L}^{pract} = 67{,}200 \times 0.736
$$

$$
C_{L}^{pract} = 49{,}459.2 \approx 49{,}460 \text{ pphpd}
$$

Step 5: Alleviating the bottleneck:

The bottleneck is the **90-second signaling headway**. Strategies to relieve it include:
1. **Upgrade to Communications-Based Train Control (CBTC)** with moving block signaling, which can reduce $h_{min}$ to 60–75 s.
2. **Operational smoothing** by maintaining uniform speeds and reducing train bunching.
3. **Eliminate track bottlenecks** such as flat junctions, replacing them with grade-separated flyovers.

If $h_{min}$ were reduced to 75 s, the new capacity would be:
$$
C_{L}^{new} = \dfrac{3600 \times 1680}{75} = 80{,}640 \text{ pphpd}
$$

This represents a **20% capacity increase** purely through signaling upgrades.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency:** Mixing headway in minutes and capacity formulas expecting seconds. Always convert headway and dwell time to **seconds** before applying the 3600 multiplier.
> 2. **Forgetting to Compute Both $C_w$ and $C_s$:** Many students compute only one and assume it is the bottleneck. KTU examiners specifically check whether you **compare** both and identify the minimum.
> 3. **Confusing Train Capacity with Hourly Flow:** A 6-car train with 240 pax/car has $C_t = 1440$ pax — this is **not** per hour. Multiply by frequency (3600/h) to get the flow.
> 4. **Ignoring Effective Headway Rule:** Always use $h_{eff} = \max(h_{min}^{sig}, t_d)$. If you use only the signaling value when dwell is larger, you will overestimate capacity.
> 5. **Skipping the Bottleneck Discussion in 14-Mark Answers:** A 14-mark question without explicit bottleneck identification and engineering recommendations will be docked **at least 2–3 marks**.
> 6. **Using $|x|$ in markdown tables:** Always use `\vert` or `\mid` in mathematical expressions inside tables to avoid breaking the markdown.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Line capacity = Train capacity × Minimum(way, station) capacity.** The bottleneck governs everything.
- **The fundamental capacity formula is:** $C_L = \dfrac{3600 \times N_{car} \times P_{car}}{h_{eff}}$
- **Effective headway** $h_{eff} = \max(h_{min}^{signaling}, t_d)$ is the binding constraint.
- **Way capacity** $C_w = 3600 / h_{min}$ is in **vehicles per hour** — convert to passengers via train capacity.
- **Station capacity** $C_s = 3600 \times n_{berths} / t_d$ — more berths and faster dwell mean higher capacity.
- **Practical capacity = Theoretical × Load Factor × Reliability Factor** — typically 70–85% of theoretical.
- **Dwell time components:** $t_d = t_{open} + t_{change} + t_{close}$ where $t_{change}$ is the time for passenger alighting/boarding.
- **Standard crush loads (remember for KTU):** Bus = **6–7 pax/m²**; Metro = **4–6 pax/m²**; LRT = **5 pax/m²**.
- **Bottleneck alleviation strategies:** CBTC signaling, off-board fare collection, multiple platform berths, wider doors, longer trains.
- **Unit to memorize:** pphpd = **passengers per hour per direction** — always quote capacity in this unit in KTU answers.
- **Always compare $C_w$ and $C_s$** before declaring a bottleneck — KTU examiners test this explicitly.
- **The Systems Approach** is the *philosophical* foundation of transit capacity — it views capacity as an emergent property of interacting subsystems, not a single value.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Capacities of Different Modes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECET833 - PUBLIC TRANSPORTATION SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Capacities of Different Modes ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of public transportation engineering, the **Capacity of a Transport Mode** is defined as the maximum number of passengers (or passengers per unit time) that a particular transit system can carry past a given point or section, under specified operational conditions, prevailing roadway/track conditions, and environmental constraints, while maintaining acceptable service quality. The capacity of a public transport mode is generally expressed in two complementary forms: 1. **Vehicle (or Carrier) Capacity** – The maximum number of passengers that a single vehicle (bus, car, tram, coach, etc.) can accommodate at a given loading standard. 2. **System (or Line) Capacity** – The maximum number of passengers that can be transported past a fixed point (e.g., a section, station, or bottleneck) per unit time, typically per hour. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Capacity is NOT just the seating capacity of a vehicle. It is a *system-level* performance parameter that depends on the vehicle size, frequency of service, loading standard, and infrastructure constraints. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy: The Highway Pipeline** > Imagine a public transport system as a **water pipeline**. The *diameter of the pipe* is analogous to the *vehicle capacity* (how many people fit inside a single bus or train), while the *flow rate of water through the pipe per hour* is analogous to the *system capacity* (how many people pass a point per hour). A small pipe with high pressure (frequent small vehicles) can move as much water as a large pipe with low pressure (occasional large vehicles). Capacity, therefore, is not just about vehicle size — it is about the **combined effect of vehicle size × frequency of dispatch**. This is the same reason a fleet of 30-seater mini-buses running every 2 minutes can move the same number of people as a 90-seater double-decker running every 6 minutes. The system capacity is the same, but the *service quality* (waiting time, comfort) differs. ### 1.3 Standard Capacity Metrics Used in KTU Examinations The following units and definitions are **standardized in KTU 2024 Scheme Public Transportation Systems** and must be memorized: | Metric | Symbol | Standard Unit | Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | Vehicle Capacity | $C_v$ | passengers/vehicle | Persons inside one transit vehicle | | System (Line) Capacity | $C$ | passengers/hour (pphpd) | Persons past a section per hour, one direction | | Frequency | $f$ | vehicles/hour | Service headway inverse | | Headway | $h$ | minutes/vehicle | Time gap between successive vehicles | | Loading Standard | $LS$ | passengers/m² or seats + standees | Comfort benchmark | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical Distinction for Exams:** "pphpd" stands for **Passengers Per Hour Per Direction** — the canonical capacity unit for transit systems worldwide and a guaranteed 2-mark question in KTU exams. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Capacity Framework for Public Transport Modes The capacity of any public transport mode is a function of four hierarchical parameters: **Step 1 — Vehicle Capacity ($C_v$):** Determined by the physical dimensions of the vehicle, the seating arrangement, and the maximum allowed standee density. **Step 2 — Maximum Frequency ($f_{max}$):** Limited by the minimum headway achievable on the infrastructure (signal spacing, dwell time at stops, terminal turnaround, etc.). **Step 3 — System Capacity ($C$):** The product of vehicle capacity and the maximum frequency. **Step 4 — Practical Capacity (Offered Capacity):** Reduced from the theoretical maximum to account for peak vs. off-peak operations, schedule adherence, and load factor targets. ### 2.2 The Master Capacity Equation The fundamental relationship used universally in transit capacity analysis is: $$ C = C_v \times f_{max} = \frac{C_v}{h_{min}} $$ where: - $C$ = System (line) capacity in **pphpd** - $C_v$ = Vehicle capacity in passengers - $f_{max}$ = Maximum achievable frequency in **vehicles/hour** - $h_{min}$ = Minimum headway in **hours** (or in minutes divided by 60) ### 2.3 Minimum Headway Components The minimum headway is not a single value; it is the sum of several time-consuming activities on a transit corridor: $$ h_{min} = t_{dwell} + t_{signal} + t_{clearance} + t_{margin} $$ where: - $t_{dwell}$ = Dwell time at stations/stops (boarding + alighting time) - $t_{signal}$ = Signal cycle time or block occupation time - $t_{clearance}$ = Safety clearance between successive vehicles - $t_{margin}$ = Recovery / schedule margin ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Unit | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $C = C_v \times f$ | System capacity (basic) | pphpd | | 2 | $f = \dfrac{60}{h}$ | Frequency from headway (h in minutes) | veh/hr | | 3 | $C = \dfrac{60 \cdot C_v}{h}$ | Capacity with headway in minutes | pphpd | | 4 | $h_{min} = t_d + t_s + t_c + t_m$ | Minimum achievable headway | seconds | | 5 | $C_v = S + A \cdot \rho$ | Vehicle capacity = seats + (area × standee density) | persons | | 6 | $PCU = \dfrac{C_v}{n_{cars}}$ | Passenger Car Unit (per car) | PCU | | 7 | $C_{lane} = \dfrac{3600}{h_{bus}} \cdot C_{bus}$ | Bus lane capacity | pphpd | | 8 | $C_{rail} = \dfrac{3600}{h_{train}} \cdot n_{cars} \cdot C_{car}$ | Rail line capacity | pphpd | > [!NOTE] > **Where $C_v = S + A \cdot \rho$** — this is the *standing-allowed* vehicle capacity. $S$ = number of seats, $A$ = standing area in m², $\rho$ = standee density (usually **4 to 6 persons/m²** in KTU problems, with **5 persons/m²** as the most common standard). ### 2.5 Engineering Utility of Capacity Analysis The capacity of a mode is the *first design parameter* in any transit feasibility study. Before choosing a mode (BRT, LRT, Metro, Mono-rail), engineers compute the **Peak-Hour Peak-Direction Demand (PHPD)** and compare it against the capacity of each candidate mode. The selected mode must offer a capacity at least **1.2 × PHPD** to maintain a comfortable reserve margin. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Engineering Use:** Kochi Metro was designed for a capacity of ~**45,000 pphpd** to handle projected 2035 demand. This figure was derived directly from the capacity formula using 3-car trains, $C_v \approx 975$, and a planned minimum headway of 3 minutes — making capacity analysis the foundation of every metro project in India. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of System Capacity from First Principles Let us start from the basic definition and arrive at the master capacity equation step-by-step. **Step 1:** Define the number of passengers moved past a point per unit time. By definition, the system capacity is the product of how many passengers each vehicle carries and how many vehicles pass the point per unit time. $$ C = (\text{Passengers per vehicle}) \times (\text{Vehicles per hour}) $$ **Step 2:** Substitute the standard symbols. $$ C = C_v \times f $$ **Step 3:** Express frequency in terms of headway. Frequency is the reciprocal of headway. If $h$ is given in minutes, then frequency in vehicles per hour is: $$ f = \frac{60}{h} $$ **Step 4:** Substitute back into the capacity equation. $$ C = C_v \times \frac{60}{h} = \frac{60 \cdot C_v}{h} $$ This is the **fundamental capacity formula** used in all KTU numerical problems. ### 3.2 Derivation of Vehicle Capacity with Standees **Step 1:** Total capacity of a transit vehicle = seated passengers + standing passengers. $$ C_v = S + N_{stand} $$ **Step 2:** The number of standees depends on the available standing area and the standee density. $$ N_{stand} = A_{stand} \times \rho $$ **Step 3:** Substituting back: $$ C_v = S + A_{stand} \times \rho $$ This is the form used in KTU problems where a bus or coach is described with a given floor area, seat count, and standee density. ### 3.3 Derivation of Bus Lane Capacity **Step 1:** On a dedicated bus lane, the constraint is the minimum time spacing between two successive buses. $$ h_{min} = t_{dwell} + t_{clearance} $$ **Step 2:** Maximum frequency on the bus lane: $$ f_{max} = \frac{3600}{h_{min}} $$ (The factor 3600 converts seconds to hours, giving frequency in **buses/hour**.) **Step 3:** Bus lane capacity: $$ C_{lane} = f_{max} \times C_{bus} = \frac{3600 \cdot C_{bus}}{h_{min}} $$ ### 3.4 Derivation of Rail Line Capacity **Step 1:** A rail train consists of $n$ coaches, each with its own car capacity $C_{car}$. Total train capacity: $$ C_{train} = n \times C_{car} $$ **Step 2:** Maximum throughput on a rail line is limited by signalling headway $h_{signal}$ (in seconds). $$ f_{max}^{rail} = \frac{3600}{h_{signal}} $$ **Step 3:** Rail line capacity: $$ C_{rail} = \frac{3600 \cdot n \cdot C_{car}}{h_{signal}} $$ ### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard) > **Problem:** A city operates standard buses on a BRT corridor. Each bus has 45 seats and a standing area of 6 m². The allowed standee density is 5 persons/m². The minimum achievable headway at the busiest section is 90 seconds. Compute (a) vehicle capacity, (b) frequency, and (c) line capacity in pphpd. **Solution:** **(a) Vehicle Capacity:** $$ C_v = S + A_{stand} \times \rho = 45 + (6 \times 5) = 45 + 30 = 75 \text{ passengers/bus} $$ **(b) Frequency:** $$ f = \frac{3600}{h_{min}} = \frac{3600}{90} = 40 \text{ buses/hour} $$ **(c) Line Capacity:** $$ C = C_v \times f = 75 \times 40 = 3000 \text{ pphpd} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Verification:** A typical 3000 pphpd is characteristic of a **moderately-utilized BRT corridor** — the result is realistic and consistent with operational data from cities like Ahmedabad (Janmarg BRT) and Bogotá (TransMilenio). ### 3.6 Analytical Capacity Matrix — Comparison of Modes The following table compiles the **typical operational capacity ranges** for major public transport modes. These values are the standard reference figures for KTU theory questions and are repeatedly asked in viva voce. | Mode | Vehicle Capacity ($C_v$) | Min. Headway | Frequency (veh/hr) | Line Capacity (pphpd) | |---|---|---|---|---| | Walking (1 m wide pedestrian) | 1 person/m | ~0.5 s | 7,200 | **~4,000 – 6,000** | | Bicycle | 1 person | ~1 s | 3,600 | ~2,000 – 3,500 | | Auto-rickshaw / Shared Taxi | 3 – 6 | 10 s | 360 | ~1,500 – 2,000 | | Mini-bus | 20 – 30 | 30 s | 120 | ~3,000 – 4,000 | | Standard Bus (single) | 60 – 80 | 60 s | 60 | ~4,000 – 6,000 | | Articulated Bus | 120 – 160 | 60 s | 60 | ~7,000 – 9,000 | | Bi-articulated Bus | 180 – 240 | 60 s | 60 | **~12,000 – 18,000** | | Tram / LRT | 200 – 300 | 90 s | 40 | ~8,000 – 12,000 | | Metro (6-car) | 1,500 – 2,000 | 90 – 120 s | 30 – 40 | **~40,000 – 80,000** | | Heavy Rail (10-car) | 2,500 – 3,000 | 120 – 180 s | 20 – 30 | **~50,000 – 90,000** | > [!NOTE] > **KTU Examiner Insight:** When asked "Which mode has the highest capacity?" the correct answer is **Heavy Rail / Metro**, with line capacities of **50,000 – 90,000 pphpd**. Buses are limited to ~18,000 pphpd even in their most extreme bi-articulated form. This 4–5× capacity gap is precisely why metros are built in cities exceeding 4–5 million population. ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Hierarchical Capacity Framework (Block Architecture) The capacity of any public transport mode is best understood as a **four-tier functional hierarchy**, where each tier depends on the previous one. The Mermaid block below maps this layered architecture. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[TIER 1 INFRASTRUCTURE CONSTRAINTS] --> B[TIER 2 VEHICLE DESIGN] B --> C[TIER 3 OPERATIONAL PARAMETERS] C --> D[TIER 4 SYSTEM CAPACITY] A1[Lane Width
Track Gauge
Signal Spacing] --> A B1[Vehicle Length
Floor Area
Door Configuration] --> B C1[Min Headway
Dwell Time
Loading Standard] --> C D1[Passengers Per Hour
Per Direction pphpd] --> D D --> E[FINAL OUTPUT MODE COMPARISON] E --> E1[Bus 4000 to 6000 pphpd] E --> E2[BRT 10000 to 18000 pphpd] E --> E3[LRT 8000 to 12000 pphpd] E --> E4[Metro 40000 to 80000 pphpd] ``` ### 4.2 Capacity Calculation Flowchart (Solved-Problem Architecture) The following block diagram shows the **logical sequence of calculations** a KTU student should follow when solving any capacity-based numerical problem. ```mermaid flowchart TD P[PROBLEM STATEMENT] --> S1[STEP 1 IDENTIFY MODE] S1 --> S2[STEP 2 FIND VEHICLE CAPACITY Cv] S2 --> S2a[If bus Cv = S + A x rho] S2a --> S3[STEP 3 FIND HEADWAY h min] S3 --> S3a[Sum of dwell signal clearance margin] S3a --> S4[STEP 4 COMPUTE FREQUENCY f = 3600 divided by h min] S4 --> S5[STEP 5 COMPUTE SYSTEM CAPACITY C = Cv x f] S5 --> R[RESULT IN pphpd] R --> V[VALIDATE WITH REAL WORLD RANGE] V --> V1{Is value realistic} V1 --> V2[Yes Mark Answer] V1 --> V3[No Recheck assumptions] ``` ### 4.3 Mode-Capacity Mapping (Functional Architecture) This diagram shows how each transit mode connects to its typical capacity envelope and dominant application context. ```mermaid flowchart LR M1[Walking] --> CAP1[4000 to 6000 pphpd] M2[Bicycle] --> CAP2[2000 to 3500 pphpd] M3[Auto Rickshaw] --> CAP3[1500 to 2000 pphpd] M4[Standard Bus] --> CAP4[4000 to 6000 pphpd] M5[Articulated Bus] --> CAP5[7000 to 9000 pphpd] M6[BRT Bi articulated] --> CAP6[12000 to 18000 pphpd] M7[Tram LRT] --> CAP7[8000 to 12000 pphpd] M8[Metro Heavy Rail] --> CAP8[40000 to 80000 pphpd] CAP1 --> APP1[Last mile access] CAP2 --> APP2[Last mile access] CAP3 --> APP3[Feeder service] CAP4 --> APP4[City bus routes] CAP5 --> APP5[High density corridors] CAP6 --> APP6[Mega city corridors] CAP7 --> APP7[Medium city corridors] CAP8 --> APP8[Megacity mass transit] ``` ### 4.4 Block Diagram — Factors Reducing Theoretical Capacity A theoretical capacity of 90,000 pphpd (heavy rail) is rarely achieved. This block diagram explains the **derating factors** that bring practical capacity to 60–70% of theoretical. ```mermaid flowchart TD T[Theoretical Capacity C] --> F1[Schedule Adherence Loss] T --> F2[Peak vs Off Peak Imbalance] T --> F3[Vehicle Maintenance Downtime] T --> F4[Loading Standard Relaxation] T --> F5[Turnaround and Terminal Delay] F1 --> P[PRACTICAL CAPACITY] F2 --> P F3 --> P F4 --> P F5 --> P P --> R[Typically 60 to 70 percent of theoretical] ``` > [!NOTE] > **KTU Practical Takeaway:** Whenever you compute capacity in an exam, the question often asks for **"practical capacity"** — apply a derating factor of **0.7 to 0.8** to your theoretical result unless the question explicitly states "theoretical maximum." ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** Define the term *line capacity* of a public transport mode. What is its standard unit? **Model Answer:** Line capacity is defined as the maximum number of passengers that can be transported past a fixed section of a transit corridor per unit time in one direction, under prevailing operational and infrastructure conditions. It is the product of vehicle capacity and the maximum achievable service frequency. The standard unit is **pphpd (Passengers Per Hour Per Direction)**. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Distinguish between *vehicle capacity* and *system capacity*. Give one example each. **Model Answer:** *Vehicle capacity* refers to the maximum number of passengers that a single transit vehicle (bus, tram, or train) can carry at a given loading standard; for example, a standard city bus has a vehicle capacity of 70–80 passengers. *System capacity*, on the other hand, refers to the total number of passengers that can pass a fixed point in one hour on the transit corridor; for example, a metro line may have a system capacity of 60,000 pphpd. Vehicle capacity is in **passengers/vehicle**, while system capacity is in **pphpd**. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) #### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023]** **(a)** A BRT corridor operates with articulated buses having 60 seats and 10 m² of standing area. The standard standee density is 5 persons/m². If the minimum headway achievable is 75 seconds, calculate: 1. Vehicle capacity of the bus. **(3 marks)** 2. Maximum frequency. **(3 marks)** 3. BRT line capacity in pphpd. **(3 marks)** 4. Comment on whether this corridor can handle a peak demand of 9,000 pphpd. **(1 mark)** **(b)** Compare the line capacity of a BRT system, a Light Rail Transit (LRT), and a Metro system. List the three key infrastructure parameters that cause the capacity differences. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question A:** **Part (a) Solution:** **Step 1 — Vehicle Capacity:** $$ C_v = S + A_{stand} \times \rho = 60 + (10 \times 5) = 60 + 50 = 110 \text{ passengers/bus} $$ **Step 2 — Maximum Frequency:** $$ f = \frac{3600}{h_{min}} = \frac{3600}{75} = 48 \text{ buses/hour} $$ **Step 3 — Line Capacity:** $$ C = C_v \times f = 110 \times 48 = 5280 \text{ pphpd} $$ **Step 4 — Comparison with Peak Demand:** Since the calculated line capacity (5,280 pphpd) is **less than** the peak demand (9,000 pphpd), this BRT corridor **cannot handle** the projected peak demand. The system is capacity-deficient and would require upgrading to a higher-capacity mode such as LRT or Metro. --- **Part (b) Solution:** | Mode | Typical Line Capacity (pphpd) | |---|---| | BRT (Articulated buses) | 7,000 – 9,000 | | LRT (Tram) | 8,000 – 12,000 | | Metro / Heavy Rail | 40,000 – 80,000 | The three key infrastructure parameters causing the capacity differences are: 1. **Vehicle Size and Configuration:** Metros use multi-car trains (4–10 cars) with high floor area, providing massive per-vehicle capacity. BRT uses single or articulated buses with far smaller capacity. 2. **Minimum Headway Achievable:** Metros use modern Communication-Based Train Control (CBTC) signalling, allowing headways as low as 90 seconds. BRT systems, limited by road traffic and bus bunching, cannot go below 60–90 seconds. 3. **Right-of-Way and Segregation:** Metros operate on fully grade-separated, dedicated track with no crossings, allowing maximum frequency. BRT, even with dedicated lanes, is affected by intersections and pedestrian crossings that limit throughput. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** **(a)** A metro system operates 6-car trains. Each car has 50 seats and a standing area of 18 m². The standee density allowed is 6 persons/m². The signalling system permits a minimum headway of 120 seconds. Calculate: 1. Capacity of one car. **(2 marks)** 2. Capacity of one train. **(2 marks)** 3. Maximum train frequency per hour. **(2 marks)** 4. Maximum line capacity in pphpd. **(3 marks)** **(b)** Explain why walking, despite being the slowest mode, has a respectable line capacity of 4,000–6,000 pphpd. Discuss the conditions under which pedestrian capacity can drop sharply. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for Question B:** **Part (a) Solution:** **Step 1 — Capacity of One Car:** $$ C_{car} = 50 + (18 \times 6) = 50 + 108 = 158 \text{ passengers/car} $$ **Step 2 — Capacity of One Train:** $$ C_{train} = 6 \times 158 = 948 \text{ passengers/train} $$ **Step 3 — Maximum Train Frequency:** $$ f = \frac{3600}{120} = 30 \text{ trains/hour} $$ **Step 4 — Maximum Line Capacity:** $$ C = C_{train} \times f = 948 \times 30 = 28{,}440 \text{ pphpd} $$ This is characteristic of a **medium-capacity metro** like the Kochi Metro or Nagpur Metro. --- **Part (b) Solution:** Despite walking speeds of only 4–5 km/h, pedestrian capacity reaches 4,000–6,000 pphpd because: 1. **Very short headway:** Pedestrians can follow each other with a time gap of just 0.5–1 second, allowing a frequency of 3,600–7,200 persons/hour across a 1 m wide walkway. 2. **No vehicle size constraint:** Unlike a bus with a fixed capacity, pedestrians flow continuously — the system behaves like a fluid. 3. **Short trip lengths:** Walking is used for short trips (under 1–2 km), so the corridor does not need to support long-haul speeds, freeing capacity for volume. **Conditions under which pedestrian capacity drops sharply:** - **Crowding beyond 4 persons/m²:** Walk speed drops sharply and flow becomes unstable. - **Obstructions on footpath:** Trees, poles, hawker encroachments, and broken pavement drastically reduce effective width. - **Conflicts at intersections:** Uncoordinated pedestrian signals and vehicle turning movements reduce effective green time. - **Gradient:** Up-slopes above 5% reduce walking speed by 10–20%. - **Adverse weather:** Rain, heat, and poor lighting reduce both demand and effective capacity. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Traps in Capacity Questions:** > 1. **Forgetting to convert units** — headway is often given in **seconds**; frequency formula uses **3600** (not 60). Using 60 instead of 3600 is the #1 mistake, costing 3–4 marks. > 2. **Ignoring standee area** — many students compute capacity using only seats. Always check whether the vehicle is *standard* (no standees) or *urban transit* (standees allowed). In KTU problems, urban buses and metros always allow standees. > 3. **Confusing "per direction" with "both directions"** — capacity is reported per direction. If a corridor has 6,000 pphpd per direction, the total two-way flow is 12,000 — read the question carefully. > 4. **Applying the same headway to different modes** — bus headways and metro headways differ by an order of magnitude. Never assume h = 60 s for everything. > 5. **Skipping the practical-vs-theoretical distinction** — if the question says "practical capacity," multiply your answer by 0.7–0.8. Missing this loses 1–2 marks. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Capacities of Different Modes** - **Capacity has two forms:** Vehicle capacity ($C_v$, in passengers) and Line/system capacity ($C$, in pphpd). - **Master equation:** $C = C_v \times f = \dfrac{3600 \cdot C_v}{h_{min}}$ — memorize this in all three forms. - **Standard units to remember:** pphpd, vehicles/hour, persons/m² (for standee density), seconds (for headway). - **Standee density standard:** 4–6 persons/m²; **5 persons/m²** is the most common exam value. - **Headway components:** $h_{min} = t_{dwell} + t_{signal} + t_{clearance} + t_{margin}$. - **Mode capacity hierarchy (low to high):** Walking (~5,000) → Bus (~6,000) → Articulated Bus (~9,000) → BRT (~18,000) → LRT (~12,000) → Metro (~80,000 pphpd). - **Highest-capacity urban mode:** Metro / Heavy Rail, with up to 80,000–90,000 pphpd. - **Lowest-cost high-capacity mode:** BRT, offering metro-like capacity (up to 18,000 pphpd) at a fraction of metro capital cost. - **Practical capacity** is **60–80% of theoretical** — derate by 0.7 to 0.8 unless the question says "theoretical maximum." - **Loading standards matter:** Cramming more standees increases capacity but reduces service quality and safety. - **The capacity formula applies to *all* modes** — bus, tram, metro, mono-rail, suburban rail, BRT — only the inputs change, the structure does not.

topics.title: Level Service Measures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET833 - PUBLIC TRANSPORTATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Level Service Measures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Level of Service (LOS)** in public transportation is a **qualitative measure** used to describe the operational performance and quality of a transit service as perceived by the passengers. It is a graded framework (typically ranging from **LOS A** to **LOS F** or **1 to 6**) that combines quantitative service attributes (frequency, speed, reliability, comfort, etc.) into discrete categories reflecting how well the service meets passenger expectations and engineering design thresholds.

As per the **Transit Capacity and Quality of Service Manual (TCQSM)**, published by the Transportation Research Board (TRB), LOS is divided into two principal dimensions:
1. **Service Availability** – whether the transit service is offered to a potential user.
2. **Service Quality** – how well the service performs in terms of comfort, convenience, and reliability.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** LOS measures form the cornerstone of transit performance evaluation. A student must remember that LOS is a **passenger-centric** measure, not a purely operator-centric metric. The frame of reference is the rider's experience.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a restaurant. A 5-star restaurant where:
- You are seated immediately (low wait time = high frequency).
- The food arrives within minutes of ordering (high reliability).
- The table is spacious and not crowded (low passenger load).
- The restaurant is open whenever you are hungry (long hours of service).

A 1-star restaurant, in contrast, makes you wait hours, the food is often delayed, the place is over-packed, and it operates only at inconvenient hours.

**Transit LOS works exactly the same way.** The more "stars" the transit system earns, the higher its LOS, and the more attractive it is to the public, leading to higher ridership, modal shift from private vehicles, and sustainable urban mobility.

### 1.3 Core LOS Categories (TCQSM Standard)

| LOS Category | Description | Rider Perception |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| **LOS A** | Excellent | Best possible operating conditions, minimal delays |
| **LOS B** | Good | Slight inconvenience, still attractive |
| **LOS C** | Adequate | Noticeable inconvenience, acceptable for many |
| **LOS D** | Marginal | Approaches the limit of acceptable service |
| **LOS E** | Poor | Undesirable, many users may shift modes |
| **LOS F** | Failure | Overcrowding, extreme delays, unacceptable |

> [!NOTE]
> The boundaries between LOS grades are defined by **quantitative thresholds** that differ for each LOS measure. For example, a headway of 10 min may be LOS A, while 60 min could be LOS E or F.

### 1.4 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

- **Standard Design Hour Factor (K):** Typically **0.10 to 0.15** for urban transit.
- **Directional Distribution Factor (D):** Often **0.50 to 0.65**.
- **Standard Passenger Space:** **0.5 m² per standing passenger** (crush load).
- **Design Load Factor:** **1.25 to 1.50** (during peak hour).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Framework of LOS Measures

The TCQSM organizes LOS measures into a **two-tier hierarchy** that is universally accepted and tested in KTU examinations.

**Tier 1 – Service Availability (Can the user access the service?)**
- **Hours of Service:** Time span during which the transit operates.
- **Service Frequency:** Headway or vehicles per hour on a route.
- **Service Coverage:** Percentage of population/area within walking distance of stops.

**Tier 2 – Service Quality (How good is the ride?)**
- **Passenger Load (Crowding):** Ratio of passengers to seats or capacity.
- **Reliability:** On-time performance, headway adherence.
- **Travel Speed:** Average journey speed including stops.

### 2.2 Operational Logic of Each Measure

#### A. Hours of Service
- Measures the **time window** during which transit is available.
- LOS A: 18–24 hours of operation.
- LOS F: Less than 6–8 hours.
- **Why it matters:** Longer hours attract choice riders (office-goers, students).

#### B. Service Frequency and Headway
- **Frequency (f):** Number of vehicles per hour on a route.
- **Headway (H):** Time interval between two consecutive vehicles.
- A low headway → high frequency → less wait time → higher LOS.
- **Why it matters:** Wait time is the single largest deterrent to transit use.

#### C. Passenger Load Factor
- Measures the **degree of crowding** inside the vehicle.
- LOS A: All seats occupied, few standees (load factor ≤ 1.0).
- LOS F: Crush load (load factor > 2.0).
- **Why it matters:** Crowding directly affects comfort and passenger safety.

#### D. Reliability
- On-Time Performance (OTP).
- Headway Adherence Ratio.
- **Why it matters:** Uncertainty in arrival deters riders; reliability builds trust.

#### E. Travel Speed
- Ratio of distance traveled to total journey time (including stops).
- **Why it matters:** Compared to private modes, slow transit loses competitive edge.

#### F. Service Coverage
- Percentage of population within **400 m** (5-min walk) of a transit stop.
- **Why it matters:** Coverage brings transit "to the doorstep" of potential users.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Description** | **Units** |
|:---|:---|:---:|
| $H = \dfrac{60}{f}$ | Headway = 60 divided by frequency | minutes |
| $f = \dfrac{60}{H}$ | Frequency = 60 divided by headway | vehicles/hour |
| $PLR = \dfrac{P}{S}$ | Passenger Load Ratio = passengers to seats | dimensionless |
| $LF = \dfrac{P}{C}$ | Load Factor = passengers to total capacity | dimensionless |
| $W = \dfrac{H}{2}$ | Average wait time for random passenger arrivals | minutes |
| $OTP = \dfrac{N_{on\text{-}time}}{N_{total}} \times 100$ | On-Time Performance percentage | percent |
| $S = \dfrac{D}{T}$ | Travel speed = distance divided by time | km/hr |
| $Cov = \dfrac{A_{served}}{A_{total}} \times 100$ | Service coverage area ratio | percent |
| $\tau_{d} = \tau_{a} + \tau_{r}$ | Total door-to-door travel time | minutes |
| $CV_{H} = \dfrac{\sigma_{H}}{\mu_{H}}$ | Coefficient of variation of headway (reliability index) | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Where:**
> - $H$ = Headway, $f$ = Frequency
> - $P$ = Passengers on board, $S$ = Seats, $C$ = Vehicle capacity
> - $D$ = Distance, $T$ = Time, $S$ = Speed
> - $N_{on\text{-}time}$ = Number of on-time arrivals
> - $CV_{H}$ = Coefficient of Variation of Headway (a key reliability measure)

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Urban Planners** use LOS to design route networks and decide on frequencies.
- **Transit Operators** (KSRTC, BMTC, BEST) use LOS to allocate buses and identify weak links.
- **Policy Makers** use LOS as a benchmark for funding allocation.
- **Sustainable Cities** rely on LOS to encourage modal shift from private cars to public transport, reducing congestion and emissions.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Headway–Frequency Relationship

Let us derive the relationship between headway and frequency from first principles.

**Step 1:** Suppose a transit service operates for a total duration of $T$ minutes in a day on a particular route.

**Step 2:** If $N$ vehicles (buses/trains) are dispatched in that duration, the **frequency** $f$ is defined as:

$$f = \dfrac{N}{T} \text{ (vehicles per minute)}$$

**Step 3:** The **headway** $H$ is the time gap between two consecutive vehicles, which is the inverse of frequency (per minute basis):

$$H = \dfrac{1}{f} = \dfrac{T}{N} \text{ (minutes)}$$

**Step 4:** To express $f$ in **vehicles per hour**, multiply by 60:

$$f_{hour} = 60 \times f = \dfrac{60 N}{T}$$

**Step 5:** Therefore, the headway in minutes, using the hourly frequency $f_{hour}$:

$$H = \dfrac{60}{f_{hour}} \text{ (minutes)}$$

**Conversion Logic:** Since 1 hour = 60 minutes, dividing 60 by the number of vehicles dispatched per hour yields the inter-arrival time in minutes.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Average Passenger Wait Time (Random Arrival)

**Step 1:** Assume passenger arrivals at the stop are **random** and follow a uniform distribution over the headway interval $H$.

**Step 2:** A passenger arriving at a random instant within interval $H$ will wait, on average, half the headway:

$$W_{avg} = \dfrac{0 + H}{2} = \dfrac{H}{2}$$

**Step 3:** Substituting $H = 60/f$:

$$W_{avg} = \dfrac{60}{2f} = \dfrac{30}{f} \text{ (minutes)}$$

**Conversion Logic:** The expected value of a uniform distribution over $[0, H]$ is $H/2$. This is a classic result from probability theory.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** This $H/2$ formula assumes a **deterministic headway**. In real operations with variable headways, the average wait time increases and is given by $W = \dfrac{H}{2}(1 + CV_{H}^{2})$, where $CV_{H}$ is the coefficient of variation of headway.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Passenger Load Factor and Its LOS Implication

**Step 1:** Define the **load factor** $LF$ as the ratio of passengers on board $P$ to the design capacity $C$ of the vehicle:

$$LF = \dfrac{P}{C}$$

**Step 2:** For **seated load** (when $P \leq S$, where $S$ is the number of seats):

$$LF_{seated} = \dfrac{P}{S}$$

**Step 3:** TCQSM defines the following LOS thresholds for **bus transit load factor**:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{LOS A:} & \quad LF \leq 0.50 \\
\text{LOS B:} & \quad 0.50 < LF \leq 0.75 \\
\text{LOS C:} & \quad 0.75 < LF \leq 1.00 \\
\text{LOS D:} & \quad 1.00 < LF \leq 1.25 \\
\text{LOS E:} & \quad 1.25 < LF \leq 1.50 \\
\text{LOS F:} & \quad LF > 1.50
\end{aligned}$$

**Conversion Logic:** A load factor of 1.0 means every seat is occupied; values above 1.0 indicate standees, with 1.50 representing crush-load conditions.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Standard Style)

**Problem:** A city bus route has a peak-hour demand of **1800 passengers per hour** (pphpd). Each bus has a capacity of **60 passengers**, and the round-trip time including layover is **30 minutes**. Determine:
- (a) The required frequency and headway.
- (b) The fleet size required.
- (c) The average passenger wait time.
- (d) The resulting LOS grade based on load factor.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** Capacity per bus = 60 pax. Required frequency:

$$f = \dfrac{1800}{60} = 30 \text{ buses/hour}$$

$$H = \dfrac{60}{30} = 2 \text{ minutes}$$

**Part (b):** Round-trip time $t_{RT} = 30$ minutes. Each bus completes:

$$N_{trips} = \dfrac{60}{30} = 2 \text{ trips/hour}$$

Therefore, the fleet required to sustain 30 buses/hour:

$$F = f \times t_{RT} = 30 \times \dfrac{30}{60} = 15 \text{ buses}$$

**Part (c):** Average wait time (random arrival):

$$W_{avg} = \dfrac{H}{2} = \dfrac{2}{2} = 1 \text{ minute}$$

**Part (d):** Load factor = $1800 / (30 \times 60) = 1.00$. This corresponds to **LOS C** as per TCQSM thresholds (since $0.75 < LF \leq 1.00$).

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: LOS Thresholds for Key Measures

| **Measure** | **LOS A** | **LOS B** | **LOS C** | **LOS D** | **LOS E** | **LOS F** |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Headway (min) | $\leq 10$ | $10$–$14$ | $15$–$20$ | $21$–$30$ | $31$–$60$ | $> 60$ |
| Hours of Service (hr/day) | $19$–$24$ | $17$–$18$ | $14$–$16$ | $12$–$13$ | $9$–$11$ | $< 9$ |
| Passenger Load (LF) | $\leq 0.50$ | $\leq 0.75$ | $\leq 1.00$ | $\leq 1.25$ | $\leq 1.50$ | $> 1.50$ |
| On-Time Performance (%) | $\geq 95$ | $90$–$94$ | $85$–$89$ | $80$–$84$ | $70$–$79$ | $< 70$ |
| Travel Speed (km/hr, Bus) | $\geq 30$ | $24$–$29$ | $18$–$23$ | $14$–$17$ | $10$–$13$ | $< 10$ |
| Service Coverage (%) | $\geq 90$ | $80$–$89$ | $70$–$79$ | $60$–$69$ | $50$–$59$ | $< 50$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of LOS Evaluation

The following Mermaid diagram maps the complete architecture of how LOS measures interact within a transit system.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Public Transportation System] --> B[Service Availability Dimension]
    A --> C[Service Quality Dimension]
    A --> D[Accessibility Dimension]
    
    B --> B1[Hours of Service]
    B --> B2[Service Frequency and Headway]
    B --> B3[Route Coverage and Span]
    
    C --> C1[Passenger Load Factor]
    C --> C2[Travel Speed]
    C --> C3[On Time Performance]
    C --> C4[Headway Adherence]
    
    D --> D1[Stop Accessibility]
    D --> D2[Pedestrian Network]
    D --> D3[First and Last Mile Connectivity]
    
    B1 --> E[LOS Grading A to F]
    B2 --> E
    B3 --> E
    C1 --> E
    C2 --> E
    C3 --> E
    C4 --> E
    D1 --> E
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    
    E --> F[Passenger Satisfaction Index]
    E --> G[Mode Shift Potential]
    E --> H[Operator Performance Audit]
    
    F --> I[Sustainable Urban Mobility]
    G --> I
    H --> I
```

### 4.2 Process Flow of LOS Calculation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step 1 Collect Field Data] --> P2[Step 2 Compute Performance Metrics]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3 Compare with TCQSM Thresholds]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4 Assign LOS Grade A to F]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5 Identify Critical Measures]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6 Recommend Service Improvements]
    P6 --> P7[Step 7 Reevaluate After Intervention]
```

### 4.3 Interrelationship Between LOS Measures

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    F[Frequency] --> W[Wait Time]
    W --> ATT[Access Time Total]
    CV[Headway Variability] --> W
    RT[Reliability] --> SP[Service Perception]
    PL[Passenger Load] --> SP
    SP --> LOS[Overall LOS Grade]
    ATT --> LOS
    RT --> LOS
    PL --> LOS
    HS[Hours of Service] --> LOS
    SC[Service Coverage] --> LOS
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** A change in any single measure (for example, increasing frequency) cascades through the system, reducing wait time, improving perception, and ultimately raising the overall LOS grade. This is why transit planners treat LOS as a **systemic**, not isolated, measure.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1: Define Level of Service (LOS) in public transportation systems. Mention the six standard LOS categories.

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

Level of Service (LOS) is a qualitative measure that describes the operational performance and quality of a transit service from the passenger's perspective. It combines quantitative attributes such as frequency, reliability, speed, and comfort into graded categories.

The six standard LOS categories are:
- **LOS A** – Excellent (best service).
- **LOS B** – Good (slight inconvenience).
- **LOS C** – Adequate (acceptable for many).
- **LOS D** – Marginal (approaching unacceptable).
- **LOS E** – Poor (unattractive, riders may shift).
- **LOS F** – Failure (extreme delays, overcrowding).

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for listing categories, 1 mark for brief explanation.)*

---

#### Question 2: What is meant by headway in transit operations? How is it related to service frequency?

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Headway (H)** is the time interval between two consecutive transit vehicles travelling in the same direction on the same route. It is measured in **minutes**.

**Service frequency (f)** is the number of vehicles dispatched per hour on a route, measured in **vehicles per hour**.

The relationship is:

$$H = \dfrac{60}{f} \text{ (minutes)}$$

A higher frequency corresponds to a lower headway and vice-versa. Lower headways reduce passenger wait time and improve LOS.

*(Award 1 mark each for definition, relationship, and explanation.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Students often write $H = 1/f$ without converting to consistent units. Always specify **headway in minutes** and **frequency in vehicles per hour**, then apply $H = 60/f$.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Confusing **Passenger Load Ratio (PLR)** with **Load Factor (LF)**. PLR uses seats in the denominator; LF uses total vehicle capacity.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Forgetting to apply the random-arrival assumption ($W = H/2$) versus scheduled-arrival ($W = 0$). Use the appropriate assumption as stated in the question.
> - **Mark Loss Zone:** LOS grading questions require **citing the standard (TCQSM)**; not mentioning the source costs 1 mark.
> - **Diagram Credit:** If the question asks for a sketch or framework, missing the LOS hierarchy diagram may cost up to 2 marks.

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) – Option 1

**Explain the Level of Service (LOS) framework for public transportation as per TCQSM. Discuss the major service availability and service quality measures used to evaluate transit LOS.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**Model Answer:**

**Part (a) – LOS Framework and Service Availability (7 Marks):**

The **Transit Capacity and Quality of Service Manual (TCQSM)** is the industry-standard reference (published by TRB) used to define and evaluate LOS in public transportation. LOS is a **passenger-centric** qualitative grading system, typically expressed as **A through F**, with A being the best and F being the worst.

The TCQSM organizes LOS into **two principal dimensions**:

**1. Service Availability:** This dimension addresses *whether* a transit service is offered and *how easily* it can be accessed. The three primary sub-measures are:

- **Hours of Service:** Duration during which the transit is operational. LOS A corresponds to 19–24 hours; LOS F corresponds to less than 9 hours.
- **Service Frequency (Headway):** The number of vehicles per hour. LOS A: headway $\leq 10$ min; LOS F: headway $> 60$ min.
- **Service Coverage:** Percentage of population within 400 m walking distance of a stop. LOS A: $\geq 90$ percent; LOS F: $< 50$ percent.

**2. Service Quality:** This dimension addresses *how well* the service performs once the passenger is on board. It is discussed in Part (b).

**Part (b) – Service Quality Measures (7 Marks):**

The key service quality measures under TCQSM are:

- **Passenger Load Factor (LF):** Ratio of passengers to capacity.
  - LOS A: $LF \leq 0.50$; LOS F: $LF > 1.50$.
- **Reliability (On-Time Performance):** Percentage of trips arriving on schedule.
  - LOS A: $\text{OTP} \geq 95$ percent; LOS F: $\text{OTP} < 70$ percent.
- **Travel Speed:** Speed of the transit vehicle along the route including stops.
  - For buses: LOS A: $\geq 30$ km/hr; LOS F: $< 10$ km/hr.

**Formulas:**

$$H = \dfrac{60}{f}, \quad W = \dfrac{H}{2}, \quad LF = \dfrac{P}{C}, \quad OTP = \dfrac{N_{on\text{-}time}}{N_{total}} \times 100$$

**Conclusion:** The TCQSM LOS framework is essential for transit planning as it allows engineers to benchmark service quality, prioritize improvements, and align transit with passenger expectations and sustainability goals.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) – Option 2

**A bus route has a peak-hour ridership of 2400 passengers per hour. The bus capacity is 50 passengers, and the round-trip time including terminal layover is 40 minutes. Calculate: (a) the required frequency and headway, (b) the fleet size needed, and (c) the average passenger wait time. If the design capacity is exceeded by 20 percent during the peak, what is the resulting LOS grade based on the load factor?** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**

**Model Answer:**

**Part (a) – Frequency and Headway (4 Marks):**

Given: Peak demand $Q = 2400$ pphpd, Bus capacity $C = 50$ pax.

$$\begin{aligned}
f &= \dfrac{Q}{C} = \dfrac{2400}{50} = 48 \text{ buses/hour} \\
H &= \dfrac{60}{f} = \dfrac{60}{48} = 1.25 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}$$

**Part (b) – Fleet Size (4 Marks):**

Round-trip time $t_{RT} = 40$ minutes. Each bus takes 40 min to complete one cycle; therefore, in 60 minutes:

$$\text{Trips per bus per hour} = \dfrac{60}{40} = 1.5 \text{ trips/hour}$$

Fleet size required:

$$F = f \times \dfrac{t_{RT}}{60} = 48 \times \dfrac{40}{60} = 32 \text{ buses}$$

**Part (c) – Average Wait Time (2 Marks):**

Assuming random passenger arrivals:

$$W_{avg} = \dfrac{H}{2} = \dfrac{1.25}{2} = 0.625 \text{ minutes} = 37.5 \text{ seconds}$$

**Part (d) – LOS Grade Based on Load Factor (4 Marks):**

If design capacity is exceeded by 20 percent:

$$LF = 1.20$$

Using TCQSM thresholds: $1.00 < LF \leq 1.25$ corresponds to **LOS D (Marginal Service)**.

**Conclusion:** The route operates at LOS D, indicating the service is approaching its capacity limit and the operator should consider increasing frequency or using higher-capacity buses.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (High-Density):**

- **LOS** is a **qualitative, passenger-centric** grading system (A to F) defined by **TCQSM (TRB)**.
- **Two Dimensions:** Service Availability (Hours, Frequency, Coverage) and Service Quality (Load, Reliability, Speed).
- **Six Categories:** A (Excellent), B (Good), C (Adequate), D (Marginal), E (Poor), F (Failure).
- **Headway–Frequency:** $H = 60/f$ (minutes) and $f = 60/H$ (vehicles per hour).
- **Average Wait Time:** $W = H/2$ (random arrival); $W = H(1 + CV_{H}^{2})/2$ (variable headway).
- **Load Factor:** $LF = P/C$; **Passenger Load Ratio:** $PLR = P/S$.
- **On-Time Performance (OTP):** Percentage of trips arriving within ± 5 min of schedule.
- **LOS Thresholds to Memorize:** Headway (10/15/30/60 min), LF (0.5/0.75/1.0/1.25/1.5), OTP (95/85/70 percent).
- **Travel Speed Benchmarks for Buses:** LOS A: $\geq 30$ km/hr; LOS F: $< 10$ km/hr.
- **Coverage Standard:** 400 m walking distance (5-min walk) to nearest stop.
- **TCQSM = Transit Capacity and Quality of Service Manual** – the only authoritative source cited in KTU exams.
- **Fleet Size Formula:** $F = f \times (t_{RT}/60)$ buses.
- **High-Yield Tip:** Always state the assumption (random vs scheduled arrival) before using the wait time formula.
- **Common Pitfalls:** Unit mismatch (vehicles/hour vs vehicles/minute), confusing PLR with LF, and forgetting the $60$ factor in headway-frequency conversions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Speed of Transit Service
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECET833 - PUBLIC TRANSPORTATION SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Speed of Transit Service ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of public transportation engineering, **Speed of Transit Service** is defined as the rate at which a transit vehicle covers distance between two points, expressed in **kilometres per hour (km/h)** or **metres per second (m/s)**.
