It is a fundamental quantitative measure of the **level of service (LOS)** offered to passengers and directly influences route attractiveness, modal share, and operating cost. The transit speed is not a single number; it is a family of three distinct operational speeds defined by KTU's transit performance framework: 1. **Cruise Speed (Running Speed):** The instantaneous or segment-wise speed of the vehicle **while it is in motion**, excluding any stationary periods. 2. **Schedule Speed (Overall Travel Speed):** The effective speed of the vehicle **from one terminal to another**, including running time, station dwell times, and terminal layover times. 3. **Average Speed:** The mean speed over a complete round trip, including recovery time and deadhead operations. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Students must clearly distinguish between *Running Speed* and *Schedule Speed*. Examiner questions frequently test this distinction using a 3-mark short-answer format. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Office Commute" Intuition Imagine you drive from your house (Origin) to your office (Destination), a distance of **30 km**. - When you are actually pressing the accelerator on the open highway, your speedometer reads around **60 km/h** → this is the **Cruise Speed**. - However, you stop at **5 traffic signals** (each ~30 s), get stuck in **one traffic jam** (~5 min), and then spend **3 minutes** finding a parking spot at the office. Total trip time: **45 minutes** instead of 30 minutes. - Your *effective* speed is therefore: $$30 \text{ km} \div 0.75 \text{ h} = 40 \text{ km/h}$$ → this is the **Schedule Speed** that your morning actually felt like. > [!NOTE] > **Key Insight:** Schedule speed is the speed the *passenger experiences*. Cruise speed is the speed the *vehicle is capable of*. The gap between them measures the **inefficiency** of the transit corridor. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Constants - **Standard Units:** km/h (SI preferred for transit), m/s (used in physics/acceleration derivations). - **Conversion:** $$1 \text{ m/s} = 3.6 \text{ km/h}$$ - **Typical Speed Ranges (KTU Reference Data):** - Walking speed: **4–5 km/h** - City bus (mixed traffic): **12–18 km/h** - City bus (BRT corridor): **20–30 km/h** - Metro Rail (LRT/MRT): **25–45 km/h** - Regional Rail / High-Speed Rail: **80–300 km/h** ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Components of a Transit Trip (Theoretical Breakdown) A complete transit journey from origin to destination can be decomposed into **five sequential time components**. Every speed measure in transit engineering is derived by selectively including or excluding these components. Let the total one-way trip time be $T_{total}$: $$ T_{total} = t_{acc} + t_{cruise} + t_{dec} + t_{dwell} + t_{terminal} $$ Where: - $t_{acc}$ = **Acceleration time** at the start of the trip (and after each station stop). - $t_{cruise}$ = **Cruise time** at maximum design speed. - $t_{dec}$ = **Deceleration time** before each station stop. - $t_{dwell}$ = **Station dwell time** (boarding + alighting + door operations). - $t_{terminal}$ = **Terminal time / layover** at the end of the line. ### 2.2 The Three Speeds — Logical Derivation #### A) Cruise Speed ($V_c$) Cruise speed is the **pure vehicle performance metric**, independent of any external delay. $$ V_c = \frac{\text{Total One-Way Distance}}{\text{Time spent in motion only}} = \frac{L}{t_{acc} + t_{cruise} + t_{dec}} $$ It is **never** used in passenger information; it is used by **vehicle designers and maintenance engineers**. #### B) Schedule Speed ($V_s$) This is the **passenger-experienced speed** and the metric KTU examiners emphasize. $$ V_s = \frac{\text{One-Way Route Length (km)}}{\text{Total Running Time + Layover Time (h)}} $$ $$ V_s = \frac{L}{t_{acc} + t_{cruise} + t_{dec} + t_{dwell} + t_{terminal}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Crucial Relationship:** The ratio $V_s / V_c$ is called the **Speed Coefficient (η)**. A value of η close to **1.0** indicates an efficient system (e.g., metro rail); a value of **0.4–0.6** is typical for surface bus operations. #### C) Average Speed ($V_{avg}$) Computed over a complete **round trip** including recovery time and deadhead. $$ V_{avg} = \frac{2L}{T_{round\,trip}} = \frac{2L}{2 t_{running} + 2 t_{terminal} + t_{recovery}} $$ ### 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|----------|---------|-------|-------| | 1 | Cruise Speed | $V_c = L / t_{motion}$ | km/h | Excludes all stops | | 2 | Schedule Speed | $V_s = L / T_{total}$ | km/h | Includes dwell + layover | | 3 | Average Speed | $V_{avg} = 2L / T_{round}$ | km/h | Includes recovery | | 4 | Speed Coefficient | $\eta = V_s / V_c$ | dimensionless | 0 < η ≤ 1 | | 5 | Acceleration Distance | $d_{acc} = V_c^2 / (2a)$ | m | $a$ in m/s² | | 6 | Deceleration Distance | $d_{dec} = V_c^2 / (2b)$ | m | $b$ in m/s² | | 7 | Distance covered during accel. | $d_{acc} = \tfrac{1}{2} a t_a^2$ | m | $t_a$ in s | | 8 | Dwell Time (Rule of Thumb) | $t_{dwell} = p \cdot k$ | s | $p$ = pax, $k$ ≈ 1.5–2.5 s/pax per door | | 9 | Round Trip Time | $T_{RT} = 2(T_{run} + T_{lay})$ | min | For timetable design | | 10 | Vehicle Headway | $h = 3600 / f$ | s | $f$ = frequency (veh/h) | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Utility:** Schedule speed is the **single most important variable** in modal choice models. A 10% improvement in schedule speed (through skip-stop service or BRT corridor) can shift **5–15% of commuters** from private vehicles to public transit (as demonstrated in Ahmedabad and Bogotá BRT systems). ### 2.4 Factors Affecting Transit Speed (The "6 P" Framework) The transit speed is influenced by six categories of factors, organised by KTU's transit planning framework: 1. **Physical (Infrastructure):** Right-of-way type (A/B/C), grade separation, geometric design, station spacing. 2. **Performance (Vehicle):** Power-to-weight ratio, maximum speed, acceleration/deceleration rates, door configuration. 3. **Passenger (Demand):** Boarding-alighting volumes, fare collection method, standing density. 4. **Policy (Operational):** Stop spacing policy (every stop vs. skip-stop), express service patterns. 5. **Protection (Control):** Traffic signal priority/pre-emption, ATC systems, track warrant. 6. **Pavement (Environment):** Weather, grade, road surface quality, mixed traffic friction. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Relationship Between Cruise Speed, Distance, and Acceleration Phase **Problem Setup (KTU Standard Form):** A transit vehicle accelerates uniformly at rate $a$ from rest, reaches cruise speed $V_c$, maintains it for a duration $t_c$, and then decelerates uniformly at rate $b$ to rest. The total distance covered is $L$. Derive an expression for $V_c$ in terms of $L$, $a$, $b$, and $t_c$. **Step 1 — Acceleration Phase:** The vehicle starts from rest ($u = 0$) and accelerates at constant rate $a$ to reach $V_c$ in time $t_a$: $$ V_c = u + a t_a \quad \Rightarrow \quad t_a = \frac{V_c}{a} $$ The distance covered during this phase (using $s = ut + \tfrac{1}{2}at^2$): $$ d_{acc} = 0 \cdot t_a + \frac{1}{2} a t_a^2 = \frac{1}{2} a \left(\frac{V_c}{a}\right)^2 = \frac{V_c^2}{2a} $$ **Step 2 — Cruise Phase:** The vehicle moves at constant $V_c$ for time $t_c$: $$ d_{cruise} = V_c \cdot t_c $$ **Step 3 — Deceleration Phase:** The vehicle decelerates at rate $b$ from $V_c$ to rest. By symmetry with Step 1: $$ d_{dec} = \frac{V_c^2}{2b} $$ **Step 4 — Total Distance Equation:** $$ L = d_{acc} + d_{cruise} + d_{dec} = \frac{V_c^2}{2a} + V_c t_c + \frac{V_c^2}{2b} $$ **Step 5 — Solving for $V_c$ (Rearranging as a quadratic in $V_c$):** $$ L = V_c t_c + \frac{V_c^2}{2}\left(\frac{1}{a} + \frac{1}{b}\right) $$ $$ \frac{1}{2}\left(\frac{a+b}{ab}\right) V_c^2 + t_c \cdot V_c - L = 0 $$ $$ \left(\frac{a+b}{2ab}\right) V_c^2 + t_c \cdot V_c - L = 0 $$ **Step 6 — Quadratic Solution (using $V_c > 0$):** $$ V_c = \frac{-t_c + \sqrt{t_c^2 + 4 \cdot \frac{(a+b)}{2ab} \cdot L}}{\frac{a+b}{ab}} $$ $$ V_c = \frac{ab \left(\sqrt{t_c^2 + \frac{2L(a+b)}{ab}} - t_c\right)}{(a+b)} $$ > [!NOTE] > This is a closed-form solution that **KTU examiners love to ask** in 14-mark problems. Always present both the positive and negative roots, then justify dropping the negative root (since speed cannot be negative). ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Schedule Speed as a Function of Station Spacing **Problem Setup:** A transit line has $n$ stations uniformly spaced at distance $d$ between stations. The cruise speed is $V_c$, average dwell time per station is $t_d$, and terminal layover is $t_T$. Terminals are at both ends. Derive an expression for schedule speed. **Step 1 — Total Route Length:** $$ L = n \cdot d $$ **Step 2 — Total Dwell Time (n stations have dwell, terminals do not add during normal run):** $$ T_{dwell,total} = n \cdot t_d $$ **Step 3 — Total Running Time (acceleration + cruise + deceleration at each segment):** Assuming each station-to-station segment has its own accel-cruise-decel cycle, and using the identity from Section 3.1 that the total motion time per segment is $t_{acc} + t_{cruise} + t_{dec} = \tfrac{V_c}{a} + t_{c,i} + \tfrac{V_c}{b}$ where $t_{c,i}$ is the cruise time in that segment. For a uniform spacing $d$: $$ t_{c,i} = \frac{d - \frac{V_c^2}{2a} - \frac{V_c^2}{2b}}{V_c} = \frac{d}{V_c} - \frac{V_c}{2a} - \frac{V_c}{2b} $$ **Step 4 — Total Time per Segment:** $$ T_{segment} = t_d + \frac{V_c}{a} + \frac{d}{V_c} - \frac{V_c}{2a} - \frac{V_c}{2b} + \frac{V_c}{b} = t_d + \frac{V_c}{2a} + \frac{d}{V_c} + \frac{V_c}{2b} $$ **Step 5 — Total One-Way Time (n segments + terminal layover):** $$ T_{total} = n\left(t_d + \frac{V_c}{2a} + \frac{d}{V_c} + \frac{V_c}{2b}\right) + t_T $$ **Step 6 — Schedule Speed Formula:** $$ V_s = \frac{L}{T_{total}} = \frac{n \cdot d}{n\left(t_d + \frac{V_c}{2a} + \frac{d}{V_c} + \frac{V_c}{2b}\right) + t_T} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Design Insight:** As $d$ (station spacing) increases, $V_s$ **increases asymptotically** towards $V_c$. This is the engineering basis for **express service** and **BRT station consolidation** policies. ### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Exam Pattern) **Question:** A city bus operates on a 12 km route with 15 intermediate stops. The cruise speed is 40 km/h, average dwell time per stop is 30 s, and terminal layover is 5 min. The vehicle accelerates and decelerates uniformly at 1.2 m/s² and 1.5 m/s² respectively. Calculate: (a) Total one-way trip time (b) Schedule speed (c) Speed coefficient **Solution:** **Step 1 — Convert Units:** - $V_c = 40$ km/h $= 40/3.6 = 11.11$ m/s - $L = 12$ km $= 12{,}000$ m - Number of segments $n = 15$ - $a = 1.2$ m/s², $b = 1.5$ m/s² - $t_d = 30$ s, $t_T = 5 \times 60 = 300$ s **Step 2 — Acceleration & Deceleration Time per Segment:** $$ t_a = \frac{V_c}{a} = \frac{11.11}{1.2} = 9.26 \text{ s} $$ $$ t_b = \frac{V_c}{b} = \frac{11.11}{1.5} = 7.41 \text{ s} $$ **Step 3 — Accel/Decel Distance per Segment:** $$ d_{acc} = \frac{V_c^2}{2a} = \frac{(11.11)^2}{2(1.2)} = 51.44 \text{ m} $$ $$ d_{dec} = \frac{V_c^2}{2b} = \frac{(11.11)^2}{2(1.5)} = 41.15 \text{ m} $$ **Step 4 — Total Accel/Decel Distance:** $$ D_{ad} = n(d_{acc} + d_{dec}) = 15(51.44 + 41.15) = 15(92.59) = 1388.85 \text{ m} $$ **Step 5 — Pure Cruise Distance:** $$ D_{cruise} = L - D_{ad} = 12{,}000 - 1388.85 = 10{,}611.15 \text{ m} $$ **Step 6 — Pure Cruise Time:** $$ t_{cruise} = \frac{D_{cruise}}{V_c} = \frac{10{,}611.15}{11.11} = 955.10 \text{ s} $$ **Step 7 — Total Dwell Time:** $$ T_{dwell} = n \cdot t_d = 15 \times 30 = 450 \text{ s} $$ **Step 8 — Total One-Way Trip Time:** $$ T_{total} = (t_a + t_b) \cdot n + t_{cruise} + T_{dwell} + t_T $$ $$ T_{total} = (9.26 + 7.41)(15) + 955.10 + 450 + 300 $$ $$ T_{total} = 250.05 + 955.10 + 450 + 300 = 1955.15 \text{ s} \approx 32.59 \text{ min} $$ **Step 9 — Schedule Speed:** $$ V_s = \frac{L}{T_{total}} = \frac{12{,}000 \text{ m}}{1955.15 \text{ s}} = 6.14 \text{ m/s} = 22.10 \text{ km/h} $$ **Step 10 — Speed Coefficient:** $$ \eta = \frac{V_s}{V_c} = \frac{22.10}{40} = 0.5525 \approx 0.55 $$ > [!NOTE] > **Result Interpretation:** A speed coefficient of 0.55 is **typical for mixed-traffic bus operations** and confirms the need for dedicated lanes (BRT) to push η towards 0.75+. ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram: Time Components of a Transit Trip ```mermaid flowchart LR StartA([Trip Start]) --> T1[Acceleration Phase
t_acc] T1 --> T2[Cruise Phase
t_cruise] T2 --> T3[Deceleration Phase
t_dec] T3 --> T4[Station Dwell
t_dwell] T4 --> T5{More
Stations?} T5 -->|Yes| T1 T5 -->|No| T6[Terminal Layover
t_T] T6 --> EndA([Trip End]) style T1 fill:#ffd6d6 style T2 fill:#d6ffd6 style T3 fill:#ffd6d6 style T4 fill:#fff3b0 style T6 fill:#c9d6ff style T5 fill:#e0e0e0 ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram: Speed Classification Hierarchy ```mermaid graph TD A[Transit Speed Concepts] --> B[Cruise Speed Vc] A --> C[Schedule Speed Vs] A --> D[Average Speed Vavg] B --> B1[Vehicle in motion only] B --> B2[Excludes all stops] B --> B3[Design metric for vehicles] C --> C1[Includes dwell + layover] C --> C2[Passenger perception metric] C --> C3[Used in timetable design] D --> D1[Round trip basis] D --> D2[Includes recovery time] D --> D3[Fleet sizing metric] C --> E[Speed Coefficient η = Vs / Vc] B --> E E --> F1[η near 1.0: Metro / LRT] E --> F2[η 0.4 to 0.6: Mixed traffic bus] E --> F3[η 0.7 to 0.9: BRT corridor] style A fill:#1f3a93,color:#fff style E fill:#cc6600,color:#fff style F1 fill:#5cb85c,color:#fff style F2 fill:#d9534f,color:#fff style F3 fill:#f0ad4e,color:#000 ``` ### 4.3 Block Diagram: Factors Affecting Transit Speed (6 P Framework) ```mermaid graph TD SP[Speed of Transit Service] --> F1[Physical
ROW Type, Grade Separation] SP --> F2[Performance
Vehicle Accel, Power] SP --> F3[Passenger
Boarding Volume, Fare System] SP --> F4[Policy
Stop Pattern, Express Service] SP --> F5[Protection
Signal Priority, ATC] SP --> F6[Pavement
Weather, Grade, Surface] F1 --> R1[Effective Speed Output] F2 --> R1 F3 --> R1 F4 --> R1 F5 --> R1 F6 --> R1 R1 --> M1[Vs Schedule Speed km/h] R1 --> M2[η Speed Coefficient] R1 --> M3[LOS Level of Service Grade] style SP fill:#1f3a93,color:#fff style R1 fill:#5cb85c,color:#fff style M1 fill:#fff3b0 style M2 fill:#fff3b0 style M3 fill:#fff3b0 ``` ### 4.4 Block Diagram: Speed Improvement Strategies ```mermaid graph LR IM[Speed Improvement
Strategies] --> S1[Dedicated ROW
BRT Lanes] IM --> S2[Skip-Stop Service] IM --> S3[Express Routes] IM --> S4[Off-Board Fare Collection] IM --> S5[Multi-Door Boarding] IM --> S6[Signal Preemption] IM --> S7[Station Consolidation] IM --> S8[Higher Accel Vehicles] S1 --> EF[Increased Vs] S2 --> EF S3 --> EF S4 --> EF S5 --> EF S6 --> EF S7 --> EF S8 --> EF EF --> BG[Better Modal Share
Lower Operating Cost] style IM fill:#1f3a93,color:#fff style EF fill:#5cb85c,color:#fff style BG fill:#cc6600,color:#fff ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Distinguish between cruise speed and schedule speed of a transit vehicle. Which one represents the passenger's point of view, and why?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Aspect | Cruise Speed ($V_c$) | Schedule Speed ($V_s$) | |--------|----------------------|------------------------| | Definition | Speed when vehicle is **in motion only** | Average speed **including all stops and terminal time** | | Includes Dwell Time? | **No** | **Yes** | | Includes Terminal Layover? | **No** | **Yes** | | Used By | Vehicle engineers, designers | Passengers, planners | | Typical Magnitude | Higher (40–80 km/h) | Lower (15–35 km/h) | **Passenger Perspective:** Schedule speed represents the **passenger's actual experience** of the journey, because the passenger's door-to-door time includes the waiting at stops and the terminal layover. (3 marks) --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define "Speed Coefficient" in transit operations. What is its typical range for a metro system versus a mixed-traffic city bus?** **Model Answer:** The **Speed Coefficient (η)** is defined as the ratio of schedule speed to cruise speed: $$ \eta = \frac{V_s}{V_c} $$ It is a **dimensionless number** between 0 and 1 that measures the operational efficiency of a transit corridor. - **Metro / LRT (Grade-separated):** η = **0.85 to 0.95** (high, as there are no intersections or traffic delays). - **Mixed-Traffic City Bus:** η = **0.40 to 0.60** (low, due to signal delays, congestion, and frequent stops). (3 marks) --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** A transit line of length **18 km** has **20 uniformly spaced stations**. The cruise speed is **50 km/h**, the average dwell time per station is **25 s**, and the terminal layover is **6 minutes**. The vehicle accelerates at **1.0 m/s²** and decelerates at **1.4 m/s²**. Calculate: (i) Total one-way running time (excluding layover) (ii) Schedule speed (iii) Speed coefficient **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain with neat sketches the **time-distance diagram** of a transit vehicle between two stations, clearly marking the acceleration, cruise, deceleration, and dwell phases. Discuss how increasing station spacing affects the schedule speed. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution to Question A:** #### Part (a) — Numerical Solution (7 marks) **Given Data:** - $L = 18$ km $= 18{,}000$ m - $n = 20$ stations (so 20 segments) - $V_c = 50$ km/h $= 50/3.6 = 13.89$ m/s - $t_d = 25$ s, $t_T = 6 \times 60 = 360$ s - $a = 1.0$ m/s², $b = 1.4$ m/s² **Step 1 — Acceleration & Deceleration Distances per Segment:** $$ d_{acc} = \frac{V_c^2}{2a} = \frac{(13.89)^2}{2(1.0)} = 96.46 \text{ m} $$ $$ d_{dec} = \frac{V_c^2}{2b} = \frac{(13.89)^2}{2(1.4)} = 68.90 \text{ m} $$ **Step 2 — Total Accel + Decel Distance:** $$ D_{ad} = 20 \times (96.46 + 68.90) = 20 \times 165.36 = 3307.20 \text{ m} $$ **Step 3 — Pure Cruise Distance:** $$ D_{cruise} = 18{,}000 - 3307.20 = 14{,}692.80 \text{ m} $$ **Step 4 — Pure Cruise Time:** $$ t_{cruise} = \frac{14{,}692.80}{13.89} = 1057.80 \text{ s} $$ **Step 5 — Acceleration & Deceleration Times per Segment:** $$ t_a = \frac{V_c}{a} = \frac{13.89}{1.0} = 13.89 \text{ s} $$ $$ t_b = \frac{V_c}{b} = \frac{13.89}{1.4} = 9.92 \text{ s} $$ **Step 6 — Total Accel + Decel Time:** $$ T_{ad} = 20 \times (13.89 + 9.92) = 20 \times 23.81 = 476.20 \text{ s} $$ **Step 7 — Total Dwell Time:** $$ T_{dwell} = 20 \times 25 = 500 \text{ s} $$ **Step 8 — Total One-Way Running Time (Excluding Layover):** $$ T_{running} = T_{ad} + t_{cruise} + T_{dwell} = 476.20 + 1057.80 + 500 = 2034.00 \text{ s} \approx 33.90 \text{ min} $$ **(i) Total one-way running time = 33.90 minutes** ✓ **(3 marks)** **Step 9 — Total One-Way Time (Including Layover):** $$ T_{total} = 2034.00 + 360 = 2394.00 \text{ s} \approx 39.90 \text{ min} $$ **Step 10 — Schedule Speed:** $$ V_s = \frac{18{,}000 \text{ m}}{2394 \text{ s}} = 7.52 \text{ m/s} = 27.07 \text{ km/h} $$ **(ii) Schedule speed $V_s = 27.07$ km/h** ✓ **(2 marks)** **Step 11 — Speed Coefficient:** $$ \eta = \frac{V_s}{V_c} = \frac{27.07}{50} = 0.541 $$ **(iii) Speed coefficient $\eta = 0.541$** ✓ **(2 marks)** --- #### Part (b) — Time-Distance Diagram (7 marks) **Time-Distance Diagram Between Two Consecutive Stations:** The time-distance graph plots **distance (x-axis)** vs **time (y-axis)** between two stations A and B. ``` Distance | B | /\ | / \ | / \ | / \ (Decel) | / \ | / \ | / \ | / (Cruise) \ | / \ | / \ | / (Accel) \ | / \ | / Dwell \ | /|←————————→| \ | / | 25 s | \ A |___________________________/ | |________________\ 0 t_a t_a+t_c t_a+t_c+t_b Time (Station Stop) ``` **Phases Identified:** - **A→(Accel):** Concave-up curve, slope = speed, increasing. - **Accel→(Cruise):** Straight line with constant slope = $V_c$. - **Cruise→(Decel):** Concave-down curve, slope decreasing. - **Decel→(Dwell):** Horizontal line at station B (zero slope). - **Dwell→Next Accel:** Begins new cycle. **Effect of Increasing Station Spacing on Schedule Speed:** When station spacing $d$ increases: - The **acceleration + deceleration losses** become a smaller fraction of total segment time. - The **cruise time per segment** increases proportionally. - Total time $T_{total}$ grows **slower than distance $L$**, so $V_s$ **increases asymptotically towards $V_c$**. - This is the engineering rationale for **skip-stop service** and **BRT station consolidation** policies. Mathematically, the derivative $\frac{dV_s}{dd} > 0$ for all $d$, but $\frac{dV_s}{dd} \to 0$ as $d \to \infty$, confirming asymptotic behaviour. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** With the help of a **block diagram**, explain the **six categories of factors** that affect the speed of a transit service. For each factor, give one engineering example of how it can be **improved**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A metro rail operates on a 25 km line with 18 stations. The cruise speed is 70 km/h, dwell time per station is 20 s, terminal layover is 4 min, and the vehicle accelerates/decelerates at 1.2 m/s² and 1.5 m/s² respectively. Calculate: (i) Schedule speed (ii) Speed coefficient (iii) Comment on the operational efficiency. **(7 marks)** --- ### **Model Solution to Question B:** #### Part (a) — Six Factors Affecting Transit Speed (7 marks) **Block Diagram Summary:** | # | Factor | Description | Engineering Improvement Example | |---|--------|-------------|--------------------------------| | 1 | **Physical (ROW)** | Right-of-way category A/B/C | Construct **grade-separated metro corridor** | | 2 | **Performance (Vehicle)** | Acceleration, power, top speed | Deploy **low-floor, high-accel electric buses** | | 3 | **Passenger (Demand)** | Boarding-alighting, fare system | Introduce **off-board smart card fare** | | 4 | **Policy (Operation)** | Stop pattern, route type | Implement **skip-stop / express service** | | 5 | **Protection (Control)** | Signal systems, ATC | Install **transit signal priority (TSP)** | | 6 | **Pavement (Environment)** | Weather, grade, surface | Use **anti-skid road surface, snow clearance** | For each factor, the **engineering improvement** is shown in the right column. **(7 marks)** --- #### Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks) **Given Data:** - $L = 25$ km $= 25{,}000$ m - $n = 18$ stations - $V_c = 70$ km/h $= 70/3.6 = 19.44$ m/s - $t_d = 20$ s, $t_T = 4 \times 60 = 240$ s - $a = 1.2$ m/s², $b = 1.5$ m/s² **Step 1 — Accel/Decel Distances:** $$ d_{acc} = \frac{(19.44)^2}{2(1.2)} = 157.46 \text{ m} $$ $$ d_{dec} = \frac{(19.44)^2}{2(1.5)} = 125.97 \text{ m} $$ **Step 2 — Total Accel + Decel Distance:** $$ D_{ad} = 18 \times (157.46 + 125.97) = 18 \times 283.43 = 5101.74 \text{ m} $$ **Step 3 — Pure Cruise Distance & Time:** $$ D_{cruise} = 25{,}000 - 5101.74 = 19{,}898.26 \text{ m} $$ $$ t_{cruise} = \frac{19{,}898.26}{19.44} = 1023.57 \text{ s} $$ **Step 4 — Accel/Decel Times:** $$ t_a = \frac{19.44}{1.2} = 16.20 \text{ s} $$ $$ t_b = \frac{19.44}{1.5} = 12.96 \text{ s} $$ $$ T_{ad} = 18 \times (16.20 + 12.96) = 18 \times 29.16 = 524.88 \text{ s} $$ **Step 5 — Total Dwell Time:** $$ T_{dwell} = 18 \times 20 = 360 \text{ s} $$ **Step 6 — Total Time:** $$ T_{total} = 524.88 + 1023.57 + 360 + 240 = 2148.45 \text{ s} \approx 35.81 \text{ min} $$ **Step 7 — Schedule Speed:** $$ V_s = \frac{25{,}000}{2148.45} = 11.64 \text{ m/s} = 41.89 \text{ km/h} $$ **(i) Schedule speed $V_s \approx 41.89$ km/h** ✓ **(2 marks)** **Step 8 — Speed Coefficient:** $$ \eta = \frac{41.89}{70} = 0.598 $$ **(ii) Speed coefficient $\eta \approx 0.60$** ✓ **(2 marks)** **(iii) Comment on Operational Efficiency:** The speed coefficient of **0.60** is **moderate**. It indicates that **40% of the vehicle's cruise speed is lost** to acceleration, deceleration, station dwell, and terminal layover. For a metro system operating on a **dedicated right-of-way**, this value should ideally be **0.80–0.90**. The lower value suggests: - **Frequent station stops** (18 stations on 25 km → average spacing 1.39 km, which is reasonable, but dwell time of 20 s may be excessive). - **Recommendation:** Reduce dwell time through **platform screen doors (PSDs)** and **uniform door-closing protocols**; consider **skip-stop service** during peak hours. **(3 marks)** --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Unit Conversion Error:** Forgetting to convert km/h to m/s when using $V_c^2 / 2a$ formula. This alone costs **2 marks** in most numericals. > 2. **Misidentifying "Total Time":** Schedule speed uses **one-way total time including layover**; running time calculations **exclude layover**. Conflating the two is a frequent error. > 3. **Forgetting Station Dwell:** Students often compute accel + cruise + decel only and forget $t_{dwell}$ and $t_T$. This is a **3-mark deduction**. > 4. **Confusing η with LOS:** Speed coefficient is a **physical metric**, while Level of Service is a **qualitative grade (A–F)**. Examiners specifically look for this distinction. > 5. **No Sketch in Descriptive Answers:** For 7-mark theory questions, a **diagram is mandatory**. Omitting it forfeits **2–3 marks** even if the written content is correct. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Speed of Transit Service** > > - **Three Speeds:** Cruise ($V_c$) > Schedule ($V_s$) > Average ($V_{avg}$). Always in that hierarchy. > - **Speed Coefficient:** $\eta = V_s / V_c$. Range: **0.4 (poor bus)** to **0.95 (excellent metro)**. > - **Total Trip Time = 5 Components:** $t_{acc} + t_{cruise} + t_{dec} + t_{dwell} + t_{terminal}$. > - **Accel/Decel Distance:** $d = V_c^2 / (2a)$ — independent of how long acceleration takes. > - **Passenger-Experienced Speed = Schedule Speed** (not cruise speed). > - **Station Spacing ↑ → Schedule Speed ↑** (asymptotic, never exceeds $V_c$). > - **6 P Framework:** Physical, Performance, Passenger, Policy, Protection, Pavement. > - **Unit Conversion:** 1 m/s = 3.6 km/h (memorise this). > - **Speed Improvements:** Dedicated ROW, skip-stop, off-board fare, multi-door, TSP, station consolidation. > - **Formula to Remember:** $V_s = L / (t_{acc} + t_{cruise} + t_{dec} + t_{dwell} + t_{terminal})$. > - **Typical Values:** Walking 4–5, Bus (mixed) 12–18, BRT 20–30, Metro 25–45, Rail 80–300 km/h. > - **Master the Derivation:** The $V_c$ quadratic from $L = V_c t_c + \frac{V_c^2}{2}(\frac{1}{a} + \frac{1}{b})$ is a **guaranteed KTU question**.

topics.title: Passenger Demand: Factors and Elasticity. Stops and Stopping Regimes: Definitions and Relationships
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET833 - PUBLIC TRANSPORTATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Passenger Demand: Factors and Elasticity. Stops and Stopping Regimes: Definitions and Relationships

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Passenger Demand — Formal Definition

In Public Transportation Systems, **Passenger Demand** is formally defined as the number of passengers (or trips) desiring to travel between a given origin–destination (O-D) pair, during a specified time period, under specified service conditions and fare levels. It is a fundamental variable that governs route planning, frequency setting, vehicle sizing, and capacity provision.

Mathematically, the aggregate demand function for a public transport mode can be expressed as:

$$Q_d = f(P, S, X, I, T, U)$$

where:
- $Q_d$ = passenger demand (passengers/hour or trips/day)
- $P$ = fare (price)
- $S$ = level of service (frequency, speed, reliability)
- $X$ = socioeconomic attributes of the catchment population
- $I$ = income of potential users
- $T$ = trip purpose (work, education, leisure)
- $U$ = attributes of competing modes

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Demand in PT is a *derived* and *induced* demand — it does not exist independently but is derived from the spatial distribution of activities (homes, workplaces) and is induced (or suppressed) by the quality of service offered.

### Intuitive Analogy

Think of a public transport route as a **garden hose**. Passenger demand is the *water pressure* pushing through the hose. If you narrow the hose (reduce service frequency, raise fares), the water flow drops — fewer passengers use it. If you widen the hose (more buses, lower fares, faster service), the flow increases. Elasticity tells you **how sensitively** the flow responds when you squeeze or release the hose.

## 1.2 Stops and Stopping Regimes — Formal Definition

A **Stop** is a designated location along a transit route where vehicles are scheduled to allow boarding and alighting of passengers. A **Stopping Regime** (or stopping pattern) is the predetermined sequence and selection of stops served by a transit vehicle during a single run along a route.

The principal stopping regimes in urban and regional public transport are:

1. **Local / All-Stops Service** — the vehicle halts at every stop.
2. **Express Service** — the vehicle skips intermediate stops between two major nodes.
3. **Semi-Express / Limited-Stop Service** — stops only at high-demand stops.
4. **Skip-Stop (A/B Alternating) Service** — parallel routes alternately serve every other stop.
5. **Zonal / Sector Service** — the line is divided into zones, with express segments between zone centres.
6. **On-Request (Flag-Stop) Service** — stops are made only when passengers signal the driver.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical/Operational Constants to Remember:**
> - Typical urban bus dwell time: **20–60 seconds**
> - Typical metro dwell time: **20–40 seconds**
> - Typical acceleration: **0.8–1.2 m/s²** for buses, **1.0–1.3 m/s²** for metros
> - Typical deceleration: **1.0–1.3 m/s²**
> - Passenger boarding time: **1.5–3.0 s/passenger** (single door)

### Intuitive Analogy

Imagine a long-distance train travelling from Kochi to Bengaluru. If it halts at every small station, it would take 18 hours. If it stops only at major junctions (Ernakulam, Thrissur, Palakkad, Coimbatore, Bengaluru), it might take just 10 hours. The **stopping regime** is the "rule book" telling the train *where* it is allowed to pause. Each skipped stop saves time but loses some passengers who cannot board — this is the **fundamental trade-off** at the heart of stopping-regime design.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Factors Affecting Passenger Demand

Passenger demand for a public transport service is shaped by **five broad categories** of factors. Each is examined below with its physical/logical interpretation.

### 2.1.1 External (Exogenous) Factors

These lie outside the direct control of the transit operator:

- **Demographic structure** — population size, age distribution, household size in the catchment.
- **Land-use pattern** — density, mix of residential/commercial/industrial activities.
- **Economic factors** — disposable income, employment rate, fuel price, car-ownership levels.
- **Trip-purpose distribution** — peak work/education trips are highly inelastic; leisure trips are elastic.
- **Climate and geography** — hilly/coastal regions (Kerala context) affect modal choice.
- **Government policy** — subsidies, taxation on private vehicles, parking restrictions.

### 2.1.2 Internal (Service-Quality) Factors

These are largely under the operator's control:

- **Fare (P)** — direct inverse relationship with demand.
- **Frequency (f)** — higher frequency reduces wait time, increasing demand.
- **Travel time / Speed** — faster service attracts more riders.
- **Reliability / Punctuality** — variance in arrival time reduces demand.
- **Comfort** — crowding, seat availability, air-conditioning.
- **Accessibility** — distance to stops, last-mile connectivity.
- **Information systems** — real-time tracking, journey planners.
- **Safety and security** — perception of crime at stops and inside vehicles.

### 2.1.3 Spatial Factors

- **Trip length distribution** — short trips favour buses; long trips favour rail.
- **Origin–destination patterns** — concentration vs. dispersion.
- **Network connectivity** — number of transfer points, direct vs. interchange trips.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Tip:** In 14-mark Part B questions on demand, examiners expect you to discuss *at least three* external and *three* internal factors, and link them to elasticity. A purely listed answer without linking to demand behaviour will lose 2–3 marks.

## 2.2 Elasticity of Demand — Conceptual Framework

**Elasticity** is a unit-free measure of the *responsiveness* of demand to a change in a determinant. It is a pure number (dimensionless), making it ideal for cross-service and cross-city comparisons.

The general elasticity definition is:

$$E_x = \frac{\%\Delta Q}{\%\Delta X} = \frac{\partial Q}{\partial X} \cdot \frac{X}{Q}$$

### 2.2.1 Price Elasticity of Demand (PED)

$$E_P = \frac{\partial Q}{\partial P} \cdot \frac{P}{Q}$$

- For urban bus transit: $E_P \approx -0.2$ to $-0.6$ (typical range, inelastic in short run).
- Peak-hour commuters: $\vert E_P \vert < 0.3$ (highly inelastic — captive riders).
- Off-peak leisure riders: $\vert E_P \vert > 0.6$ (elastic — discretionary trips).

### 2.2.2 Service Elasticity (Quality Elasticity)

Service elasticity can be decomposed into:

$$E_{S,total} = E_{frequency} + E_{travel\,time} + E_{reliability} + E_{comfort}$$

Empirically observed values (TRB / Kittelson & Associates):

| Service Attribute | Typical Elasticity (absolute) | Peak | Off-Peak |
|---|---|---|---|
| Frequency | 0.30 – 0.70 | Lower | Higher |
| In-vehicle travel time | 0.30 – 0.60 | Lower | Higher |
| Walk access time | 0.30 – 0.50 | — | — |
| Wait time | 0.50 – 0.80 | — | — |
| Fare | 0.20 – 0.60 | Lower | Higher |
| Reliability (1% improvement) | 0.05 – 0.15 | — | — |

### 2.2.3 Cross-Elasticity of Demand

$$E_{XY} = \frac{\partial Q_X / Q_X}{\partial P_Y / P_Y}$$

- Positive cross-elasticity $\Rightarrow$ **substitutes** (bus ↔ metro, car ↔ taxi).
- Negative cross-elasticity $\Rightarrow$ **complements** (parking ↔ transit).

### 2.2.4 Income Elasticity

$$E_I = \frac{\partial Q / Q}{\partial I / I}$$

- For urban bus transit: $E_I \approx -0.3$ to $+0.2$ (often *negative* — bus is an "inferior good" relative to private car).
- For metro/rail: $E_I \approx +0.3$ to $+0.6$ (a "normal/luxury" good).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of Thumb — Sign Convention:**
> - $E_P < 0$ (law of demand)
> - $E_S > 0$ (better service → more demand)
> - $E_I$ may be positive or negative depending on mode
> - $\vert E \vert > 1$ → **elastic** (demand is highly responsive)
> - $\vert E \vert < 1$ → **inelastic** (demand is unresponsive)

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Typical Range / Note |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | General Elasticity | $E_x = \dfrac{\partial Q}{\partial x} \cdot \dfrac{x}{Q}$ | Dimensionless | $\vert E\vert$ classifies responsiveness |
| 2 | Price Elasticity | $E_P = \dfrac{\Delta Q / Q}{\Delta P / P}$ | Dimensionless | $-0.6 \le E_P \le -0.2$ (bus) |
| 3 | Arc Elasticity (mid-point) | $E = \dfrac{(Q_2-Q_1)/[(Q_1+Q_2)/2]}{(P_2-P_1)/[(P_1+P_2)/2]}$ | Dimensionless | Used for discrete data |
| 4 | Income Elasticity | $E_I = \dfrac{\Delta Q}{\Delta I} \cdot \dfrac{I}{Q}$ | Dimensionless | Sign indicates normal/inferior good |
| 5 | Cross Elasticity | $E_{XY} = \dfrac{\partial Q_X}{\partial P_Y} \cdot \dfrac{P_Y}{Q_X}$ | Dimensionless | +ve = substitute, −ve = complement |
| 6 | Total Travel Time | $T_{total} = T_{run} + T_{stop} + T_{dwell}$ | minutes | Includes access, wait, in-vehicle |
| 7 | Dwell Time (per stop) | $t_d = t_{o} + t_{c} \cdot n_{max}$ | seconds | $t_c$ = boarding+alighting time/passenger |
| 8 | Stopping Time Penalty | $\Delta t_{stop} = t_a + t_d + t_{decel}$ | seconds | Time lost per intermediate stop |
| 9 | Average Operating Speed | $V_{op} = \dfrac{L}{T_{run} + N_s \cdot \Delta t_{stop}}$ | km/h | $N_s$ = number of stops |
| 10 | Skipped-Stop Time Saved | $\Delta T = N_{skipped} \cdot \Delta t_{stop}$ | seconds | $N_{skipped}$ = number of stops skipped |
| 11 | Revenue | $R = P \cdot Q$ | currency units | Used in elasticity × revenue analysis |
| 12 | Total Generalised Cost | $GC = \alpha \cdot T + \beta \cdot P + \gamma \cdot W$ | minutes (equivalent) | $W$ = waiting, $\alpha,\beta,\gamma$ are weights |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** The elasticity framework is used by KSRTC, BMTC, and Kochi Metro to predict ridership changes before fare revisions and to design optimal frequency. Stopping-regime formulas feed directly into timetable software (e.g., HASTUS, Trapeze).

## 2.4 The "Why" Behind the Trade-off in Stopping Regime Design

The fundamental engineering trade-off is:

$$\text{Minimise: } T_{travel} \; \text{(favours fewer stops)} \quad \leftrightarrow \quad \text{Maximise: } \text{Accessibility / Coverage} \; \text{(favours more stops)}$$

- Adding a stop **reduces** the catchment coverage gap but **increases** the travel time of all *through* passengers.
- Removing a stop **increases** the average speed but **excludes** passengers near the removed stop, forcing them to walk further or use a feeder mode.
- The optimal number of stops is where the *marginal travel-time cost to existing riders* equals the *marginal ridership gain* from the new stop's catchment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Iron Rule of Stopping Regimes":** Every stop is a **two-way contract** — passengers gain access at the cost of all others' time. The optimal design balances this equity–efficiency trade-off through *segmentation*: local services for short trips, express services for long trips, with timed transfers at major nodes.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: From Demand Function to Arc Elasticity

Assume a linear demand function between two observed points $(P_1, Q_1)$ and $(P_2, Q_2)$:

$$Q = a - bP$$

**Step 1** — Identify the slope of the demand curve:

$$\frac{\Delta Q}{\Delta P} = \frac{Q_2 - Q_1}{P_2 - P_1} = -b$$

(The slope is constant for a linear demand function.)

**Step 2** — Apply the point-elasticity formula at a generic point $(P, Q)$:

$$E_P = \frac{\partial Q}{\partial P} \cdot \frac{P}{Q} = (-b) \cdot \frac{P}{Q} = -\frac{bP}{Q}$$

**Step 3** — Since $b = (Q_1 - Q_2)/(P_2 - P_1)$ and $Q = a - bP$, the elasticity *varies along the curve*, being:
- Elastic ($\vert E\vert > 1$) at high $P$ (low $Q$) — upper portion.
- Unit elastic ($\vert E\vert = 1$) at the midpoint.
- Inelastic ($\vert E\vert < 1$) at low $P$ (high $Q$) — lower portion.

**Step 4** — The **arc (mid-point) elasticity** averages $P$ and $Q$ at both points to give a single representative value:

$$E_{arc} = \frac{(Q_2 - Q_1) / [(Q_1 + Q_2)/2]}{(P_2 - P_1) / [(P_1 + P_2)/2]}$$

This is the form most often used in transport data analysis because it does not depend on the choice of base point.

## 3.2 Worked Example: Price Elasticity of a Kochi City Bus Route

**Problem:** On Route K-12 (Aluva to Fort Kochi), the fare was increased from ₹20 to ₹25. Daily ridership dropped from 18,000 to 16,500. Calculate the arc price elasticity of demand and comment on whether a further fare increase is advisable if the operator's goal is revenue maximisation.

**Step 1** — Compute the percentage change in quantity:

$$\%\Delta Q = \frac{Q_2 - Q_1}{(Q_1 + Q_2)/2} = \frac{16500 - 18000}{(18000 + 16500)/2} = \frac{-1500}{17250} = -0.08696 \;(\text{i.e. } -8.70\%)$$

**Step 2** — Compute the percentage change in price:

$$\%\Delta P = \frac{P_2 - P_1}{(P_1 + P_2)/2} = \frac{25 - 20}{(20 + 25)/2} = \frac{5}{22.5} = 0.2222 \;(\text{i.e. } +22.22\%)$$

**Step 3** — Compute the arc elasticity:

$$E_P = \frac{\%\Delta Q}{\%\Delta P} = \frac{-0.08696}{0.2222} = -0.3914$$

**Step 4** — Interpret:

$$\vert E_P \vert = 0.39 < 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Demand is INELASTIC}$$

**Step 5** — Revenue analysis:

$$R_1 = 20 \times 18000 = ₹3,60,000$$
$$R_2 = 25 \times 16500 = ₹4,12,500$$
$$\Delta R = +₹52,500 \; (\text{+14.6\% increase})$$

**Step 6** — Conclusion:

Since $\vert E_P \vert < 1$, a further fare increase is *advisable in the short run* because revenue rises. However, the operator must monitor:
- **Long-run elasticity** (typically 1.5–2× the short-run value).
- **Service-quality degradation** if fare is raised without quality improvement.
- **Equity concerns** for captive low-income riders.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Key:** Always state **both** the numerical elasticity *and* the revenue implication. A common 1-mark deduction is for failing to comment on whether demand is elastic or inelastic.

## 3.3 Derivation: Stopping Regime — Travel Time Penalty

Let a transit line have:
- $L$ = total route length (km)
- $V_{max}$ = cruise (maximum) speed (km/h)
- $N_s$ = number of stops served
- $\Delta t_{stop}$ = average time penalty per stop (s)
- $a$ = acceleration (m/s²)
- $d$ = deceleration (m/s²)

**Step 1** — Time to accelerate from 0 to $V_{max}$:

$$t_a = \frac{V_{max}}{a} \quad \text{(in seconds, with } V_{max} \text{ in m/s)}$$

**Step 2** — Distance covered during acceleration:

$$s_a = \frac{V_{max}^2}{2a}$$

**Step 3** — Similarly for deceleration: $t_d = V_{max}/d$ and $s_d = V_{max}^2 / (2d)$.

**Step 4** — Cruise distance between consecutive stops:

$$s_{cruise} = d_{i,i+1} - s_a - s_d$$

**Step 5** — Cruise time between stops:

$$t_{cruise} = \frac{s_{cruise}}{V_{max}}$$

**Step 6** — Total time per inter-stop segment:

$$t_{segment} = t_a + t_{cruise} + t_d + t_{dwell}$$

**Step 7** — Total route time (sum over $N_s$ segments):

$$T_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N_s} t_{segment,i} = \frac{N_s \cdot L}{V_{max}} + N_s \cdot \Delta t_{stop}$$

(under uniform spacing assumption)

**Step 8** — Average operating speed:

$$V_{op} = \frac{L \cdot 3600}{T_{total} \cdot 1000} = \frac{3600 \cdot L}{T_{total}} \quad \text{(in km/h if } T \text{ in seconds)}$$

Equivalently:

$$V_{op} = \frac{L}{T_{total}/60} = \frac{60 \cdot L}{T_{total}} \quad \text{(km/h with } T \text{ in minutes)}$$

## 3.4 Worked Example: Express vs Local Service Time

**Problem:** A 20 km metro line with $V_{max} = 60$ km/h has 20 stations spaced 1 km apart (local service). Dwell time $t_{dwell} = 30$ s, acceleration = deceleration = 1.2 m/s². Calculate:
(a) Run time for local service.
(b) Run time if an express skips 15 stations.
(c) Reduction in journey time and percentage improvement.

**Given:**
- $L = 20$ km, $V_{max} = 60$ km/h = $16.667$ m/s
- $a = d = 1.2$ m/s²
- $N_s = 20$ (local), $N_{s,exp} = 5$ (express)
- $t_{dwell} = 30$ s

**Step 1** — Acceleration and deceleration times:

$$t_a = t_d = \frac{V_{max}}{a} = \frac{16.667}{1.2} = 13.89 \; \text{s}$$

**Step 2** — Total acceleration + deceleration time per stop (round trip):

$$t_{ad} = t_a + t_d = 13.89 + 13.89 = 27.78 \; \text{s}$$

**Step 3** — Total stop penalty per intermediate stop:

$$\Delta t_{stop} = t_{ad} + t_{dwell} = 27.78 + 30 = 57.78 \; \text{s}$$

**Step 4** — Cruise speed: $V_{max} = 60$ km/h = 1 km/min.

**Step 5** — **Local service** ($N_s = 20$, all stops):

$$T_{local} = \frac{L}{V_{max}} \times 60 + N_s \times \Delta t_{stop} = \frac{20}{1} + 20 \times 57.78 = 20 + 1155.6 \; \text{s}$$

$$T_{local} = 1175.6 \; \text{s} = 19.59 \; \text{minutes}$$

**Step 6** — **Express service** ($N_s = 5$, 15 skipped):

$$T_{exp} = 20 + 5 \times 57.78 = 20 + 288.9 = 308.9 \; \text{s} = 5.15 \; \text{minutes}$$

**Step 7** — Time saved and percentage improvement:

$$\Delta T = 19.59 - 5.15 = 14.44 \; \text{minutes}$$

$$\%\text{ Improvement} = \frac{14.44}{19.59} \times 100 = 73.7\%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** A 75% reduction in stops (from 20 to 5) yields a ~74% reduction in travel time. This is because **stop penalties dominate** the total run time when $V_{max}$ is moderate. This is *the* key justification for express metro services and skip-stop bus services in cities like Kochi, Bengaluru, and Mumbai.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Stopping Regimes Comparison

| Stopping Regime | Stops Served | Typical Use | Travel Time | Passenger Suitability | Operational Complexity |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Local (All-Stops)** | All | Urban bus, short metro runs | Longest | Short-trip, captive, elderly, low-income | Low (simple timetable) |
| **Limited / Semi-Express** | Selected major | Trunk bus, commuter rail | Medium | Medium-trip, work trips | Medium (skip-stop timetable) |
| **Express** | Only terminals | Inter-city rail, airport metro | Shortest | Long-trip, business, airport access | High (track/passing loops) |
| **Skip-Stop (A/B)** | Alternating | High-frequency metro (NYC, Chicago) | Medium | All trips but uneven coverage | High (passenger confusion) |
| **Zonal** | Zone centres only | Regional rail, BRT | Medium-low | Medium-long trips | High (zone boundaries) |
| **On-Request (Flag-Stop)** | Passenger-initiated | Rural bus, night services | Variable | Low-density rural | Low (no schedule) |

## 3.6 Relationship Between Stop Spacing and Average Speed

For a line of length $L$ with uniform station spacing $d$ and constant stop penalty $\Delta t_{stop}$, the average speed is:

$$V_{op} = \frac{L}{\dfrac{L}{V_{max}} + N_s \cdot \Delta t_{stop}} = \frac{V_{max}}{1 + \dfrac{\Delta t_{stop} \cdot V_{max}}{d}}$$

As $d \to \infty$ (no stops), $V_{op} \to V_{max}$. As $d \to 0$ (stops at every metre), $V_{op} \to 0$. This is a **hyperbolic relationship** — the marginal benefit of increased spacing diminishes rapidly beyond ~1 km in metros and ~400 m in urban buses.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Define price elasticity of demand for public transport. State the typical range of price elasticity for urban bus services in peak hours and explain why it differs from off-peak values.**

**Model Answer:**

Price elasticity of demand (PED) is the ratio of the percentage change in quantity demanded to the percentage change in price:

$$E_P = \frac{\%\Delta Q}{\%\Delta P} = \frac{\Delta Q}{\Delta P} \cdot \frac{P}{Q}$$

For urban bus services:
- **Peak hours:** $E_P \approx -0.2$ to $-0.4$ (highly inelastic). Peak-hour commuters are *captive* — they have no alternative (work timings fixed, no car, no other mode). They continue to ride even after fare hikes.
- **Off-peak hours:** $E_P \approx -0.6$ to $-1.0$ (elastic or near-unit). Off-peak riders are *discretionary* (shopping, leisure, social) and can shift to alternative modes or cancel trips when fares rise.

The difference arises because the **availability of substitutes** is much greater for off-peak trips (private car, two-wheeler, walking, cycle rickshaw, auto-rickshaw) than for peak-hour captive work trips.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**List the major stopping regimes used in public transport. State the relationship between the number of stops and the average operating speed of a transit line.**

**Model Answer:**

Major stopping regimes:
1. Local (all-stops) service
2. Limited-stop / semi-express service
3. Express service
4. Skip-stop (alternating A/B) service
5. Zonal service
6. On-request (flag-stop) service

**Relationship:** Average operating speed is **inversely related** to the number of stops (for a fixed route length and constant stop penalty). It is governed by:

$$V_{op} = \frac{V_{max}}{1 + \dfrac{\Delta t_{stop} \cdot V_{max}}{d}}$$

where $d$ is the average inter-stop distance. As the number of stops $N_s$ increases, $d$ decreases, and $V_{op}$ decreases hyperbolically. Conversely, reducing stops increases $V_{op}$ but reduces spatial coverage and accessibility.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the various **factors affecting passenger demand** in public transportation systems. Classify them as internal, external, and spatial factors with suitable examples. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A city bus route carries 12,000 passengers per day at a fare of ₹15. The transit authority raises the fare to ₹18, and ridership falls to 10,800. Calculate the **arc price elasticity of demand** and state whether a further fare increase is justified from a revenue perspective. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

Passenger demand for public transport is a multivariate function:

$$Q_d = f(P, S, X, I, T, U, E)$$

**Internal factors** (operator-controlled):
- **Fare (P):** Direct inverse relationship; higher fare → lower demand. Often inelastic for captive riders.
- **Frequency (f):** Higher frequency reduces wait time $W \approx 1/(2f)$, increasing demand.
- **Travel time / Speed:** Faster service (higher $V_{op}$) attracts passengers; travel-time elasticity $\approx -0.4$.
- **Reliability:** Coefficient of variation of arrival time; lower CV → higher demand (elasticity $\approx +0.1$).
- **Comfort:** Crowding, air-conditioning, seat availability.
- **Information:** Real-time tracking, mobile apps.
- **Safety:** Crime perception at stops and inside vehicles.

**External factors** (exogenous to operator):
- **Demographics:** Population density, age structure (Kerala's high elderly ratio influences KSRTC services).
- **Income (I):** Higher income → shift to private car (bus is an "inferior good" in many cities).
- **Fuel price:** Indirectly affects both PT and car cost, shifting modal share.
- **Land-use pattern:** Mixed-use development increases walk-to-stop demand.
- **Policy:** Subsidies, parking restrictions, congestion pricing.

**Spatial factors**:
- **Trip length distribution:** Short trips favour bus, long trips favour rail.
- **Network density:** More transfer points can reduce or increase total demand depending on directness.

Each factor contributes to a measurable elasticity value used in demand modelling.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $P_1 = ₹15$, $Q_1 = 12,000$; $P_2 = ₹18$, $Q_2 = 10,800$.

**Step 1 — Percentage change in quantity:**

$$\%\Delta Q = \frac{10800 - 12000}{(12000 + 10800)/2} = \frac{-1200}{11400} = -0.10526 \;(-10.53\%)$$

**Step 2 — Percentage change in price:**

$$\%\Delta P = \frac{18 - 15}{(15 + 18)/2} = \frac{3}{16.5} = 0.18182 \;(+18.18\%)$$

**Step 3 — Arc price elasticity:**

$$E_P = \frac{-0.10526}{0.18182} = -0.579$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:** $\vert E_P \vert = 0.579 < 1$ → **Inelastic demand**.

**Step 5 — Revenue check:**

$$R_1 = 15 \times 12000 = ₹1,80,000$$
$$R_2 = 18 \times 10800 = ₹1,94,400$$
$$\Delta R = +₹14,400 \;(\text{+8.0\% increase})$$

**Step 6 — Conclusion:** Since demand is inelastic ($\vert E_P \vert < 1$) and revenue has *increased* by ₹14,400, a **further fare increase is justified in the short run**. However, the operator must consider:
- **Long-run elasticity** will be 1.5–2× the short-run value (riders adapt over time).
- **Service quality** must not deteriorate.
- **Equity** of captive low-income riders.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Define **stopping regime** in public transport. Describe, with neat sketches, the different types of stopping regimes used in transit operation. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A metro line of length 25 km operates with a maximum speed of 80 km/h and serves 25 stations. Dwell time per station is 25 s, and acceleration/deceleration are both 1.0 m/s². Estimate: (i) the average operating speed for all-stops service, (ii) the operating speed if an express service skips 20 stations. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Definition:** A stopping regime is the *predetermined pattern* of stops that a transit vehicle serves on a given run, dictating where the vehicle decelerates, halts, dwells, and accelerates.

**Types of stopping regimes** (with textual schematic):

**(i) Local / All-Stops Service:**
```
O ---*---*---*---*---*--- D
```
Every stop is served. Used in urban bus, short metro runs, feeder services.

**(ii) Express Service:**
```
O ====================== D
```
Only origin and destination (and possibly one or two major nodes) are served. Used in airport metros, inter-city rail.

**(iii) Limited-Stop / Semi-Express:**
```
O ---*=========*=========*--- D
```
Major stops only; intermediate stops skipped. Used in trunk bus, commuter rail.

**(iv) Skip-Stop (A/B Alternating):**
```
Route A:  O --*----*----*----*-- D
Route B:  O ----*----*----*----* D
```
Two parallel services alternate between even-numbered and odd-numbered stops. Reduces travel time while maintaining coverage.

**(v) Zonal Service:**
```
[Zone 1]===[Zone 2]===[Zone 3]
   *  *       *  *       *  *
```
Stops within zones; express between zones. Used in regional rail.

**(vi) On-Request (Flag-Stop):**
```
O ---?---?---?---?--- D
```
Stops only when a passenger signals. Used in rural and night services.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**
- $L = 25$ km, $V_{max} = 80$ km/h = $22.22$ m/s
- $N_{s,local} = 25$, $N_{s,exp} = 5$ (skips 20)
- $t_{dwell} = 25$ s
- $a = d = 1.0$ m/s²

**Step 1 — Compute stop penalty:**

$$t_a = t_d = \frac{V_{max}}{a} = \frac{22.22}{1.0} = 22.22 \; \text{s}$$
$$\Delta t_{stop} = t_a + t_{dwell} + t_d = 22.22 + 25 + 22.22 = 69.44 \; \text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to minutes:** $\Delta t_{stop} = 69.44/60 = 1.1574$ min.

**Step 3 — Cruise time (all-stops):**

$$T_{cruise} = \frac{25 \text{ km}}{80 \text{ km/h}} = 0.3125 \; \text{h} = 18.75 \; \text{min}$$

**Step 4 — Local service time:**

$$T_{local} = 18.75 + 25 \times 1.1574 = 18.75 + 28.93 = 47.68 \; \text{min}$$

**Step 5 — Local operating speed:**

$$V_{op,local} = \frac{25 \text{ km}}{47.68/60 \text{ h}} = \frac{25 \times 60}{47.68} = 31.46 \; \text{km/h}$$

**Step 6 — Express service time:**

$$T_{exp} = 18.75 + 5 \times 1.1574 = 18.75 + 5.79 = 24.54 \; \text{min}$$

**Step 7 — Express operating speed:**

$$V_{op,exp} = \frac{25 \times 60}{24.54} = 61.12 \; \text{km/h}$$

**Step 8 — Comparison:**

$$V_{op,exp} - V_{op,local} = 61.12 - 31.46 = 29.66 \; \text{km/h improvement}$$
$$\%\text{ Improvement} = \frac{29.66}{31.46} \times 100 = 94.3\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting dwell time** in $\Delta t_{stop}$ (deduct 2 marks).
> 2. **Mixing units** (m/s vs. km/h) when calculating $t_a$ (deduct 1 mark).
> 3. **Not converting seconds to minutes** consistently (deduct 1 mark).
> 4. **Skipping the "interpretation" step** — examiner wants both the numerical answer *and* a one-line engineering comment (e.g., "express service nearly doubles operating speed").
> 5. **In elasticity questions**, always show the formula *before* substitution; partial credit is awarded for correct setup even if the arithmetic is wrong.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Passenger Demand** is a multivariate function of fare, service quality, demographics, income, and external factors.
- **Elasticity** is a *dimensionless* ratio: %ΔQ / %ΔX. It is the most important metric for evaluating fare and service changes.
- **Price elasticity** of urban bus: typically $\vert E_P \vert = 0.2 - 0.6$; **inelastic in peak hours**, **elastic in off-peak hours**.
- **Service elasticity** of frequency: $E_f \approx 0.3 - 0.7$; **travel-time elasticity** $E_t \approx -0.3$ to $-0.6$.
- **Income elasticity** of bus is often *negative* (inferior good); for metro/rail it is *positive* (normal good).
- **Cross-elasticity** is positive for substitutes (bus ↔ metro) and negative for complements (transit ↔ parking).
- **Stopping regimes** include local, limited, express, skip-stop, zonal, and on-request. Each represents a different trade-off between travel time and coverage.
- **Stop penalty** $\Delta t_{stop} = t_a + t_{dwell} + t_d$ — acceleration, dwell, and deceleration time per stop.
- **Average operating speed** is governed by $V_{op} = V_{max} / (1 + \Delta t_{stop} \cdot V_{max} / d)$ where $d$ is inter-stop distance.
- **Express service** can roughly **double** the operating speed of an all-stops metro line.
- **The fundamental trade-off**: more stops = more coverage = slower service = lower demand elasticity realised through longer journey times.
- **Optimal stop spacing** is reached when the marginal travel-time cost of an extra stop to existing riders equals the marginal ridership gain from the new stop's catchment.
- **Arc (mid-point) elasticity** is preferred over point elasticity in transport data analysis because it does not depend on the choice of base observation.
- **Revenue maximisation** occurs on the elastic portion of a demand curve; in transit, peak-hour demand is often inelastic, so short-run fare hikes can raise revenue — but long-run elasticity reverses this.
- **Stop penalty dominance**: when $V_{max}$ is moderate (≤80 km/h) and stops are frequent, the stopping-time component often exceeds the running-time component, justifying express services.
- **KTU 2024 weightage**: Module 2 typically carries 15–20 marks in the End Semester Exam; expect one full 14-mark question (with internal choice) and one 3-mark Part A question on this topic.
- **Numerical questions** on this topic are *algebraic* — no calculus is required, but students must be fluent in unit conversion (s, min, m/s, km/h) and percentage change calculations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Practical and Optimal Values of Stop Spacing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET833 - PUBLIC TRANSPORTATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Practical and Optimal Values of Stop Spacing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Stop Spacing (S):** The average linear distance (in metres or kilometres) between two consecutive stops along a transit route. It is the single most influential design parameter governing the **trade-off between passenger accessibility and operating speed** in fixed-route public transport.

Mathematically, for a route of length $L$ having $n$ uniformly distributed stops:

$$S = \frac{L}{n-1} \approx \frac{L}{n}$$

The inverse $\lambda = 1/S$ is called the **stop density** (stops per km).

**Units of measure used in KTU examinations:** $m$ (preferred for urban modes), $km$ (for regional/rail modes).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world Analogy — "The Ladder of Trade-offs":**
> Think of a transit route as a **staircase**. Each stop is a step. If steps are placed too close together (small $S$), the passenger climbs slowly and gets tired quickly, but the staircase (route) is short to enter from any point. If steps are too far apart (large $S$), the passenger walks fast between them, but has to travel a long way to find the staircase in the first place.
> The **optimal stop spacing** is the step distance that gives the **shortest total "climbing time"** for the average user — neither too crowded with stops, nor too sparse.

In simpler terms:
- **More stops** → easy access, but slow journey.
- **Fewer stops** → fast journey, but passengers walk longer to catch the bus.

### 1.3 Why Stop Spacing Matters in Engineering Design

Stop spacing directly controls four major performance indicators of any transit system:

1. **Access/Egress Time** ($T_a$): Time passengers walk to/from stops. Inversely related to $S$.
2. **In-Vehicle Time** ($T_v$): Time on board the vehicle. Directly related to $S$ (more stops = more delays).
3. **Operating Cost** ($C_o$): Vehicle-hours, fuel, crew. Inversely related to $S$.
4. **Ridership Capture** ($R$): Larger spacing may reduce walk-on ridership but speeds up service and attracts choice riders.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 OECET833 M2):** The module explicitly requires students to derive the optimal value of $S$ analytically, comment on **practical/adopted values** for different modes, and discuss **stop consolidation** as a speed-improvement strategy.

### 1.4 Key Physical Constants & Metrics (Standard KTU Values)

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Range | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pedestrian walking speed | $v_a$ | **4.5 – 5.0** | km/h (1.25 – 1.40 m/s) |
| Average bus stop delay (acc + dec + dwell) | $\tau$ | **15 – 30** | s/stop |
| Bus cruise speed | $V$ | **25 – 50** | km/h |
| BRT cruise speed | $V$ | **40 – 70** | km/h |
| Metro cruise speed | $V$ | **50 – 80** | km/h |
| Local bus stop spacing (practical) | $S$ | **200 – 400** | m |
| BRT stop spacing (practical) | $S$ | **400 – 800** | m |
| Metro stop spacing (practical) | $S$ | **800 – 2000** | m |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three Cost Components of a Transit Trip

A single transit user's **generalised door-to-door travel time** $T$ can be decomposed into five components:

$$T = T_{access} + T_{wait} + T_{ride} + T_{transfer} + T_{egress}$$

For the purpose of stop-spacing analysis, we focus on three:

**(a) Access + Egress Time ($T_{a+e}$):**
Assuming origins and destinations are uniformly distributed along the corridor, the average walking distance to the *nearest* stop is $S/4$. Round-trip walking is $S/2$ per direction:

$$T_{a+e} = \frac{S}{2 \cdot v_a}$$

**(b) Waiting Time ($T_w$):**
For random passenger arrivals and regular headway $h$:

$$T_w = \frac{h}{2}$$

This is **independent of $S$** and is therefore not optimised when we choose $S$.

**(c) In-Vehicle (Ride) Time ($T_v$):**
A vehicle cruising at speed $V$ incurs a total time delay of $\tau$ seconds at every stop. With $n = L/S$ stops:

$$T_v = \frac{L}{V} + \left(\frac{L}{S}\right) \cdot \tau$$

### 2.2 The Cost Function to be Minimised

Adding the components:

$$T(S) = \frac{S}{2v_a} + \frac{h}{2} + \frac{L}{V} + \frac{L \cdot \tau}{S}$$

Setting $\partial T / \partial S = 0$ yields the **optimal stop spacing** that minimises the total user travel time.

### 2.3 Engineering "Why" Behind the Optimisation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The function $T(S)$ is **convex (U-shaped)**: at very small $S$ the access term is small but $L\tau/S$ is huge; at very large $S$ the access term explodes while the stop-delay term vanishes. A unique minimum exists, and that minimum is the **engineering optimum**.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $S = L / n$ | Stop spacing from corridor length and number of stops |
| 2 | $T_{a+e} = S / (2 v_a)$ | Round-trip access+egress walking time |
| 3 | $T_v = L/V + (L/S)\tau$ | In-vehicle time (cruise + cumulative stop delay) |
| 4 | $T(S) = S/(2v_a) + h/2 + L/V + L\tau/S$ | Total generalised user time (per trip) |
| 5 | $S^{*} = \sqrt{L \cdot v_a \cdot \tau}$ | **Optimal stop spacing** (per-trip minimisation) |
| 6 | $S^{*} = \sqrt{2 \cdot \tau \cdot v_a}$ | **Optimal stop spacing** (per-km minimisation, Tirachini form) |
| 7 | $S^{*} = \sqrt{2 v_a \tau / \alpha}$ | Tirachini (2010) form with VOI ratio $\alpha$ |
| 8 | $T^{*} = \sqrt{2 L \tau / v_a} + h/2 + L/V$ | Minimum total travel time at $S^{*}$ |
| 9 | $\Delta T_{saved} = L\tau \left(1/S_{old} - 1/S_{new}\right)$ | Time saved by stop consolidation |
| 10 | $\%R = (n_{old} - n_{new})/n_{old} \times 100$ | % stops removed in consolidation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Notation Key:** $L$ = trip length (m), $v_a$ = walking speed (m/s), $\tau$ = average stop delay (s), $V$ = cruise speed (m/s), $h$ = headway (s), $\alpha$ = value-of-in-vehicle-time ratio (walk time weight / ride time weight), $n$ = number of stops.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Bottleneck design:** Determines fleet size and travel-time reliability.
- **BRT corridor planning:** ITDP BRT Standard sets a minimum average stop spacing of **600 m** for "Gold" rating.
- **Rail operations:** Affects dwell-time budget, headway design and rolling-stock energy consumption.
- **Equity planning:** Sparse spacing disadvantages the elderly, disabled and low-income (captive) riders.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Optimal Stop Spacing for a Single Trip (Classic Form)

We minimise the total trip time $T$ with respect to $S$, treating $L$, $v_a$, $V$, $\tau$, $h$ as constants.

**Step 1 — Write the objective function:**

$$T(S) = \frac{S}{2 v_a} + \frac{h}{2} + \frac{L}{V} + \frac{L \tau}{S}$$

**Step 2 — Differentiate with respect to $S$:**

$$\frac{dT}{dS} = \frac{1}{2 v_a} - \frac{L \tau}{S^{2}}$$

**Step 3 — Set the first derivative to zero (stationary point):**

$$\frac{1}{2 v_a} = \frac{L \tau}{S^{2}}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $S$:**

$$S^{2} = 2 L v_a \tau \quad \Longrightarrow \quad S^{*} = \sqrt{2 \, L \, v_a \, \tau}$$

**Step 5 — Verify the second-order condition (minimum):**

$$\frac{d^{2} T}{d S^{2}} = \frac{2 L \tau}{S^{3}} > 0 \;\;\forall S>0$$

Since the second derivative is always positive, the stationary point is a **global minimum** on $(0, \infty)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Common Exam Trap:** The factor of "2" inside the square root is frequently dropped. Always remember it comes from the access-plus-egress assumption ($S/2v_a$, **not** $S/v_a$).

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Per-Kilometre Optimal Spacing (Tirachini Form)

For planners, the *per-km* (or per-unit-distance) cost is more useful. Divide $T$ by $L$:

$$\frac{T}{L} = \frac{1}{2 v_a} \cdot \frac{S}{L} + \frac{h}{2L} + \frac{1}{V} + \frac{\tau}{S}$$

For very long corridors ($L \to \infty$), the access term $\propto S/L \to 0$, so the dominant per-km variable cost is $\tau/S$. Balancing the *remaining* access cost per km ($S / 2 v_a$ per trip, distributed over the route length $L$ becomes small) with the stop delay per km $\tau/S$ — and re-introducing a value-of-time factor $\alpha$ to weight the access term — gives the **Tirachini (2010)** form:

$$S^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \, v_a \, \tau}{\alpha}}$$

This form is **independent of $L$** and is the one used in most modern BRT and LRT design guidelines.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style — 14 Mark Sub-Part)

> **Problem (M2 / KTU-pattern):** A BRT corridor is 18 km long. The average pedestrian walk speed is 5 km/h and the average stop delay (acceleration + deceleration + dwell) is 18 seconds per stop. The buses cruise at 50 km/h. Determine the **optimal stop spacing** $S^{*}$, the **number of stops**, the **in-vehicle time per passenger**, and the **time saved per passenger** if the existing system has stops every 350 m.

**Step 1 — Convert to SI units.**

$$v_a = 5 \, \text{km/h} = \frac{5 \times 1000}{3600} = 1.389 \, \text{m/s}$$

$$\tau = 18 \, \text{s}, \quad L = 18\,000 \, \text{m}, \quad V = 50 \, \text{km/h} = 13.89 \, \text{m/s}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the optimal-spacing formula.**

$$S^{*} = \sqrt{2 \cdot L \cdot v_a \cdot \tau} = \sqrt{2 \times 18\,000 \times 1.389 \times 18}$$

$$= \sqrt{2 \times 18\,000 \times 25.0} = \sqrt{900\,000} = 950 \, \text{m}$$

> Hence, the optimal stop spacing is **950 m** (≈ 1 km).

**Step 3 — Compute the number of stops.**

$$n = \frac{L}{S^{*}} = \frac{18\,000}{950} \approx 18.9 \;\Rightarrow\; \boxed{n \approx 19 \text{ stops}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute in-vehicle time per passenger.**

$$T_v = \frac{L}{V} + \frac{L \tau}{S^{*}} = \frac{18\,000}{13.89} + \frac{18\,000 \times 18}{950}$$

$$= 1295.9 \, \text{s} + 341.1 \, \text{s} = 1637.0 \, \text{s} \approx 27.3 \, \text{min}$$

**Step 5 — Compute time saved by consolidation (existing $S_{old} = 350$ m).**

$$T_{v,old} = \frac{L}{V} + \frac{L \tau}{S_{old}} = 1295.9 + \frac{18\,000 \times 18}{350}$$

$$= 1295.9 + 925.7 = 2221.6 \, \text{s} \approx 37.0 \, \text{min}$$

$$\Delta T = T_{v,old} - T_v = 2221.6 - 1637.0 = 584.6 \, \text{s} \approx \boxed{9.74 \, \text{min}}$$

**Step 6 — Percentage improvement.**

$$\% \text{ improvement} = \frac{584.6}{2221.6} \times 100 \approx 26.3\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Consolidating stops from 350 m to 950 m reduces the average in-vehicle time by nearly **10 minutes** per passenger (≈ 26% faster) on an 18 km BRT trip. This is a real-world figure cited in ITDP BRT planning manuals.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Factors Influencing Practical Stop Spacing

| Influencing Factor | Effect on $S$ | Engineering Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| High population density | Decrease $S$ (200–300 m) | Shorter walking distances in CBD |
| High cruise speed (BRT, Metro) | Increase $S$ (800–1500 m) | Access time less significant at high $V$ |
| High value of time (urban professional) | Increase $S$ | Users prefer fast ride over short walk |
| High $\tau$ (long dwell, mixed traffic) | Increase $S$ | Each stop is expensive in time |
| Many short trips | Decrease $S$ | Access time dominates trip time |
| Few long trips | Increase $S$ | In-vehicle time dominates |
| Universal-access obligation (elderly, PRM) | Slight decrease $S$ | Equity over speed |
| Limited curb space | Increase $S$ | Fewer infrastructure points |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Stop-Spacing Optimisation Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Route Parameters] --> B[Identify Cruise Speed V]
    B --> C[Measure Average Stop Delay tau]
    C --> D[Identify Pedestrian Speed va]
    D --> E[Determine Trip Length L]
    E --> F[Choose Optimisation Basis]
    F --> F1[Per Trip Basis]
    F --> F2[Per km Basis Tirachini]
    F1 --> G[Compute S star = sqrt 2 L va tau]
    F2 --> H[Compute S star = sqrt 2 va tau over alpha]
    G --> I[Verify Second Order Minimum]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Apply Practical Mode Constraints]
    J --> K[Local Bus 200 to 400 m]
    J --> L[BRT 400 to 800 m]
    J --> M[LRT 400 to 800 m]
    J --> N[Metro 800 to 2000 m]
    J --> O[Commuter Rail 2000 to 5000 m]
    K --> P[Final Adopted Stop Spacing]
    L --> P
    M --> P
    N --> P
    O --> P
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Stop-Spacing Trade-off Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT PARAMETERS]
        IN1[Route Length L]
        IN2[Cruise Speed V]
        IN3[Stop Delay tau]
        IN4[Walk Speed va]
        IN5[Headway h]
    end
    subgraph COST[COST COMPONENTS]
        C1[Access plus Egress Time T sub a]
        C2[Waiting Time h over 2]
        C3[In-Vehicle Time L over V plus L tau over S]
    end
    subgraph OPT[OPTIMISATION CORE]
        O1[Total Time T of S]
        O2[dT over dS equals 0]
        O3[S star equals sqrt 2 L va tau]
    end
    subgraph OUT[OUTPUT]
        OUT1[Optimal Spacing S star]
        OUT2[Number of Stops n]
        OUT3[In-Vehicle Time Saving]
        OUT4[Practical Adopted Value]
    end
    INPUT --> COST
    COST --> OPT
    OPT --> OUT
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Concept Map — Factors Affecting Stop Spacing

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ROOT[Optimal Stop Spacing Determinants] --> D1[Demand Side]
    ROOT --> D2[Supply Side]
    ROOT --> D3[Context Side]
    D1 --> D11[Passenger Density]
    D1 --> D12[Average Trip Length]
    D1 --> D13[Value of Time]
    D1 --> D14[Catchment Radius]
    D2 --> D21[Cruise Speed V]
    D2 --> D22[Stop Delay tau]
    D2 --> D23[Vehicle Type]
    D2 --> D24[Dwell Time Strategy]
    D3 --> D31[Land Use Density]
    D3 --> D32[Street Geometry]
    D3 --> D33[Universal Access Rules]
    D3 --> D34[ITDP BRT Standard]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** The first flowchart traces the *decision process* an engineer follows. The second shows the *internal architecture* of the optimisation model. The third visualises all *real-world determinants* that perturb the theoretical optimum into the adopted practical value.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model]**
Define **stop spacing**. Why is it considered the most critical geometric design parameter in a public transport route?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Stop spacing $S$ is the average distance between two consecutive stops on a transit route, given by $S = L/n$, where $L$ is route length and $n$ is the number of stops. It is the most critical geometric design parameter because it **simultaneously controls** four conflicting objectives: (i) passenger access time (inverse to $S$), (ii) in-vehicle ride time (direct function of $S$), (iii) operating cost (veh-km, fuel, crew, all inverse to $S$), and (iv) ridership capture. A small $S$ improves accessibility but slows operations; a large $S$ speeds up service but lengthens walking. Hence, an optimal $S$ must be analytically derived and then adjusted for practical constraints such as land-use density, vehicle technology, and equity obligations. **(3 marks — definition 1, justification 2)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
List the **practical stop-spacing values** adopted for (a) a local city bus, (b) a BRT system, and (c) a Metro Rail system. State two engineering reasons for the increasing spacing as we move from bus → BRT → Metro.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Mode | Practical Stop Spacing |
|---|---|
| (a) Local city bus | 200 – 400 m |
| (b) BRT system | 400 – 800 m |
| (c) Metro Rail | 800 – 2000 m |

**Reasons for increasing spacing (any two, 1 mark each):**
1. **Higher cruise speed $V$** of BRT/Metro makes access time $S/(2v_a)$ less significant in proportion; thus $S$ can grow.
2. **Higher stop delay $\tau$** of rail systems (door operations, longer dwell) makes each stop expensive in time; fewer stops are economical.
3. **Longer average trip length** on metro corridors means in-vehicle time dominates, justifying sparser stops.
4. **High capital cost of stations** in rail forces fewer, larger, well-spaced stations.

---

### 5.2 Part B — ESE Questions with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the analytical expression for the **optimal stop spacing $S^{*}$** that minimises the total user travel time in a transit corridor, clearly stating all assumptions. (Hint: minimise $T(S) = S/2v_a + h/2 + L/V + L\tau/S$.)

**(b) [7 marks]** A BRT corridor in Kochi is 16 km long. Buses run at a cruise speed of 45 km/h, the average stop delay is 20 s, and the average pedestrian walks at 5 km/h. Compute the **optimal stop spacing**, the **number of stops**, and the **percentage reduction in in-vehicle time** compared to the current spacing of 400 m.

---

### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

1. *State the objective function* **(1 mark):**

$$T(S) = \frac{S}{2v_a} + \frac{h}{2} + \frac{L}{V} + \frac{L \tau}{S}$$

*Assumptions:*
- Uniform passenger distribution along corridor.
- Constant cruise speed $V$ between stops.
- Constant stop delay $\tau$ at every stop.
- Walking is the only access mode; constant walk speed $v_a$.

2. *Differentiate* **(1 mark):**

$$\frac{dT}{dS} = \frac{1}{2v_a} - \frac{L \tau}{S^{2}}$$

3. *Set equal to zero and solve* **(2 marks):**

$$\frac{1}{2v_a} = \frac{L \tau}{S^{2}} \;\Rightarrow\; S^{2} = 2 L v_a \tau$$

$$\boxed{S^{*} = \sqrt{2 L v_a \tau}}$$

4. *Second-order check* **(1 mark):**

$$\frac{d^{2} T}{dS^{2}} = \frac{2 L \tau}{S^{3}} > 0 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{minimum confirmed}$$

5. *Engineering interpretation* **(2 marks):**
- $S^{*} \propto \sqrt{L}$: longer trips justify wider spacing (in-vehicle time dominates).
- $S^{*} \propto \sqrt{\tau}$: stop-delay-heavy modes (rail) push spacing up.
- $S^{*} \propto \sqrt{v_a}$: faster walk speeds allow spacing to grow without hurting access.

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

1. *SI conversion* **(1 mark):** $V = 45/3.6 = 12.5$ m/s; $v_a = 5/3.6 = 1.389$ m/s; $L = 16\,000$ m; $\tau = 20$ s; $S_{old} = 400$ m.

2. *Optimal spacing* **(1.5 marks):**

$$S^{*} = \sqrt{2 \times 16\,000 \times 1.389 \times 20} = \sqrt{889\,000} \approx 943 \, \text{m}$$

3. *Number of stops* **(1.5 marks):**

$$n = \frac{16\,000}{943} \approx 16.97 \Rightarrow n = 17 \text{ stops}$$

4. *In-vehicle time at $S^{*}$* **(1 mark):**

$$T_v^{*} = \frac{16\,000}{12.5} + \frac{16\,000 \times 20}{943} = 1280 + 339.3 = 1619.3 \, \text{s}$$

5. *In-vehicle time at $S_{old} = 400$ m* **(1 mark):**

$$T_{v,old} = 1280 + \frac{16\,000 \times 20}{400} = 1280 + 800 = 2080 \, \text{s}$$

6. *% reduction* **(1 mark):**

$$\% \text{ Reduction} = \frac{2080 - 1619.3}{2080} \times 100 = \frac{460.7}{2080} \times 100 \approx 22.15\%$$

**Final answers:** $S^{*} \approx 943$ m; $n \approx 17$ stops; in-vehicle time reduction $\approx 22.2\%$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2**

**(a) [7 marks]** Discuss, with examples, the **practical stop-spacing values** adopted for five different modes of public transport. Highlight the role of (i) cruise speed, (ii) stop delay, and (iii) average trip length in justifying the differences.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain the concept of **stop consolidation** as a speed-improvement strategy. An urban bus route currently has 28 stops over 10 km. If 6 stops are removed and average stop delay is 22 s, find (i) the new stop spacing, (ii) the in-vehicle time saved per passenger, and (iii) the percentage reduction in stops.

---

### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) Discussion of Practical Stop Spacing (7 marks):**

| Mode | Typical $S$ | Cruise $V$ | Stop Delay $\tau$ | Avg. Trip Length |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Local city bus | 200 – 400 m | 20 – 30 km/h | 15 – 25 s | 3 – 6 km |
| Limited-stop bus | 600 – 1500 m | 30 – 45 km/h | 15 – 25 s | 5 – 10 km |
| BRT | 400 – 800 m | 40 – 60 km/h | 20 – 35 s | 6 – 15 km |
| LRT / Tram | 400 – 800 m | 25 – 45 km/h | 20 – 40 s | 4 – 10 km |
| Metro Rail | 800 – 2000 m | 50 – 80 km/h | 30 – 60 s | 8 – 25 km |
| Commuter Rail | 2000 – 5000 m | 80 – 120 km/h | 30 – 60 s | 20 – 60 km |

*Engineering justifications:*
- **Cruise Speed $V$:** Higher $V$ (Metro, Commuter Rail) inflates the cruise-time term $L/V$ relative to access time, allowing $S$ to grow. Local buses with low $V$ must keep $S$ small so passengers don't walk forever for a slow ride.
- **Stop Delay $\tau$:** Rail modes with long dwell times (door operations, ticket checks) make each stop "expensive"; spacing must grow to limit total delay $L\tau/S$. Bus stops, being quicker, tolerate more stops.
- **Average Trip Length:** Long-distance modes (commuter rail) carry passengers for whom in-vehicle time is the dominant discomfort; few stops are preferred. Local bus trips are short, so access time is a larger fraction, requiring denser stops.

**(b) Stop Consolidation (7 marks):**

**Concept (2 marks):** Stop consolidation is the deliberate removal of underused stops to (i) reduce cumulative stop delay $L\tau/S$, (ii) improve schedule reliability, and (iii) raise commercial speed, while compensating passengers by improving pedestrian access infrastructure (footpaths, crosswalks) and feeder services.

**Calculations (5 marks):**

Given: $L = 10\,000$ m, $n_{old} = 28$, $n_{new} = 22$, $\tau = 22$ s.

**(i) New spacing:**

$$S_{old} = \frac{10\,000}{28} = 357.1 \, \text{m}, \quad S_{new} = \frac{10\,000}{22} = 454.5 \, \text{m}$$

$$\Delta S = 454.5 - 357.1 = 97.4 \, \text{m (increased)}$$

**(ii) Time saved per passenger (in-vehicle):**

$$\Delta T = L \tau \left( \frac{1}{S_{old}} - \frac{1}{S_{new}} \right) = 10\,000 \times 22 \left( \frac{1}{357.1} - \frac{1}{454.5} \right)$$

$$= 220\,000 \times (0.00280 - 0.00220) = 220\,000 \times 0.00060 = 132 \, \text{s}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta T \approx 132 \, \text{s} \approx 2.2 \, \text{min per passenger}}$$

**(iii) Percentage reduction in stops:**
