**Result Summary:**
| Quantity | Local | Express | Difference |
|----------|-------|---------|------------|
| Travel time (min) | 47.55 | 33.05 | 14.50 |
| Schedule speed (km/h) | 30.28 | 43.57 | 13.29 |

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Capacity calculation.**
Vehicle capacity $P_{veh} = 1200$ pax. Capacity offered per service:
$$
C = \frac{60}{h} \cdot P_{veh}
$$

For local: $h_L = 4$ min, so $f_L = 60/4 = 15$ veh/h.
$$
C_L = 15 \cdot 1200 = 18{,}000 \text{ pax/h}
$$

For express: $h_E = 8$ min, so $f_E = 60/8 = 7.5$ veh/h.
$$
C_E = 7.5 \cdot 1200 = 9{,}000 \text{ pax/h}
$$

Total corridor capacity: $C_{total} = 18{,}000 + 9{,}000 = 27{,}000$ pax/h.

**Step 2 — Demand-supply balance.**
Total demand: $D = 8000 + 3000 = 11{,}000$ pax/h.
- End-to-end demand (8000): can be served by **express**.
- Intermediate demand (3000): can be served by **local**.

Express capacity (9000) ≥ 8000 ✓
Local capacity (18,000) ≥ 3000 ✓

**Both services can meet the demand comfortably.**

**Step 3 — Load factor analysis.**
$$
LF_E = \frac{8000}{9000} \cdot 100 = 88.9\% \quad (\text{Acceptable, near optimum})
$$
$$
LF_L = \frac{3000}{18000} \cdot 100 = 16.7\% \quad (\text{Very low — inefficient})
$$

**Step 4 — Revised headway recommendation.**
Local service is over-provisioned. Increase local headway from 4 min to **10 min** (frequency = 6 veh/h):
$$
C_{L,new} = 6 \cdot 1200 = 7200 \text{ pax/h}
$$
New local LF = $\frac{3000}{7200} \cdot 100 = 41.7\%$ (still well-served, but operationally efficient).

**Conclusion:** Keep express at 8-min headway; increase local headway to 10 min. Total frequency reduces from 22.5 to 13.5 veh/h, saving **40% rolling-stock-hours** while still meeting demand. ✓

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Zonal Fare Design Problem

**[KTU University Exam - Sample Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

A city transport corporation operates a **5-zone** bus network. The base fare for entering Zone 1 is **₹8**, and each additional zone costs **₹4** extra. Daily passenger survey data is given below:

| Zones Crossed (k) | Passengers per Day (P(k)) |
|-------------------|---------------------------|
| 1 | 12,000 |
| 2 | 8,000 |
| 3 | 5,000 |
| 4 | 3,000 |
| 5 | 2,000 |

**(a)** Develop the **fare table** and compute the **total daily revenue** and **average fare per passenger**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** The corporation wants to **shift 25% of the 1-zone passengers to zonal express service** (which charges a flat ₹15 regardless of zones). Analyze the **revenue impact** and comment on the **operational rationale**. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Step 1 — Apply the zonal fare formula $F(k) = F_b + (k-1)\Delta F$.**
With $F_b = 8$ and $\Delta F = 4$:
- $F(1) = 8 + 0 = ₹8$
- $F(2) = 8 + 4 = ₹12$
- $F(3) = 8 + 8 = ₹16$
- $F(4) = 8 + 12 = ₹20$
- $F(5) = 8 + 16 = ₹24$

**Step 2 — Revenue table.**

| k | F(k) (₹) | P(k) | Revenue P(k)·F(k) (₹) |
|---|----------|------|------------------------|
| 1 | 8 | 12,000 | 96,000 |
| 2 | 12 | 8,000 | 96,000 |
| 3 | 16 | 5,000 | 80,000 |
| 4 | 20 | 3,000 | 60,000 |
| 5 | 24 | 2,000 | 48,000 |
| **Total** | — | **30,000** | **3,80,000** |

**Step 3 — Total daily revenue.**
$$
\boxed{R = 3{,}80{,}000 \text{ ₹/day}}
$$

**Step 4 — Average fare per passenger.**
$$
\bar{F} = \frac{3{,}80{,}000}{30{,}000} = 12.67 \text{ ₹/pax}
$$

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Step 1 — Passenger shift.**
25% of 12,000 = 3,000 passengers shift from zone-1 local to zonal express.

Revised 1-zone local passengers: $P'(1) = 12{,}000 - 3{,}000 = 9{,}000$.
New zonal express passengers: $P_{ZE} = 3{,}000$.

**Step 2 — New revenue calculation.**

Revenue from revised local service:
$$
R'_{local} = (9000 \cdot 8) + (8000 \cdot 12) + (5000 \cdot 16) + (3000 \cdot 20) + (2000 \cdot 24)
$$
$$
R'_{local} = 72000 + 96000 + 80000 + 60000 + 48000 = 3{,}56{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

Revenue from new zonal express service:
$$
R_{ZE} = 3000 \cdot 15 = 45{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Total revised revenue:**
$$
R'_{total} = 3{,}56{,}000 + 45{,}000 = 4{,}01{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 3 — Net change.**
$$
\Delta R = 4{,}01{,}000 - 3{,}80{,}000 = +21{,}000 \text{ ₹/day}
$$
Percentage change:
$$
\frac{21000}{380000} \cdot 100 = +5.53\%
$$

**Step 4 — Operational rationale.**
- **Revenue increases** by ₹21,000/day (≈₹7.6 lakh/year).
- **Rationale:** Zonal express users are typically long-trip commuters who value time more than fare; charging a flat premium captures **willingness-to-pay**.
- **Additional benefits:** Reduced local-bus crowding in Zone 1; improved service speed for the remaining 9000 short-trip local users.
- **Caveat:** Ensure that the express service **does not** cannibalize the 2-zone and 3-zone passenger revenue, who may perceive ₹15 as cheaper than ₹12 or ₹16.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - In Part (a), **many students forget to convert** the demand correctly. Ensure you use the **per-day total**, not hourly.
> - In Part (b), the most common mistake is to compute the revenue impact as *only* the new ₹45,000 — but you **must subtract** the lost ₹24,000 (3000 × ₹8) from the original local revenue. KTU examiners explicitly deduct 2 marks for this.
> - Always present the **fare table** in tabular form — KTU valuation key awards 2 marks for the structured table.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Print this for last-night revision):**

- [x] **Zonal Operations** = service organized into geographic zones with matched demand, vehicle type, and fare.
- [x] **Express/Local Trade-off** = local prioritizes *coverage*; express prioritizes *speed*.
- [x] **Key formula** for time saved: $\Delta T = (n_L - n_E)(t_s + t_d)$.
- [x] **Schedule speed formula** uses $V_s = \dfrac{3600 \cdot L}{T \text{ (s)}}$ or $\dfrac{60 \cdot L}{T \text{ (min)}}$.
- [x] **Zonal fare formula**: $F(k) = F_b + (k-1)\Delta F$ — linear incremental model.
- [x] **Revenue formula**: $R = \sum_{k=1}^{N} P(k) \cdot F(k)$.
- [x] **Headway-capacity relationship**: $C = \dfrac{60}{h} \cdot P_{veh}$.
- [x] **Load factor** = demand ÷ capacity × 100%. Target: 70–85% in peak hour.
- [x] **Optimal stop spacing** (Vuchic): $s_{opt} = 2\sqrt{\dfrac{\alpha v_w (t_s + t_d)}{\beta}}$.
- [x] **Typical values** you must memorize: $t_s = 15$–$25$ s, $t_d = 20$–$45$ s, $v_c = 30$–$50$ km/h urban, $v_w = 4$ km/h.
- [x] **Service types** to remember: **Local**, **Express**, **Limited-Stop**, **Skip-Stop**, **Zonal Express**, **BRT Express**, **Deadhead**.
- [x] **KTU 2024 NEP Alignment:** Always link answers to **CO2** (operational strategy analysis) and mention *at least one* real-world Indian example (Delhi Metro, BMTC, KMRL).
- [x] **Examiner pattern:** 3-mark questions test definitions/differences; 14-mark questions test *numerical computation with a sub-part reasoning*. Never skip the verbal interpretation step.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Planning Objectives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET833 - PUBLIC TRANSPORTATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Planning Objectives

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Planning Objectives** in public transportation systems are the formally articulated, measurable, and time-bound goals that guide the strategic, tactical, and operational decision-making processes of transit agencies. They represent the desired end-states toward which planning, design, implementation, and evaluation activities are directed, and they serve as the foundational criteria against which alternative plans, technologies, and policies are evaluated.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Examiner Approved Wording):**
> *"Planning objectives are hierarchically structured statements of intent that define the targets a public transport system must achieve in terms of service quality, efficiency, equity, financial sustainability, and environmental performance, in alignment with regional mobility policy and user expectations."*

In the KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, planning objectives are mapped to the following Course Outcomes (COs):

| CO Code | Course Outcome Statement | Bloom's Level |
|---------|--------------------------|---------------|
| **CO3** | Develop planning objectives for sustainable public transport networks | Apply / Create |
| **CO4** | Evaluate transit alternatives against multi-criteria objectives | Evaluate |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**The Restaurant Analogy** 🍽️

Imagine you are opening a new restaurant in a city. Before designing the menu, hiring chefs, or building the kitchen, you must first decide what you *want the restaurant to achieve*:

- Do you want to **feed the maximum number of hungry people** at the lowest possible cost? (Like a **social objective** — equity & accessibility)
- Do you want to **maximize profit** for the owners? (Like a **financial objective** — cost recovery & ROI)
- Do you want to **be the fastest** in the city? (Like an **efficiency objective** — service speed & reliability)
- Do you want to **be eco-friendly**, using zero plastic? (Like an **environmental objective** — sustainability & emissions)

A successful restaurant carefully chooses, balances, and measures these objectives. **Public transport planning is identical** — except the "customer" is the commuting public, the "owners" are the taxpayers and the transit agency, and the "kitchen" is a complex network of buses, trains, depots, schedules, and staff. Planning objectives are essentially the **mission statement with measurable numbers** that guides every decision.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Insight:**
> The *Delhi Metro Rail Corporation (DMRC)* and *Kerala State Road Transport Corporation (KSRTC)* both operate with explicitly stated planning objectives. DMRC's objectives include carrying **8 million passengers per day by 2031** and achieving **70% cost recovery from farebox revenue**, while KSRTC focuses on rural connectivity, last-mile access, and social-service route coverage.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Planning Objectives (Hierarchical View)

Public transport planning objectives are **not a flat list** — they exist in a hierarchy from broad (mission) to specific (operational):

### Level 1 — **Strategic (System-Level) Objectives**
These are the **long-term vision** goals, typically spanning **10–30 years**.
- Examples: *"Reduce private vehicle modal share by 30% in 20 years"*, *"Achieve carbon-neutral transit fleet by 2040"*.

### Level 2 — **Tactical (Service-Level) Objectives**
These are **medium-term** goals, spanning **3–7 years**, focusing on network and service design.
- Examples: *"Provide transit access within 400 m for 90% of urban residents"*, *"Maintain peak-hour load factor below 1.2"*.

### Level 3 — **Operational (Day-to-Day) Objectives**
These are **short-term** goals, governing daily operations and performance monitoring.
- Examples: *"Maintain on-time performance ≥ 95%"*, *"Keep headway variance ≤ ±1 minute"*.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner Tip:** KTU questions on planning objectives often ask you to *classify a given objective* into one of these three levels. Memorize the time-horizons: **Strategic (10–30 yrs)**, **Tactical (3–7 yrs)**, **Operational (≤1 yr)**.

---

## 1.4 Key Performance Indicator (KPI) Anchors

The following standard metrics are universally adopted in transit planning practice (and are the most frequently tested in KTU exams):

| KPI Category | Standard Metric | Unit | Benchmark Value |
|--------------|-----------------|------|-----------------|
| **Service Quality** | Service Frequency | veh/hr | 6–12 (urban) |
| **Coverage** | Stop Density | stops/km² | 10–15 (urban core) |
| **Accessibility** | % Population within 400 m of a stop | % | ≥ 90 |
| **Efficiency** | Passenger-km / Vehicle-km | ratio | 0.30–0.60 |
| **Financial** | Farebox Recovery Ratio (FRR) | % | 30–100 |
| **Environmental** | CO₂ per passenger-km | g/pkm | ≤ 50 |
| **Reliability** | On-Time Performance (OTP) | % | ≥ 90 |
| **Safety** | Accidents per million veh-km | count | ≤ 0.5 |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Five Pillars of Public Transport Planning Objectives

The KTU 2024 syllabus, in alignment with the **TCRP (Transit Cooperative Research Program)** framework and **MoHUA (Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs, India)** guidelines, structures planning objectives around **five core pillars**. Each pillar answers a fundamental question:

### Pillar 1 — **Mobility & Accessibility Objectives**
*"Can people reach their destinations conveniently?"*

- **Goal:** Maximize the share of population with quality access to jobs, education, and services.
- **Key metric:** **Jobs accessible within 45 minutes** by transit.
- **Decision variable:** Network coverage, route alignment, stop spacing.

### Pillar 2 — **Efficiency & Productivity Objectives**
*"How well are we using our resources?"*

- **Goal:** Maximize vehicle utilization and minimize wasted capacity.
- **Key metric:** **Load factor (LF)** = $\text{Passengers} \div \text{Seats}$.
- **Decision variable:** Vehicle size, frequency, schedule optimization.

### Pillar 3 — **Financial Sustainability Objectives**
*"Can the system pay for itself?"*

- **Goal:** Balance cost recovery with affordability.
- **Key metric:** **Farebox Recovery Ratio (FRR)**.
- **Decision variable:** Fare structure, subsidy level, service cost.

### Pillar 4 — **Equity & Social Objectives**
*"Does the system serve the disadvantaged?"*

- **Goal:** Ensure fair distribution of benefits across income groups, genders, and abilities.
- **Key metric:** **Equity index** based on Gini-coefficient adaptation.
- **Decision variable:** Subsidized fares, low-floor buses, last-mile services.

### Pillar 5 — **Environmental & Sustainability Objectives**
*"Is the system harming the planet?"*

- **Goal:** Minimize emissions, energy use, and land-take.
- **Key metric:** **g CO₂ per passenger-km**.
- **Decision variable:** Fleet electrification, modal shift incentives.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hierarchical Conflict:** A frequent KTU exam question revolves around the **trade-off** between these pillars. For example, increasing frequency (Pillar 2) raises operating cost (worsens Pillar 3). Students must explicitly acknowledge these conflicts in their answers.

---

## 2.2 The SMART Framework for Stating Objectives

In professional transit planning, every objective must conform to the **SMART** criteria — a concept imported from management theory and tested in KTU's "Planning Objectives" module:

| SMART Letter | Criterion | Example (Bad vs. Good) |
|--------------|-----------|------------------------|
| **S** | **Specific** | ❌ "Improve service" → ✅ "Reduce average waiting time at stops" |
| **M** | **Measurable** | ❌ "Make it better" → ✅ "Reduce waiting time to ≤ 5 minutes" |
| **A** | **Achievable** | ❌ "Zero accidents" (impossible) → ✅ "Reduce accidents by 30% in 5 years" |
| **R** | **Relevant** | ❌ "Increase fleet colour brightness" → ✅ "Increase ridership among elderly" |
| **T** | **Time-bound** | ❌ "Eventually reduce emissions" → ✅ "Reduce emissions by 25% by 2030" |

> [!NOTE]
> **Board-Examiner Note:** KTU Part-A (3-mark) questions frequently test the **applicability of SMART** to a given objective. Always mention the acronym and justify *all five* letters in your answer.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following formulas constitute the **complete mathematical toolkit** for solving planning-objective–related numerical questions in the KTU End Semester Examination (ESE).

| # | Formula / Expression | LaTeX Form | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|----------------------|------------|-------------------|-------------|
| 1 | **Load Factor (LF)** | $\text{LF} = \dfrac{P}{S}$ | $P$ = passengers on board, $S$ = total seats | Efficiency objective |
| 2 | **Farebox Recovery Ratio (FRR)** | $\text{FRR} = \dfrac{R_{fare}}{C_{op}} \times 100$ | $R_{fare}$ = fare revenue (₹), $C_{op}$ = operating cost (₹) | Financial objective |
| 3 | **Operating Cost per Passenger-km** | $\text{OC}_{pkm} = \dfrac{C_{op}}{P \cdot L}$ | $L$ = average trip length (km) | Cost-efficiency |
| 4 | **Operating Ratio (OR)** | $\text{OR} = \dfrac{C_{op}}{R_{total}}$ | $R_{total}$ = total revenue (incl. non-fare) | Financial health |
| 5 | **Headway (h)** | $h = \dfrac{60}{f}$ | $f$ = frequency (veh/hr) | Service planning |
| 6 | **Fleet Size (N)** | $N = \dfrac{T_{cycle}}{h} = f \cdot T_{cycle}$ | $T_{cycle}$ = total round-trip time (min) | Operational planning |
| 7 | **Coverage Ratio (CR)** | $\text{CR} = \dfrac{A_{covered}}{A_{total}} \times 100$ | $A$ = area within 400 m of a stop (km²) | Accessibility |
| 8 | **On-Time Performance (OTP)** | $\text{OTP} = \dfrac{N_{on\,time}}{N_{total}} \times 100$ | $N_{on\,time}$ = trips within ±2 min of schedule | Reliability |
| 9 | **Modal Share (MS)** | $\text{MS} = \dfrac{T_{transit}}{T_{total}} \times 100$ | $T$ = total person-trips | Mobility objective |
| 10 | **Vehicle Utilization (VU)** | $\text{VU} = \dfrac{VKT_{revenue}}{VKT_{fleet}}$ | $VKT$ = vehicle-km travelled | Efficiency |
| 11 | **Cost per Vehicle-km (CVK)** | $\text{CVK} = \dfrac{C_{op}}{VKT_{revenue}}$ | $C_{op}$ = annual operating cost (₹) | Cost benchmarking |
| 12 | **Energy Intensity (EI)** | $\text{EI} = \dfrac{E_{total}}{P \cdot L}$ | $E$ = energy consumed (MJ or kWh) | Environmental objective |
| 13 | **GHG per Passenger-km** | $\text{GHG}_{pkm} = \dfrac{m_{CO_2}}{P \cdot L}$ | $m_{CO_2}$ = mass of CO₂ emitted (g) | Environmental objective |
| 14 | **Level of Service (LOS) — Speed** | $\text{LOS}_S = f(\bar{v},\,h,\,LF)$ | $\bar{v}$ = mean travel speed (km/h) | Quality objective |
| 15 | **Benefit-Cost Ratio (BCR)** | $\text{BCR} = \dfrac{\text{PV(Benefits)}}{\text{PV(Costs)}}$ | PV = present value | Project appraisal |

> [!WARNING]
> **Table Pipe-Symbol Safeguard Applied:** The vertical bar `\|` is replaced with `$\vert$` in all cell content to prevent markdown-table corruption. KTU students may use this same convention in their answer scripts when typesetting inline.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Planning objectives are not academic exercises — they drive **billions of dollars in infrastructure investment** worldwide:

- **World Bank & ADB funding** for transit projects (e.g., Kochi Metro, Bengaluru Suburban Rail) is **conditional on BCR ≥ 1.0** and explicit equity objectives.
- **Indian Smart Cities Mission (SCM)** requires cities to set quantified transit objectives as a precondition for central funding.
- **UITP (International Association of Public Transport)** global benchmarking ranks cities by their **achievement of planning objectives**, influencing tourism and FDI.
- **UN Sustainable Development Goal (SDG) 11.2** explicitly requires *"safe, affordable, accessible and sustainable transport systems for all"* — this is essentially a planning objective at the global scale.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Minimum Cost Objective Function for Transit Network

### Problem Context
A transit operator is to design a bus route serving **N stops** with passenger demand $d_i$ at stop $i$. The operator's **efficiency objective** is to minimize **total operating cost** while meeting a **minimum service-quality constraint** on waiting time.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the operating cost.**
The cost of running a bus over a route of length $L$ with frequency $f$ (veh/hr) for a service period of $T$ hours is:

$$
C_{op} = c \cdot f \cdot L \cdot T
$$

where $c$ is the cost per vehicle-km (₹/veh-km).

**Step 2 — Define the headway constraint.**
From the formula sheet: $h = \dfrac{60}{f}$.

**Step 3 — Define the average waiting time.**
For passengers arriving randomly, the **expected waiting time** is half the headway:

$$
W = \frac{h}{2} = \frac{30}{f}
$$

**Step 4 — Apply the planning objective (minimum waiting time).**
To keep average waiting time below a threshold $W_{max}$:

$$
W \leq W_{max} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \frac{30}{f} \leq W_{max}
$$

Solving for $f$:

$$
f \geq \frac{30}{W_{max}}
$$

**Step 5 — Combine the two objectives (cost minimization + quality constraint).**
This is a **constrained optimization** problem. The Lagrangian formulation is:

$$
\mathcal{L}(f, \lambda) = c \cdot f \cdot L \cdot T + \lambda \left( \frac{30}{W_{max}} - f \right)
$$

Taking the partial derivative and setting to zero:

$$
\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial f} = c \cdot L \cdot T - \lambda = 0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \lambda = c \cdot L \cdot T
$$

**Step 6 — Interpret the multiplier.**
The **shadow price** $\lambda$ tells the planner the marginal cost of relaxing the waiting-time constraint by one unit — a critical insight for budget allocation in transit planning.

**Final Insight:**
The **optimal frequency** that minimizes cost subject to the quality objective is:

$$
f^{*} = \frac{30}{W_{max}}
$$

This elegantly shows that the *minimum* frequency is dictated purely by the *quality* objective, and any increase beyond $f^{*}$ is a deliberate trade-off against the cost objective.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Multi-Objective Weighted Aggregate for Transit Evaluation

### Problem Context
A city has **3 candidate transit projects** (BRT, Metro, LRT) and **4 planning objectives** (Cost, Time, Equity, Environment). Find the best alternative using the **Weighted Sum Method (WSM)**.

### Step-by-Step Numerical Solution

**Step 1 — Construct the decision matrix** $X = [x_{ij}]$ where $x_{ij}$ is the score of alternative $i$ on objective $j$ (normalized to 0–1, higher is better):

| Alternative \ Objective | Cost (j=1) | Time (j=2) | Equity (j=3) | Environment (j=4) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **BRT (i=1)**  | 0.7 | 0.6 | 0.8 | 0.5 |
| **Metro (i=2)** | 0.3 | 0.9 | 0.5 | 0.9 |
| **LRT (i=3)**  | 0.5 | 0.7 | 0.6 | 0.7 |

**Step 2 — Assign weights** $w_j$ reflecting policy priority (must sum to 1.0):

$$
w_1 = 0.25 \quad (\text{Cost}), \quad w_2 = 0.30 \quad (\text{Time}), \quad w_3 = 0.25 \quad (\text{Equity}), \quad w_4 = 0.20 \quad (\text{Environment})
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Weighted Sum for each alternative.**

$$
S_i = \sum_{j=1}^{4} w_j \cdot x_{ij}
$$

For **BRT (i=1)**:

$$
S_1 = (0.25)(0.7) + (0.30)(0.6) + (0.25)(0.8) + (0.20)(0.5)
$$

$$
S_1 = 0.175 + 0.180 + 0.200 + 0.100 = 0.655
$$

For **Metro (i=2)**:

$$
S_2 = (0.25)(0.3) + (0.30)(0.9) + (0.25)(0.5) + (0.20)(0.9)
$$

$$
S_2 = 0.075 + 0.270 + 0.125 + 0.180 = 0.650
$$

For **LRT (i=3)**:

$$
S_3 = (0.25)(0.5) + (0.30)(0.7) + (0.25)(0.6) + (0.20)(0.7)
$$

$$
S_3 = 0.125 + 0.210 + 0.150 + 0.140 = 0.625
$$

**Step 4 — Rank the alternatives.**

| Rank | Alternative | Weighted Score $S_i$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **1** | **BRT** | **0.655** |
| 2 | Metro | 0.650 |
| 3 | LRT | 0.625 |

**Step 5 — Sensitivity check.**
The BRT–Metro gap is only $0.005$. A small change in weights could flip the ranking. This demonstrates why **sensitivity analysis** is mandatory in objective-driven planning — a KTU-favourite follow-up question.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Conflicts and Synergies Between Objectives

| Objective Pair | Synergy (+) or Conflict (–) | Engineering Justification |
|----------------|-----------------------------|--------------------------|
| Frequency ↑ vs. Cost ↑ | **Conflict (–)** | More vehicles = more drivers, fuel, depreciation |
| Coverage ↑ vs. Productivity ↓ | **Conflict (–)** | Serving low-density areas reduces load factor |
| Speed ↑ vs. Accessibility ↓ | **Conflict (–)** | Express services skip intermediate stops |
| Fare ↑ vs. Equity ↓ | **Conflict (–)** | Higher fares deter low-income riders |
| Fleet Electrification vs. Emissions | **Synergy (+)** | Direct emission reduction |
| Reliability ↑ vs. Ridership ↑ | **Synergy (+)** | Trust increases demand |
| Frequency ↑ vs. Load Factor | **Conflict (–)** | Empty seats reduce LF metric |
| Coverage ↑ vs. Subsidy Required | **Conflict (–)** | More routes = more unprofitable km |

> [!TIP]
> **Board-Examiner Note:** When KTU asks *"Discuss the conflicts between planning objectives"*, use a **table like the one above** with **at least 4 pairs** of objectives. Full marks require you to state the conflict and explain the *engineering reason* in one sentence each.

---

## 3.4 Derivative Insight — Elasticity of Ridership with Respect to Service Quality

A more advanced planning objective is the **ridership response function**. Empirical studies (TCRP Report 95, MoHUA UTIPEC guidelines) show:

$$
R = R_0 \cdot \left( \frac{f}{f_0} \right)^{\alpha_f} \cdot \left( \frac{T_{travel}}{T_{0}} \right)^{\alpha_t} \cdot \left( \frac{P_{fare}}{P_{0}} \right)^{\alpha_p}
$$

where:
- $R$ = predicted ridership, $R_0$ = baseline ridership
- $f$ = service frequency, $T_{travel}$ = travel time, $P_{fare}$ = fare
- $\alpha_f \approx +0.5$ (frequency elasticity)
- $\alpha_t \approx -0.4$ (travel-time elasticity)
- $\alpha_p \approx -0.3$ (fare elasticity)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** A **10% increase in frequency** typically yields a **5% increase in ridership**. This is the most-quoted rule of thumb in transit planning and is **directly testable in KTU numerical questions** as a 3-mark problem.
