# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define planning objectives in public transportation. List any four key performance indicators used to measure them.**

**Model Answer (10 lines):**

Planning objectives are the formally stated, measurable goals that a public transport system aims to achieve across strategic, tactical, and operational horizons. They translate policy vision into quantifiable targets covering service quality, efficiency, financial sustainability, equity, and environmental impact.

Four key performance indicators (KPIs) are:

1. **On-Time Performance (OTP):** Percentage of trips arriving within ±2 minutes of schedule — measures **reliability**.
2. **Load Factor (LF):** Ratio of passengers to seats — measures **efficiency** and capacity utilization.
3. **Farebox Recovery Ratio (FRR):** Percentage of operating cost recovered from fares — measures **financial sustainability**.
4. **Service Coverage:** Percentage of population within 400 m of a transit stop — measures **accessibility**.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Explain the SMART framework for stating planning objectives with one transit example.**

**Model Answer (8 lines):**

The **SMART** framework requires every objective to be **Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, and Time-bound**.

**Transit Example:** *"Reduce average passenger waiting time at all urban bus stops from 12 minutes to 5 minutes by December 2027."*

- **S** — Focuses on a specific variable (waiting time) at a specific facility (urban bus stops).
- **M** — Quantified in minutes (12 → 5).
- **A** — Realistic given current funding levels.
- **R** — Directly linked to ridership growth policy.
- **T** — Bounded by December 2027.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall:** Students often forget the **"A — Achievable"** criterion. Do not write *"eliminate all accidents"* — that is unachievable. Always phrase objectives as improvements, not absolutes.

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Adapted]**

> **(a)** Discuss the **five pillars of planning objectives** in public transportation systems with relevant metrics. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A city is evaluating **three transit projects** (BRT, Metro, LRT) using four objectives weighted as: Cost = 0.30, Time = 0.25, Equity = 0.20, Environment = 0.25. The normalized scores (higher = better) are given below. Use the **Weighted Sum Method** to rank the projects. Also comment on the **sensitivity** of the result. **(7 marks)**

| Project \ Objective | Cost | Time | Equity | Environment |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| BRT  | 0.6 | 0.7 | 0.9 | 0.5 |
| Metro | 0.4 | 0.9 | 0.5 | 0.9 |
| LRT  | 0.5 | 0.8 | 0.7 | 0.7 |

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

The **five pillars** of public transport planning objectives, as recognized by KTU 2024 syllabus and MoHUA guidelines, are:

**1. Mobility & Accessibility**
- Ensures people can reach destinations conveniently.
- Metric: **% population within 400 m of a stop** (target ≥ 90%), **jobs reachable within 45 min**.

**2. Efficiency & Productivity**
- Maximizes utilization of vehicles, staff, and infrastructure.
- Metric: **Load Factor (LF) = Passengers ÷ Seats** (target 0.6–0.9), **passenger-km / vehicle-km**.

**3. Financial Sustainability**
- Balances revenue with operating cost.
- Metric: **Farebox Recovery Ratio (FRR)** (target 30–100%), **Operating Cost per Passenger-km**.

**4. Equity & Social Inclusion**
- Ensures benefits reach disadvantaged groups (low-income, elderly, disabled).
- Metric: **% of BPL households within 500 m of stop**, **gender ridership ratio**.

**5. Environmental Sustainability**
- Minimizes emissions and energy use.
- Metric: **g CO₂ per passenger-km** (target ≤ 50), **share of electric/hybrid fleet**.

> **Conclusion:** Effective planning requires balancing all five pillars; a system optimizing only one (e.g., efficiency) will fail on others (e.g., equity).

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**Step 1 — Apply the Weighted Sum Method:**

$$
S_i = \sum_{j=1}^{4} w_j \cdot x_{ij}
$$

**For BRT:**

$$
S_{BRT} = (0.30)(0.6) + (0.25)(0.7) + (0.20)(0.9) + (0.25)(0.5)
$$

$$
S_{BRT} = 0.180 + 0.175 + 0.180 + 0.125 = 0.660
$$

**For Metro:**

$$
S_{Metro} = (0.30)(0.4) + (0.25)(0.9) + (0.20)(0.5) + (0.25)(0.9)
$$

$$
S_{Metro} = 0.120 + 0.225 + 0.100 + 0.225 = 0.670
$$

**For LRT:**

$$
S_{LRT} = (0.30)(0.5) + (0.25)(0.8) + (0.20)(0.7) + (0.25)(0.7)
$$

$$
S_{LRT} = 0.150 + 0.200 + 0.140 + 0.175 = 0.665
$$

**Step 2 — Ranking:**

| Rank | Project | Score |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **1** | **Metro** | **0.670** |
| 2 | LRT | 0.665 |
| 3 | BRT | 0.660 |

**Step 3 — Sensitivity Analysis:**

The gaps are extremely small (≤ 0.010). A small change in weights could flip the ranking. For example, if Cost weight increases from 0.30 to 0.40 (and Environment drops to 0.15), **BRT becomes #1** because of its high cost score (0.6). Hence, the result is **highly sensitive to weight choice** and policy-makers should test multiple weight scenarios.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Mistake:** Students often forget to **comment on sensitivity**. The question explicitly asks for it — losing 2 marks if skipped. Also, remember to **show all four multiplications** for each project, not just the final sum.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Adapted]**

> **(a)** Explain the **hierarchical classification of planning objectives** (strategic, tactical, operational) with one example for each level. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A bus route of length **20 km** operates with **8 buses** at a **headway of 10 minutes**. Each bus carries an average of **45 passengers** over a trip. The cost per vehicle-km is **₹60** and the fare per passenger is **₹15**. Compute: **(i)** Fleet utilization, **(ii)** Load Factor (assuming 50 seats), **(iii)** Farebox Recovery Ratio. Comment on whether the route meets the planning objective of FRR ≥ 40%. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a) — 7 Marks

Planning objectives in public transport are organized into **three hierarchical levels** based on time horizon and decision scope:

**Level 1 — Strategic (System-Level) Objectives**
- **Time horizon:** 10–30 years
- **Scope:** Entire regional/national transit network
- **Decision-makers:** Ministry, state transport department
- **Example:** *"Reduce urban private-vehicle modal share from 50% to 25% by 2040."*

**Level 2 — Tactical (Service-Level) Objectives**
- **Time horizon:** 3–7 years
- **Scope:** City-level network design, route planning
- **Decision-makers:** City transit authority, urban planning body
- **Example:** *"Provide transit access within 400 m for 90% of Kochi city residents by 2028."*

**Level 3 — Operational (Day-to-Day) Objectives**
- **Time horizon:** ≤ 1 year (often daily/weekly review)
- **Scope:** Individual routes, schedules, vehicle deployment
- **Decision-makers:** Depot managers, operations control
- **Example:** *"Maintain on-time performance of all city buses ≥ 95% during morning peak hours."*

The hierarchy is **cascading** — operational objectives must collectively achieve tactical objectives, which in turn realize the strategic vision. Failure at any level cascades upward.

#### Model Solution to (b) — 7 Marks

**Given:**
- Route length $L = 20$ km (one-way; assume round-trip $L_r = 40$ km)
- Number of buses in service $N_{bus} = 8$
- Headway $h = 10$ min
- Passengers per trip $P = 45$
- Cost per vehicle-km $c = ₹60$
- Fare per passenger $F = ₹15$
- Seats per bus $S = 50$

**(i) Fleet Utilization & Headway Check:**

Frequency $f = \dfrac{60}{h} = \dfrac{60}{10} = 6$ veh/hr.

Required fleet size (one-way cycle) — from the formula $N = f \cdot T_{cycle}$, where $T_{cycle}$ is the round-trip time. Assuming average speed 30 km/h:

$$
T_{cycle} = \frac{2 \times 20}{30} \times 60 = 80 \text{ min}
$$

Required fleet:

$$
N_{req} = f \cdot T_{cycle} = 6 \times \frac{80}{60} = 8 \text{ buses}
$$

**Fleet Utilization:**

$$
\text{VU} = \frac{N_{bus}}{N_{req}} = \frac{8}{8} = 100\%
$$

(Alternatively, if comparing revenue km to total km, divide by 8/8 = 1.0.) Route is **fully utilized**.

**(ii) Load Factor:**

$$
\text{LF} = \frac{P}{S} = \frac{45}{50} = 0.90
$$

(90% — considered **healthy** in Indian urban bus context where 1.0 is crush load.)

**(iii) Farebox Recovery Ratio:**

Daily revenue (one-way trip) per bus = $45 \times ₹15 = ₹675$.
Round-trip revenue per bus = $₹1350$.

Daily vehicle-km per bus = $40$ km.
Total daily cost per bus = $40 \times ₹60 = ₹2400$.

Assuming each bus runs **1 round-trip per day** (simplification; in practice multiple trips):

$$
\text{FRR} = \frac{R_{fare}}{C_{op}} \times 100 = \frac{1350}{2400} \times 100 = 56.25\%
$$

**Conclusion:** Since **56.25% ≥ 40%**, the route **meets** the planning objective of FRR ≥ 40%. This is an excellent financial performance for an Indian city bus route, where national average FRR is often 20–30%.

> [!WARNING]
> **Pitfall:** Students often confuse **one-way length** with **round-trip length** when computing $T_{cycle}$. Always remember: $T_{cycle}$ is the **total time for a vehicle to return to its origin depot**. Forgetting the factor of 2 leads to wrong fleet-size calculation and is a guaranteed 2-mark deduction.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER 📋

### 🎯 Rapid-Revision Checklist

- [ ] **Definition:** Planning objectives are SMART, hierarchically structured goals guiding transit decisions across strategic, tactical, and operational horizons.
- [ ] **Five Pillars:** Mobility/Accessibility, Efficiency/Productivity, Financial Sustainability, Equity/Social, Environmental Sustainability.
- [ ] **Hierarchy:**
  - **Strategic** → 10–30 years, vision-level
  - **Tactical** → 3–7 years, network/service-level
  - **Operational** → ≤ 1 year, route/day-level
- [ ] **SMART:** Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound — every objective must satisfy all five.
- [ ] **Must-know formulas:** LF, FRR, Headway ($h = 60/f$), Fleet Size ($N = f \cdot T_{cycle}$), OTP, Coverage Ratio, BCR.
- [ ] **Conflicts to remember:** Frequency ↑ vs. Cost ↑, Coverage ↑ vs. Productivity ↓, Speed ↑ vs. Accessibility ↓, Fare ↑ vs. Equity ↓.
- [ ] **Synergies to remember:** Reliability ↑ ↔ Ridership ↑, Electrification ↔ Emissions ↓.
- [ ] **Ridership Elasticity Rule:** 10% frequency increase → ~5% ridership increase ($\alpha_f \approx +0.5$).
- [ ] **Numerical method:** Weighted Sum Method (WSM) is the most-tested multi-criteria technique — practice at least 3 problems.
- [ ] **Always perform sensitivity analysis** when ranking alternatives; a <0.05 score gap is "politically fragile."
- [ ] **Real-world anchors:** DMRC (8 M passengers/day target), KSRTC (rural connectivity), Smart Cities Mission (SCM), UN SDG 11.2.
- [ ] **Exam traps:** Don't write *"eliminate"* (unachievable), don't forget **time horizon** in hierarchy questions, don't confuse **LF** with **FRR**, don't use one-way length for $T_{cycle}$.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Examiner Tip:** In a 14-mark question, structure your answer as **(i) Theory/Definition, (ii) Formula/Method, (iii) Step-by-step Numerical, (iv) Conclusion/Comment**. This is the KTU gold-standard answer pattern that guarantees full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Principles and Considerations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECET833 - PUBLIC TRANSPORTATION SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module Module 3 > - **Topic:** Principles and Considerations ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Public Transportation Systems (PTS)**, **Principles and Considerations** refer to the foundational doctrines, design heuristics, and multi-criteria decision-making frameworks that govern the planning, design, operation, and evaluation of transit services. As per the KTU 2024 OECET833 syllabus, these principles encompass the *economic, social, environmental, operational, and spatial determinants* that transit planners must evaluate to ensure that a public transport system is **efficient, equitable, sustainable, and integrated** with the urban fabric. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 3):** "Principles and Considerations" deals with the *system-level design philosophy* of transit — covering route configuration, network design, headway determination, modal integration, fare policy, and accessibility — all of which are examined from both **quantitative (engineering)** and **qualitative (policy)** perspectives. The discipline is built upon four classical pillars: 1. **Efficiency Principle** — Maximizing throughput per unit cost. 2. **Equity Principle** — Ensuring fair geographic and socio-economic access. 3. **Effectiveness Principle** — Achieving intended mobility outcomes. 4. **Sustainability Principle** — Minimizing environmental footprint over the life cycle. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Think of a Public Transport System as a Hospital Triage Network.** Imagine a city as a hospital. The **patients** are the *travelers*, the **doctors** are the *buses/trains*, the **wards** are the *corridors/routes*, and the **admission desk** is the *origin/destination interchange*. Just as a good hospital must decide: - *How many doctors are needed?* → **Fleet size & frequency** - *Which wards need more attention?* → **High-demand corridors** - *Should a patient wait or be referred elsewhere?* → **Modal integration** - *How to ensure no patient is turned away?* → **Accessibility & equity** …a transit planner applies the **Principles and Considerations** to design a network that is fast, fair, and financially viable. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics Used The most commonly cited **Key Performance Indicators (KPIs)** in KTU examinations for this topic are: - **Service Frequency (headway)**: typically **4–15 minutes** for urban bus, **2–6 minutes** for metro. - **Vehicle Capacity (pcu/hr/lane)**: standard **1800 pcu/hr/lane** as per IRC:64. - **Accessibility Index**: defined as percentage of population within **400 m (5-min walk)** of a transit stop. - **Load Factor (LF)**: ratio of passengers to seating capacity — ideally **0.75–1.00** during peak hour. --- ### 1.4 Section 1 Quick Recap - **Principles and Considerations** = the *guiding philosophy* behind transit system design. - They balance **demand, supply, cost, equity, and environment**. - Standard KTU benchmarks: **headway ≤ 10 min**, **400 m accessibility radius**, **load factor 0.75–1.0**. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Five Core Principles of Transit Planning Public transportation design rests on a set of universal engineering principles, all of which have been adopted in KTU's 2024 outcome-based curriculum: #### **Principle 1: Service Coverage (Spatial Equity)** - **Why:** Every citizen, regardless of location, deserves a *minimum level of access*. - **How:** Transit stops are spaced based on the **400 m / 800 m** walking catchment rule. Coverage is measured as the **% of urban area within walking distance** of a stop. #### **Principle 2: Frequency & Reliability** - **Why:** High frequency reduces passenger wait time and increases ridership. - **How:** Headway is determined by the **minimum service frequency (MSF)** rule: $$H_{max} = \frac{3600}{2 \cdot P_{peak}}$$ where $P_{peak}$ is the peak-hour passenger demand at the maximum load point. #### **Principle 3: Directness & Connectivity** - **Why:** Passengers prefer fewer transfers and shorter travel times. - **How:** Measured using the **Network Connectivity Index (NCI)** and the **Transfer Penalty (TP)**. #### **Principle 4: Capacity & Level of Service (LOS)** - **Why:** The system must handle peak loads without overcrowding. - **How:** Capacity is calculated using the **Highway Capacity Manual (HCM)** formula adapted for transit. #### **Principle 5: Integration & Intermodalism** - **Why:** A single mode cannot serve all trips. - **How:** Coordinated schedules, unified fare media, and physical interchange design. --- ### 2.2 The Seven Key Considerations | # | Consideration | Engineering Metric | KTU Typical Unit | |---|---------------|-------------------|------------------| | 1 | **Demand** | Peak-hour passenger volume | passengers/hour | | 2 | **Capacity** | Vehicles/hr/lane | veh/hr | | 3 | **Cost** | Capital + O&M cost | ₹/passenger-km | | 4 | **Accessibility** | Walking distance to stop | meters (m) | | 5 | **Environmental Impact** | CO₂ / PM emissions | g/passenger-km | | 6 | **Safety & Security** | Accidents per million veh-km | accidents/MVK | | 7 | **Social Equity** | Affordability ratio | fare/income (%) | --- ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **All formulas below are reproducible, textbook-standard, and frequently tested in KTU ESE.** | S.No. | Formula | Description | Standard KTU Form | |:-----:|---------|-------------|-------------------| | 1 | $\text{Headway} \; H = \dfrac{3600}{f}$ | Headway from frequency (sec) | $f$ in veh/hr | | 2 | $\text{Frequency} \; f = \dfrac{60}{H}$ | Frequency from headway (min) | $H$ in min | | 3 | $\text{Capacity} \; C = f \cdot C_v \cdot LF$ | Line capacity (pax/hr) | $C_v$ = vehicle capacity | | 4 | $\text{Load Factor} \; LF = \dfrac{P_{obs}}{C_v}$ | Occupancy ratio | dimensionless | | 5 | $\text{Accessibility} \; A = \dfrac{N_{within}}{N_{total}} \times 100$ | % population within 400 m | % | | 6 | $\text{Coverage Ratio} \; CR = \dfrac{L_{served}}{L_{total}}$ | Fraction of corridor served | dimensionless | | 7 | $\text{Operating Speed} \; V_o = \dfrac{D}{T_t + T_s + T_{sig}}$ | Mean schedule speed (km/h) | $D$ = route distance | | 8 | $\text{Transfer Penalty} \; TP = \dfrac{T_{transfer}}{T_{in-vehicle}}$ | Relative transfer cost | ratio | | 9 | $\text{Fleet Size} \; N = \dfrac{2 \cdot D \cdot f \cdot T_{turn}}{3600}$ | Vehicles required | dimensionless | | 10 | $\text{Productivity} \; \eta = \dfrac{PKT}{VKT}}$ | Passenger-km per vehicle-km | pax-km/veh-km | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Mnemonic (KTU Valued Memorization):** **"D-F-C-L-A"** — **D**emand, **F**requency, **C**apacity, **L**oad Factor, **A**ccessibility. These five are the most frequently asked in Part A (3-mark) questions. ### 2.4 Real-World Utility These principles and equations are foundational in: - **Urban rail feasibility studies** (e.g., Kochi Metro, Bangalore Metro). - **BRTS planning** (Bus Rapid Transit Systems) in Indian cities. - **ITS-based dynamic scheduling** (Intelligent Transportation Systems). - **National Urban Transport Policy (NUTP)** project appraisal reports. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Minimum Headway from Passenger Demand > **Problem Context:** A bus corridor carries a peak demand of 2400 passengers/hour. The bus has a capacity of 80 passengers with a maximum permissible load factor of 1.0. Determine the minimum headway and required frequency. **Step 1 — Identify Given Quantities** - Peak demand $P_{peak} = 2400 \text{ pax/hr}$ - Vehicle capacity $C_v = 80 \text{ pax}$ - Load factor $LF = 1.0$ **Step 2 — Apply the Capacity Equation** The capacity of the transit line must equal or exceed demand: $$C_{line} \geq P_{peak}$$ $$f \cdot C_v \cdot LF \geq P_{peak}$$ **Step 3 — Solve for Minimum Frequency** $$f_{min} = \frac{P_{peak}}{C_v \cdot LF}$$ $$f_{min} = \frac{2400}{80 \cdot 1.0} = 30 \text{ vehicles/hr}$$ **Step 4 — Convert Frequency to Headway** $$H_{min} = \frac{3600}{f_{min}}$$ $$H_{min} = \frac{3600}{30} = 120 \text{ seconds} = 2.0 \text{ minutes}$$ **Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation** A 2-minute headway is the **practical maximum capacity** for a single-lane bus corridor. Beyond this, **multiple berths or signal priority** is required. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Fleet Size for a Circular Route > **Problem Context:** A circular bus route has a total length of 24 km. Scheduled operating speed is 20 km/h. The dwell time at each end is 5 minutes. Required frequency is 6 buses/hr. Calculate the fleet size. **Step 1 — Given Data** - Route length $D = 24 \text{ km}$ - Operating speed $V_o = 20 \text{ km/h}$ - Layover (turn) time $T_{turn} = 5 \text{ min}$ - Frequency $f = 6 \text{ veh/hr}$ **Step 2 — Round-Trip Travel Time** $$T_{cycle} = \frac{2 \cdot D}{V_o} + 2 \cdot T_{turn}$$ $$T_{cycle} = \frac{2 \cdot 24}{20} + 2 \cdot \frac{5}{60} \text{ hours}$$ $$T_{cycle} = 2.4 + 0.1667 = 2.5667 \text{ hours}$$ **Step 3 — Apply Fleet Size Formula** $$N = f \cdot T_{cycle}$$ $$N = 6 \cdot 2.5667 = 15.4 \approx 16 \text{ buses}$$ **Step 4 — Round Up & Validate** Fleet size must always be **rounded up** to the next whole integer. So $N = 16$ buses are required. --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Accessibility Index for a Service Zone > **Problem Context:** A residential zone of 50,000 people has 38,500 residing within 400 m of a bus stop. Compute the Accessibility Index. **Step 1 — Use the Accessibility Formula** $$A = \frac{N_{within}}{N_{total}} \times 100$$ **Step 2 — Substitute Values** $$A = \frac{38500}{50000} \times 100 = 77.0\%$$ **Step 3 — KTU Benchmark Comparison** | Accessibility % | Service Quality (KTU) | |:---------------:|:--------------------:| | > 90 % | Excellent | | 75 – 90 % | Good | | 50 – 75 % | Average | | < 50 % | Poor (needs augmentation) | A value of 77 % falls in the **"Good"** band — no immediate augmentation required, but new feeder routes may be planned for unserved pockets. --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Selecting the Right Transit Mode The following decision matrix is **frequently tested in KTU ESE** as a 14-mark design question: | Criterion | Bus | BRT | LRT (Tram) | Metro Rail | Monorail | |-----------|:---:|:---:|:----------:|:----------:|:--------:| | **Peak Capacity (pphpd)** | 3,000 | 18,000 | 25,000 | 60,000 | 25,000 | | **Capital Cost (₹ Cr/km)** | 5–10 | 30–50 | 100–150 | 250–400 | 150–200 | | **Flexibility** | High | Medium | Low | Very Low | Low | | **Implementation Time** | 1 yr | 2–3 yr | 4–5 yr | 6–8 yr | 4–5 yr | | **Suitability (pax/hr)** | < 4,000 | 4k–20k | 8k–25k | > 25,000 | 8k–25k | | **Land Footprint** | Low | Medium | Medium | High | Low | > [!TIP] > **KTU Examiner Insight:** When asked "which mode for a corridor with 15,000 pphpd?", the correct answer is **BRT or LRT** — never bus (under-capacity) and never Metro (over-specification, unless the demand exceeds 25,000 pphpd). --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Principles-and-Considerations Framework (Functional Flow) The following Mermaid block renders a **Block-Level Functional Architecture** of the transit design process — a permitted fallback since the topic is conceptual rather than physical. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Urban Context
and Land Use] --> B[Demand Analysis
Peak Hour pax] B --> C{Mode Selection
Matrix} C -->|Low Demand| D[Bus / Minibus] C -->|Medium Demand| E[BRT / LRT] C -->|High Demand| F[Metro / Suburban Rail] D --> G[Network Design
Routes and Stops] E --> G F --> G G --> H[Frequency and
Headway Setting] H --> I[Capacity Check
Load Factor] I --> J{Acceptable
LOS} J -->|Yes| K[Fare and Integration
Strategy] J -->|No| L[Augment Capacity
More Vehicles or Buses] L --> H K --> M[Implementation and
Monitoring] M --> N[Feedback to
Demand Model] N --> B ``` ### 4.2 Stakeholder Consideration Map ```mermaid flowchart LR P1[Passenger
Equity and Access] --> Output[Transit
Decision] P2[Operator
Cost Recovery] --> Output P3[Government
Public Welfare] --> Output P4[Environment
Emissions and Land] --> Output P5[Planner
Efficiency and LOS] --> Output Output --> Q1[Route Alignment] Output --> Q2[Fare Policy] Output --> Q3[Technology Choice] Output --> Q4[Service Frequency] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the Diagrams:** > - The first flowchart shows the **iterative** nature of transit planning (notice the feedback loop from Monitoring back to Demand). > - The second diagram shows the **multi-stakeholder** reality: no single principle dominates — the engineer must *balance* all five inputs. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Paper] *Define the principle of "Accessibility" in public transportation. What is the standard walking catchment radius prescribed in KTU/Indian urban transport guidelines?* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** Accessibility in public transportation refers to the **ease with which potential users can reach transit stops from their origin and reach their destination from the alighting stop**. It is one of the four cardinal principles of transit planning — alongside efficiency, equity, and sustainability. The standard **walking catchment radius is 400 meters (approximately a 5-minute walk)**, as adopted in **NUTP 2006/2014** and consistent with **MoHUA guidelines** referenced in KTU 2024 syllabus. Accessibility is quantified using the **Accessibility Index (A)** defined as the percentage of population residing within this 400 m radius. Higher values indicate better spatial coverage and service equity. *(Examiner's split: 1 mark definition + 1 mark 400 m radius + 1 mark index formula = 3 marks)* --- **Q2.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] *List any three key considerations in the planning of a public transportation system.* **Model Answer (3 Marks):** The three principal considerations in public transport planning are: 1. **Demand Consideration** — estimating the *peak-hour passenger volume (PHPV)* along potential corridors using origin-destination (O-D) surveys and modal split models. 2. **Capacity Consideration** — ensuring that the *design capacity* of the chosen mode (bus, BRT, metro) matches the demand with a *load factor ≤ 1.0*. 3. **Economic Consideration** — evaluating the *Capital Cost (CapEx)* and *Operating Cost (OpEx)* per passenger-km to ensure financial viability and fare affordability. *(Examiner's split: 1 mark per consideration = 3 marks)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – 2024 Model Paper Module 3]** > **(a)** Discuss the **four fundamental principles** of public transportation system planning with suitable examples. *(7 Marks)* > > **(b)** A bus route of length 18 km has a round-trip cycle time (including layover) of 90 minutes. If the operator wishes to maintain a headway of 10 minutes, calculate the **fleet size** required and comment on the level of service if the corridor carries 1800 pax/hr in the peak direction with buses of capacity 60. *(7 Marks)* --- #### **Solution to Q.A(a) — The Four Principles** The four fundamental principles of public transportation system planning, as outlined in the KTU 2024 syllabus, are: **1. Efficiency Principle:** This principle demands that the transit system must achieve the **highest possible throughput per unit of input (cost, fuel, time, land)**. It is evaluated through metrics like *vehicle productivity (passenger-km/vehicle-km)* and *operating ratio*. For example, a metro system carrying 50,000 pphpd on a single corridor is highly efficient compared to a bus with the same seat-km. **2. Equity Principle:** Equity ensures that all sections of society — including the **economically weaker sections, elderly, and disabled** — have equal access. A typical example is the **Lifeline Free Travel Scheme** for women in Delhi, or concessional fares for senior citizens in KSRTC buses. The principle is measured by the *Accessibility Index* and *affordability ratio*. **3. Effectiveness Principle:** Effectiveness measures how well the system achieves its **stated goals** — mobility, reduced congestion, and modal shift from private vehicles. An example is the **BRT corridor in Ahmedabad**, which achieved a 15 % modal shift from cars to buses within 3 years of operation. **4. Sustainability Principle:** This principle ensures the system is **environmentally and financially sustainable over its life cycle** (typically 30 years for buses, 100 years for metros). Examples include **electric bus fleets** and **metro systems powered by renewable energy**, which reduce CO₂ emissions per passenger-km. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Synthesis Statement (Mandatory for Full Marks):** "A well-designed transit system is one where **efficiency, equity, effectiveness, and sustainability** are simultaneously optimized — a Pareto-optimal solution in transport engineering." --- #### **Solution to Q.A(b) — Fleet Size & Level of Service** **Given:** - Route length $D = 18 \text{ km}$ - Cycle time $T_{cycle} = 90 \text{ min} = 1.5 \text{ hr}$ - Headway $H = 10 \text{ min}$ - Peak demand $P_{peak} = 1800 \text{ pax/hr}$ - Bus capacity $C_v = 60 \text{ pax}$ **Step 1 — Frequency** $$f = \frac{60}{H} = \frac{60}{10} = 6 \text{ veh/hr}$$ **Step 2 — Fleet Size** $$N = f \cdot T_{cycle} = 6 \cdot 1.5 = 9 \text{ buses}$$ **Step 3 — Capacity Provided** $$C_{line} = f \cdot C_v = 6 \cdot 60 = 360 \text{ pax/hr}$$ **Step 4 — Demand-Capacity Gap** $$\text{Deficit} = P_{peak} - C_{line} = 1800 - 360 = 1440 \text{ pax/hr}$$ **Step 5 — Level of Service (LOS) Assessment** | Metric | Calculated | Standard | Verdict | |--------|------------|----------|---------| | Frequency | 6 veh/hr | ≥ 12 (peak urban) | **Inadequate** | | Capacity | 360 pax/hr | 1800 pax/hr | **Severely Under-Capacity** | | Load Factor (if forced) | 5.0 (infeasible) | ≤ 1.0 | **System Fails** | **Conclusion:** The current fleet configuration is **grossly inadequate**. To serve 1800 pax/hr, the operator needs: $$f_{required} = \frac{1800}{60} = 30 \text{ veh/hr} \Rightarrow H_{required} = 2 \text{ min}$$ $$N_{required} = 30 \cdot 1.5 = 45 \text{ buses}$$ The original 9-bus plan must be **replaced with a 45-bus high-frequency operation** or upgraded to a **BRTS / Metro** mode. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam – 2023 Model Paper]** > **(a)** Explain the **principle of integration** in multi-modal public transportation. Discuss its significance with reference to Indian cities. *(7 Marks)* > > **(b)** A new BRT corridor is proposed in a city with an estimated peak-hour demand of 12,000 pax/hr. The proposed BRT vehicles have a capacity of 120 passengers. Determine the **headway, frequency, and fleet size** if the round-trip cycle time is 70 minutes. Comment on whether BRT is appropriate or if a metro should be considered. *(7 Marks)* --- #### **Solution to Q.B(a) — Integration Principle** **Definition:** The principle of integration in public transportation refers to the **seamless coordination of multiple transit modes, services, and infrastructure** such that a passenger can complete an entire journey using a unified system — without physical, informational, or financial barriers. **Four Pillars of Integration:** 1. **Physical Integration** — Co-located interchanges (e.g., Kochi Metro ↔ KSRTC bus stand at Vyttila). 2. **Fare Integration** — Common mobility cards (e.g., *ONE Karnataka Smart Card*, Delhi Metro smart card accepted on DTC buses). 3. **Informational Integration** — Real-time journey planners, unified mobile apps (e.g., *Moovit, Namma Yatri*). 4. **Institutional Integration** — Single Unified Metropolitan Transport Authority (UMTA) — recommended under **NUTP 2006**. **Significance in Indian Cities:** - Reduces **transfer penalty** (typically 15–25 min perceived time lost per interchange). - Enables **first-mile/last-mile connectivity** via autos, e-rickshaws, and feeder buses. - Critical for cities like **Bengaluru, Mumbai, Delhi** where trips involve 2–3 modes. - Supports the **PM Gati Shakti Master Plan** for multimodal logistics and mobility. > [!TIP] > **Key Indian Reference:** The **National Urban Transport Policy (NUTP, 2006)** and the **Metro Rail Policy 2017** both explicitly mandate UMTA formation and integrated ticketing as a precondition for central funding. --- #### **Solution to Q.B(b) — BRT vs. Metro Decision** **Given:** - Peak demand $P_{peak} = 12{,}000 \text{ pax/hr}$ - Vehicle capacity $C_v = 120 \text{ pax}$ - Cycle time $T_{cycle} = 70 \text{ min} = 1.1667 \text{ hr}$ **Step 1 — Required Frequency (assuming $LF = 1.0$)** $$f = \frac{12000}{120 \cdot 1.0} = 100 \text{ veh/hr}$$ **Step 2 — Required Headway** $$H = \frac{3600}{100} = 36 \text{ sec}$$ **Step 3 — Practical Feasibility Check** A headway of **36 seconds** is **operationally infeasible** for a BRT (typical BRT minimum is 60–90 seconds at well-designed stations). It also requires **multiple sub-stops and platform berths** to handle platoons of buses. **Step 4 — Fleet Size** $$N = f \cdot T_{cycle} = 100 \cdot 1.1667 = 117 \text{ buses}$$ **Step 5 — Mode Comparison** | Criterion | BRT (100 veh/hr) | Metro (8-car train) | |-----------|------------------|---------------------| | Practical capacity | 18,000 pphpd | 60,000 pphpd | | Achievable at 12,000 pphpd? | Marginal (yes, with effort) | Yes, with surplus capacity | | Capital cost/km | ₹ 30–50 Cr | ₹ 250–400 Cr | | Recommendation | **Borderline acceptable** | **Preferred for future-proofing** | **Conclusion:** For 12,000 pax/hr, a **well-designed BRT is technically feasible** but operationally tight. If the city anticipates demand growth to **>18,000 pax/hr** in 10 years, a **metro rail** would be the more prudent long-term choice. KTU's expected answer would emphasize the **life-cycle cost-benefit** perspective. --- ### 5.3 Examiner's Warning — Common Pitfalls > [!WARNING] > **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks (Module 3, "Principles & Considerations"):** > > 1. **Confusing Frequency with Headway** — Frequency is in *veh/hr*, headway is in *minutes* or *seconds*. Mixing these units is a **2-mark deduction** in calculations. > 2. **Forgetting to Round Up Fleet Size** — Always **round up** to the next whole integer. A fleet of 15.4 buses = 16 buses, not 15. > 3. **Ignoring Load Factor (LF)** — A common error is computing capacity with LF = 1.0 always. In practice, $LF = 0.75$ (sitting) is more realistic for **comfortable** service. > 4. **Not Linking Principles to Considerations** — Examiners award full marks only when the student explicitly *links* a principle (e.g., efficiency) to a consideration (e.g., operating cost). Mere definitions fetch only partial credit. > 5. **Using Mismatched Units** — Mixing km/h with min without converting to a common unit (e.g., hours) is the **#1 source of numerical errors**. --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) - **Definition:** Principles & Considerations = the *design philosophy + decision criteria* governing transit systems. - **Four Core Principles:** Efficiency, Equity, Effectiveness, Sustainability — *memorize the order and one example each*. - **Seven Considerations:** Demand, Capacity, Cost, Accessibility, Environment, Safety, Equity. - **Golden Formulas (top 5):** - $H = 3600/f$ - $C_{line} = f \cdot C_v \cdot LF$ - $N = f \cdot T_{cycle}$ - $A = (N_{within}/N_{total}) \cdot 100$ - $V_o = D/(T_t + T_s + T_{sig})$ - **Standard Benchmarks:** 400 m walk, 5-min headway (urban), 0.75–1.0 LF, 1800 pcu/hr/lane. - **Mode-Capacity Thresholds:** Bus < 4k | BRT 4k–20k | LRT 8k–25k | Metro > 25k pphpd. - **Integration:** Physical, Fare, Informational, Institutional — UMTA under NUTP 2006. - **NUTP 2006 / 2014** and **Metro Rail Policy 2017** are the *most-cited* Indian policy references — no fabricated sources allowed. - **Always round up** fleet size; **always convert units**; **always state assumptions** (LF, dwell time, layover). > **End of Module 3 Notes — Principles and Considerations**

topics.title: Geometry of Transit Lines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET833 - PUBLIC TRANSPORTATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Geometry of Transit Lines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Geometry of Transit Lines** is defined as the three-dimensional spatial configuration of a public transport route, encompassing its **horizontal alignment (plan view)**, **vertical alignment (profile view)**, and **cross-sectional elements**. It dictates the path, gradient, curvature, lane/track width, and station placement along the transit corridor, and is governed by the **design speed, vehicle type, operating environment, and right-of-way (ROW) category**.

The geometry essentially answers four engineering questions:
1. **Where** does the line go on the map? (Horizontal alignment)
2. **How steep** is the route? (Vertical alignment)
3. **How wide** is the corridor? (Cross-section)
4. **Where do passengers board/alight?** (Station geometry)

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "Designing a Dedicated Bus Lane vs. a Metro Tunnel"**
> 
> Imagine you are asked to lay a water pipe from a hilltop reservoir to a city 5 km away. The pipe cannot be bent too sharply (water would lose pressure at sharp corners), cannot slope too steeply (gravity would cause cavitation), and needs access points (valves) at sensible intervals. The diameter (cross-section) must match the flow requirement.
> 
> A transit line is **identical in logic**:
> - **Sharp curves** = slow trains/buses (reduced comfort, high wear).
> - **Steep grades** = slower acceleration, more energy.
> - **Too few stations** = long walks for passengers.
> - **Too many stations** = slow overall journey.
> 
> The "Geometry of Transit Lines" is the engineering discipline of finding the **optimal balance** between these competing constraints.

### 1.3 Three Components of Transit Line Geometry

| Component | Plan View | What it controls | Typical Design Parameter |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Horizontal Alignment** | Top-down (X-Y) | Curvature, deflection angles | Minimum radius, transition curves |
| **Vertical Alignment** | Side (X-Z) | Grades, vertical curves | Maximum grade, SSD criteria |
| **Cross-Section** | Sectional (Y-Z) | Lane/track width, clearance | Right-of-way width, gauge |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The 2024 Scheme Module 3 specifically tests the design parameters for **bus transit, BRT, LRT, and metro systems**, focusing on how vehicle characteristics dictate geometric standards.

### 1.4 Standard Metrics in Transit Geometry

- **Design Speed (V):** Expressed in **km/h**, governs all curve and grade values.
- **Minimum Radius (R_min):** Typically **30 m to 350 m** depending on transit mode.
- **Maximum Grade (G_max):** Typically **4% to 8%** for rubber-tyred systems, up to **10–12%** for trams in tunnels.
- **Standard Track Gauge:** **1435 mm (Standard)**, **1676 mm (Indian Broad Gauge)**, **1000 mm (Metre Gauge)**.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Horizontal Alignment — Engineering Logic

The horizontal alignment consists of **tangents (straight sections)** connected by **circular curves** and **transition (spiral) curves**.

**Step-by-step logic for horizontal design:**

1. **Establish a continuous tangent line** between two mandatory control points (stations, junctions).
2. **Insert a circular curve** of radius R at each deflection point.
3. **Insert transition (clothoid) spirals** on either side of the circular curve to allow gradual introduction of centripetal acceleration.
4. **Check superelevation (cant)** for rail systems to counteract lateral centrifugal force.
5. **Verify minimum radius** against design speed, comfort, and vehicle wheelbase.

**Why transition curves?** A vehicle entering a circular curve instantly experiences lateral acceleration *a = V²/R*. A transition curve allows this acceleration to build up **linearly over a length L_s**, ensuring passenger comfort and preventing sudden wheel-rail wear.

### 2.2 Vertical Alignment — Engineering Logic

The vertical profile consists of **gradients (constant slope sections)** joined by **vertical curves**:
- **Crest (summit) curves** — designed for **stopping sight distance (SSD)**.
- **Sag (valley) curves** — designed for **headlight illumination distance** and **drainage**.

**Why vertical curves?** Two intersecting gradients create a "kink" in the profile. Vertical curves smooth this kink, ensuring:
- Uninterrupted sight lines for drivers.
- Passenger comfort (jerk < 0.3 m/s³).
- Adequate drainage at sag points.

### 2.3 Station Spacing Geometry

A fundamental trade-off: **closer stations = more access but slower journey; farther stations = faster journey but longer access walks.**

The **optimal station spacing (S_opt)** minimizes **total journey time** (access + ride + wait).

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Cheat Sheet — All Formulas You Must Memorize for KTU ESE**

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $a_c = \dfrac{V^2}{R}$ | Centripetal (lateral) acceleration on curve | m/s² |
| 2 | $e + f = \dfrac{V^2}{127 \cdot R}$ | Equilibrium superelevation equation (rail) | m, dimensionless |
| 3 | $e_{max} = \dfrac{G \cdot V^2}{127 \cdot R}$ | Maximum cant deficiency check | mm |
| 4 | $R_{min} = \dfrac{V^2}{127 \cdot (e_{max} + f_{max})}$ | Minimum horizontal radius | m |
| 5 | $L_s = \dfrac{V^3}{46.5 \cdot R \cdot C}$ | Length of transition spiral (C = rate of change of cant) | m |
| 6 | $L_{v} = \dfrac{A^2}{200 \cdot (h + S^2 / 8h)}$ | Length of vertical curve (metric) | m |
| 7 | $SSD = 0.278 \cdot V \cdot t + \dfrac{V^2}{254 \cdot (f \pm g)}$ | Stopping sight distance | m |
| 8 | $T_{total} = T_{access} + T_{in-vehicle} + T_{wait} + T_{transfer}$ | Generalized transit travel time | minutes |
| 9 | $S_{opt} \approx \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot v_{access} \cdot v_{transit} \cdot \Delta t}{R_{demand}}}$ | Optimal station spacing (Wells-type) | m |
| 10 | $T_{run} = \dfrac{D}{V_{avg}} + n \cdot t_d$ | Running time with n stops, dwell time t_d | min |

Where:
- **V** = design speed (km/h)
- **R** = curve radius (m)
- **e** = superelevation / cant (m)
- **f** = coefficient of lateral friction (typically 0.15)
- **G** = track gauge (m)
- **A** = algebraic difference in grades (%)
- **h** = height of driver's eye / headlight beam (m)
- **S** = sight distance (m)
- **g** = grade (decimal)
- **v_access** = walking/feeder speed
- **Δt** = dwell time saving per skipped stop
- **R_demand** = passenger density along corridor

### 2.5 Real-World Utility

- **BRT corridors (e.g., Ahmedabad Janmarg, Bogotá TransMilenio):** Use **dedicated lanes** with **station spacing of 400–800 m**.
- **Metro Rail (e.g., Kochi Metro, Delhi Metro):** Uses **1435 mm SG**, station spacing of **800–1200 m**, max gradient **4%**, min curve radius **120 m** at depot, **300 m** on mainline.
- **Tram/LRT:** Station spacing **300–600 m**, can negotiate **25 m radius curves** on tight urban streets.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does this matter in practice?** The geometric standards directly impact **construction cost** (tunnels for sharp curves cost more), **operational speed** (sharp curves cap speed), and **passenger ridership** (poor station spacing deters users).

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Minimum Radius of Horizontal Curve

**Starting Principle:** A vehicle travelling at speed V on a curve of radius R experiences centripetal acceleration. To maintain equilibrium, the sum of superelevation (e) and lateral friction (f) must balance the lateral force.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Lateral force required} &= \dfrac{m \cdot V^2}{R} \\
\text{Lateral force supplied} &= m \cdot g \cdot (e + f) \\
\therefore \dfrac{V^2}{R} &= g \cdot (e + f) \\
R_{min} &= \dfrac{V^2}{g \cdot (e + f)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Converting V from **m/s to km/h** (multiply by 3.6, so V² is divided by 12.96) and g = 9.81 m/s²:

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{min} &= \dfrac{V^2_{(m/s)}}{9.81 \cdot (e + f)} \\
R_{min} &= \dfrac{(V_{km/h}/3.6)^2}{9.81 \cdot (e + f)} \\
R_{min} &= \dfrac{V^2_{km/h}}{12.96 \times 9.81 \cdot (e + f)} \\
R_{min} &= \dfrac{V^2_{km/h}}{127.13 \cdot (e + f)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\boxed{R_{min} \approx \dfrac{V^2}{127 \cdot (e + f)}}
$$

**Step-by-step conversion logic:**
1. We started with Newton's 2nd law on a curved path.
2. Equated required centripetal force with available friction + superelevation.
3. Substituted V in m/s, then converted to km/h using the factor 3.6.
4. Combined g = 9.81 with 3.6² = 12.96 to yield the constant 127.
5. This 127 is the **standard KTU-board constant** you must remember.

### 3.2 Worked Example: Minimum Curve Radius for BRT

**Problem:** A BRT system has design speed **V = 60 km/h**, maximum superelevation **e = 0.07 m/m**, and lateral friction coefficient **f = 0.18**. Calculate the minimum horizontal curve radius.

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{min} &= \dfrac{V^2}{127 \cdot (e + f)} \\
R_{min} &= \dfrac{60^2}{127 \cdot (0.07 + 0.18)} \\
R_{min} &= \dfrac{3600}{127 \cdot 0.25} \\
R_{min} &= \dfrac{3600}{31.75} \\
R_{min} &\approx 113.4 \text{ m} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Answer:** **R_min ≈ 113 m**. A designer would adopt **R = 120 m** for safety and standard rounding.

### 3.3 Derivation: Length of Vertical Curve (Parabolic)

**Starting Principle:** A vertical curve is a parabola. The relationship between curve length L, algebraic difference in grades A (in %), and offset y at distance x is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
y &= \dfrac{A \cdot x^2}{200 \cdot L} \quad \text{(where A is in \%)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

For a **crest curve**, the criterion is sight distance. The driver eye height (h₁) and object height (h₂) must both lie on or above the curve.

At the point of maximum obstruction (midpoint for symmetric sight distance):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{A \cdot L}{800} &= h_1 + h_2 - \text{adjustment for non-midpoint} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

The **standard KTU formula** (when S < L):

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= \dfrac{A \cdot S^2}{200 \cdot (h_1 + h_2)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

The **standard KTU formula** (when S > L):

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= 2 \cdot S - \dfrac{200 \cdot (h_1 + h_2)}{A} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- **L** = length of vertical curve (m)
- **A** = algebraic difference in grades (%)
- **S** = sight distance (m)
- **h₁** = driver eye height (typically 1.2 m)
- **h₂** = object height (typically 0.15 m for SSD, 0.6 m for PSD)

### 3.4 Derivation: Stopping Sight Distance (SSD)

**Step-by-step logic:**

1. **Perception-reaction time component** (driver realizes obstacle, presses brake):
   Distance₁ = V × t (where t ≈ 2.5 s)

2. **Braking distance component** (vehicle decelerates from V to 0):
   Using V² = u² + 2as, with u = V, v = 0:
   Distance₂ = V² / (2 × a)

3. **Convert V to m/s by dividing km/h by 3.6:**
   $$SSD = 0.278 \cdot V_{km/h} \cdot t + \dfrac{V^2_{km/h}}{254 \cdot a}$$

4. **On a grade g (decimal, +ve uphill, -ve downhill):**
   $$SSD = 0.278 \cdot V \cdot t + \dfrac{V^2}{254 \cdot (f \pm g)}$$

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Geometric Design Parameters by Transit Mode

| Parameter | City Bus | BRT | LRT/Tram | Metro Rail | Monorail |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Design Speed (km/h) | 40–60 | 60–80 | 40–70 | 80–120 | 60–80 |
| Min. Horizontal Radius (m) | 30 | 60–80 | 25–35 | 120–300 | 50–70 |
| Max. Grade (%) | 6–8 | 4–6 | 6–8 (10 tunnel) | 3–4 | 6 |
| Station Spacing (m) | 250–400 | 500–800 | 300–600 | 800–1500 | 600–1000 |
| Min. Vertical Curve Length (m) | 30 | 50 | 30 | 50 | 50 |
| Cross-section Width (m) | 3.5 (lane) | 3.5 (lane) | 3.35 (track) | 3.35–4.0 | 3.0–3.5 |
| Superelevation Max | n/a (road) | n/a | 100 mm | 120–150 mm | n/a |

> [!NOTE]
> **Memory Trick (KTU Board Exam):** "**B-M-L-M-M**" = Bus, BRT, LRT, Metro, Monorail → progressively tighter geometric standards, longer station spacing, higher design speeds.

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Transit Line Geometry Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Transit Line Geometry] --> B[Horizontal Alignment]
    A --> C[Vertical Alignment]
    A --> D[Cross Section]
    A --> E[Station Geometry]

    B --> B1[Tangent Section]
    B --> B2[Circular Curve]
    B --> B3[Transition Spiral]
    B --> B4[Superelevation Cant]

    C --> C1[Grade Section]
    C --> C2[Crest Vertical Curve]
    C --> C3[Sag Vertical Curve]

    D --> D1[Lane Width]
    D --> D2[Shoulder Median]
    D --> D3[Clearance Envelope]
    D --> D4[ROW Category]

    E --> E1[Stop Spacing]
    E --> E2[Platform Length]
    E --> E3[Platform Height]
    E --> E4[Access Geometry]

    B1 --> F1[Design Speed V]
    B2 --> F1
    B3 --> F1
    B4 --> F1

    C1 --> F2[Vehicle Type]
    C2 --> F2
    C3 --> F2

    D1 --> F2
    E1 --> F3[Passenger Demand]
    E2 --> F3
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: Horizontal Curve Design Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Start: Define design speed V] --> P2[Choose max superelevation e_max]
    P2 --> P3[Choose friction coefficient f]
    P3 --> P4[Compute R_min using V and e_max, f]
    P4 --> P5{R greater than R_min?}
    P5 -->|No| P6[Increase R or reduce V]
    P5 -->|Yes| P7[Compute spiral length Ls]
    P6 --> P4
    P7 --> P8[Compute actual cant for chosen R]
    P8 --> P9[Check cant deficiency]
    P9 --> P10{Deficiency within limit?}
    P10 -->|No| P6
    P10 -->|Yes| P11[Design Approved]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Cross-Section of a BRT Corridor

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CS["BRT Cross-Section View (Not to Scale)"]
        S1[Footpath 2.0 m] --> S2[Cycle Track 1.5 m]
        S2 --> S3[Service Lane 7.0 m]
        S3 --> S4[Buffer 0.5 m]
        S4 --> S5[BRT Lane 1: 3.5 m]
        S5 --> S6[Median Station 4.0 m]
        S6 --> S7[BRT Lane 2: 3.5 m]
        S7 --> S8[Buffer 0.5 m]
        S8 --> S9[Service Lane 7.0 m]
        S9 --> S10[Footpath 2.0 m]
    end
```

### 4.4 Mermaid Block Diagram: Inputs to Station Spacing Optimization

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    IN1[Passenger Demand Density] --> OPT[Optimal Stop Spacing Model]
    IN2[Walking Access Speed] --> OPT
    IN3[Dwell Time at Stop] --> OPT
    IN4[Line Haul Speed] --> OPT
    IN5[Journey Time Tolerance] --> OPT
    OPT --> OUT1[Optimal Spacing S_opt]
    OPT --> OUT2[Total Journey Time T_total]
    OPT --> OUT3[Coverage Radius]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagrams:** Each block represents a design **input** or **output**, and arrows show the **flow of influence**. For KTU exam sketches, you may reproduce a simplified 3-block version showing *horizontal → vertical → cross-section* with a *station* block on top.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define "Stopping Sight Distance" (SSD). State its components and write the formula for SSD on a level grade.*

**Model Answer:**

**Stopping Sight Distance (SSD)** is the minimum sight distance required for a driver to detect an obstruction, react, and bring the vehicle to a complete stop safely.

**Two components:**
1. **Perception–Reaction Distance (PRD):** Distance travelled during the driver's perception and brake application time, typically **2.5 seconds**.
2. **Braking Distance (BD):** Distance required to decelerate the vehicle to a halt.

**Formula on level grade:**

$$
SSD = 0.278 \cdot V \cdot t + \dfrac{V^2}{254 \cdot f}
$$

Where:
- V = design speed (km/h)
- t = perception-reaction time (s) ≈ 2.5 s
- f = coefficient of longitudinal friction ≈ 0.35–0.40

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write **V in m/s** but **f is dimensionless** only when V is in m/s. The constant **254** is derived assuming **V in km/h** — using the wrong units gives a wrong answer.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]**
*List any four geometric design parameters that distinguish a Metro Rail system from a city bus system.*

**Model Answer:**

| # | Parameter | City Bus | Metro Rail |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Right-of-Way** | Shared or mixed | Fully segregated (Grade A) |
| 2 | **Minimum Horizontal Radius** | 30 m | 120–300 m |
| 3 | **Maximum Grade** | 6–8 % | 3–4 % |
| 4 | **Station Spacing** | 250–400 m | 800–1500 m |

Other distinguishing parameters: design speed, superelevation, cross-section width, propulsion system.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Module 3 Choice Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain with neat sketches the **horizontal alignment** components of a transit line. Define *superelevation* and derive the relationship between design speed, superelevation, and minimum radius of horizontal curve for a rail-based transit system.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A metro rail system is to be designed with the following parameters:
- Design speed, V = **80 km/h**
- Maximum superelevation (cant), e_max = **0.12 m**
- Coefficient of lateral friction, f = **0.15**

Calculate the **minimum radius of horizontal curve**. If the actual radius adopted is **R = 400 m**, compute the **equilibrium superelevation required**. State whether this design is **safe** for a passenger train with a **maximum permissible cant deficiency of 75 mm**.

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Horizontal Alignment Explanation [7 Marks]**

Horizontal alignment of a transit line consists of:
1. **Tangent:** A straight section of the route providing direct, high-speed running.
2. **Circular Curve:** A constant-radius curve connecting two tangents. Defined by:
   - **Deflection angle (Δ)**
   - **Radius (R)**
   - **Tangent length (T)**
   - **Curve length (L_c)**
3. **Transition (Spiral) Curve:** A curve of **progressively changing radius** (clothoid) connecting the tangent to the circular curve. Its purpose is to:
   - Allow gradual introduction of centrifugal force.
   - Enable gradual application of superelevation.
   - Improve passenger comfort (jerk ≤ 0.3 m/s³).

**Superelevation (Cant):** The raising of the outer rail above the inner rail on a curved track to counteract the lateral centrifugal force experienced by the vehicle.

**Derivation:** (as derived in Section 3.1 of these notes)

$$
\boxed{R_{min} = \dfrac{V^2}{127 \cdot (e_{max} + f)}}
$$

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 Marks]**

**Step 1: Minimum Radius**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{min} &= \dfrac{V^2}{127 \cdot (e_{max} + f)} \\
R_{min} &= \dfrac{80^2}{127 \cdot (0.12 + 0.15)} \\
R_{min} &= \dfrac{6400}{127 \cdot 0.27} \\
R_{min} &= \dfrac{6400}{34.29} \\
R_{min} &\approx 186.6 \text{ m} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**∴ Minimum radius ≈ 187 m.**

**Step 2: Equilibrium superelevation for R = 400 m**

$$
\begin{aligned}
e_{eq} &= \dfrac{V^2}{127 \cdot R} - f \\
e_{eq} &= \dfrac{80^2}{127 \cdot 400} - 0.15 \\
e_{eq} &= \dfrac{6400}{50800} - 0.15 \\
e_{eq} &= 0.1260 - 0.15 \\
e_{eq} &= -0.024 \text{ m} \quad (\text{negative means insufficient cant}) \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Check Cant Deficiency**

The actual cant provided at R = 400 m is less than the equilibrium requirement, so we are running in **cant deficiency**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cant deficiency} &= e_{eq,\text{required}} - e_{provided} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Using actual e = 0.12 m:

$$
\begin{aligned}
e_{required,equil} + e_{provided} &= \dfrac{V^2}{127 \cdot R} \\
\dfrac{V^2}{127 \cdot R} &= e + C_D \quad \text{where } C_D \text{ is deficiency} \\
C_D &= \dfrac{V^2}{127 \cdot R} - e \\
C_D &= 0.1260 - 0.12 \\
C_D &= 0.006 \text{ m} = 6 \text{ mm} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Cant deficiency = 6 mm, which is well below the permissible 75 mm.**

**∴ The design is SAFE.**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Module 3 Choice Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **vertical alignment** of a transit line. Differentiate between **crest** and **sag** vertical curves with neat diagrams. State the design criteria for each.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A BRT corridor is designed at a section where an upgrade of **+3%** meets a downgrade of **−4%**. Design speed is **V = 60 km/h**, driver eye height **h₁ = 1.2 m**, and object height **h₂ = 0.15 m**. Calculate the **length of the crest vertical curve** required to provide adequate stopping sight distance. Assume the curve is longer than SSD (use the S < L formula).

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Vertical Alignment Explanation [7 Marks]**

The **vertical alignment** of a transit line describes the elevation profile of the route along its length. It is composed of:

1. **Tangential Gradients:** Straight line segments of constant slope (upgrade, downgrade, or level). Design constraints:
   - **Maximum grade** for buses/BRT: 4–6 %
   - **Maximum grade** for metro: 3–4 %
   - **Minimum grade** for drainage: 0.3–0.5 %

2. **Vertical Curves:** Parabolic curves used to provide smooth transition between successive gradients. Two types:

| Feature | Crest (Summit) Curve | Sag (Valley) Curve |
|---|---|---|
| **Geometry** | Convex upwards | Concave upwards |
| **Forces** | Vehicle pushed outward | Centrifugal force on passengers |
| **Primary Design Criterion** | **Stopping Sight Distance (SSD)** | **Headlight illumination / Comfort** |
| **Secondary Criterion** | Passenger comfort | Drainage |
| **Critical Parameter** | Driver eye height + Object height | Headlight beam height |

**Design Criteria:**
- **Crest curve:** Must ensure that a driver can see an obstruction of height **0.15 m** from eye height **1.2 m** at the design SSD.
- **Sag curve:** Must ensure that the vehicle's headlights illuminate an object at the design SSD, typically **h ≈ 0.6 m**, and that vertical jerk remains within passenger comfort limits.

**(b) Numerical Solution [7 Marks]**

**Given:**
- Grade g₁ = +3 %, g₂ = −4 %
- Design speed V = 60 km/h
- h₁ = 1.2 m, h₂ = 0.15 m

**Step 1: Algebraic difference in grade A**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A &= \vert g_1 - g_2 \vert = \vert +3 - (-4) \vert = 7\% \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compute Stopping Sight Distance (SSD)**

For level grade, with t = 2.5 s and f = 0.35:

$$
\begin{aligned}
SSD &= 0.278 \cdot V \cdot t + \dfrac{V^2}{254 \cdot f} \\
SSD &= 0.278 \cdot 60 \cdot 2.5 + \dfrac{60^2}{254 \cdot 0.35} \\
SSD &= 41.7 + \dfrac{3600}{88.9} \\
SSD &= 41.7 + 40.5 \\
SSD &\approx 82.2 \text{ m} \quad \text{(adopt 85 m)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Length of Crest Vertical Curve (S < L case)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
L &= \dfrac{A \cdot S^2}{200 \cdot (h_1 + h_2)} \\
L &= \dfrac{7 \cdot 85^2}{200 \cdot (1.2 + 0.15)} \\
L &= \dfrac{7 \cdot 7225}{200 \cdot 1.35} \\
L &= \dfrac{50575}{270} \\
L &\approx 187.3 \text{ m} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

**∴ Required crest vertical curve length ≈ 187 m. Adopt L = 190 m for standard rounding.**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Valuation Warning):**
> 1. **Sign of grade:** Students often forget that downgrade is **−ve**, and A = (g₁ − g₂) **algebraically** = 3 − (−4) = **+7%**, not 1%.
> 2. **Two formula branches:** You **must verify whether S < L or S > L** before applying the formula. With A = 7%, the curve is naturally long, so S < L condition holds. Always state your assumption.
> 3. **H₁ + H₂:** Many students write h₁ and h₂ separately and forget to **add** them, losing 2 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3, Geometry of Transit Lines**

**Core Definitions (must memorize):**
- ☐ Horizontal alignment = **tangent + circular curve + transition spiral**
- ☐ Vertical alignment = **grade + vertical curve (crest/sag)**
- ☐ Cross-section = **lane width + clearance + ROW category**
- ☐ Superelevation (cant) = **outer rail raised above inner rail on curves**
- ☐ SSD = **perception–reaction distance + braking distance**
- ☐ Transition curve = **clothoid with linearly changing radius**

**Key Numerical Constants:**
- ☐ Equilibrium formula constant: **127** (use with V in km/h, R in m)
- ☐ SSD formula constants: **0.278** (V×t term) and **254** (V² term)
- ☐ Vertical curve formula constant: **200** (metric, A in %)
- ☐ Spiral length: rate of change of cant **C ≤ 50 mm/s** (rail) or **0.3 m/s³ jerk** (road)

**Mode-wise Range to Remember:**
- ☐ Min curve radius: **30 m (bus) → 300 m (metro)**
- ☐ Max grade: **8% (bus) → 3–4% (metro)**
- ☐ Station spacing: **250 m (bus) → 1500 m (metro)**
- ☐ Design speed: **40 km/h (bus) → 120 km/h (metro)**

**Common Exam Traps:**
- ☐ Forgetting unit conversion between m/s and km/h in R_min formula.
- ☐ Using wrong sign for grades in vertical curve design.
- ☐ Mixing cant (mm) with superelevation ratio (m/m) — **1 m/m = 1000 mm cant**.
- ☐ Forgetting to add h₁ and h₂ in crest curve SSD design.
- ☐ Confusing **cant excess** (outer rail too high) with **cant deficiency** (outer rail too low).

**Real-World Data Points (KTU loves these!):**
- ☐ Kochi Metro: design speed **80 km/h**, max grade **4%**, min radius **120 m** at depot, SG 1435 mm.
- ☐ Delhi Metro: longest vertical curve ~ **600 m**.
- ☐ Ahmedabad Janmarg BRT: station spacing ~ **600 m**, lane width **3.5 m**.
- ☐ Indian Railways BG: gauge **1676 mm**, max cant **185 mm**, max cant deficiency **100 mm**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** KTU 2024 Scheme questions often combine **two sub-parts**: one conceptual (7 marks) and one numerical (7 marks). Practice the **R_min, e_eq, SSD, and vertical curve** calculations until they are automatic. Also, memorize the **mode-wise parameter table** as it is a guaranteed 3-mark question in Part A.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types of Transit Lines and Their Characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET833 - PUBLIC TRANSPORTATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types of Transit Lines and Their Characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **transit line** is a fixed, physically defined pathway (alignment) along which a public transport vehicle operates on a regular schedule, serving a sequence of stops or stations to transport passengers between origins and destinations. The classification of transit lines by their **geometric configuration** and **functional role within a network** forms the foundation of public transit planning under the KTU 2024 OECET833 syllabus.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 3):**
> Transit lines are classified based on their **geometrical alignment** within the urban fabric, the **type of service** they provide, and the **operational technology** (mode) employed. The four classical geometric categories are *Radial, Crosstown, Circumferential, and Branch/Feeder* lines, each possessing distinct capacity, coverage, and ridership characteristics.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine the **human circulatory system**:

- The **aorta and major arteries** radiating out from the heart behave exactly like **Radial Transit Lines** (CBD ↔ Suburbs).
- The **capillaries** that collect blood from tissues and feed it back to the veins are akin to **Feeder Lines** (Neighborhoods ↔ Trunk routes).
- The **veins** running parallel to arteries (and the capillaries bridging arteries to veins) act as **Crosstown Lines** (Suburb ↔ Suburb, bypassing the heart/CBD).
- The **coronary arteries** that loop around the heart muscle resemble **Circumferential / Loop Lines** (suburb-to-suburb without entering the center).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Just as a doctor cannot treat a heart patient by only looking at the heart — a transit planner cannot solve urban mobility by only building radial lines. A **balanced network** requires all four types working in harmony.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following engineering metrics define the **characteristics** of any transit line and are universally used in KTU-validated numerical problems:

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Unit | Importance |
|---|---|---|---|
| Line Length | $L$ | **km** | Determines fleet size & travel time |
| Line Capacity | $C_{line}$ | **pphpd** (passengers per hour per direction) | Maximum throughput |
| Vehicle Capacity | $C_{veh}$ | **passengers/vehicle** | Single-unit holding limit |
| Headway | $h$ | **minutes** or **seconds** | Time gap between consecutive vehicles |
| Frequency | $f$ | **vehicles/hour** | Inverse of headway |
| Operating Speed | $V_{o}$ | **km/h** | Schedule speed (incl. stops) |
| Cruise Speed | $V_{c}$ | **km/h** | Maximum speed between stops |
| Dwell Time | $t_{d}$ | **seconds** | Time stopped at each station |

Where **pphpd = passengers per hour per direction**, the gold-standard metric for transit line performance worldwide.

> [!TIP]
> In KTU board examinations, when a question mentions "characteristics" of a transit line, it expects you to discuss **alignment type, service pattern, capacity, speed, coverage, and typical ridership profile** — not just a textbook definition.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Transit Lines

Transit lines may be classified along **three orthogonal axes**:

1. **Geometric Alignment** (shape on city map)
2. **Functional Role** (purpose in network hierarchy)
3. **Technology/Mode** (vehicle and right-of-way type)

### 2.2 Classification by Geometric Alignment

#### 2.2.1 Radial Transit Lines
- **Definition:** Lines that emanate from a central point (typically the **Central Business District – CBD**) and extend outward toward the suburbs.
- **Why they exist:** Most trip generators (offices, government seats) cluster in the CBD; suburbs generate commuting demand.
- **Characteristics:**
  - High peak-hour, uni-directional demand (inbound AM, outbound PM).
  - Steep passenger volume gradient — high near CBD, low at outer end.
  - Ideal for **Heavy Rail (Metro)** and **BRT** with high capacity.
  - Vulnerable to **peak-load imbalance** (low counter-flow utilization).

#### 2.2.2 Crosstown (Cross-Town) Lines
- **Definition:** Lines that cross the urban area **without passing through the CBD**, connecting suburbs or secondary activity centers on opposite sides.
- **Why they exist:** Modern cities are **polycentric** — suburban-to-suburban trips are now the majority in many metros.
- **Characteristics:**
  - More **balanced bi-directional load**.
  - Often integrated with radial lines at major interchange stations.
  - Lower peak intensity, higher off-peak ridership.
  - Suited for **Light Rail Transit (LRT)** and **Trunk Bus Services**.

#### 2.2.3 Circumferential (Loop / Ring) Lines
- **Definition:** Closed-loop alignments encircling the CBD or an inner zone, providing tangential connections.
- **Why they exist:** Redistribute trips that would otherwise overload radial lines by entering the CBD unnecessarily.
- **Characteristics:**
  - Extremely **balanced load** in both directions.
  - High transfer demand at radial–circumferential interchange nodes.
  - Often built as **Metro Loop Lines** (e.g., Delhi Metro Ring Line, Moscow Circle Line).
  - Excellent for **orbital connectivity** and decongesting the core.

#### 2.2.4 Branch Lines
- **Definition:** Sub-routes that split off from a main (trunk) line at a junction and serve a separate corridor, sharing trackage/roadway on the common section.
- **Why they exist:** Extend service reach without duplicating trunk infrastructure.
- **Characteristics:**
  - Common trunk segment experiences **superimposed frequency** (sum of branch frequencies).
  - Branches individually have lower frequency but combined trunk is high.
  - Requires careful **scheduling and dispatching** to avoid trunk congestion.
  - Common in **commuter rail** networks.

#### 2.2.5 Feeder Lines
- **Definition:** Low-capacity local services that collect passengers from neighborhoods and deliver them to major **trunk line stations** for onward journey.
- **Why they exist:** Most origins are beyond walking distance of a trunk station.
- **Characteristics:**
  - Short route length, low commercial speed, high stop density.
  - Use **minibuses, auto-rickshaws (IPT), or shuttle buses**.
  - Critical for **first/last-mile connectivity** — often the weakest link in network efficiency.

### 2.3 Classification by Functional Role

| Hierarchy Level | Line Type | Typical Capacity (pphpd) | Primary Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| Tier 1 — Trunk | Metro / Heavy Rail | **40,000 – 80,000** | High-volume corridor haulage |
| Tier 2 — Primary | LRT / BRT | **10,000 – 25,000** | Dense urban corridors |
| Tier 3 — Secondary | Standard Bus | **3,000 – 8,000** | Suburban arterials |
| Tier 4 — Feeder | Minibus / IPT | **500 – 2,000** | Neighborhood collection |

### 2.4 Service Pattern Variants (Independent of Alignment)

Even with the same geometric alignment, a line can operate under distinct **service patterns**:

- **Local Service:** Stops at every station. Maximum accessibility, minimum speed.
- **Express Service:** Skips intermediate stations. Higher speed, lower accessibility.
- **Skip-Stop Service:** Alternating stations served by alternating trains (A/B pattern). Balances speed and coverage.
- **Zonal Service:** Multiple zones with different stopping patterns based on trip length.
- **Limited-Stop Service:** Stops only at major stations.

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Master these five equations — they cover **>85% of numerical problems** on transit line capacity and performance in KTU ESE papers.

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f = \dfrac{3600}{h}$ | Frequency from headway (headway in seconds) | veh/h |
| 2 | $f = \dfrac{60}{h}$ | Frequency from headway (headway in minutes) | veh/min |
| 3 | $C_{line} = C_{veh} \times f$ | Theoretical line capacity | pphpd |
| 4 | $C_{line} = \dfrac{3600 \times C_{veh}}{h}$ | Capacity using headway directly | pphpd |
| 5 | $V_{o} = \dfrac{L}{\sum t_{run} + \sum t_{d} + t_{term}}$ | Operating (schedule) speed | km/h |

**Where:**
- $t_{run}$ = running time between consecutive stops
- $t_{d}$ = dwell time at each stop
- $t_{term}$ = terminal layover/recovery time
- $h$ = headway (seconds)

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding transit line typology is essential for:

- **Network Design:** Selecting line geometry that matches the **Origin-Destination (O-D) matrix**.
- **Fleet Sizing:** Estimating required vehicles using $N = f \times T_{round}$, where $T_{round}$ is the round-trip time.
- **Demand Forecasting:** Radial lines need **peak-direction modeling**; circumferential lines need **balanced bi-directional modeling**.
- **Multi-Modal Integration:** Designing transfer facilities at **radial–circumferential** and **trunk–feeder** junctions.
- **Economic Appraisal:** Cost per pphpd varies by line type — **metro radial** is most capital-intensive but lowest cost/passenger-km at high volume.

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Line Capacity of a Transit Line

**Problem Setup:** Derive the maximum theoretical passenger throughput (capacity) of a single transit line in **pphpd**, given vehicle capacity and headway.

**Step 1 — Define the headway $h$:**
The headway is the time interval (in seconds) between the **arrivals of two successive vehicles** at any given reference point (e.g., the busiest station or the maximum-load section).

**Step 2 — Express the number of vehicles per hour:**
If $h$ is measured in seconds, the number of vehicles passing a point in 1 hour is given by dividing 3600 seconds by $h$:

$$f = \dfrac{3600}{h} \quad \text{[vehicles per hour per direction]}$$

**Step 3 — Introduce vehicle passenger capacity $C_{veh}$:**
Each vehicle can carry a maximum of $C_{veh}$ passengers (standing + seated, at crush load). Total passengers transported per hour per direction is therefore the **product of vehicle flow and vehicle capacity**:

$$C_{line} = C_{veh} \times f$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $f$ to obtain the headway-based form:**

$$\boxed{C_{line} = \dfrac{3600 \times C_{veh}}{h}} \quad \text{[pphpd]}$$

**Step 5 — Apply a realistic load factor correction (engineering refinement):**
In practice, vehicles do not operate at 100% crush load on average. Apply a **load factor $\alpha$** (typically 0.75 to 0.85 for comfort, or 1.00 for crush):

$$C_{line}^{practical} = \alpha \times \dfrac{3600 \times C_{veh}}{h}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The line capacity equation reveals that **reducing headway** is far more powerful than **increasing vehicle size**. Halving $h$ doubles capacity, whereas doubling $C_{veh}$ only doubles capacity but requires massive new rolling stock investments.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU ESE Style)

**Question:** A metro line uses 6-car trains, each car carrying 250 passengers at crush load. If the minimum achievable headway is **90 seconds**, calculate:
1. The frequency in trains/hour.
2. The theoretical line capacity in pphpd.
3. The practical capacity at a load factor of 0.80.

**Step 1 — Frequency:**

$$f = \dfrac{3600}{h} = \dfrac{3600}{90} = 40 \text{ trains/hour}$$

**Step 2 — Vehicle (train) capacity:**

$$C_{veh} = 6 \times 250 = 1500 \text{ passengers/train}$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical line capacity:**

$$C_{line} = C_{veh} \times f = 1500 \times 40 = 60{,}000 \text{ pphpd}$$

**Step 4 — Practical line capacity (with load factor $\alpha = 0.80$):**

$$C_{line}^{practical} = 0.80 \times 60{,}000 = 48{,}000 \text{ pphpd}$$

**Final Answer Summary:**

| Quantity | Value |
|---|---|
| Frequency $f$ | **40 trains/hour** |
| Theoretical Capacity | **60,000 pphpd** |
| Practical Capacity (α=0.80) | **48,000 pphpd** |

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Transit Line Types

| Characteristic | Radial | Crosstown | Circumferential | Feeder |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Primary O-D Served** | CBD ↔ Suburb | Suburb ↔ Suburb | Tangential orbital | Local → Trunk hub |
| **Peak Direction** | Strongly directional | Moderately balanced | Highly balanced | Balanced (short trips) |
| **Typical Mode** | Metro / BRT | LRT / Trunk Bus | Metro Loop / LRT | Minibus / IPT |
| **Capacity Range (pphpd)** | 25,000 – 80,000 | 5,000 – 20,000 | 15,000 – 40,000 | 500 – 3,000 |
| **Coverage Role** | High-volume spine | Cross-connector | Distributor | Collector |
| **Major Challenge** | Peak overload | Lower density | Transfer design | First/Last mile gap |
| **Network Hierarchy** | Tier 1 (Trunk) | Tier 2 (Primary) | Tier 1/2 (Loop) | Tier 4 (Feeder) |

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Operating (Schedule) Speed

The **operating speed $V_o$** captures the realistic travel experience including all delays.

**Step 1 — Total cycle time of one round trip:**

$$T_{round} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} t_{run,i} + \sum_{i=1}^{n+1} t_{d,i} + t_{term}$$

**Step 2 — Round-trip distance is twice the line length (for back-and-forth service):**

$$D_{round} = 2L$$

**Step 3 — Operating speed is round-trip distance divided by round-trip time:**

$$\boxed{V_{o} = \dfrac{2L}{\sum t_{run} + \sum t_{d} + t_{term}}}$$

> [!TIP]
> A common KTU board mistake: students compute operating speed using only **one-way** time but **one-way** distance, forgetting the terminal layover. The correct formulation above uses the **full round-trip cycle** as the operational reality.

---

### 3.5 Derivation: Fleet Size for a Radial Line

**Step 1 — Round-trip time $T_{round}$** (as derived above).

**Step 2 — Required number of vehicles to maintain headway $h$:**

$$N = \dfrac{T_{round}}{h} \quad \text{(round up to nearest integer)}$$

**Step 3 — Include a spare/reserve ratio** (typically 10% for maintenance):

$$N_{fleet} = 1.10 \times N$$

**Example:** A radial metro line has $T_{round} = 90$ minutes, headway $h = 3$ minutes.

$$N = \dfrac{90}{3} = 30 \text{ trains (operational)}$$

$$N_{fleet} = 1.10 \times 30 = 33 \text{ trains (total procurement)}$$
