## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Differentiate between Radial and Circumferential transit lines with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — structured presentation expected):**

| Parameter | Radial Line | Circumferential Line |
|---|---|---|
| **Alignment** | Originates at CBD and extends outward | Forms a loop around CBD or inner zone |
| **Trip Purpose** | CBD ↔ Suburb commuting | Suburb ↔ Suburb (tangential) |
| **Load Profile** | Highly peak-directional | Balanced bi-directional |
| **Example** | Delhi Metro Yellow Line (HUDA City Centre ↔ Samaypur Badli) | Delhi Metro Pink Line (Majlis Park ↔ Shiv Vihar loop segments) |

Radial lines carry the **dominant commuter flow** into the city core during peak hours, while circumferential lines distribute trips **around the core** without forcing them through the congested CBD — both are essential for a balanced network.

*(Marks distribution: 1 for definition of each + 1 for comparison/example)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, SAMPLE]**

**Define "Feeder Line" in public transportation. Why is it considered the weakest link in transit network efficiency?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **feeder line** is a low-capacity local transit service (typically minibus, shared auto, or shuttle bus) that **collects passengers from residential neighborhoods and delivers them to major trunk-line stations** for onward journey to the CBD or other destinations.

It is considered the **weakest link** because:

1. **First/Last-Mile Problem:** Trunk transit cannot serve door-to-door; passengers must rely on feeder modes for the first and last leg of the journey.
2. **Quality Inconsistency:** Feeder services are often informally operated, with irregular schedules, poor vehicle condition, and lack of integration.
3. **Multi-Modal Transfer Penalty:** Passengers experience waiting time, walking, and ticketing friction at interchange points, reducing the overall attractiveness of public transit.
4. **Underinvestment:** Feeder infrastructure (depots, terminals, IPT stands) typically receives far less capital than metro/BRT projects.

*(Marks distribution: 1 for definition + 2 for the four points above — at least 3 points needed for full 2 marks)*

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> In Module 3 questions on transit line types, students **commonly lose 3–4 marks** for the following mistakes:
> 1. **Confusing line geometry (radial/crosstown) with line mode (metro/BRT).** These are independent classifications — geometry describes shape, mode describes technology.
> 2. **Forgetting the load factor** when computing practical capacity — only theoretical capacity is rarely acceptable.
> 3. **Using one-way time** instead of **round-trip time** in operating speed and fleet size calculations.
> 4. **Omitting units** (pphpd, km/h, veh/h) — KTU examiners deduct ½ mark per missing unit in numericals.
> 5. **Writing generic definitions** without relating them to **capacity, speed, and demand** characteristics — Module 3 specifically demands the "characteristics" angle.

---

#### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Choice 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]**

**(a)** Classify transit lines based on **geometric alignment** and explain the **characteristics of each type** with respect to passenger demand pattern, capacity, and typical mode. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A BRT corridor uses **18-metre articulated buses** with a **crush load of 165 passengers** per bus. The system operates at a **minimum headway of 2 minutes** during peak hours. Calculate:
   (i) Peak frequency in buses/hour.
   (ii) Theoretical line capacity in pphpd.
   (iii) Practical capacity at a load factor of 0.85.
   (iv) Fleet size required for a **round-trip time of 75 minutes**, including a 10% maintenance reserve. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question A**

**Part (a) — Geometric Classification of Transit Lines (7 marks)**

Transit lines are classified by geometric alignment into **four classical categories**:

**1. Radial Transit Lines (2 marks)**
- **Definition:** Lines that radiate outward from a central node (CBD) toward suburban terminals.
- **Demand Pattern:** Highly **peak-directional** — strong inbound flow in AM peak, outbound in PM peak; weak counter-flow utilization.
- **Capacity Required:** Very high (25,000–80,000 pphpd) due to corridor concentration.
- **Typical Mode:** Heavy Rail / Metro, BRT on dedicated right-of-way.

**2. Crosstown Lines (1.5 marks)**
- **Definition:** Lines crossing the urban area **without entering the CBD**, connecting suburbs or secondary activity centers.
- **Demand Pattern:** **Bi-directionally balanced**; lower peak intensity but stronger off-peak.
- **Capacity Required:** Moderate (5,000–20,000 pphpd).
- **Typical Mode:** LRT, trunk bus services, suburban rail.

**3. Circumferential / Loop Lines (1.5 marks)**
- **Definition:** Closed-loop alignments encircling the CBD, providing tangential connections.
- **Demand Pattern:** **Balanced** bi-directional; significant transfer demand at radial-loop interchange nodes.
- **Capacity Required:** Moderate to high (15,000–40,000 pphpd).
- **Typical Mode:** Metro loop lines, ring road BRT.

**4. Branch and Feeder Lines (2 marks)**
- **Branch Lines:** Sub-routes diverging from a trunk line, sharing the common trunk section. Frequency **superimposes** on the trunk.
- **Feeder Lines:** Short, local services that collect passengers from neighborhoods to trunk stations. Low capacity (500–3,000 pphpd), high stop density, first/last-mile role.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $C_{veh} = 165$ passengers/bus
- $h = 2$ minutes $= 120$ seconds
- $T_{round} = 75$ minutes
- $\alpha = 0.85$ (load factor)
- Reserve ratio $= 10\%$

**(i) Peak Frequency:**

$$f = \dfrac{3600}{h} = \dfrac{3600}{120} = 30 \text{ buses/hour}$$

*(1 mark)*

**(ii) Theoretical Line Capacity:**

$$C_{line}^{theory} = C_{veh} \times f = 165 \times 30 = 4950 \text{ pphpd}$$

*(1.5 marks)*

**(iii) Practical Capacity:**

$$C_{line}^{practical} = \alpha \times C_{line}^{theory} = 0.85 \times 4950 = 4207.5 \approx \mathbf{4208 \text{ pphpd}}$$

*(1.5 marks)*

**(iv) Fleet Size:**

Operational fleet:

$$N = \dfrac{T_{round}}{h} = \dfrac{75 \text{ min}}{2 \text{ min}} = 37.5 \rightarrow 38 \text{ buses (rounded up)}$$

Total procurement with 10% reserve:

$$N_{fleet} = 1.10 \times 38 = 41.8 \rightarrow \mathbf{42 \text{ buses}}$$

*(3 marks: 1 for round-trip formula, 1 for calculation, 1 for reserve application and rounding)*

---

#### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, SAMPLE]**

**(a)** Compare **Radial and Crosstown transit lines** in terms of: alignment, peak-hour demand pattern, capacity range, typical mode, and network hierarchy. Highlight which type is more suitable for a **monocentric city** versus a **polycentric city**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A metro line has the following operational data:
   - One-way line length: $L = 25$ km
   - Number of intermediate stations: 20
   - Average running time between stations: $t_{run} = 90$ seconds
   - Average dwell time at each station: $t_d = 30$ seconds
   - Terminal layover time: $t_{term} = 8$ minutes
   - Vehicle (train) capacity: $C_{veh} = 1800$ passengers
   - Headway: $h = 4$ minutes

Calculate:
   (i) One-way travel time.
   (ii) Round-trip time.
   (iii) Operating speed $V_o$ in km/h.
   (iv) Theoretical line capacity in pphpd. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution — Question B**

**Part (a) — Radial vs. Crosstown Comparison (7 marks)**

| Parameter | Radial Line | Crosstown Line |
|---|---|---|
| **Alignment** | CBD ↔ Suburb (linear outward) | Suburb ↔ Suburb (crosses city, bypasses CBD) |
| **Peak Demand** | Strongly directional (AM inbound / PM outbound) | Moderately balanced bi-directional |
| **Capacity Range** | 25,000 – 80,000 pphpd | 5,000 – 20,000 pphpd |
| **Typical Mode** | Metro, BRT (high capacity) | LRT, trunk bus (medium capacity) |
| **Network Hierarchy** | Tier 1 (Trunk spine) | Tier 2 (Primary connector) |

*(4 marks for the table — 1 mark per row × 4 key rows, partial credit for 3 rows)*

**City Type Suitability (3 marks):**

- **Monocentric City** (e.g., older colonial cities like old Mumbai, Kolkata, London): **Radial lines dominate** because nearly all peak trips are CBD-bound. The high directional demand justifies high-capacity metro/BRT on radial corridors.
- **Polycentric City** (e.g., modern Bengaluru, Gurugram, Washington D.C.): **Crosstown and Circumferential lines are essential** because trip origins and destinations are distributed across multiple activity nodes. A purely radial network would force all trips through congested central corridors, increasing travel time and reducing network efficiency.
- Modern transit planning always combines **radial trunk + crosstown/loop connectors + feeder distribution** for maximum coverage.

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- $L = 25$ km (one-way)
- $n = 20$ intermediate stations → 21 running segments (from terminal to terminal)
- $t_{run} = 90$ s per segment
- $t_d = 30$ s per station; stations to stop at = 20 intermediate + 2 terminals = 22 stops (or simply sum of 20 intermediate + 2 terminal dwell events)
- $t_{term} = 8$ min = 480 s (terminal layover at end of each one-way trip)
- $C_{veh} = 1800$ pax
- $h = 4$ min = 240 s

**Note on dwell counting:** In standard KTU formulation, dwell time is summed over **all stops including terminals on the way**. We'll count 20 intermediate dwell events for one-way running.

**(i) One-way travel time:**

Running time for 21 segments:

$$T_{run} = 21 \times 90 = 1890 \text{ s}$$

Dwell time (20 intermediate stations):

$$T_{dwell} = 20 \times 30 = 600 \text{ s}$$

One-way travel time:

$$T_{one-way} = 1890 + 600 = 2490 \text{ s} = 41.5 \text{ min}$$

*(1.5 marks)*

**(ii) Round-trip time:**

Terminal layover occurs at **each end of the one-way trip**. For a round trip, layover occurs at both terminals:

$$T_{round} = 2 \times T_{one-way} + 2 \times t_{term}$$

$$T_{round} = 2 \times 41.5 + 2 \times 8 = 83 + 16 = 99 \text{ min}$$

*(1.5 marks)*

**(iii) Operating speed:**

Round-trip distance = $2 \times 25 = 50$ km

$$V_o = \dfrac{D_{round}}{T_{round}} = \dfrac{50 \text{ km}}{99/60 \text{ h}} = \dfrac{50 \times 60}{99} = \mathbf{30.30 \text{ km/h}}$$

*(2 marks: 1 for formula, 1 for calculation with unit)*

**(iv) Theoretical line capacity:**

$$f = \dfrac{3600}{h} = \dfrac{3600}{240} = 15 \text{ trains/hour}$$

$$C_{line} = C_{veh} \times f = 1800 \times 15 = \mathbf{27{,}000 \text{ pphpd}}$$

*(2 marks: 1 for frequency, 1 for capacity with unit)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Types of Transit Lines and Their Characteristics**

- [ ] **Four Geometric Types:** Radial, Crosstown, Circumferential, Branch/Feeder — memorize the shape, demand pattern, and mode for each.
- [ ] **Radial lines** are high-capacity, high-directionality, and CBD-anchored — best for monocentric cities.
- [ ] **Crosstown lines** are balanced-load, polycentric-friendly, and connect suburbs without entering CBD.
- [ ] **Circumferential/Loop lines** redistribute orbital trips and relieve radial congestion.
- [ ] **Branch lines** superimpose frequency on a common trunk segment — careful dispatching required.
- [ ] **Feeder lines** are first/last-mile collectors; weak link in network quality.
- [ ] **Service Patterns:** Local, Express, Skip-Stop, Limited-Stop — each trades speed for accessibility.
- [ ] **Master these formulas:**
  - $f = 3600 / h$
  - $C_{line} = C_{veh} \times f$ (theoretical)
  - $C_{line}^{practical} = \alpha \times C_{line}^{theory}$
  - $V_o = D_{round} / T_{round}$
  - $N_{fleet} = 1.10 \times (T_{round} / h)$
- [ ] **Always** express capacity in **pphpd** (passengers per hour per direction) — gold standard metric.
- [ ] **Always** use **round-trip time** (not one-way) for fleet size and operating speed.
- [ ] **Always** apply **load factor** for practical capacity unless explicitly told "crush load / theoretical."
- [ ] **Network Hierarchy:** Trunk (Metro) → Primary (LRT/BRT) → Secondary (Bus) → Feeder (Minibus/IPT).
- [ ] **KTU pitfall:** Don't confuse **alignment type** (radial/crosstown) with **mode type** (metro/BRT/bus) — they are independent classifications.
- [ ] **Real-world link:** Delhi Metro (Radial + Loop), Mumbai Suburban Rail (Radial), Bengaluru Namma Metro (Radial + Crosstown in expansion) — study these for project-based exam questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transfers in Transit Networks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET833 - PUBLIC TRANSPORTATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Transfers in Transit Networks

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## Formal Academic Definition

In public transportation engineering, a **transfer** refers to the act of a passenger disembarking from one transit vehicle (or route) and boarding another transit vehicle (or route) to continue their journey toward a destination that cannot be reached by a single, direct service. A **Transit Network** is a system of interconnected routes and schedules that necessitates transfers for the majority of passengers when the network is route-based rather than line-based.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme and modern transit planning literature, a transfer is not merely a passenger inconvenience — it is a **fundamental design parameter** that quantifies the accessibility, efficiency, and modal quality of a transit system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Transfers are classified as a "User Cost" factor. They directly affect the **generalized cost** function of a transit trip, which includes in-vehicle travel time, waiting time, walking time, and a **transfer penalty (α)** measured in equivalent minutes.

---

## Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are at the **Kochi International Airport (COK)** and want to reach **Thrissur Railway Station**. There is no single bus that drops you at the exact railway station entrance. So, you:

1. Take **Bus Route A** from the airport to the **Vytilla Mobility Hub**.
2. Wait for **5 minutes**.
3. Board **Bus Route B** from Vytilla to Thrissur.

That change of bus at Vytilla is a **transfer**. In real-world transit engineering, a transfer is like a **gear change in a bicycle** — without it, you cannot adapt to the changing terrain, but each gear change (transfer) costs you a small amount of energy (time and convenience).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** Passengers perceive transfers as being more onerous than in-vehicle travel time. Industry-standard research suggests that **1 transfer ≈ 10 to 30 minutes of equivalent in-vehicle time** (depending on service quality, weather, and passenger demographics).

---

## Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following standard metrics are universally applied in transit engineering for evaluating transfers:

- **Transfer Penalty (α):** Typically **5 to 30 minutes** of equivalent in-vehicle travel time per transfer.
- **Maximum Wait Time at Transfer (W_max):** Standardly **15 minutes** for urban bus systems; **30 minutes** for regional rail.
- **Minimum Connection Time (MCT):** **2 to 5 minutes** for bus-to-bus; **5 to 10 minutes** for bus-to-rail.
- **Dwell Time at Pulse Point:** Coordinated to **0 to 3 minutes** for synchronized transfers.
- **Walking Distance (L_walk):** Ideally **≤ 200 meters** for on-street transfers; **≤ 500 meters** for intermodal terminals.

---

## Why Transfers Matter in Network Design

A transit network fundamentally has two structural choices:

| Network Type | Description | Transfer Intensity |
|--------------|-------------|-------------------|
| **Direct (Hub-and-Spoke Line) Network** | Every passenger rides one vehicle from origin to destination | **Zero transfers**, but low network coverage |
| **Transfer (Route/Grid) Network** | Routes intersect at nodes; passengers change vehicles | **1 to 3 transfers** typical, high coverage |

Modern transit agencies (e.g., London Transport, BMRCL Bangalore, KMRL Kochi) deliberately design **transfer-based networks** because they achieve higher geographic coverage with fewer route-kilometers operated.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## Operational Breakdown of a Transfer

A transfer event in a transit network can be decomposed into the following sequential components:

1. **Alighting Time (t_a):** Time taken for the passenger to disembark the first vehicle. Standard value: **0.05 to 0.10 min/passenger**.
2. **Walking Time (t_walk):** Time to physically move from the alighting platform/stop to the boarding platform/stop. Calculated as $t_{walk} = \dfrac{L_{walk}}{v_{walk}}$, where **v_walk = 4 to 5 km/h (≈ 80 m/min)**.
3. **Waiting Time (t_wait):** Time spent at the boarding stop until the next vehicle arrives. For uncoordinated (random) arrivals, $E[t_{wait}] = \dfrac{H}{2}$, where **H = Headway** of the boarding route.
4. **Boarding Time (t_b):** Time to board the second vehicle. Standard value: **0.05 to 0.10 min/passenger**.

The **Total Transfer Time (TTT)** for a single transfer is therefore:

$$
TTT = t_a + t_{walk} + t_{wait} + t_b
$$

---

## The "Why" Behind Each Step

- **Why a penalty?** Passengers experience disutility (mental fatigue, perceived risk of missing the next bus, exposure to weather). Engineers convert this disutility into a measurable time-equivalent.
- **Why waiting time dominates?** Empirical research shows that waiting time is perceived as **2 to 3 times more burdensome** than in-vehicle time. A 10-minute wait feels like 20-30 minutes of riding time.
- **Why synchronize?** If the second vehicle is scheduled to arrive just as the first vehicle departs (i.e., wait time → 0), the transfer is "seamless" and the penalty is minimized.

---

## Types of Transfers in Transit Networks

### 1. Timed Transfer (Synchronous Transfer)
Routes are **scheduled to meet** at a specific time and location. The second vehicle waits (or is held) for the first vehicle's arrival. This is also called a **"Pulse Point"** or **"Transfer Center"** operation.

### 2. Uncoordinated Transfer (Asynchronous Transfer)
Passengers arrive randomly at a transfer point. Wait time follows a **uniform distribution** between **0 and H** (headway), giving an expected wait of **H/2**.

### 3. Guaranteed Transfer
The transit agency promises that a passenger holding a valid transfer will be accommodated on the next vehicle, even if it requires the vehicle to wait briefly.

### 4. Physical vs. Virtual Transfer
- **Physical Transfer:** Passenger walks between two distinct stops.
- **Virtual Transfer:** Two routes serve the **same stop**, requiring only a wait (no walking).

### 5. Intermodal Transfer
Transfer between two different modes (e.g., **Bus → Metro**, **Metro → Suburban Rail**, **Auto-Rickshaw → Bus**).

---

## KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Units | Engineering Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Expected Wait Time (Random Arrival) | $E[W] = \dfrac{H}{2}$ | minutes | Default for uncoordinated transfers |
| Expected Wait Time (Coordinated) | $E[W] = t_{hold}$ | minutes | Timed transfer design |
| Walking Time | $t_{walk} = \dfrac{L_{walk}}{v_{walk}}$ | minutes | Terminal layout planning |
| Total Transfer Time | $TTT = t_a + t_{walk} + t_{wait} + t_b$ | minutes | Generalized cost calculation |
| Generalized Cost (with penalty) | $C = IVTT + \alpha_1 \cdot t_{walk} + \alpha_2 \cdot t_{wait} + \beta \cdot n_{transfer}$ | minutes | Mode choice modeling |
| Transfer Penalty (β) | $\beta = 10$ to $30$ min/transfer | minutes/transfer | KTU standard range |
| Headway-based Capacity | $C_{route} = \dfrac{60}{H} \cdot V$ | passengers/hour | Frequency design |
| Minimum Connection Time | $MCT = t_a^{max} + t_{walk} + t_{sched}^{slack}$ | minutes | Timetable robustness |
| Pulse Point Dwell Time | $t_{pulse} = \max(t_{arrival,i}) - \min(t_{arrival,j})$ | minutes | Synchronization constraint |
| Passenger Perception Weight | $W_{wait} = 2 \cdot W_{ride}$ | dimensionless | KTU Highway Capacity Manual (HCM)-style weighting |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical for KTU Exams:** The **Generalized Cost Function** is the single most-tested equation in transit planning problems. Always state all assumptions (e.g., "assuming uncoordinated, random passenger arrival").

---

## Real-World Engineering Utility

The transfer concept is applied by:

- **Urban Planners:** Designing BRT corridors and metro feeder systems where last-mile connectivity relies on transfers.
- **Scheduling Engineers:** Building periodic timetables with **control points** (Timed Transfer Points or TTPs).
- **Operations Researchers:** Solving the **Vehicle Scheduling Problem (VSP)** and **Crew Scheduling Problem (CSP)** to minimize passenger transfer time.
- **Policy Makers:** Setting fare integration policies (e.g., **single-ticket transfer** vs **pay-per-ride**).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## Derivation 1: Expected Waiting Time at a Transfer Point

### Problem Context
A passenger arrives randomly at a transfer point. The boarding route has a **headway H (minutes)**. Derive the expected waiting time.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Define the random variables.**
Let the passenger arrival time (relative to the last vehicle's departure) be a uniform random variable $T$, where $T \sim U(0, H)$.

**Step 2: Formulate the waiting time.**
The waiting time $W$ for the next vehicle is:

$$
W = H - T
$$

**Step 3: State the probability density function.**
The PDF of $T$ is:

$$
f_T(t) = \dfrac{1}{H} \quad \text{for } 0 \le t \le H
$$

**Step 4: Compute the expected value.**

$$
E[W] = E[H - T] = H - E[T]
$$

**Step 5: Compute $E[T]$ for the uniform distribution.**

$$
E[T] = \int_{0}^{H} t \cdot f_T(t) \, dt = \int_{0}^{H} t \cdot \dfrac{1}{H} \, dt
$$

$$
E[T] = \dfrac{1}{H} \left[ \dfrac{t^2}{2} \right]_{0}^{H} = \dfrac{1}{H} \cdot \dfrac{H^2}{2} = \dfrac{H}{2}
$$

**Step 6: Substitute back to get the final result.**

$$
E[W] = H - \dfrac{H}{2} = \dfrac{H}{2}
$$

> **Final Result:** $E[W] = \dfrac{H}{2}$ — *A passenger arriving randomly at a transfer point waits, on average, half the headway of the boarding route.*

### Real-World Interpretation
If a metro train arrives at a transfer station **every 6 minutes** ($H = 6$), the average random passenger waits **3 minutes**. This is why agencies try to reduce headways (increase frequency) on feeder routes.

---

## Derivation 2: Generalized Cost of a Trip with One Transfer

### Problem Context
A passenger travels from Origin (O) to Destination (D) using two routes with a single transfer. Derive the total generalized cost in equivalent minutes.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Identify the cost components.**
For a 2-leg journey, the components are:

- $IVTT_1$ = In-vehicle travel time on Route 1 (O to Transfer Point)
- $W_1$ = Waiting time for Route 1 (origin)
- $W_2$ = Waiting time for Route 2 (at transfer point)
- $t_{walk}$ = Walking time at the transfer point
- $IVTT_2$ = In-vehicle travel time on Route 2 (Transfer Point to D)
- $\beta$ = Transfer penalty (in equivalent minutes)

**Step 2: Assign weights to each component.**
Perceived weights (w) for each component are:

- In-vehicle time: weight = **1**
- Walk time: weight = **2**
- Wait time: weight = **2**
- Transfer penalty: weight = **1** (already in equivalent minutes)

**Step 3: Construct the generalized cost equation.**

$$
C = (1 \cdot IVTT_1) + (2 \cdot W_1) + (2 \cdot W_2) + (2 \cdot t_{walk}) + (1 \cdot IVTT_2) + \beta
$$

**Step 4: Substitute the random-wait result $W = H/2$.**

$$
C = IVTT_1 + IVTT_2 + 2 \cdot \dfrac{H_1}{2} + 2 \cdot \dfrac{H_2}{2} + 2 \cdot t_{walk} + \beta
$$

**Step 5: Simplify.**

$$
C = IVTT_1 + IVTT_2 + H_1 + H_2 + 2 \cdot t_{walk} + \beta
$$

**Step 6: Insert a numerical example.**
Let $IVTT_1 = 12$ min, $IVTT_2 = 18$ min, $H_1 = 10$ min, $H_2 = 8$ min, $t_{walk} = 2$ min, $\beta = 15$ min.

$$
C = 12 + 18 + 10 + 8 + 2(2) + 15
$$

$$
C = 12 + 18 + 10 + 8 + 4 + 15 = 67 \text{ equivalent minutes}
$$

> **Final Result:** The trip is perceived as **67 minutes** of equivalent travel time, even though the actual clock time may be only **50 minutes**.

---

## Derivation 3: Optimal Headway for a Feeder Route

### Problem Context
A feeder route of length **L** (km) connects a residential area to a metro station. The route operates with a single bus of capacity **V** seats and average speed **v** (km/h). Demand rate is **λ** (passengers/minute). Derive the optimal headway $H^*$.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Express the round-trip time.**

$$
T_{RT} = \dfrac{2L}{v}
$$

**Step 2: Express the cycle time as headway + round-trip time.**
For a single bus running continuously:

$$
H = T_{RT} = \dfrac{2L}{v}
$$

**Step 3: Express the passenger wait time (random arrival).**

$$
E[W] = \dfrac{H}{2} = \dfrac{L}{v}
$$

**Step 4: Express the total system cost per unit time.**
Total cost per minute = (Operating cost) + (User cost) = $\dfrac{c_{bus}}{H} + \lambda \cdot \dfrac{H}{2}$

where $c_{bus}$ = operating cost per vehicle-cycle.

**Step 5: Minimize total cost with respect to H.**
Take derivative and set to zero:

$$
\dfrac{dC}{dH} = -\dfrac{c_{bus}}{H^2} + \dfrac{\lambda}{2} = 0
$$

**Step 6: Solve for $H^*$.**

$$
H^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 c_{bus}}{\lambda}}
$$

> **Final Result:** The optimal headway balances operator cost ($c_{bus}/H$) against passenger wait cost ($\lambda H / 2$).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## Diagram 1: Components of a Transfer Event (Sequential Flow)

The following Mermaid block renders the **chronological structure** of a single transfer event, from alighting to boarding.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Passenger on Vehicle 1] --> B[Alighting Time: 0.1 min]
    B --> C[Walking Time: t_walk]
    C --> D{Transfer Type}
    D -->|Timed| E[Wait Time: t_hold ~ 0 to 3 min]
    D -->|Random| F[Wait Time: H/2]
    E --> G[Boarding Time: 0.1 min]
    F --> G
    G --> H[Passenger on Vehicle 2]
    H --> I[Total Transfer Time: TTT]
```

**Engineering Interpretation of the Diagram:**
- The branch labeled **Timed** corresponds to a **Pulse Point** where vehicles are synchronized.
- The branch labeled **Random** corresponds to an **uncoordinated stop** where the passenger bears the full headway-based wait.
- The **TTT node** aggregates all sub-components for input into the generalized cost function.

---

## Diagram 2: Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Transfer-Based Transit Network

This block diagram represents how routes, schedules, and transfer points interact at a network level.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ORIGIN[Origin Zone]
        O[Passenger Origin]
    end
    subgraph ROUTE1[Route 1 - Feeder]
        R1S[Route 1 Schedule]
        R1V[Route 1 Vehicle]
    end
    subgraph TP[Transfer Point / Pulse Hub]
        TPS[Timed Transfer Scheduler]
        MCT[Minimum Connection Time Logic]
        PEN[Transfer Penalty Module]
    end
    subgraph ROUTE2[Route 2 - Trunk]
        R2S[Route 2 Schedule]
        R2V[Route 2 Vehicle]
    end
    subgraph DEST[Destination Zone]
        D[Passenger Destination]
    end
    O --> R1S
    R1S --> R1V
    R1V --> TPS
    TPS --> MCT
    MCT --> PEN
    PEN --> R2S
    R2S --> R2V
    R2V --> D
```

**Engineering Interpretation:**
- The **Timed Transfer Scheduler (TPS)** block is the algorithmic brain that aligns vehicle arrivals.
- The **Minimum Connection Time (MCT)** block ensures that scheduled connections respect walking and alighting/boarding buffers.
- The **Transfer Penalty (PEN)** block injects the **β (beta)** parameter into the cost model for mode choice.
- This architecture mirrors the modules used in real transit planning software (e.g., **EMME, PTV Visum, Cube Voyager**).

---

## Diagram 3: Transfer Penalty Decision Matrix

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is Transfer Timed?}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Walking Distance}
    Q1 -->|No| P1[High Penalty: 20-30 min]
    Q2 -->|Less than 100 m| P2[Low Penalty: 5-10 min]
    Q2 -->|100 to 300 m| P3[Medium Penalty: 10-15 min]
    Q2 -->|Greater than 300 m| P4[High Penalty: 20-30 min]
    Q1 -->|Yes with Shelter| P5[Lowest Penalty: 5-8 min]
```

**Engineering Interpretation:**
- This flowchart guides planners in selecting the appropriate **β** value for cost-benefit analysis.
- A **timed transfer with shelter and short walking** yields the lowest penalty, encouraging ridership.
- A **long, unsheltered, uncoordinated transfer** yields the highest penalty, suppressing ridership.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define a "Transfer" in the context of public transit networks. Mention the standard range of transfer penalty (β) in equivalent minutes. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
A *transfer* is the act of a passenger alighting from one transit vehicle and boarding another to continue their journey. It occurs because most route-based transit networks cannot offer direct service between all origin-destination pairs. The **transfer penalty (β)** is the additional perceived time cost borne by passengers for each transfer, typically valued at **10 to 30 equivalent minutes** per transfer in KTU 2024 Scheme analyses. (3 marks)

---

### Question 2: What is a "Timed Transfer Point" (TTP)? State one advantage. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
A **Timed Transfer Point (TTP)**, also called a *Pulse Point*, is a designated stop or station where multiple routes are **scheduled to arrive and depart simultaneously** (or within a defined window) to facilitate easy passenger interchange. **Advantage:** It drastically reduces passenger waiting time and the perceived transfer penalty, often bringing the transfer wait to **0 to 3 minutes** instead of H/2. (3 marks)

---

## Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): Transfer Time Calculation & Cost Modeling

**(a)** A passenger makes a journey using two bus routes with the following parameters:
- In-vehicle time on Route 1: **15 minutes**
- Headway of Route 1: **10 minutes**
- Walking time at transfer: **3 minutes**
- Headway of Route 2: **12 minutes**
- In-vehicle time on Route 2: **20 minutes**
- Transfer penalty β = **15 minutes**
- Weighting factors: walk = 2, wait = 2, ride = 1

Calculate the **generalized cost** of the trip in equivalent minutes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss **two engineering strategies** that a transit agency can adopt to reduce the transfer penalty in a bus-based network. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**Step 1: List all components.**

| Component | Value | Weight |
|---|---|---|
| IVTT_1 | 15 min | 1 |
| W_1 (random) | H/2 = 10/2 = 5 min | 2 |
| t_walk | 3 min | 2 |
| W_2 (random) | H/2 = 12/2 = 6 min | 2 |
| IVTT_2 | 20 min | 1 |
| β | 15 min | 1 |

**Step 2: Apply the generalized cost equation.**

$$
C = (1 \cdot IVTT_1) + (2 \cdot W_1) + (2 \cdot t_{walk}) + (2 \cdot W_2) + (1 \cdot IVTT_2) + \beta
$$

**Step 3: Substitute.**

$$
C = (1 \cdot 15) + (2 \cdot 5) + (2 \cdot 3) + (2 \cdot 6) + (1 \cdot 20) + 15
$$

$$
C = 15 + 10 + 6 + 12 + 20 + 15
$$

$$
C = 78 \text{ equivalent minutes}
$$

**Answer:** The generalized cost of the trip is **78 equivalent minutes**. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Strategy 1: Implement Timed Transfer Points (TTPs).**
Schedule multiple routes to arrive at a common hub within a small window (e.g., 0-3 minutes) so that the wait time for the second vehicle approaches zero. This converts an uncoordinated transfer into a coordinated one, reducing $W_2$ from H/2 to ~0. (3.5 marks)

**Strategy 2: Increase Frequency on Feeder Routes.**
Reducing the headway $H$ on the boarding route directly reduces the random wait time $E[W] = H/2$. For example, halving the headway from 12 min to 6 min reduces $W$ from 6 min to 3 min. (3.5 marks)

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Pulse Scheduling and Network Design

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the concept of **Pulse Scheduling** in a transit network. How does it differ from a **Pulse Point** in terms of operational logic? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A circular feeder route of length **12 km** operates at an average speed of **24 km/h** with a single bus. Demand at the transfer point is **0.5 passengers per minute**. If the operating cost of the bus is **₹120 per cycle**, calculate the **optimal headway** and the **minimum total system cost per minute**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to Part (a)

**Definition:** Pulse Scheduling is a **timetabling strategy** in which multiple routes are designed to converge at a central hub (Pulse Point) at the same scheduled time, allow passengers to transfer simultaneously, and then depart. (3 marks)

**Operational Difference:**

| Aspect | Pulse Point | Pulse Scheduling |
|---|---|---|
| **Nature** | Physical location (a stop/hub) | Time-based coordination method |
| **Function** | Place where transfers occur | The schedule logic that aligns arrivals |
| **Tool** | Infrastructure (platforms, signage) | Timetables and software (e.g., Hastus) |

**Sketch description:** A central circle (Pulse Point) with arrows showing all routes arriving from cardinal directions, pausing simultaneously, and then departing. (4 marks)

#### Model Solution to Part (b)

**Step 1: Recall the optimal headway formula.**

$$
H^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 c_{bus}}{\lambda}}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute values.**

$$
H^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 120}{0.5}} = \sqrt{480} \approx 21.91 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Step 3: Compute the total cost at optimal headway.**
At optimum, the operator cost equals the user cost, so:

$$
C_{min} = 2 \cdot \dfrac{c_{bus}}{H^*} = 2 \cdot \dfrac{120}{21.91} \approx 10.95 \text{ ₹/min}
$$

**Step 4: Verification using round-trip time constraint.**
The round-trip time is $T_{RT} = \dfrac{2L}{v} = \dfrac{2 \times 12}{24} = 1$ hour = **60 minutes**.

Since $H^* \approx 21.91$ min < $T_{RT} = 60$ min, a single bus can maintain the optimal headway. (If $H^*$ were greater than $T_{RT}$, multiple buses would be required.)

**Answer:** Optimal headway ≈ **21.91 min**; Minimum system cost ≈ **₹10.95 per minute**. (7 marks)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to multiply weights** (especially on wait and walk time) — students frequently compute $C = IVTT + W + t_{walk} + \beta$ and lose 2-3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing random wait (H/2) with scheduled wait (t_hold)** — these are different. KTU questions often specify which one to use.
> 3. **Not stating assumptions** — examiners award partial credit for explicitly stating "random arrival, uncoordinated transfer, β = 15 min."

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A *transfer* is a passenger's change of vehicle; it is a *design feature*, not a *failure*, of route-based transit networks.
- **Generalized Cost Equation** (the most-tested formula): $C = IVTT + 2 \cdot t_{walk} + 2 \cdot t_{wait} + \beta \cdot n_{transfer}$.
- **Expected Wait Time (Random):** $E[W] = \dfrac{H}{2}$.
- **Expected Wait Time (Timed):** $E[W] = t_{hold} \approx 0$ to $3$ min.
- **Transfer Penalty (β):** Standard range is **10 to 30 equivalent minutes** per transfer.
- **Pulse Point:** A physical transfer hub where timed transfers occur.
- **Pulse Scheduling:** A timetable strategy that synchronizes vehicle arrivals at Pulse Points.
- **Walking Speed:** Standard assumption is **80 m/min (4.8 km/h)**.
- **Minimum Connection Time (MCT):** Typical values are **2-5 min (bus-bus)** and **5-10 min (bus-rail)**.
- **Optimal Headway Formula:** $H^* = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 c_{bus}}{\lambda}}$.
- **Real-World Examples in Kerala:** Kochi Metro feeder buses at **Aluva** and **Edappally** stations; KSRTC buses at **Vyttila Mobility Hub**; suburban rail transfers at **Ernakulam Junction**.
- **Engineering Best Practice:** Always combine **infrastructure (Pulse Points)** with **operational policy (timed schedules)** to minimize β.
- **Common Mistake:** Assuming β = 0 for "free" transfers — this is incorrect because passengers always perceive a *psychological* penalty regardless of fare.
- **KTU Cognitive Levels Tested:** *Remember* (definition of transfer), *Understand* (types of transfers), *Apply* (cost equation problems), *Analyze* (optimal headway derivation).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Analysis of Metro Network Geometric Forms
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET833 - PUBLIC TRANSPORTATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Analysis of Metro Network Geometric Forms

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Metro Network Geometric Form** refers to the **topological configuration** (spatial arrangement) of a rapid transit system's routes, lines, and stations when projected onto a two-dimensional plan view of the urban area it serves. It is a deterministic outcome of city morphology (shape, size, density distribution), demand corridors, and long-range master planning decisions.

In formal terms, a metro network is a **graph** $G = (V, E)$ where:

- $V$ = set of vertices (stations/interchange nodes)
- $E$ = set of edges (track segments between stations)

The **geometric form** characterizes the *spatial pattern* of $E$ and $V$ relative to the city's central business district (CBD), suburban nuclei, and geographic barriers (rivers, coastlines, hills).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The geometric form is *not* the network length or technology — it is purely the **shape and pattern** of route alignment, often studied as part of network planning modules.

---

## 1.2 The Six Canonical Geometric Forms

| # | Form | Alternative Name | Best Suited City Type |
|---|------|------------------|------------------------|
| 1 | **Radial** | Spoke / Star | Monocentric, circular cities (e.g., historic European capitals) |
| 2 | **Grid** | Lattice / Orthogonal | Rectangular planned cities (e.g., Manhattan-like grids) |
| 3 | **Ring / Circumferential** | Loop / Orbital | Polycentric cities needing circumferential links |
| 4 | **Radial–Circumferential** | Hybrid / Wheel-and-Spoke | Large monocentric cities (e.g., Moscow, Delhi, Beijing) |
| 5 | **Linear / Corridor** | Axial | Coastal, riverside, or narrow valley cities |
| 6 | **Branch / Tree** | Dendritic | Low-density suburban extensions |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Circulatory System

> [!TIP]
> **Think of metro networks like the human blood-circulation system:**
> - The **heart** = the CBD (Central Business District).
> - **Arteries** = radial lines carrying heavy peak-hour flow from suburbs to the center.
> - **Veins** = circumferential/ring lines that distribute flow around the periphery *without forcing it all through the heart*.
> - **Capillaries** = grid/feeder networks that provide last-mile access to dense neighborhoods.
> - A healthy "circulatory" metro system balances **radial (in-out) demand** with **tangential (across) demand**, just as a healthy body balances pulmonary and systemic circulation.

A city that is **purely radial** (only arteries) suffers from "central congestion" — every cross-town trip must detour through the heart, exactly like a body whose veins are missing. A **grid** system distributes flow like a capillary bed, but wastes energy on diagonal trips. The **radial–circumferential hybrid** is the engineered optimum, mirroring the closed loop of the circulatory system.

---

## 1.4 Why Geometric Form Matters

> [!NOTE]
> The choice of geometric form directly determines:
> 1. **Average passenger journey time** (number of transfers × wait + travel)
> 2. **Network coverage** of population and employment
> 3. **CBD loading** (overcrowding vs. balanced load)
> 4. **Construction cost** (route-km × tunnel/elevated factor)
> 5. **Future extensibility** and resilience to demand shifts

Thus, **analysis of geometric forms** is the single most consequential *strategic* decision in metro master planning, preceding even technology choice (rail gauge, rolling stock, signaling).

---

## 1.5 Key Performance Vocabulary (Standard KTU Metrics)

- **Directness Ratio (DR):** How close the metro path is to the straight-line distance.
- **Connectivity Index:** How well nodes are linked (graph-theory based).
- **Network Density:** Track-km per unit area / population.
- **Coverage:** % of population within walking distance (typically **500 m** or **800 m**) of a station.
- **Average Number of Transfers (ANT):** Mean transfers per journey.
- **Transfer Penalty:** Time cost incurred per transfer (typically **5–8 minutes**).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Analytical Framework — How Geometric Forms Are Evaluated

The KTU 2024 scheme evaluates geometric forms along **four orthogonal axes**:

1. **Topological efficiency** — measured by graph-theoretic indices.
2. **Travel efficiency** — measured by directness, transfers, and journey time.
3. **Spatial coverage** — measured by station catchment.
4. **Demand responsiveness** — measured by alignment with major O–D corridors.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Why Behind Each Form:**
> - **Radial** form exists because most pre-20th-century cities grew outward from a single core; CBD-bound commuting is the dominant flow.
> - **Grid** form exists because orthogonal streets and rectangular land parcels allow efficient right-of-way acquisition.
> - **Ring** form exists because polycentric cities (multiple employment hubs) generate strong tangential demand that radial lines cannot serve.
> - **Radial–Circumferential** exists because it captures *both* peak radial demand *and* off-peak tangential demand, but at the cost of higher capital outlay.
> - **Linear** form exists because geographic barriers (coast, river, valley) constrain alignment options.
> - **Branch** form exists because low-density suburbs cannot justify frequent service on independent lines, so branch lines share trunk infrastructure.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Examination Gold)

> [!TIP]
> **Memorize the table below — these are the formulas most frequently asked in KTU 2024 ESE questions on this module.**

| # | Formula / Index | Expression | Variables & Units | Typical KTU Use |
|---|------------------|------------|--------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Network Density (Track)** | $\rho = \dfrac{L}{A}$ | $L$ = total route length (km); $A$ = served area (km²); $\rho$ in **km/km²** | Compares cities of different sizes |
| 2 | **Network Density (Population)** | $\sigma = \dfrac{L}{P}$ | $P$ = population (millions); $\sigma$ in **km/million** | Cross-city benchmarking |
| 3 | **Directness Ratio (per O–D pair)** | $DR_{ij} = \dfrac{d_{ij}^{\text{shortest}}}{d_{ij}^{\text{metro}}}$ | $d^{\text{shortest}}$ = straight-line distance (km); $d^{\text{metro}}$ = metro path length (km); **DR ≤ 1** | Lower DR ⇒ circuitous network |
| 4 | **Average Network Directness** | $\overline{DR} = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{i,j} DR_{ij}$ | $N$ = number of O–D pairs | Network-wide efficiency |
| 5 | **Beta Index (Connectivity)** | $\beta = \dfrac{e}{v}$ | $e$ = edges (links); $v$ = vertices (stations) | $\beta > 1$ ⇒ redundant/cyclical network |
| 6 | **Gamma Index (Connectivity)** | $\gamma = \dfrac{e}{3(v-2)}$ | For planar graph max edges = $3(v-2)$ | $0 \le \gamma \le 1$ |
| 7 | **Alpha Index (Circuity)** | $\alpha = \dfrac{\mu}{2v-5}$ | $\mu$ = cyclomatic number; $\mu = e - v + p$; $p$ = connected components | $\alpha \to 1$ ⇒ highly connected |
| 8 | **Cyclomatic Number** | $\mu = e - v + p$ | $p$ = number of connected subgraphs | Counts independent loops |
| 9 | **Average Degree** | $\bar{k} = \dfrac{2e}{v}$ | Sum of node degrees / $v$ | Station-level connectivity |
| 10 | **Coverage Ratio** | $C = \dfrac{P_{c}}{P_{T}}$ | $P_{c}$ = population in catchment; $P_{T}$ = total | Within **500 m** or **800 m** radius |
| 11 | **Catchment Radius Standard** | $r_{c} = 500 \text{ m}$ to $800 \text{ m}$ | Walking threshold (KTU default: **500 m**) | Station spacing benchmark |
| 12 | **Varg's Ideal Route Spacing** | $S_{\text{ideal}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot l \cdot v_{m}}{V_{w}}}$ | $l$ = access distance; $v_{m}$ = metro speed; $v_{w}$ = walking speed | Optimal parallel route spacing |
| 13 | **Average Transfers (ANT)** | $\bar{T} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{k=1}^{N} t_{k}$ | $t_{k}$ = transfers on $k$-th trip | Network legibility |
| 14 | **Route Directness** | $RD = \dfrac{L_{a}}{L_{b}}$ | $L_{a}$ = airline distance; $L_{b}$ = route length | $RD \to 1$ ideal |

> **Standard KTU Constant:** Walking speed $v_{w} = 4 \text{ km/h}$ (or equivalently $1.11 \text{ m/s}$). Use $v_{w} = 5 \text{ km/h}$ only for brisk commuter walks.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas feed directly into:

- **Feasibility Reports** submitted to MoHUA (Ministry of Housing & Urban Affairs, India) for metro DPRs.
- **Cost-Benefit Analysis (CBA)** for multilateral funding (World Bank, ADB, JICA).
- **Multi-criteria decision analysis (MCDA)** for selecting the *optimal* geometric form for a specific city.
- **Climate & sustainability audits** — lower DR ⇒ lower energy per passenger-km.

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 ESE questions, the examiner expects you to (a) state the formula, (b) define every variable, (c) plug in numerical values with correct units, and (d) interpret the result qualitatively (e.g., *"DR = 0.72 indicates the network adds ~28% circuity — within acceptable limits for a radial–circumferential system"*).

---

## 2.4 Comparative Analysis of Geometric Forms

| Criterion | Radial | Grid | Ring | Radial–Circumferential | Linear | Branch |
|-----------|--------|------|------|-------------------------|--------|--------|
| **CBD Accessibility** | Excellent | Moderate | Poor | Excellent | Poor | Moderate |
| **Cross-town (tangential) service** | Poor | Good | Excellent | Good | Poor | Very Poor |
| **Transfer requirement** | High | Low | High | Moderate | Low | High |
| **Construction cost / km²** | High | Moderate | High | **Very High** | Low | Low |
| **Geographic flexibility** | Low | High | Moderate | Low | Low | High |
| **Best city shape** | Circular | Rectangular | Polycentric | Large circular | Linear | Suburban |
| **Real example** | Moscow (early) | Barcelona L9 | Tokyo Yamanote | **Delhi, Beijing, London** | Kochi, Mumbai L1 | Frankfurt S-Bahn |
| **Beta index range** | 0.8–1.1 | 1.1–1.4 | 0.9–1.2 | **1.3–1.7** | 0.7–1.0 | 0.6–0.9 |

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner pitfall:** Students often confuse *route alignment* (curved vs. straight) with *geometric form* (topology). Geometric form is about **which nodes connect to which**, not the curvature of any single line.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Directness Ratio and its Bounds

**Statement:** For any O–D pair $(i,j)$, the Directness Ratio is bounded by $0 < DR_{ij} \le 1$, with equality to 1 only when the metro path equals the Euclidean distance.

**Step 1 — Definition (Base):**

$$
DR_{ij} \;=\; \dfrac{d_{ij}^{\text{straight-line}}}{d_{ij}^{\text{metro}}}
$$

**Step 2 — By the triangle inequality of metric spaces,** the straight-line distance is *never* greater than any path through the network:

$$
d_{ij}^{\text{straight-line}} \;\le\; d_{ij}^{\text{metro}}
$$

**Step 3 — Dividing both sides by $d_{ij}^{\text{metro}}$ (which is always positive for connected networks):**

$$
DR_{ij} \;=\; \dfrac{d_{ij}^{\text{straight-line}}}{d_{ij}^{\text{metro}}} \;\le\; 1
$$

**Step 4 — Trivial lower bound:** Straight-line distance is always positive for distinct stations, so $DR_{ij} > 0$.

**Step 5 — Therefore:**

$$
0 \;<\; DR_{ij} \;\le\; 1
$$

**Engineering interpretation:** $DR = 1$ implies the metro line is the *exact* straight line between two stations — physically impossible for city networks due to buildings, curves, and gradients. Practical $DR$ values for KTU problems:

- $DR = 0.95$ → excellent (rare)
- $DR = 0.75$–$0.85$ → very good (modern radial–circumferential)
- $DR = 0.55$–$0.70$ → acceptable (older radial networks)
- $DR < 0.50$ → poor (overly circuitous, likely misplanned)

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Cyclomatic Number and its Geometric Meaning

**Statement:** The cyclomatic number $\mu$ equals the maximum number of **independent cycles** (loops) in a planar network.

**Step 1 — Euler's formula for a connected planar graph:**

$$
v - e + f \;=\; 2
$$

where $v$ = vertices, $e$ = edges, $f$ = faces (including the outer face).

**Step 2 — For a planar graph, the maximum number of edges is achieved when the graph is *triangulated* (all faces are triangles).** In a triangulated planar graph, every face has 3 edges, and each edge is shared by 2 faces. So:

$$
3f \;=\; 2e \quad\Longrightarrow\quad f \;=\; \dfrac{2e}{3}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute into Euler's formula:**

$$
v - e + \dfrac{2e}{3} \;=\; 2
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $e$:**

$$
v - \dfrac{e}{3} \;=\; 2
\quad\Longrightarrow\quad
e_{\max} \;=\; 3v - 6
$$

**Step 5 — The cyclomatic number is the *excess* of actual edges over the edges of a spanning tree (which is the minimum connected graph, $e_{\min} = v - 1$ for a connected component):**

$$
\mu \;=\; e - e_{\min} \;=\; e - (v - 1) \;=\; e - v + 1
$$

For a graph with $p$ connected components (subgraphs):

$$
\mu \;=\; e - v + p
$$

**Engineering interpretation:**
- $\mu = 0$ → tree (no loops, no alternative routes); typical of a **branch** or **linear** form.
- $\mu > 0$ → redundancy; typical of **grid** and **radial–circumferential** forms.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** A higher $\mu$ means the network can reroute passengers during disruptions — a key resilience metric post-2020 in Indian metros.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Average Network Directness from O–D Matrix

**Setup:** A metro network has $n$ stations generating an $n \times n$ O–D demand matrix $q_{ij}$. Each pair has a metro-path length $d_{ij}^{m}$ and straight-line distance $d_{ij}^{s}$.

**Step 1 — Demand-weighted Directness Ratio:**

$$
\overline{DR}_{w} \;=\; \dfrac{\sum_{i \neq j} q_{ij} \cdot DR_{ij}}{\sum_{i \neq j} q_{ij}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $DR_{ij} = d_{ij}^{s} / d_{ij}^{m}$:**

$$
\overline{DR}_{w} \;=\; \dfrac{\sum_{i \neq j} q_{ij} \cdot \dfrac{d_{ij}^{s}}{d_{ij}^{m}}}{\sum_{i \neq j} q_{ij}}
$$

**Step 3 — Expanded form (useful for KTU numerical problems):**

$$
\overline{DR}_{w} \;=\; \dfrac{\displaystyle\sum_{i \neq j} \dfrac{q_{ij} \cdot d_{ij}^{s}}{d_{ij}^{m}}}{\displaystyle\sum_{i \neq j} q_{ij}}
$$

**Interpretation:** Stations with very high demand (e.g., CBD, airport) carry more weight; thus planners try to ensure **high-demand pairs have $DR \to 1$**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Worked Example — A Pure Radial Network

**Problem (KTU-style):** A proposed radial metro for a circular city has 4 lines meeting at a central interchange. Each line has 6 stations. Calculate $v$, $e$, $\beta$, $\gamma$, and $\alpha$.

**Step 1 — Count vertices ($v$):**
- 4 lines × 6 stations = 24, but the **central interchange** is shared by all 4 lines.
- $v = 24 - 3 = 21$ (subtract 3 duplicate counts of the central station).

**Step 2 — Count edges ($e$):**
- Each line has 5 inter-station segments.
- 4 lines × 5 = 20 edges.
- $e = 20$.

**Step 3 — Beta Index:**

$$
\beta \;=\; \dfrac{e}{v} \;=\; \dfrac{20}{21} \;\approx\; 0.95
$$

**Step 4 — Gamma Index:**

$$
\gamma \;=\; \dfrac{e}{3(v-2)} \;=\; \dfrac{20}{3 \times 19} \;=\; \dfrac{20}{57} \;\approx\; 0.35
$$

**Step 5 — Alpha Index:**

$$
\mu \;=\; e - v + p \;=\; 20 - 21 + 1 \;=\; 0
$$

$$
\alpha \;=\; \dfrac{\mu}{2v - 5} \;=\; \dfrac{0}{37} \;=\; 0
$$

**Interpretation:** $\alpha = 0$ confirms a **pure radial (tree-like) network has no cycles** — any disruption on a single branch isolates those stations. This is the classic weakness of the radial form.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Worked Example — Converting Radial to Radial–Circumferential

**Problem:** Add a single ring line of 12 stations connecting the outer termini of the 4 radial lines. Recalculate the indices.

**Step 1 — New vertices:**
- Original radial: $v_1 = 21$.
- Ring adds 12 new stations: $v = 21 + 12 = 33$.

**Step 2 — New edges:**
- Original radial edges: 20.
- Ring has 12 segments.
- Plus 4 connecting edges from each radial terminus to its nearest ring station: 4 edges.
- $e = 20 + 12 + 4 = 36$.

**Step 3 — Beta Index:**

$$
\beta \;=\; \dfrac{36}{33} \;\approx\; 1.09
$$

**Step 4 — Cyclomatic Number:**

$$
\mu \;=\; e - v + p \;=\; 36 - 33 + 1 \;=\; 4
$$

> **Meaning:** 4 independent cycles are now present. The radial–circumferential form supports **4 alternative routing options** during disruptions.

**Step 5 — Alpha Index:**

$$
\alpha \;=\; \dfrac{\mu}{2v - 5} \;=\; \dfrac{4}{61} \;\approx\; 0.066
$$

**Step 6 — Compare indices:**

| Form | $\beta$ | $\gamma$ | $\mu$ | $\alpha$ |
|------|---------|----------|-------|----------|
| Pure Radial | 0.95 | 0.35 | 0 | 0.000 |
| Radial–Circumferential | 1.09 | 0.39 | 4 | 0.066 |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** Even a single ring line dramatically improves all connectivity indices. This is why **almost every Indian metro DPR (Delhi, Bengaluru, Nagpur, Pune) recommends a radial–circumferential master plan**.

---

## 3.6 Coverage Ratio Calculation — Varg's Spacing Formula

**Problem:** A city has uniform population density $\rho = 15{,}000$ persons/km². Walking speed $v_w = 4$ km/h. Average metro speed $v_m = 32$ km/h. Access distance $l = 0.5$ km. Find the ideal parallel route spacing $S_{\text{ideal}}$.

**Step 1 — Apply Varg's equation:**

$$
S_{\text{ideal}} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot l \cdot v_{m}}{v_{w}}}
$$

**Step 2 — Plug in values:**

$$
S_{\text{ideal}} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 0.5 \times 32}{4}}
\;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{32}{4}}
\;=\; \sqrt{8}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute:**

$$
S_{\text{ideal}} \;\approx\; 2.83 \text{ km}
$$

**Interpretation:** Parallel metro corridors should be spaced approximately **2.83 km** apart to optimize the trade-off between access distance and in-vehicle travel time. This is why Indian metro plans typically have line spacings of **2.5–3.5 km** in dense cores.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Visual Atlas of Metro Network Geometric Forms

> The following Mermaid diagrams provide a top-down topological view of each geometric form. Stations are nodes; lines are edges.

## 4.2 Decision Flowchart — Selecting a Geometric Form

```mermaid
graph TD
    START[START CITY ANALYSIS] --> Q1{CITY SHAPE?}
    Q1 -- CIRCULAR MONOCENTRIC --> R1[CONSIDER RADIAL]

    Q1 -- POLYCENTRIC --> R3[CONSIDER RING]
    Q1 -- COASTAL RIVER LINEAR --> R4[CONSIDER LINEAR]
    Q1 -- LARGE MONOCENTRIC --> R5[CONSIDER RADIAL CIRCUMFERENTIAL]

    R2 --> CHECK
    R3 --> CHECK
    R4 --> CHECK
    R5 --> CHECK
    CHECK -- YES --> ADD1[ADD CIRCUMFERENTIAL RING]
    CHECK -- NO --> FINAL[ADOPT BASE FORM]
    ADD1 --> FINAL
```

```mermaid
    OUT3 --> DEC
    DEC --> FINAL[OPTIMAL GEOMETRIC FORM]
```
