<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]**
*Define the term "geometric form" of a metro network. List the six canonical forms and identify which form is most suitable for a circular monocentric city with a strong CBD.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A metro network's geometric form is the topological arrangement of its lines and stations on a plan view, defined as a graph $G(V,E)$ where $V$ = stations and $E$ = track segments (1 mark). The six forms are: radial, grid, ring/circumferential, radial–circumferential, linear/corridor, and branch/tree (1 mark). A circular monocentric city is best served by a **radial** or **radial–circumferential** form, with the latter preferred if tangential demand exceeds 20% of total trips (1 mark).

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A]**
*State the formula for the Cyclomatic Number of a metro network. What does a value of $\mu = 0$ indicate about the network's topology?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The cyclomatic number is given by $\mu = e - v + p$ (1.5 marks), where $e$ = edges, $v$ = vertices, $p$ = connected subgraphs. A value of $\mu = 0$ indicates the network is a **tree** (or a forest) with no cycles (1 mark) — meaning there is only one path between any two stations, offering no redundancy against disruptions (0.5 marks).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 ESE, Part B questions on this module carry 14 marks and offer internal choice. The two alternatives below are designed to be of equal difficulty and to test different cognitive levels (per Revised Bloom's Taxonomy).

---

### Question B — Option A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3, Q7a/b]**

A metropolitan planning authority proposes a metro for a city with the following network data:
- Number of stations: $v = 25$
- Number of inter-station links: $e = 30$
- The network is fully connected: $p = 1$

**(a)** Calculate the Beta Index, Gamma Index, Cyclomatic Number, and Alpha Index for this network. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Interpret the results. If a new ring line is proposed that adds 8 stations, 8 ring segments, and 4 connector links to existing radial lines, recalculate the Alpha Index and comment on the change in network resilience. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Beta Index:**

$$
\beta \;=\; \dfrac{e}{v} \;=\; \dfrac{30}{25} \;=\; 1.20
$$

Since $\beta > 1$, the network is **redundant** (has cycles) (1 mark).

**Step 2 — Gamma Index:**

$$
\gamma \;=\; \dfrac{e}{3(v-2)} \;=\; \dfrac{30}{3 \times 23} \;=\; \dfrac{30}{69} \;\approx\; 0.435
$$

This means the network uses about **43.5%** of the maximum possible edges for a planar graph of this size (1 mark).

**Step 3 — Cyclomatic Number:**

$$
\mu \;=\; e - v + p \;=\; 30 - 25 + 1 \;=\; 6
$$

There are **6 independent cycles** in the network (1 mark).

**Step 4 — Alpha Index:**

$$
\alpha \;=\; \dfrac{\mu}{2v - 5} \;=\; \dfrac{6}{2(25) - 5} \;=\; \dfrac{6}{45} \;\approx\; 0.133
$$

This is a **moderately connected** planar network (1 mark).

**Tabulation (1 mark):**

| Index | Value | Interpretation |
|-------|-------|----------------|
| $\beta$ | 1.20 | Redundant |
| $\gamma$ | 0.435 | Moderate |
| $\mu$ | 6 | 6 cycles |
| $\alpha$ | 0.133 | Moderately connected |

(1 mark for the tabulation)

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Updated vertex count:**
$v_{\text{new}} = 25 + 8 = 33$ (1 mark)

**Step 2 — Updated edge count:**
$e_{\text{new}} = 30 + 8 + 4 = 42$ (1 mark)

**Step 3 — Updated cyclomatic number:**

$$
\mu_{\text{new}} \;=\; e_{\text{new}} - v_{\text{new}} + p \;=\; 42 - 33 + 1 \;=\; 10
$$

(1 mark)

**Step 4 — Updated Alpha Index:**

$$
\alpha_{\text{new}} \;=\; \dfrac{\mu_{\text{new}}}{2v_{\text{new}} - 5} \;=\; \dfrac{10}{2(33) - 5} \;=\; \dfrac{10}{61} \;\approx\; 0.164
$$

(1 mark)

**Step 5 — Change in Alpha:**

$$
\Delta\alpha \;=\; 0.164 - 0.133 \;=\; 0.031
$$

(0.5 marks)

**Step 6 — Interpretation:**
- The new Alpha Index (0.164) is **23% higher** than the original (1 mark).
- The cyclomatic number increased from 6 to 10, providing **4 additional independent alternative routes** during disruptions (0.5 marks).
- The network resilience improves substantially, justifying the ring-line capital investment (0.5 marks).
- The ring line also enables **tangential trips** that previously required detouring through the CBD (0.5 marks).

---

### Question B — Option B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 3, Q8a/b Alternative]**

A proposed corridor metro for a coastal city has 10 stations arranged linearly. The straight-line distance from Station 1 to Station 10 is **9.2 km**, while the actual metro alignment length is **10.4 km**. The catchment radius is set at **500 m** and the uniform population density is **18,000 persons/km²**.

**(a)** Compute the Directness Ratio for the corridor. If demand weighting yields an O–D matrix with 8 significant pairs, and the weighted sum of straight-line distances is 41.6 km while the weighted sum of metro distances is 52.0 km, calculate the **demand-weighted average Directness Ratio**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Estimate the population covered by the linear metro within a 500 m buffer (assume a 1-km-wide strip on each side, total length 10.4 km). Also compute the **Varg's ideal route spacing** for this corridor assuming $v_m = 30$ km/h and $v_w = 4$ km/h. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Directness Ratio for end-to-end pair:**

$$
DR_{1,10} \;=\; \dfrac{d^{\text{straight}}}{d^{\text{metro}}} \;=\; \dfrac{9.2}{10.4} \;\approx\; 0.885
$$

(2 marks)

**Step 2 — Demand-weighted Directness Ratio formula:**

$$
\overline{DR}_{w} \;=\; \dfrac{\sum q_{ij} \cdot d_{ij}^{s}}{\sum q_{ij} \cdot d_{ij}^{m}}
$$

Given:
- $\sum q_{ij} \cdot d_{ij}^{s} = 41.6$ km
- $\sum q_{ij} \cdot d_{ij}^{m} = 52.0$ km
- These are the *demand-weighted* sums.

(1 mark for stating the formula)

**Step 3 — Plug in values:**

$$
\overline{DR}_{w} \;=\; \dfrac{41.6}{52.0} \;=\; 0.800
$$

(2 marks)

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

A weighted DR of 0.80 indicates that, on average, passengers travel **20% extra distance** compared to the straight line (1 mark). For a linear corridor, this is acceptable, as alignment constraints (curves, gradients) and station locations force deviation from the straight line (1 mark).

#### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Catchment area calculation:**

- Strip length $L = 10.4$ km
- Strip width on both sides: $2 \times 0.5 = 1.0$ km
- Catchment area $A_c = 10.4 \times 1.0 = 10.4 \text{ km}^2$

(2 marks)

**Step 2 — Population covered:**

$$
P_{c} \;=\; A_c \times \rho \;=\; 10.4 \times 18{,}000 \;=\; 187{,}200 \text{ persons}
$$

(2 marks)

**Step 3 — Varg's ideal route spacing:**

$$
S_{\text{ideal}} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \cdot l \cdot v_{m}}{v_{w}}} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 0.5 \times 30}{4}} \;=\; \sqrt{7.5} \;\approx\; 2.74 \text{ km}
$$

(2 marks)

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

The corridor's natural spacing along its own length is captured by station-to-station distance, but the **lateral** parallel route spacing of **2.74 km** suggests that if a parallel metro line were added in the future, it should be placed approximately 2.74 km away to optimally balance access and in-vehicle time (1 mark).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE MARKS in this module (verified by KTU 2023–2024 valuation reports):**
> 1. **Unit mistakes in Varg's formula** — Forgetting to use $v_w$ in km/h consistently; some students substitute m/s without conversion. **Always use km/h.**
> 2. **Confusing $p$ (connected components) with $f$ (faces)** in Euler's formula. $p = 1$ for a connected metro network.
> 3. **Forgetting the absolute value / positive bound** for $DR$ — stating $DR \le 0$ or omitting the bound.
> 4. **Mixing $e_{\max} = 3v - 6$ (planar) with $e_{\max} = v(v-1)/2$ (complete graph)** in the Gamma Index denominator. KTU uses the **planar** version: $3(v-2)$.
> 5. **Not stating the assumption of uniform population density** when computing coverage — examiners deduct 0.5–1 mark for this omission.
> 6. **In alpha-index derivation, writing $2v - 5$ without explaining it is the maximum $\mu$ for a connected planar graph.** Always justify.
> 7. **Answering the "best suited city shape" without citing at least one real-world example** (e.g., "Delhi Metro uses radial–circumferential"). Examiners reward applied knowledge.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print and pin to your study wall!**

- [ ] Geometric form = **topology**, not curvature or technology.
- [ ] Six forms: **Radial, Grid, Ring, Radial–Circumferential, Linear, Branch**.
- [ ] **Radial–Circumferential** is the engineering optimum for large monocentric cities.
- [ ] **Directness Ratio** $DR = d^{\text{straight}} / d^{\text{metro}}$, bounded in $(0, 1]$.
- [ ] **Beta Index** $\beta = e / v$; $> 1$ implies cycles.
- [ ] **Gamma Index** $\gamma = e / 3(v-2)$; uses **planar** maximum.
- [ ] **Alpha Index** $\alpha = \mu / (2v-5)$; measures circuit redundancy.
- [ ] **Cyclomatic Number** $\mu = e - v + p$; counts independent loops.
- [ ] **Coverage radius** standard: **500 m** (KTU default) to 800 m.
- [ ] **Varg's spacing** $S = \sqrt{2 l v_m / v_w}$; use km/h units.
- [ ] **Walking speed** constant: $v_w = 4 \text{ km/h}$.
- [ ] **Demand-weighted** $\overline{DR}_w$ weighs high-O–D pairs more heavily.
- [ ] **Tree networks** have $\mu = 0$, $\alpha = 0$ — zero redundancy.
- [ ] **Real-world anchor examples:** Delhi = Radial–Circumferential; Moscow = Radial–Circumferential; Tokyo Yamanote = Ring; Barcelona L9 = Grid; Kochi = Linear; Frankfurt S-Bahn = Branch.
- [ ] In **Part A**, expect definition + listing question (3 marks).
- [ ] In **Part B**, expect numerical computation + interpretation (14 marks with internal choice).
- [ ] **Always state units** — even in derivations.
- [ ] **Always justify** denominator choices (e.g., $3(v-2)$ for planar graphs).
- [ ] **Always interpret** the numerical result qualitatively in your conclusion.
- [ ] Geographic form is a **planning decision** — it is *fixed* for decades and *very expensive* to retrofit, so choose carefully.

> **Final KTU Mantra:** *"Form follows demand, demand follows form. Get the geometry right, and the technology will follow."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transit System Statistics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET833 - PUBLIC TRANSPORTATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Transit System Statistics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Transit System Statistics** refers to the systematic quantitative measurement, compilation, analysis, and reporting of operational, performance, and demand-related data of a public transportation system. It is the statistical backbone of transit planning, enabling engineers and planners to evaluate service efficiency, financial viability, and modal share. According to KTU 2024 scheme terminology, transit statistics comprise three primary categories:

1. **Supply-side statistics** – describing what is provided (VMT, Vehicle Hours, Fleet Size, Revenue Miles).
2. **Demand-side statistics** – describing what is consumed (Ridership, PMT, Boardings, Load Factor).
3. **Performance/efficiency statistics** – ratios linking supply to demand (Cost per PMT, Passengers per VMT, Service Frequency).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Transit statistics is not just numerical reporting — it is the **decision-making tool** for route rationalization, fare revision, fleet procurement, and NUTP (National Urban Transport Policy) compliance reporting.

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **water pipeline system** delivering water to a city. To manage it efficiently, the engineer needs to know:

| Pipeline Reality | Transit Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Litres pumped per day | Vehicle Miles Traveled (VMT) |
| Litres reaching households | Passenger Miles Traveled (PMT) |
| Pipe leakage percentage | Deadhead Mile percentage |
| Pressure at the tap | Service Frequency / Headway |
| Revenue from water bills | Farebox Revenue |

Just as the pipeline engineer tabulates daily inflow vs. delivery to detect losses, a transit engineer tabulates **supply vs. utilization** to detect inefficiency. **Transit System Statistics is that "plumber's logbook" for a city's buses and trains.**

### 1.3 Core Physical Constants / Standard Metrics

The following metrics are standardized by **American Public Transportation Association (APTA)** and adopted in Indian transit benchmarking:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Standard Metrics in Transit Statistics:**
> - **Service Hour** = **1 vehicle operating for 1 hour**
> - **Revenue Mile** = **1 vehicle mile during which passengers can board**
> - **Deadhead Mile** = **1 vehicle mile operated without passengers (non-revenue)**
> - **PMT** = **Passenger Mile Travelled** (the product of passenger count and distance)
> - **VMT** = **Vehicle Mile Travelled** (the product of vehicle count and distance)

### 1.4 Why Transit Statistics Matter in B.Tech Practice

For a KTU B.Tech Civil Engineering graduate working with **Kerala State Road Transport Corporation (KSRTC)**, **Kochi Metro Rail Limited (KMRL)**, or **urban local bodies**, transit statistics underpins:

- **Route performance evaluation** (which route to retain/cancel)
- **Fare box recovery ratio analysis** (financial sustainability)
- **Fleet sizing and procurement** (bus and metro coach induction)
- **Service level benchmarking** under **MoHUA Service Level Benchmarks (SLBs)**
- **Environmental impact assessment** (PMT-based CO₂ reduction calculation)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Logical Flow of Transit Statistical Analysis

The complete statistical workflow for a transit system follows this sequence:

**Step 1 → Data Collection Stage**
- Automatic Passenger Counters (APCs)
- Automatic Vehicle Location (AVL) systems
- Smart card tap-in/tap-out logs (e.g., KMRL Kochi Metro)
- Manual boarding-alighting surveys
- Origin-Destination (O-D) surveys

**Step 2 → Data Aggregation Stage**
- Raw data is aggregated into hourly, daily, and route-wise summaries
- Peak hour identification (typically 8–11 AM and 5–8 PM in Indian cities)
- Direction-wise (up/down) separation

**Step 3 → Statistical Computation Stage**
- Apply the formula sheet below to compute key performance indicators (KPIs)
- Compute averages, standard deviations, and percentile distributions
- Generate time-series and load profile curves

**Step 4 → Benchmarking and Decision Stage**
- Compare with **MoHUA SLB targets** (e.g., 50% population within 500 m of PT stop)
- Identify underperforming routes (load factor < 30%)
- Trigger service modifications

### 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Metric

| Metric | Why it is computed | Engineering insight it provides |
|---|---|---|
| **VMT (Vehicle Miles Travelled)** | Measures total vehicle supply | Indicates fuel/energy consumption and wear |
| **PMT (Passenger Miles Travelled)** | Measures total passenger mobility delivered | Quantifies the "social benefit" of the system |
| **Vehicle Hours** | Measures service time supplied | Drives labour cost (driver wages) |
| **Load Factor** | Ratio of actual to capacity usage | Detects over-crowding or under-utilization |
| **Service Frequency** | Vehicles per hour on a route | Determines wait time and modal attractiveness |
| **Headway** | Time gap between consecutive vehicles | Directly affects passenger perception of service |
| **Farebox Recovery Ratio (FRR)** | Revenue ÷ Operating cost | Measures financial self-sustainability |
| **Cost per PMT** | Total cost ÷ PMT | Measures unit efficiency of mobility delivery |
| **Reliability Index** | On-time trips ÷ Scheduled trips | Measures schedule adherence quality |
| **Passenger Density** | PMT ÷ VMT | Average occupancy per vehicle-mile |

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Table – All Key Transit Statistics Formulas**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Unit | Description |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Passenger Miles Travelled (PMT) | $\text{PMT} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \cdot d_i$ | passenger-km | Sum of (passengers × distance) over all trips |
| 2 | Vehicle Miles Travelled (VMT) | $\text{VMT} = \sum V \cdot L$ | vehicle-km | Sum of (vehicles × route length) |
| 3 | Average Headway | $h = \dfrac{60}{f}$ | minutes | Where $f$ = service frequency (vehicles/hr) |
| 4 | Cycle Time | $C = t_{\text{run}} + t_{\text{layover}}$ | minutes | Round-trip time including layover |
| 5 | Fleet Size | $N = \dfrac{C}{h}$ | vehicles | Minimum buses/coaches required |
| 6 | Service Frequency | $f = \dfrac{N}{C} \cdot 60$ | veh/hr | Vehicles dispatched per hour |
| 7 | Load Factor (LF) | $\text{LF} = \dfrac{\text{Passengers carried}}{\text{Seating + Standee capacity}} \times 100$ | % | Capacity utilization |
| 8 | Peak Hour Factor (PHF) | $\text{PHF} = \dfrac{V_{\text{peak hour}}}{4 \cdot V_{15\text{-min peak}}}$ | dimensionless | Demand peaking indicator |
| 9 | Passenger Density | $\rho = \dfrac{\text{PMT}}{\text{VMT}}$ | pax/veh-km | Average load per vehicle-km |
| 10 | Cost per PMT | $C_{\text{PMT}} = \dfrac{C_{\text{total}}}{\text{PMT}}$ | ₹/pax-km | Unit cost of mobility |
| 11 | Cost per VMT | $C_{\text{VMT}} = \dfrac{C_{\text{total}}}{\text{VMT}}$ | ₹/veh-km | Operating cost intensity |
| 12 | Farebox Recovery Ratio (FRR) | $\text{FRR} = \dfrac{R_{\text{fare}}}{C_{\text{operating}}} \times 100$ | % | Financial self-sufficiency |
| 13 | Average Fare | $\bar{F} = \dfrac{R_{\text{total}}}{P_{\text{total}}}$ | ₹/pax | Mean revenue per passenger |
| 14 | Revenue Speed | $v_{\text{rev}} = \dfrac{D_{\text{rev}}}{T_{\text{rev}}}$ | km/hr | Average speed carrying passengers |
| 15 | Vehicle Utilization | $\eta_v = \dfrac{\text{VMT per day}}{\text{Fleet size}}$ | km/veh-day | Daily mileage per vehicle |
| 16 | Deadhead % | $\text{DH}\% = \dfrac{\text{Deadhead miles}}{\text{Total miles}} \times 100$ | % | Wasted service proportion |
| 17 | Service Reliability | $R = \dfrac{N_{\text{on-time}}}{N_{\text{scheduled}}} \times 100$ | % | Schedule adherence |
| 18 | Mean Wait Time | $\bar{W} = \dfrac{h}{2}$ | minutes | Average passenger wait (random arrival) |
| 19 | Modal Split | $S_m = \dfrac{T_m}{\sum T_i} \times 100$ | % | Share of mode $m$ in total trips |
| 20 | Productivity Index | $\text{PI} = \dfrac{\text{PMT per day}}{\text{Number of employees}}$ | pax-km/emp | Labour productivity |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note on Units:** In Indian practice, distances are measured in **kilometres** (not miles), but the formula symbols **VMT** and **PMT** remain the same as per international transit literature. The KTU board expects students to use the **"PKT"** (Passenger Kilometres Travelled) and **"VKT"** (Vehicle Kilometres Travelled) terminology in Indian numerical problems, but accept the international notation as equivalent.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Urban Transport Planning:** Transit statistics form the **empirical base for the Four-Step Travel Demand Model** (trip generation, distribution, mode split, assignment).
- **Project Appraisal (MoHUA, KSIIDC, KMRTC):** Detailed Project Reports (DPRs) for metro and BRTS systems require PMT, VKT, FRR projections for 30 years.
- **Operational Tendering (PPP models):** Gross Cost Contracts use **vehicle-km cost**, while Net Cost Contracts use **farebox revenue** — both are derived from transit statistics.
- **Environmental Auditing:** Carbon footprint = (VKT × emission factor per km) — directly sourced from VMT statistics.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivations of Key Statistical Relationships

#### Derivation 1: Relationship between Cycle Time, Headway, and Fleet Size

**Statement:** The minimum fleet size required to operate a route at a given headway is the ratio of cycle time to headway.

**Step 1 – Definition of Cycle Time:**
A vehicle completing a round trip takes a "running time" $t_{\text{run}}$ (time to traverse the route end-to-end one way, summed for both directions) plus a "layover time" $t_{\text{layover}}$ (recovery time at terminals). Therefore:

$$
C = t_{\text{run}} + t_{\text{layover}}
$$

**Step 2 – Definition of Headway:**
The headway $h$ is the time interval between two consecutive vehicles arriving at a stop. If $f$ vehicles per hour leave the terminal, then:

$$
h = \frac{60}{f} \quad \text{(minutes)}
$$

**Step 3 – Steady-State Fleet Requirement:**
In steady-state operation, a vehicle that departs the origin terminal returns to the same terminal exactly after one cycle time $C$. Therefore, in any single instant, the number of vehicles "in the system" is the number that have departed in the last $C$ minutes, which is:

$$
N = \frac{C}{h}
$$

**Step 4 – Substitution of $f$ into $N$:**

$$
N = \frac{C \cdot f}{60} = \frac{f \cdot C}{60}
$$

This confirms that **fleet size is linearly proportional to service frequency** and **cycle time**.

---

#### Derivation 2: Derivation of Mean Wait Time for Random Passenger Arrivals

**Assumption:** Passengers arrive randomly at a stop (Poisson arrival process) and the service is **regular** (constant headway $h$).

**Step 1 – Probability Density Function:**
The arrival time of a passenger relative to the previous vehicle is uniformly distributed in $[0, h]$:

$$
f(t) = \frac{1}{h}, \quad 0 \leq t \leq h
$$

**Step 2 – Expected Wait Time:**

$$
\bar{W} = \int_{0}^{h} t \cdot f(t) \, dt
$$

**Step 3 – Integration:**

$$
\bar{W} = \int_{0}^{h} t \cdot \frac{1}{h} \, dt = \frac{1}{h} \cdot \left[\frac{t^2}{2}\right]_{0}^{h}
$$

**Step 4 – Result:**

$$
\bar{W} = \frac{1}{h} \cdot \frac{h^2}{2} = \frac{h}{2}
$$

Hence, the **mean wait time = half the headway**. This is a classical result in transit planning and is the theoretical basis for the service quality indicator in MoHUA Service Level Benchmarks.

---

#### Derivation 3: Derivation of Load Factor Relationship

**Step 1 – Define Vehicle Capacity:**
For a transit vehicle with $S$ seats and standing allowance for $St$ standees:

$$
\text{Capacity} = S + St
$$

**Step 2 – Define Passengers Carried:**
At any instant, the actual number of passengers on the vehicle is $P_{\text{act}}$.

**Step 3 – Load Factor Definition:**

$$
\text{LF} = \frac{P_{\text{act}}}{S + St} \times 100 \quad (\%)
$$

**Step 4 – Time-Averaged Load Factor:**
If a vehicle makes $n$ stops, with passenger count $P_i$ after stop $i$, the time-averaged load factor over the trip is:

$$
\overline{\text{LF}} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \cdot d_i}{\left(\sum d_i\right) \cdot (S + St)} \times 100
$$

where $d_i$ is the distance from stop $i$ to stop $i+1$. This is the load factor used in **schedule audit** by KSRTC and metro operators.

---

#### Derivation 4: Farebox Recovery Ratio in Terms of PMT and VMT

**Step 1 – Revenue Expression:**
Average fare $\bar{F}$ per passenger. Total passengers = $P_{\text{total}}$. Total revenue:

$$
R_{\text{fare}} = \bar{F} \cdot P_{\text{total}}
$$

**Step 2 – Cost Expression:**
Operating cost per vehicle-km = $c_{\text{op}}$. Total cost:

$$
C_{\text{operating}} = c_{\text{op}} \cdot \text{VMT}
$$

**Step 3 – FRR Substitution:**

$$
\text{FRR} = \frac{\bar{F} \cdot P_{\text{total}}}{c_{\text{op}} \cdot \text{VMT}} \times 100
$$

**Step 4 – Introduce Passenger Density:**
Let $\rho = \dfrac{\text{PMT}}{\text{VMT}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{total}} \cdot \bar{d}}{\text{VMT}}$, where $\bar{d}$ = average trip length. Then:

$$
\frac{P_{\text{total}}}{\text{VMT}} = \frac{\rho}{\bar{d}}
$$

**Step 5 – Final Form:**

$$
\text{FRR} = \frac{\bar{F} \cdot \rho}{c_{\text{op}} \cdot \bar{d}} \times 100
$$

This shows that FRR improves when **passenger density** rises or **average fare** rises, and falls when **unit operating cost** rises — the fundamental tension in transit financial management.

---

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

> **Problem:** A KSRTC bus depot operates 40 buses on 5 routes. The following data is collected for one day:
>
> - Total passengers carried = **12,000**
> - Average trip length = **18 km**
> - Total distance operated (revenue) = **4,800 km**
> - Total cycle time (average) = **90 min**
> - Deadhead distance = **600 km**
> - Total fare collected = **₹96,000**
> - Total operating cost = **₹1,60,000**
> - Seating capacity per bus = **50**, Standee capacity = **20**
> - Average actual occupancy per trip = **42 passengers**
> - Scheduled trips = **120**, On-time trips = **96**
>
> **Compute:** (a) PMT, VMT, Load Factor, Service Frequency, Deadhead %, FRR, Mean Wait Time (b) If the bus operator wants to maintain a maximum headway of 10 min, how many additional buses are required?

#### Solution (a) – All Key Statistical Indicators

**Step 1 – Compute PMT:**

$$
\text{PMT} = P_{\text{total}} \times \bar{d} = 12{,}000 \times 18 = 216{,}000 \text{ passenger-km}
$$

**Step 2 – Compute VMT (Revenue):**
Given as 4,800 km. **VMT = 4,800 veh-km**.

**Step 3 – Compute Total Distance Operated (with Deadhead):**

$$
\text{Total km} = 4{,}800 + 600 = 5{,}400 \text{ veh-km}
$$

**Step 4 – Load Factor:**

$$
\text{LF} = \frac{42}{50 + 20} \times 100 = \frac{42}{70} \times 100 = 60\%
$$

**Step 5 – Service Frequency:**
We need to interpret the cycle time. With 40 buses distributed over the operational span. The average headway is:

$$
f = \frac{N}{C} \cdot 60 = \frac{40}{90} \cdot 60 = 26.67 \text{ vehicles/hr (fleet-wide average)}
$$

For a single route, divide by number of routes:

$$
f_{\text{route}} = \frac{26.67}{5} \approx 5.33 \text{ veh/hr/route}
$$

**Step 6 – Deadhead Percentage:**

$$
\text{DH}\% = \frac{600}{5{,}400} \times 100 = 11.11\%
$$

**Step 7 – Farebox Recovery Ratio:**

$$
\text{FRR} = \frac{96{,}000}{1{,}60{,}000} \times 100 = 60\%
$$

**Step 8 – Mean Wait Time:**
For an individual route with $f_{\text{route}} = 5.33$ veh/hr:

$$
h_{\text{route}} = \frac{60}{5.33} = 11.25 \text{ min}
$$

$$
\bar{W} = \frac{h}{2} = 5.625 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 9 – Service Reliability:**

$$
R = \frac{96}{120} \times 100 = 80\%
$$

**Step 10 – Cost per PKT:**

$$
C_{\text{PKT}} = \frac{1{,}60{,}000}{216{,}000} = 0.741 \text{ ₹/pax-km}
$$

#### Solution (b) – Additional Buses for 10 min Headway

**Step 1 – Required Frequency on a Single Route:**

$$
f_{\text{req}} = \frac{60}{10} = 6 \text{ veh/hr per route}
$$

**Step 2 – Required Fleet per Route:**

$$
N_{\text{req}} = \frac{f_{\text{req}} \cdot C}{60} = \frac{6 \times 90}{60} = 9 \text{ buses/route}
$$

**Step 3 – Total Required Fleet:**

$$
N_{\text{total, req}} = 9 \times 5 = 45 \text{ buses}
$$

**Step 4 – Additional Buses Needed:**

$$
\Delta N = 45 - 40 = \mathbf{5 \text{ additional buses}}
$$

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram: Transit Statistics Data Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Data Sources] --> B[Data Aggregation Engine]
    A1[APCs - Automatic Passenger Counters] --> A
    A2[AVL - Automatic Vehicle Location] --> A

    A4[Manual Surveys and O-D Studies] --> A
    A5[GPS and Ticket Vending Machines] --> A

    B --> C[Statistical Computation Layer]
    C --> D1[Supply Metrics - VKT, VHT, Fleet Size]
    C --> D2[Demand Metrics - PMT, Ridership, Trips]
    C --> D3[Performance Ratios - LF, FRR, Cost per PKT]
    C --> D4[Reliability Metrics - OTP, Mean Wait Time]

    D1 --> E[Decision Support Dashboard]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E

    E --> F1[Route Rationalization]

```

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    Sup --> Sup2[VHT - Vehicle Hours Travelled]

    Dem --> Dem1[PMT - Passenger Km Travelled]

```

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]**
*Define the term "Passenger Kilometres Travelled" (PKT). How is it different from "Vehicle Kilometres Travelled" (VKT)? Why is the ratio PKT/VKT considered an important transit efficiency indicator?*

**Model Answer:**
PKT (or PMT) is the cumulative distance travelled by all passengers, computed as:

$$
\text{PKT} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} P_i \cdot d_i
$$

VKT is the cumulative distance operated by all vehicles, computed as:

$$
\text{VKT} = \sum_{j=1}^{m} V_j \cdot L_j
$$

**Difference:** PKT is a **demand-side metric** (passenger mobility delivered), VKT is a **supply-side metric** (vehicle service provided).

**Importance of PKT/VKT ratio:** The ratio (also called **passenger density** or **average load**) indicates how efficiently the supplied vehicle-km are being utilized by passengers. A higher ratio implies better asset utilization, lower per-passenger cost, and a more financially sustainable service. It is a key input to compute the **Cost per PKT** and the **Farebox Recovery Ratio**.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]**
*What is "Farebox Recovery Ratio" (FRR)? What does an FRR of 130% indicate, and what does an FRR of 45% indicate? Why are most urban transit systems in India unable to achieve 100% FRR?*

**Model Answer:**
Farebox Recovery Ratio is the percentage of operating cost recovered from fare revenue alone:

$$
\text{FRR} = \frac{R_{\text{fare}}}{C_{\text{operating}}} \times 100
$$

- **FRR = 130%:** The system is generating **30% surplus** over operating cost from fares alone — extremely rare; usually seen in highly dense metro corridors like **Hong Kong MTR (City Section)** or peak-hour premium intercity trains.
- **FRR = 45%:** The system recovers less than half of its operating cost from fares — typical of Indian city buses; the gap is met by **government subsidy** or **cross-subsidization** (advertising, real estate).

**Why Indian systems fall below 100%:**
1. Low fare levels (politically regulated)
2. High diesel/electricity cost
3. Low passenger density due to private vehicle competition
4. Social service obligation (concessions to students, senior citizens)
5. Inefficient route planning leading to low load factors

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**(a)** Define the following terms with formulae: **VKT, PKT, Load Factor, Headway, and Farebox Recovery Ratio.** Explain the role of these statistics in transit planning. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A city bus route is 18 km long (one-way). Buses take 45 minutes one-way and 15 minutes layover. The average occupancy is 60 passengers, and the bus capacity is 80. Headway is 12 minutes. Compute: (i) Cycle time, (ii) Fleet size, (iii) PKT per bus per round trip, (iv) Load factor, (v) Daily PKT for 14 round trips per bus per day. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution:

**(a) Definitions:**

- **VKT (Vehicle Kilometres Travelled):** Total kilometres operated by all vehicles. $VKT = \sum V \cdot L$.
- **PKT (Passenger Kilometres Travelled):** $\text{PKT} = \sum P_i \cdot d_i$, representing total passenger mobility delivered.
- **Load Factor (LF):** $\text{LF} = \dfrac{\text{Passengers carried}}{\text{Capacity}} \times 100$. Measures vehicle utilization.
- **Headway (h):** Time gap between two consecutive vehicles, $h = 60/f$ where $f$ is frequency.
- **FRR:** $\text{FRR} = \dfrac{R_{\text{fare}}}{C_{\text{operating}}} \times 100$, the financial self-sufficiency ratio.

**Role in transit planning:** These metrics form the empirical base for **route design, fleet procurement, fare policy, and subsidy planning**. They enable benchmarking against MoHUA Service Level Benchmarks and support informed decision-making in B.Tech practice.

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Cycle Time:**

$$
C = t_{\text{run}} + t_{\text{layover}} = 45 + 15 = 60 \text{ min}
$$

**(ii) Fleet Size:**

$$
N = \frac{C}{h} = \frac{60}{12} = 5 \text{ buses}
$$

**(iii) PKT per Bus per Round Trip:**
One-way distance = 18 km. Round trip = 36 km. Average occupancy = 60.

$$
\text{PKT/bus/trip} = 60 \times 36 = 2{,}160 \text{ pax-km}
$$

**(iv) Load Factor:**

$$
\text{LF} = \frac{60}{80} \times 100 = 75\%
$$

**(v) Daily PKT for 14 Round Trips per Bus:**

$$
\text{PKT}_{\text{daily}} = 14 \times 2{,}160 = 30{,}240 \text{ pax-km per bus}
$$

For the entire fleet (5 buses):

$$
\text{PKT}_{\text{total}} = 5 \times 30{,}240 = 1{,}51{,}200 \text{ pax-km/day}
$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]

**(a)** What is "Mean Wait Time"? Derive the expression for mean wait time for passengers arriving randomly at a transit stop with constant headway. State two real-world situations where this assumption fails. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following data is collected for an urban bus system in one month:
- Total passengers carried = **9,00,000**
- Total revenue km operated = **6,00,000 km**
- Total operating cost = **₹90,00,000**
- Total fare revenue = **₹54,00,000**
- Total deadhead km = **40,000 km**
- Scheduled trips = **3,000**, On-time trips = **2,400**
- Total vehicles = **120**
- Total employee strength = **600**

Compute: (i) Passenger density, (ii) Average fare per passenger, (iii) FRR, (iv) Deadhead %, (v) Cost per PKT (assume average trip length = 12 km), (vi) Service reliability, (vii) Productivity index. **(7 marks)**

##### Model Solution:

**(a) Derivation of Mean Wait Time:**

**Definition:** Mean wait time $\bar{W}$ is the average duration a passenger spends waiting at a stop for the next vehicle.

**Assumption:** Passengers arrive randomly (Poisson process) and the vehicle headway is constant at $h$ minutes. Then arrival time of a passenger relative to the previous vehicle is uniformly distributed in $[0, h]$, with probability density $f(t) = \dfrac{1}{h}$.

$$
\bar{W} = \int_{0}^{h} t \cdot f(t) \, dt = \int_{0}^{h} \frac{t}{h} \, dt = \frac{1}{h} \cdot \left[ \frac{t^2}{2} \right]_{0}^{h} = \frac{h}{2}
$$

**Two situations where assumption fails:**
1. **Peak-hour bunching:** When headway collapses to near-zero (buses arrive in clusters), actual mean wait time is much higher than $h/2$.
2. **Schedule-based services (e.g., Indian Railways):** Passengers consult printed/digital timetables; wait time becomes the time gap between scheduled arrival and the next train — the random uniform assumption does not hold.

**(b) Numerical Computations:**

**(i) Passenger Density:**

First compute VMT = 6,00,000 + 40,000 = 6,40,000 veh-km.
PKT = 9,00,000 × 12 = 1,08,00,000 pax-km.

$$
\rho = \frac{\text{PKT}}{\text{VKT}} = \frac{1{,}08{,}00{,}000}{6{,}40{,}000} = 16.875 \text{ pax/veh-km}
$$

**(ii) Average Fare per Passenger:**

$$
\bar{F} = \frac{54{,}00{,}000}{9{,}00{,}000} = ₹6 \text{ per passenger}
$$

**(iii) Farebox Recovery Ratio:**

$$
\text{FRR} = \frac{54{,}00{,}000}{90{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 60\%
$$

**(iv) Deadhead %:**

$$
\text{DH}\% = \frac{40{,}000}{6{,}40{,}000} \times 100 = 6.25\%
$$

**(v) Cost per PKT:**

$$
C_{\text{PKT}} = \frac{90{,}00{,}000}{1{,}08{,}00{,}000} = ₹0.833 \text{ per pax-km}
$$

**(vi) Service Reliability:**

$$
R = \frac{2400}{3000} \times 100 = 80\%
$$

**(vii) Productivity Index:**

$$
\text{PI} = \frac{1{,}08{,}00{,}000}{600} = 18{,}000 \text{ pax-km per employee}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit confusion trap:** Mixing up **VKT** and **PKT** is the single most common error. Always ask: "Am I multiplying by passengers or by vehicles?" — this distinction decides whether your answer is in pax-km or veh-km.
> 2. **Deadhead inclusion:** Many students compute deadhead % using the wrong denominator. The correct denominator is **total km = revenue km + deadhead km**, not revenue km alone.
> 3. **Mean wait time formula trap:** The formula $\bar{W} = h/2$ applies **only for regular services with random arrival**. Do not apply it to scheduled services like Indian Railways or metro services where passengers align with the timetable.
> 4. **FRR interpretation:** Examiners want students to **interpret** the FRR value, not just calculate it. Always state what the percentage implies about the financial health of the system.
> 5. **Cycle time includes layover:** Forgetting the layover time leads to gross under-estimation of fleet size — a very common mistake worth 3-4 marks.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Transit System Statistics**

- ☐ **PKT (PMT)** = Sum of (passengers × distance) — the **core demand metric**.
- ☐ **VKT (VMT)** = Sum of (vehicles × distance) — the **core supply metric**.
- ☐ **Load Factor (LF)** measures vehicle utilization; ideal range is **60–80%** for buses.
- ☐ **Headway $h = 60/f$** where $f$ is vehicles per hour; mean wait = $h/2$ for random arrival.
- ☐ **Cycle Time $C$** = running time + layover time; **Fleet Size $N = C/h$**.
- ☐ **FRR** = Fare Revenue ÷ Operating Cost; Indian city buses typically have **40–70%** FRR.
- ☐ **Cost per PKT** = Total Cost ÷ PKT; the lower, the more efficient.
- ☐ **Deadhead %** should be kept **below 10%** for efficient operations.
- ☐ **Reliability = (On-time ÷ Scheduled) × 100**; MoHUA benchmark is **≥ 90%**.
- ☐ **Passenger Density = PKT ÷ VKT**; a direct indicator of asset utilization.
- ☐ Data sources: **APCs, AVL, Smart Cards, O-D Surveys**.
- ☐ MoHUA Service Level Benchmarks: Comfortable LF ≤ 100%, FRR should improve with efficiency, share of public transport ≥ 40% for sustainable cities.
- ☐ For random Poisson arrival, mean wait time is exactly **half the headway**.
- ☐ **Cycle time** always includes terminal layover — a 5-10 minute layover is standard.
- ☐ Transit statistics feeds back into **route rationalization, fare revision, and fleet procurement** — closing the planning loop.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Route Choice and Assignment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OECET833 - PUBLIC TRANSPORTATION SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Route Choice and Assignment # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Route Choice — Formal Academic Definition > [!NOTE] > **Route Choice** is the behavioral process by which a traveler, given a set of feasible alternative paths connecting a particular Origin–Destination (O–D) pair in a transportation network, selects one specific route to traverse based on perceived attributes such as travel time, travel cost, distance, comfort, and reliability. In the context of **Public Transportation Systems**, route choice behavior is governed by the comparative attractiveness of: - **In-vehicle travel time (IVTT)** — time spent inside the transit vehicle. - **Out-of-vehicle travel time (OVTT)** — walking to/from stops, waiting, and transfer times. - **Fare / generalized cost** — monetary cost converted to equivalent time units. - **Service frequency / headway** — inverse of waiting time expectation. - **Crowding penalties** — discomfort due to occupancy levels. Mathematically, the choice probability for a path $k$ between an O–D pair $r\text{-}s$ is given by a discrete choice function $P(k \mid r,s)$, where the set of alternatives $K_{rs}$ represents all reasonable paths. ## 1.2 Route Assignment — Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Traffic/Route Assignment** is the fourth (and final) classical step of the sequential Urban Transportation Planning System (UTPS) model. It is the procedure by which the O–D trip matrix (derived from Trip Generation, Distribution, and Mode Choice) is loaded onto the physical transportation network, producing link flows, volumes, and resultant levels of service. The classical assignment problem seeks to determine link volumes $x_a$ for every link $a \in A$ of the network $G(N,A)$ such that: - All O–D demand is satisfied. - The chosen path for each traveler minimizes (or maximizes) a perceived utility. - Flow conservation holds on every node. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The Morning Commuter's Dilemma** > > Imagine you drive daily from **Kalamassery** (Origin) to **Rajagiri School** (Destination) in Kochi. You have three route options: > 1. **NH-66 Bypass** — Fastest, but toll-road with no scenic view. > 2. **Marine Drive** — Slow due to signals, but visually pleasant. > 3. **Local roads via Thoppumpady** — Shortest distance, but congested during school hours. > > **Route Choice** is *your brain's daily decision-making* of which road to pick based on time, cost, comfort, and habit. > > **Route Assignment** is what the **Kerala PWD traffic engineer** does when he observes 5,000 such commuters making the same choice and mathematically loads them onto a digital map to predict congestion, plan flyovers, or design a new metro corridor. This is the foundational distinction: **Route Choice = micro-level traveler decision**; **Route Assignment = macro-level network loading operation**. ## 1.4 Standard Metrics and Units | Metric | Symbol | Unit | |---|---|---| | Link flow | $x_a$ | vehicles/hour (or PCU/hr) | | Link travel time | $t_a$ | minutes | | O–D demand | $q_{rs}$ | trips/hour | | Path flow | $f_k^{rs}$ | trips/hour | | Path travel time | $C_k^{rs}$ | minutes | | Number of links | $A$ | dimensionless | | Number of nodes | $N$ | dimensionless | | Network | $G(N,A)$ | graph | The BPR (Bureau of Public Roads) function, used universally to model link performance, is: $$ t_a = t_a^0 \left[ 1 + \alpha \left( \frac{x_a}{C_a} \right)^{\beta} \right] $$ where $t_a^0$ is the **free-flow travel time**, $C_a$ is the **practical capacity** of the link, and the standard parameters are $\alpha = 0.15$, $\beta = 4.0$. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Classification of Route Choice Models Route choice models are broadly classified based on two fundamental dimensions: 1. **Behavioral assumption** (how decisions are made). 2. **Treatment of network congestion** (whether travel times are fixed or dependent on flow). ### 2.1.1 Deterministic vs. Stochastic Models | Criterion | Deterministic | Stochastic | |---|---|---| | Travel time perception | Same for all travelers | Varies (perception error) | | Perceived cost | Equal to actual cost | Actual + random error $\varepsilon$ | | Choice mechanism | Shortest path only | Probability-weighted across paths | | Output | Single path per O–D pair | Probability distribution | | Examples | All-or-Nothing, User Equilibrium | Logit, Probit, Dial's STOCH | ### 2.1.2 User Equilibrium vs. System Optimal > [!IMPORTANT] > **Wardrop's First Principle (User Equilibrium — UE):** > *The journey times on all the routes actually used are equal and less than those which would be experienced by any single vehicle on any unused route.* > [!IMPORTANT] > **Wardrop's Second Principle (System Optimal — SO):** > *The average journey time is minimum; i.e., the total travel time over the whole network is minimized.* In **Public Transportation**, the transit assignment analogue is: - **Schedule-based assignment** — exact timetables (used in urban rail metro networks like Kochi Metro). - **Frequency-based assignment** — expected waiting time = $1/(2f)$ where $f$ is service frequency. ## 2.2 Detailed Derivation Logic ### 2.2.1 All-or-Nothing (AON) Assignment **Operational Logic:** 1. Compute the **shortest path** between every O–D pair using a minimum-cost algorithm (Dijkstra's, Bellman–Ford). 2. Load the **entire O–D demand** of that pair onto that single shortest path. 3. Repeat for all O–D pairs. 4. Sum the contributions on each link → final link volume $x_a$. **Mathematical Formulation:** $$ x_a = \sum_{rs} \sum_{k \in K_{rs}} q_{rs} \cdot \delta_{a,k}^{rs} $$ where $\delta_{a,k}^{rs} = 1$ if link $a$ lies on path $k$ of O–D pair $r\text{-}s$, and $0$ otherwise. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** AON ignores congestion feedback and produces unrealistic loading (it dumps everything on the shortest path even if it becomes oversaturated). It is rarely used in practice for road assignment but is a **building block** of the Frank–Wolfe algorithm. ### 2.2.2 Incremental Assignment **Operational Logic:** 1. Divide total O–D matrix into $n$ equal fractions: $q_{rs}/n$. 2. For each fraction, recompute shortest paths using **current** link travel times. 3. Assign the fraction onto the new shortest path. 4. Update $t_a(x_a)$ and proceed to the next fraction. This produces a **gradual loading** that approximates equilibrium. ### 2.2.3 Iterative / Capacity Restraint Assignment A continuous variant of incremental assignment. At iteration $n$: $$ t_a^{n} = t_a^0 \left[ 1 + \alpha \left( \frac{x_a^{n}}{C_a} \right)^{\beta} \right] $$ The flow is iteratively adjusted until $\vert x_a^{n+1} - x_a^n \vert < \varepsilon$. ### 2.2.4 User Equilibrium (Frank–Wolfe / Beckmann Formulation) **Beckmann's Mathematical Programming Formulation:** $$ \min \; Z(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{a \in A} \int_0^{x_a} t_a(\omega) \, d\omega $$ $$ \text{subject to:} $$ $$ \sum_{k \in K_{rs}} f_k^{rs} = q_{rs} \quad \forall r,s $$ $$ f_k^{rs} \geq 0 \quad \forall k, r, s $$ $$ x_a = \sum_{rs} \sum_{k} f_k^{rs} \cdot \delta_{a,k}^{rs} $$ The first-order (KKT) conditions of this convex program reproduce **Wardrop's User Equilibrium** exactly. ### 2.2.5 Stochastic User Equilibrium (SUE) — Logit Model If travelers perceive random utility, the choice probability of path $k$ is given by the **Multinomial Logit (MNL)**: $$ P(k \mid r,s) = \frac{\exp(-\theta C_k^{rs})}{\sum_{j \in K_{rs}} \exp(-\theta C_j^{rs})} $$ where $\theta > 0$ is the **scale parameter** (higher $\theta \Rightarrow$ less randomness, sharper choice). For the **transit-specific** logit (used in route choice for buses/metro): $$ P(k) = \frac{e^{V_k}}{\sum_j e^{V_j}} $$ where $V_k = \beta_1 T_{IVTT,k} + \beta_2 T_{OVTT,k} + \beta_3 F_k + \beta_4 W_k$, with $T$ = times, $F$ = fare, $W$ = walk access. ### 2.2.6 Dial's STOCH Algorithm (1971) A "reasonable" path in Dial's algorithm is one in which the **first link moves the traveler closer** to the destination, and the **last link moves away** from the origin — i.e., links with $\text{likelihood} = \exp(\theta \cdot L_a)$ where $L_a$ is the link's "closeness measure." ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula / Concept | Expression | Boundary / Domain | Engineering Use | |---|---|---|---| | BPR Link Performance Function | $t_a = t_a^0 \left[ 1 + \alpha (x_a/C_a)^{\beta} \right]$ | $\alpha = 0.15,\ \beta = 4.0$ | Volume-delay modeling | | Beckmann Objective | $\min \sum_a \int_0^{x_a} t_a(\omega) d\omega$ | $x_a \geq 0$ | UE assignment program | | Wardrop UE Condition | $C_k^{rs} \geq u_{rs}$ if $f_k^{rs} > 0$ | $u_{rs}$ = minimum O–D time | Equilibrium check | | Multinomial Logit | $P(k) = e^{V_k}/\sum_j e^{V_j}$ | $\sum_k P(k) = 1$ | Stochastic route choice | | Expected Wait Time (Random Arrival) | $E[W] = 1/(2f)$ | $f$ = vehicles/hour | Frequency-based transit | | Generalized Cost | $GC = T_{IVTT} + \phi_w T_w + \phi_t T_t + \alpha F$ | $\phi_w, \phi_t$ weights | Multimodal path cost | | Path Travel Time | $C_k^{rs} = \sum_a t_a \cdot \delta_{a,k}^{rs}$ | Sum over $a$ | Path cost computation | | Link Flow Conservation | $x_a = \sum_{rs} \sum_k f_k^{rs} \delta_{a,k}^{rs}$ | $\delta \in \{0,1\}$ | Demand loading | | Flow Conservation at Nodes | $\sum_{a \in IN(n)} x_a = \sum_{a \in OUT(n)} x_a$ | All $n \in N$ | Network equilibrium | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Utility:** These formulas underpin commercial transportation planning software like **CUBE Voyager, EMME, TransCAD, PTV Visum**, and the Indian **UTTIPEC/CUTS** models used for DPRs of metro projects in Delhi, Kochi, Bengaluru. ## 2.4 Route Choice in Public Transport — Special Considerations Public transport assignment differs from private vehicle assignment because: - **Common lines problem** — a passenger at a stop with multiple routes doesn't know which bus arrives first. - **Boarding/alighting capacity** — vehicle capacity is binding. - **Schedule coordination** — transfers must align. - **Strategic vs. adaptive choice** — strategic choice (planned at home) vs. en-route choice (real-time at stop). For **Kochi Metro Phase II** or **Mumbai Metro Line 7**, schedule-based assignment using 4-step models with sub-modal split between feeder bus, walk-access, and e-rickshaw access is standard practice. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Beckmann's Objective from Wardrop's Principle ### Step 1 — Statement of User Equilibrium Consider an O–D pair $r\text{-}s$ with demand $q_{rs}$ and a set of paths $K_{rs}$. Wardrop's first principle states: $$ \text{If } f_k^{rs} > 0, \text{ then } C_k^{rs} = \min_{j \in K_{rs}} C_j^{rs} $$ This can be written as a **variational inequality**: $$ \sum_{rs} \sum_{k \in K_{rs}} C_k^{rs,*}\left( f_k^{rs} - f_k^{rs,*} \right) \geq 0 \quad \forall \text{ feasible } \mathbf{f} $$ where $(\cdot)^*$ denotes the equilibrium solution. ### Step 2 — Construct Beckmann's Function Define the objective: $$ Z(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{a \in A} \int_0^{x_a} t_a(\omega) \, d\omega $$ ### Step 3 — Compute the Gradient The partial derivative with respect to link flow $x_a$ is: $$ \frac{\partial Z}{\partial x_a} = t_a(x_a) $$ ### Step 4 — Form the Lagrangian $$ L(\mathbf{f}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}) = \sum_a \int_0^{x_a(\mathbf{f})} t_a(\omega) d\omega - \sum_{rs} \lambda_{rs} \left( \sum_k f_k^{rs} - q_{rs} \right) $$ ### Step 5 — Stationarity (KKT Conditions) $$ \frac{\partial L}{\partial f_k^{rs}} = \sum_a t_a \cdot \delta_{a,k}^{rs} - \lambda_{rs} = 0 \quad \text{if } f_k^{rs} > 0 $$ Recognize $\sum_a t_a \delta_{a,k}^{rs} = C_k^{rs}$, so: $$ C_k^{rs} = \lambda_{rs} \quad \text{whenever } f_k^{rs} > 0 $$ This is exactly Wardrop's condition: all used paths have equal travel time $\lambda_{rs}$, and unused paths have $C_k^{rs} \geq \lambda_{rs}$. ### Step 6 — Convexity Verification Since $\frac{\partial t_a}{\partial x_a} = \frac{\alpha \beta t_a^0}{C_a^{\beta}} x_a^{\beta - 1} > 0$ for $x_a > 0$ (BPR with $\beta \geq 1$), the integral is strictly convex, guaranteeing a **unique global minimum**, which is the UE solution. ## 3.2 Worked Example — Logit Route Choice for a Transit Corridor ### Problem Statement Between **Aluva** and **MG Road (Kochi Metro)**, three route alternatives exist with the following generalized cost (in minutes, lower = better): | Route | In-Vehicle Time | Walk Time | Wait Time | Fare (₹) | Transfers | |---|---|---|---|---|---| | R1 (Direct Metro) | 25 | 8 | 3 | 35 | 0 | | R2 (Metro + Feeder Bus) | 22 | 5 | 8 | 45 | 1 | | R3 (Ordinary Bus) | 55 | 4 | 12 | 18 | 0 | Weights: $\beta_1 = -0.05$ per min (IVTT), $\beta_2 = -0.10$ per min (OVTT), $\beta_3 = -0.02$ per ₹ (Fare), $\beta_4 = -0.50$ per transfer. ### Step 1 — Compute Utility of Each Route Using the linear additive utility $V_k = \beta_1 T_{IVTT} + \beta_2 T_{OVTT} + \beta_3 F + \beta_4 N_{tr}$: For R1: $V_1 = (-0.05)(25) + (-0.10)(8+3) + (-0.02)(35) + (-0.50)(0) = -1.25 - 1.10 - 0.70 + 0 = -3.05$ For R2: $V_2 = (-0.05)(22) + (-0.10)(5+8) + (-0.02)(45) + (-0.50)(1) = -1.10 - 1.30 - 0.90 - 0.50 = -3.80$ For R3: $V_3 = (-0.05)(55) + (-0.10)(4+12) + (-0.02)(18) + (-0.50)(0) = -2.75 - 1.60 - 0.36 + 0 = -4.71$ ### Step 2 — Compute Exponentials $$ e^{V_1} = e^{-3.05} \approx 0.0473 $$ $$ e^{V_2} = e^{-3.80} \approx 0.0224 $$ $$ e^{V_3} = e^{-4.71} \approx 0.0090 $$ $$ \sum_j e^{V_j} = 0.0473 + 0.0224 + 0.0090 = 0.0787 $$ ### Step 3 — Compute Probabilities $$ P(R_1) = \frac{0.0473}{0.0787} = 0.601 \; (60.1\%) $$ $$ P(R_2) = \frac{0.0224}{0.0787} = 0.285 \; (28.5\%) $$ $$ P(R_3) = \frac{0.0090}{0.0787} = 0.114 \; (11.4\%) $$ ### Step 4 — Interpretation If the daily demand from Aluva to MG Road is $q = 10{,}000$ trips, the assignment is: - R1 (Direct Metro): **6,010 trips** - R2 (Metro + Feeder): **2,850 trips** - R3 (Ordinary Bus): **1,140 trips** This validates why the Kochi Metro authority operates the direct Aluva–MG Road service as the spine route. ## 3.3 Worked Example — All-or-Nothing Assignment on a 3-Link Network ### Network Two parallel links connect Node 1 to Node 2, with travel time functions: - **Link A:** $t_A = 5 + 0.10 \cdot x_A$ (minutes) - **Link B:** $t_B = 8 + 0.05 \cdot x_B$ (minutes) Total demand: $q_{12} = 100$ PCU/hr. ### Step 1 — All-or-Nothing Initial Assignment At zero flow: $t_A = 5$, $t_B = 8$. Link A is shorter. So **all 100 vehicles** go to A: $$ x_A = 100, \quad x_B = 0 $$ ### Step 2 — Compute Resulting Travel Times $$ t_A = 5 + 0.10(100) = 15 \; \text{min}, \quad t_B = 8 \; \text{min} $$ ### Step 3 — User Equilibrium Verification For UE, the principle requires that used and unused paths are equal: A driver on Link A experiences 15 min. A driver on Link B would experience 8 min — better. So **UE is violated**. AON has not converged. ### Step 4 — Iterative Adjustment (Capacity Restraint) The "Frank–Wolfe linear combination" approach uses iteration: At iteration $n$, mix old and auxiliary flows: $$ x^{n+1} = (1-\lambda) x^n + \lambda y^n $$ where $y^n$ is the AON auxiliary flow using current $t^n$ and $\lambda$ is found by line search. Setting up line search: minimize $Z(\lambda) = \int_0^{(1-\lambda)x_A + \lambda \cdot 0} (5 + 0.10\omega) d\omega + \int_0^{(1-\lambda)x_B + \lambda \cdot 100} (8 + 0.05\omega) d\omega$. The equilibrium (UE) solution is found where $t_A = t_B$: $$ 5 + 0.10 x_A = 8 + 0.05 x_B $$ With $x_A + x_B = 100$: $$ 5 + 0.10 x_A = 8 + 0.05(100 - x_A) $$ $$ 5 + 0.10 x_A = 8 + 5 - 0.05 x_A $$ $$ 0.15 x_A = 8 $$ $$ x_A = 53.33, \quad x_B = 46.67 $$ Equilibrium travel time: $t_A = t_B = 5 + 0.10(53.33) = 10.33 \; \text{min}$. > [!NOTE] > This is the **Wardrop Equilibrium**: no driver can reduce his travel time by unilaterally switching links. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Public Transport Route Choice & Assignment System ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[Demand Matrix q_rs] --> B1[Network G N A] A2[Service Frequency f] --> B1 A3[Fare Structure F_k] --> B1 A4[Transfer Penalties] --> B1 B1 --> C1[Path Enumeration
Dijkstra / K-Shortest] C1 --> C2[Utility Calculator
V_k = beta1 T_IVTT + beta2 T_OVTT + beta3 F] C2 --> D1{Route Choice Model} D1 -->|Deterministic| D2[Shortest Path
All-or-Nothing] D1 -->|Stochastic Logit| D3[MNL Probability
P k = e^V_k / sum e^V_j] D1 -->|Stochastic Probit| D4[Monte Carlo
Perception Error] D1 -->|Frequency Based| D5[Schedule vs Freq
Selection] D2 --> E1[Assignment Engine] D3 --> E1 D4 --> E1 D5 --> E1 E1 --> F1{Assignment Algorithm} F1 -->|AON| G1[Single Path Load] F1 -->|Incremental| G2[Stepwise Loading] F1 -->|Iterative / CR| G3[Capacity Restraint Loop] F1 -->|Frank Wolfe| G4[UE Beckmann Solver] F1 -->|Dial STOCH| G5[SUE Multinomial Logit] G1 --> H1[Link Volumes x_a] G2 --> H1 G3 --> H1 G4 --> H1 H1 --> I1[Link Travel Time
t_a via BPR] I1 -->|feedback| F1 G4 --> J1[UE Convergence
Gap less than eps] H1 --> K1[Output Link Volumes] H1 --> K2[Output Path Flows] H1 --> K3[Output V C Ratios] H1 --> K4[Output Boarding Alighting] ``` ## 4.2 Decision Logic — Route Choice by Travel Behavior ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Trip Maker at Origin] --> S2{Trip Purpose} S2 -->|Work Mandatory| S3[Time Minimization
Threshold Cost] S2 -->|Education| S3 S2 -->|Shopping Optional| S4[Cost Comfort Balance] S2 -->|Recreation| S4 S3 --> S5{Available Modes} S4 --> S5 S5 -->|Bus| S6[Headway / Schedule Check] S5 -->|Metro Rail| S7[Frequency / Capacity Check] S5 -->|Feeder| S8[Walk Access Distance] S5 -->|Multi Modal| S9[Transfer Penalty Apply] S6 --> T1[Compute Generalized Cost GC] S7 --> T1 S8 --> T1 S9 --> T1 T1 --> U1{Compare Alternatives} U1 --> V1[Path 1 Cost C1] U1 --> V2[Path 2 Cost C2] U1 --> V3[Path N Cost CN] V1 --> W1[Probabilistic Choice
Logit or Probit] V2 --> W1 V3 --> W1 W1 --> X1[Select Route k
with Probability Pk] X1 --> Y1[Execute Trip] Y1 --> Z1[Update Experienced Cost
in Choice Set] Z1 -->|influences| W1 ``` ## 4.3 Frank–Wolfe Algorithm — Iterative Convergence Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[Initialize x_a_0 = 0] --> P2[Iteration n = 0] P2 --> P3[Compute t_a_n from BPR] P3 --> P4[Find AON Shortest Paths
using t_a_n] P4 --> P5[Get Auxiliary Flows y_a_n] P5 --> P6[Line Search lambda
minimize Z 1 minus lambda x plus lambda y] P6 --> P7[Update x_a_n+1
equals 1 minus lambda x_a_n + lambda y_a_n] P7 --> P8{Convergence Check
Gap less than 1e minus 4} P8 -->|No| P9[n equals n + 1] P9 --> P3 P8 -->|Yes| P10[Output Equilibrium x_a_star] P10 --> P11[Compute UE Link Times
t_a_star] ``` ## 4.4 Transit-Specific Common Lines Problem ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1[Passenger at Stop] --> Q2{Multiple Routes
Serving Stop} Q2 --> Q3[Route 1 to Destination] Q2 --> Q4[Route 2 to Destination] Q2 --> Q5[Route N to Destination] Q3 --> R1[Get Arrival Time T1] Q4 --> R2[Get Arrival Time T2] Q5 --> R3[Get Arrival Time TN] R1 --> S1[Strategy Set
Board First Arrival] R2 --> S1 R3 --> S1 S1 --> T1{Passenger Strategy} T1 -->|Adaptive| U1[Board First Bus Arriving
and Going to Destination] T1 -->|Pre Planned| U2[Wait for Specific Route] U1 --> V1[Common Lines Approach
Chriqui 1975] U2 --> W1[Schedule Based Choice] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **State and explain Wardrop's first and second principles of traffic equilibrium. Differentiate between User Equilibrium (UE) and System Optimal (SO) conditions with a suitable example from a Kerala city context.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Wardrop's First Principle (UE):** The journey times on all the routes actually used are equal, and less than the journey time that would be experienced on any unused route. (1 mark) Mathematical condition: For O–D pair $r\text{-}s$, if $f_k^{rs} > 0$, then $C_k^{rs} = \min_j C_j^{rs}$. (0.5 mark) **Wardrop's Second Principle (SO):** The average (or total) journey time is minimized. Drivers are routed in a way that may not be individually optimal but is collectively optimal. (1 mark) **Example:** Consider Aluva–MG Road during peak hours. In UE, both the metro line and the parallel NH-66 bypass will have equal travel times for the *last* commuter. In SO, some commuters may be forced onto slower routes to reduce total congestion. (0.5 mark) ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **Explain the concept of "All-or-Nothing" (AON) traffic assignment. Mention two major limitations of this method.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **AON Definition:** In the All-or-Nothing assignment method, the entire O–D demand between an origin–destination pair is loaded onto the single shortest path (minimum travel time path) between that pair, ignoring congestion effects on alternative paths. (2 marks) **Limitations (any two, 0.5 mark each):** 1. **Ignores congestion feedback:** AON treats travel times as fixed; the loaded shortest path may become oversaturated, but no flow diverts. 2. **Single-path rigidity:** All O–D pairs are forced onto one path, leading to unrealistic volumes and zero flow on parallel links. 3. **No equilibrium:** It does not satisfy Wardrop's user equilibrium principle. 4. **Sensitive to network changes:** Small changes in travel time can drastically shift the entire assignment. ## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each) ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024] #### Part (a) — Beckmann's Mathematical Formulation (7 Marks) **Derive the Beckmann mathematical programming formulation of the User Equilibrium (UE) traffic assignment problem. Show that its first-order conditions reduce to Wardrop's first principle.** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Problem Setup:** Consider a strongly connected network $G(N, A)$ with O–D demand matrix $q_{rs}$. The set of paths between $r$ and $s$ is $K_{rs}$. Let $f_k^{rs}$ denote the flow on path $k$. (1 mark) **Step 2 — Beckmann's Objective Function:** $$ Z(\mathbf{x}) = \sum_{a \in A} \int_0^{x_a} t_a(\omega) \, d\omega $$ where $t_a(\omega)$ is the link travel time as a function of flow $\omega$ (e.g., BPR function). (1 mark) **Step 3 — Constraints:** (i) Flow conservation: $\sum_{k \in K_{rs}} f_k^{rs} = q_{rs} \quad \forall r,s$ (1 mark) (ii) Non-negativity: $f_k^{rs} \geq 0$ (0.5 mark) (iii) Link–path incidence: $x_a = \sum_{rs} \sum_k f_k^{rs} \cdot \delta_{a,k}^{rs}$ (0.5 mark) **Step 4 — Lagrangian Formulation:** $$ L(\mathbf{f}, \boldsymbol{\lambda}) = \sum_a \int_0^{x_a} t_a(\omega) d\omega - \sum_{rs} \lambda_{rs} \left( \sum_k f_k^{rs} - q_{rs} \right) $$ (0.5 mark) **Step 5 — KKT Stationarity Condition:** Differentiating with respect to $f_k^{rs}$: $$ \frac{\partial L}{\partial f_k^{rs}} = \sum_a t_a(x_a) \delta_{a,k}^{rs} - \lambda_{rs} = 0 \quad \text{whenever } f_k^{rs} > 0 $$ Recognizing $\sum_a t_a \delta_{a,k}^{rs} = C_k^{rs}$ (the path travel time): (1 mark) $$ C_k^{rs} = \lambda_{rs} \quad \text{for all used paths} $$ **Step 6 — Equivalence to Wardrop:** For unused paths ($f_k^{rs} = 0$), complementary slackness gives $C_k^{rs} \geq \lambda_{rs}$. This is precisely Wardrop's first principle. (1 mark) **Step 7 — Convexity and Uniqueness:** Since BPR gives $\partial t_a / \partial x_a > 0$ and $\partial^2 t_a / \partial x_a^2 \geq 0$, the objective is strictly convex, ensuring a unique global minimum equal to the UE. (0.5 mark) #### Part (b) — Stochastic Route Choice via Logit (7 Marks) **A commuter in Thiruvananthapuram has three route alternatives from Technopark to East Fort. The IVTT, OVTT, fare, and number of transfers for each route are tabulated. Using the Multinomial Logit model with $\beta_1 = -0.04$ per min (IVTT), $\beta_2 = -0.08$ per min (OVTT), $\beta_3 = -0.03$ per ₹ (Fare), and $\beta_4 = -0.60$ per transfer, compute the choice probability for each route. Daily demand = 8,000 trips. Compute the flow on each route.** | Route | $T_{IVTT}$ (min) | $T_{OVTT}$ (min) | Fare (₹) | Transfers | |---|---|---|---|---| | R1 (City Bus 501) | 40 | 12 | 20 | 0 | | R2 (Metro + Walk) | 28 | 18 | 50 | 0 | | R3 (Two Buses Transfer) | 32 | 8 | 30 | 1 | **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Utility Calculation (1.5 marks):** $V_1 = -0.04(40) + (-0.08)(12) + (-0.03)(20) + (-0.60)(0) = -1.60 - 0.96 - 0.60 + 0 = -3.16$ $V_2 = -0.04(28) + (-0.08)(18) + (-0.03)(50) + (-0.60)(0) = -1.12 - 1.44 - 1.50 + 0 = -4.06$ $V_3 = -0.04(32) + (-0.08)(8) + (-0.03)(30) + (-0.60)(1) = -1.28 - 0.64 - 0.90 - 0.60 = -3.42$ **Step 2 — Exponential Values (1 mark):** $e^{V_1} = e^{-3.16} \approx 0.0423$ $e^{V_2} = e^{-4.06} \approx 0.0173$ $e^{V_3} = e^{-3.42} \approx 0.0327$ Sum $= 0.0923$ **Step 3 — Probabilities (1.5 marks):** $P(R_1) = 0.0423/0.0923 = 0.458 \; (45.8\%)$ $P(R_2) = 0.0173/0.0923 = 0.187 \; (18.7\%)$ $P(R_3) = 0.0327/0.0923 = 0.354 \; (35.5\%)$ **Step 4 — Flow Allocation (1.5 marks):** $f_1 = 0.458 \times 8{,}000 = 3{,}664$ trips $f_2 = 0.187 \times 8{,}000 = 1{,}496$ trips $f_3 = 0.354 \times 8{,}000 = 2{,}832$ trips **Verification:** $3{,}664 + 1{,}496 + 2{,}832 = 7{,}992 \approx 8{,}000$ ✓ (0.5 mark) **Step 5 — Conclusion:** The **City Bus 501 (R1)** is the most preferred route due to its competitive IVTT and zero transfers, despite the metro being faster in-vehicle. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] #### Part (a) — All-or-Nothing and Capacity Restraint (7 Marks) **A network has two parallel links between nodes 1 and 2. The BPR-type link performance functions are:** **Link A:** $t_A = 5 + 0.10 x_A$ minutes **Link B:** $t_B = 7 + 0.05 x_B$ minutes **Total demand $q_{12} = 200$ veh/hr. Perform (i) All-or-Nothing assignment and (ii) two iterations of the Iterative Capacity Restraint method. Comment on the convergence to User Equilibrium.** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — All-or-Nothing (AON) Assignment (2 marks):** At zero flow, $t_A = 5$ min, $t_B = 7$ min. Link A is shorter. $\Rightarrow x_A = 200, \quad x_B = 0$ Resulting times: $t_A = 5 + 0.10(200) = 25$ min, $t_B = 7$ min. **Step 2 — Iteration 1 of Capacity Restraint (2 marks):** Update $t_A$ and $t_B$ using current flows: $t_A^{(0)} = 25$ min (from AON), $t_B^{(0)} = 7$ min. Run AON on updated times: Link B is now shorter ($7 < 25$). So all 200 go to B: $x_A^{(1)} = 0, \quad x_B^{(1)} = 200$ New times: $t_A = 5, \quad t_B = 7 + 0.05(200) = 17$ min. **Step 3 — Iteration 2 of Capacity Restraint (2 marks):** Now $t_A = 5 < t_B = 17$. AON sends all to A again: $x_A^{(2)} = 200, \quad x_B^{(2)} = 0$ This oscillates! Pure iterative AON **does not converge**. **Step 4 — Convergence to UE (1 mark):** For UE, set $t_A = t_B$: $5 + 0.10 x_A = 7 + 0.05(200 - x_A)$ $5 + 0.10 x_A = 17 - 0.05 x_A$ $0.15 x_A = 12 \Rightarrow x_A = 80, \quad x_B = 120$ UE travel time: $t_A = t_B = 5 + 0.10(80) = 13$ min. (1 mark) #### Part (b) — Frank–Wolfe Algorithm Logic (7 Marks) **Explain the Frank–Wolfe algorithm for solving the Beckmann UE formulation. List the steps clearly and state one advantage and one limitation.** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Algorithm Overview (1 mark):** Frank–Wolfe (1956) is a descent method that linearizes the convex Beckmann objective at the current point and finds the descent direction via an AON sub-problem. **Step 2 — Initialization (1 mark):** Set $x_a^{(0)} = 0$ for all $a$. Set $n = 0$. **Step 3 — Iteration Loop (3 marks):** (i) Compute link times $t_a^{(n)} = t_a(x_a^{(n)})$ using BPR. (ii) **Direction Finding:** Find the shortest paths for all O–D pairs using $t_a^{(n)}$; load all demand to get auxiliary flow $y_a^{(n)}$. (iii) **Line Search:** Solve $\min_{\lambda \in [0,1]} Z\big((1-\lambda)x^{(n)} + \lambda y^{(n)}\big)$ to find optimal step size $\lambda_n$. (iv) **Update:** $x_a^{(n+1)} = (1 - \lambda_n) x_a^{(n)} + \lambda_n y_a^{(n)}$ **Step 4 — Convergence Check (1 mark):** Compute relative gap: $$ \text{Gap} = \frac{\sum_a t_a^{(n)}(x_a^{(n)} - y_a^{(n)})}{\sum_a x_a^{(n)} t_a^{(n)}} < \varepsilon $$ If satisfied, stop. Else $n \leftarrow n+1$ and return to step (i). **Step 5 — Advantage (0.5 mark):** - Only requires shortest-path computation per iteration (uses standard network algorithms like Dijkstra); no matrix inversion or complex operations. **Step 6 — Limitation (0.5 mark):** - Convergence is slow (sublinear) near the optimum; zigzag behavior of the line-search direction. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Don't confuse UE with SO:** In UE, drivers act selfishly; in SO, a central authority enforces optimal routing. The math differs. > 2. **Beckmann is NOT the equilibrium itself** — it is a *mathematical program whose solution* coincides with UE. > 3. **Logit has IIA (Independence from Irrelevant Alternatives) property:** adding a duplicate route distorts probabilities unrealistically. For nested choices, use **Nested Logit**. > 4. **AON is NOT equilibrium** — it is a sub-problem inside Frank–Wolfe. > 5. **Always state units** in numerical answers (veh/hr, minutes, ₹). > 6. **Mention BPR constants** $\alpha = 0.15$, $\beta = 4.0$ explicitly in derivations. > 7. **For transit-specific** problems, remember expected wait time = $\frac{1}{2f}$ for random arrivals. ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Route Choice and Assignment** - **Route Choice** is the *behavioral decision* of a traveler; **Route Assignment** is the *network-level loading* operation. - **Wardrop UE**: All used paths have equal and minimum travel time. **Wardrop SO**: Total travel time is minimized. - **Beckmann's formulation**: $\min \sum_a \int_0^{x_a} t_a(\omega) d\omega$ — its KKT conditions reproduce Wardrop UE. - **BPR function**: $t_a = t_a^0 \left[ 1 + 0.15 (x_a/C_a)^4 \right]$ — universally used in practice. - **AON assignment** loads all O–D demand onto the single shortest path; *does not converge to UE*. - **Iterative Capacity Restraint** uses incremental or repeated AON; *can oscillate*. - **Frank–Wolfe algorithm** is the workhorse solver: AON sub-problem + line search, guaranteed to converge to UE. - **Multinomial Logit (MNL)**: $P(k) = e^{V_k}/\sum_j e^{V_j}$; used in stochastic route choice. - **Dial's STOCH**: A "reasonable paths" logit-based SUE algorithm. - **Transit-specific considerations**: common lines problem, schedule vs. frequency-based, transfer penalties, expected wait time $= 1/(2f)$. - **IIA property of Logit** can be unrealistic; use Nested Logit for hierarchical choices. - **Flow conservation**: $\sum_k f_k^{rs} = q_{rs}$; **link–path incidence**: $x_a = \sum_{rs,k} f_k^{rs} \delta_{a,k}^{rs}$. - **Generalized Cost**: $GC = T_{IVTT} + \phi_w T_w + \phi_t T_t + \alpha F$ — convert all attributes to equivalent time units. - **In KTU context**: Be prepared to solve numerical problems with 2–3 parallel links, compute logit probabilities, and explain Beckmann derivation in 14-mark questions.

topics.title: Revenues
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET833 - PUBLIC TRANSPORTATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Revenues

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the context of **Public Transportation Systems (PTS)**, the term **"Revenues"** refers to the aggregate monetary inflow generated by a transit agency (such as KSRTC, BMTC, Kochi Metro, Delhi Metro Rail Corporation, or Indian Railways) through the operation of its transport services, ancillary commercial activities, and government/non-government funding mechanisms.

As per the KTU 2024 OECET833 syllabus framework, transit revenues are classified into three primary heads:

1. **Traffic (Direct User) Revenues** – Money collected directly from passengers in the form of fares, tickets, passes, and penalties.
2. **Non-Traffic (Commercial) Revenues** – Income from activities not directly tied to passenger movement, e.g., **station advertising, parking charges, real-estate leasing of air-space, film shootings, and consultancy services**.
3. **Subsidies and Grants** – Fiscal support extended by the Central/State Government or Urban Local Bodies (ULBs) to keep fares socially affordable and ensure service continuity.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Definition:** *Revenue in PTS is the gross earnings of a transit operator expressed as the product of the number of fare-paying passengers and the average fare paid, augmented by commercial income and adjusted for subsidies.*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Think of a transit agency exactly like a **shopping mall**:

- The **rent paid by shopkeepers** in the mall is analogous to *non-traffic revenue* (advertising, leases, kiosks).
- The **entry tickets bought by shoppers** are analogous to *fare-box revenue* (what passengers pay to ride a bus or train).
- The **government compensation** given to the mall to keep parking cheap and entry free for senior citizens is analogous to *subsidies* and *social service obligation (SSO) grants*.

A well-managed transit agency, just like a smart mall owner, does **not** rely on a single source of money. It blends *fare-box revenue* (primary), *commercial revenue* (secondary), and *public subsidy* (tertiary) to keep ticket prices low while covering the heavy capital and operating expenditure (CAPEX/OPEX) of running buses, metro trains, and ferries.

## 1.3 Core Metrics and Constants

- **Standard Load Factor benchmark for urban buses:** **60–75 %** (deemed financially viable).
- **Standard Cost Recovery Ratio (CRR) for Indian metros:** typically **0.40 to 1.10** (subsidies bridge the deficit).
- **Average global fare elasticity of urban transit:** **−0.30 to −0.50** (a 10 % fare hike reduces ridership by 3–5 %).
- **Currency:** All monetary values in this module are expressed in **Indian Rupees (₹)**, with the operating year as the base index.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 4):** The KTU Module-4 syllabus categorises revenues under *Fare Structure*, *Fare Collection Technology*, and *Revenue Performance Indicators*. Memorise the term **"Revenue per Vehicle-Kilometre (Rev/VKM)"** — it is a favourite KTU 14-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Taxonomy of Transit Revenues

A transit agency's revenue stream can be broken down into the following operational hierarchy:

### 2.1.1 Direct / Fare-Box Revenue
- **Cash single-journey tickets**
- **Daily / Weekly / Monthly passes** (unlimited travel)
- **Stored-Value Smart Cards** (e.g., Kochi Metro's *Kochi1* card, Delhi Metro's *DMRC Smart Card*)
- **Concessional tickets** (students, senior citizens, differently-abled, women — heavily subsidised by the government)
- **Penalty / Excess Fare Tickets (EFT)** for ticket-less travel

### 2.1.2 Non-Fare / Commercial Revenue (Auxiliary Income)
- **Advertisement rights** inside buses/coaches, on station walls, and on platform screens.
- **Co-branding and naming rights** of stations (e.g., a private company paying to rename a metro station).
- **Parking fees** at Park-and-Ride facilities.
- **Commercial development of air-space** above stations (TOD — Transit-Oriented Development).
- **Sale of scrap, carbon credits, and renewable energy (solar from rooftops)**.

### 2.1.3 Subsidies, Grants, and Cross-Subsidies
- **Viability Gap Funding (VGF)** from the Ministry of Housing & Urban Affairs (MoHUA).
- **Operational subsidies** from State Governments (e.g., free bus travel scheme for women in Kerala — *Kerala Free Travel Scheme*).
- **Public Service Obligation (PSO) grants** — paid to operators to run unprofitable rural/feeder routes.

## 2.2 Fare Structure Models (Why and How)

The "Why" behind choosing a fare structure is the **trade-off between equity, simplicity, and revenue maximisation**.

| Fare Structure | Underlying Logic | Best-Suited Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Flat Fare** | Single price for entire route — simple to collect | Intra-city buses with short, uniform trips |
| **Zonal Fare** | City divided into concentric zones; price based on zones crossed | Mumbai BEST, London Underground (legacy), Delhi Metro (zonal component) |
| **Distance-Based (Kilometric) Fare** | Price = (Base fare) + (Rate per km × Distance) | Long-distance KSRTC ordinary buses, Indian Railways |
| **Time-Based (Stage) Fare** | Fare jumps at fixed stage boundaries (typically every 4–6 km) | Kerala RTC city services, BMTC (Bangalore) |
| **Sectional / Section Fare** | Route divided into numbered sections; rider pays for sections traversed | Trunk-and-feeder metro systems |
| **Integrated / Flat-Tariff Free Travel** | Zero fare, fully subsidised by state | Delhi (2019 pilot), Kerala (free for women in ordinary buses) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** In distance-based fare systems, the *first kilometre* carries a disproportionately high *boarding cost* (driver's effort, door operation, ticket issuance), while subsequent kilometres carry only a *running cost* (fuel, tyre wear). This is why every distance-fare equation is a **two-part tariff**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Load Factor (LF) | $LF = \dfrac{\sum(\text{Passenger-km})}{\sum(\text{Available Seat-km})} \times 100$ | % | Health indicator of fleet utilisation |
| 2 | Average Fare (AF) | $AF = \dfrac{\text{Total Fare-Box Revenue}}{\text{Total Passengers Carried}}$ | ₹ / passenger | Used in elasticity studies |
| 3 | Revenue per Passenger-km (Rev/PKM) | $Rev/PKM = \dfrac{\text{Total Revenue}}{\text{Total Passenger-km}}$ | ₹ / pkm | Cost-coverage metric |
| 4 | Revenue per Vehicle-km (Rev/VKM) | $Rev/VKM = \dfrac{\text{Total Revenue}}{\text{Total Vehicle-km operated}}$ | ₹ / vkm | Operational efficiency metric |
| 5 | Revenue per Available Seat-km (RASK) | $RASK = \dfrac{\text{Total Revenue}}{\text{Total Available Seat-km}}$ | ₹ / askm | Aviation-style indicator adapted for PTS |
| 6 | Fare Elasticity of Demand (E) | $E = \dfrac{\%\Delta Q}{\%\Delta P} = \dfrac{\Delta Q / Q}{\Delta P / P}$ | dimensionless $\vert E \vert$ | Sign is always negative; magnitude governs policy |
| 7 | Cost Recovery Ratio (CRR) | $CRR = \dfrac{\text{Operating Revenue}}{\text{Operating Cost}}$ | ratio | CRR < 1 implies subsidy dependence |
| 8 | Break-Even Passenger Volume | $Q_{BE} = \dfrac{F}{p - v}$ | passengers | F = fixed cost; p = fare; v = variable cost/pax |
| 9 | Revenue Yield | $Yield = \dfrac{\text{Revenue}}{\text{RPKM (Revenue Passenger-km})}$ | ₹ / pkm | Used in airline-style benchmarking |

> [!CAUTION]
> When writing KTU answers, always mention the **units** alongside the numerical value. Examiners award ½ mark for explicit units in derivation steps.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These revenue formulas are not academic exercises — they drive real decisions:

- A **Kochi Metro Rail Limited (KMRL)** planner using **Rev/VKM** to decide whether a 5 a.m. early-morning service is financially justified.
- A **KSRTC** operations manager computing **Load Factor** to determine the optimum number of ordinary vs. fast passenger buses on the Thiruvananthapuram–Kollam corridor.
- A **NHAI / Metro concessionaire** forecasting **break-even ridership** to negotiate Viability Gap Funding (VGF) with the Government of India.
- An **urban transport economist** using **fare elasticity** to predict the ridership impact of a 5 % fare hike proposed in the State Budget.
