# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Two-Part Distance-Based Fare Function

The most commonly tested formula in KTU Module 4 is the **Two-Part Tariff**, which states that the total fare $F(d)$ for a journey of distance $d$ kilometres is the sum of a fixed *boarding charge* and a *variable running charge*.

Let:
- $F_b$ = fixed boarding fare (₹) — paid once on entry.
- $c_r$ = running cost per kilometre (₹/km) — proportional to distance.
- $d$ = journey distance (km).

By the principle of **additive cost decomposition**, the total fare is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
F(d) &= F_b + c_r \cdot d
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step-by-step logic:**

- When $d = 0$ (passenger boards and alights at same stop), the fare should still cover the *minimum cost of providing service*. Hence $F(0) = F_b$.
- For every additional kilometre of travel, the operator incurs a marginal cost $c_r$ (fuel, brake wear, crew time-share). This is a **linear marginal cost** assumption — valid for inter-city buses in uncongested operation.
- Therefore, the total fare grows linearly with $d$ after a one-time step at $d=0$.

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style):**

> A KSRTC bus charges a boarding fare of ₹10 and a per-kilometre running charge of ₹1.50. A passenger travels 42 km. Compute the total fare and the percentage contribution of the boarding charge to the total fare.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F_b &= ₹10 \\
c_r &= ₹1.50\ \text{per km} \\
d &= 42\ \text{km} \\
F(42) &= F_b + c_r \cdot d \\
&= 10 + (1.50)(42) \\
&= 10 + 63 \\
&= ₹73
\end{aligned}
$$

The boarding-charge percentage of the total fare is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%F_b &= \frac{F_b}{F(42)} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{10}{73} \times 100 \\
&\approx 13.70\ \%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** For short trips, the boarding charge dominates the fare (high *minimum fare effect*); for long trips, the running charge dominates. This is why a flat fare of ₹20 on a 2-km route is unfair to short-distance commuters compared to a stage-fare system.

## 3.2 Derivation: Load Factor from First Principles

The **Load Factor (LF)** is the ratio of *revenue-producing capacity* to *total supplied capacity*.

Consider one bus operating for a day:
- It runs $V$ vehicle-kilometres.
- It has $S$ seats.
- Therefore, total **available seat-kilometres** $= S \times V$.

Suppose the bus carries $P$ passenger-kilometres over the day (sum of (passengers × distance) for every trip segment).

By definition:

$$
\begin{aligned}
LF &= \frac{\text{Capacity utilised}}{\text{Capacity offered}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{P}{S \times V} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

**Numerical Example:**

A city bus has 50 seats and runs 200 km in a day. It carries 6,000 passenger-km in revenue service. Compute the Load Factor.

$$
\begin{aligned}
LF &= \frac{6000}{50 \times 200} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{6000}{10000} \times 100 \\
&= 60\ \%
\end{aligned}
$$

A 60 % LF is considered *healthy* for an Indian city bus (KTU benchmark: **60–75 %**).

## 3.3 Derivation: Break-Even Passenger Volume

A transit operator wishes to know the minimum number of passengers $Q_{BE}$ per day that will cover all costs.

Let:
- $F$ = Total daily **fixed cost** (depreciation, insurance, driver salary, permits) in ₹/day.
- $v$ = **Variable cost per passenger** (additional fuel, ticketing) in ₹/pax.
- $p$ = **Average fare per passenger** in ₹/pax.
- $Q$ = Number of passengers per day.

**Total Cost function:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC &= F + v \cdot Q
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total Revenue function:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
TR &= p \cdot Q
\end{aligned}
$$

**Break-even condition** (zero operating profit/loss):

$$
\begin{aligned}
TR &= TC \\
p \cdot Q_{BE} &= F + v \cdot Q_{BE} \\
p \cdot Q_{BE} - v \cdot Q_{BE} &= F \\
Q_{BE} \cdot (p - v) &= F \\
Q_{BE} &= \frac{F}{p - v}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Worked Example:**

> A metro station has a daily fixed cost of ₹2,00,000 (staff, electricity, depreciation). Variable cost per passenger is ₹5. Average fare is ₹25. How many passengers per day are required to break even?

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{BE} &= \frac{F}{p - v} \\
&= \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{25 - 5} \\
&= \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{20} \\
&= 10{,}000\ \text{passengers/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

If the station handles fewer than 10,000 passengers per day, it will run at an operational loss and require subsidy.

## 3.4 Derivation: Fare Elasticity from Log-Linear Form

Fare elasticity $E_f$ is the responsiveness of ridership $Q$ to a change in fare $P$. The **point-elasticity** formula is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_f &= \frac{dQ / Q}{dP / P} = \frac{dQ}{dP} \cdot \frac{P}{Q}
\end{aligned}
$$

If demand is assumed to follow a **linear demand curve** $Q = a - bP$, then:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dQ}{dP} &= -b \\
E_f &= -b \cdot \frac{P}{Q} = -b \cdot \frac{P}{a - bP}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation Matrix (Critical for KTU 14-mark answers):**

| Magnitude of $\vert E_f \vert$ | Demand Behaviour | Policy Implication |
|---|---|---|
| $\vert E_f \vert > 1$ | **Elastic** — ridership changes more than fare | Avoid fare hikes; revenue will fall |
| $\vert E_f \vert = 1$ | **Unit elastic** — proportional change | Revenue unchanged with fare revision |
| $\vert E_f \vert < 1$ | **Inelastic** — ridership barely changes | Safe to hike fares; revenue will rise |
| $\vert E_f \vert = 0$ | **Perfectly inelastic** | Captive riders (e.g., daily office commuters) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For Indian urban bus transit**, empirical studies place $\vert E_f \vert$ in the range **0.30 to 0.60** — i.e., demand is *inelastic*. This means a 10 % fare hike reduces ridership by only 3–6 %, **increasing** total revenue by approximately 4–7 %. Always quote this in your KTU answer.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Revenue Components Summary

| Component | Source | Typical % of Total Revenue (Indian Metro/Bus) | Variability |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fare-box revenue** | Tickets, smart cards, passes | **70 – 85 %** | Highly elastic to ridership |
| **Advertising** | In-station, in-vehicle media | **3 – 8 %** | Stable, contract-based |
| **Parking & TOD** | Park-and-ride, real-estate lease | **2 – 5 %** | Growing (KMRL, DMRC models) |
| **Subsidies / VGF / PSO** | Central & State Governments | **10 – 25 %** (off-balance) | Policy-dependent |
| **Penalty / EFT** | Ticket-less travel fines | **0.5 – 1 %** | Negligible |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram: Revenue Flow Architecture of a Transit Agency

The following **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** maps how money enters and circulates inside a typical Indian urban transit operator (e.g., a metro rail corporation).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Passenger Demand] --> B[Fare Collection System]
    A1[Advertisers and Lessees] --> C[Non-Fare Commercial Wing]
    GOV[Central and State Government] --> D[Subsidy and VGF Cell]
    B --> E[Revenue Pool Account]
    C --> E
    D --> E
    E --> F1[Operating Expenditure]
    E --> F2[Debt Servicing]
    E --> F3[Asset Renewal and CAPEX]
    E --> F4[Reserve and Surplus Fund]
    F1 --> G1[Staff Salary and Diesel]
    F1 --> G2[Electricity and Maintenance]
    F1 --> G3[Overhead and Admin]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Passenger demand drives the fare collection system. The revenue pool is the centralised treasury that then discharges operating expenses, debt obligations, capital investments, and contingency reserves.

## 4.2 Process Diagram: Fare Structure Selection Logic

The choice of fare structure is governed by operator objectives, passenger profile, and route geometry.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START([Operator Decision Point]) --> Q1{Route Length Profile}
    Q1 -->|Short and uniform| F1[Flat Fare]
    Q1 -->|Long and variable| Q2{Distance Awareness of Passengers?}
    Q2 -->|Yes| F2[Distance Based Kilometric Fare]
    Q2 -->|No| F3[Zonal Fare]
    Q1 -->|Mixed corridor| Q3{Equity Priority?}
    Q3 -->|High| F4[Sectional or Stage Fare]
    Q3 -->|Low| F2
    F1 --> END([Final Tariff Notification])
    F2 --> END
    F3 --> END
    F4 --> END
```

## 4.3 Concept Map: Revenue Performance Indicators (RPIs)

This diagram shows how a single fleet's data feeds multiple performance indicators.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    DATA[Raw Operational Data] --> VKM[Vehicle Kilometres]
    DATA --> PKM[Passenger Kilometres]
    DATA --> ASK[Available Seat Kilometres]
    DATA --> REV[Total Revenue]
    VKM --> I1[Rev per VKM]
    ASK --> I2[Load Factor Percent]

    REV --> I5[Cost Recovery Ratio]
    DATA --> COST[Total Operating Cost]
    COST --> I5
    I1 --> REPORT[Annual Revenue Performance Report]
    I2 --> REPORT
    I3 --> REPORT
    I4 --> REPORT
    I5 --> REPORT

```

## 4.4 Schematic: Fare Elasticity Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    OBS[Observed Fare Hike] --> CALC[Calculate Elasticity E]
    CALC --> DEC{Is absolute E greater than 1?}
    DEC -->|Yes Elastic| R1[Ridership falls more than revenue rises]
    DEC -->|No Inelastic| R2[Ridership stable revenue increases]
    R1 --> POL1[Avoid Fare Hike Consider Service Improvement]
    R2 --> POL2[Proceed with Modest Fare Hike]
    POL1 --> OUT([Policy Action])
    POL2 --> OUT
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "Revenue per Vehicle-Kilometre" and state its significance in transit performance evaluation.**

**Model Answer:**

Revenue per Vehicle-Kilometre (Rev/VKM) is the total revenue earned per unit of vehicle distance operated, calculated as:

$$
Rev/VKM = \frac{\text{Total Revenue (₹)}}{\text{Total Vehicle-km operated}}
$$

It is expressed in **₹ per vkm**.

**Significance:**
1. It normalises revenue against operational effort, allowing comparison between routes of different lengths.
2. It is the primary indicator used by operators (e.g., KSRTC, DMRC) to identify *high-yield* and *low-yield* corridors.
3. A declining Rev/VKM over years is an early warning of fare-box erosion due to fare evasion, mode shift, or operational inefficiency.

> [!TIP]
> Write the unit **₹/vkm** explicitly to fetch the unit-mark.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**What is "Load Factor" in public bus transport? What is the generally accepted healthy range?**

**Model Answer:**

Load Factor (LF) is the ratio of passenger-kilometres actually carried to the seat-kilometres available, expressed as a percentage:

$$
LF = \frac{\text{Passenger-km carried}}{\text{Available seat-km}} \times 100
$$

A **healthy Load Factor** for an Indian city bus lies in the range **60 % to 75 %**.

A load factor below 50 % indicates over-supply (too many buses or empty routes), while a load factor persistently above 90 % indicates overcrowding, poor service quality, and likely revenue loss from ticketless travel.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

`[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 4]`

**(a)** Explain the various **fare structures** adopted in public transportation systems with suitable examples. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A KSRTC ordinary bus operates on a route where the **boarding fare is ₹8** and the **per-kilometre charge is ₹1.20**. On a particular day, the bus completes **3 round trips of 60 km each** with an average **load factor of 65 %** and **50 seats**. Calculate:
- (i) The total fare collected from a passenger travelling end-to-end.
- (ii) The total daily revenue if the bus carries passengers for its full route length at the computed average occupancy. (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**(a) Fare Structures in Public Transportation:**

The major fare structures are:

1. **Flat Fare:** A single uniform fare for the entire journey regardless of distance. *Example:* Old Delhi Ho-Ho sightseeing bus, certain intra-city auto-rickshaw prepaid booths.

2. **Zonal Fare:** The city is divided into concentric zones. The fare depends on the number of zones crossed. *Example:* Legacy London Underground, DMRC zonal component.

3. **Distance-Based (Kilometric) Fare:** Fare is a linear function of distance. *Example:* KSRTC long-distance services, Indian Railways.

4. **Stage / Sectional Fare:** Route is divided into stages; fare is fixed per stage irrespective of exact km. *Example:* Kerala RTC city services, BMTC Bengaluru.

5. **Time-Based Fare:** Fare depends on journey duration (common in metered taxis).

6. **Integrated / Subsidised Zero Fare:** State fully underwrites the cost. *Example:* Pink Tickets for women in DTC Delhi, Kerala Free Travel Scheme for women in ordinary buses.

**Comparative evaluation:**

| Structure | Simplicity | Equity | Revenue Yield |
|---|---|---|---|
| Flat | High | Low | Variable |
| Zonal | Medium | Medium | Medium |
| Distance-Based | Low | High | High |
| Stage | High | Medium | High |

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1 — Fare for end-to-end journey:**

The route length (one-way) = 60 km.

$$
\begin{aligned}
F(60) &= F_b + c_r \cdot d \\
&= 8 + (1.20)(60) \\
&= 8 + 72 \\
&= ₹80
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Total seat-km offered per day:**

The bus does 3 round trips of 60 km each, so one-way distance = 60 km and round-trip = 120 km.

Total vehicle-km per day = $3 \times 120 = 360$ vkm.

Total available seat-km per day = $50 \times 360 = 18{,}000$ seat-km.

**Step 3 — Actual passenger-km carried:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Passenger-km} &= LF \times \text{Seat-km} \\
&= 0.65 \times 18{,}000 \\
&= 11{,}700\ \text{pkm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Average fare per passenger-km (assumed uniform two-part tariff):**

The full-route fare is ₹80 for 60 km, so the per-km fare is $80/60 = ₹1.333$ per pkm.

**Step 5 — Total daily revenue:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Revenue} &= (\text{Rate per pkm}) \times (\text{Total pkm}) \\
&= 1.333 \times 11{,}700 \\
&\approx ₹15{,}596
\end{aligned}
$$

**Alternative Method (per trip):** Passengers per trip = $0.65 \times 50 = 32.5$ pax. Fare per pax (end-to-end) = ₹80. Revenue per trip = $32.5 \times 80 = ₹2{,}600$. Three round trips = 6 single-direction trips.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total Revenue} &= 6 \times 2600 = ₹15{,}600\ \text{(rounded)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Both methods yield the same order of magnitude (the minor difference is due to rounding in load factor application). The correct answer is **≈ ₹15,600 per day**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)

`[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 4]`

**(a)** Discuss the various **sources of revenue** for an urban transit agency, distinguishing between **fare-box** and **non-fare-box** revenue. (7 Marks)

**(b)** The **fixed cost** of operating a feeder metro line is **₹3,00,000 per day**, and the **variable cost per passenger** is **₹8**. The current **fare is ₹30 per passenger**. The line handles **8,000 passengers per day**.
- (i) Calculate the **break-even passenger volume**.
- (ii) Determine whether the current operation is in **profit, loss, or break-even**.
- (iii) If the operator wants a **profit of ₹50,000 per day**, what should be the new fare? (7 Marks)

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**(a) Sources of Revenue:**

Urban transit agencies typically receive revenue from three broad streams:

**1. Fare-Box (Traffic) Revenue:**
- Single-journey tickets
- Daily, weekly, and monthly passes
- Stored-value smart cards (e.g., KMRL *Kochi1*, DMRC Smart Card)
- Concessional tickets (students, senior citizens, women)
- Excess Fare Tickets (EFT) for ticketless travel

**2. Non-Fare-Box (Commercial) Revenue:**
- In-station and in-vehicle advertising
- Station naming rights and co-branding
- Parking charges at Park-and-Ride lots
- Real-estate leasing of air-space (TOD)
- Sale of scrap and renewable energy (solar rooftop)
- Carbon-credit sales

**3. Subsidies and Grants:**
- Viability Gap Funding (VGF) from MoHUA
- State operational subsidies (e.g., Kerala's free bus travel scheme for women)
- Public Service Obligation (PSO) grants for unprofitable rural/feeder routes

The Indian average fare-box share is 70–85 %; the global best practice is to push non-fare-box revenue above 25 % (achieved by Hong Kong MTR and Tokyo Metro through aggressive TOD).

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

Given: $F = ₹3{,}00{,}000$/day, $v = ₹8$/pax, $p = ₹30$/pax, $Q = 8{,}000$ pax/day.

**(i) Break-Even Volume:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_{BE} &= \frac{F}{p - v} \\
&= \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{30 - 8} \\
&= \frac{3{,}00{,}000}{22} \\
&\approx 13{,}636\ \text{pax/day}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Profit/Loss at Current Volume:**

Total Revenue at $Q = 8{,}000$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
TR &= p \cdot Q = 30 \times 8000 = ₹2{,}40{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

Total Cost at $Q = 8{,}000$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
TC &= F + v \cdot Q = 3{,}00{,}000 + (8)(8000) = 3{,}00{,}000 + 64{,}000 = ₹3{,}64{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

Profit/Loss:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Net} &= TR - TC = 2{,}40{,}000 - 3{,}64{,}000 = -₹1{,}24{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

The line is incurring a **daily loss of ₹1,24,000** because the actual volume (8,000) is well below the break-even volume (≈ 13,636).

**(iii) New fare to earn ₹50,000 profit:**

We need: $TR - TC = 50{,}000$, with $Q = 8{,}000$ (assumed unchanged).

$$
\begin{aligned}
(p_{new} \cdot Q) - (F + v \cdot Q) &= 50{,}000 \\
p_{new} \cdot 8000 &= 50{,}000 + 3{,}00{,}000 + 64{,}000 \\
p_{new} \cdot 8000 &= 4{,}14{,}000 \\
p_{new} &= \frac{4{,}14{,}000}{8000} \\
p_{new} &= ₹51.75
\end{aligned}
$$

The new fare should be **₹51.75 per passenger** to achieve a daily profit of ₹50,000 (assuming ridership remains unchanged — in reality, the higher fare would reduce ridership per the elasticity principle, requiring an iterative check).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Common Mark-Deduction Points:**
> 1. **Unit omission:** Students frequently write "₹" without specifying the denominator (e.g., per pkm, per vkm, per day). Always include the full unit.
> 2. **Sign of elasticity:** Students write the fare elasticity as a *positive* number. Always state it as **negative** and discuss the absolute value separately.
> 3. **Confusing Load Factor with Occupancy Ratio:** Load Factor is on a *kilometre* basis (passenger-km/seat-km), not passenger/seat count.
> 4. **Forgetting the "no. of trips × direction" multiplier** in revenue calculations for round-trip bus services.
> 5. **Two-part tariff mis-derivation:** Writing $F = c_r \cdot d$ and forgetting the boarding charge $F_b$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Definition:** Revenue = Fare-box income + Commercial income + Subsidies.
- **Fare Structures:** Flat, Zonal, Distance-based (kilometric), Stage, Sectional, Subsidised zero-fare.
- **Two-Part Tariff Equation:** $F(d) = F_b + c_r \cdot d$ — board fare + running fare.
- **Load Factor Formula:** $LF = (\text{Passenger-km}/\text{Available Seat-km}) \times 100$. Healthy range = **60–75 %**.
- **Rev/VKM Formula:** Total Revenue / Total Vehicle-km. Used to compare corridor performance.
- **Rev/PKM Formula:** Total Revenue / Total Passenger-km. Used to benchmark fare yield.
- **Fare Elasticity Formula:** $E_f = (\% \Delta Q) / (\% \Delta P)$. Indian bus elasticity magnitude = **0.30–0.60** (inelastic).
- **Cost Recovery Ratio (CRR):** Operating Revenue / Operating Cost. CRR < 1 ⇒ subsidy needed.
- **Break-Even Volume:** $Q_{BE} = F / (p - v)$. Always greater than zero; depends on fixed cost and contribution margin $(p - v)$.
- **Revenue Stream Mix (Indian Metro):** Fare-box 70–85 %, Commercial 5–13 %, Subsidies 10–25 %.
- **Non-Fare Revenue Sources:** Advertising, parking, station naming rights, TOD air-space, carbon credits, scrap sale.
- **Subsidies:** VGF (Central), State PSO grants, social obligation concessions (women, students, PwD).
- **Smart-Card Advantage:** Reduces cash handling, increases average fare per passenger by 8–12 % (KMRL/DMRC data), enables origin-destination data analytics.
- **KTU Numerical Favourite:** Two-part tariff fare calculation, break-even ridership, elasticity interpretation — practise all three types.
- **Common Misconception:** A higher fare *always* increases revenue — **false** when demand is *elastic* ($\vert E_f \vert > 1$).
- **Unit Hygiene:** Always write ₹/vkm, ₹/pkm, ₹/pax, pax/day, % — fetch easy half-marks.
- **Valuation Tip:** Mention the *sign* and *magnitude* of elasticity separately, and tie it to a real Indian example (KSRTC, DMRC, BMTC) for full credit.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Costs and Operating Ratio
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET833 - PUBLIC TRANSPORTATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Costs and Operating Ratio

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Definition of Transportation Costs

In the context of **Public Transportation Systems**, *costs* represent the total monetary expenditure incurred by a transit agency to provide mobility services to the public. These costs encompass every financial outflow required to plan, operate, maintain, and administer the transit fleet, infrastructure, and human resources.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *Transportation cost is the economic value of resources (vehicle, labor, fuel, infrastructure, and overhead) consumed in the production and delivery of a unit of public transport service, typically measured per vehicle-kilometre (VKM) or per passenger-kilometre (PKM).*

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

Think of running a public bus service like running a **tiffin delivery kitchen**:
- **Fixed costs** are like the rent of the kitchen, the price of the cooking vessels, and the cook's monthly salary — these are paid whether you make 10 tiffins or 100 tiffins.
- **Variable costs** are like rice, vegetables, and cooking gas — the more tiffins you deliver, the more you spend.
- **Total revenue** is the money collected from customers.
- **Operating Ratio** simply asks: *"For every ₹100 I earn, how much am I spending?"* — if the answer is more than ₹100, the kitchen is losing money!

### 1.3 Standard Cost Metrics Used by KTU / MoHUA

> [!NOTE]
> The **Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs (MoHUA)** and **UITP (Union Internationale des Transports Publics)** follow these key performance metrics:
> - **Cost per Vehicle-Kilometre (VKM)** — primary indicator of operating efficiency
> - **Cost per Passenger-Kilometre (PKM)** — efficiency per unit of service consumed
> - **Cost per Passenger Trip** — average cost of moving one passenger
> - **Revenue per VKM** — earning efficiency

### 1.4 Definition of Operating Ratio

The **Operating Ratio (OR)** is the most widely used single-number financial performance indicator for a transit agency.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition:** *Operating Ratio is the ratio of total operating expenses to total revenue earned from operations during a specific accounting period, expressed as a percentage or a decimal fraction.*

$$OR = \frac{\text{Total Operating Expenses}}{\text{Total Operating Revenue}}$$

- If **OR < 1.0 (or 100%)** → the system generates a **surplus** (profit).
- If **OR = 1.0** → the system **breaks even**.
- If **OR > 1.0** → the system runs at a **deficit** (loss), requiring subsidy.

> [!NOTE]
> Most urban public bus systems in India (BMTC, BEST, DTC, KSRTC) historically have **OR between 1.4 and 2.0**, meaning they spend ₹140–₹200 to earn ₹100 — a chronic deficit funded by government subsidy.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Transit Costs

Transit costs are broadly classified along three dimensions:

#### A. By Behaviour with Respect to Output (Service Volume)

**(i) Fixed Costs**
These costs remain **constant in total** irrespective of the level of service (kilometres operated, passengers carried). They are time-dependent, not distance-dependent.

> Examples: Depreciation of buses, salaries of permanent administrative staff, insurance premiums, garage rent, licensing fees, interest on capital loans.

**(ii) Variable Costs**
These costs **vary directly with the level of service** — more kilometres operated means more expense.

> Examples: Fuel / diesel / electricity, tyres, lubricants, crew wages paid per kilometre, vehicle maintenance proportional to wear, toll charges.

**(iii) Semi-Variable (Mixed) Costs**
Costs with both a fixed component and a variable component, e.g., electricity (fixed connection charge + per-unit charge), maintenance supervisor's salary (fixed) + per-bus inspection cost (variable).

#### B. By Traceability / Allocation

| Cost Type | Description | Example in Transit |
|-----------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Direct Costs** | Identifiable with a single service or vehicle | Fuel for Bus #423, driver's salary |
| **Indirect Costs (Overheads)** | Common to all services; must be apportioned | Central office rent, GM's salary, IT systems |
| **Joint Costs** | Incurred to produce multiple services simultaneously | A depot serving both AC and non-AC buses |
| **Sunk Costs** | Already incurred and irrecoverable | Cost of an old scrapped vehicle |

#### C. By Function

- **Vehicle Operating Costs (VOC)** — fuel, tyres, maintenance, crew
- **Vehicle Ownership Costs** — depreciation, insurance, registration
- **Overhead / Administrative Costs** — management, billing, marketing
- **Infrastructure Costs** — depot, terminals, track (for rail/metro)

### 2.2 Cost Behaviour — The Unit-Cost Paradox

A critical concept in transit economics is the **economy of scale** with respect to ridership:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Concept:** As **load factor** (passengers per vehicle) increases, the **cost per passenger-kilometre decreases** sharply because the fixed and semi-fixed costs of the vehicle are spread over more paying passengers. This is why transit authorities aim for **higher occupancy** rather than empty luxury travel.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Unit |
|---|---------|-------------|------|
| 1 | $OR = \dfrac{E}{R}$ | Operating Ratio = Expenses ÷ Revenue | Dimensionless / % |
| 2 | $CRR = \dfrac{R}{E}$ | Cost Recovery Ratio = Revenue ÷ Expenses | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $FBRR = \dfrac{R_{\text{pass}}}{E}$ | Fare-Box Recovery Ratio | Dimensionless |
| 4 | $VKM = N_{\text{bus}} \times D_{\text{day}} \times N_{\text{days}}$ | Vehicle-Kilometres in period | km |
| 5 | $PKM = VKM \times LF$ | Passenger-Kilometres = VKM × Load Factor | PKM |
| 6 | $C_{VKM} = \dfrac{E_{\text{total}}}{VKM}$ | Cost per Vehicle-Kilometre | ₹/km |
| 7 | $C_{PKM} = \dfrac{E_{\text{total}}}{PKM}$ | Cost per Passenger-Kilometre | ₹/PKM |
| 8 | $C_{\text{trip}} = \dfrac{E_{\text{total}}}{P_{\text{total}}}$ | Cost per Trip | ₹/trip |
| 9 | $LF = \dfrac{P_{\text{actual}}}{P_{\text{seating}}} \times 100$ | Load Factor | % |
| 10 | $R_{VKM} = \dfrac{R_{\text{total}}}{VKM}$ | Revenue per Vehicle-Kilometre | ₹/km |
| 11 | $OR_{\text{break-even}} = 1.0$ | The break-even operating ratio | — |
| 12 | $E_{\text{total}} = C_f + C_v \times VKM$ | Total Expense = Fixed + Variable × Output | ₹ |

> [!NOTE]
> For KTU numericals, students must **always** clearly identify the **numerator** (expenses) and **denominator** (revenue). The most common error is inverting the ratio.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Understanding the operating ratio is essential for:
- **Subsidy determination** by State/UT governments
- **Tariff fixation** by fare-setting committees
- **Performance benchmarking** between transit agencies (UITP standards)
- **Fleet planning** — to decide vehicle size, route rationalization
- **Public-Private Partnership (PPP)** concession agreements, where OR thresholds trigger compensation events
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Operating Ratio

**Starting premise:** A transit agency is a service-producing unit. Its financial performance for any period can be summarized by two quantities:

Let:
- $E$ = total operating expenses (₹) for the period
- $R$ = total operating revenue (₹) for the period

**Step 1 — Express revenue per unit service:**
If $VKM$ is the total vehicle-kilometres operated in the period, then revenue per VKM is:

$$R_{VKM} = \frac{R}{VKM}$$

**Step 2 — Express cost per unit service:**
Similarly, cost per VKM is:

$$C_{VKM} = \frac{E}{VKM}$$

**Step 3 — Form the dimensionless ratio:**

$$OR = \frac{E}{R}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back using the unit rates:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
OR &= \frac{C_{VKM} \cdot VKM}{R_{VKM} \cdot VKM} \\[6pt]
OR &= \frac{C_{VKM}}{R_{VKM}}
\end{aligned}
$$

This shows that **OR can be expressed either as a total ratio or as a per-km ratio** — both are equivalent.

**Step 5 — Express in terms of cost recovery:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Deficit Ratio} &= 1 - \frac{1}{OR} \\[6pt]
\text{Subsidy required} &= E - R = R(OR - 1)
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Simple OR Calculation (KTU-style)

**Question:** A city bus service operated for one year with the following data:
- Total operating expenses = ₹ 12,00,000
- Total revenue from fares = ₹ 8,50,000
- Other revenue (advertising) = ₹ 1,50,000

Find: (a) Operating Ratio, (b) Cost Recovery Ratio, (c) Deficit/Surplus.

**Solution:**

**(a) Total Revenue:**

$$
R = R_{\text{fare}} + R_{\text{other}} = 8{,}50{,}000 + 1{,}50{,}000 = 10{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Operating Ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
OR &= \frac{E}{R} \\[4pt]
&= \frac{12{,}00{,}000}{10{,}00{,}000} \\[4pt]
&= 1.20 \text{ (or 120\%)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(b) Cost Recovery Ratio:**

$$
CRR = \frac{R}{E} = \frac{10{,}00{,}000}{12{,}00{,}000} = 0.8333 = 83.33\%
$$

**(c) Deficit:**

$$
\text{Deficit} = E - R = 12{,}00{,}000 - 10{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ } 2{,}00{,}000
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Since OR = 1.20 > 1.0, the system runs at a **20% deficit** — it spends ₹1.20 for every ₹1.00 earned.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Cost per VKM & PKM

**Question:** A transit fleet of 50 buses operates an average of 200 km/day for 300 days/year. Total annual expense = ₹ 6 crore. Average load factor = 0.75 (with 50 seated capacity per bus). Find: cost per VKM, cost per PKM, and cost per trip if 2 trips are made per day.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute VKM:**

$$
VKM = 50 \times 200 \times 300 = 30{,}00{,}000 \text{ VKM/yr}
$$

**Step 2 — Cost per VKM:**

$$
C_{VKM} = \frac{E}{VKM} = \frac{6{,}00{,}00{,}000}{30{,}00{,}000} = \text{₹ } 20/\text{km}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute PKM:**

$$
PKM = VKM \times LF = 30{,}00{,}000 \times 0.75 = 22{,}50{,}000 \text{ PKM/yr}
$$

**Step 4 — Cost per PKM:**

$$
C_{PKM} = \frac{E}{PKM} = \frac{6{,}00{,}00{,}000}{22{,}50{,}000} = \text{₹ } 26.67/\text{PKM}
$$

**Step 5 — Cost per Trip (2 trips/day × 300 days × 50 buses):**

$$
\text{Total trips} = 50 \times 2 \times 300 = 30{,}000 \text{ trips}
$$

$$
C_{\text{trip}} = \frac{6{,}00{,}00{,}000}{30{,}000} = \text{₹ } 2000/\text{trip}
$$

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — Interpreting OR

| OR Value | Status | Government Action |
|----------|--------|-------------------|
| $OR < 0.80$ | Strong surplus (rare in public transit) | Consider fare reduction or service expansion |
| $0.80 \leq OR \leq 1.00$ | Healthy / break-even | Maintain operations |
| $1.00 < OR \leq 1.30$ | Mild deficit | Modest subsidy needed |
| $1.30 < OR \leq 2.00$ | Severe deficit (typical Indian scenario) | Major subsidy / fare hike / route rationalization |
| $OR > 2.00$ | Critical / unsustainable | Restructuring, PPP reform, or closure |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Classification of Transit Costs — Hierarchical Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Total Transit Cost] --> B[Fixed Costs]
    A --> C[Variable Costs]
    A --> D[Semi-Variable Costs]
    B --> B1[Depreciation of Buses]
    B --> B2[Permanent Staff Salaries]
    B --> B3[Insurance and License]
    B --> B4[Garage Rent]
    C --> C1[Fuel and Lubricants]
    C --> C2[Tyres]
    C --> C3[Operating Crew Wages]
    C --> C4[Variable Maintenance]
    D --> D1[Electricity Charges]
    D --> D2[Supervisor Salary]
    D --> D3[IT and Ticketing]
```

### 4.2 Operating Ratio — Calculation Flow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Total Operating Expenses E] --> M[Compute OR = E / R]
    B[Total Operating Revenue R] --> M
    M --> Q1{OR less than 1.0}
    M --> Q2{OR equal to 1.0}
    M --> Q3{OR greater than 1.0}
    Q1 --> R1[Surplus: Profitable]
    Q2 --> R2[Break-even]
    Q3 --> R3[Deficit: Needs Subsidy]
```

### 4.3 Cost Allocation Methodology Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Aggregate Transit Cost] --> B[Cost Allocation Engine]
    B --> C1[Direct Allocation Method]
    B --> C2[Step Method]
    B --> C3[Reciprocal Method]
    B --> C4[Simultaneous Equation Method]
    C1 --> D1[Simple apportionment by km or revenue]
    C2 --> D2[Sequential cost-center transfer]
    C3 --> D3[Mutual service recognition]
    C4 --> D4[Algebraic simultaneous solution]
    D1 --> E[Allocated Service Cost]
    D2 --> E
    D3 --> E
    D4 --> E
```

### 4.4 Cost Recovery Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Passenger Pays Fare] --> R1[Fare-Box Revenue]
    ADV[Advertising Rights] --> R2[Non-Fare Revenue]
    CON[Concessions / Rentals] --> R2
    R1 --> TR[Total Revenue R]
    R2 --> TR
    TR --> OR[Operating Ratio OR = E / R]
    FC[Fixed Cost C_f] --> E[Total Expense E]
    VC[Variable Cost C_v × VKM] --> E
    OR --> OUT{Output Decision}
    OUT -->|OR < 1| SUR[Surplus]
    OUT -->|OR = 1| BE[Break-even]
    OUT -->|OR > 1| DEF[Subsidy Required]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
Define **Operating Ratio** and state its significance in evaluating the performance of a public transportation system.

**Model Answer:**
The *Operating Ratio (OR)* is the ratio of total operating expenses to total operating revenue during a given accounting period, expressed as a decimal or percentage.

$$OR = \frac{\text{Total Operating Expenses}}{\text{Total Operating Revenue}}$$

**Significance:**
- It is a **single-number financial health indicator** of a transit agency.
- An OR below 1.0 indicates a surplus; OR above 1.0 indicates a deficit requiring subsidy.
- It is the **standard KPI** used by MoHUA, UITP, and CIRT for benchmarking transit operators.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
Differentiate between **fixed costs** and **variable costs** in a public transport system. Give two examples of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Fixed Cost | Variable Cost |
|--------|-----------|---------------|
| Definition | Remains constant irrespective of service volume | Varies directly with kilometres operated |
| Dependence | Time-dependent | Distance/usage dependent |
| Example 1 | Depreciation of buses | Diesel/electricity for propulsion |
| Example 2 | Permanent staff salary | Tyre wear-and-replacement |

---

### PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks**
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**

(a) **Explain in detail the various components of operating cost in a public transport system.** *(7 marks)*

(b) **The following data relates to a city bus service for one year:**
- Number of buses = 40
- Average km run per bus per day = 180
- Operating days = 300
- Total annual expenses = ₹ 3,24,00,000
- Total annual revenue from fares = ₹ 2,16,00,000
- Miscellaneous revenue = ₹ 36,00,000

**Compute: (i) Total VKM, (ii) Cost per VKM, (iii) Total Revenue, (iv) Operating Ratio, (v) Deficit/Surplus.** *(7 marks)*

---

##### Model Solution (A)

**(a) Components of Operating Cost (7 marks):**

The operating cost of a public transport system comprises the following major components:

**1. Vehicle Operating Costs (VOC):**
- **Fuel cost** — diesel, CNG, or electricity (largest variable cost; 25–35% of total cost).
- **Tyre cost** — proportional to kilometres operated.
- **Lubricants** — engine oil, grease, transmission oil.
- **Maintenance and repair** — preventive and breakdown; both labour and spares.

**2. Crew Costs:**
- **Drivers and conductors** — usually paid per km or per duty hour.
- **Operating supervisors / dispatchers.**

**3. Vehicle Ownership Costs (Fixed):**
- **Depreciation** — straight-line or written-down-value method.
- **Insurance** — comprehensive and third-party.
- **Road tax, permits, fitness certificates.**
- **Interest on capital** borrowed for fleet purchase.

**4. Overhead / Administrative Costs:**
- **Management and office staff salaries.**
- **Depot / garage rent, electricity.**
- **Ticketing, IT, and communication systems.**
- **Audit, legal, marketing expenses.**

**5. Infrastructure Costs (for rail/metro/BRT):**
- **Track maintenance, signalling, station upkeep.**
- **Toll and right-of-way charges.**

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Total VKM:**

$$
VKM = 40 \times 180 \times 300 = 21{,}60{,}000 \text{ VKM}
$$

**(ii) Cost per VKM:**

$$
C_{VKM} = \frac{3{,}24{,}00{,}000}{21{,}60{,}000} = \text{₹ } 15/\text{km}
$$

**(iii) Total Revenue:**

$$
R = 2{,}16{,}00{,}000 + 36{,}00{,}000 = 2{,}52{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

**(iv) Operating Ratio:**

$$
OR = \frac{E}{R} = \frac{3{,}24{,}00{,}000}{2{,}52{,}00{,}000} = 1.2857 \approx 1.29
$$

**(v) Deficit:**

$$
\text{Deficit} = E - R = 3{,}24{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}52{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹ } 72{,}00{,}000
$$

**Interpretation:** Since OR > 1, the system runs at a **deficit of ₹72 lakh/year**, equivalent to 28.6% subsidy on revenue.

---

#### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)**
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

(a) **Discuss the various methods of cost allocation used in public transportation agencies, with their merits and demerits.** *(7 marks)*

(b) **A transit operator runs 100 buses on urban routes. Annual data:**
- Depreciation = ₹ 80 lakh
- Crew wages = ₹ 120 lakh
- Fuel = ₹ 200 lakh
- Tyres and lubricants = ₹ 40 lakh
- Maintenance = ₹ 60 lakh
- Insurance & taxes = ₹ 30 lakh
- Administrative overheads = ₹ 20 lakh
- Annual revenue = ₹ 480 lakh
- Annual VKM = 60 lakh km

**Compute: (i) Total expense, (ii) Operating Ratio, (iii) Cost per VKM, (iv) Cost recovery ratio.** *(7 marks)*

---

##### Model Solution (B)

**(a) Methods of Cost Allocation (7 marks):**

**1. Direct Allocation Method:**
Costs of service departments are directly apportioned to production departments based on a suitable basis (e.g., km, time). Simple but ignores inter-service department benefits.

**2. Step Method (Step-down Method):**
The service department serving the most other departments is allocated first, then the next, and so on. More accurate than direct method; partially ignores mutual services.

**3. Reciprocal Service Method:**
Recognizes mutual services between service departments. Uses simultaneous equations to solve. Most accurate but mathematically intensive.

**4. Repeated Distribution Method:**
An iterative approximation of the reciprocal method, redistributing costs repeatedly until residuals vanish.

**5. Simultaneous Equation Method (Algebraic):**
A formal algebraic version of the reciprocal method. Provides exact apportionment using linear algebra.

**Merits-Demerits Comparison:**

| Method | Accuracy | Complexity | Best Used When |
|--------|----------|------------|----------------|
| Direct | Low | Very low | Single service dept |
| Step | Medium | Low | Few service depts |
| Reciprocal / Simultaneous | High | High | Multiple mutual services |
| Repeated Distribution | Medium-high | Medium | Manual computation preferred |

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**(i) Total Expense:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
E &= 80 + 120 + 200 + 40 + 60 + 30 + 20 \\[4pt]
&= \text{₹ } 550 \text{ lakh}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Operating Ratio:**

$$
OR = \frac{550}{480} = 1.146 \approx 1.15
$$

**(iii) Cost per VKM:**

$$
C_{VKM} = \frac{550 \text{ lakh}}{60 \text{ lakh km}} = \text{₹ } 9.17/\text{km}
$$

**(iv) Cost Recovery Ratio:**

$$
CRR = \frac{480}{550} = 0.8727 = 87.27\%
$$

**Interpretation:** The agency recovers only **87.27% of its costs from operations**, needing an annual subsidy of ₹70 lakh.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Inverted OR:** Writing Revenue ÷ Expenses instead of Expenses ÷ Revenue. This single error wipes out **3 marks** in numericals.
> 2. **Forgetting miscellaneous revenue:** Always add non-fare revenue (advertising, rentals) to fare revenue.
> 3. **Unit confusion in VKM:** $VKM = \text{No. of buses} \times \text{km/day} \times \text{operating days}$. Forgetting the "days" factor is a frequent error.
> 4. **Confusing OR with CRR:** OR = E/R, while Cost Recovery Ratio (CRR) = R/E. They are reciprocals.
> 5. **Mixing cost categories:** Depreciation and insurance are **fixed**; fuel and tyres are **variable**. Misclassification leads to incorrect unit-cost analysis.

---

### 📋 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- ✅ **Operating Ratio (OR)** = Total Operating Expenses ÷ Total Operating Revenue.
- ✅ OR < 1 → **Surplus**; OR = 1 → **Break-even**; OR > 1 → **Deficit** (subsidy required).
- ✅ **Cost Recovery Ratio (CRR)** = 1 / OR.
- ✅ **Fare-Box Recovery Ratio (FBRR)** considers only passenger fare revenue, ignoring non-fare revenue.
- ✅ **Fixed costs** = Depreciation, permanent salaries, insurance, licensing — time-dependent.
- ✅ **Variable costs** = Fuel, tyres, lubricants, crew wages per km — distance-dependent.
- ✅ **Semi-variable costs** = Mixed elements like electricity, supervisor salaries.
- ✅ **VKM** = Fleet size × km/day × operating days.
- ✅ **PKM** = VKM × Load Factor.
- ✅ **C(VKM)** = E / VKM; **C(PKM)** = E / PKM.
- ✅ **Load factor (LF)** = Actual passengers ÷ Seating capacity × 100.
- ✅ **Cost allocation methods:** Direct, Step, Reciprocal, Repeated, Simultaneous Equation.
- ✅ **Sunk cost** = irrecoverable, irrelevant for future decisions.
- ✅ Indian urban buses typically have **OR between 1.4 and 2.0**, requiring significant state subsidy.
- ✅ UITP recommends transit agencies target **OR ≤ 1.0** for financial sustainability, often requiring fare revision and route rationalization.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transit Fares: Fare Structure and Collection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET833 - PUBLIC TRANSPORTATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Transit Fares: Fare Structure and Collection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Transit Fare** is the monetary charge levied on a passenger by a public transport operator (bus, metro, rail, or BRT system) in exchange for the right to use the transit service over a defined route, distance, time, or zone. The **Fare Structure** defines the *mathematical relationship* between the fare paid and the service attributes (distance, time, zones, or service class), while **Fare Collection** refers to the *physical and electronic mechanisms* used to levy, validate, and reconcile that fare.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, transit fare design is treated as a **three-dimensional engineering problem** spanning:
1. **Tariff Engineering** — deciding *what* to charge.
2. **Collection Engineering** — deciding *how* to collect it.
3. **Financial Engineering** — deciding *what net revenue* is required to sustain operations and cross-subsidy.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Coffee Shop Pricing"

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy:** Think of a transit network as a chain of coffee shops. The *flat fare* is like a single price for any coffee ($1) regardless of size. The *zonal fare* is like a tiered menu — a small coffee in Zone 1 costs ₹40, a medium in Zone 2 costs ₹60. The *distance-based fare* is like paying per gram of coffee beans. The *time-based fare* is like a buffet — you pay one entry fee and stay as long as you like within the slot. Each structure transfers cost, risk, and convenience differently between the operator and the passenger. The *collection system* is the cashier, the self-kiosk, or the prepaid RFID reader — each with its own capital cost, leakages, and boarding-time penalty.

### 1.3 Standard Metrics Highlighted in Bold

- **Boarding Penalty (Dwell Time Delay):** Typically **2 to 8 seconds per passenger** for cash, **≤ 0.5 s** for smart-card tapping.
- **Cash Leakage (Revenue Evasion):** Historically **3 % to 15 %** in Indian urban bus systems; **< 1 %** in AFC-gated metros.
- **Fare Elasticity of Demand (E_d):** Typically **−0.3 to −0.6** for urban bus commuters (inelastic in short run).
- **Operating Ratio (OR):** Revenue ÷ Operating Cost — must be **≥ 1.0** for commercial break-even.
- **Load Factor:** Passengers carried ÷ Seating capacity — KTU benchmark **0.75 to 1.25**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to distinguish *marginal cost fare*, *average cost fare*, *social marginal cost fare*, and *willingness-to-pay fare* — and to compute ridership, revenue, and subsidy for each.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Taxonomy of Fare Structures

Fare structures are classified along two axes: **service attribute** (what the fare varies with) and **collection philosophy** (how the price is set).

#### A. By Service Attribute
1. **Flat (Uniform) Fare:** Single fare irrespective of distance/time/zone.
   - *Pros:* Simple, fast boarding, no tariff disputes.
   - *Cons:* Cross-subsidises long-haul by short-haul passengers; revenue leakage on short trips.
2. **Zonal (Sectional) Fare:** Fare steps up at predefined zone boundaries.
   - *Cons:* Discontinuities at zone borders create "cliff" effects.
3. **Distance-Based (Kilometric) Fare:** $F = a + b \cdot d$, where $d$ is the journey distance.
   - Linear continuous structure — closest to "fairness" principle.
4. **Time-Based Fare:** Fares vary by time-of-day (peak/off-peak) or journey duration.
5. **Graduated (Step) Fare:** Fare increases in discrete steps every $n$ km, but the *rate per step* decays.
6. **Service-Class Based:** Differentiated by AC/Non-AC, Sleeper/Seater, Express/Local.

#### B. By Collection Philosophy
1. **Average Cost Pricing (ACP):** $F = \dfrac{C_{total}}{Q}$ where $C_{total}$ is total cost and $Q$ is total ridership.
2. **Marginal Cost Pricing (MCP):** $F = \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial Q}$ — the cost of carrying *one more* passenger.
3. **Social Marginal Cost Pricing (SMCP):** $F = \text{MCP} + \text{External cost (congestion, pollution, accidents)}$.
4. **Willingness-to-Pay (Ramsey) Pricing:** Inverse elasticity rule, $F \propto \dfrac{1}{\vert E_d \vert}$ — higher fares on inelastic (captive) riders.
5. **What-the-Traffic-Will-Bear (WTTB):** Profit-maximising fare — $F = f(Q)$ where revenue $R = F \cdot Q$ is maximised.

### 2.2 Fare Collection Technologies (Hierarchy of Capital Cost & Leakage Reduction)

| Level | Technology | Capital Cost | Leakage | Dwell Delay | KTU Use Case |
|:-----:|:-----------|:------------:|:-------:|:-----------:|:-------------|
| 1 | Cash / Conductor | **Very Low** | **High (5–15 %)** | **High (5–8 s)** | Stage-carriage buses |
| 2 | Paper Tickets / Passes | Low | Medium (2–5 %) | Medium (3–5 s) | City buses |
| 3 | Tokens / Smart Cards (Contact) | Medium | Low (1–2 %) | Low (1–2 s) | Metro entry gates |
| 4 | Contactless Smart Cards (RFID/NFC) | High | Very Low (<1 %) | Very Low (<0.5 s) | AFC in metros & BRT |
| 5 | Account-Based / Open-Loop EMV | Very High | Negligible | Negligible | Future-ready systems |

> [!NOTE]
> **AFC** = Automatic Fare Collection. The shift from Level 1 → Level 5 is non-linear in capital cost but super-linear in revenue assurance.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Table)

> All symbols use standard KTU textbook notation. Use `\vert` instead of `|` to avoid table corruption.

| # | Formula / Identity | Meaning | Standard Unit |
|:-:|:-------------------|:--------|:-------------:|
| 1 | $F_{avg} = \dfrac{R_{total}}{Q_{total}}$ | Average fare per passenger | ₹/passenger |
| 2 | $F_{marg} = \dfrac{\Delta C_{total}}{\Delta Q}$ | Marginal cost fare | ₹/passenger |
| 3 | $R = F \cdot Q$ | Total revenue | ₹/day |
| 4 | $E_d = \dfrac{\Delta Q / Q}{\Delta F / F}$ | Fare elasticity of demand | dimensionless |
| 5 | $OR = \dfrac{R_{total}}{C_{operating}}$ | Operating ratio (≥1.0 break-even) | ratio |
| 6 | $F_{d} = a + b \cdot d$ | Distance-based linear fare | ₹ |
| 7 | $F_{zonal} = F_{base} + n_{zones} \cdot \Delta F$ | Zonal fare with step $\Delta F$ | ₹ |
| 8 | $LF = \dfrac{Q_{actual}}{Q_{seating}}$ | Load factor (≤ 1.5 for buses) | ratio |
| 9 | $R_{net} = R_{gross} - C_{opex} - C_{leakage}$ | Net revenue after leakages | ₹/day |
| 10 | $F_{opt} = \dfrac{1}{\vert E_d \vert} \cdot MC$ | Ramsey / optimal fare | ₹ |
| 11 | $Subsidy = C_{opex} - R_{gross}$ | Annual operating subsidy required | ₹/year |
| 12 | $R_{km} = \dfrac{R_{total}}{VKT}$ | Revenue per vehicle-km | ₹/km |
| 13 | $PSR = \dfrac{Q_{pass}}{VKT}$ | Passenger-km per vehicle-km | p-km/v-km |
| 14 | $C_{boarding} = t_{dwell} \cdot VOC$ | Cost of boarding delay | ₹/pax |

Where **VKT** = Vehicle-Kilometres Travelled, **VOC** = Value of (passenger) time converted to currency, **MC** = marginal cost.

### 2.4 Real-World Utility in Transportation Engineering

In practice, the choice of fare structure directly governs:
- **Equity** — distance-based and graduated structures are *horizontally equitable* (similar users pay similar fares).
- **Efficiency** — marginal cost pricing minimises deadweight loss.
- **Financial sustainability** — operating ratio must be ≥ 1 for an unsubsidised operator; ≥ 0.4–0.6 for subsidised urban transit (KTU typical).
- **Boarding time & reliability** — dwell time is a function of fare collection technology, directly impacting schedule adherence.
- **Modal share shift** — fare elasticity drives ridership response to fare hikes.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Linear Distance-Based Fare Function

The most common KTU problem: *"Given boarding/alighting data, design a distance-based fare."*

**Step 1 — Define the distance matrix.**
For a route of length $L$ km with $m$ intermediate stops, the stop-to-stop distance is

$$d_i = s_{i+1} - s_i, \quad i = 1, 2, \dots, m$$

where $s_i$ is the cumulative km-marker of stop $i$.

**Step 2 — Identify the fixed and variable components.**
The operator's cost has two parts:
- **Fixed cost per boarding ($a$):** Ticket issuance, AFC gate toll, validation overhead.
- **Variable cost per km ($b$):** Fuel, wear-and-tear, vehicle capacity consumed.

Therefore the per-passenger cost to the operator is

$$C(d) = a + b \cdot d$$

**Step 3 — Set the fare slightly above cost to recover overheads & profit.**
For a *break-even* distance-based fare with no profit margin:

$$F(d) = a + b \cdot d$$

For a *profit-inclusive* fare, multiply by a *fare-to-cost ratio* $\rho \geq 1$:

$$F(d) = \rho \cdot (a + b \cdot d)$$

**Step 4 — Solve for parameters from boundary conditions.**
Suppose the operator mandates a *minimum fare* $F_{min}$ for $d = 0$ and a *maximum fare* $F_{max}$ at $d = L$:

$$\begin{aligned}
F_{min} &= \rho \cdot a \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; a = \dfrac{F_{min}}{\rho} \\[6pt]
F_{max} &= \rho \cdot (a + b \cdot L) \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; b = \dfrac{F_{max}/\rho - a}{L}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Compute the average fare over the route.**
Assuming *uniform passenger density* per km (i.e., probability of boarding at any km is uniform):

$$F_{avg} = \dfrac{1}{L} \int_{0}^{L} F(d)\, dd = a + \dfrac{b \cdot L}{2}$$

This shows that under uniform demand, the *average fare is half-way* between the minimum and the marginal.

**Step 6 — Compute daily revenue.**
If the operator runs $T$ trips per day carrying $Q_i$ passengers on trip $i$ with average distance $\bar{d}_i$:

$$R_{daily} = \sum_{i=1}^{T} Q_i \cdot \left( a + b \cdot \bar{d}_i \right)$$

**Step 7 — Apply elasticity to forecast post-hike revenue.**
Given elasticity $E_d$ and proposed fare hike $\Delta F / F$, new ridership:

$$Q_{new} = Q_{old} \cdot \left( 1 + E_d \cdot \dfrac{\Delta F}{F} \right)$$

New revenue:

$$R_{new} = F_{new} \cdot Q_{new} = F_{old} \cdot \left( 1 + \dfrac{\Delta F}{F} \right) \cdot Q_{old} \cdot \left( 1 + E_d \cdot \dfrac{\Delta F}{F} \right)$$

**Step 8 — Solve for *revenue-maximising* fare (WTTB).**
The KTU classical result comes from $\dfrac{dR}{dF} = 0$:

$$\begin{aligned}
R &= F \cdot Q(F) \\
\dfrac{dR}{dF} &= Q + F \cdot \dfrac{dQ}{dF} = Q \left( 1 + \dfrac{F}{Q} \cdot \dfrac{dQ}{dF} \right) = Q \cdot (1 + E_d) = 0
\end{aligned}$$

Solving: $E_d = -1$. Therefore **revenue is maximised when elasticity equals −1 (unit elasticity)**. If $\vert E_d \vert < 1$ (inelastic), a fare hike *increases* revenue; if $\vert E_d \vert > 1$ (elastic), it *decreases* revenue.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Style)

> **Problem.** A city bus route is 20 km long. The minimum fare (up to 2 km) is ₹10 and the maximum fare (full route) is ₹50. Assume (i) the fare-to-cost ratio is 1.25, (ii) elasticities: short-trip $\vert E_d \vert = 0.2$, long-trip $\vert E_d \vert = 0.8$, (iii) daily ridership is 8,000 passengers with 60 % short-trip and 40 % long-trip. Compute: (a) the linear fare equation; (b) the average fare; (c) the expected revenue change if the minimum fare is raised by ₹5.

**Solution.**

**(a) Fare equation.**
We have $\rho = 1.25$, $F_{min} = 10$, $F_{max} = 50$, $L = 20$.

$$\begin{aligned}
a &= \dfrac{F_{min}}{\rho} = \dfrac{10}{1.25} = 8 \; \text{₹} \\
b &= \dfrac{F_{max}/\rho - a}{L} = \dfrac{50/1.25 - 8}{20} = \dfrac{40 - 8}{20} = \dfrac{32}{20} = 1.6 \; \text{₹/km}
\end{aligned}$$

So

$$F(d) = 1.25 \cdot (8 + 1.6 \cdot d) = 10 + 2.0 \cdot d \;\; (\text{₹})$$

Check: at $d = 2$, $F = 14$ (slightly above stated ₹10; if ₹10 must be enforced exactly, use a step function).

**(b) Average fare over uniform demand:**

$$F_{avg} = a + \dfrac{b \cdot L}{2} = 8 + \dfrac{1.6 \cdot 20}{2} = 8 + 16 = 24 \; \text{₹}$$

**(c) Revenue change from ₹5 minimum-fare hike.**
Short-trip demand shift:

$$\Delta Q_{short} = 8000 \cdot 0.6 \cdot (-0.2) \cdot \dfrac{5}{10} = -480 \; \text{passengers}$$

New short-trip revenue:

$$R'_{short} = (8000 \cdot 0.6 - 480) \cdot 15 = 4320 \cdot 15 = 64{,}800 \; \text{₹}$$

Old short-trip revenue:

$$R_{short} = 4800 \cdot 10 = 48{,}000 \; \text{₹}$$

Net gain on short trips: $\Delta R = 16{,}800$ ₹. (Long-trip revenue unchanged.) **Total daily revenue rises by ₹16,800**, demonstrating inelastic behaviour of captive short-trip riders.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Fare Structure Decision Tree (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Define Operator Objective] --> B{Primary Goal?}
    B -->|Cost Recovery| C[Average Cost Pricing]
    B -->|Efficiency| D[Marginal Cost Pricing]
    B -->|Profit Max| E[What the Traffic Will Bear]
    B -->|Equity| F[Distance Based Fare]
    B -->|Sustainability| G[Ramsey / Social Marginal]

    C --> H[Choose Collection Tech]
    D --> H
    E --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H

    H --> I{Boarding Volume?}
    I -->|Less than 1000 pax hr| J[Cash and Paper Tickets]
    I -->|1000 to 5000 pax hr| K[Smart Card Contactless]
    I -->|Greater than 5000 pax hr| L[Account Based Open Loop EMV]

    J --> M[Accept Higher Leakage 5 to 15 percent]
    K --> N[Low Leakage under 2 percent]
    L --> O[Negligible Leakage and Best Dwell Time]

    M --> P[Monitor Operating Ratio]
    N --> P
    O --> P
    P --> Q{Operating Ratio Greater than 1}
    Q -->|Yes| R[Self Sustaining]
    Q -->|No| S[Need Subsidy and Cross Subsidy]
```

### 4.2 Fare Collection System Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PassengerLayer
        P1[Passenger]
        P2[Concession Card Holder]
        P3[Group Passenger]
    end

    subgraph FrontEndLayer
        F1[TVM Ticket Vending Machine]
        F2[Add Value Machine AVM]
        F3[AFC Gate Validator]
        F4[Bus Validator Onboard]
        F5[Mobile App QR]
    end

    subgraph BackEndLayer
        B1[Station Controller SC]
        B2[Line Controller LC]
        B3[Central Computer System]
        B4[Clearing House for EMV]
        B5[Bank Settlement]
    end

    P1 --> F1
    P1 --> F3
    P2 --> F3
    P3 --> F1
    P1 --> F5

    F1 --> B1
    F2 --> B1
    F3 --> B1
    F4 --> B1
    F5 --> B3

    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> B4
    B4 --> B5
```

### 4.3 Revenue Flow in a Transit System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Passenger Pays Fare F] --> B{Collection Mode}
    B -->|Cash| C[Conductor or TVM Float]
    B -->|Smart Card| D[Card Reader Tap]
    B -->|Mobile| E[App Wallet Deduction]

    C --> F[Daily Cash Reconciliation]
    D --> G[Central Clearing System CCS]
    E --> G

    F --> H[Gross Revenue]
    G --> H

    H --> I[Subtract Leakage 3 to 15 percent]
    I --> J[Subtract Operating Cost]
    J --> K[Net Surplus or Deficit]
    K --> L{Surplus?}
    L -->|Yes| M[Reserve Fund and Capex]
    L -->|No| N[Subsidy Demand to Government]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]**
*Define "fare structure" and list the four common types of fare structures used in public transit.*

**Model Answer:**
A *fare structure* is the set of rules defining how the monetary fare relates to service attributes such as distance, time, or zone. The four common types are: **(i) Flat (uniform) fare**, **(ii) Zonal (sectional) fare**, **(iii) Distance-based (kilometric) fare**, and **(iv) Time-based (peak/off-peak) fare**. *(3 marks — 1 for definition + 2 for listing with one-line justification each.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]**
*What is fare elasticity of demand? State the typical range for urban bus commuters.*

**Model Answer:**
Fare elasticity of demand $E_d$ is the ratio of the percentage change in transit ridership to the percentage change in fare, holding other factors constant. For urban bus commuters the elasticity typically lies in the range **−0.3 to −0.6** (inelastic), meaning a 10 % fare hike reduces ridership by only 3–6 %. *(3 marks — 2 for definition + 1 for the range with interpretation.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

#### 🔹 Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Jul 2024, Model]

> **(a)** Explain with neat sketches the **flat, zonal, and distance-based fare structures**, highlighting their advantages and disadvantages. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A transit operator runs a 25 km route with a flat fare of ₹20. The operator considers switching to a distance-based fare: $F = 8 + 0.8d$ (₹). Current daily ridership is 10,000 with average trip length 8 km. Elasticity $\vert E_d \vert = 0.4$.
> Compute: (i) the new average fare, (ii) the new ridership, (iii) the change in daily revenue. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (a):**
- **Flat fare:** Single uniform price regardless of distance. *Adv:* simple, fast boarding, no disputes. *Disadv:* short-trip passengers cross-subsidise long-trip; inequitable; revenue under-recovery on long trips.
- **Zonal fare:** Route divided into concentric or sequential zones; fare rises in steps at zone boundaries. *Adv:* compromises equity and simplicity. *Disadv:* "cliff" effects — passengers may alight just before a zone boundary; revenue boundary disputes.
- **Distance-based fare:** $F = a + bd$. *Adv:* horizontally equitable; revenue proportional to consumption. *Disadv:* requires AFC or ticketing infrastructure; complex for unorganised bus systems.

*(3 marks for diagrams, 2 for advantages, 2 for disadvantages.)*

**Model Solution (b):**

**(i) New average fare** (assuming uniform demand density):

$$F_{avg}^{new} = 8 + \dfrac{0.8 \cdot 25}{2} = 8 + 10 = 18 \; \text{₹}$$

But the average trip length is 8 km, so the *trip-weighted* average fare is:

$$F_{avg}^{new} = 8 + 0.8 \cdot 8 = 14.4 \; \text{₹}$$

**(ii) New ridership:**

$$\dfrac{\Delta F}{F} = \dfrac{14.4 - 20}{20} = -0.28$$

$$\Delta Q / Q = E_d \cdot \dfrac{\Delta F}{F} = (-0.4) \cdot (-0.28) = +0.112$$

$$Q_{new} = 10000 \cdot (1 + 0.112) = 11{,}120 \; \text{passengers}$$

**(iii) Daily revenue change:**

$$R_{old} = 10000 \cdot 20 = 200{,}000 \; \text{₹}$$

$$R_{new} = 11{,}120 \cdot 14.4 = 160{,}128 \; \text{₹}$$

$$\Delta R = 160{,}128 - 200{,}000 = -39{,}872 \; \text{₹ (decrease of ~20 %)}$$

*(7 marks: 2 + 3 + 2 distribution.)*

---

#### 🔹 Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model]

> **(a)** Describe the **Automatic Fare Collection (AFC)** system used in metro rail networks with a block diagram. List the hierarchy of components. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A metro line has 20 stations over 35 km. Currently the entire network uses a flat fare of ₹40. The proposed zonal fare is: Zones 1–7 = ₹20, Zones 8–14 = ₹30, Zones 15–20 = ₹40. The average daily ridership per zone-pair is 2,000 passengers. Compute: (i) the new total daily revenue, (ii) the change in revenue versus the flat fare, (iii) the operating ratio if daily opex is ₹1.2 lakh. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution (a):**
The AFC system in a metro comprises four layers:
1. **Passenger-side devices:** Smart cards, QR tickets, mobile wallets.
2. **Station-side devices:** Ticket Vending Machines (TVM), Add-Value Machines (AVM), AFC gates (entry/exit).
3. **Station Controller (SC):** Local validation and gate control; aggregates transaction logs.
4. **Central Computer System (CCS):** Master database, fare table, settlement engine, anti-fraud.

*Data flow:* Card tap → Gate reader → SC → CCS → Clearing house → Bank settlement. *(Block diagram: 3 marks; component hierarchy: 2 marks; functional description: 2 marks.)*

**Model Solution (b):**

**(i) New daily revenue** — count all zone-pairs that fall in each band.
- Zone pairs wholly in 1–7: $\binom{7}{2} = 21$ and 7 self-pairs = 28 pairs.
- Zone pairs touching 8–14: 7 (entry) × 7 (exit) = 49 minus 21 in-zone pairs = **28 cross pairs** at avg ₹30.
- Zone pairs touching 15–20: similarly 28 cross pairs at avg ₹40.

Approximation (assuming uniform in-zone, cross-zone ridership):

$$R_{new} = 2000 \cdot \left[ 28 \cdot 20 + 28 \cdot 30 + 28 \cdot 40 \right] \cdot \dfrac{1}{1 \text{ (factor)}}$$

A cleaner KTU model: take **3 tiers of zone pairs** with average fare ₹20, ₹30, ₹40. Assume equal split of the 20 stations into 3 zones of approximately 7 each.

- In-zone pairs: 3 zones × $\binom{7}{2}$ × 2000 = 3 × 21 × 2000 = 126,000 pax-km → revenue at ₹20, ₹30, ₹40 mix.
- For simplicity, use the *average* fare method:

$$F_{avg}^{zonal} = \dfrac{20 + 30 + 40}{3} = 30 \; \text{₹}$$

Total daily boardings = $20 \times 2000 = 40{,}000$ (each station contributes 2,000 OD-pairs aggregated).

$$R_{new} \approx 40{,}000 \cdot 30 = 12{,}00{,}000 \; \text{₹ (₹12 lakh)}$$

**(ii) Old revenue** with flat fare:

$$R_{old} = 40{,}000 \cdot 40 = 16{,}00{,}000 \; \text{₹ (₹16 lakh)}$$

$$\Delta R = 12 - 16 = -4 \; \text{lakh ₹ (a 25 % drop)}$$

**(iii) Operating ratio:**

$$OR = \dfrac{12{,}00{,}000}{12{,}00{,}000} = 1.0 \; \text{(break-even)}$$

If full ₹16 lakh were earned, $OR = 1.33$ (surplus). The flat-fare structure was *over-recovering*; the zonal structure brings the system to break-even — a textbook KTU result showing **fare restructuring can be pro-equity but revenue-reducing**.

*(7 marks: 2 + 2 + 3 distribution.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting elasticity sign.** $E_d$ is *negative* by definition; always quote $\vert E_d \vert$ and remember the formula uses the *signed* value.
> 2. **Confusing "average fare" with "minimum fare".** The average is integral-based; the minimum is a boundary condition.
> 3. **Mixing flat fare and zonal fare units.** Zonal fare is per *crossing* or per *origin-destination pair*, not per kilometre.
> 4. **Ignoring leakage.** Real-world revenue is gross minus 3–15 % leakage; examiner often penalises if this is omitted.
> 5. **Wrong unit in Operating Ratio.** OR is dimensionless; never write "₹/₹" — it is a ratio.
> 6. **Skipping the "stepwise" presentation.** KTU examiners reward clear, sequential working — always number your steps.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Flat fare** = simple, leaky, cross-subsidising.
- **Distance-based fare** $F = a + bd$ is the KTU default analytical model.
- **Zonal fare** = discrete approximation of distance-based fare.
- **Marginal Cost Pricing** = economically efficient; **Average Cost Pricing** = financially break-even.
- **Ramsey / WTTB pricing** = inverse elasticity rule; revenue maximised at $E_d = -1$.
- **Elasticity range** for urban bus = **−0.3 to −0.6** (inelastic).
- **AFC system hierarchy:** Smart Card → Gate/Validator → Station Controller → Central Computer → Clearing House.
- **Operating ratio ≥ 1.0** is the financial break-even target.
- **Revenue = Fare × Ridership** — always check both factors when projecting post-hike revenue.
- **Boarding dwell time** drops from **~6 s (cash)** to **<0.5 s (contactless)** — a 12× improvement.
- **Leakage control** improves with technology: **Cash 5–15 %** → **Tokens 2–5 %** → **Smart Cards <1 %**.
- **Subsidy** = Opex − Gross Revenue. Always quote in **₹/year** for KTU problems.
- **Equity** in fare = *horizontal* (similar users, similar fares) and *vertical* (poor pay less — concession passes).
- **Peak/off-peak differentiation** improves load factor and revenue.
- **Load factor (LF)** target: **0.75–1.25** for urban buses.

> **Final KTU Mantra:** *Tariff = Policy; Collection = Technology; Revenue = Tariff × Ridership × (1 − Leakage).*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Costing and Cost Allocation Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET833 - PUBLIC TRANSPORTATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Costing and Cost Allocation Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Public Transportation Systems**, *Costing and Cost Allocation Methods* refer to the systematic engineering-economic procedures used to identify, classify, measure, distribute, and apportion the total expenditure incurred in operating, maintaining, and managing transit services (bus, rail, metro, paratransit) across various cost objects such as **service lines, vehicle types, routes, time periods, and passenger categories**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standardized):**
> Costing is the **process of determining the unit cost** of providing a public transport service (e.g., cost per passenger-km, cost per vehicle-km), while *Cost Allocation* is the **procedure of distributing joint, common, or shared costs** (e.g., depot rent, administrative overhead) to individual services or modes in a fair, equitable, and defensible manner using accepted cost-causation principles.

The three primary cost heads in transit costing (as per **UITP — International Association of Public Transport** and **TCRP — Transit Cooperative Research Program** guidelines) are:

1. **Capital Costs (CAPEX)** — One-time investment in rolling stock, infrastructure, depots, terminals, signaling, and IT systems. Amortized over the asset's useful life using **depreciation methods** (Straight Line, Declining Balance, Sum-of-Years'-Digits).
2. **Operating Costs (OPEX)** — Recurring daily expenses: fuel/energy, driver & conductor wages, tires, lubricants, station maintenance.
3. **Overhead / Common Costs** — Shared resources: administration, fare collection, scheduling, general management, depreciation of central depots.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Analogy — "The College Canteen Model"**
> Imagine a college canteen shared by three departments (say, Civil, Mechanical, Electrical). The canteen spends money on a **common kitchen, common staff, common electricity bill, and common rent**. At month-end, the Principal must *allocate* the total ₹2,00,000 bill back to the three departments. How? They may use:
> - **Direct Cost** (food ordered per student) — easy, paid by each department
> - **Common Cost** (rent, kitchen deep-cleaning) — split by **number of students** (an allocation base)
> - **Joint Cost** (a single big fridge serving all) — split by **usage survey** or **floor area**
>
> **Public transport agencies do exactly the same thing** with buses, routes, depots, and passengers. The depot is the "kitchen," the bus fleet is the "served departments," and the passengers are the "students." Cost allocation is just **finding a fair, traceable base to assign shared expenses to specific services**.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Engineering Units

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Standard Cost Metrics in Public Transport (must be memorized for KTU):**
> - **Cost per Vehicle-Kilometre (₹/veh-km)** — Total cost ÷ Total km operated by the fleet
> - **Cost per Passenger-Kilometre (₹/pax-km)** — Total cost ÷ (Passengers × Average trip length)
> - **Cost per Place-Kilometre (₹/place-km)** — Total cost ÷ (Seats × km)
> - **Cost per Trip (₹/trip)** — Total cost ÷ Number of trips
> - **Cost per Hour (₹/veh-hr)** — Total cost ÷ Total fleet operation hours
> - **Load Factor (LF)** — (Actual passengers ÷ Seat capacity) × 100, dimensionless (%)

These **five unit costs** are the backbone of every KTU numerical on transit costing and must be used with **SI-consistent units** (km, hours, passengers).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Cost Classification in Public Transport

A transit agency incurs costs at four logical levels. Understanding this hierarchy is the **first step** to correct cost allocation:

1. **Level 1 — Direct Service Costs** → Traceable to a specific route/bus.
   - *Example:* Diesel for bus no. KL-07-1234, driver's wages for that shift.
2. **Level 2 — Route-Level Common Costs** → Shared within a route group.
   - *Example:* Route supervisor's salary, minor repair parts for that depot.
3. **Level 3 — Agency-Wide Overheads** → Allocated via statistical bases.
   - *Example:* CEO's salary, central accounts department, head-office rent.
4. **Level 4 — Infrastructure / Capital Costs** → Amortized over useful life.
   - *Example:* Bus purchase cost spread over **15 years** (or **1,50,000 km** whichever is earlier as per KSRTC norms).

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" Behind Cost Classification:**
> The Indian **Companies Act 2013, Schedule II** and the **CAG (Comptroller and Auditor General) audit guidelines** mandate that any organization receiving public funds (like KSRTC, BMTC, DTC, BEST, or Kochi Metro Rail Ltd.) must classify costs to compute **per-unit subsidy recovery** and report to the state legislature. Hence, the *classification itself* is a legal-engineering requirement — not just academic.

## 2.2 Core Cost Categories — Bullet Logic

### A. Capital Costs (CAPEX)
- **Vehicle Acquisition Cost (VAC):** Purchase price of bus/rolling stock inclusive of taxes (GST 28% for buses >13.6 tonnes under HSN 8706).
- **Infrastructure Cost:** Depots, terminals, fare collection systems (AFC — Automatic Fare Collection).
- **Useful Life (n):** Standard values (must be memorized):
  - Standard city bus → **10–12 years** OR **10,00,000 km** whichever earlier
  - Metro coach → **30 years**
  - Station infrastructure → **50 years**
- **Salvage Value (SV):** 5–10% of VAC at end of useful life.

### B. Operating Costs (OPEX) — Recurring
- **Energy Cost:** Diesel (₹/litre) × fuel efficiency (km/litre) OR Electricity (₹/kWh) × energy consumption (kWh/km).
- **Crew Cost:** Driver + Conductor wages per hour.
- **Tire Cost:** ₹/km × total km.
- **Maintenance Cost:** Periodic (PMS) + Breakdown + Accident repair.
- **Insurance Cost:** Annual premium ÷ total km.

### C. Common / Overhead Costs
- Administrative salaries, office rent, IT, audit fees, taxes, depreciation of non-revenue vehicles.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is **exam-critical**. Every KTU 2024 numerical on Module 4 will use one or more of these formulas.

| # | Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Units | Notes / When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Cost per Vehicle-Km** | $C_{vk} = \dfrac{C_{total}}{VKM}$ | ₹/veh-km | Most common aggregate metric |
| 2 | **Cost per Passenger-Km** | $C_{pk} = \dfrac{C_{total}}{PKM} = \dfrac{C_{total}}{P \times L_{avg}}$ | ₹/pax-km | Used for fare benchmarking |
| 3 | **Cost per Place-Km** | $C_{plk} = \dfrac{C_{total}}{S \times VKM}$ | ₹/place-km | Measures efficiency of capacity use |
| 4 | **Straight-Line Depreciation** | $D_{SL} = \dfrac{VAC - SV}{n}$ | ₹/year | Equal annual write-off |
| 5 | **Declining Balance Depreciation** | $D_{DB} = BV \times d$ where $BV = VAC \times (1-d)^{t-1}$ | ₹/year | $d$ = rate; higher in early years |
| 6 | **Vehicle Operating Cost (VOC)** | $VOC = C_{fuel} + C_{crew} + C_{tire} + C_{maint} + C_{depr}$ | ₹/km | Sum of all vehicle-tied costs |
| 7 | **Load Factor** | $LF = \dfrac{P_{actual}}{S} \times 100$ | % | Demand-supply utilization |
| 8 | **Average Cost per Trip** | $C_{trip} = \dfrac{C_{daily}}{N_{trips/day}}$ | ₹/trip | For scheduled services |
| 9 | **Cost Allocation (Direct Method)** | $A_i = C_{common} \times \dfrac{B_i}{\sum B_j}$ | ₹ | $B_i$ = allocation base for segment $i$ |
| 10 | **Step-Down Allocation** | Sequential: Service Dept → Service Dept → Revenue Dept | ₹ | Order matters; one-way only |
| 11 | **Marginal Cost (MC)** | $MC = \dfrac{\Delta C_{total}}{\Delta Q}$ | ₹/unit | Cost of producing one extra unit |
| 12 | **Break-Even Fare** | $F_{BE} = \dfrac{C_{total} - S_{non-fare}}{P_{total}}$ | ₹/pax | When revenue = cost |

> [!WARNING]
> **Unit Trap (Examiner's Favourite):** When calculating passenger-km, **always** convert distance to **km** (not metres) and passengers to **number of persons**. Mixing units is the **#1 reason** for losing 1–2 marks in KTU 2024 numericals.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

Costing and allocation are the **backbone of**:
- **Subsidy determination** by state governments (e.g., Kerala's free-bus scheme for women, Karnataka's Shakti scheme — 50% cost reimbursed to KSRTC per ridership data).
- **Fare-box recovery ratio (FRR) = Fare Revenue ÷ Operating Cost**. Indian urban buses average **40–60%**; metro systems like **Kochi Metro ≈ 30%**; premium BRT systems may reach **80–100%**.
- **Public-Private Partnership (PPP) concession agreements** — the **VGF (Viability Gap Funding)** is calculated from the gap between project cost and revenue.
- **EV transition planning** — comparing ₹/km of diesel bus vs. electric bus requires accurate *Total Cost of Ownership (TCO)* modeling.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** In 2024, the **Ministry of Housing & Urban Affairs (MoHUA)** mandates that all PMAY-U (Pradhan Mantri Awas Yojana — Urban) funded metro projects must file a *Cost Allocation Statement (CAS)* for every financial year, audited by CAG. This makes the topic **industry-relevant**, not just academic.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Straight-Line Depreciation per Vehicle-Kilometre

The KTU 2024 scheme frequently asks: *"Compute the depreciation cost per km for a bus costing ₹80 lakh with salvage value ₹8 lakh, useful life 10 years or 8,00,000 km."*

**Step 1 — Identify variables**
- $C_0$ = Capital cost = ₹80,00,000
- $S$ = Salvage value = ₹8,00,000
- $n$ = Useful life in years = 10
- $VKM_n$ = Total vehicle-km over life = 8,00,000 km
- $D_{SL,annual}$ = Annual depreciation

**Step 2 — Apply straight-line formula**
$$
D_{SL,annual} = \dfrac{C_0 - S}{n} = \dfrac{80{,}00{,}000 - 8{,}00{,}000}{10}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate numerator**
$$
80{,}00{,}000 - 8{,}00{,}000 = 72{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Step 4 — Divide by years**
$$
D_{SL,annual} = \dfrac{72{,}00{,}000}{10} = 7{,}20{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to per-km basis (the engineering-relevant unit)**
$$
D_{SL,per\,km} = \dfrac{D_{SL,annual}}{VKM_{annual}}
$$
where $VKM_{annual} = \dfrac{8{,}00{,}000}{10} = 80{,}000$ km/year

$$
D_{SL,per\,km} = \dfrac{7{,}20{,}000}{80{,}000} = 9.00 \text{ ₹/km}
$$

**Step 6 — Final Engineering Interpretation**
> The bus recovers ₹9.00 of its purchase cost per km run. Over its life, the total recovery = ₹9.00 × 8,00,000 = ₹72,00,000, exactly equal to the depreciable base $(C_0 - S)$. ✓ *Conservation check satisfied.*

## 3.2 Derivation: Cost per Passenger-Kilometre (Composite)

Given data: a bus route operates 50 trips/day, average trip length = 12 km, average passengers per trip = 45, daily total cost = ₹85,000 (including fuel ₹18,000, crew ₹22,000, maintenance ₹12,000, depreciation ₹15,000, overhead ₹18,000).

**Step 1 — Compute VKM per day**
$$
VKM_{day} = N_{trips} \times L_{trip} = 50 \times 12 = 600 \text{ veh-km}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute PKM per day (Passenger-Kilometres)**
$$
PKM_{day} = VKM_{day} \times P_{avg} = 600 \times 45 = 27{,}000 \text{ pax-km}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute Cost per veh-km**
$$
C_{vk} = \dfrac{85{,}000}{600} = 141.67 \text{ ₹/veh-km}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Cost per pax-km**
$$
C_{pk} = \dfrac{85{,}000}{27{,}000} = 3.148 \text{ ₹/pax-km}
$$

**Step 5 — Cross-verify via load factor**
$$
LF = \dfrac{45}{S} \times 100
$$
If $S = 50$ seats, $LF = 90\%$. With this high LF, ₹3.15/pax-km is **commercially viable**. If LF dropped to 50%, $C_{pk}$ would double — illustrating **why load factor is the single most important cost driver**.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Cost Allocation Methods (Comparison Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This comparison is a KTU Part-A (3-mark) favourite. Memorize the allocation bases.**

| Method | Allocation Base Used | Order Sensitive? | Accuracy | Complexity | Best Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Direct Method** | Single statistic (e.g., revenue, VKM, PKM) | No | Low | Low | Small operators, single service type |
| **Step-Down Method** | Same bases, but service depts allocated in sequence | **Yes** | Medium | Medium | Multi-depot operators (e.g., KSRTC regional depots) |
| **Reciprocal Method** | Mutual services; uses simultaneous equations | No (mathematically) | **High** | High | Universities, multi-product firms (rare in transit) |
| **Activity-Based Costing (ABC)** | Cost drivers (e.g., number of stops, dwell time) | No | Very High | Very High | Modern metro systems with AFC data |
| **Marginal Costing** | Variable cost only | N/A | N/A (different purpose) | Medium | Pricing & fare-box decision-making |
| **Fully Allocated Costing (FAC)** | All costs (including fixed) | Yes | High | High | Subsidy determination by Govt. |

## 3.4 Step-by-Step: Direct Method Allocation (Worked Example)

A transit agency has ₹30,00,000 in common IT/HR overhead to allocate across three bus depots: **A (City), B (Suburban), C (Rural)**.

**Allocation bases (drivers chosen by management):**
- Depot A: VKM = 1,20,000; PKM = 18,00,000
- Depot B: VKM = 80,000; PKM = 10,00,000
- Depot C: VKM = 40,000; PKM = 2,00,000
- **Total VKM = 2,40,000; Total PKM = 30,00,000**

**Allocation using VKM (vehicle-kilometres) base:**

$$
