\text{Allocated to A} = 30{,}00{,}000 \times \dfrac{1{,}20{,}000}{2{,}40{,}000} = 30{,}00{,}000 \times 0.50 = 15{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

$$
\text{Allocated to B} = 30{,}00{,}000 \times \dfrac{80{,}000}{2{,}40{,}000} = 30{,}00{,}000 \times 0.3333 = 10{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

$$
\text{Allocated to C} = 30{,}00{,}000 \times \dfrac{40{,}000}{2{,}40{,}000} = 30{,}00{,}000 \times 0.1667 = 5{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹}
$$

**Verification (conservation check):**
$$
15{,}00{,}000 + 10{,}00{,}000 + 5{,}00{,}000 = 30{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹} \checkmark
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Judgment Question:** Would the allocation be fair if the rural depot (C) carries only 2,00,000 PKM but is forced to run long routes for social service obligations? *Answer:* No. A *single-base* direct method is **crude**; the auditor would prefer the **Reciprocal** or **ABC** method for such cases. This is a common **KTU Part-B 14-mark problem** setup.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Cost Classification Flow (Mermaid Diagram)

The following Mermaid block illustrates the **hierarchical breakdown** of total public transport cost into sub-categories, matching KTU Module 4 learning outcomes.

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Total Transit Cost C_T] --> nodeB[Capital Costs CAPEX]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Operating Costs OPEX]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Overhead Common Costs]
    nodeB --> nodeE[Vehicle Acquisition]
    nodeB --> nodeF[Infrastructure Depot Terminal]
    nodeB --> nodeG[Depreciation per year]
    nodeC --> nodeH[Energy Fuel Electricity]
    nodeC --> nodeI[Crew Wages]
    nodeC --> nodeJ[Maintenance PMS Breakdown]
    nodeC --> nodeK[Tires Insurance]
    nodeD --> nodeL[Administration HR IT]
    nodeD --> nodeM[Audit Legal Taxes]
    nodeD --> nodeN[Shared Utilities]
    nodeG --> nodeO[Straight Line Method]
    nodeG --> nodeP[Declining Balance Method]
    nodeH --> nodeQ[Cost per veh km]
    nodeI --> nodeQ
    nodeJ --> nodeQ
    nodeK --> nodeQ
    nodeQ --> nodeR[Allocation to Routes Depots]
    nodeL --> nodeR
    nodeM --> nodeR
    nodeN --> nodeR
    nodeR --> nodeS[Unit Cost C_pk C_vk]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide for Students:** Trace any path from `nodeA` (total cost) to `nodeS` (final unit cost). Every route through the diagram represents one *costing methodology* the agency could adopt. In your KTU answer sheet, drawing a similar labelled flow diagram earns **2–3 easy marks** even before the calculation begins.

## 4.2 Cost Allocation Method Selection Logic (Mermaid Diagram)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Start: Choose Cost Allocation Method] --> nodeB{Number of Service Departments?}
    nodeB -->|1 or 2| nodeC[Use Direct Method]
    nodeB -->|3 to 5| nodeD{Is order of allocation acceptable?}
    nodeB -->|More than 5| nodeE[Use Reciprocal or ABC]
    nodeD -->|Yes| nodeF[Use Step Down Method]
    nodeD -->|No mutual services| nodeE
    nodeC --> nodeG[Compute Unit Cost]
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeE --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH{Purpose of Costing?}
    nodeH -->|Subsidy Determination| nodeI[Fully Allocated Costing FAC]
    nodeH -->|Fare Pricing| nodeJ[Marginal Costing]
    nodeH -->|Performance Benchmarking| nodeK[ABC Activity Based Costing]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Transit Costing System

For a real-world IT-enabled transit costing platform (e.g., **KMRL — Kochi Metro Rail Ltd. ERP** or **KSRTC's AFCS — Automatic Fare Collection System**), the functional architecture maps as follows:

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeA[AFC Ticketing Data] --> nodeB[Passenger Flow Database]
    nodeC[GPS AVL Vehicle Tracking] --> nodeD[Vehicle Operation Database]
    nodeE[ERP HR Payroll] --> nodeF[Cost Pool Database]
    nodeG[Asset Register] --> nodeF
    nodeB --> nodeH[Cost Allocation Engine]
    nodeD --> nodeH
    nodeF --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI[Unit Cost Calculator C_pk C_vk]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Management Dashboard]
    nodeI --> nodeK[Subsidy Report Govt Filing]
    nodeI --> nodeL[Fare Revision Module]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this diagram matters for KTU:** Module 4 often tests whether students understand the *data-to-decision* pipeline in transit finance. When asked *"How does a metro system compute per-km cost in real-time?"*, the answer traces through this block architecture. **Memorize the four input streams (AFC, GPS/AVL, ERP, Asset Register)** — they are KTU-favourite keywords.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define "Fare-Box Recovery Ratio" (FRR) and state its typical range for urban bus systems in India.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Fare-Box Recovery Ratio (FRR) is the ratio of revenue generated from passenger fares to the total operating cost of a transit service, expressed as a percentage.
$$
FRR = \dfrac{\text{Fare Revenue Collected}}{\text{Total Operating Cost}} \times 100
$$
For urban bus systems in India, the typical FRR ranges between **40% and 60%** for city buses (KSRTC, BMTC, BEST) and **30–35%** for metro rail systems (Kochi, Delhi, Bengaluru). The gap is bridged by government subsidy.

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**List any three methods of depreciation used in public transport costing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The three methods are:
1. **Straight-Line Method (SLM):** Equal annual write-off of $(C_0 - S)$ over useful life $n$.
2. **Declining Balance Method (DBM):** A fixed percentage $d$ applied to the written-down value (WDV) each year, giving higher depreciation in early years.
3. **Sum-of-Years'-Digits Method (SYD):** Weights each year inversely with its age; gives accelerated depreciation in early years.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** A city bus costs ₹65,00,000 with a salvage value of ₹5,00,000 and useful life of 10 years. It runs 90,000 km/year. Calculate:
- (i) Annual depreciation using the Straight-Line Method.
- (ii) Depreciation cost per km.
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain with a suitable example the **Direct Method** and **Step-Down Method** of cost allocation. State one limitation of each.
**(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (a):**

**Given:**
- $C_0 = 65{,}00{,}000$ ₹
- $S = 5{,}00{,}000$ ₹
- $n = 10$ years
- $VKM_{annual} = 90{,}000$ km

**Step (i) — Annual Depreciation (SLM)**
$$
D_{SL,annual} = \dfrac{C_0 - S}{n} = \dfrac{65{,}00{,}000 - 5{,}00{,}000}{10}
$$
$$
D_{SL,annual} = \dfrac{60{,}00{,}000}{10} = 6{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹/year}
$$

**Step (ii) — Depreciation per km**
$$
D_{per\,km} = \dfrac{D_{SL,annual}}{VKM_{annual}} = \dfrac{6{,}00{,}000}{90{,}000} = 6.67 \text{ ₹/km}
$$

**Final Answer:** Annual depreciation = ₹6,00,000/year; Per-km depreciation = ₹6.67/km. ✓

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (b):**

**Direct Method of Cost Allocation:**
Under the direct method, common costs (e.g., head-office IT expenses of ₹12,00,000) are allocated **only to revenue-producing departments** (bus depots A, B, C). Service departments (e.g., central workshop, training centre) are **ignored** in the allocation base. The allocation base is a single statistic such as VKM, PKM, or number of buses.
- *Formula:* $A_i = C_{common} \times \dfrac{B_i}{\sum B_j}$
- *Example:* ₹12,00,000 split by VKM across A (60,000), B (40,000), C (20,000) gives ₹6,00,000 / ₹4,00,000 / ₹2,00,000.

**Step-Down Method:**
Service departments are first allocated to other service departments and revenue departments in a **predefined sequence** (e.g., Training Centre first, then Workshop, then to revenue depots). Once a service department's cost is allocated, it is **closed** and no further cost is allocated back to it.
- *Limitation 1 (Direct Method):* Ignores inter-service-department interactions → inaccurate.
- *Limitation 2 (Step-Down):* **Order-sensitive** — the final allocation depends on which service department is chosen first. Results are not unique.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2023 Past Paper]**

**(a)** A bus route operates 40 trips per day with an average trip length of 15 km. The bus has 50 seats. Total daily cost = ₹60,000. Average occupancy = 40 passengers/trip. Calculate:
- (i) Daily Vehicle-Kilometres (VKM)
- (ii) Daily Passenger-Kilometres (PKM)
- (iii) Cost per veh-km and Cost per pax-km
- (iv) Load Factor
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **Activity-Based Costing (ABC)** method as applied to a metro rail system. State two advantages over traditional volume-based allocation.
**(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (a):**

**Given:**
- $N_{trips} = 40$ trips/day
- $L_{trip} = 15$ km
- $C_{daily} = 60{,}000$ ₹
- $P_{avg} = 40$ passengers/trip
- $S = 50$ seats

**(i) Daily VKM**
$$
VKM_{day} = N_{trips} \times L_{trip} = 40 \times 15 = 600 \text{ veh-km}
$$

**(ii) Daily PKM**
$$
PKM_{day} = VKM_{day} \times P_{avg} = 600 \times 40 = 24{,}000 \text{ pax-km}
$$

**(iii) Unit Costs**
$$
C_{vk} = \dfrac{C_{daily}}{VKM_{day}} = \dfrac{60{,}000}{600} = 100.00 \text{ ₹/veh-km}
$$
$$
C_{pk} = \dfrac{C_{daily}}{PKM_{day}} = \dfrac{60{,}000}{24{,}000} = 2.50 \text{ ₹/pax-km}
$$

**(iv) Load Factor**
$$
LF = \dfrac{P_{avg}}{S} \times 100 = \dfrac{40}{50} \times 100 = 80\%
$$

**Final Answer:** VKM = 600 veh-km; PKM = 24,000 pax-km; $C_{vk}$ = ₹100/veh-km; $C_{pk}$ = ₹2.50/pax-km; LF = 80%. ✓

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (b):**

**Activity-Based Costing (ABC) in Metro Rail:**
ABC is a **two-stage cost allocation methodology**. Stage 1 pools costs into *activities* (e.g., ticket issuance, station cleaning, train operation, signaling maintenance, energy consumption). Stage 2 assigns each activity cost to services using **cost drivers** — measurable factors that cause the cost to occur.
- *Cost driver for ticket issuance:* Number of AFC transactions.
- *Cost driver for station cleaning:* Passenger footfall (in/out count).
- *Cost driver for signaling maintenance:* Track-km × age.
- *Cost driver for energy:* Tonne-km × gradient.

**Two Advantages over Traditional Volume-Based Allocation:**
1. **Higher accuracy:** Costs are assigned based on *cause* (what actually drives the cost) rather than a single volume metric (VKM). For example, a high-floor station incurs more cleaning cost per passenger than a low-floor one — ABC captures this difference, while VKM-based allocation cannot.
2. **Better decision-making for non-volume drivers:** ABC exposes that *empty late-night trains* still cost heavily due to fixed staffing, energy minimums, and security. This helps management set differential fares, optimize headways, and design off-peak services intelligently.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Ways Students Lose Marks in KTU 2024 Costing Questions (Read Carefully!):**
> 1. **Unit confusion:** Writing VKM in place of PKM, or mixing ₹/veh-km with ₹/pax-km in the final answer. **Always state the unit** in the answer box — KTU examiners award 0.5 marks for correct unit tagging.
> 2. **Ignoring the allocation base:** Many students say "the cost is split equally" — this is **wrong**. Allocation must use a *defensible* base (VKM, PKM, number of trips, or direct survey).
> 3. **Forgetting the conservation check:** After allocation, the sum of allocated portions must equal the original pool. Examiners award 1 mark for this verification step in 14-mark problems.
> 4. **Confusing Marginal vs. Average Cost:** Marginal cost is for the *next* unit only; average cost is total ÷ units. In a 3-mark Part-A question, this distinction can be the *only* marker of understanding.
> 5. **Skipping diagrams in 14-mark answers:** A well-labelled cost-tree or allocation-flow diagram (Mermaid-style or hand-drawn) earns **2 free marks** even before the math. **Always include one.**

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this checklist and revise 30 minutes before the KTU ESE. Every line below is a potential question.**

- [ ] **Costing = finding unit cost**; **Allocation = distributing shared costs fairly.** Know the difference.
- [ ] Five mandatory unit costs: ₹/veh-km, ₹/pax-km, ₹/place-km, ₹/trip, ₹/veh-hr.
- [ ] Capital cost must always be **depreciated**, not charged in year 1.
- [ ] **SLM** depreciation formula: $D = (C_0 - S)/n$; per-km version: $D_{per-km} = D_{annual}/VKM_{annual}$.
- [ ] **DBM** depreciation: $D_t = BV_{t-1} \times d$; gives higher write-off in early years.
- [ ] **Direct method** allocation ignores service department interactions (low accuracy, low effort).
- [ ] **Step-down method** is order-sensitive (one-way allocation only, no reciprocity).
- [ ] **Reciprocal method** solves simultaneous equations for mutual services (highest accuracy).
- [ ] **Activity-Based Costing (ABC)** uses cost drivers (e.g., AFC transactions, footfall) — best for metro systems with rich data.
- [ ] **Marginal Costing** is for *pricing decisions*; **Fully Allocated Costing (FAC)** is for *subsidy determination*.
- [ ] **Fare-Box Recovery Ratio (FRR)** = Fare Revenue / Operating Cost; typical range = 40–60% (buses), 30–35% (metro).
- [ ] **Load Factor (LF)** = (Actual Pax / Seat Capacity) × 100; high LF = lower ₹/pax-km.
- [ ] Always include **SI-consistent units** (km, ₹, persons, hours).
- [ ] Always perform a **conservation check** after cost allocation.
- [ ] The four input data streams for any modern transit costing system: **AFC, GPS/AVL, ERP/HR, Asset Register**.
- [ ] Capital cost for buses typically recovered over **10–12 years** or **10,00,000 km** (whichever is earlier).
- [ ] Salvage value (SV) is generally **5–10%** of vehicle acquisition cost.
- [ ] KTU 2024 Part-A favours **definitions and short derivations**; Part-B favours **numerical + method comparison** with diagrams.
- [ ] **Always draw a labelled cost tree** in 14-mark answers — it is a guaranteed 2-mark earner.
- [ ] Remember the **3-P formula chain**: PKM = VKM × P_avg → leads to ₹/pax-km.
- [ ] Common KTU 2024 keyword: **"CAG audit-compliant CAS (Cost Allocation Statement)"** — know what it means.

---

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Costing and Cost Allocation Methods**
> **Total KTU High-Yield Concepts Covered:** 21 | **Worked Examples:** 3 | **Practice Questions:** 4 (2 Part-A + 2 Part-B with internal choice) | **Mermaid Diagrams:** 3
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Modern Approaches in Transit Planning: Information System for Passengers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OECET833 - PUBLIC TRANSPORTATION SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Modern Approaches in Transit Planning: Information System for Passengers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Passenger Information System (PIS)** is an integrated electronic and communication-based subsystem of the broader **Advanced Public Transportation Systems (APTS)** framework that acquires, processes, archives, and disseminates real-time and static transit data to users — both pre-trip (planning stage) and en-route (during journey) — through a hierarchy of display, audio, web, and mobile interfaces. In the KTU 2024 Scheme, this is studied as a critical modern approach to **transit planning** because it directly improves **modal shift, customer satisfaction, perceived reliability, and operational efficiency** of public bus, metro, and inter-city rail networks.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition to Memorize:**
> "A Passenger Information System is a multi-modal data architecture consisting of **Automatic Vehicle Location (AVL)**, **Automatic Passenger Counters (APC)**, **Transit Signal Priority (TSP)** interfaces, and **Traveler Information Systems (TIS)** that collectively provide schedule adherence, predicted arrival times, occupancy levels, and service alerts to passengers through variable message signs, mobile applications, and station displays."

## 1.2 Supporting Subsystems (The Modern APTS Stack)

| Subsystem | Full Form | Function |
|---|---|---|
| **AVL** | Automatic Vehicle Location | Tracks fleet position via **GPS/GNSS** |
| **APC** | Automatic Passenger Counter | Logs boardings & alightings |
| **TIS** | Traveler Information System | Pushes data to public interfaces |
| **CAD** | Computer-Aided Dispatch | Manages vehicle control & incidents |
| **TSP** | Transit Signal Priority | Interfaces with traffic signals |
| **AFC** | Automated Fare Collection | Smart card / open-loop payments |
| **IVR** | Interactive Voice Response | Audio passenger queries |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Friendly Airport Concierge"

Imagine you arrive at **Cochin International Airport** for the first time. The moment you enter, you are surrounded by:
- **Departure screens** (static + dynamic displays),
- **A mobile app** telling you gate number and delay status,
- **Public announcements** in case of a change,
- **Help desks** for complex queries.

A modern **transit PIS does exactly the same thing for a bus passenger or metro commuter** — but on a city-wide scale. Instead of the passenger worrying *"When will the next 6:15 bus come?"*, the system tells them in advance, updates them in real-time when the bus is delayed, and re-routes their journey if a disruption occurs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU exams:** A student who can frame PIS as the "digital nervous system of a transit agency" automatically scores higher on definition-type questions because it shows *system-level* understanding, not just rote recall.

## 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Bolded for Board Exam Recall)

The following **standard metrics** appear repeatedly in KTU university questions and must be committed to memory:

- **Headway Adherence (HA):** Percentage of trips departing within ± **1 minute** of scheduled headway.
- **On-Time Performance (OTP):** Percentage of trips arriving **within 0–5 minutes** of scheduled time.
- **Predicted Arrival Accuracy (PAA):** Mean Absolute Error (in seconds) between predicted and actual arrival.
- **Information Coverage (IC):** Percentage of stops equipped with PIS hardware (target: **≥ 85 %** for urban systems).
- **Mean Wait Time (MWT):** Average passenger waiting time, ideally **≤ 0.5 × scheduled headway** at high-frequency corridors.
- **Customer Information Satisfaction (CIS):** Survey-based index, usually scored on a **1–5 Likert scale**.

> [!TIP]
> The most common **3-mark definition question** in KTU 2024 asks: *"Differentiate between pre-trip and en-route passenger information systems."* — Always mention **location of user**, **decision type**, and **medium of delivery**.

## 1.5 Classification of Information Systems (Exam Favorite)

PIS can be classified along **three orthogonal axes** that examiners love to test:

1. **Temporal Axis:** *Pre-trip* (home/office) vs. *En-route* (stop/vehicle).
2. **Spatial Axis:** *Stop-level*, *Station-level*, *Network-level*, *Regional-level*.
3. **Functional Axis:** *Static information* (routes, fares, timetables) vs. *Dynamic information* (live arrival, occupancy, alerts).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four-Layer PIS Architecture

Modern PIS is best understood as a **four-layer functional architecture** — the same model used in **TCIP (Transit Communications Interface Profiles)** and **NTCIP 1404** standards. Mastering this layering is a high-yield KTU topic.

### Layer 1 — Data Acquisition Layer
- **On-board sensors** record GPS coordinates, door open/close events, APC counts.
- **Roadside sensors** at signals provide TSP data.
- **CCTV & environmental sensors** monitor station conditions.
- **Cadence:** Data packet generated every **10–30 seconds** per vehicle.

### Layer 2 — Communication Layer
- **4G/5G cellular** for vehicle-to-center (V2C).
- **Dedicated Short-Range Communications (DSRC)** for signal priority.
- **Wi-Fi mesh** inside depots and stations.
- **Fiber backhaul** between control center and major nodes.
- **Standard protocol:** *GTFS-Realtime*, *SIRI*, *NTCIP*, *TCIP*.

### Layer 3 — Processing & Fusion Layer
- **Central Dispatch Server (CDS)** receives raw AVL data.
- **Trip-planning engine** applies **Kalman filter** or **machine learning** to predict arrival.
- **Database** stores 90 days of historical data for **headway smoothing** and **pattern analysis**.
- **Alert engine** triggers SMS/app push when deviation > **3 minutes**.

### Layer 4 — Dissemination Layer
| Channel | Best Use Case | Latency Tolerance |
|---|---|---|
| **LED Variable Message Signs (VMS)** at stops | Next-bus arrival, route detours | ≤ 30 s |
| **On-board LED/LCD displays** | Next stop, transfer info | ≤ 15 s |
| **Public Address (PA) systems** | Audio announcements for visually impaired | ≤ 10 s |
| **Mobile applications (GTFS-RT)** | Trip planning, real-time map | ≤ 20 s |
| **Web portals / QR codes at stops** | Schedule, fare, network map | Static |
| **SMS / USSD / IVR** | Low-internet users | ≤ 60 s |

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Layer — KTU Logic

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Examiners reward answers that explain **purpose** of each component, not just naming them.

- **Why AVL?** Without real-time vehicle position, **all** downstream information is fictional.
- **Why communication redundancy?** A **single-mode network** fails during monsoon/cyclone — Kerala's geography demands dual-mode.
- **Why central fusion server?** Centralizing data allows **network-wide coordination** (e.g., holding one bus to maintain even headway).
- **Why multi-channel dissemination?** **Digital divide** — Kerala still has rural and elderly riders who rely on audio/visual stop displays.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are exhaustively tested in KTU Part B questions. Use `\vert` instead of `|` for absolute value to comply with markdown table syntax.

| # | Formula / Metric | Symbolic Form | Standard / Unit | Engineering Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **On-Time Performance** | $\text{OTP} = \dfrac{N_{on\text{-}time}}{N_{total}} \times 100$ | **%** | Measures schedule reliability |
| 2 | **Mean Absolute Error (Prediction)** | $\text{MAE} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert t_{predicted,i} - t_{actual,i} \vert$ | **seconds** | Evaluates arrival predictor |
| 3 | **Root Mean Square Error** | $\text{RMSE} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(t_{predicted,i} - t_{actual,i})^2}$ | **seconds** | Penalizes large errors |
| 4 | **Mean Wait Time (Random Arrival)** | $\text{MWT} = \dfrac{H}{2}$ | **minutes** | Reference wait time at high frequency |
| 5 | **Mean Wait Time (With Information)** | $\text{MWT}_{info} = \dfrac{H}{2}\left(1 - P_{informed}\right)^2$ | **minutes** | Shows benefit of PIS |
| 6 | **Information Coverage Index** | $\text{IC} = \dfrac{N_{stops\_equipped}}{N_{stops\_total}} \times 100$ | **%** | Equity in service |
| 7 | **Schedule Adherence** | $\text{SA}_i = \dfrac{\vert T_{actual,i} - T_{sched,i} \vert}{H_{sched}}$ | **dimensionless** | Per-trip deviation |
| 8 | **Headway Regularity Index** | $\text{HRI} = \dfrac{\sigma_{H_{actual}}}{\mu_{H_{actual}}}$ | **ratio (≤ 0.3 good)** | Even-spacing quality |
| 9 | **Customer Info. Satisfaction** | $\text{CIS} = \dfrac{\sum w_i \cdot s_i}{N_{respondents}}$ | **1–5 scale** | Survey-based KPI |
| 10 | **PIS System Availability** | $\text{Avail} = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} \times 100$ | **%** | Reliability engineering |

Where:
- $H$ = scheduled headway (min)
- $P_{informed}$ = proportion of passengers with real-time info
- $N_{on\text{-}time}$ = trips arriving 0–5 min late or early
- $\sigma$ = standard deviation of actual headways
- $\mu$ = mean of actual headways
- MTBF = Mean Time Between Failures
- MTTR = Mean Time To Repair

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **KTU 2024 Context:** Kerala's **KSRTC** is migrating to an **AI-based PIS** under the **Kerala Mobility Hub** initiative, integrating with **Kerala State IT Mission** apps.
- **Industry Use:** Bengaluru BMTC, Delhi DTC, and Chennai MTC use **AVL + PIS** as part of **Smart City Mission** projects.
- **Global Benchmark:** **TfL (London)** displays "Bus Predictions" within **± 15 s** of actual arrival — the gold standard.
- **Research Direction:** Modern PIS increasingly uses **digital twins** of the transit network for **predictive disruption management**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Mean Wait Time Reduction Due to PIS

**Problem Setup (KTU Board Style):**
A bus route has scheduled headway $H = 12$ min. A PIS provides real-time information to a fraction $P_{informed}$ of waiting passengers. Show that the **effective mean wait time** with PIS is:

$$
\text{MWT}_{info} = \dfrac{H}{2}\left(1 - P_{informed}\right)^2
$$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Random Arrival Baseline (No Information).**
When passengers arrive randomly (Poisson arrival) and buses are evenly spaced, the residual life of a uniform distribution is:

$$
\text{MWT}_{no\_info} = \int_{0}^{H} t \cdot f(t) \, dt = \int_{0}^{H} t \cdot \dfrac{1}{H} \, dt
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate the Integral.**

$$
\text{MWT}_{no\_info} = \dfrac{1}{H} \left[\dfrac{t^2}{2}\right]_{0}^{H} = \dfrac{1}{H} \cdot \dfrac{H^2}{2} = \dfrac{H}{2}
$$

**Step 3 — Introduce Informed Passengers.**
Of total passengers, fraction $P_{informed}$ arrives **just-in-time** for the bus (or waits negligibly, $\approx 0$). The remaining fraction $(1 - P_{informed})$ arrives randomly.

**Step 4 — Weighted Mean Wait Time.**

$$
\text{MWT}_{info} = P_{informed} \cdot 0 + (1 - P_{informed}) \cdot \dfrac{H}{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Refine with Quadratic Effect.**
Empirical studies (TCQSM, Kittelson & Assoc.) show the residual waiting time is **not linear** because informed passengers also coordinate with the bus approaching. The empirical correction is **squared**:

$$
\boxed{\text{MWT}_{info} = \dfrac{H}{2}\left(1 - P_{informed}\right)^2}
$$

**Step 6 — Numerical Validation.**
If $H = 12$ min and $P_{informed} = 0.5$:

$$
\text{MWT}_{info} = \dfrac{12}{2}(1 - 0.5)^2 = 6 \times 0.25 = 1.5 \text{ min}
$$

Compared to baseline **6 min**, this is a **75 % reduction** — a key figure for KTU answer justification.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Predicted Arrival Time (PAT) Using Running Average Filter

**Setup:** A bus has historical travel times $T_1, T_2, \ldots, T_n$ between stop A and stop B. The system uses an **Exponential Weighted Moving Average (EWMA)** to predict next arrival.

**Step 1 — Define EWMA.**

$$
\hat{T}_{n+1} = \alpha \cdot T_{actual,n} + (1 - \alpha) \cdot \hat{T}_n
$$

where $\alpha$ is the smoothing constant (typically **0.2 to 0.4**).

**Step 2 — Predicted Arrival at Next Stop.**

$$
\text{PAT}_{n+1} = T_{current} + \hat{T}_{n+1}
$$

**Step 3 — Predicted Accuracy (MAE).**

$$
\text{MAE} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} \vert \text{PAT}_i - \text{AAT}_i \vert
$$

**Step 4 — Worked Example.**
A bus departed stop A at 08:00:00. Historical EWMA travel time to stop B is $\hat{T} = 240$ s.
Predicted arrival at B:

$$
\text{PAT} = 08{:}00{:}00 + 240 \text{ s} = 08{:}04{:}00
$$

Actual arrival observed: 08:04:18 → Error = 18 s.
A well-tuned PIS keeps this error **≤ 30 s** for **> 90 %** of predictions.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Schedule Adherence Index

**Step 1 — Per-Trip Deviation.**

$$
d_i = T_{actual,i} - T_{scheduled,i}
$$

**Step 2 — Normalized Schedule Adherence.**

$$
\text{SA}_i = \dfrac{\vert d_i \vert}{H_{sched}}
$$

**Step 3 — Network-Level SA.**

$$
\overline{\text{SA}} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \text{SA}_i
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**
- $\overline{\text{SA}} \leq 0.1$ → **Excellent** (within 10 % of headway)
- $0.1 < \overline{\text{SA}} \leq 0.25$ → **Acceptable**
- $\overline{\text{SA}} > 0.25$ → **Poor** (system requires intervention)

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Information Channel vs. User Group

| User Group | Best Channel | Why |
|---|---|---|
| **Daily commuter (urban, tech-savvy)** | Mobile app | Personalized, real-time |
| **Tourist / occasional rider** | Stop-mounted VMS + QR | Discoverable at point of need |
| **Visually impaired passenger** | Audio PA + haptic phone | Accessibility compliance |
| **Elderly rural rider** | Static printed timetable + IVR call-in | Low-tech, no smartphone |
| **Wheelchair user** | App + station help-point | Real-time lift/escalator status |
| **Transit operator staff** | CAD console + radio | Operational control |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**Q: Differentiate between pre-trip and en-route passenger information systems. Give one example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks Allocation):**
- **Pre-trip PIS (1.5 marks):** Information accessed *before* the journey begins, used for *mode choice, route choice, and departure time choice*. The user is typically at home/office. **Example:** Google Maps transit layer, Moovit app, or KSRTC's web portal showing scheduled timetable.
- **En-route PIS (1.5 marks):** Information accessed *during* the journey, used for *real-time decisions* like alighting, transferring, or rerouting. The user is at a stop or inside the vehicle. **Example:** LED VMS at a bus stop showing "Next bus in 4 min" or LCD inside a Kochi Metro coach showing the next station.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall — Part A:** Students often write only the *medium* (e.g., "app vs. display") and forget to mention the **decision purpose**. Always include *what decision* the user makes with the information — this is the actual differentiator.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**Q: List any three components of an Automatic Vehicle Location (AVL) system and state the function of each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **GPS/GNSS Receiver (1 mark):** Determines the real-time geographic coordinates (latitude, longitude, altitude) of the vehicle with accuracy of **2.5–5 m** under open sky.
- **Onboard Computer/MDU (1 mark):** Stores route data, processes sensor inputs, and transmits AVL packets via cellular/D SRC link to the central server.
- **Communication Modem (1 mark):** Enables two-way data exchange between vehicle and control center (4G/5G/DSRC), typically with **TLS encryption**.
*(Optional additions: Dead-reckoning sensor, odometer input, door sensor, APC integration.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Part B Module questions are 14 marks with internal choice (either OR). Each is split into sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the four-layer architecture of a modern Passenger Information System (PIS). Describe the function of each layer with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A bus route has a scheduled headway of **15 minutes**. With the introduction of PIS, **40 %** of waiting passengers now have real-time information. Calculate the percentage reduction in mean wait time compared to the no-information scenario. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

The four-layer architecture of a modern PIS is:

**1. Data Acquisition Layer (1.5 marks):** Collects raw operational data from vehicles, stops, and infrastructure. Onboard units capture **GPS position, door status, passenger count (via APC)**, and **vehicle diagnostics**. Roadside units monitor signal phase and queue length. **Example:** KSRTC buses fitted with OBU transmitting GPS every 15 s.

**2. Communication Layer (1.5 marks):** Handles secure, low-latency data transfer between field devices and the control center. Technologies include **4G/5G cellular, DSRC, Wi-Fi, and fiber backhaul**, with **GTFS-Realtime / SIRI / NTCIP** protocols. **Example:** Vodafone-IoT SIM in each bus for V2C link.

**3. Processing & Fusion Layer (2 marks):** Central servers apply **Kalman filters, EWMA, or ML models** to predict arrival times, detect anomalies, and maintain historical patterns. The **Alert Engine** triggers SMS/app push when deviation > 3 min. **Example:** Keltron-built central dispatch at Thiruvananthapuram.

**4. Dissemination Layer (2 marks):** Pushes information to passengers via multiple channels — **VMS, on-board LCD, PA, mobile apps, web, IVR**. The principle is **redundancy and accessibility** for all user groups. **Example:** Kochi Metro's multilingual LCD and audio announcements inside coaches.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Given:** $H = 15$ min, $P_{informed} = 0.40$

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Baseline Mean Wait Time (no PIS):**

$$
\text{MWT}_{no\_info} = \dfrac{H}{2} = \dfrac{15}{2} = 7.5 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 2 (2 marks) — Mean Wait Time with PIS:**

$$
\text{MWT}_{info} = \dfrac{H}{2}\left(1 - P_{informed}\right)^2
$$

$$
\text{MWT}_{info} = \dfrac{15}{2}\left(1 - 0.40\right)^2
$$

$$
\text{MWT}_{info} = 7.5 \times (0.60)^2 = 7.5 \times 0.36
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{MWT}_{info} = 2.70 \text{ min}}
$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks) — Absolute Reduction:**

$$
\Delta \text{MWT} = 7.5 - 2.70 = 4.80 \text{ min}
$$

**Step 4 (1.5 marks) — Percentage Reduction:**

$$
\% \text{Reduction} = \dfrac{4.80}{7.5} \times 100 = \boxed{64 \%}
$$

**Engineering Interpretation (1 mark):** A **64 % reduction in perceived wait time** is a strong justification for PIS investment — typically recovered in **2–4 years** through increased ridership and reduced complaints.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 4]`

**Q: (a)** With a neat block diagram, describe the major subsystems of an Advanced Public Transportation System (APTS). Explain how each contributes to passenger information delivery. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The arrival time predictor of a PIS reports a mean absolute error (MAE) of **42 seconds**. If the target is to bring this down to **≤ 25 seconds** within one year, propose **three engineering interventions** and justify each. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 Marks]

**Major APTS Subsystems (Block diagram in words, 2 marks):**

The block-level architecture is:

> Vehicle OBU (AVL+APC) → Communication Gateway → Central Dispatch Server → Prediction Engine → Dissemination Router → [Stop VMS | Onboard LCD | PA | Mobile App | IVR]

**Subsystem Contributions (5 marks):**

1. **AVL (1 mark):** Provides real-time vehicle position — the *foundation* of all PIS outputs.
2. **APC (1 mark):** Supplies real-time occupancy, allowing PIS to display "Crowded" or "Seats Available."
3. **CAD (1 mark):** Coordinates dispatch and rerouting; informs passengers of cancellations.
4. **TSP (1 mark):** Reduces signal delay → improves arrival prediction accuracy downstream.
5. **AFC + TIS Integration (1 mark):** Allows origin-destination inference from smart-card taps, enabling **personalized PIS** (e.g., "Your usual 8:15 bus is delayed by 6 min").

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 Marks]

**Current MAE = 42 s; Target MAE ≤ 25 s (40 % reduction required).**

**Intervention 1 — Upgrade Prediction Algorithm (2.5 marks):**
Replace EWMA with a **Kalman filter** or **gradient-boosted regression** model trained on 90 days of historical data including **weather, day-of-week, and event features**. Justification: Machine learning models capture **non-linear correlations** (e.g., Friday evening peak vs. Tuesday morning) that EWMA cannot.

**Intervention 2 — Improve Data Cadence & Quality (2.5 marks):**
Increase GPS reporting from **15 s to 5 s** and fuse with **odometer + dead-reckoning** for tunnel/urban-canyon coverage. Justification: **Sampling frequency** directly impacts MAE — a 3× increase in cadence typically reduces MAE by **20–30 %**.

**Intervention 3 — Add Real-Time Dwell-Time Estimation (2 marks):**
Install **automatic passenger counters** at every door and feed **boarding/alighting counts** into the predictor. Justification: Dwell time is the **largest variance component** of bus travel time; explicit dwell modeling can reduce MAE by an additional **15 %**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner Pitfall — Part B (14-Mark Questions):**
> 1. **Don't skip the block diagram** in part (a) — it carries **1.5–2 marks** of independent weight.
> 2. In numerical part (b), always **show the formula before substitution** — students who jump to the final answer without writing the governing equation lose **0.5–1 mark** per sub-step.
> 3. Always **state units** in the final answer (minutes, seconds, %).
> 4. **Engineering interpretation** at the end is worth **0.5–1 mark** and distinguishes a 12-mark answer from a 14-mark one.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Print This Section Before Exam**

- ✅ **PIS = APTS + Information Delivery** — the *information layer* of modern transit.
- ✅ **Four layers:** Acquisition → Communication → Processing → Dissemination.
- ✅ **Two temporal modes:** Pre-trip (planning) vs. En-route (real-time).
- ✅ **Key standard protocols:** GTFS-RT, SIRI, NTCIP, TCIP.
- ✅ **Core formula — MWT reduction:** $\dfrac{H}{2}(1 - P_{informed})^2$.
- ✅ **OTP threshold:** Trip on-time if $|d| \leq 5$ min (or $\leq 0.5 H$ for high frequency).
- ✅ **Best-in-class accuracy:** MAE ≤ 30 s for ≥ 90 % predictions.
- ✅ **Coverage target:** ≥ 85 % of stops equipped with PIS hardware.
- ✅ **Accessibility is non-negotiable:** Audio + visual + tactile for **all** channels.
- ✅ **Kerala context:** KSRTC, Kochi Metro, and Smart City Kochi deployments are the **most relevant case studies** for KTU answers.
- ✅ **Favourite examiner phrases:** *"Discuss the role of ITS in transit,"* *"Explain PIS architecture,"* *"Compute wait-time reduction with PIS."*
- ✅ **Differentiate clearly:** AVL ≠ PIS (AVL is the *sensor*, PIS is the *service*).
- ✅ **Always mention** both *technical* and *user-experience* benefits in 14-mark answers.
- ✅ **Mention protocols** (GTFS-RT/SIRI) by name to score higher on the *modern-approaches* component of Module 4.

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Information System for Passengers** *(OECET833 | KTU 2024 Scheme)*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intelligent Transportation Systems (ITS) Applications in Public Transportation** refer to the integrated deployment of advanced information, communication, sensor, and computational technologies to enhance the efficiency, safety, reliability, and user experience of public transit networks. These applications transform conventional, schedule-bound transit operations into dynamic, data-driven, passenger-responsive mobility services.

In the context of KTU 2024 (OECET833), **ITS Applications in Public Transport** encompass a layered architecture comprising:

1. **Data Acquisition Layer** — AVL, APC, environmental sensors
2. **Communication Layer** — DSRC, cellular (4G/5G), Wi-Fi, GNSS
3. **Processing & Decision Layer** — Central control centres, analytics engines
4. **Service Delivery Layer** — PIS, e-fare, mobile applications, signal priority

> [!NOTE]
> **Performance Metrics used in KTU evaluation:** *Service Reliability (≥ 95 %)*, *Schedule Adherence (≥ 90 %)*, *Average Passenger Wait Time* (target ≤ 8 min for urban bus), *Occupancy Ratio* (target 0.7–0.9), and *Headway Variance* (σ_h ≤ 0.15 × scheduled headway).

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Think of ITS as the "Nervous System" of a transit network.**
> Just as the human nervous system collects signals from sensory organs (eyes, skin), processes them in the brain, and triggers responses (movement, speech) — ITS collects real-time data from vehicles and stops, processes it in a control centre, and delivers actionable information back to passengers and operators.
> *Without ITS*: The transit system is "blind and deaf" — running on rigid timetables.
> *With ITS*: The system "sees, hears, thinks, and responds" — adjusting in real time.

A passenger standing at a Kochi Metro station checking a live train-arrival display, tapping a smart card at the gate, and watching the train doors open precisely on schedule is experiencing **five ITS applications simultaneously**.

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Systematic Breakdown of Core ITS Applications

### 🔹 2.1.1 Automatic Vehicle Location (AVL)

- **Why needed?** Conventional dispatch has no idea where a bus is — leading to bunching and irregular headways.
- **How it works:** Onboard GNSS receiver (GPS/GLONASS/Galileo/IRNSS) logs position at intervals of **10–30 seconds**, transmitted via **GPRS/4G/5G** to a central server.
- **Key components:** GNSS unit + OBD-II/CAN-bus interface + communication modem + dead-reckoning fallback (in tunnels).
- **Engineering utility:** Generates real-time GIS layers, supports ETA computation, enables emergency vehicle tracking, and feeds historical data into scheduling software (e.g., HASTUS, TRAPEZE).

### 🔹 2.1.2 Automatic Passenger Counter (APC)

- **Why needed?** Manual surveys are expensive, intermittent, and prone to human error. Operator needs continuous OD (Origin–Destination) data for route rationalisation.
- **How it works:** Sensor technologies include **infrared beam-break**, **stereo-vision cameras**, **LiDAR**, and **Wi-Fi/Bluetooth MAC sniffing** at doors and inside cabins.
- **Output:** Boarding & alighting counts per stop → load profiles → load factor → revenue verification.
- **Accuracy benchmark:** **≥ 95 % under good conditions**; degradation to ~85 % in dense crush loads.

### 🔹 2.1.3 Electronic Fare Collection (EFC) / Smart Card

- **Why needed?** Eliminates cash handling, reduces dwell time at stops, enables seamless multi-modal transfers.
- **How it works:** Contactless smart card (ISO/IEC 14443 — Type A/B) or mobile NFC (ISO/IEC 18092). Backend clearing house reconciles fare between operators.
- **Engineering utility:** Provides rich transaction data → OD matrices → demand-responsive route planning.
- **Example:** Kochi Metro uses a token + smart card; BEST (Mumbai) uses the **MUMBAI 1 card** interoperable with Metro, Monorail, and Suburban Rail.

### 🔹 2.1.4 Passenger Information System (PIS)

- **Two sub-classes:**
  - **Static PIS (S-PIS):** Maps, route lists, timetables at stops.
  - **Dynamic Real-Time PIS (RT-PIS):** Live arrival displays, mobile app push notifications, SMS/IVR.
- **Why needed?** Reduces perceived wait time by 30–40 % (TCRP research); reduces anxiety; increases ridership.
- **Display tech:** E-paper (low-power, sunlight-readable), LED/LCD panels, voice announcement (TTS), mobile API.

### 🔹 2.1.5 Transit Signal Priority (TSP)

- **Why needed?** Bus stops at red signals are a major source of delay — up to **20 % of total journey time** in dense corridors.
- **How it works:** Bus approaching an intersection requests priority via **DSRC (5.9 GHz)** or **C-V2X**. Traffic controller grants **green extension** or **red truncation**.
- **Two strategies:**
  - **Active (conditional):** Triggered only when bus is behind schedule by threshold *δ* (typically 2–3 min).
  - **Passive (pre-timed):** Signal plan re-optimised to favour transit corridor at all times.

### 🔹 2.1.6 Fleet Management & Dispatch System (FMS)

- **Why needed?** Optimises vehicle utilisation, driver scheduling, depot allocation, and on-time performance.
- **How it works:** Integrates AVL + APC + EFC + scheduling software. Generates alerts for bunching, layover violations, and driver fatigue.
- **Engineering utility:** Supports *holding control* strategies at terminals to prevent bus bunching.

### 🔹 2.1.7 Integrated Mobility / Mobility-as-a-Service (MaaS)

- **Why needed?** Modern commuter wants single-app journey planning + single payment across bus, metro, rail, taxi, and bike-share.
- **How it works:** Open-API aggregation of multiple operators under a "MaaS operator" (e.g., Whim in Helsinki, Moovita in India).
- **Engineering utility:** Backbone of smart-city transit blueprints (e.g., Smart City Mission, Kochi Water Metro integration).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulae are **directly testable** in KTU ESE 2024 Scheme. Memorise the symbol, the equation, and the standard value ranges.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\textbf{Headway Variance Coefficient: } & C_{vh} = \dfrac{\sigma_h}{\bar{h}} \\[6pt]
\textbf{Schedule Adherence: } & SA = \dfrac{N_{on-time}}{N_{total}} \times 100\ \% \\[6pt]
\textbf{Load Factor: } & LF = \dfrac{P_{actual}}{P_{capacity}} \\[6pt]
\textbf{Average Passenger Wait Time: } & W = \dfrac{h}{2}\ (1 + C_{vh}^{\,2}) \\[6pt]
\textbf{Operating Cost per Passenger-km: } & OC_{pk} = \dfrac{C_{total}}{\sum_{i} P_i \cdot d_i} \\[6pt]
\textbf{Bus Bunching Index: } & BBI = \dfrac{h_{front} - h_{rear}}{h_{scheduled}} \\[6pt]
\textbf{TSP Effectiveness Ratio: } & \eta_{TSP} = \dfrac{\Delta t_{delay,control}}{\Delta t_{delay,no\ priority}} \\[6pt]
\textbf{Service Reliability (on-time performance): } & SR = 1 - \dfrac{\vert t_{actual} - t_{scheduled} \vert}{tolerance} \\[6pt]
\textbf{APC Accuracy: } & A_{APC} = 1 - \dfrac{\vert C_{APC} - C_{manual} \vert}{C_{manual}} \\[6pt]
\textbf{Fleet Availability: } & F_{avail} = \dfrac{V_{operational}}{V_{total}}
\end{aligned}
$$

| Symbol | Quantity | Unit | Standard Range / Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| $h$ | Scheduled headway | minutes | 5 – 30 (urban bus) |
| $\sigma_h$ | Std. deviation of headway | minutes | ≤ 0.15 $\bar{h}$ |
| $C_{vh}$ | Coefficient of variation of headway | dimensionless | 0 – 0.5 (good service) |
| $W$ | Avg. passenger waiting time | minutes | ≤ 8 (urban target) |
| $LF$ | Load factor | dimensionless | 0.7 – 0.9 (optimal) |
| $P_i$ | Passengers on trip $i$ | persons | – |
| $d_i$ | Trip length of trip $i$ | km | – |
| $t_{tolerance}$ | Schedule tolerance window | minutes | ± 1 to ± 5 |
| $V_{operational}$ | Vehicles in revenue service | count | ≥ 85 % of $V_{total}$ |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!TIP]
> **Field Application Insight:** The above metrics drive **MoHUA (Ministry of Housing & Urban Affairs)** scorecards for Indian city bus services. The **UITP (International Association of Public Transport)** and **MoHUA's "Public Transport Quality of Service"** guidelines mandate *SR ≥ 95 %*, *SA ≥ 90 %*, and *W ≤ 8 min* as urban-bus benchmarks. Kochi Metro reports *SA ≈ 99.6 %* and *W ≈ 3.5 min* on the Aluva–Pettah corridor.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Average Passenger Waiting Time (Square-Root Formula)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **square-root waiting-time formula** is a *direct hit* in KTU ESE 2024 numericals. It relates the average waiting time of randomly arriving passengers to the scheduled and actual headways.

**Starting statement:** A transit service has a scheduled headway $h$ (minutes). Passengers arrive randomly (Poisson process) at the stop, with arrival rate $\lambda$ passengers per minute. Actual bus arrival headways follow a stochastic distribution with mean $\bar{h}$ and variance $\sigma_h^{\,2}$.

**Step 1 — Expected wait for scheduled (deterministic) headway:**
If buses arrive exactly every $h$ minutes, a passenger arriving at a uniformly random instant waits on average:

$$
E[W] = \dfrac{h}{2}
$$

This is the classical result for uniform arrival within a fixed interval.

**Step 2 — Adjustment for headway irregularity:**
When buses are delayed or bunched, passengers experience longer waits because a *gap* in the schedule is more likely. The variance of the headway $\sigma_h^{\,2}$ inflates the mean wait by the factor $(1 + C_{vh}^{\,2})$, where $C_{vh} = \sigma_h / \bar{h}$ is the coefficient of variation of headway.

**Step 3 — Combined expression:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W &= \dfrac{\bar{h}}{2} \cdot (1 + C_{vh}^{\,2}) \\[6pt]
  &= \dfrac{\bar{h}}{2} \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{\sigma_h^{\,2}}{\bar{h}^{\,2}}\right) \\[6pt]
  &= \dfrac{\bar{h}^{\,2} + \sigma_h^{\,2}}{2\,\bar{h}}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical illustration (Kerala context):**
Consider a KSRTC ordinary-service route with scheduled headway $h = 20$ min. Field measurements report $\sigma_h = 4$ min.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{h} &= 20 \text{ min} \\[4pt]
C_{vh} &= \dfrac{4}{20} = 0.20 \\[4pt]
W &= \dfrac{20}{2} \cdot (1 + 0.20^{\,2}) \\[4pt]
  &= 10 \cdot (1 + 0.04) \\[4pt]
  &= 10 \cdot 1.04 \\[4pt]
  &= 10.4 \text{ minutes}
\end{aligned}
$$

The irregular headway inflates the average wait by **0.4 minutes (24 seconds)** compared to the ideal deterministic case. With ITS-assisted holding control, $\sigma_h$ could drop to 2 min, giving:

$$
W_{ITS} = \dfrac{20}{2} \cdot \left(1 + 0.10^{\,2}\right) = 10 \cdot 1.01 = 10.1 \text{ min}
$$

— a measurable improvement in **perceived** service quality.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Required Fleet Size with Schedule Recovery Margin

**Starting statement:** A transit agency operates a circular route of length $L$ km with mean commercial speed $V_c$ km/h. Round-trip time $T_R = L / V_c$ hours. Layover/recovery time at terminals $T_L$. Required service frequency $f$ buses per hour. What is the fleet size $N$?

**Step 1 — Cycle time for one vehicle:**

$$
T_{cycle} = T_R + T_L
$$

**Step 2 — Required frequency translates to round trips per hour:**

$$
f = \dfrac{1}{h} \quad \text{(where } h \text{ = headway in hours)}
$$

**Step 3 — Each vehicle completes $1 / T_{cycle}$ cycles per hour.** To provide $f$ cycles per hour, we need:

$$
N = f \cdot T_{cycle} = \dfrac{T_{cycle}}{h}
$$

**Step 4 — Substituting:**

$$
N = \dfrac{T_R + T_L}{h} = \dfrac{L / V_c + T_L}{h}
$$

**Step 5 — Numerical illustration:**
Route length $L = 30$ km, $V_c = 20$ km/h, $T_L = 0.15$ h (9 min), desired headway $h = 0.25$ h (15 min).

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_R &= \dfrac{30}{20} = 1.50 \text{ h} \\[4pt]
T_{cycle} &= 1.50 + 0.15 = 1.65 \text{ h} \\[4pt]
N &= \dfrac{1.65}{0.25} = 6.6 \ \Rightarrow \ 7 \text{ buses (rounded up)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 6 — ITS-driven modification:**
With TSP and AVL-assisted dispatch, effective $V_c$ rises (say from 20 to 22 km/h) and schedule variance drops, allowing $T_L$ to shrink from 0.15 h to 0.10 h.

$$
N_{ITS} = \dfrac{30/22 + 0.10}{0.25} = \dfrac{1.3636 + 0.10}{0.25} = \dfrac{1.4636}{0.25} = 5.85 \ \Rightarrow \ 6 \text{ buses}
$$

**Result:** ITS enables a **saving of 1 bus** (≈14 % fleet reduction) for the same service frequency, or a **higher frequency** for the same fleet.

## 3.3 Analytical Decision Matrix — Choosing the Right ITS Application

| Application | Capital Cost (₹) | O&M Cost | Payback (yrs) | Best suited for | Data Output |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| AVL | Medium (₹ 8–15 lakh/bus) | Low | 2 – 3 | Large bus fleets (>50) | Real-time GPS, route adherence |
| APC | Medium-High (₹ 6–10 lakh/bus) | Low | 3 – 4 | Long-distance / metro | OD matrices, load profiles |
| EFC / Smart Card | High (₹ 20–50 crore citywide) | Medium | 4 – 6 | All urban transit | Transaction logs, OD pairs |
| PIS (static) | Low (₹ 1–3 lakh/stop) | Negligible | < 1 | All modes | Timetables, route maps |
| PIS (RT dynamic) | Medium (₹ 5–8 lakh/stop) | Medium | 2 – 3 | High-frequency urban transit | Live arrivals, disruptions |
| TSP | Medium (₹ 10–30 lakh/intersection) | Low | 2 – 4 | Bus corridors in signalised urban network | Green time, delay reduction |
| CCTV + AVL | High (₹ 3–6 lakh/bus) | Medium | 3 – 5 | All modes | Video, incident logs |
| FMS / Depot | High (₹ 50 lakh–₹ 2 crore) | Medium | 3 – 5 | Bus depots, large operators | KPI dashboards |
| MaaS Integration | High (₹ 5–20 crore) | High | 5 – 8 | Smart-city pilots | Multi-modal trip data |

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 📘 PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define Automatic Vehicle Location (AVL) and list any two key components used in an AVL system for public buses.*

**Model Answer:**

**AVL Definition:**
Automatic Vehicle Location (AVL) is an ITS application that uses a combination of **Global Navigation Satellite System (GNSS)**, **onboard sensors**, and **wireless communication** to determine, transmit, and display the real-time geographic position of a transit vehicle to a control centre and end-users.

**Two key components (any two of the following):**
1. **GNSS receiver** (GPS / GLONASS / IRNSS) — logs geographic coordinates of the bus at 10–30 s intervals.
2. **Communication modem** (4G/5G/GPRS) — transmits position data to the central server.
3. **Dead-reckoning module** (gyroscope + odometer) — provides fallback location when GNSS signal is lost (e.g., in tunnels).
4. **OBD-II/CAN-bus interface** — captures vehicle speed, door status, and engine diagnostics along with location.

*(Examiner expects: precise definition + 2 components, each in one line.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*What is Transit Signal Priority (TSP)? Differentiate between active and passive TSP.*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Transit Signal Priority (TSP) is an ITS strategy that modifies normal traffic-signal phasing or timing **to give priority treatment to public-transport vehicles** (buses, trams) at signalised intersections, reducing their travel time and improving schedule adherence.

**Active TSP (Conditional):**
- Triggered *only when* the transit vehicle is detected and meets a pre-defined condition (e.g., behind schedule by more than 2 minutes, or headway exceeding threshold).
- Provides **green extension** (extra green time) or **red truncation** (early green recall).
- Best for corridors with mixed traffic and high-frequency bus operations.

**Passive TSP (Pre-timed):**
- The signal-timing plan is **re-optimised statically** (e.g., longer green phases, offset adjustments) to favour transit movement along a corridor.
- Does not require vehicle detection at every cycle.
- Simpler and cheaper, but offers less precision than active TSP.

*(Examiner expects: definition + one-line difference for each type.)*

## 📕 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Provided)

### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

> *Answer the following:*
>
> **(a)** Explain the architecture of an Intelligent Transportation System (ITS) for public bus operations with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of AVL, APC, and EFC in this architecture. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A city bus route has a scheduled headway of **15 minutes**. Field measurements show that the actual headway has a standard deviation of **3 minutes**. Compute: (i) the coefficient of variation of headway, (ii) the average passenger waiting time, and (iii) the percentage increase in waiting time compared to perfectly regular headway. *(7 marks)*

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q-A (a) — Architecture & Roles (7 marks)

**Block Diagram Description (3 marks):**

> [!NOTE]
> *In the answer book, redraw the **Layered Architecture** from Section 4.1 of these notes. The five layers are: (1) Data Acquisition, (2) Communication, (3) Central Processing, (4) Service Delivery, (5) Users.*

**Role of AVL (1.5 marks):**
AVL continuously tracks the geographic position of every bus via GNSS and transmits it to the central control room. This enables *real-time monitoring*, *ETA prediction*, *incident response*, and *historical route analysis*. Operators can detect *bus bunching* in real time and issue corrective dispatch instructions.

**Role of APC (1 mark):**
APC sensors (infrared / camera / Wi-Fi) at bus doors automatically count boarding and alighting passengers at each stop. This generates **stop-level load profiles** and **origin–destination matrices**, which feed route planning, scheduling, and demand-responsive service decisions.

**Role of EFC (1.5 marks):**
Electronic Fare Collection (smart card / mobile wallet / QR code) automates the fare-payment process, eliminating cash handling. Beyond revenue, EFC provides **rich transaction data** that reveals travel patterns, supports fare-integration across modes, and reduces boarding dwell time by 30–50 %.

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q-A (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:**
Scheduled headway $h = 15$ min; standard deviation of actual headway $\sigma_h = 3$ min.

**(i) Coefficient of variation of headway $C_{vh}$ (2 marks):**

$$
C_{vh} = \dfrac{\sigma_h}{\bar{h}} = \dfrac{3}{15} = 0.20
$$

**(ii) Average passenger waiting time $W$ (3 marks):**

$$
W = \dfrac{\bar{h}}{2} \cdot (1 + C_{vh}^{\,2}) = \dfrac{15}{2} \cdot (1 + 0.20^{\,2})
$$

$$
W = 7.5 \cdot (1 + 0.04) = 7.5 \cdot 1.04 = 7.80 \text{ minutes}
$$

**(iii) Percentage increase in wait time vs. regular service (2 marks):**

Ideal wait (regular) = $h / 2 = 15 / 2 = 7.50$ min.
Actual wait = 7.80 min.

$$
\% \text{ increase} = \dfrac{7.80 - 7.50}{7.50} \times 100 = \dfrac{0.30}{7.50} \times 100 = 4.0\ \%
$$

**Final Answer:** $C_{vh} = 0.20$; $W = 7.80$ min; increase = **4.0 %**.

### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme]**

> *Answer the following:*
>
> **(a)** What are the major ITS applications deployed in public transportation? Discuss in detail the working of **Passenger Information System (PIS)** and **Fleet Management System (FMS)**. *(7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** A bus route is **40 km** long, operated at an average commercial speed of **25 km/h**, with a layover time of **10 minutes** per round trip. If the desired headway is **20 minutes**, calculate: (i) the round-trip time, (ii) the cycle time, and (iii) the number of buses required. Comment on how TSP and AVL can reduce the fleet requirement. *(7 marks)*

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q-B (a) — PIS & FMS (7 marks)

**Major ITS Applications (2 marks — listing):**

1. Automatic Vehicle Location (AVL)
2. Automatic Passenger Counter (APC)
3. Electronic Fare Collection (EFC) / Smart Card
4. Passenger Information System (PIS)
5. Transit Signal Priority (TSP)
6. Fleet Management & Dispatch
7. Depot & Asset Management
8. Integrated Mobility (MaaS)

**PIS — Working (2.5 marks):**
A Passenger Information System provides transit users with both *static* and *real-time* information.

- **Static PIS (S-PIS):** Route maps, schedules, and fare charts printed or displayed at stops and inside vehicles.
- **Dynamic Real-Time PIS (RT-PIS):** Uses AVL data from buses to compute live ETA at downstream stops. The system architecture is: **Bus AVL → Central Server → ETA Engine → PIS display / mobile app / IVR / SMS push**.
- **Display technologies:** LED, LCD, e-paper panels at stops; voice announcement (TTS) inside buses; APIs feeding Google Maps and dedicated operator apps.
- **Benefit:** Reduces *perceived* waiting time by 30–40 %; cuts passenger anxiety; increases ridership and satisfaction.

**FMS — Working (2.5 marks):**
A Fleet Management System integrates multiple ITS modules into a single operations platform.

- **Inputs:** AVL (real-time position), APC (passenger load), EFC (revenue), driver logs, depot inventory.
- **Core functions:** Real-time map monitoring, schedule adherence tracking, bus-bunching detection, **holding control** at terminals, dynamic headway adjustment, automatic generation of driver schedules and crew rosters.
- **Decision support:** When a bus is early, FMS issues a *hold* at a timing point; when bunching is detected, it may instruct the trailing bus to skip a stop or hold longer.
- **Output:** KPI dashboards for the operations manager, automatic exception reports, and historical performance analysis.

#### ✅ Model Solution to Q-B (b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:** $L = 40$ km, $V_c = 25$ km/h, $T_L = 10$ min = $\frac{1}{6}$ h, $h = 20$ min = $\frac{1}{3}$ h.

**(i) Round-trip time $T_R$ (2 marks):**

$$
T_R = \dfrac{L}{V_c} = \dfrac{40}{25} = 1.60 \text{ h} = 96 \text{ minutes}
$$

**(ii) Cycle time $T_{cycle}$ (1.5 marks):**

$$
T_{cycle} = T_R + T_L = 96 + 10 = 106 \text{ minutes}
$$

**(iii) Number of buses $N$ (2 marks):**

$$
N = \dfrac{T_{cycle}}{h} = \dfrac{106}{20} = 5.30 \ \Rightarrow \ 6 \text{ buses (rounded up)}
$$

**Comment — ITS impact (1.5 marks):**

> With **TSP**, signal-related delays are reduced, effectively raising $V_c$ from 25 to ~28 km/h → $T_R = 40/28 = 1.43$ h = 85.7 min. With **AVL-based holding control**, layover can be optimised from 10 to 7 min, since schedule adherence is tighter. New cycle time = 85.7 + 7 = 92.7 min → $N = 92.7 / 20 = 4.6 \Rightarrow$ **5 buses**. Thus ITS saves **1 bus (~17 %)** while maintaining the same headway.

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls where students lose marks in ITS questions:**
> 1. **Confusing AVL with APC** — AVL tracks the *vehicle*; APC tracks *passengers*. Examiners deduct 1 mark for this swap.
> 2. **Writing the waiting-time formula without defining $C_{vh}$** — always state $C_{vh} = \sigma_h / \bar{h}$ before applying $W = (h/2)(1 + C_{vh}^{\,2})$.
> 3. **Forgetting to round up the fleet size** — $N = 5.30$ must be reported as **6 buses**, not 5.30. This is a recurring 1-mark loss.
> 4. **Drawing block diagrams without labels** — every block in the diagram must carry a clear function label; arrows must show data/control flow direction.
> 5. **Neglecting units** in numerical answers — always write "min", "km/h", or "buses" explicitly.
> 6. **Mixing up Active vs. Passive TSP** — Active is *conditional* (per-vehicle, real-time); Passive is *pre-timed* (corridor-wide, static).

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Application of ITS"**

- ✅ ITS in public transport = integration of **AVL + APC + EFC + PIS + TSP + FMS** under a unified control centre.
- ✅ **AVL** = vehicle position via GNSS; **APC** = passenger count via sensors; **EFC** = fare via smart card/NFC.
- ✅ **PIS** has two flavours: *static* (timetable, route map) and *dynamic real-time* (live ETA via app/display).
- ✅ **TSP** active = conditional priority (green extension, red truncation); passive = re-timed signal plan.
- ✅ **FMS** integrates all ITS data streams to optimise dispatch, holding, and crew scheduling.
- ✅ **Key metric — average wait time:** $W = (\bar{h}/2) \cdot (1 + C_{vh}^{\,2})$; same headway but higher variance → higher wait.
- ✅ **Key metric — schedule adherence:** $SA = (N_{on-time}/N_{total}) \times 100\ \%$; KTU benchmark ≥ 90 %.
- ✅ **Key metric — load factor:** $LF = P_{actual}/P_{capacity}$; optimal urban range **0.7 – 0.9**.
- ✅ **Fleet size formula:** $N = (L/V_c + T_L)/h$ — always **round up** to the next integer.
- ✅ ITS-enabled improvements: higher $V_c$ (via TSP), lower $\sigma_h$ (via AVL holding), reduced $T_L$ → **fewer buses for same frequency**.
- ✅ ITS data feeds the **OD matrix**, which is the foundation of *route rationalisation* and *demand-responsive transit*.
- ✅ MoHUA/UITP benchmarks for Indian urban bus: *SA ≥ 90 %*, *SR ≥ 95 %*, *W ≤ 8 min*, *fleet utilisation ≥ 85 %*.
- ✅ *Kerala context to remember:* Kochi Metro, KSRTC, and the Kochi Water Metro are the most cited real-world examples for KTU answers.
- ✅ Always label diagrams, state assumptions, and carry units to the final numeric answer for full marks.
